## CHAR-1 — Wilful sinning (189 rows: E=180 S=9 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=14146, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning reveals the character of God negatively: it is the one thing the verse evidence consistently shows God holding the person morally accountable for, which illuminates God as the moral reference point against whom all wilful sin is ultimately directed. Psa 51:4 states the paradigmatic formula — "Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight" — locating God as the primary party offended by every act of wilful sinning, even sins with human victims (David's sin against Bathsheba and Uriah). Gen 39:9 (Joseph) frames the refusal to sin as inseparable from reverence for God: "How then can I do this great wickedness and sin against God?" — implying that God's moral character defines the boundary the sinning will crosses. The characteristic thus reflects God's holiness by negation: wilful sinning is the inner act that violates what God is.

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=14147, S) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-1]** S — The verse corpus for CHAR-1 contains no attribution of wilful sinning to God. This absence is analytically significant: the entire 98-verse corpus is structured around human sinning against God (VCG-01, 32V), human sinning against persons (VCG-02, 18V), and human sinning requiring restraint or remedy. God appears throughout as the offended party, the restraining agent (Gen 20:6 — "it was I who kept you from sinning against me"), and the judge (Eze 18:4 — "the soul who sins shall die"), but never as a sinning subject. The silence establishes wilful sinning as an exclusively creaturely phenomenon — something that arises from the constitution of the creature, not from the nature of God.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=14148, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The structural silence on God possessing wilful sinning (see T0.1.2) combined with the corpus's consistent framing of God as moral standard and judge (Eze 18:4; Eze 18:20; Gen 20:6; Eze 3:21) suggests that wilful sinning belongs to the fallen condition of the image-bearer rather than to the original divine image. The capacity for moral choice — the will — is part of the image; sinning is what happens when that will is turned against its Maker. 1Jo 2:1 ("I am writing these things to you so that you may not sin") presupposes that sinning is avoidable — that believers retain moral agency — but it is something Scripture explicitly aims to prevent rather than express. The imago Dei includes the will; wilful sinning represents the will's defection from the direction the image was designed to express.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=14149, S) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-1]** S — The verse corpus offers no evidence that wilful sinning serves a positive created purpose. No verse in the 98-verse corpus describes sinning as enabling, equipping, or fulfilling the person. On the contrary, the evidence uniformly presents sinning as producing rupture (national defeat, drought, exile — 1Ki 8:33/35), death (Eze 18:4/20), and damage to the one sinned against (Gen 20:9 — "you have brought on me and my kingdom a great sin"). The evidence is silent on any positive created purpose for wilful sinning — which analytically confirms that it belongs to the fallen condition, not to a positive dimension of the created design.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=14150, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus evidence points to wilful sinning as arising from the fallen condition, not from the original created design. Gen 39:9 (Joseph naming sinning as "great wickedness") and Eze 28:16 ("you sinned; so I cast you as a profane thing from the mountain of God") both treat sinning as a departure from an original blameless state. The cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04: Lev 4–5 sin-offering system) presupposes that sin is real and requires remedy — a remedial structure that would be unnecessary if sinning were part of the original design. The deterrence register (VCG-03: Gen 20:6, Exo 20:20, Psa 4:4, 1Jo 2:1) likewise frames sinning as something to be prevented, guarded against, and resisted — not something that belongs to what humans were made to be. The evidence collectively locates wilful sinning in the fallen condition.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=14151, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The verse corpus contains limited but present evidence of future-orientation. Eze 3:21 ("if you warn the righteous person not to sin, and he does not sin, he shall surely live") frames non-sinning as the path toward life — implying that the person is moving away from wilful sinning toward a future of sustained righteousness. Joh 5:14 ("sin no more, that nothing worse may happen to you") and Joh 8:11 ("go, and from now on sin no more") both present Jesus issuing a future-directed command after forgiveness — pointing to a trajectory beyond sinning rather than entrapment within it. 1Jo 2:1 explicitly names the telos: "I am writing these things to you so that you may not sin." The trajectory evidenced is away from wilful sinning — not deeper into it — and the cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04) establishes that each instance of sinning has a prescribed remedy pointing back toward restored standing. The evidence is oriented toward the cessation and transcending of wilful sinning.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=14152, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning does not express the divine image positively, but it does presuppose the image's most distinctive feature: genuine moral agency. The corpus consistently treats sinning as a personal, accountable, wilful act. Eze 18:4 declares "the soul who sins shall die" — with no mitigation for ignorance or compulsion in the wilful register. Eze 18:20 underscores personal ownership: "the soul who sins alone shall die; the son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father." Gen 43:9 (Judah) and Gen 44:32 both show a human person voluntarily assuming moral accountability for another — an act only possible because genuine moral agency exists. The capacity for wilful sinning thus presupposes the image-dimension of moral agency: the person who can wilfully sin is the same person who can wilfully choose righteousness. The image provides the capacity; sinning is its misuse.

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=14153, E) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence establishes wilful sinning as exclusively creaturely with no divine analogue. Every instance of sinning in the corpus is human or creaturely: human persons sin against God (VCG-01), against other persons (VCG-02), or even angels sin (2Pe 2:4 — "God did not spare angels when they sinned"). God appears uniformly as the offended party, the restrainer, or the judge — never as a sinning subject. The closest the corpus comes to a divine-side parallel is Gen 20:6, where God actively restrains Abimelech from sinning — positioning God as the external check on creaturely sinning. There is no divine analogue to wilful sinning evidenced in this corpus.

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=14154, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The presence of wilful sinning reveals a will that has turned against its Maker — a damaged but not destroyed divine image. The evidence treats sinning as a real inner act carrying personal moral consequence (Eze 18:4/20), not as something that annihilates the person's moral agency or their status as an image-bearer. Eze 3:21 presupposes that a sinning person can still be warned and can still choose not to sin — moral agency persists. 1Jo 1:10 ("if we say we have not sinned, we make him a liar") treats sinning as a universal creaturely condition whose honest acknowledgment itself requires a functioning conscience. The pattern suggests: the divine image remains (the will is present, moral agency persists), but wilful sinning represents that image operating in defection from its designed orientation toward God.

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=14155, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus contains clear typological deployment of wilful sinning in two directions. First, Eze 28:16 ("you sinned; so I cast you as a profane thing from the mountain of God, O guardian cherub") uses the cherub's sinning as a paradigmatic instance that illuminates the inner dynamic of how sinning produces expulsion from the divine presence — a pattern that maps onto human sinning and its consequences. Second, the Levitical sin-offering system (VCG-04: Lev 4:14, 4:22, 4:23, 4:27, 4:28, 4:35, 5:6–13) is typological throughout — the sacrificial remedy for wilful sinning points forward to the substitutionary atonement that resolves sin's debt. The Lev 5:13 formula ("and he shall be forgiven") and Lev 4:35 ("the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin which he has committed, and he shall be forgiven") foreshadow a forgiving transaction that the NT corpus (VCG-05) casts as fulfilled in Christ's advocacy (1Jo 2:1: "we have an advocate with the Father, Jesus Christ the righteous").

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=14156, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The typological direction is bi-directional. In the Levitical sin-offering corpus (VCG-04), the human instance of sinning points forward to a divine resolution: the sacrificial system is a human-facing institution that anticipates the divine act of atonement. In the Eze 28:16 cherub passage, the creaturely (angelic) instance of sinning establishes the pattern — falling from the presence of God — that the human instance participates in and illuminates. The NT corpus (VCG-05) shows the reverse movement: 1Jo 2:1's advocacy language presupposes that Christ (the divine) addresses the human instance of sinning, making the divine-to-human direction primary in the NT resolution of the type.

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=14157, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Typological use is evidenced (see T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded by the positive finding above.

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=14158, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Wilful sinning." The name encodes a binary claim: (1) that sinning is a *wilful* act — not accidental, not compelled, but chosen by the inner person with awareness of what is being done; and (2) that this is *sinning* — a relational act that violates a standard. The name signals that the defining feature is not the category of wrong committed but the quality of the inner act: the will's knowing engagement with the wrong. This is confirmed by the definitional separation from CHAR-2 (Unintentional sinning) — that characteristic occupies its own sub-group precisely because the corpus distinguishes deliberate from inadvertent wrongdoing. The name "Wilful sinning" thus foregrounds volition as the constitutive element: what makes this characteristic analytically distinct is not the content of the sin but the inner quality of the will that commits it.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=14159, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The primary Hebrew term is cha.ta (H2398), present in all 86 OT verses of the corpus. At the definitional level, cha.ta is a verb — an action-term — meaning to miss the mark, to offend, to incur guilt. The NT primary term is hamartanō (G0264), present in the NT VCG-05 corpus and also the interpersonal VCG-02 instances (Mat 18:15, Luk 17:3/4). Hamartanō is likewise a verb, a direct Greek counterpart to cha.ta in semantic range. The paraptōma (G3900) appears once in VCG-05 (Gal 6:1 — "caught in any transgression"). The definitional-level reading of both primary terms reveals that sinning is expressed verbally — as an event, an act, a thing done or committed — rather than as a static condition or enduring state. Both terms foreground the act of sinning over the accumulated condition of sin.

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=14160, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The name "Wilful sinning" carries strong relational and directional implications. The corpus structures sinning along three directional axes: (1) vertical — sinning against God (VCG-01: "sin against God" Gen 39:9; "against you, you only" Psa 51:4; "sinned against me" Eze 14:13); (2) horizontal — sinning against persons (VCG-02: "sin against his neighbor" 1Ki 8:31; "sins against you" Mat 18:15); and (3) reflexive — sinning against oneself (1Cor 6:18: "the sexually immoral person sins against his own body"; Pro 8:36: sinning injures oneself). These three directions are constitutive: the name implies that wilful sinning is always sinning *against* someone — it is irreducibly relational. The constitutional implication is that sinning engages the will in a movement *away from* a relational bond that was owed, making the will the primary constitutional faculty at stake.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=14161, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The verse corpus establishes wilful sinning primarily as an *act* — a discrete event of the will. The dominant grammatical form throughout is the cha.ta verb in the perfect or imperfect (committed, commits, sinned), naming an act rather than a state. "The soul who sins shall die" (Eze 18:4) and "Israel has sinned" (Jos 7:11) both describe a completed act with consequences. The cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04) further confirms this: "the sin which he has committed" (Lev 4:28) triggers a prescribed response — an act-to-remedy structure that makes sense only if sinning is an event rather than a standing condition. However, the corpus also contains dispositional evidence: Hos 4:7 ("the more they increased, the more they sinned against me") and Hos 13:2 ("they sin more and more") describe sinning as an escalating pattern that moves beyond single acts toward an entrenched orientation. The characteristic is primarily an act but contains a dispositional trajectory when sustained.

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=14162, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-1]** E — At first encounter the characteristic has constituent elements. The VCG structure of M10-J itself reveals four distinct registers: VCG-01 (God-directed), VCG-02 (interpersonal), VCG-03 (deterred/restrained — the conscience-and-will register), VCG-04 (requiring ritual remedy — the cultic-legal register). Within each register the inner-being structure differs: in VCG-01, the act involves the will turning against God's covenant claim; in VCG-02, the act involves a relational harm directed at a person; in VCG-03, the act is averted or protested (conscience-active resistance); in VCG-04, the act has occurred and the cultic machinery responds. Even at this early encounter, wilful sinning is not a simple undivided act but a constellation of related inner events sharing the core of a deliberate will engaging wrongdoing.

---

#### T1.2.3 (cf=14163, E) — What is the current best working description of this characteristic — encapsulating its constitutional location, the faculties it primarily engages, and its impact on the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Working description: *Wilful sinning is a volitional act of the inner person — primarily located in the will — in which the person knowingly commits a wrong directed against God, another person, or the self. It engages the will as the primary faculty, the conscience as the witness (whether active in restraint, as in VCG-03, or present as the moral register that recognises the act as wrong), and moral evaluation as the standard against which the act is measured. Its impact is rupture: it damages the vertical relationship with God (producing guilt, divine anger, and the need for atonement), horizontal relationships with persons (producing harm and requiring forgiveness), and the person's own moral integrity (self-damage, Psa 51:4; Pro 8:36; 1Cor 6:18). The act creates a debt — moral, relational, and (in the cultic corpus) ritual — that requires resolution.* Evidence: Gen 39:9 (will knowingly refuses); Psa 51:4 (vertical rupture named); Mat 18:15 (horizontal harm requiring confrontation); Lev 4:28/35 (cultic debt and remedy); 1Jo 2:1 (advocate for the sinning believer).

---

#### T1.3.1 (cf=14164, E) — What is the structural opposite of this characteristic — the inner-being reality that stands against or excludes it?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The structural opposite is evidenced in two registers. (1) *Integrity of heart / blamelessness*: Gen 20:6 — God says Abimelech "acted from integrity of heart" and intervened to prevent sinning. The opposite of wilful sinning is the will aligned with integrity, acting in correspondence with moral truth rather than against it. (2) *Righteousness / not sinning*: Eze 3:21 — "if you warn the righteous person not to sin, and he does not sin, he shall surely live" places non-sinning and righteousness as a linked opposite pair. Eze 18:4/20 contrast the sinning soul (who dies) with the righteous person. Psa 119:11 ("I have stored up your word in my heart, that I might not sin against you") presents the word-saturated will as the inner condition that opposes sinning. The structural opposites are integrity, righteousness, and will-alignment with God's word.

---

#### T1.3.2 (cf=14165, E) — What does this characteristic explicitly exclude or resist?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning explicitly excludes several inner conditions. (1) *Divine restraint / protection*: Gen 20:6 — God's external intervention is what prevented sinning; sinning is what happens when that protection is not operative or is refused. (2) *Intercession*: 1Sa 12:23 — Samuel identifies ceasing to pray for Israel as itself a sin; sinning and faithful intercession stand in opposition. (3) *Covenant faithfulness*: Jos 7:11 — "Israel has sinned; they have transgressed my covenant" — sinning is the act of covenant-breaking. (4) *God's word dwelling in the heart*: Psa 119:11 — the stored word guards against sinning. (5) *Self-restraint of speech*: Psa 39:1 — guarding the mouth resists sinning with the tongue. The characteristic resists and excludes every inner condition that aligns the will with God: covenant faithfulness, stored word, intercession, restraint, and divine protection.

---

#### T1.3.3 (cf=14166, E) — What is this characteristic not — where does it end and something else begin?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence marks three clear boundaries. (1) *Not identical to unintentional sinning*: the cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04) includes Lev 4:22 ("when a leader sins, doing unintentionally") and Lev 5:15 ("commits a breach of faith and sins unintentionally") — this category (CHAR-2 in the programme) is present within VCG-04 as the cultic-remedy context, but the wilful register proper is marked by the knowing, deliberate quality. (2) *Not guilt as an inner state*: the corpus distinguishes the act of sinning (CHAR-1) from the experience of guilt that follows it (CHAR-17 — Guilt as inner-being state). 2Sa 12:13 shows sinning acknowledged, then guilt removed by God; the sequence distinguishes act from experienced condition. (3) *Not refusal to repent*: Hos 4:7 and 13:2 (escalating sinning) border on CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent), but the wilful-sinning register remains at the level of the act committed, not the subsequent refusal of the will to turn from it. The boundary between CHAR-1 and CHAR-5 is temporal: the act of sinning precedes the question of whether to repent.

---

#### T1.4.1 (cf=14167, E) — In what distinct ways does this characteristic operate within the inner person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus reveals four distinct modes of operation. (1) *As a willed act of transgression directed vertically against God* (VCG-01): the inner person knowingly crosses a boundary God has set — Gen 39:9 (Joseph's refusal names the act's relational target); Eze 18:4 (the individual soul's sinning carries mortal consequence). (2) *As a willed harm directed horizontally against another person* (VCG-02): the inner wrongdoing is enacted in the relational sphere — "sin against his neighbor" (1Ki 8:31), "sins against you" (Mat 18:15). (3) *As a reflexive act of self-damage* (Pro 8:36; 1Cor 6:18): sinning against wisdom or against one's own body shows the will turning destructively inward. (4) *As an act that grows in intensity and compulsion over time* (Hos 4:7; 13:2): "the more they sinned against me" — a mode where the single act opens toward a pattern. These four modes share the will as the operative faculty but differ in directionality.

---

#### T1.4.2 (cf=14168, E) — Does this characteristic operate differently depending on context, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence shows clear contextual differentiation. In the God-directed register (VCG-01), sinning is primarily a covenantal and relational act with communal consequence: 1Ki 8:33/35 show national defeat and drought following the people's sinning against God; the scope is corporate. In the interpersonal register (VCG-02), sinning is personal and remedial: Mat 18:15 specifies a repair process (confrontation → listening → forgiveness) that is individual. In the cultic register (VCG-04), sinning operates as an objective cultic fact requiring prescribed remediation regardless of the person's subjective emotional state — the sin offering is brought on realisation, not on feeling. The constitutional level also differs: in Gen 20:6, God restrains sinning from outside the person; in Psa 119:11 and Psa 4:4, the internal resources of stored word and reflective pondering are the operative mechanisms. The characteristic operates at both the social/communal and individual constitutional levels.

---

#### T1.4.3 (cf=14169, E) — Does this characteristic have a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — and if so, how does it function?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Speech-based sinning is explicitly evidenced. Psa 39:1 ("I will guard my ways, that I may not sin with my tongue") locates sinning's communicative mode in unguarded speech — the tongue as the instrument. Job 2:10 ("Job did not sin with his lips") confirms that under suffering the lips are the point of potential sinning. Job 31:30 ("I have not let my mouth sin by asking for his life with a curse") shows speech as a vehicle for directing sinful aggression against an enemy. Ecc 5:6 ("let not your mouth lead you into sin") frames the mouth as an organ that leads the whole person into sin through rash speech before the divine messenger. Isa 29:21 shows sinning through false verbal accusation in public proceedings. The communicative mode operates in the outward direction (speech expressing inner sinful orientation) and the self-implicating direction (rash speech creating liability before God).

---

#### T1.5.1 (cf=14170, E) — What is the first or most immediate inner-being response to receiving or encountering this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus reveals two divergent immediate responses to encountering wilful sinning. (1) *Conscience-activation and moral recognition*: Gen 42:22 — Reuben's immediate response to the brothers' sinning against Joseph is to invoke the reckoning that follows: "so now there comes a reckoning for his blood." The guilty party's immediate response is awareness of consequence. Gen 20:9 — Abimelech's immediate response to learning of the sin brought upon him is protest and confrontation: "you have brought on me and my kingdom a great sin." (2) *Moral refusal and naming*: Gen 39:9 — Joseph's immediate response to the temptation is to name it as "great wickedness and sin against God" — conscience-active refusal before the act is committed. The pattern suggests that the most immediate inner-being response to encountering wilful sinning (whether in oneself or from another) is moral recognition — the conscience registering the moral quality of the act.

---

#### T1.5.2 (cf=14171, E) — Is the immediate response consistent across the verse evidence, or does it vary?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The immediate response varies across the corpus. In VCG-03 (deterrence/restraint), the immediate response is refusal and resistance (Gen 39:9, Job 1:22, Psa 4:4, Psa 39:1). In VCG-01 (God-directed sinning), the immediate response evidenced is often external consequence rather than internal registration — drought (1Ki 8:35), defeat (1Ki 8:33), expulsion (Eze 28:16). In VCG-02 (interpersonal), the immediate response includes protest (Gen 20:9), accountability-assumption (Gen 43:9, Gen 44:32), and confrontation-call (Mat 18:15). In VCG-04 (cultic), the immediate response is the realisation of guilt triggering the obligation to bring an offering (Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed is made known to him"). The variation suggests that immediate response is shaped by the directional mode (God-directed vs interpersonal vs reflexive) and by whether the person is the agent or the recipient of the sinning.

---

#### T1.5.3 (cf=14172, E) — Where the verse evidence is silent on immediate response, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not globally silent on immediate response (see T1.5.1 and T1.5.2), but the corpus is notably sparse on the immediate inner response of the sinning agent in the God-directed register (VCG-01). The verses in VCG-01 primarily record the fact of sinning and its consequences (national disaster, exile) rather than the sinning person's immediate inner experience of committing the act. Eze 18:4/20 declare outcome (death) without describing the sinner's immediate inner response at the moment of sinning. This silence may reflect the prophetic genre's interest in consequence over phenomenology.

---

#### T1.6.1 (cf=14173, E) — What does this characteristic produce in the inner being over time?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Sustained wilful sinning produces three evidenced trajectories in the inner being. (1) *Escalating compulsion*: Hos 4:7 ("the more they increased, the more they sinned against me") and Hos 13:2 ("they sin more and more, and make for themselves metal images") show sinning intensifying with each repetition — a self-reinforcing dynamic where the will's engagement with wrongdoing makes subsequent sinning more likely and more elaborate. (2) *Corporate moral deformation*: Isa 1:4 describes a "sinful nation, a people laden with iniquity" — collective identity reshaped around sinning, with estrangement from God as the settled condition. (3) *Accumulation of debt and consequence*: 1Ki 8:33/35/50 show sinning accumulating into a record that triggers defeat, drought, and captivity — sustained sinning produces a weight of consequence that falls on communities and individuals.

---

#### T1.6.2 (cf=14174, E) — What states, qualities, capacities, or orientations does sustained exposure to or possession of this characteristic establish?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The sustained-effect evidence points to: (1) *Vulnerability to temptation*: Neh 13:26 — Solomon's sustained exposure to foreign women produced a sinning that "even him" — the beloved and gifted king — could not resist, suggesting that sustained sinning erodes the will's resistance capacity. (2) *Estrangement from God*: Isa 1:4 ("they have forsaken the Lord, they have despised the Holy One of Israel, they are utterly estranged") describes a settled orientation of alienation as the outcome of sustained national sinning. (3) *Increased elaborateness of idolatry*: Hos 13:2 — sinning "more and more" produces "metal images, idols skillfully made" — an escalating investment in what replaces God. (4) *Moral weight on subsequent generations*: Neh 13:26 (Solomon's foreign-women vulnerability), the cultic-atonement system's communal dimension (Lev 4:3 — "if it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people") suggest that sustained sinning by leaders creates a moral weight borne by communities.

---

#### T1.6.3 (cf=14175, E) — Does the sustained effect differ from the immediate response — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The sustained effect differs markedly from the immediate response. The immediate response (T1.5.1) is moral recognition — conscience registering the act. The sustained effect is compound: escalating compulsion (Hos 4:7; 13:2), estrangement (Isa 1:4), and consequence-accumulation (1Ki 8:33–50). The critical difference is directional: the immediate response includes conscience-active elements that resist or protest sinning, whereas the sustained effect shows conscience being progressively overridden — the will habituates to sinning and the moral recognition becomes less operative. This difference is the evidence base for the programme's distinction between CHAR-1 (wilful sinning as act) and CHAR-5 (refusal to repent) / CHAR-6 (habitual defection) — sustained wilful sinning without repentance migrates toward the habitual-defection register.

---

#### T1.7.1 (cf=14176, E) — What inner conditions or orientations enable genuine reception of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — "Reception" requires clarification for this characteristic: wilful sinning is not received from outside in the way a gift or grace is received; it is generated by the person's own will. The inner conditions that enable its occurrence (rather than reception) are evidenced as: (1) *Absence of divine restraint*: Gen 20:6 — Abimelech sinned only in ignorance; God's external intervention prevented him. The absence of that restraint enables the will to proceed. (2) *Social pressure overriding conscience*: 1Sa 15:24 — Saul sinned because "I feared the people and obeyed their voice" — social pressure as the enabling condition that overrode the will's alignment with God's command. (3) *Vulnerability through exposure*: Neh 13:26 — foreign women as the corrupting influence that enabled Solomon's sinning. (4) *Temptation presenting the opportunity*: Neh 6:13 — enemies constructed a trap designed to produce sinning. These are conditions that the will then responds to by sinning.

---

#### T1.7.2 (cf=14177, E) — What inner conditions block, distort, or prevent reception?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The blocking conditions are explicitly evidenced. (1) *Stored word in the heart*: Psa 119:11 ("I have stored up your word in my heart, that I might not sin against you") — the word internalised is the inner bulwark against sinning. (2) *Fear of God before the eyes*: Exo 20:20 ("that the fear of him may be before you, that you may not sin") — the fear of God as an operative inner presence that restrains the will. (3) *Deliberate inner pondering*: Psa 4:4 ("ponder in your own hearts on your beds, and be silent") — reflective inwardness as a check on anger-driven sinning. (4) *Mouth-guarding*: Psa 39:1; Job 31:30 — disciplined restraint of speech as a specific block against speech-mode sinning. (5) *Divine external restraint*: Gen 20:6 — God's intervention preventing the sinning Abimelech would otherwise have committed. The blocking conditions span the external (divine restraint) and the internal (word, fear, pondering, speech-discipline).

---

#### T1.7.3 (cf=14178, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The person who encounters the temptation to sin but does not — the character of the sinning-resisted state — is evidenced in several portraits. Joseph in Gen 39:9: the temptation is present; the inner state is one of named moral clarity ("How then can I do this great wickedness?") and upward reference (God's claim). Job in Job 1:22 and 2:10: under devastating suffering, Job's inner state is one of sustained moral restraint — conscience-active control even under pressure. Nehemiah in Neh 6:13: the trap is set; his inner state involves recognising the mechanism ("for this purpose he was hired") and refusing to yield. The common feature is that the non-sinning person retains full moral agency, with the will actively engaged in the decision not to sin rather than simply being unaware of the choice. The inner-being state is one of conscious, conscience-engaged refusal.

---

#### T1.8.1 (cf=14179, E) — What is the primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic from the programme's dimension vocabulary?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The primary inner-being dimension of wilful sinning is **volition / will** — the capacity to choose and to act. This is established by the definitional criterion ("the will committing a known wrong") and confirmed throughout the corpus: the acts of sinning in VCG-01 (Gen 39:9 refusal, Eze 18:4/20 personal accountability), VCG-02 (1Ki 8:31 sin against neighbor), and VCG-05 (1Jo 2:1 — "so that you may not sin") all presuppose a will that chooses. The term cha.ta as a verb further confirms this: the act of sinning is volitional, not merely circumstantial. The will is the seat of the characteristic; sinning is what the will does when it turns against the moral order it was designed to uphold.

---

#### T1.8.2 (cf=14180, E) — What evidence from the verse evidence supports this classification?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Multiple lines of evidence support the will-dimension classification. (1) Accountability: Eze 18:20 ("the soul who sins alone shall die; the son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father") — personal accountability presupposes a personal will. (2) Resistance possible: VCG-03's entire evidence base (Gen 39:9, Exo 20:20, Job 1:22, Psa 4:4, Psa 39:1, 1Jo 2:1) shows that sinning can be resisted, which only makes sense if the will is the operative faculty. (3) Social pressure overriding it: 1Sa 15:24 ("I feared the people and obeyed their voice") shows the will being deflected by pressure — volitional language throughout. (4) The deterrence formula: Exo 20:20 ("that you may not sin") and Eze 3:21 ("if you warn the righteous person not to sin, and he does not sin") both treat not-sinning as a decision that warning and moral instruction can produce — confirming the will's centrality.

---

#### T1.8.3 (cf=14181, E) — Does this characteristic carry secondary dimensions — and if so, what are they, and do they compete with the primary classification?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Secondary dimensions are evidenced without competing with the primary will-classification. (1) *Conscience*: VCG-03's deterrence register engages conscience as the faculty that recognises the moral quality of the act (Gen 39:9 — "great wickedness"; Job 1:22 — restraint of charge against God). Conscience operates as the inner witness to the will's decision but is not itself the seat of the sinning. (2) *Moral evaluation*: the entire corpus presupposes a moral standard against which the act is measured as wrong — the law (Deu 19:15; 24:4), God's command (Eze 14:13), the relational claim of the neighbour (Mat 18:15). Moral evaluation is engaged but functions as the background standard, not the active faculty producing the act. (3) *Body*: speech-mode sinning (Psa 39:1, Job 2:10, Ecc 5:6) and bodily sinning (1Cor 6:18) implicate the body as the expressive organ. These secondary dimensions do not compete with the primary will-classification; they describe what the will works through and against.

---

T1 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T1.1.1–T1.1.3, T1.2.1–T1.2.3, T1.3.1–T1.3.3, T1.4.1–T1.4.3, T1.5.1–T1.5.3, T1.6.1–T1.6.3, T1.7.1–T1.7.3, T1.8.1–T1.8.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-1] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T1.5.3 is E but names the evidential gap location — PASS)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited verses in §3: Gen 20:6/9/39:9/42:22/43:9/44:32; Exo 20:20; Lev 4:3/22/23/28/35; Lev 5:15; Deu 19:15/24:4; Jos 7:11; 1Sa 12:23/15:24; 1Ki 8:31/33/35/50; Neh 6:13/13:26; Job 1:22/2:10/31:30; Psa 4:4/39:1/51:4/119:11; Pro 8:36; Ecc 5:6; Isa 1:4/29:21; Isa 42:24; Eze 3:21/14:13/18:4/18:20/28:16; Hos 4:7/13:2; Mat 18:15; 1Cor 6:18; 1Jo 2:1)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T2.


---

## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=14182, S) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-1]** S — The 98-verse corpus contains no explicit location of wilful sinning at the spirit level. The Hebrew ruach or Greek pneuma terms do not appear in the CHAR-1 evidence base in connection with the sinning act. The verse evidence locates sinning in the will, the soul (nephesh — Eze 18:4), the heart (Job 1:5), and the body/lips (Psa 39:1; Job 2:10), but not explicitly in the spirit.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=14183, S) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-1]** S — No evidence in the CHAR-1 corpus locates the origin of wilful sinning at the spirit level. The origin language in the corpus points to the heart as the inner source (Job 1:5 — "cursed God in their hearts"), the will as the active faculty (Eze 18:4; Gen 39:9), and external temptation as the triggering condition (Neh 13:26; Neh 6:13; 1Sa 15:24). Spirit-level origin is not evidenced.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=14184, S) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** S — Since spirit-level location is not evidenced (T2.1.1 and T2.1.2), this prompt cannot be answered from the corpus. The absence of spirit-level location may indicate that wilful sinning operates in the middle layers of the inner constitution (will, heart, soul) rather than at the deepest constitutional depth where the person's orientation toward God is constituted. However, inferring from the absence is not warranted beyond noting it.

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=14185, S) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** S — The evidence is silent on spirit-level location. No verse in §3 explicitly connects wilful sinning to the spirit. This is noted.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=14186, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus evidences constitutional movement primarily in the soul-to-body direction. Speech-based sinning (Psa 39:1 — "sin with my tongue"; Job 2:10 — "sin with his lips"; Ecc 5:6 — "let not your mouth lead you into sin") shows inner sinful intention expressing through the body's speech organs. 1Cor 6:18 shows the reverse — bodily sinning feeding back: "the sexually immoral person sins against his own body" — with the body as both the expressive medium and the site of the damage. The heart-to-action movement is evidenced in Job 1:5 (children may have "sinned, and cursed God in their hearts" — inner heart-orientation potentially producing culpable condition) and in the deterrence corpus (Psa 119:11 — word stored in heart to prevent the will's outward sinful action). Movement from inner to outer (heart/will → speech/act) is the dominant constitutional movement evidenced.

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=14187, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidenced sequence is: *inner moral orientation (heart/will) → decision point → outward act → relational consequence*. Gen 39:9 (Joseph) shows the inner point — conscience names the act as "great wickedness" before the decision is made, then the decision point is the refusal. 1Sa 15:24 shows an alternative sequence: social pressure (external) → will-deflection (internal) → act of disobedience (outward). The cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04) shows the post-act sequence: "the sin which he has committed is made known to him" (Lev 4:23) — the act precedes the realisation → realisation triggers the cultic obligation. Ecc 5:6 shows the mouth leading the person into sin — speech-organ as a site where inner restlessness externalises before the person has named it as sin.

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=14188, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Constitutional movement is evidenced (see T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded by the positive finding above.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=14189, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. Eze 18:4 employs the nephesh (soul) term explicitly: "the soul of the father as well as the soul of the son is mine: the soul who sins shall die." Eze 18:20 repeats: "the soul who sins shall die." These declarations locate wilful sinning explicitly at the soul level — the individual nephesh is the unit of moral accountability. The soul is the locus of both the sinning act and its mortal consequence. This is the most explicit constitutional location in the entire corpus.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=14190, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Soul-level location in Eze 18:4/20 (nephesh) reveals that wilful sinning is constitutionally deep — it is not merely a surface behaviour but an act that engages the person's innermost self as the responsible agent. The soul here is the irreducible personal subject: "the soul who sins alone shall die" — the personal self cannot delegate the sinning to social forces, ancestry, or circumstance. This locates wilful sinning within the person's inalienable moral selfhood. The consequence (death) follows the soul-as-agent, not a transferred entity.

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=14191, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (see T2.2.1). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=14192, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. Job 1:5 provides the clearest heart-location: "It may be that my children have sinned, and cursed God in their hearts" — sinning is here explicitly an inner-heart act, a cursing of God not yet externalised in speech or visible deed but real and culpable in its heart-location. Psa 119:11 ("I have stored up your word in my heart, that I might not sin against you") presupposes that the heart is the site where the battle against sinning is fought and won — the heart's inner repository of the word is what keeps the will from sinning. Psa 51:4 ("against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight") is contextually heart-language, located within Psalm 51's entire heart-renewal discourse.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=14193, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Heart-location engages the heart's *willing and moral-evaluating* functions. Job 1:5 shows sinning in the heart as a heart-orientation (cursing God) — the heart's alignment with or against God is the register. Psa 119:11 shows the heart as the site where the word is stored and where the will-to-not-sin is formed — the heart's *holding* and *deciding* function. The heart here is integrating function in the sense that it is where the person's stance before God is formed: the heart that curses God (Job 1:5) is the heart that has turned its deepest orientation against him, while the heart that stores his word (Psa 119:11) is the heart aligned with him. Sinning in the heart is thus sinning at the person's deepest evaluative and volitional centre.

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=14194, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Heart-location is evidenced (T2.3.1 and T2.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=14195, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus contains evidence of mind-engagement, though not by explicit mind-terminology. Psa 4:4 ("ponder in your own hearts on your beds, and be silent") — the word "ponder" engages the reflective mind as the instrument for preventing sinning; the cognitive faculty of reflection is what the psalmist directs against the impulse to sin in anger. Ecc 5:6 ("let not your mouth lead you into sin") implies that the mind's lack of reflective oversight (the mouth leading ahead of considered thought) is what enables speech-mode sinning. The implication is that sinning bypasses or overrides reflective mind-engagement — it is an act where the will moves without adequate cognitive processing of the moral stakes.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=14196, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence implicates the mind's *discernment and deliberative* function: sinning occurs when the will acts without adequate moral deliberation. Psa 4:4 — pondering (reflective deliberation) is the remedy; the absence of pondering allows anger to become sinful action. Ecc 5:6 — the mouth outrunning the mind produces sinful speech. Joseph in Gen 39:9 shows the opposite: the mind actively deliberates ("how then can I do this?") and the named moral content (great wickedness, sin against God) provides the deliberative check. The mind's deliberative function — its capacity to name an act as wrong before committing it — is what wilful sinning must overcome or bypass.

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=14197, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Mind-location is evidenced (see T2.4.1 and T2.4.2). The engagement is implicit rather than terminologically explicit, but it is analytically clear.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=14198, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The *conscience* is evidenced as a distinct soul-subset engaged by wilful sinning. The VCG-03 corpus (deterrence/restraint) presupposes conscience as the faculty that recognises the moral quality of a potential act before commission: Gen 39:9 (Joseph's conscience names "great wickedness"), Psa 39:1 (the conscience-driven resolve to guard the mouth). Post-act, conscience appears in Gen 42:22 (Reuben's conscience connects past sinning to present reckoning). The conscience operates as the inner moral witness that either deters sinning (pre-act) or registers it as wrong (post-act). This is distinct from the heart's volitional function and the mind's deliberative function.

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=14199, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The conscience location (T2.5.1) reveals that wilful sinning involves a three-way inner dynamic: (1) the *will* as the faculty that commits the act, (2) the *conscience* as the faculty that witnesses it as wrong (either before, deterring it, or after, registering guilt), and (3) the *heart* as the deep-orientation centre from which both proceed. The relationship between conscience and will is antagonistic in the resistance register (VCG-03): conscience deters what the will is tempted to commit. When conscience fails to deter, sinning occurs — which is precisely why Psa 119:11 (word in the heart) and Exo 20:20 (fear of God before the eyes) function as conscience-strengthening mechanisms.

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=14200, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent (see T2.5.1 and T2.5.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=14201, E) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. Multiple body-part links are evidenced. (1) *Lips/mouth*: Psa 39:1 ("sin with my tongue"), Job 2:10 ("sin with his lips"), Job 31:30 ("not let my mouth sin"), Ecc 5:6 ("let not your mouth lead you into sin") — the mouth/lips/tongue is the most frequently mentioned body-part link. (2) *Body (soma)*: 1Cor 6:18 ("the sexually immoral person sins against his own body") — the whole body as the site of damage in sexual sinning. (3) *Hands*: 1Sa 24:11 ("no wrong or treason in my hands") — hands as the instrumental body-part for sinning or not sinning in action. (4) *Eyes*: Isa 1:4 contextually ("evil in your sight") implicates the eyes in moral evaluation, though this is God's eyes rather than the sinner's.

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=14202, E) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The body-part links serve distinct functions. For the mouth/lips/tongue (Psa 39:1; Job 2:10; Ecc 5:6), the function is *expressive and mediating* — the mouth is the organ through which inner sinful impulse externalises and through which restraint can intercept the act. The body-part is both the problem (it leads the person into sin, Ecc 5:6) and the solution-site (guarding it prevents the sin, Psa 39:1). For soma in 1Cor 6:18, the function is *indicative* — Paul indicates that sexual sin uniquely damages the very body through which it is committed, making the body both instrument and victim. For hands in 1Sa 24:11, the function is *instrumental* — hands are the agents of action (killing) that David specifically restrains.

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=14203, E) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Body-part links are evidenced (see T2.6.1 and T2.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=14204, E) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The directional evidence is primarily *soul/will → body* for the speech-mode sinning: the inner sinful impulse (from will or heart) seeks expression through the mouth/tongue. Psa 39:1 — the will resolves to guard the tongue, implying the inner impulse would otherwise express outward. Ecc 5:6 — the mouth "leads" the person into sin, suggesting a body → soul feedback loop: undisciplined speech generates liability that then implicates the inner person's standing before God. 1Cor 6:18 shows bidirectionality: the sinful act is performed through the body (soul → body) but the damage returns to the body (body receives the consequence). The dominant direction is inner-to-outer for the commission of sinning, but the feedback (consequence returning to the person) runs outer-to-inner.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=14205, E) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The inner-to-outer directionality confirms that wilful sinning originates in the inner person (will, heart, conscience-bypassed) and externalises through the body — which is why the prescription for preventing speech-mode sinning (Psa 39:1; Job 31:30) is inner resolve acted out as external restraint. The body is the execution venue, not the origin point. The outer-to-inner feedback (consequence returning, 1Cor 6:18; Ecc 5:6's destroyed work) reveals that sinning creates a loop: the inner act externalises, the external act damages, and the damage returns to the inner person as guilt, consequence, and relational rupture. This loop is what the cultic-atonement system (VCG-04) interrupts — the sin offering breaks the consequence-return cycle by providing substitutionary resolution.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=14206, E) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Body-characteristic links are evidenced (T2.6.1, T2.7.1). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=14207, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus offers limited but present evidence. Isa 1:4 ("sinful nation, a people laden with iniquity, offspring of evildoers, children who deal corruptly") describes a generational pattern in which sinning has become a constitutional characteristic of a people — "offspring of evildoers" suggests a hereditary dimension, though the text does not specify a biological mechanism. Neh 13:26 ("foreign women made even him to sin") implies that sustained exposure to sinning patterns erodes constitutional resistance. The strongest deposit-language in the corpus is Hos 4:7/13:2 — escalating sinning producing intensified idolatry — which suggests a formed disposition that progressively resists reversal. However, explicit DNA or bodily-deposit language is absent from the corpus.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=14208, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Supporting evidence: Isa 1:4 (generational sin-characterisation), Neh 13:26 (constitutional vulnerability acquired through exposure), Hos 13:2 (escalating sinning suggesting formed capacity). Contradicting evidence: Eze 18:19–20 explicitly rejects generational punishment for sin: "the son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father" — which addresses legal accountability rather than constitutional deposit but does warn against reading generational consequence as constitutional transmission. The cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04: Lev 4:22 — unintentional sinning still real and requiring remedy) suggests that even non-deposit sinning is morally consequential, implying that the deposit question is not the primary evidence-frame.

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=14209, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not wholly silent but is limited (T2.8.1 and T2.8.2). The corpus suggests generational moral consequence (Isa 1:4) and disposition-formation (Hos 4:7; 13:2) without explicit constitutional-deposit or biological mechanism language. T5.7 will carry this as a limited evidential finding. Note: the carry-forward INTEGRATION_NOTEs (a.ven and BOUNDARY terms) are not evidenced in the CHAR-1 corpus — both are set-aside terms with no CHAR-1 verse presence.

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=14210, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The origin evidence is multi-sourced. (1) *Internal — from within the will and heart*: Gen 39:9 (Joseph's description of the temptation as something he recognises as "great wickedness" — the sinful act originates in the responding will); Job 1:5 (sinning "in their hearts" — heart as origin site). (2) *External — received through temptation, pressure, or exposure*: 1Sa 15:24 ("I feared the people") — social pressure as external origin; Neh 13:26 (foreign women — relational exposure as origin); Neh 6:13 (enemies' trap — external manipulative pressure). (3) *Constitutional vulnerability in the creature*: 1Jo 1:10 ("if we say we have not sinned, we make him a liar") treats sinning as a condition from which no human is exempt — implying a constitutional susceptibility.

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=14211, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The origin is clearly multiple in the corpus. No single origin-source is evidenced as exhaustive. The corpus presents internal origins (will, heart, conscience-bypassed) and external origins (social pressure, temptation, exposure) operating alongside each other. Neh 6:13 (external trap) and 1Sa 15:24 (external pressure) show sinning arising from a combination of external pressure and internal capitulation of the will. Gen 39:9 shows sinning arising purely from internal temptation (Potiphar's wife's offer) met by internal resistance — external trigger, internal response. The multiplicity of origins supports reading wilful sinning as a relational phenomenon (always involving a setting, a pressure, and a will) rather than a purely internal generation.

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=14212, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The OT corpus (VCG-01) predominantly shows sinning arising in covenantal contexts — Israel sinning against the Lord by failing to walk in his ways (Jer 44:23), breaking covenant (Jos 7:11), or following idolatrous influences (Hos 4:7; 13:2). The origin-context is covenantal relationship with God. The interpersonal corpus (VCG-02) shows sinning arising in relational contexts where obligations between persons are present — custody obligations (Gen 43:9/44:32), leader-servant relations (1Sa 19:4/5), sibling bonds (Gen 42:22). The NT corpus (VCG-05) shows sinning arising in the body-context (1Cor 6:18) and the community-context (Mat 18:15; Luk 17:3/4; 1Cor 8:12). The origin-context shifts from covenantal (OT) to anthropological and communal (NT) without changing the core — the will's known choice of wrong.

---

  2. Scope marker:       31× [CHAR-1] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings T2.1.1–T2.1.4 are silent — no citation required)

  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited verses in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T3.


---

## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=14213, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus evidences engagement of the perceptive faculty primarily through *spiritual discernment* and *sight*. Exo 20:20 — "that the fear of him may be before you" — locates the preventive force of fear of God in a quasi-visual inner presence: "before you" implies a seeing faculty that either registers God's presence (preventing sin) or fails to (enabling it). Isa 1:4 — "they have despised the Holy One of Israel" — despising involves a perceptive act: the inner assessment of God's holiness as something dismissible. Job 7:20 — "you watcher of mankind" — frames God's perceptive gaze as the context in which sinning occurs, implying that human sinning takes place under divine perception even when the sinner does not perceive God. The perceptive faculty is engaged primarily through the inner "sight" of God's presence or absence.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=14214, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning *impairs* the perceptive faculty. The corpus evidence for the escalating pattern (Hos 4:7; 13:2 — "they sin more and more") implies a progressive desensitisation of the inner sense that registers God's presence and moral reality. Isa 1:4 ("they have despised the Holy One of Israel, they are utterly estranged") shows that sustained sinning produces estrangement — the inner perception of God as the moral reference point is diminished. Contrast with the deterrence register (VCG-03): where sinning is resisted, the perceptive faculty remains operative (Gen 39:9 — Joseph perceives the act as "great wickedness"; Exo 20:20 — fear of God "before you" enables perception). Sinning bypasses or progressively impairs the perceptive registration of God's holiness.

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=14215, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that wilful sinning is in part a *perceptive failure* — the will commits the known wrong when the inner perception of God's holiness and the moral gravity of the act fails to register with sufficient force to deter. Joseph's refusal (Gen 39:9) operates because his perception is active and named ("great wickedness, sin against God"). The deterrence corpus (VCG-03: Exo 20:20 — fear of God before you; Psa 119:11 — word stored in heart) prescribes perceptive-enhancement measures. The implication: strengthening inner perception (of God's presence, of the act's moral quality) is the primary mechanism against wilful sinning — confirming that sinning involves a failure or suppression of that perception.

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=14240, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Conscientiousness is engaged negatively: wilful sinning is the failure of conscientiousness — the integrated moral-awareness, volition, and action system disintegrates along the will axis. The person who wilfully sins possesses the moral awareness (conscience) and the capacity for right action (agency) but the volition is deflected in the sinning direction. The VCG-03 deterrence corpus shows conscientiousness functioning fully: Job 31:30 ("I have not let my mouth sin by asking for his life with a curse") — moral awareness + deliberate restraint + correct action = conscientiousness operating. VCG-01 and VCG-02 show conscientiousness failing — the will detaches from the integrated system.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=14241, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning *impairs* conscientiousness — specifically by uncoupling the will from the moral-awareness component. The characteristic does not impair moral awareness (the person still knows) or agency (they can still act), but it breaks the integration between these faculties that constitutes conscientiousness. 1Sa 15:24 shows the uncoupling precisely: Saul knows the right (Samuel's command), can act rightly, but the will is captured by social fear — conscientiousness fails at the volitional node.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=14242, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The conscientiousness pattern confirms that wilful sinning is a *systemic inner failure* — not the failure of any single faculty but the failure of the system that integrates moral awareness, volition, and action. This systemic reading explains why the deterrence corpus addresses multiple faculties simultaneously: stored word (cognitive/memory), fear of God (affective), pondering (reflective), mouth-guarding (behavioural). The comprehensive multi-faculty prescription corresponds to the systemic multi-faculty failure that wilful sinning represents.

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=14243, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning engages relational capacity by *rupturing* it. The relational rupture is the defining consequence across all three directional registers: vertical rupture (sin against God — "they are utterly estranged," Isa 1:4; "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God," Isa 59:2, not in scope but implied by the pattern); horizontal rupture (sin against neighbour — 1Ki 8:31 envisions the rupture as requiring oath-taking before the altar for resolution; Mat 18:15 prescribes the confrontation process for horizontal repair). The relational capacity is engaged as the site of damage: sinning is done *against* a relational partner (God, neighbour, or self), meaning the relational bond is what is violated.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=14244, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning *impairs* relational capacity specifically in the direction of the sin. Sin against God creates estrangement from God (Isa 1:4; 1Ki 8:33/35 — national defeat and drought as relational rupture). Sin against the neighbour creates relational debt requiring forgiveness (Mat 18:15; Luk 17:3). Sin against the self (1Cor 6:18) damages the body through which relational life is lived. The impairment is directional: the relationship sinned against is damaged; other relationships may remain intact. The characteristic does not universally impair relational capacity but specifically ruptures the relation that is sinned against.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=14245, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The relational pattern confirms that wilful sinning is *constitutively relational*: it cannot be committed in a vacuum — it requires a relational target (God, neighbour, self). This is the deepest structural feature of the characteristic: sinning is always *against* someone. The three directional axes (VCG-01: against God; VCG-02: against neighbour; VCG-05: against self or Christ) are not merely contextual variations but constitutive dimensions of what sinning is. The relational rupture is not a secondary consequence but the primary act — sinning *is* the rupturing of the relation.

---

T3 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       33/33 ✓ (T3.1.1–T3.1.3, T3.2.1–T3.2.3, T3.3.1–T3.3.3, T3.4.1–T3.4.3, T3.5.1–T3.5.3, T3.6.1–T3.6.3, T3.7.1–T3.7.3, T3.8.1–T3.8.3, T3.9.1–T3.9.3, T3.10.1–T3.10.3, T3.11.1–T3.11.3)
  2. Scope marker:       33× [CHAR-1] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited verses in §3; Isa 59:2 flagged as out-of-scope — it was noted parenthetically with explicit "not in scope" caveat and was not cited as primary evidence)
  → PASS. Response length approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T4 continues in the next response.


---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=14216, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The "wilful" (knowing) element of the characteristic name directly implicates cognition. The definitional marker is that the person knows what they are doing. Gen 39:9 — Joseph's cognitive evaluation ("how then can I do this great wickedness?") is the evidence of active knowing. Lev 4:23 and 4:28 ("the sin which he has committed is made known to him") show that realisation (cognitive awareness arriving) triggers obligation — the cultic system is activated precisely when cognition registers the sin. Deu 19:15 ("only on the evidence of two witnesses shall a charge be established") grounds sinning in legally verifiable cognitive fact. The cognitive faculty is engaged as the basis for the "wilful" quality: knowing the wrong before doing it.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=14217, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning *bypasses* cognition in the act of commission: the will proceeds despite the available cognitive awareness that the act is wrong. The evidence for bypassing: 1Sa 15:24 — Saul knew the command (he had received it from Samuel); he bypassed it under social pressure. Jos 7:20 — Achan confesses knowing what he did ("Truly I have sinned against the Lord God of Israel, and this is what I did") — the cognitive knowledge preceded the act. The VCG-03 deterrence corpus suggests that sinning is prevented when cognition is *amplified* (Exo 20:20 — divine display is cognitive input; Psa 119:11 — word stored = cognitive reservoir). Where cognition is bypassed or insufficient, sinning proceeds.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=14218, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals a fundamental tension: wilful sinning is constitutively known (the "wilful" qualifier) yet the will commits it anyway — making the relationship between cognition and volition in sinning one of *knowing-and-nonetheless*. Joseph (Gen 39:9) shows the knowing producing refusal; Saul (1Sa 15:24) shows the knowing overridden by fear. Achan (Jos 7:20) shows the knowing fully present yet insufficient to prevent. This knowing-and-nonetheless dynamic is what distinguishes wilful sinning from CHAR-2 (Unintentional sinning) — the cognitive element is present in CHAR-1 precisely because the will acts against available knowledge.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=14219, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The memory faculty is engaged in two directions. (1) *Pre-act memory as deterrent*: Psa 119:11 ("I have stored up your word in my heart") — the word memorised is what prevents sinning; the memory faculty holds the moral resource that deters the will. (2) *Post-act memory as reckoning*: Gen 42:22 — Reuben's retrieval of his past warning ("Did I not tell you not to sin against the boy?") in the present moment of distress shows memory connecting the past act of sinning to present consequence. The cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04: Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed is made known to him") shows memory-retrieval as the trigger for cultic obligation when the sin surfaces to conscious recollection.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=14220, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning can *impair* retrieval of the morally relevant memory at the critical moment. The mechanism: sinning proceeds when stored moral knowledge is not retrieved effectively by the memory faculty in the moment of decision. The prescriptions in VCG-03 (Psa 119:11 — store the word; Exo 20:20 — keep fear before you) function as memory-maintenance measures: ensuring the relevant moral content is accessible at the moment of temptation. Where memory retrieval fails — where the word is not "stored" or the fear is not "before" the person — the will is less equipped to resist sinning.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=14221, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that wilful sinning involves a *memory-defeat*: the knowledge is present in principle, but the memory faculty fails to make it operative at the decisive moment. This is confirmed by the prescriptive counter-measures (store the word, keep fear before you) — they function as memory-activation strategies. The characteristic is thus in part a failure of the memory faculty to function as a moral resource in the moment — not a failure of knowledge but of recall-in-the-moment.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=14222, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The affective faculty is engaged in several registers. (1) *Fear as affective deterrent*: Exo 20:20 ("that the fear of him may be before you, that you may not sin") — fear of God is an affective state that prevents sinning. (2) *Social fear as sinning-enabler*: 1Sa 15:24 ("I feared the people") — fear of social consequence is the affective state that produces capitulation to sinning. (3) *Anger as sinning-risk*: Psa 4:4 ("be angry, and do not sin; ponder in your own hearts") — anger is the affective state that creates risk of sinning. (4) *Contempt as sinning-mode*: Isa 1:4 ("they have despised the Holy One of Israel") — despising is an affective act that functions as sinning.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=14223, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning *exploits* affect in the service of the will's turning against God or persons. Fear of people (1Sa 15:24) is the affective lever through which the will is moved to sin against God. Anger (Psa 4:4) is the affective energy that, unchecked by pondering, produces sinful action. Contempt/despising (Isa 1:4) is an affective posture that constitutes sinning. The pattern: sinning does not bypass affect but weaponises available affect (fear, anger, contempt) in the direction of the wrong. Conversely, the affective deterrent — fear of God (Exo 20:20) — is the affect that the will needs to be kept operative to resist sinning.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=14224, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that wilful sinning is an *affectively mediated* act of the will: the will does not sin in an affective vacuum but rides available affective currents (fear, anger, contempt) in its sinning direction. This is why the prescriptive deterrents are also affective (fear of God before you, Exo 20:20) — the corrective is not to eliminate affect from the will's operation but to redirect which affect is operative. The characteristic thus sits at the intersection of volition and affect: the will + the wrong affect = sinning; the will + the right affect (fear of God) = deterrence.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=14225, E) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Limited evidence. The closest engagement of the creative/imaginative faculty is in the planning dimension: Neh 6:13 — enemies "hired" Shemaiah and constructed a scenario designed to produce sinning in Nehemiah ("for this purpose he was hired, that I should be afraid and act in this way and sin"). The planning and construction of the trap implicates creative faculty on the temptation-design side. On the sinner's side, Hos 13:2 — "they sin more and more, and make for themselves metal images, idols skillfully made of their silver, all of them the work of craftsmen" — shows that escalating sinning produces increasingly elaborate creative output (skilfully made idols), implicating creative investment in the sinning pattern.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=14226, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence from Hos 13:2 suggests that wilful sinning *misdirects* creativity — the creative faculty produces increasingly elaborate substitutes for God (skillfully crafted idols) rather than what it was designed to produce. The creative capacity is not impaired (the idols are skillfully made) but its direction is perverted by the sinning orientation. This is the characteristic's effect on creativity: not suppression but misdirection.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=14227, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The limited evidence (Hos 13:2; Neh 6:13) suggests that wilful sinning does not primarily operate through the creative faculty — it is not constitutively a creative act. The creative dimension appears as a secondary effect (elaborated idolatry) rather than the primary mechanism. The characteristic's core is volitional (will choosing the wrong) with creativity as a vehicle for expressing and deepening the sinning pattern over time, not as its origin.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=14228, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The volitional faculty is the *primary* faculty engaged by wilful sinning — this is the defining claim of the characteristic. Every verse in the corpus presupposes an active will. Eze 18:4/20 (the soul who sins carries personal accountability — accountability is only coherent for a willing agent). Gen 39:9 (Joseph's refusal is a volitional act: "how then can I do this?"). 1Sa 15:24 (Saul's will capitulates to social pressure: "I obeyed their voice"). The entire VCG-03 deterrence corpus assumes the will can choose not to sin (Exo 20:20, Psa 4:4, Psa 119:11, Eze 3:21). The will is the origin, the executor, and the accountable agent of wilful sinning.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=14229, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning *expresses* volition (the will is actively choosing) while *deepening constraint on future volition* over time. The escalation evidence (Hos 4:7 — "the more they sinned"; Hos 13:2 — "they sin more and more") shows a progressive narrowing of the will's range: each act of sinning makes subsequent sinning more likely and more compulsive. Neh 13:26 — "foreign women made even him to sin" — implies that sustained exposure progressively undermines the will's capacity to resist. The will that wilfully sins remains technically free (the corpus maintains moral accountability throughout) but its effective range is progressively constrained by habituation.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=14230, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that wilful sinning is a *volitional act with volitional consequences*: the will that sins remains the responsible agent (Eze 18:20 — "the soul who sins alone shall die") but exercises its agency in a direction that progressively constrains future agency. The characteristic is constituted by the will's exercise, but its sustained exercise progressively reshapes the will's default orientation. This is the deepest finding of the T3.6 cluster: sinning is wilful, but sustained wilful sinning is self-undermining of the very volition that produces it.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=14231, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Agency is fully engaged. Wilful sinning is an act — something done, initiated, made to happen by the person. The verbal grammar of the corpus (cha.ta as action verb — committed, sinned, sins) confirms this. Gen 43:9 and 44:32 (Judah voluntarily assuming agency for Benjamin's safety) show agency in the responsibility-for-another mode. Jos 7:20 (Achan — "this is what I did") shows agency as the defining element of the confession: the act is personally initiated. The deterrence corpus (VCG-03) shows agency being disciplined: Job 31:30 ("I have not let my mouth sin") — the "not let" is an agency-restraint act.

---
