#### T3.7.2 (cf=14232, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning initially *expresses* full agency but progressively *impairs* it through habituation. The single act exercises agency in the sinning direction; the accumulated pattern (Hos 4:7; 13:2) shows agency increasingly captured by the sinning pattern. 1Jo 2:1 ("if anyone does sin, we have an advocate") frames the sinning believer as still an agent (not a determined automaton) but one who needs intervention — the advocate — to restore their standing before God. The impairment is not elimination of agency but its gradual colonisation by sinning's logic.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=14233, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The agency pattern confirms that wilful sinning is a *responsible act* — the corpus never excuses it as compelled or inevitable (even Saul's self-excuse in 1Sa 15:24 is narratively presented as inadequate: Samuel does not accept it as a valid defence). The moral accountability in Eze 18:4/20, the personal confession in Jos 7:20, and the deterrence corpus all presuppose that agency is real and operative. The characteristic is constituted by genuine agency — which is precisely why it is morally serious.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=14234, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Moral evaluation is definitionally engaged: the "wilful" qualifier requires that the person knows what they are doing is wrong — which presupposes a moral evaluation faculty that has assessed the act. Gen 39:9 — Joseph's moral evaluation names the act as "great wickedness" and "sin against God" — two moral evaluation registers (magnitude: great wickedness; relational: against God). Deu 19:15 — legal testimony as a formal moral evaluation mechanism. The cultic system (VCG-04) is a structured moral-evaluation apparatus: the community's obligation is triggered when the sin "becomes known" (Lev 4:14) — a community-level moral evaluation event.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=14235, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning *bypasses* moral evaluation in the act of commission. The will acts despite the available moral evaluation: Saul (1Sa 15:24) knew the command but obeyed the people's voice — the moral evaluation was available but overridden. Over time, sustained sinning *impairs* moral evaluation: Isa 1:4's people "despise the Holy One of Israel" — the moral evaluation faculty has become inverted, treating the holy as contemptible. This impairment trajectory from bypass (single act) to inversion (sustained pattern) is the moral-evaluation consequence of wilful sinning.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=14236, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The pattern reveals that wilful sinning is a *moral evaluation failure at the point of decision*: the evaluation is possible (the person knows the wrong) but the will does not act in accordance with the evaluation's conclusion. This is the precise meaning of "wilful" — not that moral evaluation is absent but that the will acts against its conclusion. The characteristic thus exposes the structural gap between knowing and doing in the fallen inner person — the space where the will detaches from the evaluation faculty's verdict and proceeds anyway.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=14237, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Conscience is engaged throughout the corpus. Pre-act: Gen 39:9 — conscience names the potential act as "great wickedness" before commission; Psa 4:4 — pondering as conscience-activating practice. Post-act: Gen 42:22 — Reuben's retrospective conscience: "Did I not tell you not to sin against the boy? But you did not listen. So now there comes a reckoning for his blood." Lev 4:23 — the sin "made known" to the offender is a conscience-event. 1Jo 1:10 — denying sinfulness is a conscience-suppression act ("we make him a liar, and his word is not in us").

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=14238, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning *bypasses* conscience at the moment of commission and *impairs* it over time. The bypass: the will commits the act despite conscience's registration of it as wrong (Saul, 1Sa 15:24; Achan, Jos 7:20). The impairment: 1Jo 1:10 — the person who "says we have not sinned" has moved from bypassing conscience to suppressing it — the conscience's witness is denied, not merely overridden. This represents a developmental trajectory from occasional bypass (single act of wilful sinning) to systematic impairment (conscience denial). The deterrence corpus (VCG-03) functions precisely to keep conscience active: Exo 20:20 (fear of God before you) is a conscience-maintenance measure.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=14239, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The conscience pattern confirms that wilful sinning is a *conscience-aware act*: the person who wilfully sins is the person whose conscience has registered the wrong but whose will has overridden it. This is the constitutive feature that distinguishes CHAR-1 (wilful sinning) from CHAR-2 (unintentional sinning): in unintentional sinning, conscience may not be engaged until after the fact; in wilful sinning, conscience is engaged and overridden. The characteristic is constituted by this conscience-overriding quality, which is why conscience-maintenance (through fear of God, stored word, pondering) is the primary preventive strategy in the corpus.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=14246, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The God-to-human direction of wilful sinning operates in two modes in the corpus. (1) *God as restrainer*: Gen 20:6 — "it was I who kept you from sinning against me. Therefore I did not let you touch her" — God acts externally toward the human to prevent the sinning act. This is the most direct God-to-human operation: God intervening in the space between temptation and commission. (2) *God as the moral standard that constitutes the sin*: the God-directed sinning in VCG-01 is structured around God as the offended party whose character defines the boundary — Eze 14:13 ("when a land sins against me by acting faithlessly"), Jer 40:3 ("because you sinned against the Lord and did not obey his voice"), Jer 44:23. God does not commit wilful sinning but establishes the relational framework within which human sinning is defined.

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=14247, S) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-1]** S — God does not extend wilful sinning toward humans; wilful sinning is a human act directed against God, not a divine operation toward the human. The question as framed (God extending this characteristic) does not apply to CHAR-1. What the evidence does show is the basis on which God *responds* to human sinning: (1) *Restraint* (Gen 20:6 — preventive grace), (2) *Judgment* (Eze 18:4 — "the soul who sins shall die"), (3) *Forgiveness on confession* (2Sa 12:13 — "the Lord also has put away your sin"). The basis for God's responses is his own moral character and covenant commitment, not an extension of the characteristic itself.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=14248, S) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-1]** S — Since God does not extend wilful sinning (T4.1.2), this prompt cannot be answered from the corpus as framed. What the corpus reveals about God's disposition toward sinning humans is: (1) *Moral seriousness* — "the soul who sins shall die" (Eze 18:4/20); (2) *Preventive care* — Gen 20:6 (active restraint to protect both Abimelech and Abraham's wife); (3) *Forgiveness available upon confession* — 2Sa 12:13; 1Ki 8:33/35/50 (Solomon's prayer structure). God's disposition toward sinning humans is one of moral seriousness combined with covenantal availability of forgiveness — not extension of the sinning characteristic itself.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=14249, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not wholly silent: God operates toward the human in the restraint mode (Gen 20:6) and in the judgment/forgiveness response mode (Eze 18:4; 2Sa 12:13; 1Ki 8:50). These God-to-human operations are addressed in T4.1.1–T4.1.3. The silence is specifically on God committing or extending wilful sinning — that direction is entirely absent.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=14250, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The human-to-God direction is the primary directional axis of VCG-01 (32 verses). Wilful sinning *directed against God* is the defining characteristic of VCG-01: Gen 39:9 ("sin against God"), Psa 51:4 ("against you, you only, have I sinned"), Eze 14:13 ("sins against me"), Jer 40:3/44:23 ("sinned against the Lord"). In each case the sinning act is the human will moving *against* God — a negative movement toward God in which the relational bond is violated rather than honoured. Conversely, VCG-03's deterrence register shows the human-to-God movement in its positive mode: Psa 119:11 ("stored up your word in my heart, that I might not sin against you") — the word-storage is a positive movement toward God that guards against the negative sinning movement.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=14251, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The sinning movement toward God requires an inner posture of deflection from God's moral claim — the will turning away from the covenantal obligation. 1Sa 15:24 shows this posture: Saul's fear of the people took priority over his fear of God — the inner posture was one of displaced allegiance. Jer 44:23 names the inner posture explicitly: "you did not obey the voice of the Lord or walk in his law and in his statutes" — non-listening and non-walking as the inner posture that constitutes sinning against God. The posture that prevents sinning (VCG-03) is named as its opposite: fear of God before you (Exo 20:20), word stored in heart (Psa 119:11).

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=14252, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The human-to-God direction of wilful sinning reveals that the relationship with God is the primary relational context in which sinning is defined. Psa 51:4 ("against you, you only, have I sinned") makes explicit what is implicit throughout VCG-01: even when sinning involves human victims (David's sin against Bathsheba and Uriah), the fundamental violation is against God. Eze 14:13 ("when a land sins against me by acting faithlessly") shows that faithlessness — covenant betrayal — is the relational content of sinning against God. The relationship with God is not a neutral backdrop but the constitutive framework within which sinning is possible: only within a covenant relationship can the breach be called sinning against God.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=14253, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is extensively present on human-to-God operation (VCG-01, 32 verses). This prompt is superseded by the positive finding in T4.2.1–T4.2.3.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=14254, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. VCG-02 (18 verses) is entirely composed of interpersonal wilful sinning — one person extending sinning toward another. Gen 42:22 (brothers sinning against Joseph), Gen 20:9 (Abraham's deception bringing sin upon Abimelech), 1Sa 19:4/5 (Jonathan warning Saul against sinning against David), Mat 18:15 (brother sinning against brother), Luk 17:3/4 (repeated sinning against another), 1Cor 8:12 (sinning against brothers by wounding their conscience). The extension is always in the sinning direction: the one who sinning acts *against* the other, violating the relational claim.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=14255, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The inner conditions that enable interpersonal sinning are evidenced as: (1) *Deception / self-protective fear*: Gen 20:9 — Abraham's fear-driven deception produced sinning against Abimelech's household. (2) *Hostile intent*: 1Sa 19:5 — sinning against innocent blood by killing David without cause — the hostile will as enabling condition. (3) *Contempt for the neighbour*: Pro 14:21 ("whoever despises his neighbor is a sinner") — the inner attitude of contempt enables and constitutes the sinning. (4) *Impulsive action*: Pro 19:2 — haste without knowledge misses the way — the impulsive will bypassing discernment enables sinning. (5) *Wounding disregard for another's conscience*: 1Cor 8:12 — acting without regard for the weak brother's conscience enables sinning against him.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=14256, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning is not received before being given — it is generated by the sinner's own will in the moment of decision. However, the corpus identifies enabling conditions that precede interpersonal sinning: (1) Gen 20:9 shows that Abraham first held the inner posture of fear-driven deception before extending sinning to Abimelech; (2) Pro 14:21 shows that contempt of the neighbour precedes and constitutes the sinning; (3) 1Sa 15:24 shows that fear of people precedes the sinning act against God. The "received" condition is not sinning-from-another but the inner dispositions (fear, contempt, deception) that make the sinning act possible.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=14257, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The giving direction is extensively evidenced (VCG-02, 18 verses). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=14258, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The recipient of sinning is extensively evidenced in VCG-02. Gen 20:9 — Abimelech receives sinning from Abraham: "you have brought on me and my kingdom a great sin." Gen 42:22 — Joseph received sinning from his brothers: "Do not sin against the boy." 1Sa 19:4/5 — David as the would-be recipient of Saul's sinning: "Let not the king sin against his servant David." Mat 18:15 — the brother who has been sinned against and must decide whether to confront. 1Cor 8:12 — the weak brother whose conscience is wounded by another's sinning. The reception operates as a *harm received* — moral, relational, and sometimes physical damage inflicted on the recipient by the sinner's act.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=14259, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Reception of sinning (being sinned against) is not primarily a matter of the recipient's inner conditions — the sinning act is committed by another and lands on the recipient whether or not they are internally prepared. However, the corpus does evidence the recipient's inner conditions that shape the response to sinning received: (1) *Moral clarity about what was done*: Gen 20:9 (Abimelech's clear protest), 1Sa 19:4/5 (Jonathan's clear naming of Saul's potential wrong). (2) *The capacity to forgive*: Mat 18:21 (Peter asking how many times to forgive a sinning brother), Luk 17:3/4 (the obligation to forgive repeated sinning upon repentance). The inner condition that shapes the reception's aftermath is the recipient's moral discernment and willingness to forgive.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=14260, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The person who is sinned against but refuses to be overcome by it — who does not allow the sinning received to corrupt their own inner state — is evidenced in David (1Sa 24:11: "I have not sinned against you, though you hunt my life to take it") and Jeremiah (Jer 37:18: "What wrong have I done to you or your servants or this people?"). Their inner-being state is one of moral clarity and conscientious self-examination: they are confident their own hands are clean even as another sins against them. The sinning received does not produce reciprocal sinning in them — the inner state is one of conscious integrity maintained under pressure.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=14261, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced (VCG-02; T4.4.1–T4.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=14262, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The corpus shows clear differentiation. Within existing bonds: Gen 42:22 (brothers sinning against a brother — deep family bond), 1Sa 19:4/5 (king sinning against a servant who has done only good — overlord/servant bond), Mat 18:15 (brother sinning against a brother within the community of disciples). Across bonds: Gen 40:1 (cupbearer and baker sinning against their lord — authority relationship). The evidence shows that sinning within existing bonds is treated as particularly grievous — Jonathan frames sinning against innocent David as a moral horror ("Why then will you sin against innocent blood?", 1Sa 19:5) precisely because the king-servant bond makes the sinning a betrayal. Sinning against relational partners magnifies the moral weight.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=14263, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The characteristic crosses covenantal boundaries. VCG-01 is primarily covenantal (Israel sinning against the Lord — the covenant framework defines the sin). But VCG-02 includes sinning across non-covenantal boundaries: Gen 20:9 (Abraham sinning against Abimelech — a pagan king outside the covenant); Gen 42:22 (Joseph — a Hebrew sinned against by his brothers before the Sinai covenant). Mat 18:15/21 (community of disciples context — a new covenant setting). The characteristic is not confined to the Mosaic covenant but operates wherever relational bonds and moral claims exist. Eze 18:4/20 (individual soul-level accountability) operates without covenantal specification.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=14264, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The relational scope is comprehensive: VCG-01 encompasses the human-God relation; VCG-02 encompasses all human-human bonds (family, king-servant, community, neighbour); VCG-05 adds the self-relation (1Cor 6:18 — sinning against one's own body) and the Christ-relation (1Cor 8:12 — sinning against a brother reaches Christ). The scope thus encompasses the full relational map of the human person: God, other humans, self, and mediately Christ. No relational category is excluded from the scope of wilful sinning.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=14265, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent (T4.5.1–T4.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=14266, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. 2Pe 2:4 provides direct evidence: "God did not spare angels when they sinned, but cast them into hell and committed them to chains of gloomy darkness to be kept until the judgment." Angels committed wilful sinning — the same characteristic (cha.ta/hamartanō) that the human corpus addresses. Their sinning resulted in immediate divine judgment and permanent imprisonment. The angels are thus participants in wilful sinning, not merely observers or tempters. Additionally, the Eze 28:16 cherub passage ("you sinned; so I cast you as a profane thing from the mountain of God") evidences a guardian cherub's sinning — a spiritual being of high standing whose sinning produced expulsion from God's presence.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=14267, E) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The corpus evidences adversarial activity in the temptation/trap structure. Neh 6:13 — enemies construct a scenario specifically designed to cause Nehemiah to sin: "for this purpose he was hired, that I should be afraid and act in this way and sin." The trap is externally constructed adversarial pressure aimed at producing sinning. Gen 20:6 — God's restraint of Abimelech from sinning implies that without divine intervention the sinning would have occurred — adversarial conditions are assumed as the default. The temptation that Joseph resists (Gen 39:9) is presented as a persistent assault ("she spoke to Joseph day after day" — context). The characteristic is a site where adversarial forces seek to provoke and produce sinning.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=14268, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-1]** S — The corpus contains no evidence of angelic ministry mediating or strengthening the *resistance to* wilful sinning, nor of angelic ministry facilitating sinning. The adversarial spiritual beings (2Pe 2:4, Eze 28:16) are themselves sinners, not agents preventing sinning. Ecc 5:6 mentions "the messenger" before whom rash speech constitutes sinning — this may be an angelic messenger (as in the LXX reading), in which case the angel functions as a witness who records the sinning rather than a mediator preventing it. No direct angelic ministry in strengthening the characteristic is evidenced.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=14269, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not wholly silent (2Pe 2:4 on angelic sinning; Neh 6:13 on adversarial temptation; Ecc 5:6 on possible angelic witness; Eze 28:16 on cherub sinning). What is silent is angelic ministry in a protective or mediating capacity — that absence is noted in T4.6.3.

---

T4 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T4.1.1–T4.1.4, T4.2.1–T4.2.4, T4.3.1–T4.3.4, T4.4.1–T4.4.4, T4.5.1–T4.5.4, T4.6.1–T4.6.4)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-1] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T4.1.2, T4.1.3, T4.4.6.3 are S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 20:6/9/39:9/40:1/42:22; 1Sa 15:24/19:4/5/24:11; 2Sa 12:13; 1Ki 8:33/35/50; Neh 6:13; Psa 51:4/119:11; Pro 14:21/19:2; Ecc 5:6; Jer 37:18/40:3/44:23; Eze 14:13/18:4/18:20/28:16; Mat 18:15/21; Luk 17:3/4; 1Cor 8:12; 2Pe 2:4 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=14270, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. Wilful sinning produces transformation in both condition and orientation. *Condition change*: the person who sins moves from a state of innocence or standing to a state of guilt and moral liability — Eze 18:4/20 ("the soul who sins shall die") declares a condition change: the sinning soul is now under mortal judgment. The cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04) presupposes this: the sin offering is required precisely because the person's condition has changed through sinning — Lev 4:35 ("the sin which he has committed"). *Orientation change*: Hos 4:7/13:2 (escalating sinning — "the more they sinned against me") shows the sinner's orientation progressively locked into the sinning direction. Isa 1:4 ("utterly estranged") names the orientation change as an outcome: estrangement from God as the directional result of sustained sinning. Both condition and orientation are transformed.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=14271, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The transformation is primarily evidenced as reversible through divine action. 2Sa 12:13 — Nathan declares: "The Lord also has put away your sin; you shall not die." The condition change (guilt → forgiven) is reversed by God's act of putting away. 1Ki 8:33/35 — Solomon's prayer structure presupposes reversibility: if the people "turn again to you and acknowledge your name and pray," God will forgive (1Ki 8:50). The cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04: Lev 4:35/5:10/5:13 — "and he shall be forgiven") shows that the condition change from sinning is reversed through the prescribed remedy. Eze 3:21 confirms reversibility at the orientation level: the righteous person warned not to sin "does not sin" — the trajectory can be held or altered. However, the escalation pattern (Hos 4:7/13:2) suggests that sustained sinning without reversal progressively narrows the window for reversal — not closing it by divine decree, but by the hardening of the inner person.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=14272, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence is not silent (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=14273, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The corpus evidences a recurring sequence. (1) *Pre-act state*: the person in a condition of temptation or pressure — the will is not yet committed (Gen 39:9: Potiphar's wife's appeal; 1Sa 15:24: the people's voice pressing; Neh 6:13: enemies' trap set). (2) *Decision point*: the will either resists (VCG-03: Joseph's refusal) or capitulates (1Sa 15:24: Saul yields). (3) *Post-act state A — guilt/liability incurred*: the sinning has occurred and a new moral condition exists — Lev 4:23 ("the sin which he has committed is made known to him"), Gen 42:22 (reckoning arrives). (4) *Post-act state B — consequences manifesting*: 1Ki 8:33/35 (defeat, drought). (5) *Post-act state C — remedy sought and granted*: Lev 4:35 (sin offering → forgiven), 2Sa 12:13 (confession → forgiveness declared).

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=14274, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The sequence (pressure/temptation → decision point → guilt-incurred → consequence → remedy) reveals that wilful sinning is not a terminal event but a *relational episode* with a prescribed arc. The Levitical system (VCG-04) codifies this arc into a structured remedial process: each sinning act opens a relational deficit that the prescribed ritual closes. The Solomon prayer corpus (1Ki 8:33–50) maps this arc at national scale: sinning → defeat/disaster → turning + prayer → forgiveness + restoration. The sequence also reveals that the will is operative at the decision point and at the remedy point: sinning is a decision and so is repentance. The characteristic is reversible precisely because both ends of the arc are volitional.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=14275, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The evidence provides a clear sequence (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=14276, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning produces change through two primary mechanisms. (1) *Relational rupture*: the act of sinning breaks the relational bond with God (VCG-01), with the neighbour (VCG-02), or with the self (1Cor 6:18) — the rupture itself is the mechanism of change; the person is now in a different relational standing. (2) *Habituation/escalation*: Hos 4:7 ("the more they increased, the more they sinned") and Hos 13:2 ("they sin more and more") show that repeated sinning forms a habit that progressively shapes the will's default orientation — a formation mechanism that gradually changes the inner person's constituted direction.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=14277, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The relational-rupture mechanism operates immediately and universally: every act of sinning produces the rupture regardless of context or repetition (Eze 18:4; Lev 4:14). The habituation/escalation mechanism is a sustained-pattern phenomenon: it requires repeated sinning over time (Hos 4:7/13:2; Isa 1:4's national pattern). A single act of sinning ruptures the relation; sustained sinning without remedy forms the person in the sinning direction. The cultic-atonement system (VCG-04) addresses the immediate rupture mechanism with an immediate remedy. The habituation mechanism requires a more comprehensive inner transformation than a single sin offering.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=14278, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Mechanism is evidenced (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=14279, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The corpus evidences sinning operating *in the context of* suffering in two modes. (1) *Sinning causing suffering*: 1Ki 8:33/35 (sinning produces national defeat and drought — suffering as the consequence of sinning). Gen 20:9 (sinning bringing a "great sin" upon Abimelech's household — collective suffering from another's sinning act). (2) *Sinning resisted under suffering*: Job 1:22 and 2:10 — Job does not sin despite losing children, wealth, and health; suffering is the context in which the refusal to sin is demonstrated. Job 7:20 ("If I sin, what do I do to you, you watcher of mankind?") is articulated from within Job's suffering — suffering becomes the context in which the possibility of sinning is interrogated.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=14280, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus evidence shows suffering *testing* the characteristic. Job's suffering (VCG-03: Job 1:22, 2:10) is the test that reveals whether sinning will occur — the suffering does not produce sinning in Job, but it creates the conditions under which sinning (charging God with wrong) would be most understandable. The wife's advice ("Curse God and die," Job 2:9) names the sinning option that suffering makes available. 1Sa 15:24 (Saul's sinning under social pressure) shows pressure/fear-of-suffering as a sinning-producing condition. The evidence distinguishes between suffering that tests the will and reveals its direction (Job — sinning resisted) and pressure/fear that deflects the will into sinning (Saul).

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=14281, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — The relationship to suffering is evidenced (T5.4.1 and T5.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=14282, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Limited evidence. The deterrence corpus (VCG-03) shows the positive arc: Psa 119:11 ("I have stored up your word in my heart, that I might not sin against you") describes an ongoing formation practice — word-storage is not a one-time act but a sustained discipline that shapes the inner person against sinning over time. Eze 3:21 ("if you warn the righteous person not to sin, and he does not sin, he shall surely live, because he took warning") shows that receiving warning and heeding it is formative — it shapes the will away from sinning toward life. The negative arc is also evidenced: Hos 4:7/13:2 (escalating sinning forming the person progressively away from God) represents character formation in the wrong direction.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=14283, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning functions in the formation arc as the *negative pole* — it is what formation must move away from. 1Jo 2:1 ("I am writing these things to you so that you may not sin") situates the epistolary formation project around sinning as the target of resistance. The formation arc that the corpus prescribes is: word stored → fear of God maintained → pondering practiced → tongue guarded → sinning resisted. Each step in this formation arc is a movement away from the sinning characteristic. The characteristic is not itself part of sanctification but the resistance of it is: not-sinning is the outcome of sanctifying formation.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=14284, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Formation-related evidence is present (T5.5.1 and T5.5.2), though it is evidenced primarily through the formation disciplines that counter sinning rather than through sinning itself as a sanctifying agent.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=14285, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Limited eschatological evidence is present. Joh 5:14 ("sin no more, that nothing worse may happen to you") implies that there is a final reckoning toward which sinning trajectories move — "nothing worse" opens an eschatological dimension of consequence. 2Pe 2:4 ("committed to chains of gloomy darkness to be kept until the judgment") shows that the angels who sinned are being held for a final judgment — the eschatological reckoning is the horizon toward which sinning accountability moves. Eze 18:4/20 ("the soul who sins shall die") has an eschatological dimension in its ultimacy — the mortal consequence is final.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=14286, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The present experience of wilful sinning anticipates, in the corpus, either *judgment* or *forgiveness* as its eschatological resolution — there is no neutral final state. 2Pe 2:4 shows judgment as the trajectory for un-remedied sinning (the angels kept for judgment). Lev 4:35 / 2Sa 12:13 / 1Jo 2:1 (advocate with the Father) show forgiveness as the eschatological resolution for sinning that is confessed and remedied. The characteristic's present operation anticipates a final accounting — the sinning act is not closed at the moment of commission but remains open before God until either judgment or forgiveness resolves it.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=14287, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced (T5.6.1 and T5.6.2), though the corpus addresses it obliquely rather than as a primary topic.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=14288, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-1]** E — T2.8 found limited evidence of constitutional deposit: Isa 1:4 (generational sinning as a national characteristic — "offspring of evildoers") and Hos 4:7/13:2 (escalating sinning producing disposition-formation). The developmental consequence of this deposit is evidenced as: (1) *Progressively constrained will*: escalating sinning (Hos 13:2 — "they sin more and more, and make for themselves metal images") shows the will progressively invested in and constrained by the sinning direction. (2) *Cultural/generational transmission*: Isa 1:4 ("offspring of evildoers, children who deal corruptly") suggests a generational consequence where the sinning pattern becomes a transmittable moral orientation. (3) *Diminished capacity for the opposite*: Neh 13:26 ("foreign women made even him to sin") implies that sustained exposure to sinning influences erodes constitutional resistance — the deposit of exposure narrows the will's effective range.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=14289, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Limited evidence. Isa 1:4 ("offspring of evildoers, children who deal corruptly") names a generational consequence in the form of a moral heritage — children shaped in the sinning pattern of their forebears. This is a social/formative transmission rather than a biological mechanism. The counter-evidence is also present: Eze 18:20 ("the son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father") — though this addresses legal accountability rather than formative transmission, it warrants caution about over-reading generational consequence from Isa 1:4 alone.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=14290, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-1]** E — T2.8 found limited deposit evidence (not none). T5.7 is addressed in T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 accordingly.

---

T5 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       21/21 ✓ (T5.1.1–T5.1.3, T5.2.1–T5.2.3, T5.3.1–T5.3.3, T5.4.1–T5.4.3, T5.5.1–T5.5.3, T5.6.1–T5.6.3, T5.7.1–T5.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       21× [CHAR-1] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Eze 18:4/20; Lev 4:14/23/35; Lev 5:10/13; 2Sa 12:13; 1Ki 8:33/35/50; Hos 4:7/13:2; Isa 1:4; Gen 20:9/39:9/42:22; Job 1:22/2:10/7:20; Psa 119:11; 1Sa 15:24; Eze 3:21; Neh 13:26; 1Jo 2:1; Joh 5:14; 2Pe 2:4; 1Cor 6:18 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.


---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=14291, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Three adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside wilful sinning in the corpus. (1) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: the confession register is directly linked to wilful sinning across the corpus — 2Sa 12:13 ("I have sinned against the Lord" — confession immediately following sinning), Jos 7:20 (Achan's confession: "Truly I have sinned against the Lord God of Israel"), 1Sa 15:24 (Saul's confession: "I have sinned"). Confession appears as the expected immediate post-act response. (2) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: the Levitical corpus (VCG-04: Lev 4:22 — "realizes his guilt"; Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed is made known to him") shows guilt-realisation occurring within the wilful-sinning context. (3) *CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent)*: Hos 4:7/13:2 show the negative trajectory from sinning to intensified sinning without turning — the absence of repentance is the adjacent characteristic at the negative end.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=14292, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals that wilful sinning is a *pivot characteristic* in the inner-being landscape: it sits between temptation (pre-act) and consequence (post-act) and is immediately flanked by conscience/guilt (CHAR-17) on the post-act side, confession (CHAR-3) as the prescribed response, and refusal to repent (CHAR-5) as the negative alternative. The characteristic does not stand alone but is constitutively connected to the entire arc of moral failure and its resolution. Its place in the landscape is as the *act-event* that triggers the entire downstream sequence.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=14293, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=14294, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The sequential relationship is consistently *preceding*: wilful sinning precedes guilt-recognition (CHAR-17), confession (CHAR-3), and either repentance or refusal to repent (CHAR-5). The Lev 4/5 corpus makes this explicit: the sin is committed → it becomes known → the offering is brought → atonement is made → forgiveness is granted (VCG-04 sequence: Lev 4:14, 4:23, 4:35). 2Sa 12:13 shows sinning preceding confession in immediate sequence. Hos 4:7/13:2 show sinning preceding intensified sinning (refusal to repent) when the remedy is not taken. Wilful sinning consistently occupies the *first position* in these sequences.

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=14295, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The relationship is primarily *causal*: wilful sinning causes the conditions that follow. It causes guilt-liability (Eze 18:4 — sinning causes death; Lev 4:14 — sinning triggers the atonement requirement). It causes relational rupture that must be addressed through confession and forgiveness. It causes consequence (1Ki 8:33/35 — national disaster following sinning). The developmental relationship is secondary: sustained sinning without remedy develops into habitual defection (CHAR-6) and refusal to repent (CHAR-5). The sequence from wilful sinning to these states is developmental (requiring repetition and time) rather than immediate and causal.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=14296, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Sequential patterns are clearly evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=14297, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning produces multiple downstream characteristics. (1) *Guilt (CHAR-17)*: Lev 4:22/23 ("realizes his guilt" — guilt arises from sinning). (2) *Conscience activation (CHAR-3 preparatory state)*: Gen 42:22 (Reuben's conscience activated by the brothers' past sinning — guilt surfacing as reckoning). (3) *Habitual defection (CHAR-6)* when unaddressed: Hos 4:7/13:2 (escalating sinning without remedy). (4) *Refusal to repent (CHAR-5)*: Isa 1:4 ("utterly estranged") — the settled estrangement is the outcome when sinning produces no turning. (5) *Contagious sin (CHAR-7)*: Gen 20:9 — Abraham's sinning brought sin on Abimelech's household without their consent.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=14298, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus evidences wilful sinning being produced by: (1) *Temptation/adversarial pressure*: Gen 39:9 (Potiphar's wife's persistent appeal), Neh 6:13 (enemies' constructed trap), Neh 13:26 (foreign women as corrupting influence) — these are external productions. (2) *Fear of persons*: 1Sa 15:24 ("I feared the people") — fear as the producing condition. (3) *Escalating compulsion*: Hos 13:2 — earlier sinning producing the next sinning — CHAR-1 is produced by prior CHAR-1 in the escalation pattern. (4) *Contempt*: Pro 14:21 ("whoever despises his neighbor is a sinner") — contempt as the inner condition producing interpersonal sinning.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=14299, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Wilful sinning is a constituent element of: (1) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: habitual defection is constituted by repeated wilful sinning that has become a settled pattern — CHAR-1 is the repeated act that constitutes CHAR-6. (2) *CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent)*: refusal to repent presupposes sinning that has occurred — CHAR-1 is the prerequisite event whose un-repented state becomes CHAR-5. Conversely, what constitutes wilful sinning itself is the will (T1.8) operating through the decision-point (T5.2.1) — no single adjacent characteristic constitutes wilful sinning; it is constituted by the will's own choosing.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=14300, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are extensively evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=14301, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The primary term cha.ta (H2398) is shared across multiple M10 characteristics: CHAR-2 (Unintentional sinning — also uses cha.ta in the Lev 4/5 unintentional register), CHAR-3 (Confession — "I have sinned," cha.ta used in confession contexts), CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression — cha.ta used for sinning whose moral quality is not registered). The NT hamartanō (G0264) similarly spans multiple characteristics. The shared vocabulary is the primary indicator that the characteristics are not entirely distinct phenomena but facets of a broader semantic field.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=14302, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. The H2398 root (cha.ta — miss, offend, incur guilt) generates multiple derived terms across M10: chat.tat (H2403B — the sin as a named offense, dominant in CHAR-13 Sin as divine record), chat.ta (H2400 — the sinner as moral character, CHAR-16), cha.ta.ah (H2401 — the sin offering category, present in VCG-04), chat.ta.ah (H2403A). This single root architecture underlies the distinctions between act (CHAR-1), character (CHAR-16), record (CHAR-13), and covering (sin offering). The root-level architecture reveals that what the programme treats as separate characteristics are grammatically and semantically related modalities of a single Hebrew semantic field.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=14303, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The vocabulary sharing reveals that CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning), CHAR-2 (Unintentional sinning), CHAR-3 (Confession), CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression), CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record), and CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character) are not conceptually independent phenomena but *perspectival facets* of the same underlying moral reality — sinning as the human condition of wrongdoing before God. The distinction the programme draws between them is analytical (differentiating the act from the character, the record from the confession) while the vocabulary treats them as a unified field. The act (CHAR-1) and the character (CHAR-16) share the same root because in Scripture the act and its moral weight on the actor are not ultimately separable.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=14304, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=14305, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-1]** E — CHAR-2 (Unintentional sinning) most closely resembles CHAR-1 in vocabulary (both use cha.ta, both use the Lev 4/5 cultic-atonement framework). The precise distinction is the quality of the will's engagement: CHAR-1 requires that the person knows what they are doing and does it — the will is engaged with knowledge of the wrong. CHAR-2 occurs when the wrong is not consciously intended — the Lev 4:22/27 formulation ("doing unintentionally any one of all the things that by the commandments of the Lord his God ought not to be done") defines CHAR-2. The distinction is at the point of inner awareness: CHAR-1 involves the will acting against known moral reality; CHAR-2 involves the will acting without that awareness, so that conscience engages only after the fact.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=14306, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The overlap is most visible in the cultic-atonement corpus (VCG-04): Lev 4:22/27 describe unintentional sinning within the same atonement framework as wilful sinning. The boundary is at the moment of inner awareness: Lev 5:17 ("though he did not know it, he is guilty") shows CHAR-2 territory — guilt without prior awareness. Lev 5:15 ("commits a breach of faith and sins unintentionally in any of the holy things of the Lord") also sits in CHAR-2. The CHAR-1 corpus is distinguished by the explicit knowing quality — Gen 39:9 (Joseph knew the act as "great wickedness"), Jos 7:20 (Achan knew "this is what I did"), 2Sa 12:13 (David confesses the known act).

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=14307, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The distinction is one of *kind* at the volitional level: CHAR-1 and CHAR-2 are not the same act at different degrees of intensity but differ in the quality of inner engagement. CHAR-1 is the will knowingly choosing the wrong; CHAR-2 is the will acting without that knowledge. This is a difference in kind (knowing vs not knowing) rather than degree (more or less serious). Both produce moral liability (both require atonement in the cultic system), confirming that the distinction is not about moral seriousness but about the inner faculty of knowledge engaged at the moment of the act.

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=14308, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=14309, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Several verses are likely to function as anchors in adjacent characteristics. Psa 51:4 ("against you, you only, have I sinned") is the paradigmatic confession verse — it is the anchor for CHAR-3 (Confession) as well as for CHAR-1. Eze 18:4/20 ("the soul who sins shall die") anchor the personal-accountability dimension that is relevant to CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state) and CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record). Lev 4:35 ("the priest shall make atonement for him for the sin which he has committed, and he shall be forgiven") is the Levitical formula that anchors CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received) as well as CHAR-1. 2Sa 12:13 (sin confessed → forgiveness declared) spans CHAR-1, CHAR-3, and CHAR-14.

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=14310, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Shared anchors (Psa 51:4, Eze 18:4/20, 2Sa 12:13, Lev 4:35) reveal that wilful sinning, confession, guilt, and forgiveness form an integrated sequence in Scripture — the same verse evidences multiple characteristics because the characteristics are not separate events but *facets of a single moral episode*. Psa 51:4's anchor function across CHAR-1 and CHAR-3 reveals that in the primary biblical evidence, sinning and confession are not separable into entirely distinct inner events but are the two faces of a single relational encounter with God: the act of sinning and the act of naming it before God.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=14311, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Shared verse anchors are identified (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=14312, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The primary dimension (will/volition) is shared with CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent — also a volitional characteristic), CHAR-6 (Habitual defection — sustained volitional pattern), and CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression — also operates through the volitional faculty). The conscience dimension (secondary) is shared with CHAR-3 (Confession — conscience names the act) and CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state — guilt as post-act conscience experience). The relational dimension is shared across most M10 characteristics, as the cluster is constituted around relational moral failure.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=14313, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The dimensional sharing reveals that the M10 cluster is structurally unified by a small set of shared dimensions (will, conscience, relational capacity) that appear across multiple characteristics in different configurations. Wilful sinning (CHAR-1) shares its primary volitional dimension with habitual defection (CHAR-6) and refusal to repent (CHAR-5) — all three are volitional characteristics. What distinguishes them is not the faculty (will) but the temporal and developmental configuration: CHAR-1 is the single act; CHAR-6 is the repeated pattern; CHAR-5 is the refusal to reverse. The dimensional sharing reveals M10's characteristics as a developmental spectrum of the same volitional phenomenon.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=14314, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-1]** E — Dimensional sharing evidence is available from the corpus analysis (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T6 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T6.1.1–T6.1.3, T6.2.1–T6.2.3, T6.3.1–T6.3.4, T6.4.1–T6.4.4, T6.5.1–T6.5.4, T6.6.1–T6.6.3, T6.7.1–T6.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-1] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited verses in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T7.


---

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=14315, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The primary Hebrew term is **cha.ta (H2398)** — a verb whose root meaning is "to miss (a target), to go wrong, to offend, to incur guilt." The root imagery of missing a mark (the physical sense appears in Judg 20:16 — slingers who would not "miss," same root) applied to the moral realm names sin as a *directional failure*: the person aimed elsewhere than the moral mark. The primary NT term is **hamartanō (G0264)** — from hamartia, likewise meaning "to miss, to err, to sin." The semantic convergence of Hebrew and Greek roots around the "missing the mark" image is analytically significant: both Testaments anchor wilful sinning in the concept of a directional failure — not a neutral act but one that misses what was aimed at. Paraptōma (G3900, once in VCG-05: Gal 6:1) means "a false step, a trespass" — accidental entanglement rather than directed missing.

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=14316, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Cha.ta (H2398) operates as: (1) **Qal verb** — the active sinning act ("the soul who sins," Eze 18:4; "I have sinned," 2Sa 12:13; "they sinned against me," Eze 14:13). (2) **Piel verb** (intensive/causative) — "making/causing to sin" (not in this corpus directly but underlying the causative sense in 1Ki 14:16 — Jeroboam who "made Israel to sin," which belongs to CHAR-7). (3) **Hiphil** — causative. In VCG-04 the Lev 4/5 corpus uses the noun form chat.tat alongside the verb. Hamartanō (G0264) is similarly verbal throughout — an action-term not a state-term. The grammatical range reveals that sinning is *event-language* not *condition-language*: the primary grammatical form names a discrete act, not a standing state. This confirms that CHAR-1 is constitutively an act rather than a condition.

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=14317, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Cha.ta (H2398) operates across: (1) *Directed violation against God* (VCG-01 — the entire God-directed corpus); (2) *Interpersonal wrong* (VCG-02 — "sin against his neighbour," "sin against innocent blood"); (3) *Cultic-legal wrong requiring ritual remedy* (VCG-04 — sin offering system); (4) *Self-directed wrong* (1Cor 6:18 — hamartanō against one's own body; Pro 8:36 — cha.ta against wisdom/self); (5) *Speech-mode wrong* (Psa 39:1, Job 2:10 — sin with tongue/lips); (6) *Missing the way through haste* (Pro 19:2 — broadest semantic reach). The semantic range is wide: from the gravest covenantal violation (Eze 14:13 — land sinning against God) to the most mundane practical missing (Pro 19:2 — haste missing the way). The breadth reveals that cha.ta covers the full spectrum of the will's directional failures.

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=14318, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The vocabulary cluster across M10 achieves some of these distinctions. The act/condition distinction is partly served by cha.ta (act) versus chat.tat (H2403B — the named sin as an ongoing record, CHAR-13's territory) versus chat.ta (H2400 — the sinner as a constituted character, CHAR-16's territory). Within CHAR-1's own corpus, paraptōma (G3900, Gal 6:1) introduces a nuance — "caught in a transgression" suggests a condition of being-caught rather than simply the act. Hamartanō throughout VCG-05 treats sinning as an action-event. The vocabulary does distinguish act from constituted character (cha.ta vs chat.ta) but does not have a dedicated term for the received/given distinction within the CHAR-1 corpus.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=14319, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus evidences the structural opposite through negative constructions rather than a dedicated antonym. "Did not sin" (lo cha.ta — Job 1:22; 2:10; 1Sa 24:11; Judg 11:27) names the opposite as the absence of the act. Eze 3:21 ("he does not sin") names the opposite as the outcome of heeding warning. The positive opposite is named in Eze 18:20 through the contrast pair: "the righteousness of the righteous shall be upon himself, and the wickedness of the wicked shall be upon himself" — righteousness as the structural opposite at the character level. Within the CHAR-1 act corpus, the structural opposite is not a single dedicated term but the absence of the sinning act (lo cha.ta) plus the presence of integrity (tummah — Gen 20:6: "integrity of heart").

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=14320, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Yes. Chat.ta (H2400) is the person-type term — "sinner" — naming the one who habitually possesses or embodies the sinning characteristic. However, this term belongs primarily to CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character) rather than to CHAR-1 directly. Within the CHAR-1 corpus, person-type language is present but secondary: "sinful nation" (Isa 1:4 — cha.ta.im, plural of chat.ta) names the people-type constituted by sinning. The person-type term confirms the connection between the act (CHAR-1) and the character (CHAR-16): repeated wilful sinning constitutes the sinner as a moral type.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=14321, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus does not contain a dedicated seeking-term for wilful sinning itself (sinning is committed, not sought). However, the corpus evidences the seeking of the *remedy* for sinning through supplication: 1Ki 8:33/35/50 (Solomon's prayer as the seeking of forgiveness for sinning — the prayer is the supplication term for the forgiven state). Psa 41:4 ("O Lord, be gracious to me; heal me, for I have sinned against you") uses the supplication form (chen + raph.ah) in the aftermath of sinning. The supplication operates at the post-sinning stage, not the sinning stage itself.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=14322, E) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The continuity is striking: cha.ta (H2398) and hamartanō (G0264) share the same "miss the mark" root semantic and the same grammatical category (verb). The LXX translates cha.ta with hamartanō throughout, establishing lexical continuity. The development visible in the NT corpus (VCG-05) is not in the basic definition but in the relational theology: sinning against "your brother" (Mat 18:15) and "against Christ" (1Cor 8:12) extends the relational scope of sinning into the Christological register — a development not present in the OT corpus. The NT also introduces the advocate (1Jo 2:1) as the specific remedy for sinning, which reflects development in the understanding of how sinning is addressed even as the act-definition remains constant.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=14323, E) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Paraptōma (G3900) represents a partially new NT deployment — "false step, trespass" — that emphasises the element of surprise or entanglement in sinning (Gal 6:1: "caught in any transgression"). This framing of sinning as something a person can be "caught in" introduces a nuance — sinning as a moral entanglement that the community must address through gentle restoration — that is less prominent in the OT cha.ta corpus. Hamartanō itself is not new (it is the LXX rendering of cha.ta) but its deployment in the 1 John corpus ("I am writing these things so that you may not sin," 1Jo 2:1) with the advocacy framework reflects a theological development in the understanding of how the sinning will is addressed under the new covenant.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=14324, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The full vocabulary arc — from cha.ta's physical "missing" (Judg 20:16) through its covenantal "sinning against God" (Eze 14:13), through the LXX hamartanō bridge, into the NT's "sin against your brother" (Mat 18:15) and "sin against your own body" (1Cor 6:18), to the paraptōma "entanglement" (Gal 6:1) — reveals that wilful sinning covers the entire relational map of the human person (God, others, self) across its full directional range (deliberate, entangled, sustained). The semantic arc also reveals that sinning is consistently treated as an *act with consequences* at every point — the vocabulary never becomes purely abstract or dispositional.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=14325, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The primary anchor verse is Psa 51:4: "Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight." Cha.ta functions here as the confessional predicate — the act that David names as the fundamental moral reality of his situation. The term functions syntactically as the verb of confession ("I have sinned"), semantically as the identifier of the act's relational target ("against you, you only"), and theologically as the premise of the entire psalm's petition for forgiveness. It locates the entire moral reality — adultery, murder, deception — within the single relational frame of sinning against God. The function is definitional: cha.ta names what all David's wrongs ultimately are in their deepest character.

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=14326, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The corpus spans multiple literary forms. (1) *Psalm* (Psa 51:4; 39:1; 119:11; 41:4): devotional/confessional poetry requires reading the cha.ta usage within the psalmist's first-person relational posture — the sinning named is personally owned, not described from outside. (2) *Narrative* (Gen 20:6/9/39:9; Jos 7:20; 1Sa 15:24; 2Sa 12:13): responsible interpretation requires attention to the narrator's moral framing — whether sinning is affirmed, denied, or corrected within the narrative logic. (3) *Prophetic oracle* (Eze 18:4/20; 14:13; Hos 4:7; 13:2; Isa 1:4): prophetic sinning-language requires reading within the covenant-warning and judgment frame — sinning here is declared as the cause of coming or present judgment. (4) *Levitical law* (Lev 4/5 — VCG-04): casuistic law requires reading sinning as a legally defined category triggering prescribed obligations.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=14327, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Two key argument structures. (1) *Eze 18:4/20 accountability argument*: premise — all souls belong to God; sub-premise — the sinning soul incurs its own consequence; conclusion — "the soul who sins shall die" and "the son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father." The argument establishes individual accountability through the soul's ownership by God. (2) *1Ki 8:33–50 covenantal restoration argument*: premise — sinning against God produces defeat and disaster; premise — the path back involves turning, naming, praying; conclusion — God will forgive and grant compassion. The argument maps sinning as the cause of rupture and prayer as the causal path to restoration.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=14328, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Four primary contextual settings. (1) *Covenantal* (VCG-01 majority — Deu 9:18; Jer 40:3/44:23; Hos 4:7; 13:2): sinning occurs within and against the Sinai covenant framework; the relational standard is the covenant Lord. (2) *Cultic/liturgical* (VCG-04 — Lev 4/5; Eze 43:23): sinning is defined relative to the sanctuary and its sacred obligations; the context is ritual purity and priestly mediation. (3) *Judicial* (Deu 19:15 — legal testimony; Gen 20:9 — Abimelech's complaint as a quasi-judicial protest): sinning is assessed in a legal-evaluative setting. (4) *Communal/discipleship* (VCG-02 NT — Mat 18:15/21; Luk 17:3/4; 1Cor 8:12): sinning is addressed within the community of disciples, requiring confrontation and forgiveness processes.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=14329, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Psa 51:4 functions as the primary anchor: "Against you, you only, have I sinned and done what is evil in your sight, so that you may be justified in your words and blameless in your judgment." This verse most fully and directly expresses the essential character of wilful sinning because: (1) it names sinning as a vertical relational act directed against God specifically; (2) it makes God the sole primary offended party even in a case involving human victims; (3) it frames sinning as the basis of God's righteous judgment — God's moral authority is established by the reality of human sinning; (4) it is voiced from the perspective of the sinning person in full moral awareness.

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=14330, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Psa 51:4 uniquely reveals the *exclusive vertical dimension* of sinning. While the corpus abundantly evidences sinning against persons (VCG-02), sinning against self (1Cor 6:18), and sinning with the lips (Psa 39:1), Psa 51:4 makes the claim that even when sinning has multiple human dimensions, its fundamental reality is vertical — against God alone ("against you, you only"). No other verse in the corpus makes this exclusive claim: Gen 39:9 names sinning against God but alongside human wrong; 2Sa 12:13 ("I have sinned against the Lord") is the confession but not the exclusivity claim. Psa 51:4's "you only" establishes that the theocentric frame is not one consideration among many but the constitutive definition of what sinning ultimately is.

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=14331, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-1]** E — **Moral psychology** (specifically the dual-process framework and moral disengagement theory) is the most useful human science lens. The wilful sinning corpus is constituted around the gap between moral knowledge and moral action — knowing the wrong and doing it anyway (1Sa 15:24; Jos 7:20; Gen 39:9 as the positive counter-case). Moral psychology's dual-process framework (System 1 automatic processes vs System 2 deliberative reasoning) illuminates the speed at which sinning occurs despite available cognitive moral knowledge. Bandura's moral disengagement theory (the mechanisms by which people disengage their moral self-standards to sin without inner conflict) illuminates the suppression of conscience visible in the escalation pattern (Hos 4:7/13:2).

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=14332, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Moral disengagement theory illuminates Isa 1:4's trajectory: "they have despised the Holy One of Israel, they are utterly estranged" — the contempt for God that follows sustained sinning corresponds precisely to Bandura's mechanism of "moral justification" and "dehumanization" (here applied to God as the moral authority): repeated sinning produces cognitive reconstruction of the moral standard as unworthy of allegiance. This explains why the deterrence corpus (Exo 20:20; Psa 119:11) functions by *re-engaging* the moral standard through specific practices (fear, word-storage) — it is a moral re-engagement strategy against the disengagement trajectory. The dual-process framework illuminates 1Sa 15:24 (Saul): social pressure (fear of people) activated a fast automatic System 1 response that overrode the slower deliberative System 2 processing that would have maintained obedience to God's command.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=14333, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-1]** E — The primary divergence: moral psychology treats moral disengagement as a process of psychological self-protection — the person avoids cognitive dissonance by adjusting their moral standards. The verse evidence treats it as a relational reality — sinning against God is not primarily about the sinner's cognitive self-management but about the damaged vertical relationship that requires divine resolution. 2Sa 12:13 — "The Lord also has put away your sin" — is not a psychological event but a relational one: God acts to remove the sin from the relational record. The human science framework has no equivalent for this: moral psychology addresses the sinner's internal cognitive state, not the divine relational accounting. The divergence reveals that wilful sinning is irreducibly relational (against God/persons) in a way that psychological frameworks, focused on the individual's inner economy, cannot fully account for.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=14334, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-1]** E — Moral psychology surfaces the question of *moral licensing* — the phenomenon where prior good behavior licenses subsequent sinning ("I've been good enough, so this one time is acceptable"). This is not explicitly evidenced in the CHAR-1 corpus as a named mechanism, though Saul's partial obedience (1Sa 15:24) has a structural resemblance: he did obey the command partially and may have regarded that partial obedience as license for the deviation. The framework also surfaces *diffusion of responsibility* (relevant to Gen 20:9 — Abraham's deception spreading sinning to Abimelech's household without their intent). These framings suggest additional analytical angles on VCG-02 interpersonal sinning that the verse evidence itself does not name but which the human science framework makes visible. No further verse investigation is required — the corpus does not need expansion, but these framings enrich the analytical description.

---

T7 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       20/20 ✓ (T7.1.1–T7.1.10, T7.2.1–T7.2.6, T7.3.1–T7.3.4)
  2. Scope marker:       20× [CHAR-1] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Eze 18:4/20; Eze 14:13; Psa 51:4/39:1/119:11/41:4; Gen 20:6/9/39:9; Jos 7:20; 1Sa 15:24/24:11; 2Sa 12:13; 1Ki 8:33/35/50; Hos 4:7/13:2; Isa 1:4; Jer 40:3/44:23; Lev 4:14/22/23/27/28/35/5:10/13; Job 1:22/2:10; Judg 11:27; Deu 19:15; Mat 18:15/21; Luk 17:3/4; 1Cor 6:18/8:12; Gal 6:1; 1Jo 2:1; 2Pe 2:4; Pro 8:36/19:2; Ecc 5:6; Neh 6:13/13:26 — all in §3)
  → PASS. All 8 tiers complete.
