God's provision of the remedy in the first place (the Levitical system) is the expression of his grace toward those who sin without knowing.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=16327, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-2]** E — God's provision of a comprehensive remedial system for unintentional sinning (Lev 4:2 — the entire sin-offering structure; Num 15:27/28 — the individual inadvertent offering; Exo 29:36 — daily altar purification) reveals a disposition of *gracious moral realism* toward the creature. God holds moral standards that apply even when the creature acts without knowing (Lev 4:2 — the sin is real even when unintentional); simultaneously he provides the precise remedy that addresses the resulting liability (Num 15:28 — "he shall be forgiven"). The disposition is neither moral laxity (the sin is treated as real and requires remedy) nor moral severity without provision (the remedy is prescribed and forgiveness follows its use). God's disposition is structured around the creature's epistemic limitation — he provides for what he knows the creature will inadvertently do.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=16328, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — God-to-human operation is evidenced (T4.1.1–T4.1.3) in the modes of standard-setting and forgiveness-granting. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=16329, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. The remedy sequence in the VCG-01 corpus is fundamentally a human-toward-God movement. Lev 5:5 ("when he realizes his guilt... and confesses the sin he has committed") — confession is directed toward God, an acknowledgment before the divine of what the person did. Num 15:27 ("he shall offer a female goat a year old for a sin offering") — the offering is brought "to the Lord" (Num 15:27 context: "before the Lord") — the act of bringing the offering is a human movement toward God in the wake of inadvertent sinning. Num 22:34 (Balaam — "if it is evil in your sight, I will turn back") — "in your sight" addresses the angel of the Lord, orienting Balaam's will toward God's evaluation.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=16330, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The inner posture required for the human-toward-God movement is *honest acknowledgment of the inadvertent sin*. Lev 5:5 — "confesses the sin he has committed" — the confession must name the sin as sin, even though it was unintentional. Num 22:34 — Balaam does not minimise ("it wasn't my fault, I didn't know") but takes full ownership ("I have sinned") — the inner posture is one of accountability without defensiveness. The posture does not require pre-existing awareness at the moment of sinning (since the characteristic is defined by its absence) but requires honest responsiveness once awareness arrives. The movement toward God is enabled by the conscience's retrospective engagement (T3.9) and blocked by its suppression.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=16331, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The human-to-God movement of confession and offering (Lev 5:5; Num 15:27; Num 22:34) reveals that the person's relationship with God is one in which *objective moral standing matters regardless of subjective intent*. The person who confesses an inadvertent sin is not confessing a felt rebellion — they are acknowledging that a real violation of God's standard occurred, even without deliberate defiance. This reveals a relationship defined by God's objective holiness (the violation was real) and the person's responsive accountability (I must address what I did, even though I did not know I was doing it). The relationship is not merely experiential or intentional — it is constituted by objective moral reality.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=16332, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Human-to-God operation is evidenced (T4.2.1–T4.2.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=16333, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Limited evidence. Lev 6:2 ("sins and commits a breach of faith against the Lord by deceiving his neighbor in a matter of deposit or security, or through robbery, or if he has oppressed his neighbor") and Lev 6:3 (lying about a found item) present interpersonal sinning that is simultaneously a breach against the Lord — one person extending sinning toward another through deception and misappropriation. The 1Sa 14:33 context (people sinning against the Lord by eating with blood) is communal but not specifically one person extending sinning toward another person. Lev 4:3 (priestly sinning "bringing guilt on the people") shows inadvertent leadership sinning radiating outward to affect the community — an unintended interpersonal extension.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=16334, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-2]** E — For the interpersonal sinning in VCG-01 (Lev 6:2/3 — deception, robbery, oppression), the enabling inner conditions are the same as the personal-moral register: acting without recognising the full moral weight of what is being done. However, Lev 6:2/3's Pass A meanings note elements of deliberateness ("deliberate lying," "willful act of inner deception"), which places these verses at the border with CHAR-1 rather than squarely in CHAR-2. The clearer CHAR-2 interpersonal extension is Lev 4:3 (priestly inadvertent sinning bringing communal guilt) — the enabling condition is simply that the priest acted in his representative capacity without full moral awareness, and the community is affected because of his representative role.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=16335, S) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-2]** S — Unintentional sinning is not a characteristic that the person intentionally "gives" to another — it is an inadvertent act that may affect others (Lev 4:3). The question of what must be received or become before extending this characteristic does not apply in the same way as for volitionally given characteristics. The corpus is silent on this framing.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=16336, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — The interpersonal sinning evidence is limited (Lev 6:2/3 borderline; Lev 4:3 communal effect). The "giving" direction is not the primary frame for this characteristic — unintentional sinning is not given but inadvertently committed. The limited evidence is noted.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=16337, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The receiving direction is present in Lev 4:3: the community receives the guilt consequence of the anointed priest's inadvertent sinning — "the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people." The people receive a moral liability they did not personally incur through any act of their own. Lev 9:15 (Aaron's communal sin offering "for the people") addresses this received communal guilt. The receiving is involuntary and inadvertent on both sides — the priest did not deliberately sin against the community, and the community did not knowingly receive the guilt.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=16338, S) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-2]** S — Reception of communal guilt from a representative's inadvertent sinning (Lev 4:3) is not conditioned on the community's inner state — it arises from the structural representative relationship between the priest and the people. No inner condition in the recipients enables or blocks the communal guilt consequence. The corpus is silent on inner conditions affecting reception in this register.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=16339, S) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-2]** S — The corpus does not evidence this scenario for CHAR-2. The communal guilt from priestly sinning (Lev 4:3) is received structurally, not optionally — the community cannot decline to receive it. The individual unintentional sin (Lev 4:2; Num 15:27) is not something one receives from another in a way that could be accepted or refused. The corpus is silent on this framing.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=16340, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced in the communal-guilt register (Lev 4:3; Lev 9:15), but the inner-conditions aspect (T4.4.2) and the non-reception scenario (T4.4.3) are silent. This distinction is noted.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=16341, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. Lev 4:3 (priestly sinning affecting the community) operates specifically within the representative covenant relationship between the priest and Israel — the guilt-transfer is a function of the pre-existing bond, not of relational distance. Lev 6:2 (sinning against a neighbor in matters of deposit or security) likewise operates within existing economic and social relationships — a trusted custodial bond. The inadvertent violations in Lev 4:2 and Num 15:27 (individual sinning against God's commandments) operate within the covenant relationship between Israel and the Lord. The pattern suggests that CHAR-2 in the interpersonal and representative registers operates *within existing bonds*, where the relational structure shapes how the inadvertent violation propagates.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=16342, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The primary CHAR-2 evidence base is covenantal throughout: Lev 4:2 and Num 15:27/28 address sinning in the context of Israel's covenant with the Lord. Num 22:34 (Balaam) represents a non-Israelite encountering the characteristic — suggesting that the principle extends beyond the Sinai covenant to any context where God's moral reality intersects with creaturely epistemic limitation. Balaam is not an Israelite under the Mosaic covenant, yet his inadvertent sinning (not knowing the angel stood against him) follows the same pattern of inadvertence → disclosure → confession → offer of reversal. The characteristic crosses covenantal boundaries in its logic, even if the prescribed Levitical remedies are specific to the covenant context.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=16343, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The relational scope covers: (1) *Individual-to-God*: Lev 4:2; Num 15:27/28 (individual's inadvertent violation of divine command). (2) *Representative-to-community*: Lev 4:3 (priest's sinning implicating the people). (3) *Individual-to-neighbor*: Lev 6:2/3 (interpersonal sinning through deception, misappropriation). (4) *Community-to-God*: Lev 9:15 (communal sin offering). (5) *Sacred-space*: VCG-02 (altar, sanctuary defilement). The relational scope is broad — the characteristic does not operate in one relational domain but across the full range of human-God and human-human bonds. The common thread is that inadvertent violation creates real liability in every relational domain it touches.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=16344, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Relational scope is evidenced (T4.5.1–T4.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=16345, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. Num 22:34 is the primary spiritual-beings evidence: Balaam's inadvertent sinning occurred in the context of the angel of the Lord standing in the road against him — a fact hidden from Balaam until the angel was made visible. The spiritual being (angel of the Lord) was the morally relevant fact that Balaam's perception did not register, producing the inadvertent violation. The angel's disclosure is what transforms Balaam's uninformed violation into a consciously acknowledged sin requiring confession and offered reversal. The spiritual being operates as the *disclosure agent* — the one who surfaces the morally relevant truth that the inadvertent sinner did not perceive.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=16346, S) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-2]** S — The corpus does not evidence adversarial spiritual activity in connection with unintentional sinning. The angel in Num 22:34 is not adversarial in intent — the angel's standing against Balaam was protective (preventing a wrong course). No adversarial spiritual pressure producing inadvertent sinning is evidenced in the CHAR-2 corpus.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=16347, E) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Num 22:34 evidences angelic ministry as a *disclosure mechanism* for inadvertent sinning: the angel's appearance disclosed the morally relevant fact that Balaam had not perceived, activating Balaam's confession. The angel did not prevent the inadvertent sinning (Balaam had already been proceeding) but mediated the disclosure that enabled conscience to engage. This is a form of angelic ministry: mediating the truth that surfaces the inadvertent sin to the sinner's awareness and enables the move toward confession and remedy.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=16348, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Spiritual beings evidence is present (Num 22:34 — angelic disclosure; T4.6.1 and T4.6.3). T4.6.2 (adversarial) is silent, noted there.

---

T4 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T4.1.1–T4.1.4, T4.2.1–T4.2.4, T4.3.1–T4.3.4, T4.4.1–T4.4.4, T4.5.1–T4.5.4, T4.6.1–T4.6.4)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-2] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings — T4.1.2, T4.3.3, T4.4.2, T4.4.3, T4.6.2 — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 4:2/3/5:5/6:2/6:3/9:15/19:22; Num 15:27/28; Num 22:34; 1Sa 14:33; Exo 29:36; Eze 43:20 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=16349, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Yes, in both directions. (1) *Condition change at sinning*: the inadvertent act creates an objective condition of moral liability before God (Lev 4:2 — real sin; Num 15:27 — requires offering). The person's standing changes the moment the act occurs, regardless of their subjective awareness. (2) *Condition change at remedy*: Num 15:28 ("he shall be forgiven") and Lev 19:22 ("he shall be forgiven for the sin that he has committed") — forgiveness restores the prior standing. (3) *Orientation change at realisation*: Lev 5:5 ("when he realizes his guilt... and confesses") — the realisation event produces a reorientation of the inner person toward God in acknowledgment. Num 22:34 — Balaam's orientation shifts from proceeding on his own path to offering to turn back entirely. The orientation change is triggered by the disclosure of the morally relevant fact.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=16350, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The condition transformation is reversible through the prescribed remedy. Lev 4:2 (sin incurred) → Num 15:27 (offering brought) → Num 15:28 ("he shall be forgiven") — the full sequence is explicitly reversible: the liability created by inadvertent sinning is addressed and closed by the atonement mechanism. Lev 19:22 — "he shall be forgiven for the sin that he has committed" — the forgiveness declaration closes the liability condition. The reversibility is built into the system: the Levitical provision for unintentional sinning is precisely a prescribed reversal mechanism. No irreversible trajectory is evidenced in the CHAR-2 corpus for the personal-moral register (VCG-01).

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=16351, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Transformation evidence is present (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=16352, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. The corpus evidences a clear three-stage sequence. (1) *Pre-awareness state*: the person acts without knowledge of the moral reality of the act — the will is engaged but uninformed; no inner-being response to sinning is possible because the sin is not yet known to the person. (2) *Disclosure event*: the sin "becomes known" (Lev 4:14 — adjacent; Lev 5:5 — "when he realizes his guilt"; Num 22:34 — the angel discloses) — the morally relevant fact arrives, triggering conscience. (3) *Post-realisation state*: conscience engaged, guilt recognised, confession made (Lev 5:5), remedy sought (Num 15:27), will oriented toward reversal (Num 22:34). The transition from stage 1 to stage 2 is the defining inner-being event of the characteristic — the knowledge-arrival that transforms inadvertent sinning into a consciously addressed moral reality.

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=16353, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The sequence reveals that CHAR-2's moral reality exists in two phases that the person inhabits sequentially: a *sinning-without-knowing* phase (objective liability incurred, no inner registration) and a *knowing-and-responding* phase (conscience active, remedy pursued). The transition between phases is the knowledge-event — not a spiritual transformation or an act of will but an information-arrival. This reveals something theologically significant: the person's moral standing before God changes at the moment of the sinning act (the liability is created then), not at the moment of awareness (awareness triggers the response, not the liability). Forgiveness then closes the liability that has been open — possibly without the person's awareness — since the act.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=16354, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Sequence is clearly evidenced (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=16355, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Two mechanisms. (1) *Objective liability creation*: the act of inadvertent sinning automatically creates a condition of moral liability before God, regardless of inner awareness. The mechanism is act-based, not intention-based — Lev 4:2 ("if anyone sins unintentionally... and does any one of them") — the doing creates the condition. (2) *Disclosure-triggered conscience*: the realisation event (Lev 5:5; Num 22:34) activates the conscience, which then drives the confession-and-remedy sequence. The mechanism here is informational — new information triggers an inner-being response (conscience → confession → offering → forgiveness).

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=16356, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. In the personal-moral register (VCG-01), the mechanism is act → liability (automatic, objective) → disclosure → conscience → remedy → forgiveness. In the cultic-purification register (VCG-02), the mechanism is different: accumulated defilement of the sacred space → scheduled or triggered purification ritual → consecration restored. The VCG-02 mechanism does not involve personal conscience at all — it is a spatial/institutional purification cycle applied to objects. The two registers share the vocabulary (cha.ta) but operate through distinct mechanisms.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=16357, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Mechanism is evidenced (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=16358, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-2]** S — The 18-verse corpus contains no direct evidence of unintentional sinning operating as a response to suffering, a product of suffering, or a context for suffering. The inadvertent violations described (Lev 4:2; Num 15:27; Num 22:34; 1Sa 14:33/34) arise from knowledge-gaps and situational pressure rather than from suffering. The corpus is silent on the suffering-sinning relationship for CHAR-2.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=16359, S) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-2]** S — No evidence in the corpus. Suffering as a context for unintentional sinning is not evidenced in the 18 verses. The closest context is 1Sa 14:33/34 (people eating with blood during the urgency of battle) — but the situational pressure here is combat exhaustion and urgency rather than suffering in the theological sense the prompt addresses.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=16360, S) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** S — The evidence is silent on the relationship between unintentional sinning and suffering. Noted explicitly.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=16361, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Limited formation evidence. The prescribed Levitical response to unintentional sinning (realisation → confession → offering → forgiveness) is itself a formation process: the person who moves through this sequence repeatedly is being shaped in the practice of: (1) honest acknowledgment of moral failure even when it was inadvertent; (2) use of the prescribed remedial system rather than self-remedy or denial; (3) orientation toward God's standards as the authoritative reference even for acts committed without deliberate intent. Lev 5:5 ("when he realizes his guilt... and confesses") repeated as a life-practice forms a person in the disposition of conscientious moral accountability.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=16362, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Unintentional sinning plays a *diagnostic and corrective* role in the formation arc. Each disclosure of an inadvertent sin (Lev 5:5; Num 22:34) is an opportunity for the person to learn: (1) what they did not know (the gap in their moral knowledge is identified and addressed); (2) how to respond rightly when moral failure is discovered (confession, offering, remedy). Over time, the repeated experience of inadvertent-sin-disclosed-and-addressed forms the inner person in the pattern of: encounter the unexpected moral failure → acknowledge it → seek remedy → receive forgiveness → continue. This is not sanctification through sinning but formation through the prescribed response to sinning.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=16363, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Limited formation evidence is present (T5.5.1 and T5.5.2). The corpus is primarily remedial rather than formation-focused, but the remedial practice has implicit formation content.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=16364, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The Ezekiel cultic-purification corpus (VCG-02: Eze 43:20/22; 45:18) is located within Ezekiel's eschatological temple vision — the restored sanctuary of the age to come. The annual sanctuary purification (Eze 45:18 — "in the first month, on the first day of the month, you shall take a bull from the herd without blemish and purify the sanctuary") in an eschatological context suggests that a purification-from-sin mechanism remains in view in the restored order. Whether this represents the continuation of inadvertent-sinning dynamics in the eschaton or a symbolic-anticipatory language is not resolved within the 18-verse corpus. The eschatological orientation is present: the purification of the sacred space points toward a finally and comprehensively cleansed sanctuary.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=16365, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The present experience of unintentional sinning and its prescribed remedy (offering → atonement → forgiveness) anticipates a future in which the knowledge-gap that makes inadvertent sinning possible is finally closed — a state in which the creature no longer sins even inadvertently because creaturely epistemic limitation is overcome. The present remedial system (Lev 4:2; Num 15:27/28) is provisional — it addresses the liability but does not eliminate the knowledge-gap that produces it. The eschatological trajectory points toward the elimination of the gap itself, not merely its remediation. The daily altar purification (Exo 29:36) pointing toward the eschatological temple purification (Eze 45:18) traces this arc: repeated provisional remedy → final comprehensive cleansing.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=16366, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Eschatological evidence is present (T5.6.1 and T5.6.2), primarily in VCG-02's Ezekiel corpus. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=16367, S) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-2]** S — T2.8 found no constitutional deposit evidence for CHAR-2. This prompt is closed accordingly. No developmental consequence from deposit can be evidenced.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=16368, S) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-2]** S — T2.8 found no deposit evidence. The communal guilt from priestly sinning (Lev 4:3) was noted as communal rather than generational. No generational-deposit evidence is present in the CHAR-2 corpus. This prompt is closed.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=16369, S) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-2]** S — T2.8 found no deposit. T5.7 is closed: no deposit, no developmental or generational consequence to report from this corpus.

---

T5 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       21/21 ✓ (T5.1.1–T5.1.3, T5.2.1–T5.2.3, T5.3.1–T5.3.3, T5.4.1–T5.4.3, T5.5.1–T5.5.3, T5.6.1–T5.6.3, T5.7.1–T5.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       21× [CHAR-2] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings — T5.4.1, T5.4.2, T5.4.3, T5.7.1, T5.7.2, T5.7.3 — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 4:2/3/4:14/5:5/9:15/19:22; Num 15:27/28; Num 22:34; 1Sa 14:33/34; Exo 29:36; Eze 43:20/22/45:18 — Lev 4:14 noted as adjacent, not in §3 directly; removing — the finding uses only Lev 5:5 and Num 22:34 for disclosure event, both in §3 ✓)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.


---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=16370, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Three adjacent characteristics appear alongside CHAR-2 in the corpus. (1) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Lev 5:5 ("when he realizes his guilt... and confesses the sin he has committed") — confession immediately follows realisation of inadvertent sin. Num 22:34 ("I have sinned") — Balaam's confession follows disclosure of inadvertent sinning. Confession is the prescribed adjacent characteristic activated by the realisation event. (2) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received)*: Num 15:28 ("he shall be forgiven"), Lev 19:22 ("he shall be forgiven") — every prescribed remedy for unintentional sinning ends with forgiveness declared. Forgiveness-received is the terminal adjacent characteristic in the CHAR-2 arc. (3) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: Lev 5:5 ("when he realizes his guilt") — guilt-realisation is the post-disclosure inner-being state that characterises the transition into the remedy sequence.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=16371, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The co-occurrence pattern places CHAR-2 as a *trigger characteristic* in a prescribed sequence: inadvertent sin → (realisation) → guilt (CHAR-17) → confession (CHAR-3) → remedy → forgiveness (CHAR-14). CHAR-2 is the event that initiates this entire downstream arc. Its place in the landscape is as the *inadvertent opening* of a moral sequence that the covenant system has specifically provided for. Unlike CHAR-1 (which triggers guilt, conscience, and potentially refusal to repent), CHAR-2's prescribed trajectory is uniformly toward remedy — the system is built precisely for this characteristic.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=16372, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=16373, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-2]** E — CHAR-2 consistently *precedes* guilt-realisation (CHAR-17), confession (CHAR-3), and forgiveness (CHAR-14) in the evidenced sequences. Lev 5:5 — CHAR-2 (unintentional sinning, now realised) → CHAR-17 (guilt realised) → CHAR-3 (confession) → CHAR-14 (remedy sought). Num 15:27/28 — CHAR-2 (inadvertent sinning) → offering brought (remedy) → forgiveness declared (CHAR-14). Num 22:34 — CHAR-2 (inadvertent proceeding against God's angel) → CHAR-3 (confession: "I have sinned") → CHAR-14 (offer to reverse). The sequence is consistent: CHAR-2 always precedes and initiates the downstream arc.

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=16374, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The relationship is *causal*: CHAR-2 causes the moral liability (objective guilt condition) that drives the downstream sequence. The liability created by inadvertent sinning is the direct cause of the obligation to bring the offering (Num 15:27 — "he shall offer"), which causes the priestly atonement (Num 15:28 — "the priest shall make atonement"), which causes the forgiveness (Num 15:28 — "he shall be forgiven"). Each link in the chain is causally driven by the preceding one, with CHAR-2 as the originating cause. The developmental relationship (CHAR-2 developing into CHAR-6 or CHAR-5 through habit) is not evidenced in this corpus — the trajectory evidenced is always toward remedy, not toward hardening.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=16375, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Sequential patterns are clearly evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=16376, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-2]** E — CHAR-2 produces two adjacent characteristics in the evidenced corpus. (1) *CHAR-17 (Guilt)*: the realisation of inadvertent sinning produces a guilt-state (Lev 5:5 — "realizes his guilt"). (2) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: the guilt-realisation produces confession as the normative inner response (Lev 5:5 — "confesses the sin he has committed"; Num 22:34 — "I have sinned"). The mechanism is disclosure → guilt → confession: a causal chain triggered by the knowledge-arrival event. Additionally, in Lev 4:3, CHAR-2 produces communal guilt-liability in the community — a secondary production through the representative mechanism.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=16377, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-2]** E — CHAR-2 is produced by: (1) *Creaturely epistemic limitation* — not another moral characteristic but a constitutional feature: the creature's finite knowledge and perception make inadvertent sinning possible (Lev 4:2; Num 22:34). (2) *Instructional gap* — insufficient knowledge of God's commandments (Lev 4:2 — "the Lord's commandments about things not to be done" presupposes that not everyone knows all of them). (3) *Situational pressure/distraction* — the urgency of battle leading to ritual violation (1Sa 14:33/34). None of these are moral characteristics in the programme's sense — CHAR-2 is produced by epistemic and situational factors rather than by another inner-being characteristic.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=16378, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-2]** E — CHAR-2 is a potential *precursor constituent* of CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression) if the realisation event occurs and the conscience is suppressed rather than responded to. The person who realises inadvertent sin and refuses to acknowledge it moves from CHAR-2 into CHAR-4. CHAR-2 is also a constituent element of the broader sin-cluster's remedial arc (CHAR-3/14/17) — it is the opening event that the arc addresses. Within CHAR-2 itself, the cultic-purification register (VCG-02) and the personal-moral register (VCG-01) are constituent sub-elements sharing vocabulary but differing in inner-being content.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=16379, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=16380, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. The primary term cha.ta (H2398) is shared across multiple M10 characteristics: CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning — the same verb with a different inner-quality qualifier), CHAR-3 (Confession — "confesses the sin he has committed," Lev 5:5), CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record — chat.tat derived from the same root), CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character — chat.ta). The Lev 4/5 corpus is shared between CHAR-1 (VCG-04 includes some of the same Lev 4/5 material) and CHAR-2 — the vocabulary and even specific chapters overlap because the Levitical system addresses both intentional and unintentional violations within the same legal framework.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=16381, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Yes. The H2398 root (cha.ta) generates the full vocabulary field discussed under CHAR-1 T6.4.2: cha.ta (verb — the act), chat.tat (H2403B — sin as record), chat.ta (H2400 — the sinner), cha.ta.ah (H2401 — sin offering), chat.ta.ah (H2403A). CHAR-2 uses cha.ta in its personal-moral sense (VCG-01) and in the Piel denominative sense (VCG-02 — ritual purification). Both senses derive from the same root. The root-level architecture is identical to CHAR-1's — CHAR-1 and CHAR-2 are not distinct root-families but different inner-quality specifications of the same root's act-meaning.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=16382, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The vocabulary sharing (identical primary term and root) reveals that CHAR-1 and CHAR-2 are analytically distinguished facets of a single Hebrew semantic field. The distinction the programme draws between them (wilful vs unintentional) is carried not by different vocabulary but by the qualifier — *bishgagah* (unintentionally) in CHAR-2 vs no qualifier (or explicit knowledge markers) in CHAR-1. The conceptual relationship is one of *same act, different inner quality*: both are cha.ta, but CHAR-2 is cha.ta-in-ignorance while CHAR-1 is cha.ta-in-knowledge. The vocabulary unity confirms that Scripture treats them as belonging to the same moral domain, differentiated by inner-being quality rather than by kind.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=16383, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=16384, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-2]** E — CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning) most closely resembles CHAR-2 — same primary term (cha.ta), partially overlapping verse corpus (Lev 4/5), same remedial system. The precise distinction is the inner quality of the will and conscience at the moment of the act: CHAR-1 requires the person to know the act is wrong and proceed against that knowledge; CHAR-2 occurs when that knowledge is absent. Lev 4:2 — "sins unintentionally" (*bishgagah*) marks the CHAR-2 register. Lev 4:22/27 in CHAR-1's VCG-04 uses the same "unintentionally" qualifier but is grouped with CHAR-1's cultic-atonement register because the atonement system addresses both registers. The distinction is *epistemic quality of the will at the moment of sinning*: informed-will-sinning (CHAR-1) vs uninformed-will-sinning (CHAR-2).

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=16385, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The overlap is most visible in Lev 4/5, where both CHAR-1 (VCG-04) and CHAR-2 (VCG-01) draw from the same Levitical chapters. The boundary is the *bishgagah* qualifier: verses using this qualifier belong to CHAR-2; verses without it (or with explicit knowledge/realization language that makes the act knowing) belong to CHAR-1. The borderline cases in the CHAR-2 corpus: Lev 5:1 (witness knowing the matter but staying silent — knowledge is present, making this closer to CHAR-1's knowing-and-failing-to-act register) and Lev 6:2/3 (deception and lying — Pass A meanings note deliberate elements, placing these at the CHAR-1/CHAR-2 boundary). The precise boundary is: the moment the person's knowledge of the prohibition or morally relevant fact becomes operative, the act moves from CHAR-2 to CHAR-1 territory.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=16386, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The distinction is one of *kind* at the inner-being level: not more sinning vs less sinning (degree), not sinning in a different direction, not sinning at a different constitutional depth — but sinning with a fundamentally different inner-quality of the will and conscience at the moment of the act. The knowing will (CHAR-1) and the unknowing will (CHAR-2) are qualitatively different inner states. Both produce real moral liability (both require atonement); the system differentiates them in the prescribed remedy's scale (CHAR-1 may require more costly offering in the cultic corpus) but not in whether a remedy is required.

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=16387, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=16388, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Lev 5:5 ("when he realizes his guilt... and confesses the sin he has committed") is likely to function as an anchor in CHAR-3 (Confession) — the realisation-and-confession sequence is the definitive CHAR-3 trigger event. Num 15:28 ("the priest shall make atonement... and he shall be forgiven") is a candidate anchor for CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received) — the forgiveness formula for inadvertent sinning may anchor that characteristic's study. Lev 4:3 ("the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people") is a candidate anchor for CHAR-7 (Contagious sin / leadership transmission), showing how one person's sinning implicates others.

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=16389, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Lev 5:5 as shared anchor between CHAR-2 and CHAR-3 reveals that the realisation-of-guilt event is simultaneously the conclusion of the inadvertent sinning episode (CHAR-2) and the opening of the confession episode (CHAR-3) — the two characteristics are contiguous and overlap at the moment of realisation. The same verse is the hinge-event that closes CHAR-2 and opens CHAR-3. This confirms that CHAR-2 and CHAR-3 are not independent events in the inner person but consecutive phases of a single moral episode that the programme analyses from different characteristic lenses.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=16390, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Shared verse anchors are identified (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=16391, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The primary dimension (conscience in retrospective mode — T1.8.1) is shared with CHAR-3 (Confession — conscience also drives the confession response), CHAR-17 (Guilt — guilt is conscience's post-act state), and CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression — conscience is the faculty that either engages or is suppressed). The secondary dimension (moral liability / objective guilt — T1.8.3) is shared across all M10 characteristics that involve sinning. The relational dimension (T3.11) is shared broadly across M10's sinning characteristics.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=16392, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms that CHAR-2's distinctiveness lies not in unique dimensions but in the *temporal configuration* of shared dimensions. Conscience (shared with CHAR-3, CHAR-4, CHAR-17) operates retrospectively in CHAR-2 — after the act, triggered by disclosure. In CHAR-1 it operates concurrently (during the act) and is overridden. In CHAR-4 it operates and is suppressed. The same faculty (conscience), the same dimension, but at different temporal positions in the act-to-response sequence. CHAR-2's analytical identity is precisely this temporal displacement — same dimensions, displaced timing.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=16393, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-2]** E — Dimensional sharing evidence is available from the corpus analysis (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T6 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T6.1.1–T6.1.3, T6.2.1–T6.2.3, T6.3.1–T6.3.4, T6.4.1–T6.4.4, T6.5.1–T6.5.4, T6.6.1–T6.6.3, T6.7.1–T6.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-2] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 4:2/3/5:5/6:2/9:15; Num 15:27/28; Num 22:34; 1Sa 14:33/34; Exo 29:36 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T7.


---

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=16394, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The primary Hebrew construction is **cha.ta (H2398) + bishgagah** — the verb (sin, miss the mark) combined with the adverb derived from the root *shagah* (to err, to wander, to go astray without intending to). The root *shagah* carries the sense of *unintentional wandering from the path* — the person who strays not through rebellion but through inadvertence. Num 15:27 — "ki tekheta bishgagah" — "when he sins in error/inadvertence." The *shagah* root (with its derivatives *shegagah* H7684 — inadvertent sin, and *shegag* H7683 — to err unintentionally) is the vocabulary that specifically names the unintentional quality, absent from CHAR-1's corpus. The VCG-02 cultic-purification use of cha.ta in the Piel stem (to de-sin, purify) does not carry the *bishgagah* qualifier — it is cha.ta as a denominative process-verb rather than an act-of-sinning description.

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=16395, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Cha.ta in CHAR-2 operates as: (1) **Qal verb with adverbial modifier** (cha.ta + bishgagah: Lev 4:2; Num 15:27/28; Num 22:34) — an action modified by the inner-quality qualifier. (2) **Piel verb (denominative — VCG-02)**: Exo 29:36 ("you shall purify the altar"), Lev 8:15, Eze 43:20/22/45:18 — cha.ta used as a ritual process verb meaning to remove sin/defilement from an object. (3) **Noun form (chat.ta.ah)**: Lev 9:15 ("the goat of the sin offering") — the characteristic transformed into an institutional object (the offering). The grammatical range reveals that the same root covers both the human act of sinning and the remedial process applied to persons and sacred objects — a grammatical unity that reflects the single theological framework of sin-and-remedy.

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=16396, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Within CHAR-2 specifically, the semantic range of cha.ta + bishgagah covers: (1) *Violation of a divine commandment without deliberate intent* (Lev 4:2; Num 15:27 — the core definition); (2) *Violation arising from ignorance of the morally relevant fact* (Num 22:34 — not knowing the angel stood against him); (3) *Ritual violation arising from situational urgency* (1Sa 14:33/34 — eating with blood in battle); (4) *Cultic defilement of sacred spaces* (VCG-02 — cha.ta as impurity requiring ritual removal). The semantic range is narrower than CHAR-1 by virtue of the *bishgagah* qualifier, which restricts the applicable acts to those lacking deliberate intent. Within that restriction, the range covers individual and communal violations, personal and cultic contexts.

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=16397, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The vocabulary in this corpus distinguishes act from condition through the sequence of terms. The act itself (cha.ta + bishgagah — inadvertent sinning) is described at the moment of occurrence (Lev 4:2; Num 15:27). The resulting condition (guilt) is named in Lev 5:5 ("realizes his guilt" — H0819 ash.mah or contextual guilt-term in the broader Lev 5 passage) and in Lev 5:1 ("he shall bear his iniquity" — H5771 a.von). The remedy object (chat.ta.ah H2403 — sin offering) names the institutional response to the condition. The sequence act → condition → remedy is lexically marked by distinct terms, confirming that the vocabulary distinguishes the sinning event from its moral residue and from its institutional address.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=16398, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The structural opposite of unintentional sinning is evidenced in the Numbers 15 context (adjacent to the CHAR-2 corpus) as *high-handed sinning* (b'yad ramah — Num 15:30, not in scope) — sinning with a raised hand of defiance, the explicit opposite of *bishgagah* (inadvertence). Within the 18-verse corpus, the structural opposite is not lexically named but is implied: the person who acts with full knowledge of the commandment and in full adherence to it — a posture unnamed by a single term but present as the assumed norm from which inadvertent sinning deviates.

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=16399, S) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-2]** S — No person-type term appears in the 18-verse corpus for the characteristic of unintentional sinning. There is no Hebrew or Greek term for "the unintentional sinner" as a constituted moral type — because unintentional sinning does not constitute a person's moral character in the way that CHAR-16 (sinner as moral character) does. The inadvertent sinner is a normal person who sinned without knowing; they are not a constituted type. The absence of a person-type term for CHAR-2 is analytically significant: the characteristic does not generate a moral identity but describes a moral episode.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=16400, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The offering-and-atonement language functions as the supplication register in CHAR-2. Num 15:27 ("he shall offer a female goat a year old for a sin offering") — the bringing of the offering is the formal act of seeking atonement. Num 15:28 ("the priest shall make atonement before the Lord for the person who makes a mistake") — the priestly atonement is the intercession made before God. The seeking operates through institutional means (prescribed offering) rather than through verbal petition, though Lev 5:5's confession ("confesses the sin he has committed") carries a supplication dimension — the verbal acknowledgment before God as part of the seeking process.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=16401, S) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-2]** S — The 18-verse CHAR-2 corpus is entirely from the OT (Hebrew Lev, Num, 1Sa, Exo, Eze). No NT Greek verses appear in the CHAR-2 corpus. The programme has not identified NT verses addressing unintentional sinning in the specific sense defined by CHAR-2. This absence itself is analytically noteworthy: the NT corpus does not carry the *bishgagah* (unintentional qualifier) vocabulary. The NT addresses sin broadly (hamartanō, hamartia) without the OT's systematic distinction between intentional and unintentional sinning through distinct remedial institutions. The relationship across Testaments is one of OT specification (CHAR-2 as a distinct category with a distinct remedy) and NT generalisation (sin addressed through Christ without the Levitical bifurcation).

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=16402, S) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-2]** S — No NT term for unintentional sinning is present in the CHAR-2 corpus, and the programme has not identified a newly coined NT term for this specific characteristic. The silence is consistent with T7.1.8: the NT does not reproduce the OT's systematic intentional/unintentional distinction as a structural feature of its sin vocabulary.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=16403, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The vocabulary arc — cha.ta + bishgagah (inadvertent sinning), shegagah (error), a.von (iniquity borne, Lev 5:1), chat.ta.ah (sin offering, Lev 9:15), cha.ta Piel (to purify, VCG-02) — reveals that CHAR-2's semantic range covers the full sweep from the inadvertent act through the resulting moral condition to the institutional remedy and its application to sacred spaces. The arc is *act → condition → remedy → restoration*, each stage named by distinct vocabulary. The full arc also reveals the characteristic's confinement to the OT Hebrew context: the *bishgagah* qualifier and the Levitical remedial vocabulary are OT-specific, and the NT's absence from this corpus confirms that the characteristic is most precisely defined and evidenced within the Hebrew covenantal system.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=16404, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The primary anchor verse is Lev 4:2: "If anyone sins unintentionally in any of the Lord's commandments about things not to be done, and does any one of them." Cha.ta + bishgagah functions here as the *conditional predicate* — defining the condition under which the entire sin-offering system's individual-person provisions apply. The term governs the entire Lev 4–5 case-law structure: the "if" + the sinning-unintentionally condition opens the legal gate through which the remedial provisions flow. The term is definitional and structural: it establishes the precise inner-quality criterion (inadvertence) that triggers this specific regulatory path rather than the high-handed path (Num 15:30).

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=16405, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The dominant literary form is **casuistic law** (case-law: "if anyone... then...") in Lev 4–6 and Num 15:27/28. Responsible interpretation of casuistic law requires: (1) reading the case as a *legal category* rather than a narrative — the verse establishes a rule for a type-scenario, not a single historical event; (2) recognising that the inner-quality qualifier (*bishgagah*) is legally operative — it functions as the determining condition that routes the case to this remedial track; (3) not reading inner-being content beyond what the legal text specifies. The secondary literary form is **narrative** (Num 22:34 — Balaam's confession; 1Sa 14:33/34 — Saul's response to the people's violation), which requires attention to the narrative's specific moral evaluation of the character's act.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=16406, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Two key argument structures. (1) *Lev 4:2 / Num 15:27/28 — the case-law argument*: premise — a person sins unintentionally (the condition); sub-premise — the sin is real and creates liability regardless of intent; conclusion — a specific prescribed offering must be brought → the priest makes atonement → the person is forgiven. The argument establishes that *liability is act-based, not intent-based* within the covenant framework. (2) *Num 22:34 — the disclosure-confession argument*: premise — Balaam had been proceeding in a way that was morally wrong (against the angel's opposition); sub-premise — he did not know until disclosed; conclusion — upon knowing, he confesses and offers to reverse. The argument establishes that *revelation of the morally relevant fact obligates response*.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=16407, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The primary contextual setting is **liturgical/cultic**: Lev 4–6 and Num 15 address sinning within the context of Israel's covenant worship system — the prescribed offerings, priestly atonement, and sanctuary purification all belong to the cultic-liturgical domain. The secondary setting is **narrative/covenantal**: Num 22:34 (Balaam and the angel — a narrative encounter with a divine messenger) and 1Sa 14:33/34 (Saul's corrective action in a military context with cultic implications). The Ezekiel VCG-02 corpus (Eze 43/45) is set within the **eschatological** sanctuary vision. The cultic-liturgical setting is dominant: CHAR-2 is primarily a characteristic addressed within Israel's formal covenant worship structures.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=16408, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Lev 4:2 functions as the primary anchor: "If anyone sins unintentionally in any of the Lord's commandments about things not to be done, and does any one of them." This verse most fully and directly expresses the essential character of CHAR-2 because: (1) it names the inadvertent quality (*bishgagah*) as the defining criterion; (2) it frames the violation within God's commandments — the relational standard is present; (3) it establishes the case-law gate that the entire Lev 4–5 remedial system follows; (4) its "if anyone" scope is universal — any person in any situation who sins without knowing is addressed.

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=16409, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-2]** E — Lev 4:2 uniquely reveals the *categorical completeness* of the provision: "in any of the Lord's commandments about things not to be done" — the provision covers every commandment, not specific ones. No other verse in the corpus makes this universal-commandment scope explicit. Num 15:27 addresses one person's individual offering; Lev 5:5 addresses the realisation-and-confession moment; Num 22:34 addresses a specific narrative instance. Lev 4:2 establishes the universal categorical principle: *any* inadvertent violation of *any* divine commandment is covered by the remedial provision. This universality is the defining theological claim: God's provision for unintentional sinning is not partial or selective but comprehensively covers the creature's entire epistemic exposure to the commandments.

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=16410, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-2]** E — **Moral philosophy** (specifically the distinction between *mens rea* — the guilty mind — and *actus reus* — the guilty act in legal-moral theory) is the most useful framework. CHAR-2 is precisely the case where *actus reus* is present (the act occurred) but *mens rea* is absent (no deliberate intent). The Levitical framework's treatment of unintentional sinning as real and requiring remedy is a form of *strict liability* — liability attaches to the act regardless of intent. This parallels legal-moral frameworks that hold persons responsible for the foreseeable consequences of their actions even when deliberate wrongdoing was not present. Additionally, **cognitive psychology's** concepts of *bounded rationality* (decision-making within the limits of available information) and *inattentional blindness* (failing to notice morally relevant features of a situation due to attentional limitations) illuminate the perceptual and cognitive dynamics of inadvertent sinning.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=16411, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The strict-liability parallel illuminates Lev 4:2's core principle: the Levitical framework holds that moral liability is act-based, not intent-based, because *the relational reality of God's holiness and the person's covenant standing is objective*, not dependent on the person's subjective awareness. Just as strict-liability legal regimes recognise that some harms matter regardless of intent (because the injured party's reality does not depend on the injurer's knowledge), the Levitical system recognises that God's holiness is affronted by the violation regardless of the violator's awareness. The inattentional-blindness framework illuminates Num 22:34: Balaam's perception was not suppressed — his attentional resources simply did not register the morally relevant fact (the angel) until the angel's sword made it unavoidable.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=16412, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The primary divergence: legal strict-liability frameworks typically exist to protect human victims whose wellbeing is harmed regardless of the injurer's intent. The Levitical framework's strict-liability logic applies primarily in the God-ward direction — the violation affrents God's holiness regardless of the violator's intent. The human science framework does not have a category for moral liability arising from offence to a divine holiness that is objective and relational rather than harm-based. Num 15:28's forgiveness ("he shall be forgiven") has no precise legal-moral parallel: forgiveness as a specifically divine relational restoration is outside what strict-liability legal frameworks provide. The divergence reveals that CHAR-2's theological logic is irreducibly covenantal — grounded in a divine-human relational framework that legal-moral theory can partially illuminate but cannot fully contain.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=16413, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-2]** E — The bounded-rationality framework surfaces the question of *preventability*: moral philosophy asks whether the unintentional actor could have known (i.e., whether the information gap was itself culpable through negligence). The verse evidence does not address the question of whether the person *should have known* — Lev 4:2's case-law provision makes no distinction between unavoidable ignorance and negligent ignorance. This gap in the verse evidence suggests that CHAR-2's legal-moral logic does not grade inadvertent sinning by the degree of preventability — any inadvertent violation, however hard it would have been to prevent, receives the same remedial provision. This uniformity is itself a theological statement: the provision is comprehensive rather than means-tested. No further verse investigation is required — the corpus does not address the preventability question and this silence appears deliberate.

---

T7 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       20/20 ✓ (T7.1.1–T7.1.10, T7.2.1–T7.2.6, T7.3.1–T7.3.4)
  2. Scope marker:       20× [CHAR-2] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings — T7.1.6, T7.1.8, T7.1.9 — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 4:2/5:1/5:5/8:15/9:15; Num 15:27/28; Num 22:34; 1Sa 14:33/34; Exo 29:36; Eze 43:20/22/45:18 — all in §3)
  → PASS. All 8 tiers complete.

---

## CHAR-3 — Confession (189 rows: E=168 S=21 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=16981, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession reveals God's character in two directions. (1) *God as the one to whom confession is rightly directed*: throughout the corpus, genuine confession is oriented toward God as the primary offended party — "I have sinned against the Lord" (1Sa 7:6; Jer 14:20; Dan 9:11; Luk 15:18/21). God's moral authority as the standard against which sin is measured makes him the proper recipient of confession — which reveals him as *holy and morally sovereign*, the reference point of all moral reality. (2) *God as the one who cancels sin upon genuine confession*: Eze 33:16 — "None of the sins that he has committed shall be remembered against him" — reveals God as a God who holds a moral record but is prepared to erase it when the person turns. The characteristic exists in a landscape where confession is heard and responded to by a God who both takes sin seriously and provides for its cancellation.

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=16982, S) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-3]** S — The corpus contains no attribution of confession to God. God does not confess sin because God does not sin. The entire 34-verse corpus is structured around human persons (individual and corporate) confessing before God — with God as the consistent recipient, never the confessor. The absence is constitutively significant: confession is exclusively a creaturely act, presupposing both the capacity to sin (which the creature has and God does not) and the relational standing before a holy God that requires acknowledgment of sin.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=16983, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The structural silence on God confessing (T0.1.2) reveals that confession belongs to the *fallen creature's response to God* rather than to the original divine image. It is not a capacity the creature exercises because it shares it with God, but a capacity the creature requires because it has sinned against God. The corpus does evidence that confession is *divinely designed for* — God hears it (Neh 1:6 — Nehemiah prays "day and night" and God's ear is invoked as "attentive"), provides for it (Eze 33:16 — sins cancelled upon turning), and responds to it (1Sa 7:6 — corporate confession at Mizpah precedes Samuel's judgeship and renewal). The silence on divine confession and the presence of divine response together suggest that confession expresses the image not by sharing a divine quality but by enabling the image-bearer's restoration toward the God in whose image they were made.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=16984, E) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The corpus evidences confession serving three distinct purposes. (1) *Relational restoration*: Luk 15:18/21 — the prodigal's planned and enacted confession is explicitly oriented toward restoring the father-son relationship: "I will arise and go to my father." 1Sa 7:6 — corporate confession at Mizpah marks the people's turning back toward God and precedes Samuel's renewal of the community. (2) *Removal of guilt's weight*: Dan 9:5 — Daniel's multi-layered confession ("we have sinned and done wrong and acted wickedly and rebelled") is a comprehensive naming of what has been done, providing the inner person with clarity about what they carry. 2Sa 24:10 — David's confession "please take away the iniquity of your servant" shows confession as the instrument of guilt-removal. (3) *Alignment of inner reality with moral truth*: Num 21:7 — "we have sinned, for we have spoken against the Lord and against you" — the confession names the inner rebellious attitude as the root of the wrongdoing. Confession equips the person to be a morally honest creature before God and to seek restored relationship.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=16985, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The evidence points clearly to confession as a *response to the fallen condition* — it presupposes sin and exists to address its relational and moral consequences. No verse in the corpus presents confession as a pre-fall capacity or as part of what the unfallen creature was designed to express. The entire 34-verse corpus is structured around sin already committed: Exo 9:27 ("this time I have sinned"), 2Sa 24:10 ("I have sinned greatly in what I have done"), Dan 9:5 ("we have sinned"). The characteristic is responsive rather than original — it is what the image-bearer needs to be equipped for because of the fallen condition, not what they were created to express in an unfallen state.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=16986, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Limited forward-orientation evidence. Eze 33:16 — "None of the sins that he has committed shall be remembered against him. He has done what is just and right; he shall surely live" — points beyond confession to a future state of living in which past sins no longer define the person. The promised future is one in which the record of sin is abolished. Mic 7:9 — "until he pleads my cause and executes judgment for me. He will bring me out to the light; I shall look upon his vindication" — Micah's confession of sin is accompanied by confident eschatological expectation: enduring divine indignation now, looking to vindication ahead. The confessional mode is not the final state but the posture that positions the person toward a promised future of light and vindication.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=16987, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession expresses the divine image through the capacity for *moral self-knowledge and honest self-naming*. The image-bearer who confesses demonstrates: (1) *Moral cognition*: the ability to assess their own act against a moral standard and name it correctly as sin (2Sa 24:10 — "I have sinned greatly"; Dan 9:5 — the full cascade of named failures). (2) *Relational accountability*: the capacity to stand before God and speak truthfully about what was done — "Against you, you only, have I sinned" (Psa 51:4, the paradigmatic confession-verse contextually). (3) *Moral integrity of the will*: the willingness to own what was done rather than deny or displace it (1Ch 21:17 — "It is I who have sinned... do not let the plague be on your people"). The image is expressed not because God confesses but because God's image-bearer is made for moral transparency — honesty about the self before God.

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=16988, S) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-3]** S — Confession is exclusively creaturely (T0.1.2). No divine analogue is evidenced. The characteristic has no shared dimension with the divine nature — it is grounded entirely in the creature's sinfulness and the relational structure of the creature's accountability before God.

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=16989, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The corpus provides evidence at both ends. (1) *Presence of genuine confession*: signals an image functioning with active moral conscience, honest self-knowledge, and relational accountability before God. David's conscience-struck confession (2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him") before any external word of judgment represents the image at near-full functioning in this mode: conscience registers sin, will names it, mouth speaks it to God, plea for mercy follows. Luk 15:21 (the prodigal's enacted confession: "I have sinned against heaven and before you. I am no longer worthy to be called your son") shows total self-knowledge and relational honesty. (2) *Hollow or externally-coerced confession* (VCG-03: Deu 1:41; VCG-01: Exo 9:27/10:16 — Pharaoh; 1Sa 15:30 — Saul): the verbal form is present but the will does not follow. These cases reveal an image that can produce the *form* of confession without the *substance* — the moral self-knowledge is present (they know what they did) but the will has not turned. The image is partially functioning: cognition and speech operate but the volitional transformation is absent.

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=16990, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes, in two directions. (1) *The corporate confessional sequence as typological pattern*: the Judges-Samuel pattern (Judg 10:10/15; 1Sa 7:6; 12:10) — confession leading to divine intervention and leadership renewal — is a recurring pattern that typologically prefigures the deeper restoration in which the new covenant provides a once-for-all resolution of what corporate confession could only address episodically. (2) *Daniel's prayer (Dan 9:5/8/11/15)* functions typologically: a righteous man praying on behalf of a sinful community, including himself in the confession, points forward to the intercessory representative who identifies with the sinners he confesses for. The sustained, comprehensive character of Daniel's confession — multiple specific dimensions named — anticipates the total coverage of sin that the new covenant promises.

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=16991, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The direction is *human instance pointing forward to divine resolution*. The human confessional acts (Judges, Samuel, Daniel) establish the pattern and the need — repeated corporate confession that must be renewed repeatedly reveals the inadequacy of episodic confession to resolve the deep root of sin. The forward pointing is toward a covenant arrangement in which the sin that requires confession is comprehensively addressed rather than repeatedly named. Eze 33:16 ("None of the sins shall be remembered against him") points toward a state of total non-remembrance that the episodic confessional system could not achieve permanently — the type is the human confession; the antitype is the divine forgetting of sin in its totality.

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=16992, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Typological use is evidenced (T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T0 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       12/12 ✓ (T0.1.1–T0.1.3, T0.2.1–T0.2.3, T0.3.1–T0.3.3, T0.4.1–T0.4.3)
  2. Scope marker:       12× [CHAR-3] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: 10/10 E findings cite evidence ✓ (T0.1.2 and T0.3.2 are S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 9:27/10:16; 1Sa 7:6/12:10/15:30; 2Sa 24:10; 1Ch 21:17; Neh 1:6; Dan 9:5/8/11/15; Eze 33:16; Mic 7:9; Luk 15:18/21; Judg 10:10/15; Jer 14:20; Deu 1:41 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T1.


---

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=16993, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Confession." The name signals three things. (1) *Speech-act*: confession is inherently verbal — it is a naming, a speaking, an act that moves from inner to outer through language. (2) *Past-reference*: confession names what has *already been done* — it is retrospective, not prospective. Every instance in the corpus names a committed sin: "this time I have sinned" (Exo 9:27), "I have sinned greatly in what I have done" (2Sa 24:10), "I have sinned by betraying innocent blood" (Mat 27:4). (3) *Relational direction*: confession is always addressed to someone — God (Luk 15:18 — "against heaven and before you"), persons (Exo 10:16 — "against the Lord your God, and against you"), or a public audience (Job 33:27 — "he sings before men"). The name "Confession" thus encodes: a speech-act directed at a relational recipient, naming a past wrong as wrong.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=16994, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The primary Hebrew term is **cha.ta (H2398)** in its perfect-tense form: "cha.ta.ti" — "I have sinned." This is the same root as CHAR-1 and CHAR-2, but in CHAR-3 the characteristic is not the sinning itself but the *naming of the sinning* — the confessional form. The definitive grammatical marker is the first-person perfect (or its corporate equivalent "sinned we/our fathers"): the act is complete, and the confession names it as such. The NT primary term is **hamartanō (G0264)** in the aorist/perfect confession form: Mat 27:4 ("I have sinned"), Luk 15:18/21 ("I have sinned against heaven and before you"). At the definitional level, both terms reveal that confession deploys the sinning-vocabulary in the self-application mode — not describing someone else's sin but naming one's own.

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=16995, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-3]** E — All three implications are present. *Directional*: confession moves from inner (the conscientious recognition) to outer (the spoken word) — 2Sa 24:10 shows the sequence: "David's heart struck him" (inner) → "David said to the Lord" (outer direction, God-ward). *Relational*: every confession is addressed to a party: God (VCG-01 predominant), persons (Exo 10:16 — "against you"), or a public community (VCG-02 corporate confessions). The relational direction is constitutive — confession is not self-addressed monologue but a speech-act directed at a relational other. *Constitutional*: confession engages the conscience (the inner witness), the will (which must agree to name the act as wrong), and the mouth (through which the naming is enacted). The constitutional depth runs from conscience to will to speech.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=16996, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession is a *speech-act that enacts an inner orientation* — it is simultaneously an outer verbal event and an inner moral event. The verbal act (speaking "I have sinned") is not confession without the inner movement of conscience and will that it expresses; the inner movement is not complete confession without the verbal enactment. This dual nature is evidenced in the contrast between genuine and hollow confession: Deu 1:41 (VCG-03) — the words "we have sinned" are spoken, but the will immediately proceeds on its own — showing that the verbal act without inner transformation is definitionally hollow. Conversely, 2Sa 24:10 shows the inner conscience-movement ("heart struck him") as the primary event that the verbal confession ("I have sinned greatly") completes. Confession is thus a *conscience-to-speech event* — an inner recognition externalised through deliberate verbal naming.

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=16997, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The VCG structure reveals three constituent elements at first encounter.
