---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=17051, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. Cognition is engaged through the *knowing* that grounds confession: 2Sa 19:20 — "your servant knows that I have sinned" — explicit cognitive knowledge as the basis. Dan 9:5/11 — the confession names specific theological and historical facts ("All Israel has transgressed your law and turned aside, refusing to obey your voice. And the curse and oath that are written in the Law of Moses... have been poured out upon us, because we have sinned against him") — the confession requires knowing the Law's content, knowing that Israel violated it, knowing the covenantal consequences. Confession in this mode is cognitively dense — it requires knowing what the standard was and knowing how the act deviated from it.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=17052, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession *deepens* cognition: the act of naming sin precisely articulates what the cognitive faculty has understood. Dan 9:5's taxonomy of failure ("sinned... done wrong... acted wickedly... rebelled... turning aside from your commandments") is a cognitive achievement — the confessor has differentiated the multiple dimensions of what went wrong. Confession in this extended mode is simultaneously a cognitive act (understanding the sin more fully) and a communicative act (naming it). Hollow confession (Deu 1:41) shows that cognition can be present without producing the deepening effect — the people knew they had sinned but did not engage cognitively with what that meant for their next action.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=17053, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals that the *precision of confession reflects the depth of cognitive engagement with sin*. Exo 9:27 (Pharaoh — brief, unprecise) represents minimal cognitive engagement; Dan 9:5–15 (extended, multi-dimensional) represents maximal. The characteristic invites and rewards cognitive deepening — the person who confesses more specifically is engaging their mind more fully with the moral reality of what they have done. This confirms that confession is not merely an emotional or volitional act but a cognitive-moral event.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=17054, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. Memory is engaged in confession's retrospective orientation — confession names what was *done*, requiring retrieval of the past act. Neh 1:6 — "confessing the sins... which we have sinned against you. Even I and my father's house have sinned" — requires memory spanning generations. Psa 106:6 — "Both we and our fathers have sinned" — memory of ancestral sin extended to the present generation. Dan 9:11 — the curse written in Moses's Law "have been poured out upon us, because we have sinned against him" — historical memory of the Mosaic covenant's terms. Confession thus requires an active memory faculty that retrieves both the specific past acts and the longer moral history they belong to.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=17055, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession *activates and deepens* the memory faculty by requiring its active deployment. The act of naming what was done (2Sa 24:10 — "what I have done"; 1Ch 21:17 — "I who gave command to number the people") involves retrieving the specific act from memory and holding it before God. Extended confession (Dan 9:5; Neh 1:6) activates corporate and historical memory — the confessor draws on the full moral history of their community to name the pattern within which the specific sin belongs. This extended deployment of memory is itself a formative practice.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=17056, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The memory pattern reveals that confession is a *memory-activating event*: it requires and exercises the capacity to hold the past honestly — not to forget, minimise, or reframe what was done. The corporate confessions (Psa 106:6; Jer 3:25; Lam 5:7 — "our fathers sinned") show that confession can extend memory across generations, naming the multigenerational moral reality honestly. This honest memory is what genuine confession produces and what hollow confession fails to sustain — hollow confession does not hold the past long enough to let it shape the will's future direction.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=17057, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The affective faculty is engaged in confession's shame and sorrow dimensions. Jer 3:25 — "Let us lie down in our shame, and let our dishonor cover us" — shame is the affective state that accompanies and frames the confession: the people are willing to accept shame as the appropriate inner-outer condition for what they have done. Dan 9:8 — "To us, O Lord, belongs open shame, to our kings, to our princes, and to our fathers, because we have sinned against you" — shame is specifically named as the appropriate affective consequence of the named sin. Lam 5:7 — "Our fathers sinned... and we bear their iniquities" — the emotional weight of bearing inherited iniquity carries grief-adjacent affect.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=17058, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Genuine confession *engages and accepts* the appropriate affect — shame (Jer 3:25; Dan 9:8) — without being paralysed by it. The confession of shame is not itself the goal but the honest affective accompaniment of naming sin. Mic 7:9 — bearing the Lord's indignation — shows the confessor accepting the affective weight of God's displeasure as appropriate to the sin named. Hollow confession (Deu 1:41; Exo 9:27) bypasses the affective dimension — the people immediately act without accepting shame; Pharaoh confesses without the affect of contrition.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=17059, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The affective pattern reveals that genuine confession includes an honest affective acceptance of the moral reality named: shame (Jer 3:25; Dan 9:8), indignation borne (Mic 7:9), and the emotional weight of inherited moral failure (Lam 5:7). These affective dimensions are not produced by confession but are part of the complete confessional act — the conscience names the sin, the will agrees, the mouth speaks, and the affect accepts the appropriate moral weight of what was done. Hollow confession distinguishes itself by skipping this affective acceptance.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=17060, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** S — The corpus contains no evidence of creative-faculty engagement in connection with confession. Confession is retrospective and naming-focused, not imaginative or originating.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=17061, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-3]** S — No evidence. The corpus is silent on creativity in connection with confession.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=17062, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** S — The silence on creative-faculty engagement is consistent with confession's retrospective naming structure. This prompt is closed.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=17063, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes — and the volitional faculty's engagement is the distinguishing criterion between genuine and hollow confession. 1Ch 21:17 — "It is I who have sinned and done great evil. But these sheep, what have they done? Please let your hand, O Lord my God, be against me and against my father's house" — the will claims ownership (I who have sinned), distinguishes its agency from the innocent (the sheep), and redirects the consequence onto itself — volitional engagement at full depth. Luk 15:18/21 — the prodigal's confession involves a volitional movement (arising, going) that enacts the confession as an act of the will, not merely a speech. Deu 1:41 (VCG-03) — the will confesses verbally but then moves autonomously — the volitional engagement is partial.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=17064, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Genuine confession *aligns and deepens* volition: the act of naming sin as sin before God commits the will to a moral position — by confessing "I have sinned," the will agrees with the conscience's verdict and moves toward alignment with God's moral standard. 2Sa 24:10 — "please take away the iniquity of your servant" — the confessing will is oriented toward restoration and away from the sinning direction. Hollow confession (Deu 1:41) shows that verbal confession without volitional alignment does not enable the will — the will remains on its own course.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=17065, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The volitional pattern is the key diagnostic of genuine vs hollow confession: the will must agree with and enact what the mouth says. 1Ch 21:17 shows this alignment at full depth — the will claims personal ownership, distinguishes itself from the innocent, and invites divine judgment on itself alone. This is confession as a full volitional act, not merely a verbal formula. The characteristic thus tests the will at its deepest level: will it own what it has done, or will it use the verbal form while directing the will elsewhere?

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=17066, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. Agency is engaged in two ways. (1) *Claiming agency for the sin*: 1Ch 21:17 — "Was it not I who gave command to number the people? It is I who have sinned and done great evil" — the confessor claims their own agency as the source of the sin, refusing to transfer responsibility. 2Sa 24:17 — "Behold, I have sinned, and I have done wickedly. But these sheep, what have they done?" — distinguishing the confessor's agency from the people's non-agency. (2) *Exercising agency in the confession act*: Luk 15:18 — "I will arise and go to my father" — the confession involves a deliberate agentive movement (arising and going) that enacts the inner resolution.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=17067, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Genuine confession *restores and clarifies* agency: by owning the sin as one's own act, the confessor names themselves as the responsible agent, which is the foundation for seeking remedy and making different choices going forward. 2Sa 24:10/17 — David's agency claimed → petition for mercy → the will now oriented toward remedy. Hollow confession (Deu 1:41) shows agency *misdirected*: the people claim agency for their past sin but immediately redirect their agency toward autonomous military action rather than following God's direction.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=17068, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The agency pattern reveals that confession is an *agency-claiming event*: it requires the person to identify themselves as the agent of the sin rather than displacing responsibility. This is why genuine confession is morally demanding — it requires claiming full personal or corporate agency for what was done (1Ch 21:17; 2Sa 24:17; Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned"). The moral demand is proportional to how clearly the agency-claim is owned. Hollow confession (Deu 1:41) shows the failure: the verbal claim of agency for the sin does not produce ownership of agency for the remedy.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=17069, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Moral evaluation is definitionally engaged: confession is the act of evaluating one's own past act as sin. 2Sa 24:10 — "I have sinned greatly in what I have done... I have done very foolishly" — two moral evaluative judgments: (1) the act was great sin; (2) it was foolish — both evaluative verdicts applied to his own conduct. Num 12:11 — "we have done foolishly and have sinned" — Aaron evaluates his act by moral character (foolishness) before naming it as sin. Dan 9:5/8/11 — the extended confession applies multiple evaluative categories ("done wrong," "acted wickedly," "rebelled," "shame belongs to us") — a rich moral evaluation deployed against the confessor's own history.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=17070, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Genuine confession *deepens* moral evaluation: the act of naming sin precisely requires and exercises the moral evaluation faculty. The more specific and layered the confession, the more deeply moral evaluation has operated. Num 12:11 — "done foolishly" adds a characterological dimension to the evaluation; Mat 27:4 — "betrayed innocent blood" adds a moral-victim dimension. These are not mere formulas but precise moral evaluations applied to specific acts. Hollow confession (Deu 1:41) shows moral evaluation present but truncated — the evaluation does not extend to assessing the next proposed action ("we will go up and fight") against the same standard.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=17071, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that confession is a *self-directed moral evaluation event*: the same faculty that evaluates others' acts evaluates one's own. The distinctive feature is the self-application — evaluating *my own* act as sin, not someone else's. This self-directed moral evaluation is what conscience initiates (2Sa 24:10 — "heart struck him") and what the confession expresses. The quality of the confession reflects the quality of the self-directed moral evaluation: precise, multi-dimensional evaluation produces rich confession (Dan 9:5); minimal evaluation produces the bare formula (Exo 9:27).

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=17072, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Conscience is the *primary* faculty engaged in confession — this is the brief's definition: "conscience in confessional mode." 2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him" — the conscience (heart in its moral-witness function) is the initiating faculty. Shimei's confession — "your servant knows that I have sinned" (2Sa 19:20) — is grounded explicitly in the conscience's knowledge. Judas's confession — "I have sinned by betraying innocent blood" (Mat 27:4) — names what the conscience has identified: the specific moral wrong of betraying an innocent person. Confession is conscience externalised through speech.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=17073, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Genuine confession *exercises and deepens* the conscience: speaking what the conscience has witnessed reinforces and completes the inner moral recognition. The conscience that names sin aloud (2Sa 24:10 — struck → spoken) is more fully exercised than one that registers but stays silent. Hollow confession (Deu 1:41) does not deepen the conscience — the words are produced but the conscience's witness does not shape the subsequent will-direction. The confessional exercise of conscience is deepened when the naming is followed by the actions the conscience's verdict requires: plea for mercy (2Sa 24:10), reorientation of will (Luk 15:18/21).

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=17074, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The conscience pattern confirms that CHAR-3 is constitutively conscience-in-action: the characteristic is what the conscience does when it externalises its moral witness through speech. The corpus's contrast between genuine confession (conscience driving speech that reshapes will and action — 2Sa 24:10; Luk 15:21) and hollow confession (conscience producing words without reshaping action — Deu 1:41; Exo 9:27/10:16) reveals that the conscience's full operation includes the volitional follow-through, not just the verbal naming. Confession is thus conscience's complete confessional act, not merely its initiating registration.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=17081, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — God operates toward the human in the CHAR-3 corpus in two modes connected to confession. (1) *God as the one who receives and responds to confession*: Eze 33:16 — "None of the sins that he has committed shall be remembered against him. He has done what is just and right; he shall surely live" — God's response to the person who has turned and implicitly confessed is the active cancellation of the sin-record. This is God's movement toward the confessor. (2) *God as the one whose character is affirmed by confession*: Exo 9:27 — "the Lord is in the right, and I and my people are in the wrong" — Pharaoh's confession names God's righteousness as part of its content; the confession itself is a statement about God's moral character. Confession thus reveals God to be both *the standard who is vindicated* and *the one who receives and cancels* what is confessed.

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=17082, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The basis evidenced for God's response to confession is *the turning of the will toward him*. Eze 33:16 — "He has done what is just and right; he shall surely live" — the context (Eze 33:14–16) shows God's response is conditioned on the person turning from wickedness to justice. Mic 7:9 — "until he pleads my cause and executes judgment for me" — the confessor trusts God to act on the basis of his own covenant faithfulness, not on the basis of the confessor's merit. The basis is not merit but covenantal relationship: God responds to the confession-and-turning because he is the covenant God who has bound himself to hear and respond when his people turn back.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=17083, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — God's disposition toward the confessing person is one of *merciful reception*. Eze 33:16 — sins not remembered when the person has turned — reveals a God who does not hold the confessed sin against the person indefinitely. Mic 7:9 — "He will bring me out to the light; I shall look upon his vindication" — the confessor trusts God's disposition to be ultimately restorative, moving from indignation to vindication. The corporate confessions (Judg 10:10/15; 1Sa 7:6/12:10) show God as the one before whom Israel prostrates in confession and to whom they appeal for deliverance — presupposing a God whose disposition is to receive corporate confession and respond with covenantal intervention. God's disposition is holy-and-merciful: he does not ignore the sin, but he does not permanently hold it against the one who confesses and turns.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=17084, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — God-to-human operation is evidenced (T4.1.1–T4.1.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=17085, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes — this is the dominant directional axis of CHAR-3. The overwhelming majority of confessions in the corpus are explicitly directed toward God: "I have sinned against the Lord" (1Sa 7:6), "we have sinned against you" (Judg 10:10; Dan 9:11), "I have sinned against him" (Mic 7:9), "Father, I have sinned against heaven and before you" (Luk 15:21). Confession is the human person's movement toward God after the movement away from God that sin constituted. Neh 1:6 — praying "day and night" with confession as the primary posture — shows sustained human movement toward God through the confessional posture. The direction is unambiguous: confession is the person moving back toward the God they sinned against.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=17086, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The inner posture required for the human-toward-God confessional movement is *honesty without self-protection*. 1Ch 21:17 — "It is I who have sinned and done great evil. But these sheep, what have they done?" — the posture is complete ownership of the sin without deflection, combined with concern for the innocent. Luk 15:21 — "I am no longer worthy to be called your son" — the posture includes honest self-assessment of unworthiness without using that assessment as a bargaining position (the prodigal does not demand a position; he simply names the truth). Contrast: 1Sa 15:30 (Saul) — "I have sinned; yet honor me now before the elders" — the inner posture is compromised by self-protective concern. The required posture is honest self-naming without attached conditions.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=17087, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The God-ward direction of confession reveals that the relationship with God is the *primary relational frame* within which sin is understood and addressed. Psa 51:4 (contextually adjacent to this corpus — the paradigmatic confession verse) and Dan 9:11 ("we have sinned against him") both name God as the primary party against whom sin was committed. The human-to-God movement of confession reveals that the relationship is not destroyed by sin — the sinner still has access to God through confession (Neh 1:6 — "let your ear be attentive... to hear the prayer of your servant"). The relationship is damaged but not closed: confession is the act that re-opens the closed space between the sinner and God by naming what closed it.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=17088, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Human-to-God operation is the dominant direction of the corpus (VCG-01 and VCG-02 both primarily). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=17089, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. Person-to-person confessions appear in several instances. (1) Exo 10:16 — Pharaoh confesses "against the Lord your God, and against you" — Moses as a human recipient alongside God. (2) 2Sa 19:20 — Shimei confesses "I have sinned" to the returning King David — a direct interpersonal confession. (3) Mat 27:4 — Judas confesses "I have sinned by betraying innocent blood" to the chief priests — a person-directed confession. (4) 1Sa 26:21 — Saul confesses "I have sinned" in the context of David's mercy — a relational confession between king and subject. The person-to-person direction is present but secondary to the God-directed primary axis.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=17090, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The enabling conditions for interpersonal confession parallel those for God-directed confession: (1) *Active conscience*: 2Sa 19:20 — "your servant knows that I have sinned" — the interpersonal confession is grounded in inner knowledge. (2) *Absence of self-protective calculation*: the person-directed confessions that are most genuine (2Sa 19:20 — Shimei, who comes first) show the confessor approaching without knowing the outcome — a posture of moral honesty. The compromised forms (Mat 27:4 — Judas, who immediately acts on the priests' indifference by returning the silver and hanging himself) show conscience driving the confession but the relational outcome being rejected.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=17091, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The person must first have had the conscience activated — the inner recognition must precede the interpersonal naming. 2Sa 19:20 — Shimei comes "the first" because his conscience is already active about his past wrong; the interpersonal confession flows from the prior inner recognition. The person must also have accepted that they have sinned — not merely that they might have. The equivocation present in Saul's confession (1Sa 15:30 — "I have sinned; yet honor me") shows that incomplete inner acceptance of the sin-verdict produces compromised interpersonal confession.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=17092, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — The interpersonal giving direction is evidenced (T4.3.1–T4.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=17093, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. The reception of confession is evidenced in the human relational instances. Mat 27:4 — the chief priests receive Judas's confession: "I have sinned by betraying innocent blood" — their response: "What is that to us? See to it yourself." This is the paradigm of rejected reception: the confession is heard but the recipient disengages. 1Sa 26:21 — Saul's confession received by David in the field — David's response confirms he received it: "Return, my son David" (Saul's words) implies a relational response to the confession. 2Sa 19:20 — Shimei's confession received by King David — the outcome is that David's response is affected by the confession (the king grants mercy).

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=17094, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Enabling reception: the recipient who receives confession with mercy (David receiving Shimei — 2Sa 19:20; the father receiving the prodigal — Luk 15:20/21) is characterised by a disposition of mercy and an orientation toward relational restoration. Blocking reception: Mat 27:4 — the chief priests are blocked from receiving Judas's confession by their own moral complicity in the betrayal ("What is that to us?") — they cannot receive what would implicate them. The blocking condition is the recipient's investment in the sinning act that is being confessed — they cannot acknowledge the sin without acknowledging their own role in it.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=17095, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Mat 27:4 provides the clearest evidence: the chief priests who dismiss Judas's confession respond with moral deflection ("What is that to us? See to it yourself") — their inner-being state is one of refusal to engage with the moral reality the confession names. They neither receive it (which would require acknowledging the injustice they commissioned) nor respond with mercy. The dismissal reveals an inner state of self-protective moral closure — the conscience that would be implicated by receiving the confession is closed against it. The non-receiving party's inner state is the inverse of the confessor's: where the confessor has opened to moral honesty, the non-receiver has closed against it.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=17096, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced (T4.4.1–T4.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=17097, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. Within existing bonds: the individual confessions to God (VCG-01) operate within the covenant bond — the confessor is already in a relationship with God that confession addresses and repairs. Shimei's confession (2Sa 19:20) operates within the subject-king bond. The prodigal's confession (Luk 15:21) operates within the father-son bond. These within-bond confessions presuppose a relationship that sin has damaged but not destroyed. Across relational distance: Exo 9:27/10:16 — Pharaoh, who has no covenant bond with the Lord, confesses using the covenant language ("the Lord is in the right") — a confession that operates across relational distance. Judas (Mat 27:4) confesses to the priests with whom he was in a transactional (not covenant) relationship.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=17098, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession crosses covenantal boundaries. Pharaoh's confessions (Exo 9:27/10:16) are confessions by a non-covenant party to the covenant Lord — showing that the confession-form operates across the boundary, even without the covenant relationship that would make it fully operative. Judas (Mat 27:4) confesses to the chief priests — a transactional rather than covenantal relationship. However, the corpus's most substantial and productive confessions are covenantal: the VCG-02 corporate confessions (Judg 10:10; 1Sa 7:6; Dan 9:5) all operate within Israel's covenant framework and produce covenantal renewal. The form crosses the boundary; the full productive effect is covenantal.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=17099, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The relational scope covers: (1) *Individual-to-God* (VCG-01 primary — 2Sa 24:10; Mic 7:9; Luk 15:21); (2) *Corporate-to-God* (VCG-02 — Judg 10:10; 1Sa 7:6; Dan 9:5; Psa 106:6); (3) *Individual-to-person* (Exo 10:16; 2Sa 19:20; Mat 27:4); (4) *Representative individual confessing for a community* (Neh 1:6; Dan 9:5 — Daniel including himself in the community's confession). The relational scope is comprehensive and matches the scope of sin: wherever sin creates a relational breach, confession is the appropriate naming of that breach. The dominant scope is vertical (God-directed); the secondary scope is horizontal (person-directed).

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=17100, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Relational scope is fully evidenced (T4.5.1–T4.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=17101, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-3]** S — The CHAR-3 corpus contains no direct evidence of confession operating in relation to angelic or adversarial spiritual beings. All confessions in the corpus are directed toward God, human persons, or community.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=17102, S) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-3]** S — No evidence in the corpus of adversarial spiritual activity targeting confession. The corpus is silent on this dimension.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=17103, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-3]** S — No evidence. The corpus is silent on angelic mediation of confession.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=17104, S) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** S — The evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface for CHAR-3. All relational operation in the corpus is human-to-God or human-to-human. Noted explicitly.

---

T4 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T4.1.1–T4.1.4, T4.2.1–T4.2.4, T4.3.1–T4.3.4, T4.4.1–T4.4.4, T4.5.1–T4.5.4, T4.6.1–T4.6.4)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-3] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings — T4.6.1–T4.6.4 — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 9:27/10:16; Judg 10:10; 1Sa 7:6/12:10/15:30/26:21; 2Sa 19:20/24:10; 1Ch 21:17; Neh 1:6; Psa 106:6; Eze 33:16; Dan 9:5/11; Mic 7:9; Mat 27:4; Luk 15:21 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=17105, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Genuine confession produces transformation in both condition and orientation. (1) *Condition change*: Eze 33:16 — "None of the sins that he has committed shall be remembered against him" — the condition of standing before God changes when the person turns and confession is made; the sin-record is cancelled. Genuine confession positions the person to receive forgiveness (CHAR-14), which is the condition-change event. (2) *Orientation change*: Luk 15:18–21 — the prodigal's planned and enacted confession reorients his entire trajectory: from the far country toward the father's house. The orientation change is the more visible transformation in the corpus — confession produces a reorientation of the will toward God and away from the sinning direction. Dan 9:5's comprehensive confession reorients Daniel's intercession from acknowledging failure to expecting divine action (Dan 9:17/18/19 — plea for God to hear, forgive, act).

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=17106, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The condition-transformation (sin-record cancelled — Eze 33:16) is presented as effective and complete when genuine. The orientation-transformation is evidenced as sustainable when the confession is genuine (Luk 15:21 — the prodigal's confession initiates a restored relationship that the narrative does not reverse) and unsustained when hollow (Exo 9:27/10:16 — Pharaoh's confessions are reversed when the plagues cease: the orientation returns to hardness). The reversibility thus tracks the genuineness of the confession: genuine confession produces orientation-change that is sustained; hollow confession produces apparent orientation-change that reverts under pressure.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=17107, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Transformation evidence is present (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=17108, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. The corpus evidences a clear sequence across the paradigm cases. (1) *Pre-confession state*: sin committed, conscience not yet or just beginning to engage. 2Sa 24:10 — David has numbered the people; the heart-striking occurs afterward. Luk 15:17 (context) — the prodigal "came to himself" — the moment of conscience-engagement that precedes the inner resolution. (2) *The confessional moment*: the conscience-to-speech movement — heart struck → "David said to the Lord: I have sinned greatly" (2Sa 24:10); "I will arise and go to my father" → "Father, I have sinned" (Luk 15:18/21). (3) *Post-confession state*: plea for mercy (2Sa 24:10 — "please take away the iniquity"), expectation of divine action (Mic 7:9 — "until he pleads my cause"), or relational restoration (Luk 15:22–24 — the father's reception).

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=17109, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The sequence (conscience-activated → confession-made → mercy-expected/received) reveals that confession is a *pivot-point event*: it is the hinge between the state of unacknowledged sin (before) and the state of acknowledged sin on its way to resolution (after). The confession does not itself resolve the sin (forgiveness does that — CHAR-14) but it is the event that makes resolution possible. The sequence also reveals that the confession-to-plea movement is nearly always present in genuine confession: 2Sa 24:10 ("please take away the iniquity"), Num 21:7 ("pray to the Lord that he take away the serpents"), Dan 9:17/18/19 (plea for God to hear, forgive, and act). Confession without plea is hollow (Deu 1:41 — "we have sinned" immediately followed by autonomous action, no plea to God).

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=17110, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Sequence is clearly evidenced (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=17111, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession produces change through two primary mechanisms. (1) *Naming-as-moral-positioning*: the act of naming sin as sin positions the confessor on the correct side of the moral reality — they now stand with God's verdict rather than against it. Exo 9:27 — "the Lord is in the right, and I and my people are in the wrong" — the confession explicitly positions God and self correctly relative to each other. This positioning is the mechanism by which confession changes the person's relational standing. (2) *Opening-for-divine-response*: confession creates the condition under which God's forgiveness can operate. Eze 33:16 — the person who has turned and (implicitly) confessed receives the divine response of non-remembrance. The confession opens the relational space that sin had closed, allowing God's mercy to move through it.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=17112, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. (1) *Individual confession* (VCG-01) — mechanism is primarily conscience-to-speech: the individual's inner moral recognition externalised, creating the condition for personal forgiveness and restored standing. (2) *Corporate confession* (VCG-02) — mechanism is communal acknowledgment producing communal realignment: Judg 10:10/15 — corporate confession at Mizpah followed by corporate deliverance; the mechanism is community-level moral positioning. (3) *Hollow confession* (VCG-03) — the naming mechanism operates at the verbal level only, producing no condition-change because the inner positioning (will's alignment) is absent. The mechanism that produces genuine change requires both naming and inner alignment; the hollow form has the naming without the alignment.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=17113, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Mechanism is evidenced (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=17114, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes — the corpus shows confession operating as a *response to suffering* across multiple instances. Num 21:7 — "the people came to Moses and said, 'We have sinned... pray to the Lord that he take away the serpents'" — serpent judgment is the affliction; confession is the response. Judg 10:10/15 — "the people of Israel cried out to the Lord, saying, 'We have sinned against you'" — enemy oppression is the affliction; confession is the response. 1Sa 7:6/12:10 — Philistine oppression drives the corporate confession. Dan 9:5/11 — exile and the curse of Moses's law are the affliction context; Daniel's sustained confession is the response. Lam 5:7 — "our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities" — the suffering of bearing inherited iniquity is the affliction that names the sin's origin.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=17115, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The corpus shows suffering primarily *producing and revealing* confession. Suffering produced confession: Num 21:7 — serpent judgment → confession; Judg 10:10/15 — enemy oppression → confession; Exo 9:27/10:16 — plague → Pharaoh's confession. Suffering also *tests* the quality of the confession: where the confession is genuine, the suffering moves the will toward God (Mic 7:9 — "I will bear the indignation of the Lord because I have sinned against him"); where hollow, the suffering lifts and the confession evaporates (Pharaoh — Exo 9:27/10:16 reversed when plagues cease). The contrast between these reveals that suffering reveals whether the confession is genuine: genuine confession holds through suffering; hollow confession dissolves when the affliction that produced it ends.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=17116, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — The relationship between confession and suffering is extensively evidenced (T5.4.1 and T5.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=17117, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. The corpus evidences confession's formative role in two modes. (1) *Repeated practice forming moral transparency*: Neh 1:6 — "confessing the sins... day and night" — sustained confessional practice forms the person in the posture of ongoing moral openness before God. This is not a single crisis event but a sustained formative discipline. (2) *Intercession-confessing as formation in solidarity*: Daniel (Dan 9:5) confesses on behalf of his people, including himself — "we have sinned." This representative confession forms the intercessory character: the person who habitually confesses on behalf of others develops the inner posture of identification with those they intercede for.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=17118, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession plays the role of *regular moral accountability maintenance* in the formation arc. Without it, sin accumulates unacknowledged and the person's moral transparency before God erodes. With it, the person's self-knowledge is regularly corrected and updated — the conscience's witness is regularly enacted rather than suppressed. Job 33:27 — the restored man who publicly confesses "I sinned and perverted what was right, and it was not repaid to me" — models how confession becomes part of a person's testimony and formative self-understanding: they carry the memory of their sin and God's mercy, and that memory shapes their ongoing moral posture.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=17119, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Formation evidence is present (T5.5.1 and T5.5.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=17120, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Limited but present eschatological evidence. Mic 7:9 — "I will bear the indignation of the Lord because I have sinned against him, *until* he pleads my cause and executes judgment for me. He will bring me out to the light; I shall look upon his vindication" — confession is here positioned as a present posture ("I will bear") oriented toward a future vindication ("he will bring me out to the light"). The confession does not end the moral episode; it positions the confessor toward a future divine resolution. Eze 33:16 — "he shall surely live" — points toward a future of life beyond the sin that required confession. The eschatological frame is implicit: confession is the present-tense posture that positions the person toward God's future vindication and life.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=17121, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The present experience of confession (naming sin, accepting indignation, trusting in God's response — Mic 7:9) anticipates a future in which the naming is completed by the divine verdict of vindication. The confession names what the person did; the future fullness is God naming what he will do — "He will bring me out to the light." The present confession is oriented toward a final state in which the sin named is not merely cancelled but superseded by the light of God's vindication. Job 33:27 — "I sinned... and it was not repaid to me" — the restored man's testimony anticipates a state in which the full justice of God's mercy (not repaying the sin) is publicly evident.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=17122, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Limited eschatological evidence is present (Mic 7:9; Eze 33:16; Job 33:27). The corpus is primarily present-tense in its operation but the forward-oriented elements are noted.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=17123, S) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-3]** S — T2.8 found no constitutional deposit evidence for confession. This prompt is closed.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=17124, S) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-3]** S — T2.8 found no deposit. Lam 5:7 ("our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities") evidences generational consequence of sin, not of confession. No generational consequence from confession-as-deposit is evidenced. This prompt is closed.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=17125, S) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-3]** S — T2.8 found no deposit. T5.7 is closed: no deposit, no developmental or generational consequence from this corpus.

---

T5 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       21/21 ✓ (T5.1.1–T5.1.3, T5.2.1–T5.2.3, T5.3.1–T5.3.3, T5.4.1–T5.4.3, T5.5.1–T5.5.3, T5.6.1–T5.6.3, T5.7.1–T5.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       21× [CHAR-3] ✓

  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 9:27/10:16; Num 21:7; Deu 1:41; Judg 10:10/15; 1Sa 7:6/12:10; 2Sa 24:10; Dan 9:5/11/17; Eze 33:16; Mic 7:9; Neh 1:6; Lam 5:7; Job 33:27; Luk 15:18/21 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.


---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=17126, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Three adjacent characteristics appear alongside confession consistently. (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)* — as the prior act that confession names: every individual confession in VCG-01 names a specific committed sin (2Sa 24:10 — the census; Mat 27:4 — the betrayal; Luk 15:21 — the far-country life). Confession follows sinning as its moral completion. (2) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)* — as the inner condition that precedes and grounds confession: 2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him" names the guilt-state that drives the confession; 2Sa 19:20 — "your servant knows that I have sinned" names inner knowledge-of-guilt as the basis. (3) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received)* — as the act that confession opens the door to: Eze 33:16 (sins not remembered after turning), Num 15:28 (adjacent corpus — forgiveness following the atonement the confession drives), 2Sa 24:10 — "please take away the iniquity of your servant" — the plea for forgiveness follows immediately.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=17127, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The co-occurrence pattern establishes confession as the *central hinge* of the sinning-to-forgiveness arc: it receives CHAR-1 (the sin committed) and CHAR-17 (the guilt-state experienced) as its inputs, and it produces the opening for CHAR-14 (forgiveness) as its output. Confession sits between moral failure and moral restoration — it is neither the sin (CHAR-1) nor the forgiveness (CHAR-14) but the named acknowledgment that connects the two. Its place in the inner-being landscape is as the *pivot characteristic*: the moral episode cannot move from failure to restoration without passing through the naming event that confession is.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=17128, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are clearly evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=17129, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession consistently *follows* CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning) and *precedes* CHAR-14 (Forgiveness). The sequence: sin committed (CHAR-1) → guilt recognised (CHAR-17) → confession made (CHAR-3) → forgiveness sought and received (CHAR-14). 2Sa 24:10 — "I have sinned greatly in what I have done. But now, O Lord, please take away the iniquity" — the confession immediately precedes the plea for forgiveness. Dan 9:5–19 — the confession (9:5–14) precedes the plea for God to act (9:15–19). Num 21:7 — "we have sinned... pray to the Lord that he take away the serpents" — confession precedes intercession request. The sequence is invariant in genuine confession.

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=17130, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The relationship is *causal* in both directions: sin (CHAR-1) causes the need for confession (CHAR-3); confession (CHAR-3) causes the condition in which forgiveness (CHAR-14) becomes possible. Sin creates the moral fact; confession names it; forgiveness resolves it. The sequence is also *developmental* in the Judges pattern (VCG-02): Judg 10:10/15; 1Sa 7:6; 12:10 show confession as part of a recurring cycle (sin → oppression → confession → deliverance → apostasy → repeat). Each iteration of the cycle is causal (confession causes deliverance); the pattern over time is developmental (the community is shaped by repeated cycles of confession and restoration).

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=17131, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Sequential patterns are clearly evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=17132, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession produces two adjacent characteristics. (1) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received)*: Eze 33:16 — turning and implicit confession → sins not remembered (forgiveness). 2Sa 24:10 — confession → "please take away the iniquity" (forgiveness sought). Genuine confession opens the door to forgiveness; hollow confession does not (Deu 1:41 — no forgiveness sequence follows). (2) *Intercession*: Num 21:7 — "we have sinned... pray to the Lord that he take away the serpents" — the corporate confession produces the appeal for intercessory prayer; 2Sa 24:17 — David's confession produces his intercession for the people. Confession generates both self-directed plea (for forgiveness) and other-directed intercession.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=17133, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession is produced by: (1) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: the guilt-state experienced after sin is the inner condition that drives confession. 2Sa 24:10 — the heart-struck guilt produces the verbal confession. (2) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: the sin committed is the moral fact that confession names — the sin precedes and produces the need for confession. (3) *External judgment-pressure*: Num 21:7 (serpents), Judg 10:10 (enemy oppression), Exo 9:27 (plague) — suffering produces confession when it activates conscience. Whether the production is genuine (conscience engaged by judgment) or hollow (pressure-only, conscience not engaged) distinguishes the quality of what is produced.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=17134, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Confession is a constituent element of the larger *repentance arc* in Scripture — repentance (the turning of the will) typically includes confession as its verbal component: the turn is named as the person turns (Num 21:7; Judg 10:10/15; Luk 15:21). Confession without the turning produces VCG-03's hollow form; the turn without the verbal naming remains an interior movement not yet completed as confession. The two together constitute repentance. Confession itself has CHAR-17 (guilt) and CHAR-1 (sinning) as constituent prerequisites — without prior sin and guilt there is nothing to confess.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=17135, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are extensively evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=17136, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. The primary vocabulary (cha.ta H2398 in its "I have sinned" confessional form; hamartanō G0264) is shared with CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning) and CHAR-2 (Unintentional sinning). The same root produces both the sinning-event and its confessional naming — this is expected since confession names the sinning. The confessional formula "chatati" ("I have sinned") also appears in the guilt-state corpus (CHAR-17) at the moment of realisation. The shared vocabulary across CHAR-1, CHAR-2, CHAR-3, and CHAR-17 reflects the single theological domain they occupy: the field of sinning, its inner-being consequences, and its naming.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=17137, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Yes. The H2398 (cha.ta) root generates the full vocabulary field: cha.ta (the act — CHAR-1/CHAR-2), chatati/chatanu (the confessional form — CHAR-3), chat.ta (the sinner-type — CHAR-16), chat.ta.ah/chat.tat (the sin as record or offering — CHAR-13/CHAR-14). CHAR-3 uses the root in its self-application confessional mode — "I have sinned" — which is grammatically the perfect-tense first-person form of the same root that CHAR-1 uses for the commission-act. The root-level architecture is the same as CHAR-1 and CHAR-2; the grammatical and contextual configuration (confessional mode, first-person, self-applied) is what distinguishes CHAR-3's usage.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=17138, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The vocabulary sharing reveals that sinning (CHAR-1/2), its naming as confession (CHAR-3), and the guilt-state (CHAR-17) are not conceptually independent phenomena but *facets of a single moral episode viewed from different analytical positions*. The same root appears in all four because Scripture treats the sinning, the guilt, the naming, and the record as belonging to a unified moral reality. CHAR-3 is distinguished not by different vocabulary but by the grammatical and relational configuration: the first-person, self-applied, past-tense naming. The characteristic is the same moral domain, viewed from the confessor's speech-act position.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=17139, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=17140, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-3]** E — CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state) most closely resembles CHAR-3 — both engage the conscience retrospectively regarding sin already committed. The precise distinction: CHAR-17 is the *inner state* (guilt, conviction, the heart struck — 2Sa 24:10's "David's heart struck him" is CHAR-17); CHAR-3 is the *externalised speech-act* that follows ("And David said to the Lord: I have sinned greatly" — CHAR-3). Guilt (CHAR-17) is the pre-confessional inner condition; confession (CHAR-3) is its verbal externalisation. A person can experience guilt without confessing (CHAR-4 — Conscience suppression would intercept there); confession requires the guilt-state as its precondition but is not identical to it.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=17141, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The overlap is most visible in 2Sa 24:10 — the verse contains both the guilt-state ("David's heart struck him" — CHAR-17) and the confession ("David said to the Lord: I have sinned greatly" — CHAR-3) in a single verse. The boundary is the speech-act threshold: inner recognition = CHAR-17; the verbal externalisation of that recognition to a relational recipient = CHAR-3. The single verse straddles both characteristics because in David's case the inner and outer movements are nearly simultaneous. In VCG-03 (Deu 1:41) the outer speech (CHAR-3 form) occurs without the inner reorientation completing — showing the two can diverge.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=17142, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The distinction between CHAR-3 (Confession) and CHAR-17 (Guilt) is one of *kind* — not more or less of the same thing, but two different events: an inner state (guilt) and an outer speech-act (confession). They are related causally (guilt produces confession) but are qualitatively different events. The distinction between genuine confession (VCG-01/02) and hollow confession (VCG-03) is one of *degree and constitutional depth*: both produce the verbal form; genuine confession additionally produces the volitional reorientation.

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=17143, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=17144, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-3]** E — Several verses serve as anchors in adjacent characteristics. 2Sa 24:10 — the paradigm case of conscience-initiated confession — is also a primary anchor for CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state) because "David's heart struck him" is the conscience's retrospective guilt-engagement. Dan 9:5 — the extended multi-dimensional confession — is likely a primary anchor for both CHAR-3 and for any intercessory-prayer characteristic. Eze 33:16 — "None of the sins shall be remembered against him" — is a candidate anchor for CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received), evidencing God's response to turning and implied confession. Luk 15:21 — the prodigal's enacted confession — is likely the NT anchor for both CHAR-3 and CHAR-14.

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=17145, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-3]** E — 2Sa 24:10 as shared anchor between CHAR-3 and CHAR-17 reveals what was noted in T6.5.2: the two characteristics are contiguous events within a single moral episode, and their shared anchor confirms that they cannot be fully understood separately. The verse holds both simultaneously — inner (guilt) and outer (confession) — because in Scripture's portrayal of genuine moral response these two are nearly inseparable. The shared anchor is not a classification problem but a theological reality: genuine confession is guilt's completion.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=17146, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Shared verse anchors are identified (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=17147, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The primary dimension (conscience in confessional mode) is shared with CHAR-17 (Guilt — conscience in its post-act awareness mode) and CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression — conscience's engagement refused). The secondary dimension (will — which must agree to name the sin) is shared with CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning — will as primary faculty) and CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent — will refusing to turn). The speech dimension is unique to CHAR-3 within M10's characteristics — no other M10 characteristic is constitutively speech-based. The relational dimension (directed toward God or persons) is shared broadly across M10 characteristics.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=17148, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-3]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms that CHAR-3 occupies a *convergence point* in the M10 characteristic landscape: it shares the conscience dimension with CHAR-17 (its input) and CHAR-4 (its failure alternative), the will dimension with CHAR-1 (its precondition) and CHAR-5 (its alternative trajectory), and is uniquely characterised by the speech dimension that none of the others carry. CHAR-3 is thus structurally the characteristic where conscience and will converge in verbal expression — it is the characteristic most fully engaged with all three inner faculties (conscience, will, speech) simultaneously, which explains its central hinge position in the inner-being landscape.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=17149, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-3]** E — Dimensional sharing evidence is available from the corpus analysis (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---
