---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=17429, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The cognitive faculty is centrally engaged in Heb 10:26 — "sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth" — the person has the relevant knowledge cognitively (the truth has been received) and the will acts against it. The cognitive faculty holds the truth but the will refuses to act in accordance with it. 1Ki 16:31 — the minimisation of Jeroboam's sins ("a light thing") is a cognitive assessment. The cognitive faculty is engaged in both the knowing-the-truth mode (Heb 10:26) and the false-evaluation mode (1Ki 16:31).

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=17430, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent *bypasses* cognition in the Heb 10:26 mode: the knowledge is present but the will acts against it. In the minimisation mode (1Ki 16:31), it *distorts* cognition by producing a false moral evaluation that enables continued refusal. Over time, sustained refusal may impair cognition by entrenching the false evaluation (minimisation) as the habitual cognitive framework, making accurate moral assessment progressively harder.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=17431, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals a critical distinction within CHAR-5: the Heb 10:26 mode shows the will acting against correct knowledge (knowledge present, will bypasses it), while the 1Ki 16:31 mode shows the will enabled by distorted knowledge (knowledge corrupted by minimisation). In both cases the will refuses, but the cognitive configuration differs: in one, the problem is purely volitional (know but don't turn); in the other, the problem combines volitional and cognitive failure (don't turn partly because know wrongly).

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=17432, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Memory is implicitly engaged through the inherited-pattern dimension. 2Ki 15:9 — "as his fathers had done" — the son who follows his fathers in not departing from Jeroboam's sins carries the memory of his fathers' pattern as the inherited moral framework. The memory of "this is how things have been done" provides the cognitive-memorial basis for the inherited refusal. Neh 9:29 — the corporate prayer recites the history of Israel's stubborn refusal over generations — the memory faculty holds this history as the community's self-understanding.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=17433, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent *selectively activates* the memory of inherited patterns (the fathers' example — 2Ki 15:9) while potentially suppressing or not accessing the memory of what the sinful course has cost. The person who "did not depart from the sins of Jeroboam" holds the memory of that pattern as the natural path while not engaging the memory of the consequences (loss of standing, Eze 18:24; foreclosure of remedy, Heb 10:26). Memory is engaged in the reinforcement direction (reinforcing the inherited pattern) but not in the corrective direction (recalling the costs of the sinful course).

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=17434, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The memory pattern reveals that refusal to repent is sustained partly by *selective memory*: the memory of the inherited sinful pattern is operative (providing precedent and norm), while the memory of its moral gravity and consequences is not operative. Neh 9:29's communal prayer recites the history precisely to break this selective memory — by naming what the community has repeatedly done despite warnings, the prayer aims to make the suppressed moral-memory accessible. The corrective of refusal to repent requires making the memory of the sinful course's costs as vivid as the memory of the course itself.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=17435, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The affective faculty is engaged in the dulling mode. 1Ki 16:31 — "as if it had been a light thing" — the affective register of moral gravity is dulled: Jeroboam's sins, which should produce moral revulsion or at least serious concern, produce only a light response. 2Ki 3:3's "clung to" (*davaq*) carries an attachment dimension — the will has an affective bond with the sinful course, making departure emotionally as well as volitionally difficult. 1Ti 5:20 — "so that the rest may stand in fear" — the public rebuke is aimed at producing the affect of fear in the community, implying that appropriate moral fear is what refusal to repent lacks.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=17436, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent *distorts* the affective faculty: it dulls appropriate negative affect (fear of God, revulsion at sin — 1Ki 16:31 — the light-thing assessment shows dulled moral affect) and may generate inappropriate affective attachment to the sinful course (2Ki 3:3 — "clung to"). The dulling of appropriate moral affect enables the will to continue; the affective attachment to the sinful course makes departure emotionally costly. Both distortions serve the refusal.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=17437, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The affective pattern reveals that refusal to repent is *affectively maintained* as well as volitionally chosen: the dulled moral affect (1Ki 16:31) removes the motivating force of moral revulsion; the affective attachment (2Ki 3:3 — "clung to") adds a positive pull toward the sinful course. Together these affective conditions make turning away emotionally difficult: there is insufficient negative affect to motivate departure and positive affective attachment binding the person to the sinful course. The appropriate corrective affects are fear of God (1Ti 5:20) and revulsion at sin — restoring these is part of what makes turning possible.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=17438, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** S — The corpus contains no evidence of creative-faculty engagement in connection with refusal to repent. The characteristic operates through will, heart, and inherited pattern rather than through imagination or creative origination.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=17439, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-5]** S — No evidence. The corpus is silent on creativity in connection with refusal to repent.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=17440, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** S — The silence is consistent with the characteristic's nature as a volitional-dispositional phenomenon. This prompt is closed.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=17441, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The volitional faculty is the *primary* faculty engaged — refusal to repent is constitutively a will-act. The entire corpus is volitional language: "did not depart," "did not turn," "would not obey" (Neh 9:29), "sinning deliberately" (Heb 10:26 — *hekousiōs* = willingly). The will is the faculty that has the capacity to turn and is refusing to exercise it in the direction of turning. The characteristic is impossible without an operative will — the will must be functioning for there to be a refusal; an absent or non-functioning will would be CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression) territory.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=17442, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent *misdirects* volition while keeping it fully operative. The will remains fully functioning — it is making choices, clinging to the sinful course, resisting correction (Neh 9:29 — "would not obey" is an act of will). But the direction of volitional operation is away from God and away from the available turn. Over time, sustained refusal *constrains* volition: Heb 10:26 — "there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" — the persistent-refusal will progressively forecloses the options available to it (the sacrifice is closed off). The will is not impaired in its operational capacity but progressively constrained in its available options through sustained refusal.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=17443, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The volitional pattern reveals that refusal to repent is a *sustained volitional commitment to non-departure*: each moment of "did not depart from" is itself a volitional act (or sustained non-act) that constitutes the characteristic. The cumulative effect of these sustained volitional non-departures is: the will becomes increasingly bound to the sinful course (2Ki 3:3 — "clung to"), the volitional options progressively narrow (Heb 10:26), and the will's default orientation is shaped toward continued refusal. Refusal to repent is thus a self-propagating volitional condition: each act of volitional non-departure makes the next one more likely.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=17444, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Agency is fully engaged throughout. The Kings corpus shows kings who continue to act — governing, walking, serving — in the direction of the sinful course: 1Ki 16:31 — Ahab "went and served Baal and worshiped him" — his agency is fully operational, directed toward idolatry. The sinful course does not merely persist by default; agents are actively walking in it (2Ki 13:6 — "walked in them"). Heb 10:26 — "go on sinning deliberately" — the present tense going-on names continuous agentive action.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=17445, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent *misdirects* agency without impairing its operational capacity. The person who refuses to repent remains a fully capable agent — they act, govern, worship, sin — but all their agentive outputs are directed within the sinful course rather than away from it. Over time, the accumulated agentive outputs of the sinful course (the actions performed while not departing) constitute the person's life-trajectory, making departure increasingly costly: 2Ki 15:18 — "all his days" — an entire lifetime's agency invested in the sinful course.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=17446, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The agency pattern reveals that refusal to repent is an *agency-investment process*: each agentive act performed within the sinful course (serving Baal, walking in Jeroboam's sins) represents an investment of agency in the wrong direction. The accumulation of these investments makes the sinful course progressively more entrenched — the person has more of themselves invested in it, making departure ever more demanding. This is why long-established refusal to repent (lifetime — 2Ki 15:18; generational — 2Ki 15:9) is harder to reverse than recent refusal: more of the person's agency-history is invested in the sinful course.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=17447, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Moral evaluation is engaged in its distorted mode. 1Ki 16:31 — "as if it had been a light thing" — the moral evaluation faculty is producing a verdict about the gravity of the sin (light/trivial) that is false. The false verdict enables the refusal: if the sin is "light," there is little impetus to depart from it. Heb 10:26 — the person has received "the knowledge of the truth" — the correct moral evaluation is available, but the will acts against it. The moral evaluation faculty is engaged (it produces outputs) but either produces false verdicts (minimisation mode) or its correct verdicts are bypassed by the will (deliberate sinning mode).

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=17448, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent *distorts* moral evaluation through minimisation (1Ki 16:31) and *bypasses* it in the deliberate-sinning mode (Heb 10:26 — the evaluation is correct but the will overrides it). Sustained refusal may impair moral evaluation by habituating the person to the minimisation assessment until it becomes the default cognitive framework, making accurate evaluation progressively harder.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=17449, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that refusal to repent can operate through two distinct cognitive modes: (1) *distorted evaluation* — the person does not correctly assess the gravity of their sinful course (1Ki 16:31); and (2) *bypassed evaluation* — the person correctly assesses the gravity but refuses to act on that assessment (Heb 10:26). These two modes have the same outcome (refusal) but different inner-being configurations (distorted vs bypassed cognition). The distinction has implications for correction: correcting mode 1 requires recalibrating the moral evaluation; correcting mode 2 requires re-engaging the will with what the evaluation already correctly concludes.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=17450, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The conscience is engaged in refusal to repent through its *presence but ineffectiveness* — unlike CHAR-4 (where conscience is absent or suppressed), CHAR-5 presupposes a conscience that has registered the call to turn but whose signal the will is refusing. Heb 10:26 — "after receiving the knowledge of the truth" — implies that the truth has been known (conscience-accessible) but the sinning continues deliberately. Neh 9:29 — "you warned them" — the warnings reach the community, and the community's refusal to respond is a conscience-override: the warning is received (conscience-signal delivered) but the will stiffens against it. The conscience in CHAR-5 is not absent but overridden.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=17451, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent *bypasses and progressively numbs* the conscience. Initially, the conscience may actively register the call to turn (the warnings of Neh 9:29 represent externally-delivered conscience-input that the community ignores). With sustained refusal, the conscience becomes progressively numbed to the signal — each time the warning is ignored, the conscience's signal produces less response. The endpoint of sustained refusal in the corpus is the constitutional hardening of Neh 9:29 ("stiffened their neck") — where the conscience's input no longer registers any motivating force.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=17452, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The conscience pattern reveals the critical distinction between CHAR-4 and CHAR-5: in CHAR-4, conscience is absent or suppressed (the moral signal is not being generated); in CHAR-5, conscience is present but overridden (the moral signal is generated but the will refuses to respond to it). CHAR-5 thus represents a *more advanced moral condition* than CHAR-4 in a specific sense: the person is capable of moral self-recognition (the signal exists) but is actively choosing not to act on it. This is what makes Heb 10:26 so severe — the deliberate sinning after knowledge of truth is conscience present and fully overridden.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=17459, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — God operates toward the person who refuses to repent in two modes. (1) *As the one who warns and calls to turn*: Neh 9:29 — "you warned them in order to turn them back to your law" — God's movement toward the refusing person is persistent invitational: warnings given, calling to the law, seeking their return. This reveals God's patient initiative even toward the refusing will. (2) *As the one who announces judgment when refusal persists*: Eze 18:24 — "for the treachery of which he is guilty and the sin he has committed, for them he shall die"; Heb 10:26 — "there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" — when refusal becomes definitive and sustained, God's response shifts from warning to announced consequence. God's movement toward the refusing person is thus first redemptive (warnings) and then judicial (announced consequence).

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=17460, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-5]** E — God's warnings (Neh 9:29) are based on his covenantal commitment to call Israel back to his law — the basis is relational obligation, not mechanical operation. God warns because he is Israel's covenant Lord who desires their return. God's judicial response (Eze 18:24 — death for the sinning righteous person who does not repent; Heb 10:26 — sacrifice foreclosed) is based on the moral reality of sustained deliberate refusal: the basis is the person's own persistent choice. The shift from invitational to judicial is not arbitrary but is based on the person's own sustained exercise of the will against the available turn.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=17461, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — God's disposition toward the refusing person is: (1) *Patient and persistent in invitation*: Neh 9:29 — "you warned them" — the warnings are given before judgment; God does not immediately move to judgment but warns repeatedly. (2) *Morally serious in consequence*: Eze 18:24; Heb 10:26 — when refusal persists, the consequences are real and severe. God's disposition is not one of indifference to the refusal (he continues to warn) nor one of unlimited tolerance (the consequence is real and announced). The disposition is holy patience that does not capitulate to the refusal: God maintains his moral standard ("the law") while continuing to call the refusing person toward it.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=17462, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — God-to-human operation is evidenced (T4.1.1–T4.1.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=17463, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The human-to-God movement is disrupted by refusal to repent. The corpus describes the absence of the movement toward God that repentance would constitute: 2Ki 10:31 — "not careful to walk in the law of the Lord, the God of Israel" — the movement toward God through law-walking is precisely what is withheld. Neh 9:29 — "you warned them in order to turn them back to your law. Yet they acted presumptuously" — the movement toward God (turning back to his law) is available but the will moves in the opposite direction (presumptuousness). Refusal to repent is the blocking of the human-to-God movement: the will that could move toward God in turning chooses not to.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=17464, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The corpus names the required inner posture for the human-to-God movement by its absence: 2Ki 10:31 — "with all his heart" — wholehearted commitment is the required posture; its absence ("not with all his heart") is what enables the refusal. Neh 9:29's "stubborn shoulder and stiffened their neck" describes the actual posture: bodily-metaphor resistance, the opposite of submissive receptivity. The inner posture required for movement toward God is willing surrender of the neck and shoulder to the yoke of God's law — the posture of humility and submission that refusal to repent explicitly withholds.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=17465, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The blocked human-to-God movement reveals that refusal to repent constitutes a *sustained relational posture of non-return*: the person is in relationship with God (within his covenant, subject to his law and warnings) but maintaining a direction away from him rather than toward him. The relationship is not terminated — God continues to warn (Neh 9:29), continues to call — but the person's directional movement is persistently away. Rom 6:15's emphatic rejection of "sinning under grace" reveals that even the grace-relationship does not dissolve the moral requirement to turn; the person who refuses to repent under grace is misusing the relational framework itself.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=17466, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Human-to-God operation is evidenced in its disrupted form (T4.2.1–T4.2.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=17467, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes — the transmission dimension is extensively evidenced. The Jeroboam-sin formula includes the phrase "which he made Israel to sin" (2Ki 3:3; 10:29; 13:2/6/11; 14:24; 15:9/18/24/28) — each king's refusal to repent is extended to the people through leadership. The king's non-departure from Jeroboam's sins draws the whole nation into the same non-departure. 1Ki 16:31 — Ahab's treatment of Jeroboam's sins as "a light thing" while going further to serve Baal: the leader's refusal extends and escalates the communal sinful course. The characteristic is transmitted from leader to people through the leadership's continued walking in the sinful pattern.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=17468, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The enabling conditions for transmitting refusal to repent: (1) *Authority and representative position*: every king in the corpus holds the authority through which the sinful pattern is transmitted — "he made Israel to sin" — the enabling condition is positional authority that gives the leader's inner disposition communal scope. (2) *Modelled non-departure*: 2Ki 15:9 — "as his fathers had done" — the next generation's refusal is enabled by the prior generation's modelled refusal; the enabling condition is the observable pattern of the predecessor. (3) *The sinful course presented as normative*: when the king walks in Jeroboam's sins without departing, the community receives an implicit signal that this is the normative path — the community's refusal is enabled by the leader's presentation of the sinful course as acceptable.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=17469, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The transmitting person must have: (1) *received the inherited sinful orientation and accepted it* — 2Ki 15:9 "as his fathers had done" — they themselves did not depart, making the transmission a continuation of their own inner condition; (2) *held leadership authority* that gives their inner condition communal scope — the kings who transmit the refusal hold the authority through which their personal non-departure becomes the community's non-departure; (3) *established the sinful pattern as the operative norm* — by walking in it, by treating it as light (1Ki 16:31), by not publicly marking the path as wrong.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=17470, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — The giving/transmission direction is extensively evidenced (T4.3.1–T4.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=17471, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes — the communal reception of the refusal pattern is evidenced. 2Ki 13:6 — "Israel did not depart from the sins of the house of Jeroboam, which he made Israel to sin, but walked in them" — the whole people receive and enact the refusal as a communal pattern. 2Ki 15:9 — "as his fathers had done" — each king receives the refusal-pattern from his fathers and adopts it as his own. The reception is not forced but constituted by the community's own walk: "walked in them" — they received the pattern and actively sustained it.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=17472, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Reception-enabling conditions: (1) *Existing alignment with the sinful course*: a community already walking in the sinful direction (Jeroboam's golden calves as established worship) does not need much additional influence to continue not departing; (2) *Leadership modelling*: 2Ki 15:9 — the father's example is the primary transmission mechanism; receptivity is enabled by the leader's modelled non-departure. Reception-blocking conditions: the fear of God (1Ti 5:20 — the fear produced by public rebuke in others prevents their adopting the pattern), wholehearted law-walking (2Ki 10:31 — the positive alternative).

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=17473, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-5]** E — 1Ti 5:20 provides the clearest evidence of the non-receiving person's inner state: "so that the rest may stand in fear" — the person who witnesses the public rebuke of the persistent sinner but does not adopt the pattern of persistent sinning is in a state of *fear* — moral alertness produced by seeing the consequence of refusal to repent. This fear-state is the opposite of the dull moral affect (1Ki 16:31 — "a light thing") that enables reception of the refusal. The non-receiving person retains the moral alertness that the receiving person has lost.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=17474, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced in the communal reception of the Jeroboam-pattern (T4.4.1) and the non-receiving condition (T4.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=17475, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes. The Kings corpus shows refusal to repent operating *within existing covenantal bonds*: each king stands in the Davidic kingship tradition, within Israel's covenant with the Lord, and their refusal is a refusal within that relationship. The urgency of Neh 9:29 — "you warned them in order to turn them *back* to your law" — the word "back" (*shuv*) confirms a pre-existing relational orientation that the refusal is turning away from. The depth of the relational bond (the covenant) makes the refusal more morally serious, not less — refusing to turn within an established covenant is a greater moral act than refusing within no relationship.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=17476, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The primary corpus is covenantal: Israel's covenant with the Lord is the frame for the Kings corpus and Neh 9:29. However, Rom 6:15 — "are we to sin because we are not under law but under grace?" — extends the characteristic into the new-covenant context: refusing to repent under grace is possible and explicitly rejected. Heb 10:26 — "sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth" — operates in a context where the new covenant's truth has been received. The characteristic crosses covenantal boundaries and intensifies within them: the more clearly the covenant's moral requirements are known, the more culpable the refusal to repent.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=17477, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The relational scope is: (1) *Individual-to-God* (Eze 18:24 — the formerly righteous person's personal refusal; Heb 10:26 — individual deliberate sinning); (2) *Leadership-to-community* (the Kings pattern — the leader's refusal transmitted to the people); (3) *Community-to-God* (Neh 9:29; 2Ki 13:6 — communal non-departure from the sinful course). The relational scope is wide: the characteristic operates in every relational configuration, from the most individual (Eze 18:24) to the most communal (the whole nation refusing to depart — 2Ki 13:6).

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=17478, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Relational scope is evidenced across individual, leadership, and communal levels (T4.5.1–T4.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=17479, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-5]** S — The 18-verse corpus contains no direct evidence of refusal to repent operating in relation to angelic or adversarial spiritual beings. The characteristic is presented throughout as a human-to-God relational phenomenon without spiritual-being interface.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=17480, S) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-5]** S — No direct evidence in the corpus of adversarial spiritual involvement in refusal to repent. The corpus presents refusal to repent as a self-sustaining human volitional condition — the will's own non-departure — without attributing it to adversarial spiritual pressure.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=17481, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-5]** S — No evidence. The corpus is silent on angelic mediation in connection with refusal to repent.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=17482, S) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** S — The evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface for CHAR-5. All operations in the corpus are within the God-human and human-community relational frameworks. Noted explicitly.

  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-5] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings — T4.6.1–T4.6.4 — no citation required)

  5. Distinct openings:   ✓

---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=17483, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes — in both directions, progressively. (1) *Condition change*: Heb 10:26 — "there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" — persistent deliberate refusal to repent produces a condition-change: the availability of the remedy is foreclosed. Eze 18:24 — "none of the righteous deeds he has done shall be remembered" — the person's condition before God changes from standing to liability. (2) *Orientation change*: sustained refusal produces an increasingly entrenched orientation away from God: from "did not depart from" (early kings — passive non-departure) to "clung to" (2Ki 3:3 — active attachment) to "stubborn shoulder and stiffened neck" (Neh 9:29 — constitutional hardening). The orientation change is directional: the will becomes increasingly committed to the sinful course rather than merely not departing from it.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=17484, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The corpus presents a spectrum. (1) *Reversible in principle throughout most of the corpus*: the Kings pattern implies reversibility — each king who "did not depart from" could have departed; God's warnings (Neh 9:29 — "to turn them back") presuppose that turning was possible. Eze 33:12 — "the wickedness of the wicked, he shall not fall by it when he *turns from* his wickedness" — turning is presented as always remaining available, reversing the condition. (2) *Foreclosed at the Heb 10:26 threshold*: "there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" — at the point of deliberate sinning after knowledge of truth, the reversal mechanism (sacrifice) is described as closed. The corpus thus presents: reversible across most of the trajectory of refusal, but reaching a point of irreversibility at the extreme end (Heb 10:26).

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=17485, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Transformation evidence is present (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=17486, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes — the corpus traces a developmental sequence across the Kings pattern. (1) *Initial state*: the sinful course inherited and not rejected — "as his fathers had done" (2Ki 15:9) — the will's passive acceptance. (2) *Developmental state*: "did not depart from" — the will's persistent non-departure becomes the settled pattern across a reign. (3) *Advanced state*: "clung to" (2Ki 3:3 — *davaq*) — the will actively attaches to the sinful course; the attachment is no longer merely passive. (4) *Constitutional state*: "stubborn shoulder and stiffened their neck" (Neh 9:29) — the refusal has become a bodily-metaphor constitutional posture. (5) *Terminal state*: Heb 10:26 — deliberate sinning after full knowledge, with the sacrifice foreclosed — the refusal has reached the point where the reversal mechanism is described as unavailable.

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=17487, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The sequence (inherited acceptance → sustained non-departure → active clinging → constitutional hardening → terminal foreclosure) reveals that refusal to repent is a *self-deepening progressive condition*: it does not remain static but deepens with each iteration of the refusal. Each time the will does not depart, the next non-departure becomes more natural, the will more attached, and the possibility of departure less accessible to the will's habituated orientation. The sequence also reveals a moral point of no return (Heb 10:26) — not because God withdraws the desire for turning, but because the mechanism of restoration (sacrifice) is closed by the sustained deliberate sinning.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=17488, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Sequence is evidenced across the Kings corpus and NT culmination (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=17489, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Two mechanisms. (1) *Habituation through repetition*: each king who "did not depart from" adds to the accumulated habit of non-departure — the mechanism is formative through repeated volitional non-acts. Ecc 8:12 (adjacent registered in CHAR-6 — habitual defection) shares this mechanism. The mechanism here is: will not departing → pattern established → next non-departure more natural → deeper entrenching. (2) *Leadership transmission*: the Kings formula "which he made Israel to sin" shows the mechanism of communal shaping through leadership modelling — the leader's refusal becomes the community's refusal through authority and normative influence.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=17490, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes. The habituation mechanism (1) operates at the individual level — each king personally formed in the refusal-direction through accumulated non-departures. The transmission mechanism (2) operates at the communal level — the community shaped by the leader's refusal. Neh 9:29's "warned them" shows a third mechanism: the preventive warning aimed at breaking the refusal before it consolidates — an externally-applied mechanism intended to interrupt the habituation-and-transmission cycle.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=17491, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Mechanism is evidenced (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=17492, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes — the Kings corpus as a whole is embedded in the narrative of Israel's suffering under Assyrian and Babylonian pressure, and each king's refusal to repent from Jeroboam's sins is part of the causal chain leading to that suffering. The warnings of Neh 9:29 are given precisely in the context of the accumulated afflictions that resulted from sustained refusal. Eze 18:24 — "for them he shall die" — names suffering (death) as the consequence of refusal to repent from the sinning of the formerly-righteous person. Refusal to repent thus operates as the *cause* of the afflictions the corpus narrates as consequences.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=17493, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The corpus shows suffering primarily *failing to break* refusal to repent in the Kings pattern — the warnings (Neh 9:29) that accompany the consequences of prior refusal do not produce repentance but are met with "stubborn shoulder and stiffened neck." Suffering in the Kings context does not produce the turning it is intended to produce. This reveals that refusal to repent is resilient under affliction: the suffering produced by prior refusal does not automatically reverse the refusal. The hardened will (Neh 9:29) can endure suffering without yielding — which is what makes the Heb 10:26 terminal state so severe: even the loss of the sacrifice (itself a suffering) does not move the refusing will.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=17494, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — The relationship between refusal to repent and suffering is evidenced (T5.4.1 and T5.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=17495, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes — as a *negative formative force*. The Kings pattern across generations (2Ki 15:9 — "as his fathers had done") shows refusal to repent shaping successive generations' moral orientation. Each king formed in the same refusal-pattern shapes the next, creating a multi-generational formative arc running in the wrong direction: progressively away from God rather than toward him. 2Ki 3:3's "clung to" vs 2Ki 13:2's "did not depart from" suggests deepening entrenchment across individual formation arcs — from mere non-departure to active attachment.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=17496, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent plays the role of a *negative formative constant* in the character formation arc: it shapes the person and community progressively away from the moral alignment that genuine formation toward God requires. While positive formation (toward greater likeness to God) requires the will to engage — "careful to walk in the law... with all his heart" (2Ki 10:31) — refusal to repent requires only the will's non-engagement, making it the path of least resistance. This reveals a deeply important asymmetry: positive formation requires active engagement; the negative formation of refusal-to-repent operates through passive non-departure, which is easier and therefore the default trajectory for the fallen will.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=17497, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Formation evidence is present (T5.5.1 and T5.5.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=17498, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes — toward judgment as its eschatological trajectory, in increasingly sharp terms. Eze 18:24 — "for them he shall die" — the death-consequence points toward a final divine verdict on the person who persistently sinned without turning back. Heb 10:26–27 (the broader context of 10:26's "no sacrifice remaining") — "a fearful expectation of judgment, and a fury of fire that will consume the adversaries" — names the eschatological horizon of refusal to repent: the person who deliberately persists in sinning after knowing truth faces judgment as the final state. The eschatological fullness toward which refusal to repent points is the divine judgment that cannot itself be refused.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=17499, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The present experience of refusal to repent (continued walking in the sinful course, the sacrifice closed off, the moral record of righteous deeds erased — Eze 18:24) anticipates a future of divine reckoning without available remedy. Heb 10:26 — "there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" — the present foreclosure of the sacrifice anticipates a future where the foreclosure becomes permanent: judgment without the provision that would have enabled forgiveness. The present refusal forecloses the future remedy: what the refusing will withholds now (the turn) is what would have opened the future (forgiveness and life); refusing the turn now anticipates a future of unaddressed moral liability before the judge.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=17500, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced (Eze 18:24; Heb 10:26). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=17501, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-5]** E — T2.8 found formational-constitutional deposit evidence: Neh 9:29 ("stiffened their neck" — formed posture of resistance), 2Ki 3:3 ("clung to" — active attachment), 2Ki 15:18 ("all his days" — lifetime orientation), 2Ki 15:9 ("as his fathers" — generational transmission). The developmental consequence: (1) *The will becomes progressively less responsive to the call to turn*: each iteration of refusal consolidates the will's non-departure direction, making the next refusal more natural and the possibility of departure less accessible; (2) *The communal moral environment is shaped toward the sinful course*: the leader's deposit of non-departure shapes the community's expectation of what is normal; (3) *The sacrifice becomes increasingly necessary and then foreclosed*: Heb 10:26's terminal condition is the developmental endpoint of the deposit.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=17502, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes, clearly evidenced. 2Ki 15:9 — "as his fathers had done. He did not depart from the sins of Jeroboam" — the generational consequence is explicit: the next king inherits the refusal-orientation and does not depart from it. The Jeroboam-sin formula repeated across 10+ kings over the full span of Israel's history in Kings demonstrates that refusal to repent deposits a generational moral orientation that each successor receives and sustains. The "as his fathers" formula is the evidential marker of the generational deposit: the deposit is a transmitted moral posture of non-departure that each generation receives and does not reject.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=17503, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-5]** E — T2.8 found deposit evidence. T5.7 is addressed in T5.7.1 and T5.7.2. This prompt is superseded.

---

T5 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       21/21 ✓ (T5.1.1–T5.1.3, T5.2.1–T5.2.3, T5.3.1–T5.3.3, T5.4.1–T5.4.3, T5.5.1–T5.5.3, T5.6.1–T5.6.3, T5.7.1–T5.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       21× [CHAR-5] ✓

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.


---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=17504, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Three adjacent characteristics appear consistently. (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)* — as the ongoing sinning that the refusal to repent sustains: Heb 10:26 — "go on sinning deliberately" — the deliberate ongoing sinning is both CHAR-1 (the sinning act) and CHAR-5 (the refusal to stop). (2) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)* — as the pattern the refusal to repent produces: the Kings corpus's "walked in them" (2Ki 13:6) and "all his days" (2Ki 15:18) describe the habitual defection that sustained refusal establishes. (3) *CHAR-7 (Contagious sin)* — as the communal transmission the refusal enables: "which he made Israel to sin" — the leader's refusal to repent is the mechanism through which the sinful course spreads to the community.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=17505, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The co-occurrence pattern places refusal to repent as a *sustaining characteristic*: it sustains CHAR-1 (wilful sinning) by preventing the turn that would end the sinning, sustains CHAR-6 (habitual defection) by providing the volitional commitment that makes habituation possible, and enables CHAR-7 (contagious sin) by modelling non-departure for the community. CHAR-5 is thus the inner-being mechanism that keeps multiple other characteristics active: it is the will-act that prevents the turning which would resolve them.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=17506, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are clearly evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=17507, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent consistently *follows* CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning) — the sinning must have occurred before there is a refusal to repent from it — and *precedes* CHAR-6 (Habitual defection) — the sustained non-departure produces habitual sinning over time. In the Kings pattern, the sequence is: Jeroboam sinned (CHAR-1 origin) → subsequent kings did not depart from those sins (CHAR-5) → they walked in them across their reigns (CHAR-6). Refusal to repent occupies the middle position: it follows the initial sin and produces the habitual pattern.

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=17508, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The relationship is primarily *developmental and causal*. Developmental: refusal to repent develops from initial non-departure into active clinging into constitutional hardening (T5.2). Causal: refusal to repent *causes* the habitual sinning (CHAR-6) that follows — the will's persistent non-departure is the causal mechanism through which wilful sinning (CHAR-1) becomes habitual defection (CHAR-6). The three characteristics in sequence (CHAR-1 → CHAR-5 → CHAR-6) form a causal-developmental chain: the sin initiates, the refusal to repent sustains, the habituation establishes.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=17509, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Sequential patterns are clearly evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=17510, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent produces: (1) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: the sustained walking in the sinful course (2Ki 13:6 — "walked in them") is habitual defection produced by the will's persistent non-departure; (2) *CHAR-7 (Contagious sin)*: the leader's refusal to repent transmits the sinful course to the community ("which he made Israel to sin" — 2Ki 3:3; 13:2; etc.); (3) *Judgment (eventual)*: Eze 18:24 — death; Heb 10:26 — no sacrifice remaining. Refusal to repent produces these outcomes through its sustaining and transmitting mechanisms.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=17511, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent is produced by: (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: a sin must have been committed before there is something to repent of — the wilful sinning is the precondition; (2) *CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression)* in its entry mode: the conscience that does not register the sin's gravity (Ecc 8:12 — dulled moral affect; 1Ki 16:31 — minimisation) provides less motivating force to turn; (3) *Inherited moral orientation*: 2Ki 15:9 — "as his fathers had done" — the inherited sinful pattern is the condition that makes refusal to repent the default, requiring no specific act of refusal to initiate.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=17512, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Refusal to repent is a constituent element of *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: habitual defection requires not just the single act of sinning but the sustained volitional non-departure that CHAR-5 describes — CHAR-5 is the volitional engine that drives CHAR-6. Refusal to repent also constitutes a dimension of the generational pattern that characterises Israel's history in Kings: the generational perpetuation of Jeroboam's sin is constituted by each generation's refusal to repent. Within CHAR-5 itself, the Heb 10:26 mode constitutes an intensified sub-type: deliberate sinning after full knowledge, which represents the most volitionally accountable form of the characteristic.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=17513, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are extensively evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=17514, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes. The primary term cha.ta (H2398) is shared with CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning), CHAR-2 (Unintentional sinning), CHAR-3 (Confession), CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression), CHAR-6 (Habitual defection). Within CHAR-5, the distinctive vocabulary is the *non-departure language* combined with cha.ta: "did not depart from the sins" — this phrase pattern is CHAR-5's characteristic construction, combining the sinning-vocabulary with movement-withholding language. The NT hamartanō (G0264) in its persistent-deliberate mode (1Ti 5:20 — "persist in sin"; Heb 10:26 — "go on sinning deliberately") is shared with CHAR-1 but here deployed in the sustained-refusal configuration.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=17515, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-5]** E — Yes. The H2398 root (cha.ta) generates the same vocabulary field as in CHAR-1/2/3/4. What distinguishes CHAR-5's root-level use is the grammatical construction: the root appears within "did not depart from the sins" — the root provides the noun (sins) while the movement-verbs (sur, shuv, depart, turn) provide the refusal language. CHAR-5's distinctive vocabulary contribution is thus not a different root but a different *grammatical configuration* of the shared root with movement-withholding verbs.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=17516, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The shared vocabulary (cha.ta across CHAR-1/2/3/4/5/6) confirms that all these characteristics share the same semantic domain — the domain of sinning and its inner-being dimensions. CHAR-5's conceptual relationship to the others is defined by its grammatical configuration: the same sinning-vocabulary deployed in the non-departure-from construction reveals CHAR-5 as the characteristic that *holds sinning in place* rather than committing it (CHAR-1), confessing it (CHAR-3), or suppressing awareness of it (CHAR-4). CHAR-5 is the will-act that prevents the movement from any of these toward the turn.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=17517, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=17518, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-5]** E — CHAR-6 (Habitual defection) most closely resembles CHAR-5: both involve sustained sinning over time. The precise distinction: CHAR-5 is the *volitional commitment* to non-departure (the will refusing to turn — the characteristic is the refusal); CHAR-6 is the *habitual pattern* that sustained non-departure produces (the established sinful behaviour). CHAR-5 is the inner volitional act; CHAR-6 is the settled outer pattern. The boundary: CHAR-5 is the will's decision not to turn; CHAR-6 is what the will's repeated decisions not to turn have produced as a habituated way of life. They are constitutively linked (CHAR-5 produces CHAR-6) but analytically distinct (inner volitional commitment vs outer habituated pattern).

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=17519, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The overlap is most visible in 2Ki 13:6 — "did not depart from the sins... but walked in them" — both the not-departing (CHAR-5) and the walking-in-them (CHAR-6) appear in the same verse. The boundary is between the volitional non-departure (CHAR-5 — the will's decision) and the behavioural walking-in (CHAR-6 — the enacted pattern). The same verse evidences both because in practice the volitional refusal and the habitual pattern are nearly simultaneous: when the will refuses to turn, the sinful pattern continues; when the sinful pattern continues, it represents and reinforces the will's refusal. The boundary is analytical (decision vs pattern) not always temporal.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=17520, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The distinction between CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent) and CHAR-6 (Habitual defection) is one of *kind* — they are not the same phenomenon at different intensities but different aspects of a related inner-being reality: one is a volitional commitment (CHAR-5), the other is a formed dispositional pattern (CHAR-6). The distinction between CHAR-5 and CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression) is one of *kind* at the conscience-registration node: CHAR-4 = conscience not registering; CHAR-5 = conscience registers (or knowledge is present) but will overrides.

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=17521, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=17522, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-5]** E — 2Ki 3:3 — "he clung to the sin of Jeroboam the son of Nebat, which he made Israel to sin; he did not depart from it" — is a candidate anchor for both CHAR-5 and CHAR-7 (Contagious sin — "which he made Israel to sin"). Heb 10:26 — "if we go on sinning deliberately after receiving the knowledge of the truth, there no longer remains a sacrifice for sins" — is a strong candidate anchor for CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning — the deliberate ongoing sinning) as well as CHAR-5. Neh 9:29 — "stubborn shoulder and stiffened their neck and would not obey" — may anchor studies of community-level conscience suppression (CHAR-4) or broader rebellion characteristics.

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=17523, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-5]** E — 2Ki 3:3 as shared anchor between CHAR-5 and CHAR-7 reveals that leadership's refusal to repent and the communal contagion of sin are *constitutively linked* in the Kings pattern: the leader's inner volitional non-departure is simultaneously the mechanism of communal sin-transmission. The two characteristics are not separable in the leadership context — the leader's CHAR-5 is the CHAR-7 mechanism. Heb 10:26 as shared anchor between CHAR-5 and CHAR-1 reveals that at the extreme end of refusal to repent, wilful sinning and refusal to stop sinning are inseparable: the deliberate ongoing sinning is simultaneously the act (CHAR-1) and the refusal to stop (CHAR-5).

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=17524, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Shared verse anchors are identified (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=17525, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The primary dimension (will/volition) is shared with CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning — will as the primary faculty) and CHAR-3 (Confession — will as the agreeing faculty). The secondary heart-dimension (2Ki 10:31 — not with all his heart) is shared with CHAR-3's heart-initiation. The constitutional hardening dimension (Neh 9:29 — stiffened neck) has resonance with CHAR-4's constitutional deformation (Tit 3:11 — warped). The generational transmission dimension is shared with CHAR-7 (Contagious sin — leadership transmitting to community).

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=17526, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-5]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms that CHAR-5 is not an isolated characteristic but a *volitional nexus*: it shares the will-dimension with CHAR-1 (sinning) and CHAR-3 (confessing) — the same faculty serves all three, but in different directional configurations. CHAR-5's will *refuses to turn*, CHAR-1's will *commits the wrong*, CHAR-3's will *names the wrong as wrong*. The shared dimension reveals that the will is the decisive inner faculty across the sinning-confession-refusal triad — its direction and mode determine which characteristic is instantiated.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=17527, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-5]** E — Dimensional sharing evidence is available from the corpus analysis (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.
