(1) *Separation from God*: Act 26:18 — "from darkness to light and from the power of Satan to God" — the condition places the person in a relational domain separated from God; Joh 8:21 — "where I am going, you cannot come" — the condition creates an eschatological-relational separation from Jesus; (2) *Requiring mediation*: Heb 5:1 — "appointed to act on behalf of men in relation to God, to offer gifts and sacrifices for sins" — the condition creates a relational gap between human and divine that requires priestly mediation; (3) *Debtor-creditor relationship*: Luk 11:4 — "forgive us our sins, for we ourselves forgive everyone who is indebted to us" — the relational condition is framed as a debt-obligation to God.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=14622, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *severs and blocks* the relational capacity toward God while leaving horizontal relational capacity intact (persons continue to relate to each other under the condition). Joh 8:21 — "you will die in your sin... where I am going, you cannot come" — the condition creates an absolute relational barrier that prevents access to where Jesus goes. Rom 11:27 — "when I take away their sins" — the removal of the condition is constituted as the restoration of covenantal relationship: sin is the barrier; its removal is relational restoration. Heb 1:3 — "after making purification for sins, he sat down at the right hand" — the purification is the prerequisite for the highest relational nearness (right hand of the Majesty) — sin is what prevents that nearness.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=14623, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The relational pattern reveals that sin as universal condition is at its core a *relational rupture*: the condition is defined by its effect on the person's standing before God and their access to where God is (Joh 8:21 — "where I am going, you cannot come"). The universal scope (all under sin — Rom 3:9) means every human person's relationship with God is compromised by the condition; there is no human being whose God-relationship is unaffected. The relational rupture is what the entire VCG-02 redemption register addresses: forgiveness (VCG-02) restores what the condition severed — the person's relational standing before God. Heb 2:17 — propitiation for sins — names the relational offence that the condition creates and that Christ's work addresses.

---

T3 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       33/33 ✓ (T3.1.1–T3.1.3, T3.2.1–T3.2.3, T3.3.1–T3.3.3, T3.4.1–T3.4.3, T3.5.1–T3.5.3, T3.6.1–T3.6.3, T3.7.1–T3.7.3, T3.8.1–T3.8.3, T3.9.1–T3.9.3, T3.10.1–T3.10.3, T3.11.1–T3.11.3)
  2. Scope marker:       33× [CHAR-11] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings — T3.5.1–T3.5.3 — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 4:7; Joh 8:21/9:41/15:22/16:8/9; Act 3:19/7:60/22:16/26:18; Act 13:38; Rom 2:12/3:9/20/23/4:7/8/6:12/13/16/18/20/7:5/7/8/9/14/17/20/23/25/11:27; Luk 11:4; Heb 1:3/2:17/3:13/5:1; 1Ti 5:24; 2Ti 3:6; 14:23 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Response approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T4 continues in the next response.


---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=14594, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The cognitive faculty is engaged centrally through the law's function. Rom 3:20 — "through the law comes knowledge of sin" — the law's primary cognitive function is to produce knowledge of the sin-condition in the person's mind; without the law, the condition lacks cognitive recognition. Rom 7:7 — "if it had not been for the law, I would not have known sin. For I would not have known what it is to covet if the law had not said, 'You shall not covet'" — the cognitive content of the condition becomes known through the commandment's prohibition. The cognitive faculty receives the law's content and thereby recognises what the condition involves.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=14595, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *distorts* cognition while simultaneously being what the law enables cognition to recognise. (1) *Distortion*: Heb 3:13 — "deceitfulness of sin" — the condition operates through cognitive deception, producing false self-assessment; (2) *Activation by law*: Rom 7:8/9 — "sin, seizing an opportunity through the commandment, produced in me all kinds of covetousness" — the law's cognitive content is seized by the condition to *activate* the very desire the law prohibits; the cognitive-law framework becomes the mechanism of the condition's arousal. The condition thus both evades correct cognition (deception) and exploits the cognitive framework (law) as the occasion for its full expression.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=14596, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals that the condition's relationship to cognition is complex and bidirectional: (1) the law as a cognitive framework reveals the condition (Rom 3:20 — knowledge of sin comes through law); (2) but the condition exploits the same cognitive framework for its own activation (Rom 7:8 — sin seizes the commandment). This is why Paul argues in Romans 7 that the law is not sin (Rom 7:7) — the law is good; the condition is what uses the good cognitive framework perversely. The cognitive faculty's recognition of the condition (through the law) does not by itself produce liberation from the condition (Rom 7:23 — the mind's law is overridden by the law of sin in the members).

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=14597, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Memory is engaged through the ledger/accounting dimension of the condition. Rom 4:8 — "blessed is the man against whom the Lord will not count his sin" — sin is "counted" against the person; the divine memory of the person's sin-condition constitutes the ledger. Act 3:19 — "that your sins may be blotted out" — blotting out is a memory-erasure metaphor: the divine record is cleared. 1Ti 5:24 — "the sins of some people are conspicuous, going before them to judgment, but the sins of others appear later" — the temporal dimension of when the condition's recorded content appears implies a memory-record that either surfaces now or at judgment.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=14598, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *creates a divine memory record* (the ledger) and shapes human memory through the burden it generates. 2Ti 3:6 — "burdened with sins" — the accumulated condition weighs on the person's inner carrying-capacity; the burden involves the memory of accumulated sins that weigh the person down. Act 7:60 — Stephen's dying prayer: "do not hold this sin against them" — the prayer presupposes that God's memory will hold the act against the perpetrators unless released; sin's ledger-dimension is a divine memorial record.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=14599, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The memory pattern reveals that the universal sin-condition creates a *moral record* — a divine memorial of the condition's expression — that either stands against the person (Rom 4:8 — "counted against") or is released (Act 3:19 — "blotted out"; Rom 4:7 — "covered"). The condition is not merely present-tense subjective experience but past-tense accumulated record. This memorial dimension is why forgiveness (VCG-02) is specifically framed in accounting metaphors: blotting out, covering, not counting — all operations on the record, not merely on the present experience of the condition.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=14600, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The affective faculty is engaged through the passions and desires that the condition activates. Rom 6:12 — "let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body, to make you obey its passions" — the condition operates through bodily passions as its affective instruments of rule; sin reigns by making the person obey affective desires rather than God. Rom 7:5 — "our sinful passions, aroused by the law, were at work in our members" — the sinful passions are the affective-level instruments through which the condition operates within the fleshly state. 2Ti 3:6 — "led astray by various passions" — the condition-burdened person is susceptible to multiple affective passions that lead them further from God.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=14601, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *captures and misdirects* the affective faculty. Rom 6:12 — sin deploys bodily passions as instruments of its reign — the affective capacity (passions, desires) is captured by the condition and redirected toward its sinful objects. Rom 7:5 — "sinful passions... at work in our members" — the passions are the affective content that the condition activates and deploys. The condition does not eliminate affect but misdirects it: the passions remain operative but serve sin's reign rather than God's.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=14602, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The affective pattern reveals that the universal sin-condition operates partly through the capture of the person's affective motivational system. Sin's reign (Rom 6:12 — reigning through passions) is an affective-level dominion: the person is made to *obey* the passions that sin deploys, suggesting that the affective drive overrides the cognitive-moral will. This explains why the condition's liberation is not merely cognitive (knowing what is right) but must address the affective level: Rom 6:13 — the body must be actively re-presented to God, redirecting the affective system's deployment from sin's instruments to God's instruments.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=14603, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** S — The 112-verse corpus does not explicitly engage the creative faculty in connection with sin as universal condition. The condition operates through power-domination, debt, passions, and the indwelling force — not through creative origination.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=14604, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** S — No direct evidence. The condition's relationship to creativity is not addressed in the corpus. The condition's primary modes (enslaving power, standing debt, passions-capture) are not creative-faculty operations.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=14605, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** S — The silence on creativity reflects the characteristic's focus on the person's condition before God (sin as structural state, debt, enslaving power) rather than on the person's creative productive capacity. The condition is primarily about what sin has done to the person's inner-being standing, not about what the person creates. Noted.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=14606, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — the volitional faculty is the most consistently named inner faculty across the corpus. (1) *The enslaved will*: Rom 7:17/20 — "it is no longer I who do it, but sin that dwells within me" — the will's identity is preserved but its effective control is overridden; (2) *The will's obedience as the master-choice*: Rom 6:16 — "you are slaves of the one whom you obey" — the will's act of obedience determines whose slave the person is; (3) *The will commanded to resist*: Gen 4:7 — "you must rule over it" — the volitional faculty is addressed as the faculty that must exercise governing authority against the sin-force; (4) *The will freed*: Rom 6:18 — "having been set free from sin, have become slaves of righteousness" — liberation changes the will's master-relationship.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=14607, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *enslaves and progressively captures* volition. Rom 7:14 — "sold under sin" — the will is in a slave-ownership relationship; its capacity for autonomous choosing has been compromised by the sale. Rom 6:20 — "when you were slaves of sin, you were free in regard to righteousness" — the enslaved will has no freedom toward righteousness; the will's freedom is preserved in one direction (sinning) while closed in the other (righteousness). The condition's enslavement is thus a directional impairment: the will can continue to choose within the sinful domain but cannot freely choose toward God.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=14608, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The volitional pattern reveals that sin as universal condition creates a constitutive *volitional captivity with preserved volitional identity*. The person under the condition still wills, chooses, and acts (the condition does not abolish the will) but the will's effective freedom toward God has been compromised by the enslavement: Rom 7:17/20 — "it is no longer I who do it" — the "I" remains (identity preserved) but effective agency is overridden (the enslavement operates through the person's members). This subtlety is unique to CHAR-11: the condition does not destroy the will but captures its directional freedom, producing the characteristic divided-person experience of willing the good while the condition produces the evil.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=14609, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Agency is fully engaged — but the condition operates by redirecting rather than eliminating it. Rom 6:13 — "do not present your members to sin as instruments for unrighteousness" — the agency of presenting the members is real; the person actively presents them (the presenting is a genuine agentive act). The condition captures this agency and deploys it toward sin. Rom 6:16 — "whoever you present yourselves as obedient slaves" — the act of presenting is agentive; the condition's enslavement operates through real agentive presentations, not through the abolition of agency.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=14610, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *captures and misdirects* agency. The person's agency remains operative (they present members — Rom 6:13; they obey a master — Rom 6:16; they sin — Rom 2:12/3:23) but the direction of that agency has been captured by the condition. Rom 6:20 — "you were free in regard to righteousness" — the former slaves of sin had their agency fully occupied by the sinful domain, leaving no agentive freedom for righteousness. The impairment is directional: agency toward sin is preserved; agency toward God is restricted by the condition's enslavement.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=14611, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The agency pattern reveals that the universal sin-condition does not render persons passive recipients but *active participants* in their own enslavement: "you present your members to sin as instruments" (Rom 6:13) — the person is doing the presenting. The condition's enslavement operates through the person's own agentive acts, which are the form the enslavement takes. This has a significant implication: the condition is not external imposition but internal captivity that operates through and as the person's own actions — making moral accountability real (they sin — Rom 3:23) even within the context of structural enslavement.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=14612, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The moral evaluation faculty is engaged through the condition's relationship to the law as a moral-evaluative standard. Rom 3:20 — "through the law comes knowledge of sin" — the law functions as the moral evaluation framework that reveals the condition; the person's moral evaluation faculty, informed by the law, recognises what sinning is. Rom 14:23 — "whatever does not proceed from faith is sin" — the moral evaluation faculty (calibrated by faith) determines whether an act participates in the condition or not. Joh 9:41 — "your guilt remains" — the moral evaluation of the Pharisees' own condition is the issue; they evaluate themselves as not guilty while the condition persists.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=14613, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *distorts and bypasses* moral evaluation. Heb 3:13 — "hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" — the hardening of the heart that sin's deceitfulness produces is a moral-evaluative hardening: the person's capacity to correctly evaluate their own condition is progressively diminished. Joh 9:41 — the Pharisees' false evaluation of their own seeing (while the condition persists) shows the condition distorting self-moral-evaluation: they evaluate themselves as seeing when the condition has produced blindness. The condition's self-concealing nature (perceptive pattern — T3.1.3) is also a moral-evaluative concealment.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=14614, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that the universal sin-condition generates its own moral-evaluative concealment: the person under the condition evaluates themselves as not under it (Joh 9:41 — "we see"), or the condition makes them morally insensible to its operations (Heb 3:13 — hardening). The moral evaluation faculty's distortion by the condition is itself evidence of the condition's depth: a merely superficial moral failing would leave moral evaluation intact. The condition's universality extends to the distortion of the very faculty that should detect it.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=14615, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The conscience is engaged through the condition's relationship to moral recognition and guilt. Joh 9:41 — "your guilt remains" — the conscience's verdict of guilt persists in those who claim to see but remain in the condition; conscience is the faculty whose verdict the condition overrides through self-deception. Rom 4:8 — "the Lord will not count his sin" — the blessed condition of non-counting is what the conscience needs: the conscience knows the condition's reality (it generates guilt) but the divine non-counting addresses what the conscience has registered. Act 22:16 — "wash away your sins, calling on his name" — the washing is what the conscience requires for purification.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=14616, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *progressively numbs and hardens* the conscience. Heb 3:13 — "hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" — the deceitfulness of the condition produces a hardened heart whose conscience is progressively less capable of registering the condition's presence and severity. 1Ti 5:24 — "the sins of some people are conspicuous, going before them to judgment, but the sins of others appear later" — the variation in visibility suggests that some persons' consciences are more or less engaged with their condition's reality; those whose sins "appear later" have consciences that are not currently registering.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=14617, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The conscience pattern reveals that sin as universal condition has a self-concealing relationship to the conscience: the condition progressively hardens the conscience against its own recognition (Heb 3:13). The deepening of the condition's hold involves the progressive numbing of the very faculty (conscience) that would normally detect and resist it. This is why Heb 3:13 prescribes "exhort one another every day" as the counter-measure: daily mutual exhortation is required because the condition's deceitfulness continuously works to prevent the conscience from registering what it should. The external communal voice supplements the progressively numbed internal conscience.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=14624, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — God operates toward the person under the universal sin-condition in five distinct modes. (1) *Judicial non-forgiveness*: Joh 8:21/24 — "you will die in your sin... unless you believe that I am he you will die in your sins" — Jesus declares the eschatological consequence of the condition without remedy: dying in the condition produces final separation. (2) *Judicial condemnation of sin in the flesh*: Rom 8:3 — "he condemned sin in the flesh" — God acts judicially against the condition within the incarnation. (3) *Provision of purification*: Heb 1:3 — Christ making "purification for sins" — God provides the cleansing the condition requires. (4) *Declaration of forgiveness*: VCG-02 throughout — "your sins are forgiven" (Mat 9:2; Mar 2:5; Luk 5:20) — God through Jesus declares the condition's debt-dimension addressed. (5) *Spirit conviction*: Joh 16:8/9 — the Spirit convicts the world concerning the condition — God operates through the Spirit to make the condition known to those who cannot self-recognise it.

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=14625, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-11]** E — God's response rests on two foundations simultaneously. (1) *Covenantal faithfulness*: Rom 11:27 — "this will be my covenant with them when I take away their sins" — the removal of the condition is the content of God's covenantal promise; the basis is covenantal identity and promise. (2) *Holiness requiring propitiation*: Heb 2:17 — "to make propitiation for the sins of the people" — the condition creates a standing offence against God's holiness that requires propitiation; God's response (through Christ's provision) is calibrated to his own holiness. The basis is thus both covenantal (God's promise to take away sin) and moral (God's holiness that cannot absorb sin without propitiation).

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=14626, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — God's disposition is one of *engaged mercy within maintained holiness*. (1) *Mercy*: Mat 1:21 — "he will save his people from their sins" — God's purpose is salvific, not merely judicial; the mission of Jesus names mercy as God's disposition toward those in the condition; (2) *Maintained holiness*: Heb 5:1/3 — the persistent need for sacrifice for sins reveals that God's holiness does not simply absorb the condition — the condition requires addressed mediation; (3) *Patience*: 1Th 2:16 — the accumulated measure of sins that brings wrath "at last" implies that God's response has been withheld while the measure fills — patience is part of the disposition toward those in the condition.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=14627, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — God-to-human operation is extensively evidenced across all three VCG registers (T4.1.1–T4.1.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=14628, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition *blocks and reverses* the human-to-God movement. Joh 8:21 — "where I am going, you cannot come" — the condition creates an absolute barrier to the human person's movement toward where Jesus goes; Act 26:18 — "from the power of Satan to God" — the condition places the person in the domain opposite to God; Eph 2:1 — "dead in trespasses and sins" — spiritual death prevents movement toward God. The condition is constitutively a relational barrier: the movement toward God requires addressing the condition first (repentance — Mar 1:4; forgiveness — Luk 1:77; the Spirit's conviction — Joh 16:8).

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=14629, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-11]** E — VCG-02 establishes the required inner posture for movement toward God under the condition: (1) *Faith*: Mat 9:2; Mar 2:5 — "when Jesus saw their faith, he said... your sins are forgiven" — faith is the inner posture that enables the forgiveness-declaration to address the condition; Joh 8:24 — "unless you believe that I am he you will die in your sins" — belief in Jesus is the posture that breaks the condition's fatal hold; (2) *Repentance*: Mar 1:4; Luk 3:3; Act 3:19 — baptism of repentance for forgiveness — repentance is the inner turning required; (3) *Confession*: Mat 3:6; Mar 1:5 — "confessing their sins" — the inner act of acknowledging the condition is part of the required posture.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=14630, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition's blocking of the human-to-God movement reveals that the relationship between God and the sinful person is one of *created distance*: the condition has not eliminated God's claim on the person (he continues to pursue — Mat 1:21 — "save his people"; Luk 24:47 — gospel proclaimed to all nations) but has erected a barrier that only divine action can remove. Rom 5:12 — "death through sin... spread to all" — the death-barrier is universal; every person's movement toward God is under the condition's obstruction. The relationship is thus asymmetric: God continues to move toward the person (VCG-02 — forgiveness declared); the person cannot move toward God under the condition without the condition first being addressed.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=14631, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Human-to-God operation (in its blocked form and its restored-posture form) is extensively evidenced (T4.2.1–T4.2.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=14632, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — in several forms. (1) *The Adamic transmission*: Rom 5:12 — "sin came into the world through one man... death spread to all" — Adam's sin was extended to all humanity through the representative structure; (2) *Complicity*: 1Ti 5:22 — "nor take part in the sins of others" — sharing in another's sin is a form of extension; (3) *Targeted vulnerability*: 2Ti 3:6 — "capture weak women, burdened with sins" — the condition-burdened person's vulnerability is exploited by others extending the condition's reach; (4) *Intercession*: Act 7:60 — Stephen's dying prayer "do not hold this sin against them" — the sin of the persecutors extends to a level requiring intercessory forgiveness-appeal.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=14633, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Three enabling conditions. (1) *Representative position*: Rom 5:12 — Adam's extension of the condition to all humanity was possible through his representative position as head of humanity; (2) *Shared vulnerability*: 1Ti 5:22 — hasty endorsement makes one share in the other's sin-condition — the shared human vulnerability under the condition enables extension through complicity; (3) *Predatory exploitation*: 2Ti 3:6 — those who target the condition-burdened exploit the weakness the condition creates; the enticers' own formed character (burdened with sin, led by passions) enables them to extend the condition's reach.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=14634, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-11]** E — For the Adamic transmission (Rom 5:12), Adam's position as head of humanity was the prerequisite — the extension was made possible through representative identity, not merely personal sinning. For the complicity dimension (1Ti 5:22), the person must have made a hasty endorsement that aligns them with another's sin-condition. For the predatory exploitation (2Ti 3:6), the person must themselves be operating from within the condition's own corruption. In all cases: participation in the condition is the ground from which extension to others operates.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=14635, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — The giving/extension direction is evidenced (T4.3.1–T4.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=14636, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — the reception dimension is the condition's defining feature. (1) *Universal reception through Adam*: Rom 5:12 — "death spread to all men because all sinned" — the universal condition is received by all humanity through the Adamic representative structure; Joh 9:34 — "born in utter sin" — the condition is received at birth; (2) *Reception of forgiveness as the reversal*: VCG-02 throughout — "your sins are forgiven" (Mat 9:2) — the forgiveness is received by the person from Jesus; (3) *Reception of the Spirit's conviction*: Joh 16:8 — the Spirit convicts the world — the condition's recognition is received from the Spirit's external work.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=14637, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Reception-enabling conditions for the remedy: (1) *Faith*: Mat 9:2; Joh 8:24 — the faith-posture enables reception of forgiveness; the absence of faith means dying in the condition; (2) *Repentance and confession*: Mar 1:4; Mat 3:6 — the turning and acknowledging are enabling conditions for receiving forgiveness. Reception-blocking conditions: (1) *Unbelief*: Joh 16:9 — the Spirit convicts of the specific sin of not believing — unbelief blocks reception of the remedy; (2) *False perceptive certainty*: Joh 9:41 — "now that you say, 'We see,' your guilt remains" — claiming to have no condition is what blocks the remedy's reception.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=14638, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The received-remedy inner-being state is described across VCG-02 and VCG-03. (1) *Freed and reallocated*: Rom 6:18 — "set free from sin... become slaves of righteousness" — the inner state is liberation from the old master and re-allocation to a new one; (2) *Dead to sin and alive to God*: Rom 6:11 — "consider yourselves dead to sin and alive to God in Christ Jesus" — the inner state requires cognitive self-reckoning; (3) *Sins forgiven and covered*: Rom 4:7/8 — "whose sins are covered... against whom the Lord will not count his sin" — the relational standing is transformed; (4) *Spirit-life*: Rom 8:10 — "the Spirit is life because of righteousness" — the Spirit's indwelling is the new inner condition replacing the condition's hold.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=14639, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — The receiving direction is extensively evidenced — both reception of the condition (through Adam — T4.4.1) and reception of the remedy (VCG-02 — T4.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=14640, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes. (1) *Within the covenant community*: Rom 3:9 — "all, both Jews and Greeks, are under sin" — the condition operates without distinction within the covenant community (Jews) and outside it (Greeks); even the covenant relation does not exempt persons from the condition. Heb 5:3 — the high priest must sacrifice for his own sins alongside the people's — the condition operates within the most intimate covenant-service relationship. (2) *Across relational distance*: Luk 24:47 — "repentance for the forgiveness of sins should be proclaimed to all nations" — the remedy is extended across all relational and cultural distances; the condition's universality makes the remedy's scope equally universal.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=14641, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition crosses all covenantal boundaries. Rom 3:9 — "all, both Jews and Greeks, are under sin" — the foundational statement that the condition is not restricted to those outside the covenant; Rom 2:12 — "all who have sinned without the law will perish... all who have sinned under the law will be judged" — whether under the law (covenant) or not, the condition applies and produces judgment. The universal scope of the condition (Rom 3:23 — "all have sinned") is not qualified by covenantal membership.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=14642, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The relational scope of sin as universal condition is the widest possible: every human person without exception (Rom 3:23 — "all"), in every relational and covenantal context (Rom 3:9 — Jews and Greeks), from the beginning of humanity's existence (Rom 5:12 — through one man) to the present. The scope encompasses: individual moral subjects (Joh 9:34 — "born in utter sin"), communities (Mat 3:6 — "all the country of Judea"), nations (Luk 24:47 — "all nations"), and corporate humanity (Rom 5:12 — "all men"). No human relationship exists outside the scope of the condition.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=14643, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Relational scope is extensively evidenced as universally comprehensive (T4.5.1–T4.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=14644, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes. Act 26:18 — "from the power of Satan to God" — the condition places the person within Satan's power-domain; the condition's location is in the domain of the adversarial spiritual power. Gal 1:4 — "deliver us from the present evil age" — the condition is part of the "present evil age" that has a spiritual-power dimension. Eph 2:1 — "dead in trespasses and sins" is followed (in the broader Eph 2:2 context — not in the 5-verse corpus but structurally implied) by the reference to the prince of the power of the air; the condition's domain involves the adversarial spiritual power.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=14645, E) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes. Act 26:18 — "from the power of Satan to God" — the condition positions the person within Satan's power-domain; the condition is not merely a neutral moral deficit but is situated within an adversarial spiritual power structure. Gal 1:4 — "deliver us from the present evil age" — the evil age has spiritual-adversarial dimensions that the condition is embedded in. The remedy (forgiveness and liberation) is framed as deliverance from this adversarial domain, confirming the condition's location within it.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=14646, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-11]** S — The 112-verse corpus does not evidence angelic ministry as a medium for communicating, strengthening, or mediating the universal sin-condition. The condition's transmission is through the Adamic representative structure (Rom 5:12) and the person's own sinning (Rom 3:23); angelic agents are not named as mediators of the condition.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=14647, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — The adversarial spiritual beings interface is evidenced (Act 26:18 — Satan's power domain; Gal 1:4 — present evil age). T4.6.3 is S (angelic mediation not evidenced). The condition is situated within the adversarial spiritual power domain but is transmitted through human-representative and human-sinning mechanisms, not through angelic agents. Noted.

---

T4 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T4.1.1–T4.1.4, T4.2.1–T4.2.4, T4.3.1–T4.3.4, T4.4.1–T4.4.4, T4.5.1–T4.5.4, T4.6.1–T4.6.4)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-11] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T4.6.3 is S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Mat 1:21/3:6/9:2; Mar 1:4/2:5; Luk 1:77/3:3/5:20/24:47; Joh 8:21/24/16:8/9; Act 3:19/7:60/26:18; Rom 2:12/3:9/23/4:7/8/5:12/6:11/18; Heb 1:3/2:17/5:1/3; 1Th 2:16; 1Ti 5:22; 2Ti 3:6; Gal 1:4; Eph 2:1 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=14648, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Both — in the negative direction and in its reversal. (1) *Condition change — negative*: the condition itself is the primary transformation from the created state: Rom 5:12 — "sin came into the world... and death through sin" — the entry of the condition transformed every person's condition from life-before-God to death-under-sin; Eph 2:1 — "dead in trespasses and sins" names the transformed condition. (2) *Orientation change — negative*: Act 26:18 — "from the power of Satan to God" names two opposed orientational domains; the condition places the person in the Satan-domain orientation rather than the God-domain; Rom 6:20 — "free in regard to righteousness" — the condition transforms the person's orientation so that righteousness is foreclosed. (3) *Positive transformation through remedy*: Rom 6:18 — "set free from sin... become slaves of righteousness" — the remedy reverses both condition (freed) and orientation (toward righteousness).

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=14649, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Conditionally reversible with one absolute irreversible exception. (1) *Generally reversible*: VCG-02's entire 39-verse register evidences that the condition's debt-dimension is reversible: "your sins are forgiven" (Mat 9:2); "blotted out" (Act 3:19); "take away their sins" (Rom 11:27). The condition can be addressed through Christ's atoning work and faith-repentance response. (2) *Irreversible exception*: Mat 12:31 — "the blasphemy against the Spirit will not be forgiven" — this specific form of engagement with the condition crosses a threshold that forgiveness cannot reach. (3) *Ultimate eschatological irreversibility*: Joh 8:21/24 — "you will die in your sins" — for those who remain in the condition without receiving the remedy, the final death in the condition is eschatologically irreversible; the condition's ultimate consequence (eternal separation from where Jesus goes) is final.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=14650, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Transformation evidence is extensive and multi-directional (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=14651, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — the corpus evidences both the sequence of the condition's deepening and the sequence of its reversal. (1) *Deepening sequence*: Rom 5:12 — sin enters (through one man) → death through sin → death spreads to all (the cosmic → universal sequence); Rom 7:8/9 — commandment arrives → sin seizes the opportunity → sin comes alive → the person dies (the law-encounter sequence); Heb 3:13 — deceitfulness of sin → hardening of the heart (the progressive-hardening sequence). (2) *Reversal sequence*: Mar 1:4; Act 3:19 — hearing → repentance → forgiveness → blotting out (the gospel-reception sequence); Rom 6:11 — reckoning oneself dead to sin → alive to God (the cognitive-volitional re-orientation sequence); Act 26:18 — eyes opened → turning from darkness → turning from Satan → receiving forgiveness → sanctification (the comprehensive conversion sequence).

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=14652, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The deepening sequence (sin-entry → death → universal spread; or law-arrival → sin-activation → inner death) reveals that the condition operates through definite stages that amplify its hold. The reversal sequence (hearing → repentance → forgiveness; or reckoning → freedom → sanctification → eternal life — Rom 6:22) reveals that the condition's reversal also moves through stages: the condition is not removed instantaneously in all its dimensions but progressively — forgiveness (VCG-02) addresses the debt; freedom from enslavement (VCG-03) addresses the power-dominion; sanctification (Rom 6:22) addresses the ongoing formation toward likeness.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=14653, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Sequence is extensively evidenced in both deepening and reversal directions (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=14654, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Three distinct mechanisms across the VCG registers. (1) *Federal transmission*: Rom 5:12/16 — the condition entered and spreads through representative-headship (one man's sin → judgment for all); the mechanism is corporate-representational, not individual accumulation. (2) *Law-exploitation*: Rom 7:8/9 — the condition seizes the commandment as an opportunity to produce desire and death; the mechanism uses the good law as the instrument of the condition's arousal within the person. (3) *Enslavement through obedience*: Rom 6:16 — "whoever you obey, you are slaves of" — the condition's enslavement mechanism is the person's own act of obedience to sin; each obedient act of sinning deepens the enslavement.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=14655, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes. The federal transmission mechanism (Rom 5:12) operates at the cosmic-historical origin, not in individual experience. The law-exploitation mechanism (Rom 7:8/9) operates specifically in the context of having the law — it is the condition's mechanism within the law-possessing community. The enslavement-through-obedience mechanism (Rom 6:16) operates at the individual level through concrete acts of yielding. These three mechanisms are contextually distinct: cosmic-historical, law-contextual, and individual-volitional — representing different levels at which the condition produces and deepens its hold.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=14656, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Mechanisms are extensively evidenced across three distinct contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=14657, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes. Rom 8:10 — "the body is dead because of sin" — physical suffering and mortality are the condition's consequence in the body; the body's deadness (decay, mortality) is the form that the universal condition's consequence takes in the physical dimension. 2Ti 3:6 — "burdened with sins and led astray by various passions" — the burdened state of the condition-carrier is a form of inner affliction; the condition weighs on the person. Heb 5:1 — the high priest's ongoing sacrifice for sins reveals the condition as a persistent burden requiring regular attention — the condition is not a one-time affliction but a sustained form of need.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=14658, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The corpus does not evidence suffering producing the condition (the condition precedes suffering as its cause — Rom 5:12 — death through sin). However, suffering *reveals* the condition's consequences: Rom 8:10 — "the body is dead because of sin" — the body's mortality is the suffering-dimension that reveals the condition's terminal consequence. The condition is the cause; suffering (mortality, burden) is the effect. The causal direction is condition → suffering, not suffering → condition.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=14659, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition as cause of suffering (mortality — Rom 8:10; burden — 2Ti 3:6) is evidenced. Suffering as cause of the condition is not evidenced in the corpus. Noted.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=14660, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — in both the negative and positive formation arcs. (1) *Negative formation*: Heb 3:13 — "hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" — the condition progressively forms the heart toward hardness against God through its deceitfulness; 1Th 2:16 — "filling up the measure of their sins" — the condition accumulates toward a full measure as the formation arc progresses toward its judgment endpoint. (2) *Positive formation through liberation*: Rom 6:22 — "set free from sin... the fruit you get leads to sanctification and its end, eternal life" — liberation from the condition is the precondition for the positive formation arc toward sanctification; the condition-reversal is what enables the sanctification-formation to begin.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=14661, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The condition's role in the formation arc is as the *foundational negative baseline* from which all other formation (positive or negative) operates. (1) *Negative arc baseline*: the condition is the starting point; every human person's formation begins from within the condition (Joh 9:34 — born in utter sin; Eph 2:1 — dead in trespasses and sins); all negative formation deepens the condition's hold (hardening — Heb 3:13). (2) *Positive arc precondition*: the condition must be addressed before positive formation (sanctification) can begin — Rom 6:22 frames liberation from sin as the precondition for sanctification-fruit; the condition is the barrier the positive formation arc must first clear.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=14662, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Formation evidence is present (T5.5.1 and T5.5.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=14663, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — toward two opposed eschatological endpoints. (1) *Judgment for those remaining in the condition*: Joh 8:21/24 — "you will die in your sin... you will die in your sins" — the eschatological consequence of remaining in the condition is death-in-the-condition (permanent separation from where Jesus goes); Mal 4:1 — "all evildoers will be stubble. The day that is coming shall set them ablaze" — the condition's wickedness-register (VCG-01) faces the Day of the Lord's consuming fire. 1Th 2:16 — "wrath has come upon them at last" — accumulated sin reaches its full measure and triggers final judgment. (2) *Eternal life for those freed from the condition*: Rom 6:22 — "the fruit you get leads to sanctification and its end, eternal life" — liberation from the condition is oriented toward eternal life as the eschatological fullness of the positive direction.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=14664, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The present experience of sin as universal condition anticipates two contrasted futures. For those remaining in it: Joh 8:24 — "you will die in your sins" — the present condition of being-in-sin anticipates the future of dying-in-sin as the condition's full consequence; 1Th 2:16 — the present accumulation of the measure anticipates the full measure that triggers judgment. For those freed from it: Rom 6:22 — the present fruit of sanctification anticipates eternal life as the condition's positive future; 1Cor 15:17 — "you are still in your sins" without the resurrection — conversely, the resurrection grounds the future hope of being freed from the condition permanently.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=14665, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Eschatological trajectory is extensively evidenced in both directions (T5.6.1 and T5.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=14666, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-11]** E — T2.8 identified four deposit dimensions: death, hardening, indwelling sin, accumulating measure. The developmental consequences of each. (1) *Death deposit*: Rom 6:23 — "the wages of sin is death" — the ultimate developmental consequence is physical and spiritual death; Rom 8:10 — "the body is dead because of sin" — the body's mortality is the ongoing developmental consequence of the death-deposit. (2) *Hardening deposit*: Heb 3:13 — the deceitfulness-produced hardening progressively closes the heart against correction, making the condition increasingly entrenched with each cycle. (3) *Indwelling-sin deposit*: Rom 7:17/20 — "sin that dwells within me" — the ongoing developmental consequence is the divided-person experience where the indwelling force continuously overrides the will's intent. (4) *Accumulating-measure deposit*: 1Th 2:16 — the progressive accumulation toward the full measure means the developmental consequence is wrath-at-last; the deposit grows until it triggers the final judicial response.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=14667, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — the most extensively evidenced generational consequence in the M10 corpus. Rom 5:12 — "sin came into the world through one man, and death through sin, and so death spread to all men" — the Adamic transmission is the paradigm of generational consequence: the condition entered through one man and spread to all subsequent generations of humanity. Joh 9:34 — "born in utter sin" — the birth-framing confirms that the condition is generationally inherited, received at birth from the prior generation's participation in the Adamic condition. The generational consequence is universal (all humanity, all generations) and foundational to the characteristic's definition.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=14668, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-11]** E — T2.8 found the most extensive deposit evidence in the M10 corpus. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---

T5 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       21/21 ✓ (T5.1.1–T5.1.3, T5.2.1–T5.2.3, T5.3.1–T5.3.3, T5.4.1–T5.4.3, T5.5.1–T5.5.3, T5.6.1–T5.6.3, T5.7.1–T5.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       21× [CHAR-11] ✓

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=14669, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Four adjacent characteristics appear consistently within or proximate to CHAR-11's corpus. (1) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: VCG-02's confession-at-baptism passages (Mat 3:6; Mar 1:5) are the human response to the condition; confession is the characteristic that addresses the condition's debt-dimension through acknowledgment. (2) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: the condition and guilt are closely related — Rom 4:8 ("count his sin"), Joh 9:41 ("your guilt remains") — the condition produces guilt as its inner-being experiential dimension. (3) *CHAR-12 (Sin as enslaving power)*: VCG-03's enslaving-power language (Rom 6 — slavery/mastery; Rom 7 — indwelling force) overlaps extensively with CHAR-12's territory; CHAR-11 and CHAR-12 share much of the Romans 6-7 evidence base from different analytical angles. (4) *CHAR-16 (The sinner as moral character)*: the universal condition is what makes every person a "sinner" in the CHAR-16 sense — the condition is the ground of the sinner-identity.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=14670, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-11 as the *foundational ground-condition* of the M10 landscape: it is the structural reality upon which all other sinning characteristics rest. Every other sinning characteristic (CHAR-1 through CHAR-10, CHAR-13 through CHAR-22) is either a specific expression of CHAR-11 (specific acts, patterns, or mechanisms through which the universal condition manifests), a consequence of CHAR-11 (guilt — CHAR-17; iniquity as accumulated — CHAR-18), or its antidote (confession — CHAR-3; forgiveness sought — CHAR-14). CHAR-11 is not one characteristic among peers but the bedrock condition that gives all other M10 characteristics their moral weight and significance.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=14671, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are extensively evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=14672, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-11]** E — CHAR-11 consistently *precedes* all other M10 sinning characteristics — it is the structural baseline from which specific sinning characteristics emerge. Rom 5:12 — "sin came into the world" — the universal condition enters first, and all specific acts (CHAR-1 through CHAR-10) occur within and from this universal condition. CHAR-11 also consistently *accompanies* VCG-02 (forgiveness received — CHAR-14): the condition is always the presupposition of the forgiveness-proclamation in VCG-02; CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought) cannot be understood without CHAR-11 (the condition that requires forgiveness).

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=14673, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The relationship is *causal and foundational*. CHAR-11 causally produces the conditions in which all other M10 characteristics arise: the universal sin-condition is what makes wilful sinning (CHAR-1) morally weighty (because it occurs within the context of a creature-God relationship already ruptured by the universal condition); the universal condition is what makes confession (CHAR-3) necessary (because the condition creates the debt that confession acknowledges). The relationship between CHAR-11 and the other M10 characteristics is foundational-causal: CHAR-11 is the ground; the other characteristics are the specific expressions and consequences of that ground.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=14674, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Sequential patterns (foundational-causal precedence) are evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=14675, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes — CHAR-11 produces multiple characteristics. (1) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: Joh 9:41 — "your guilt remains" — the universal condition produces the inner experience of guilt as its subjective correlate; (2) *CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character)*: the universal condition constitutes every person as a sinner — Rom 3:23 — "all have sinned" — the universal sinning is what makes every person a "sinner" by condition; (3) *CHAR-12 (Sin as enslaving power)*: the universal condition in its VCG-03 form produces the enslaving power experience — Rom 6:12-23 — the master-slave relationship; (4) *Physical death*: Rom 6:23 — "the wages of sin is death" — the condition's final consequence is the physical death it produces.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=14676, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-11]** E — CHAR-11 is produced by the Adamic representational act (Rom 5:12 — "sin came into the world through one man") — which is CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning) in its singular origin form. Adam's wilful sinning (CHAR-1 in the unique form of the representative head's deliberate transgression of a specific command) produced the universal condition (CHAR-11) that all subsequent humanity inherits. CHAR-11 is thus produced by CHAR-1 in a specific historical-representative context, though this is not the ordinary pattern (most CHAR-1 acts do not produce CHAR-11 for others).

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=14677, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-11]** E — CHAR-11 is the *foundational constituent* of the entire M10 cluster: every other sinning characteristic presupposes the universal condition as the moral-anthropological ground within which they operate. CHAR-1 (wilful sinning) occurs within CHAR-11; CHAR-5 (refusal to repent) presupposes CHAR-11 as the condition of sin from which departure would be needed; CHAR-8 (political revolt) carries the moral weight of transgression because all transgression ultimately occurs within the universal condition. Within CHAR-11, VCG-01 (wickedness-as-character), VCG-02 (redemption register), and VCG-03 (enslaving power) are its three constituent analytical registers.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=14678, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Extensive causal and constitutive relationships are evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=14679, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes. Hamartia (G0266) is the dominant vocabulary shared across M10: CHAR-1/2/3/4/5/6/9 all use hamartia or hamartanō in their corpora. The distinction between CHAR-11 and these characteristics is not lexical (same root) but grammatical-conceptual: CHAR-11 uses hamartia in the condition-register (sin as noun-power: "under sin," "sin reigned," "sin that dwells") while CHAR-1 uses it in the act-register (sinning as discrete event). Paraptōma (G3900) appears in both CHAR-11 (VCG-02 — Eph 2:1/2:5; Rom 4:25; 11:11/12) and in its own CHAR-19 register (transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing). Rish.ah (H7564 — VCG-01) is unique to CHAR-11 within M10.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=14680, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Yes. H2403B (chat.tat — Gen 4:7) shares the cha.ta root (H2398) that is the dominant root across CHAR-1 through CHAR-8. CHAR-11's OT corpus thus connects to the same root-architecture as the other M10 sinning characteristics but in its noun-form chat.tat (the sin-condition) rather than the verb-form cha.ta (the sinning act). The NT hamartia/hamartanō family similarly spans CHAR-11 (condition register) and CHAR-1/2/4/5/6 (act registers). The root architecture is shared; the condition/act distinction is the conceptual differentiation within the shared root.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=14681, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The vocabulary sharing (same roots; condition vs act grammatical differentiation) reveals that CHAR-11 is not conceptually separate from the other M10 sinning characteristics but is the *condition-register* of the same semantic field. Sin (hamartia/cha.ta) names both the condition (CHAR-11) and the specific acts (CHAR-1 through CHAR-10): the characteristic is distinguished not by having a different vocabulary but by deploying the shared vocabulary in its noun-power-condition mode rather than its verb-act mode. This confirms CHAR-11's position as the foundational register of the M10 cluster's vocabulary.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=14682, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Extensive vocabulary sharing (hamartia/hamartanō across M10; chat.tat/cha.ta root) is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=14683, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-11]** E — CHAR-12 (Sin as enslaving power) most closely resembles CHAR-11 — both address sin as a structural condition rather than a specific act, and both draw heavily from the Romans 5-8 evidence base. The precise distinction: CHAR-11 is the *universal condition* under which all persons stand — the structural state of being-under-sin and falling-short-of-glory (Rom 3:9/23 — "all are under sin... all have sinned"); CHAR-12 focuses specifically on the *enslaving power-dimension* of sin — sin as the master/tyrant that holds the person captive. CHAR-11 is the broader category (universal scope, all registers — VCG-01/02/03); CHAR-12 is the enslaving-power register specifically.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=14684, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The overlap is densest in VCG-03's Romans 6-7 corpus — passages like Rom 6:6 ("the body of sin"), Rom 6:23 ("wages of sin is death"), Rom 7:17 ("sin that dwells within me") all appear in CHAR-11's VCG-03. The precise boundary: VCG-03 is classified within CHAR-11 because it evidences sin operating as the universal structural condition in its power-form; CHAR-12 takes the enslaving-power dimension as its defining register. Where CHAR-11 addresses the universal scope and multiple registers of the condition, CHAR-12 narrows to the enslaving-power mode specifically. The boundary is register-scope rather than verse-assignment.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=14685, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The distinction is one of *scope and register*: CHAR-11 is the broadest characteristic in M10 (all persons, all registers — wickedness/debt/power); CHAR-12 focuses on the power/enslavement register specifically. The distinction is not of degree (more or less conditioned) or constitutional level (both address the deepest inner-being state) but of the *analytical scope and register*: CHAR-11 asks "what is the universal condition all persons stand in?"; CHAR-12 asks "what is the nature of sin's enslaving power specifically?"

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=14686, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=14687, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-11]** E — Several verses function as primary anchors across multiple characteristics. Rom 3:23 — "all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God" — is the definitive anchor for CHAR-11 (universal scope + glory-gap definition) and would anchor any M10 discussion of universality. Gen 4:7 — "sin is crouching at the door. Its desire is for you, and you must rule over it" — anchors both CHAR-11 (sin as power crouching) and CHAR-12 (enslaving power specifically). Rom 5:12 — "sin came into the world through one man" — anchors both CHAR-11 (the universal condition's entry) and CHAR-15 (Generational sin — the transmission across humanity). Mat 9:2 (parallel in Mar 2:5; Luk 5:20) — "your sins are forgiven" — anchors both CHAR-11 (the condition's debt addressed) and CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received).

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=14688, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The shared anchors (Gen 4:7 with CHAR-12; Rom 5:12 with CHAR-15; Mat 9:2 with CHAR-14) reveal that CHAR-11 is the *hub characteristic* of M10: its verses are the primary sources from which multiple other characteristics draw their anchoring evidence. This confirms what T6.1.2 established: CHAR-11 is the foundational condition; other characteristics are specific registers or consequences of that condition. The shared anchors are the points where the universal ground-condition intersects with specific aspects of that condition (its power-form, its transmission, its remedy).

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=14689, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified across several characteristics (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=14690, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The primary dimension (universal structural condition) is unique to CHAR-11 in its full scope — no other M10 characteristic addresses the structural condition of all persons. The sub-dimensions of CHAR-11 are shared across the cluster: the debt/guilt dimension (with CHAR-17 — Guilt as inner-being state), the power/enslavement dimension (with CHAR-12 — Sin as enslaving power), the wickedness-as-character dimension (with CHAR-16 — Sinner as moral character), the forgiveness-remedy dimension (with CHAR-14 — Forgiveness sought and received), the generational-transmission dimension (with CHAR-15 — Generational sin). CHAR-11 shares a dimension with every major sin-related characteristic in the cluster — because the universal condition encompasses every specific dimension.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=14691, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-11]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-11's unique position: it is the only characteristic that shares dimensions with every other sinning characteristic in M10. This is because CHAR-11 is not a peer-characteristic alongside others but the ground-condition that encompasses them. The other characteristics each focus on one dimension of the universal condition (CHAR-12 — its power-dimension; CHAR-17 — its guilt-dimension; CHAR-15 — its generational-dimension); CHAR-11 encompasses all dimensions simultaneously. The dimensional sharing reveals CHAR-11 as the M10 characteristic's structural foundation rather than one of its members.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=14692, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-11]** E — Dimensional sharing is extensively evidenced (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T7.
