## CHAR-13 — Sin as divine record (189 rows: E=184 S=5 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=14902, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The 205-verse corpus reveals God's character across five dimensions. (1) *God as the one who sees and records*: Exo 32:33 — "whoever has sinned against me, I will blot out of my book" — God maintains a divine register; Gen 18:20 — "the outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is great and their sin is very grave" — the sin has reached God as a measurable, audible reality. (2) *God as the one who remembers sin precisely*: Jer 14:10 — "he will remember their iniquity and punish their sins"; Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store" — the divine memory is reliable and permanent. (3) *God as the one who searches for sin*: Job 10:6 — "that you seek out my iniquity and search for my sin" (VCG-03) — God actively investigates and scrutinises the person. (4) *God as the one who blots out the record*: Isa 43:25 — "I, I am he who blots out your transgressions for my own sake, and I will not remember your sins" — God can erase the divine record as a sovereign act. (5) *God as the one who bears the burden of his people's sin*: Isa 43:24 — "you have burdened me with your sins; you have wearied me with your iniquities" — the accumulated record is experienced by God as a burden on the divine-human relationship.

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=14903, E) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Partially — in the positive keeping-record mode. God is the keeper of the book from which sinners are blotted (Exo 32:32/33) and the one who searches for sin (Job 10:6; 14:16 — VCG-03). God maintains the record; he does not bear the sinful-record status. Exo 32:33 — "whoever has sinned against me, I will blot out of my book" — God is the author of the record, not a participant in the condition recorded in it. The characteristic thus reveals God as the moral-record keeper rather than as the one who carries a sin-record before another.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=14904, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-13]** E — God does not carry a sin-record before anyone else. He is the moral record-keeper, the one before whom the human sin-record exists. The silence on God possessing the record-as-burden reveals: (1) the characteristic is constitutively relational — it is the condition of a creature standing before a perfectly righteous Creator whose memory is complete; (2) Isa 43:24 — "you have burdened me with your sins" — God is burdened by human sin but as the one receiving the offense, not as the one who has an offense-record of his own. The asymmetry is structural: God keeps the record; the creature is the subject of it.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=14905, S) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-13]** S — The corpus provides no evidence of any positive created purpose for sin as divine record. The characteristic is entirely fallen-condition: it is the record of offenses against God that accumulates in the post-fall human condition. No verse presents the divine-sin-record as a designed feature of the created person; it presupposes the fall.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=14906, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Entirely a response to the fallen condition. Psa 51:5 — "in sin did my mother conceive me" (VCG-01) — the record's origin is in the condition inherited from birth; it is not a creation-design feature. Exo 32:32/33 — the divine book from which sinners are blotted presupposes sin's prior entry into the world; the record exists because sin does. The Jeroboam-sin legacy (VCG-04, 46 verses) shows sin as a transmissible recorded pattern accumulating across generations — entirely post-fall dynamics.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=14907, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — in two eschatological directions. (1) *Positive — the new-covenant promise of divine forgetting*: Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the eschatological fullness of the new covenant is the permanent removal of the sin-record from divine memory; (2) *Final divine reckoning*: Exo 32:34 — "in the day when I visit, I will visit their sin upon them" — the deferred record has an eschatological appointment of judgment. Dan 9:24 — "seventy weeks are decreed... to put an end to sin" — the divine record of sin has an eschatological terminus in which it will be completely resolved.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=14908, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The divine image's moral accountability before God is the constitutive dimension of CHAR-13: only an image-bearer who stands in moral relationship with God can have a sin-record before God. Animals do not carry a divine sin-record. Psa 51:5 — "in sin did my mother conceive me" — the conceived state is that of an image-bearer accountable to God. The image-bearer's creation for moral relationship with God is what makes the divine-record characteristic possible: the record exists because the person stands before God as a morally accountable subject.

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=14909, E) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Exclusively creaturely. God is the keeper of the record, not its subject. Exo 32:33 — "whoever has sinned against me, I will blot out of my book" — God is the record-keeper; the humans are the entries. Amo 5:12 — "for I know how many are your transgressions" — God knows the record; he does not have one. The asymmetry is absolute: God's perfect moral record is the standard against which the human's sin-record is measured (Rom 3:23 in CHAR-11 — falling short of the glory of God).

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=14910, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The presence of a sin-record reveals the image in its primary condition of fallen moral accountability. Jer 17:1 — "the sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of diamond it is engraved on the tablet of their heart" (VCG-02) — the record is not external only but engraved internally on the heart; the image-bearer carries the record within as well as before God. Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you" — the divine record is the relational barrier; the image designed for God-relationship now stands behind a wall of recorded sin. The depth of the record's internal engraving (Jer 17:1 — iron pen, diamond point) reveals how thoroughly the fallen condition has shaped the image from within.

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=14911, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. (1) *Day of Atonement typology*: Lev 16:21 — "Aaron shall lay both his hands on the head of the live goat, and confess over it all the iniquities of the people of Israel, and all their transgressions, all their sins. And he shall put them on the head of the goat and send it away into the wilderness" — the annual ritual of sin-record being placed on the scapegoat and sent to the wilderness typologically points toward the Lamb of God (Joh 1:29 — "who takes away the sin of the world"). (2) *Moses's divine-book intercession*: Exo 32:32 — Moses's offer to be blotted from God's book if the people's sin is not forgiven typologically prefigures Christ's bearing the sin-record of the people.

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=14912, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The direction is from human-institution type to divine-act antitype. The Day of Atonement ritual (Lev 16:21 — scapegoat bearing the confession of sins and sent to the wilderness) points forward to the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world (Joh 1:29). The type is the annual human ritual that temporarily displaced the sin-record; the antitype is the once-for-all divine act that permanently removes it (Dan 9:24 — "to put an end to sin"; Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more").

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=14913, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Typological use is evidenced (T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T1.


---

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=14914, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Sin as divine record." Three signals. (1) *Sin*: the moral reality being tracked is sin — not a neutral human limitation but moral offense against God. (2) *Divine record*: the characteristic is not the subjective experience of guilt (which would be CHAR-17) but the objective moral weight existing before God — whether the person feels it or not. The record exists independently of the person's awareness. (3) *Record*: the metaphor signals documentary retention — Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store"; Exo 32:33 — "I will blot out of my book"; Jer 17:1 — "written with a pen of iron... engraved on the tablet of their heart." The name thus signals: an objective, retained, divine-witnessed moral account that is not destroyed by the person's ignorance, self-deception, or concealment from others.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=14915, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Five term-groups across the 205 verses. (1) **chat.tat (H2403B)** — the dominant term (VCG-05 — 113 verses): sin as moral fact, wrong committed, the act that enters the record. The noun form names the sin-entity as a reality requiring priestly atonement (Lev 4:23/26/28/35) or divine forgiveness. (2) **cha.ta.ah (H2401/H2403A)** — sin as objective moral category requiring covering (VCG-02 — 9 verses): Psa 32:1 — "whose sin is covered"; Jer 17:1 — "sin of Judah is written... engraved on the tablet of their heart." (3) **chet (H2399)** — personal moral liability (VCG-01 — 32 verses): Num 9:13 — "that man shall bear his sin"; Deu 24:16 — "each one shall be put to death for his own sin." The bearer-of-sin framing is distinctive: chet names the personal carrying of moral liability. (4) **cha.ta (H2398)** — the verb (numerous VCG-05 verses): "whoever has sinned against me" (Exo 32:33) — the act of sinning that creates the record entry. (5) **hamartia/hamartēma (G0266/G0265)** — NT terms: Joh 1:29 — "the sin of the world" as a collective burden lifted by the Lamb; Rom 3:25 — "former sins" passed over in divine forbearance.

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=14916, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-13]** E — All three. *Directional*: the record accumulates in one direction — from the person upward toward God; Isa 59:12 — "our sins testify against us" — the testimony runs from the person toward divine judgment. *Relational*: the record is constitutively relational — it exists before God (Lev 4:26 — "the priest shall make atonement for him for his sin, and he shall be forgiven" — the atonement addresses a relational deficit). *Constitutional*: Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the record is also internally inscribed; Psa 51:5 — "in sin did my mother conceive me" — the record begins constitutionally, at birth. The directional, relational, and constitutional dimensions are all evidenced in the 205-verse corpus.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=14917, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The corpus reveals CHAR-13 as a *standing objective moral condition* — not primarily an act (CHAR-1) or a feeling (CHAR-17) but the accumulated moral weight that sits before God as a fact. Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store" — the record is a stored reality; Exo 32:34 — "in the day when I visit, I will visit their sin upon them" — the record is a deferred moral account. The characteristic operates at the objective rather than subjective level: the record exists whether the person feels its weight or not (definition: "the person carries this record whether they feel it or not"). It is thus a *condition of standing* before God rather than an inner experience.

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=14918, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The corpus reveals four constituent elements. (1) *Individual liability*: chet (VCG-01) — "each one shall be put to death for his own sin" (Deu 24:16) — personal accountable record; (2) *Collective/cumulative dimension*: 1Sa 12:19 — "we have added to all our sins this evil" — the record accumulates across acts and across the community; (3) *Named institutional pattern*: the Jeroboam-sin legacy (VCG-04) — 46 verses of a named recorded sin-pattern transmitted across generations; (4) *Divine-surveillance register*: VCG-03 — Job's testimony of God actively searching and watching for sin (Job 10:6; 14:16) — the record is under active divine monitoring.

---

#### T1.2.3 (cf=14919, E) — What is the current best working description of this characteristic — encapsulating its constitutional location, the faculties it primarily engages, and its impact on the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Working description: *Sin as divine record (CHAR-13) is the standing objective moral condition in which every human person's sin exists before God as a retained, accumulated account — recorded, named, not hidden from divine memory, and not erased by the person's ignorance or concealment from others. The corpus evidences five sub-registers: (1) Individual moral liability (chet — VCG-01): each person bears their own sin-record; (2) Broad chat.tat record (VCG-05 — 113 verses): sin as an act that creates a moral fact requiring priestly resolution; (3) Cha.ta.ah as objective moral category requiring covering (VCG-02): the record requires a covering act; (4) The Jeroboam-sin legacy (VCG-04 — 46 verses): a named institutional sin-pattern transmitted across royal generations as a defined recorded legacy; (5) The Job surveillance register (VCG-03): God actively searching and monitoring for sin as a divine investigator. The characteristic's definitive image is Jer 17:1 — "the sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of diamond it is engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the record is both divinely maintained and internally inscribed.*

---

#### T1.3.1 (cf=14920, E) — What is the structural opposite of this characteristic — the inner-being reality that stands against or excludes it?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The structural opposite is *sin forgiven and removed from divine memory*. Multiple metaphors name it: (1) *Blotting out*: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins"; Eze 33:16 — "none of the sins that he has committed shall be remembered against him"; (2) *Casting behind the back*: Isa 38:17 — "you have cast all my sins behind your back" — removed from the divine sight; (3) *Casting into the sea*: Mic 7:19 — "you will cast all our sins into the depths of the sea" — the record disposed of permanently; (4) *Covering*: Psa 85:2 — "you covered all their sin" — the record concealed from divine judgment; (5) *The new-covenant promise*: Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the ultimate eschatological opposite of the divine record.

---

#### T1.3.2 (cf=14921, E) — What does this characteristic explicitly exclude or resist?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The divine record explicitly excludes: (1) *Concealment from God*: Num 32:23 — "your sin will find you out" — the record resists the person's attempt to hide it; Job 10:14 — "if I sin, you watch me and do not acquit me" — the record precludes acquittal through divine surveillance; (2) *Transfer of liability to another*: Deu 24:16 — "each one shall be put to death for his own sin" — the record resists being assigned to someone else; (3) *Self-exculpation through prosperity*: Hos 12:8 — "in all my labors they cannot find in me iniquity or sin" — Ephraim's wealth-based self-exculpation is explicitly unfounded; the record persists regardless of material standing.

---

#### T1.3.3 (cf=14922, E) — What is this characteristic not — where does it end and something else begin?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Three boundaries. (1) *Not CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: CHAR-13 is the objective record before God — the moral fact existing independently of the person's subjective feeling; CHAR-17 is the inner experience of guilt as a felt burden. The record exists whether felt or not; guilt (CHAR-17) is the subjective experience of the record. (2) *Not CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: CHAR-1 is the discrete act of sinning; CHAR-13 is what that act becomes once recorded — the accumulated moral account. (3) *Not CHAR-11 (Sin as universal condition)*: CHAR-11 is the structural condition all persons stand in; CHAR-13 is the specific accumulated record of particular acts within that condition.

---

#### T1.4.1 (cf=14923, E) — In what distinct ways does this characteristic operate within the inner person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Four distinct inner modes. (1) *As an internal inscription*: Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the record operates internally, inscribed on the heart's structure; (2) *As a persistent inner accusation*: Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" (VCG-02) — the record operates as an inner presence that occupies conscience continually; (3) *As a weight producing physical effects*: Psa 38:3 — "there is no health in my bones because of my sin" — the record operates on the physical body through its moral weight; (4) *As an invisible moral account affecting life-quality*: Jer 5:25 — "your sins have kept good from you" — the record operates by blocking divine blessing without the person necessarily knowing the mechanism.

---

#### T1.4.2 (cf=14924, E) — Does this characteristic operate differently depending on context, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. (1) *Individual context*: chet (VCG-01) — each person bears their own sin personally; Deu 24:16 — individual personal liability; (2) *Corporate-national context*: VCG-04 Jeroboam-sin legacy — the record operates at the national level, accumulated across royal generations, with the whole people affected by the leader's named sin-pattern; (3) *Divine-surveillance context*: VCG-03 — Job perceives God as actively searching for and watching his sin, creating a condition of personal surveillance; (4) *Covenantal context*: VCG-05 sevenfold discipline passages (Lev 26:18/21/24/28) — the record triggers covenant consequences at specific thresholds.

---

#### T1.4.3 (cf=14925, E) — Does this characteristic have a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — and if so, how does it function?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — in two registers. (1) *Prophetic declaration of the record*: Isa 58:1 — "declare to my people their transgression, to the house of Jacob their sins" — the prophet's speech makes the record known to those who have suppressed it; Mic 3:8 — "to declare to Jacob his transgression and to Israel his sin" — the Spirit-empowered prophet is specifically commissioned to voice the record. (2) *Testifying record*: Isa 59:12 — "our sins testify against us" — the record itself speaks as a witness against the person in a judicial-communicative mode.

---

#### T1.5.1 (cf=14926, E) — What is the first or most immediate inner-being response to receiving or encountering this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Multiple response patterns across the corpus. (1) *Confession*: Num 5:7 — "he shall confess his sin that he has committed" — the encounter with the record that "is made known" (Lev 4:23/28) triggers the confession-response; Psa 32:5 — "I acknowledged my sin to you... I said, 'I will confess my transgressions to the Lord'" — the immediate response to facing the record is acknowledgment. (2) *Sorrow*: Psa 38:18 — "I am sorry for my sin" — the encounter produces grief as an immediate affective response. (3) *Plea for pardon*: Exo 10:17 — Pharaoh's "forgive my sin, please, only this once" — the record encountered produces an urgent appeal for its cancellation.

---

#### T1.5.2 (cf=14927, E) — Is the immediate response consistent across the verse evidence, or does it vary?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Variable. Confession (Num 5:7; Psa 32:5), sorrow (Psa 38:18), and plea for pardon (Exo 10:17; Num 12:11) are the positive responses. But several verses show non-response or resistance: Jer 16:10 — the people cannot identify their sin when under judgment — the immediate response to the prophet's declaration is incomprehension rather than acknowledgment; Hos 12:8 — Ephraim denies the record ("they cannot find in me iniquity or sin") — self-deception is the immediate response. The response to the divine record depends on whether the conscience is engaged (confession/sorrow) or suppressed (denial/incomprehension).

---

#### T1.5.3 (cf=14928, E) — Where the verse evidence is silent on immediate response, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — For the many persons described as carrying the Jeroboam-sin legacy (VCG-04 — 46 verses of kings who "walked in the sins of Jeroboam and did not depart"), the immediate inner response to the record is not described. The corpus characterises their conduct and its consequences but not the moment-by-moment subjective experience of carrying the recorded sin-pattern.

---

#### T1.6.1 (cf=14929, E) — What does this characteristic produce in the inner being over time?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Four sustained inner-being effects. (1) *Separation from God*: Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you" — accumulated record produces relational estrangement; (2) *Rotting and decay*: Eze 33:10 — "our sins are upon us, and we rot away because of them" — the accumulated record produces inner moral decay; (3) *Physical deterioration*: Psa 38:3 — "there is no soundness in my flesh... there is no health in my bones because of my sin" — the weight of the record produces bodily consequences over time; (4) *Loss of blessing*: Jer 5:25 — "your sins have kept good from you" — sustained accumulation of the record progressively closes off the positive divine provision.

---

#### T1.6.2 (cf=14930, E) — What states, qualities, capacities, or orientations does sustained exposure to or possession of this characteristic establish?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Three established states. (1) *Accuser-testimony state*: Isa 59:12 — "our sins testify against us" — the accumulated record becomes a self-generating accusation that cannot be silenced; (2) *Hardened-face state*: Isa 3:9 — "they proclaim their sin like Sodom; they do not hide it" — when the record has grown extensive enough, conscience no longer restrains its display; the face itself bears witness to the interior record; (3) *Divine-memory vigilance state*: Jer 14:10 — "he will remember their iniquity and punish their sins" — the sustained record places the person in a state of pending divine reckoning; the record awaits its appointed day of visitation (Exo 32:34).

---

#### T1.6.3 (cf=14931, E) — Does the sustained effect differ from the immediate response — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. The immediate response is often confessional or grief-oriented (Num 5:7; Psa 38:18) — an acute response to the moment of recognition. The sustained effect is the gradual accumulation of separation from God (Isa 59:2), physical deterioration (Psa 38:3), and moral rotting (Eze 33:10). The sustained effect also involves the hardening of the face (Isa 3:9 — openly proclaimed without shame), suggesting that immediate conscience-engagement (acute response) gives way to diminished conscience over time if the record is not addressed.

---

#### T1.7.1 (cf=14932, E) — What inner conditions or orientations enable genuine reception of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Four enabling conditions. (1) *Awareness when sin is made known*: Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed is made known to him" — the conscience's ability to receive the disclosure of the record is the primary enabling condition; (2) *Conscience engagement*: Num 5:6 — "that person realizes his guilt" — the internal realization triggers the response mechanism; (3) *Humility before God*: Psa 32:5 — "I acknowledged my sin... I did not cover my iniquity" — the person's willingness not to cover their sin enables reception; (4) *Memory of prior failure*: Gen 41:9 — "I remember my offenses today" — the cupbearer's memory of past failure enables current acknowledgment.

---

#### T1.7.2 (cf=14933, E) — What inner conditions block, distort, or prevent reception?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Three blocking conditions. (1) *Self-deception through prosperity*: Hos 12:8 — "in all my labors they cannot find in me iniquity or sin" — material success produces a false sense of moral clearance; (2) *Suppression of the record*: Psa 32:5 — "I did not cover my iniquity" (the positive) implies that covering is the blocking condition; Jer 16:10 — the people's inability to identify their sin shows a suppression that blocks reception; (3) *Hardened face*: Isa 3:9 — "they proclaim their sin like Sodom; they do not hide it" — conscience has been so thoroughly suppressed that the record no longer produces shame; the blocking condition has become permanent display without awareness of its gravity.

---

#### T1.7.3 (cf=14934, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Jer 16:10 — the people under judgment ask "What is our iniquity? What is the sin that we have committed against the Lord our God?" — the non-receiving state is incomprehension: the record is there (God sees it; judgment has come) but the person cannot identify it in themselves. Isa 3:9 — "they proclaim their sin like Sodom; they do not hide it" — the alternative non-receiving state: the record has produced such conscience-deadness that the person exhibits their sin publicly without awareness of its gravity. Both states involve a failure of the inner conscience to correctly interpret the moral reality of the record before God.

---

#### T1.8.1 (cf=14935, E) — What is the primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic from the programme's dimension vocabulary?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The primary dimension is **moral-accountability standing before God** — not a specific faculty but the person's total relational-moral position. The characteristic is defined by its standing before God, not by its engagement of a specific inner faculty. Amo 5:12 — "for I know how many are your transgressions and how great are your sins" — God's knowledge of the record is the defining dimension; the record exists before divine omniscience. Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store" — the storage is in the divine memory. The primary dimension is thus the person's standing in God's moral memory.

---

#### T1.8.2 (cf=14936, E) — What evidence from the verse evidence supports this classification?

**[CHAR-13]** E — (1) Exo 32:33 — "whoever has sinned against me, I will blot out of my book" — the person's standing in the divine book is the operative moral reality; (2) Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the erasure of divine memory is the defining liberation; (3) Job 10:6 — "seek out my iniquity and search for my sin" — divine scrutiny of the person's standing is the characteristic's primary mode; (4) Gen 18:20 — "their sin is very grave" — the gravity is measured by what has reached God as an objective reality, not by the person's own internal assessment.

---

#### T1.8.3 (cf=14937, E) — Does this characteristic carry secondary dimensions — and if so, what are they, and do they compete with the primary classification?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — three secondary dimensions. (1) *Internal-inscription dimension*: Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the record has a secondary heart-level constitutional dimension; the sin is written within as well as before God; (2) *Physical-consequence dimension*: Psa 38:3 — "no health in my bones because of my sin" — the record produces bodily consequences as a secondary dimension; (3) *Conscience-awareness dimension*: Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — the record's persistent presence in the conscience is a secondary inner-dimension, distinct from the primary objective-standing-before-God dimension.

---

---

## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=14938, E) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — through the separation from God that the record produces. Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you so that he does not hear" — the separation is at the spirit level: the person's capacity for God-relationship (the spirit's primary orientation) has been disrupted by the accumulated record. Act 26:18 (referenced in CHAR-12 but its principle applies here) — the darkness/light distinction that sin creates operates at the spirit level. The record's most fundamental effect — hiding God's face — is a spirit-level consequence: it severs the deepest channel between the person and God.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=14939, E) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Not primarily. The record originates in acts (chat.tat as committed acts — Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed"), in the heart's disposition (Deu 15:9 — "an unworthy thought in your heart"), and through inherited condition (Psa 51:5 — "in sin did my mother conceive me"). Jer 17:1 — the sin of Judah is engraved on the heart — the inscription is heart-level. The record thus originates in the heart's dispositional failures and the will's sinning acts rather than at the spirit level specifically; it produces spirit-level consequences (separation from God — Isa 59:2) without originating there.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=14940, E) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The spirit-level consequence (Isa 59:2 — separation and God's hidden face) reveals that the divine record's ultimate harm operates at the deepest constitutional level: it disrupts the person's most fundamental relational capacity — the spirit's access to God. The record is not merely a legal notation but a relational barrier at the spirit level. Jer 31:34's new-covenant promise — "I will remember their sin no more" — promises spirit-level restoration: the erasure of the record restores the spirit's God-access.

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=14941, E) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Spirit-level consequence (Isa 59:2 — separation from God; God's face hidden) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=14942, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — extensively evidenced. The record moves from inner (heart-level inscription — Jer 17:1) to outer (physical consequences — Psa 38:3; national consequences — Lev 26:18-28) and from outer consequences back to inner (the body's deterioration affecting the inner person — Psa 38:3 — "no health in my bones because of my sin" producing inner sorrow — Psa 38:18 — "I am sorry for my sin"). The record also moves from individual (chet — VCG-01 — personal liability) to corporate (VCG-04 — Jeroboam-sin affecting the whole nation) and back to individual (Deu 24:16 — each dies for their own sin).

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=14943, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Multiple movement sequences are evidenced. (1) *Inner-to-outer*: Deu 15:9 — "an unworthy thought in your heart" → grudging eye → withholding → the worker's cry reaching God → the employer "guilty of sin" — the sequence runs from inner thought to outer social act to divine register; (2) *Record-to-consequence*: Lev 26:18/21/24/28 — accumulated sins → sevenfold divine discipline — the stored record triggers covenant consequences; (3) *Individual-to-corporate*: VCG-04 — Jeroboam's personal sin → "he made Israel to sin" (1Ki 14:16) — the individual leader's recorded sin becomes the nation's recorded inheritance; (4) *Record-to-separation*: Isa 59:2 — accumulated sins → God's face hidden → prayer not heard — the record moves progressively from inner condition to outer relational consequence.

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=14944, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Constitutional movement is extensively evidenced across all levels (T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=14945, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. Mic 6:7 — "the sin of my soul" — the soul is explicitly named as the locus of the sin requiring atonement. The soul-level location is confirmed through the personal accountability language: chet (VCG-01) — "each one shall be put to death for his own sin" (Deu 24:16) — the "own sin" is owned at the soul level of the individual person as a moral subject. The characteristic's definition — "the person carries this record whether they feel it or not" — names the soul-level person as the carrier.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=14946, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Soul-level location (Mic 6:7 — "sin of my soul") reveals that the divine record is not merely a legal external notation but is carried at the level of the person's innermost selfhood. The soul is the level at which the record produces its most personal burden: Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — the persistent inner presence of the record is a soul-level experience of the divine accounting. Every moral subject as a soul carries a sin-record; this is what makes the characteristic universal at the soul level.

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=14947, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (Mic 6:7; Deu 24:16; Psa 51:3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=14948, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — most explicitly in the corpus. Jer 17:1 — "the sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of diamond it is engraved on the tablet of their heart" (VCG-02) — the heart is explicitly named as the internal inscription-site of the divine record. The record is not only before God externally but engraved within at the heart's constitutional centre. Additionally: Deu 15:9 — "an unworthy thought in your heart" — the heart is where the sin originates before it enters the divine record; 1Ki 15:3 — "his heart was not wholly true to the Lord his God" — the divided heart's state is the inner source of the sin-pattern walked in.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=14949, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Heart-location (Jer 17:1 — iron pen, diamond point engraving on the heart's tablet) reveals that the divine record has a dual location: before God externally and within the person internally. The engraving metaphor is the most analytically significant image in the 205-verse corpus for constitutional location: the sin is inscribed in the person's innermost moral core with such permanence ("iron pen... diamond point") that it cannot be self-erased. The heart bears the record as an internal impression while God maintains it as an external account. The record's internal inscription explains why it persists even when hidden from others: the engraving is within.

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=14950, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Heart-location is most explicitly evidenced of any constitutional level (Jer 17:1). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=14951, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The mind engages the record primarily through knowledge and memory. Gen 41:9 — "I remember my offenses today" — the cupbearer's mind recalls a past failure at a moment of moral relevance; Jer 13:23 (VCG-03) — "how many are my iniquities and my sins?" — Job's mind attempts but cannot self-enumerate the record; it requires divine disclosure. Pro 24:9 — "the devising of folly is sin" — the mind's cognitive act of scheming folly is itself a sin that enters the record.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=14952, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Mind-location (Gen 41:9 — remembering offenses; Job 13:23 — seeking to know the record; Pro 24:9 — devising folly as sin) reveals that the divine record engages the mind at two points: (1) the mind's acts can create entries in the record (Pro 24:9 — cognitive devising of folly is sin); (2) the mind can partially access the record through memory (Gen 41:9) but cannot fully enumerate it without divine disclosure (Job 13:23 — needing God to "make me know my transgression and my sin").

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=14953, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Mind-location is evidenced (Gen 41:9; Job 13:23; Pro 24:9). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=14954, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — *Conscience* as a distinct soul-level faculty is evidenced. Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — the conscience's persistent awareness of the record is the primary soul-level location beyond heart and mind. Num 5:6 — "that person realizes his guilt" — the conscience's realisation of guilt is the moment the divine record becomes accessible to inner awareness. The conscience is the faculty that mediates between the objective divine record and the subjective inner-being experience of it.

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=14955, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Conscience-level location (Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me"; Num 5:6 — "realizes his guilt") reveals that the divine record has a conscience-facing dimension: the objective record held by God has a corresponding inner signal in the conscience that (when functioning) makes the record accessible to the person's own awareness. The characteristic's definition ("the person carries this record whether they feel it or not") notes the conscience's optional engagement: the record is objective; conscience engagement is variable. When conscience functions, it mirrors the divine record internally; when suppressed, the record remains but the inner signal is silenced.

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=14956, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Conscience-level location is evidenced (Psa 51:3; Num 5:6). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=14957, E) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — multiple body-part links. (1) *Bones*: Psa 38:3 — "there is no health in my bones because of my sin" — the bone-level deterioration is the body's register of the sin-record's weight; (2) *Flesh*: Psa 38:3 — "there is no soundness in my flesh because of your indignation" — the flesh is the body-level expression of the record's consequence; (3) *Lips/mouth*: Isa 6:7 — "this has touched your lips; your guilt is taken away, and your sin atoned for" — the lips are the body-part through which Isaiah's sin was located and atoned for; Psa 59:12 — "for the sin of their mouths, the words of their lips" — the mouth is the organ through which sin is generated and enters the record; (4) *Hands*: Isa 31:7 — "your hands have sinfully made" — the hands as the active instruments creating the record-generating act.

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=14958, E) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The body-part links (bones — Psa 38:3; lips — Isa 6:7; Psa 59:12; hands — Isa 31:7) perform three functions. (1) *Naming the instrument of sin*: the hands (Isa 31:7), the mouth (Psa 59:12) are named as the body-parts through which specific sins are enacted and the record entries are created; (2) *Naming the site of consequence*: the bones and flesh (Psa 38:3) are where the record's weight registers physically — the body bears the evidence of the record's moral cost; (3) *Naming the site of atonement*: Isa 6:7 — the lips are the specific body-part where the divine action of atonement addresses the sin; the coal touches the place of the sin's origination.

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=14959, E) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Multiple body-part links are evidenced (Psa 38:3 — bones/flesh; Isa 6:7 — lips; Psa 59:12 — mouth; Isa 31:7 — hands). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=14960, E) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The direction is bidirectional. (1) *Body-outward generating the record*: the hands making idols (Isa 31:7), the mouth producing sinful speech (Psa 59:12) — body-to-record direction; the body's acts create entries in the divine record. (2) *Record-inward affecting the body*: Psa 38:3 — "no health in my bones because of my sin" — the record's weight produces physical deterioration; the divine record (held before God) produces bodily consequences. The direction thus flows both ways: body-acts → record-entry → physical-consequences → body-deterioration.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=14961, E) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The bidirectional body-record link (body acts generating the record; record weight deteriorating the body) reveals that the body is simultaneously an instrument of sin-record creation and a recipient of the record's consequences. Psa 38:3 — the bones bear the consequence of sin (moral → physical direction) while the hands and mouth that sinned (physical → moral direction) are the instruments. The body is thus a two-way channel: it creates entries in the divine record through sinning acts and then bears the physical weight of what has been recorded.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=14962, E) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Body-characteristic link is extensively evidenced and bidirectional (T2.7.1 and T2.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=14963, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — the most extensive deposit evidence in M10. Four deposit forms. (1) *Internal heart-engraving*: Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the sin-record is inscribed internally as a permanent deposit on the heart's constitutional structure; the iron pen and diamond point indicate irreversibility without divine intervention; (2) *Separation from God*: Isa 59:2 — accumulated record produces a structured relational barrier as its deposit; (3) *National-institutional legacy*: VCG-04 — the Jeroboam-sin deposit persists across royal generations (46 verses) as a named institutional inheritance; (4) *Stored divine record*: Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store" — the divine side of the deposit is the sealed, stored record awaiting reckoning.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=14964, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Supporting: Jer 17:1 (heart-engraving — permanent inscription); Isa 59:2 (separation — relational deposit); VCG-04 (Jeroboam-pattern — institutional deposit persisting 46 verses worth of kings); Hos 13:12 (kept in store — divine retention deposit). Contradicting or complicating: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins" — the deposit is not irremovable; Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the new covenant promise erases the deposit from divine memory; Eze 33:16 — "none of the sins shall be remembered against him" — individual repentance can erase the deposit's record-against-me function. The deposit is real and deep but not permanently irremovable.

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=14965, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Deposit evidence is the most extensive in M10: heart-engraving (Jer 17:1), relational separation (Isa 59:2), institutional legacy (VCG-04), stored divine record (Hos 13:12). T5.7 will carry: the heart-engraving deposit is permanent without divine action; the institutional legacy deposit spans generations; the divine-record deposit awaits eschatological reckoning; the deposit can be addressed by divine erasure (Isa 43:25; Jer 31:34; Eze 33:16) but not by human effort alone.

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=14966, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Multiple constitutional origins. (1) *From the heart's unworthy thoughts*: Deu 15:9 — "an unworthy thought in your heart" — the record originates in the heart's inner disposition before any outer act; (2) *From the will's sinning acts*: chat.tat (VCG-05) — the committed act (Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed") creates the record entry; (3) *From inherited birth-condition*: Psa 51:5 — "in sin did my mother conceive me" — the record-bearing condition is received at birth, prior to any individual act; (4) *From corporate-representational transmission*: VCG-04 — Jeroboam's sin becomes Israel's recorded inheritance through the king's representational role.

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=14967, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Multiple — and constitutively so. The 205-verse corpus explicitly demonstrates at least four distinct constitutional origins (heart's thought, will's act, birth-condition, corporate transmission — T2.9.1). No single origin covers all 205 verses: chet (VCG-01) emphasises personal-liability origin; Psa 51:5 emphasises birth-condition origin; VCG-04 emphasises corporate-representational origin; VCG-05's broad chat.tat corpus emphasises the act-origin. The characteristic's scope is precisely its multi-originary nature.

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=14968, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. In the individual moral context: the origin is in the person's own act or thought (Deu 15:9 — heart's thought → sin; Lev 4:23 — committed act). In the prophetic-national context: the origin is corporate — Jeroboam's sin becomes Israel's inherited record (VCG-04 — 46 verses). In the priestly-liturgical context (Lev 4-5; 16:21 — Day of Atonement): the origin presupposes already-accumulated record requiring priestly mediation. In the Job-surveillance context (VCG-03): the origin is scrutinised by God — Job can neither identify nor quantity his record without divine disclosure.

---

---

## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=14969, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The perceptive faculty is engaged through its failure and its restoration in relation to the record. Job 10:6 — "that you seek out my iniquity and search for my sin" (VCG-03) — Job perceives God as actively scrutinising him; the perception of divine surveillance is the operative inner dynamic. Num 32:23 — "your sin will find you out" — the sin's eventual self-revelation engages the perceptive faculty: what was hidden will become visible. Isa 3:9 — "the look on their faces bears witness against them; they proclaim their sin like Sodom" — the sinners' own faces have become perceptible testimony to their record; the perceptive faculty registers what the conscience no longer conceals.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=14970, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *distorts and eventually impairs* the perceptive faculty. Jer 16:10 — "they say to you, 'Why has the Lord pronounced all this great evil against us? What is our iniquity? What is the sin?'" — the people cannot perceive their own record even under judgment; the perceptive faculty is impaired by suppression of the conscience. Hos 12:8 — "in all my labors they cannot find in me iniquity or sin" — Ephraim's prosperity distorts perception of the record: wealth is mistaken for moral clearance. Conversely, when the record is made known (Lev 4:23 — "made known to him"), the perceptive faculty is restored to accurate moral self-assessment.

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=14971, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The perceptive pattern reveals that the divine record operates partly through perceptive concealment: the record exists before God regardless of whether the person perceives it, but the person's perceptive engagement with it is variable and distortable. Jer 16:10 (incomprehension under judgment), Hos 12:8 (prosperity-induced blindness), and Isa 3:9 (sin publicly displayed without shame) represent the full perceptive failure spectrum — from genuine incomprehension to wealth-induced blindness to conscience-deadened display. The perceptive faculty's restoration is the first step toward the record being addressed (Lev 4:23 — "made known to him" → brings offering).

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=14996, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Conscientiousness — the integrated moral awareness, volition, and action system — is engaged both in the generation and the addressing of the record. On the generation side: Isa 5:18 — "draw sin as with cart ropes" — conscientious effort deployed toward sin-accumulation; Isa 30:1 — "carry out a plan... add sin to sin" — conscientiousness misdirected toward sin-accumulation. On the addressing side: Num 5:7 — "he shall confess his sin... make full restitution" — conscientious engagement in acknowledging and repairing the record.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=14997, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *captures and misdirects* conscientiousness in the generation phase (Isa 5:18 — effortful sin-drawing; Isa 30:1 — deliberate plan-executing contrary to God). The record *activates* conscientiousness in the addressing phase: the moment the record "is made known" (Lev 4:23), the entire conscientiousness system is required to respond — awareness recognised, will directed toward the prescribed remedy, action taken (bringing the offering). The record thus activates the full conscientiousness system when conscience engages it correctly.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=14998, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The conscientiousness pattern reveals that the divine record is both the product of conscientiousness misdirected (sin deliberately accumulated — Isa 5:18; 30:1) and the stimulus for conscientiousness rightly directed (the made-known sin → confession → restitution → offering cycle — Lev 4:23; Num 5:7). The record thus stands at the hinge of conscientiousness: it is what conscientiousness produced when misdirected, and it is what conscientiousness must address when correctly engaged.

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=14999, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The relational capacity is constitutively engaged: the divine record is fundamentally a relational account between the person and God. Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God" — the record creates a relational barrier; Gen 18:20 — "the outcry against Sodom and Gomorrah is great" — the sin has reached God as an audible relational disturbance. The record is the moral content of a damaged relationship: Isa 43:24 — "you have burdened me with your sins" — the accumulation of the record is experienced by God as a relational burden.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=15000, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *severs and blocks* relational capacity toward God. Isa 59:2 — separation between person and God; God's face hidden; prayer not heard — the record progressively closes off the relational channels. Jer 5:25 — "your sins have kept good from you" — the record blocks the positive relational provision God would otherwise give. The record's erasure restores relational capacity: Jer 31:34 — when God remembers sins no more, the relational channel is fully restored; 2Ch 6:25/26/27 — forgiveness of the sin enables return to the land and restoration of divine hearing.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=15001, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The relational pattern reveals that CHAR-13 is at its core a *relational rupture quantified*: the divine record is the accounting of what separates the person from God, accumulated entry by entry. Isa 59:2 — "made a separation" — the record is the separator; its removal is the restoration of the relationship. The relational restoration metaphors — blotting out (Isa 43:25), casting into the sea (Mic 7:19), casting behind the back (Isa 38:17), remembering no more (Jer 31:34) — are all relational metaphors: they name the removal of what blocks the God-relationship. The divine record is thus defined by its relational consequence: the record stands between the person and God; its removal is the relationship's restoration.

---
