---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=14972, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The cognitive faculty is engaged through the law's disclosure function and through accusation. Rom 3:20 (CHAR-11 corpus, but relevant principle) — the law provides the cognitive framework for recognising the record. Directly in CHAR-13: Pro 24:9 — "the devising of folly is sin" — the cognitive act of deliberate scheming creates a record entry; the mind's devising is itself sinful. Job 13:23 — "how many are my iniquities and my sins? Make me know my transgression and my sin" — Job's cognitive faculty cannot self-enumerate the record; he requires divine cognitive disclosure to know what the record contains.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=14973, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *impairs accurate self-cognition* while being fully known to God. Job 13:23 — Job cannot know the extent of his own record without divine disclosure; the cognitive faculty's self-assessment is inherently limited. Jer 16:10 — the people's cognitive question ("What is our iniquity?") under judgment shows the record has accumulated beyond their cognitive self-awareness. Pro 24:9 — conversely, the cognitive act of devising folly creates the record: active scheming is morally registered even if the schemer does not cognise it as sin.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=14974, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals an asymmetry between divine and human cognition of the record: God knows it fully (Amo 5:12 — "for I know how many are your transgressions and how great are your sins") while the human's cognitive access to their own record is partial and often distorted. This asymmetry is the characteristic's epistemic structure: the record is fully known to God and partially (or wrongly) known to the person. Isa 58:1 — the prophetic function is precisely to bridge this asymmetry: "declare to my people their transgression, to the house of Jacob their sins."

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=14975, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The memory faculty is the primary faculty engaged — on both the divine and human sides. (1) *Divine memory*: Jer 14:10 — "he will remember their iniquity and punish their sins"; Hos 8:13 — "now he will remember their iniquity and punish their sins" — the divine memory retains the record and triggers judgment from it. The liberation metaphors are memory-erasure metaphors: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins"; Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more." (2) *Human memory*: Gen 41:9 — "I remember my offenses today" — the cupbearer's memory of past failure resurfaces; Psa 25:7 — "Remember not the sins of my youth" — the psalmist pleads for God not to hold the past record in memory against him.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=14976, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The divine-record characteristic *is constituted by divine memory*: the record exists as God's memorial of every sin committed. It deepens divine memory's function (every sin is retained — Hos 13:12 — "kept in store") while the characteristic's resolution is precisely divine memory-erasure. For human memory: the record can deepen human memory of past failures (Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me"; Gen 41:9 — "I remember my offenses today") or be suppressed through wilful forgetting (Jer 16:10 — people cannot identify their sin). The characteristic thus operates through, and in some cases against, the human memory faculty.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=14977, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The memory pattern reveals that CHAR-13 is uniquely memory-constituted among M10 characteristics: the divine record is divine memory made judicial — every sin is retained in God's memory as a standing moral account. Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the new-covenant liberation is defined as divine memory-cancellation, not merely divine forgiveness-declaration. The characteristic thus reveals: sin's ultimate hold over the person is not primarily in inner experience or constitutional damage but in divine memory's retention of the moral account — and liberation is the permanent cancellation of that retention.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=14978, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — the record produces affective responses when conscience-accessible. Psa 38:18 — "I am sorry for my sin" — the record produces sorrow as its affective consequence; Neh 9:2 — "stood and confessed their sins and the iniquities of their fathers" — the corporate assembly's affective posture before the record is penitential standing. Psa 38:3 — the physical consequences of the record ("no health in my bones") produce what is implicitly an affective state of illness and distress. Eze 33:10 — "our sins are upon us, and we rot away because of them. How then can we live?" — the affective dimension is despair: the weight of the record produces hopelessness.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=14979, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *deepens negative affect* when conscience is engaged: Psa 38:18 — sorrow; Eze 33:10 — despair. But when conscience is suppressed, the record *bypasses affect*: Isa 3:9 — sin proclaimed without shame — the affect of shame is absent despite the record's presence. The record exists independently of the affective state; affect is the conscience's variable response to the record, not a constitutive feature of the record itself.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=14980, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The affective pattern reveals that the divine record does not require affective engagement to exist — the record is objective (the definition: "whether they feel it or not"). Affect is the optional inner-being register of the record when conscience is functional: sorrow (Psa 38:18), despair (Eze 33:10), and hopelessness (Eze 33:10 — "how then can we live?") are the primary negative affects the record produces. The positive resolution of the record (forgiveness and erasure) produces the corresponding positive affect: Psa 32:1 — "blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven" — blessedness is the positive affective state of having the record addressed.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=14981, E) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Limited evidence. Pro 24:9 — "the devising of folly is sin" — the cognitive-creative act of scheming folly creates a record entry. The creative faculty in its inverted form (devising folly rather than creating good) is engaged as a sin-generating mechanism that enters the record. Isa 30:1 — "who carry out a plan, but not mine" — the creative plan-making that departs from God's purposes adds sin to sin; the creative faculty deployed against God's will is a record-generating activity.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=14982, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *captures and misdirects* the creative faculty. Pro 24:9 — the devising (creative cognitive act) of folly is itself sin; the creative capacity is deployed toward the wrong object and generates a record entry. Isa 30:1 — the plan-making faculty (creative will) that pursues alliances not of God's Spirit adds to the sin-record. The creative faculty's operation against God produces record entries; it is captured by sinful purpose rather than creative goodness.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=14983, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The creative pattern reveals that the divine record is not only generated by obvious moral failures but also by the active cognitive-creative deployment of the person's planning and devising capacities toward ends contrary to God (Pro 24:9; Isa 30:1). The record captures what the mind devises and what the will plans, not only what the body does overtly. This confirms the record's comprehensive scope: it is sensitive to inner creative-cognitive movements as well as outer acts.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=14984, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — the volitional faculty is engaged at the generation and the departure levels. (1) *Will generating record entries*: Lev 26:18/21/24/28 — "if you will not listen to me" — the wilful refusal is what triggers sevenfold discipline; the will's persistent refusal accumulates the record; (2) *Will departing from the record*: Eze 18:21 — "if a wicked person turns away from all his sins that he has committed" — the volitional turning is what reverses the record's account against the person; Eze 33:14/16 — "if he turns from his sin" — the will's turn is what enables the record's erasure; (3) *Will clinging to the record*: VCG-04 — "he did not depart from the sins of Jeroboam" — the will's persistent clinging perpetuates the institutional legacy.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=14985, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *accumulates through volitional persistence* and *is addressed through volitional turning*. The will's sustained choices in one direction deepen the record (Lev 26:18-28 — sevenfold escalation; VCG-04 — kings who did not depart). The will's decisive turn enables the record's erasure (Eze 18:21; 33:14/16). The record thus tracks the will's directional history precisely: VCG-04's "he walked in" and "he did not depart" language is the volitional vocabulary of record accumulation.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=14986, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The volitional pattern reveals that the divine record is the precise moral archive of the will's history: each volitional choice either adds to or (through repentance) moves toward erasure of the record. VCG-04's 46 verses of kings who "walked in" and "did not depart from" the sins of Jeroboam are the clearest evidence: the record is constituted by sustained volitional direction. The will's "turning away" (Eze 18:21) is the key to addressing the record: not a cognitive acknowledgement alone but a volitional redirection that enables divine erasure.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=14987, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Agency is engaged as both the generator and the addresser of the record. (1) *Agency generating the record*: Isa 5:18 — "who draw sin as with cart ropes" — the active, effortful drawing of sin toward the self is a fully agentive act that creates the record; Isa 30:1 — "who carry out a plan... that they may add sin to sin" — the deliberate agentive execution of plans against God accumulates the record. (2) *Agency addressing the record*: Num 5:7 — "he shall confess his sin that he has committed. And he shall make full restitution" — the agentive confession and restitution are the human side of the record-addressing mechanism.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=14988, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *deepens through agentive accumulation*: Isa 5:18 — actively drawing sin with cart ropes names maximum agency in record-creation; Isa 30:1 — "add sin to sin" — deliberate accumulation. However, the record *does not eliminate agency*: the will can turn (Eze 18:21) and the person can confess and make restitution (Num 5:7) — these are genuine agentive responses to the record. The record is the history of the agency's past choices; it does not foreclose future agentive change.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=14989, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The agency pattern reveals that sin as divine record is the product of genuine human agency: the record is filled by real agentive choices (drawing sin, adding sin, walking in sin, not departing). This is what makes the record a moral account rather than a fatalistic condition: each entry was made by the person's own agency. This agency-grounding of the record is also what makes genuine penitential agency possible: the turn of Eze 18:21 is meaningful precisely because the prior record was genuinely the person's own.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=14990, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The moral evaluation faculty is engaged through the record's accusation function. Isa 59:12 — "our sins testify against us" — the sins function as witnesses in a moral-legal adjudication; the moral evaluation faculty is the target of this testimony. Jer 16:10 — the people's moral evaluation of their own conduct ("What is our sin?") fails to identify what the divine record contains; the moral evaluation faculty is impaired. 1Sa 12:19 — "we have added to all our sins this evil" — the people's moral evaluation correctly identifies the record as cumulative and dangerous; this is the positive function of the faculty.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=14991, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *reveals the failure of the moral evaluation faculty* most precisely: Job 13:23 — Job cannot evaluate the extent of his own record; Jer 16:10 — the people under judgment cannot evaluate their own sin; Hos 12:8 — prosperity impairs Ephraim's moral self-evaluation. The record exceeds the person's own moral evaluation capacity — God's assessment of the record is more accurate than the person's self-assessment (Amo 5:12 — "I know how many are your transgressions"). The characteristic thus reveals the inherent limitation of self-directed moral evaluation.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=14992, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that the divine record is the objective standard against which self-directed moral evaluation fails: the person's self-assessment consistently underestimates or misidentifies the record (Job 13:23; Jer 16:10; Hos 12:8). The prophetic function bridges this gap (Isa 58:1; Mic 3:8 — declaring the record to those who cannot self-evaluate it). This confirms that moral evaluation requires an external witness (the prophet, the law — Rom 3:20) to function accurately in relation to the divine record.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=14993, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The conscience is the primary mediating faculty between the objective divine record and the person's subjective awareness of it. Num 5:6 — "that person realizes his guilt" — conscience is the realisation-faculty that makes the objective record subjectively accessible; Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed is made known to him" — the making-known is what conscience does when functioning. Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — the conscience's persistent presentation of the record to the person's awareness is its characteristic operation.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=14994, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *challenges conscience to engage and is damaged by conscience failure*. When the record is not addressed, conscience is progressively suppressed: Isa 3:9 — sin proclaimed publicly without shame names the conscience-deadened state. When the record is acknowledged (Psa 32:5 — "I acknowledged my sin"), conscience is engaged and the path to resolution opens. The record's accumulation without conscience engagement produces the hardened-face state (Isa 3:9); conscience engagement produces sorrow (Psa 38:18), confession (Num 5:7), and the path to forgiveness.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=14995, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The conscience pattern reveals that CHAR-13 is the objective correlate of conscience: the divine record is what conscience (when functioning) registers and presents to the person's awareness. The record exists independently (objectively before God — definition: "whether they feel it or not") while conscience is the subjective-signal faculty that connects the objective record to the person's inner awareness. Conscience is thus the faculty through which the divine record becomes experientially real to the person — and its suppression is what allows the record to accumulate without inner warning.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=15002, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — God operates toward the person bearing the divine record in four modes. (1) *Divine scrutiny*: Job 10:6 — "that you seek out my iniquity and search for my sin" (VCG-03); Job 10:14 — "if I sin, you watch me and do not acquit me" — God's active surveillance of the record directed toward the individual. (2) *Divine visitation of the record*: Exo 32:34 — "in the day when I visit, I will visit their sin upon them" — God's deferred enforcement of the record at its appointed time. (3) *Divine erasure of the record*: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins"; Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — God's sovereign act of record-cancellation directed toward the person. (4) *Covenant discipline*: Lev 26:18/21/24/28 — God's sevenfold escalating discipline in response to the accumulated record, directed toward the people to provoke turning.

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=15003, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-13]** E — God's response to the divine record rests on three bases. (1) *Holiness that cannot ignore the record*: Jos 24:19 — "he is a holy God. He is a jealous God; he will not forgive your transgressions or your sins" — Joshua's warning names God's holiness and jealousy as the basis for the record's moral force; (2) *Covenant faithfulness that promises record-erasure*: Jer 31:34 — the new covenant's record-erasure is grounded in God's own covenantal initiative; (3) *His own name's honour*: Isa 43:25 — "I, I am he who blots out your transgressions for my own sake" — the basis of record-cancellation is not the person's merit but God's own sovereign purpose for his name's glory.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=15004, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — God's disposition is one of *patient scrutiny moving toward either judgment or erasure*. The sevenfold discipline of Lev 26:18-28 reveals a patient escalating response designed to provoke turning rather than immediately destroying. Exo 32:34 — "in the day when I visit" — the deferred reckoning reveals patience. Isa 43:25 — "for my own sake" — the erasure reveals mercy grounded in God's own character. Amos 5:12 — "I know how many are your transgressions" — God's complete knowledge of the record does not require the person to confess what God already knows; the knowledge is already complete. The disposition is thus patient-knowing-and-ready-to-erase rather than eager to condemn.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=15005, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — God-to-human operation is extensively evidenced across scrutiny, visitation, erasure, and covenant-discipline modes (T4.1.1–T4.1.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=15006, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record *blocks and burdens* the human person's movement toward God. Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you so that he does not hear" — the record severs both divine attention and divine response to prayer, blocking the human-to-God movement at the communication level. Exo 34:9 — Moses's prayer — "pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for your inheritance" — the movement toward God requires first naming and petitioning for the record's removal; the record is the obstacle that must be addressed before God-directed movement can succeed.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=15007, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Acknowledgment*: Psa 32:5 — "I acknowledged my sin to you, and I did not cover my iniquity; I said, 'I will confess my transgressions to the Lord'" — the posture of non-concealment and active acknowledgment is the first requirement; (2) *Turning*: Eze 18:21 — "turns away from all his sins that he has committed and keeps all my statutes and does what is just and right" — the volitional turning from sin is the required posture for the record to be addressed; (3) *Dependence on divine erasure*: Psa 51:2 — "wash me thoroughly from my iniquity, and cleanse me from my sin" — the posture of dependence on God's action rather than self-cleansing is the foundational human-to-God posture under the record.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=15008, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The disruption reveals that the divine record is precisely a *relational barrier*: Isa 59:2 — the record makes separation and hides God's face. The barrier has two faces: it blocks God's hearing (divine-to-human communication blocked) and it blocks the person's effective prayer (human-to-God movement blocked). The dual blockage reveals the record's function as a comprehensive relational wall: accumulated sin does not merely inconvenience the relationship but structurally prevents its full operation until addressed.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=15009, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Human-to-God operation (in its blocked and restored forms) is extensively evidenced (T4.2.1–T4.2.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=15010, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — primarily through the Jeroboam-sin legacy (VCG-04). 1Ki 14:16 — "he will give Israel up because of the sins of Jeroboam, which he sinned and made Israel to sin" — Jeroboam's recorded sin is extended to Israel; the leader's divine-record entry becomes the nation's inheritance. Exo 32:21 — "what did this people do to you that you have brought such a great sin upon them?" (VCG-02) — Aaron's leadership failure extended a great sin onto the people. Gen 20:9 — "you have brought on me and my kingdom a great sin" (VCG-02) — Abraham's deception transferred a sin-record entry onto Abimelech's household.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=15011, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Representative authority*: VCG-04 — the Jeroboam-pattern extension operates through royal representational authority; the king's record-generating acts extend to the people through his role as their covenantal leader. Exo 32:21 — Aaron's priestly-leadership role is what enabled his sin's extension to the people; (2) *Relational proximity and dependence*: Gen 20:9 — Abraham's deception affected Abimelech because Abimelech had received Abraham in relational trust; the extending person's record impacts those who depend on or are relationally bound to them.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=15012, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The extending person must hold a position of influence, authority, or relational access that makes their sin-record transferable. VCG-04 — the kings who extended the Jeroboam-sin to Israel had first received the royal role (exalted "out of the dust" — 1Ki 16:2 — "since I exalted you out of the dust and made you leader over my people Israel"). The extension-capacity is grounded in the authority-position received: having been made leader, their record-generating acts become the nation's burden. Aaron likewise (Exo 32:21) had received the priestly-leadership role whose authority made his failure extend to all.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=15013, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — The giving/extension direction is extensively evidenced through the Jeroboam-sin legacy (VCG-04 — 46 verses), Aaron's priestly failure (Exo 32:21), and Abraham's deception (Gen 20:9). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=15014, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — through multiple reception modes. (1) *Inherited birth-condition*: Psa 51:5 — "in sin did my mother conceive me" (VCG-01) — the record-bearing condition is received at conception, prior to any individual act; (2) *Corporate inheritance through leaders*: VCG-04 — Israel received Jeroboam's sin-record as national inheritance through 46 verses of royal succession; (3) *Priestly loading*: Lev 16:21 — "he shall put them on the head of the goat" — the people's sins are loaded onto the scapegoat in the Day of Atonement ritual; (4) *Unexpected reception*: Gen 20:9 — Abimelech received Abraham's sin-record through deception; he was "about to have sinned" without knowledge.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=15015, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Reception-enabling conditions for the remedy: (1) *Realisation and acknowledgment*: Num 5:6 — "that person realizes his guilt" — conscience engagement enables reception of the remedy process; Psa 32:5 — "I acknowledged my sin" — active acknowledgment opens the remedy path; (2) *Turning*: Eze 18:21 — "turns away from all his sins" — the volitional turn enables divine erasure. Reception-blocking: (1) *Concealment*: Psa 32:5's positive implies that "covering" (the opposite) blocks reception; (2) *Self-deception*: Hos 12:8 — "they cannot find in me iniquity or sin" — prosperity-based self-exculpation blocks remedy reception; (3) *Hardening*: VCG-04 — "he did not depart from" — volitional persistence in the record blocks its erasure.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=15016, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The addressed-record inner-being state is described through multiple metaphors. (1) *Blessedness*: Psa 32:1 — "blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered" — the inner state is beatitude; (2) *Relief from physical weight*: Psa 38:3 — "no health in my bones because of my sin" implies its opposite: health restored when the record is addressed; (3) *Restored relationship with God*: Jer 31:34 — when God remembers sins no more, the new-covenant relational fullness is restored; (4) *No condemnation from the record*: Eze 33:16 — "none of the sins that he has committed shall be remembered against him" — the record generates no further accusation; the person's inner state is free from the record's testimony.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=15017, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced across birth-condition, corporate inheritance, priestly ritual, and unexpected transfer (T4.4.1). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=15018, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. Within existing covenantal bonds: Lev 26:18-28 — the sevenfold discipline is specifically covenant-internal; the record's consequences escalate within the established God-Israel relationship. The record's weight is heavier within the covenant precisely because the relational expectations are higher (1Ki 14:22 — Judah "provoked him to jealousy... more than all that their fathers had done"). Across relational distance: Gen 18:20 — Sodom's sin is great enough to reach God as an outcry even from outside the covenant community; the divine record operates without respect to covenantal membership.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=15019, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The record crosses all covenantal boundaries. Gen 18:20 — Sodom (outside the covenant) has a sin-record before God; Amo 5:12 — "I know how many are your transgressions" is addressed to Israel within the covenant; Rom 3:25 — "former sins" encompassed all humanity in divine forbearance. The divine record is not covenantally bounded: every person, in every relationship with or without the covenant, stands with a sin-record before God. What differs across the covenantal boundary is the specific content of the record (covenant-specific obligations generating covenant-specific entries) rather than the record's existence.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=15020, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The relational scope is universal: Joh 1:29 — "the sin of the world" — the Lamb takes away the sin-record of the entire world, not merely the covenant community. The 205-verse corpus spans individual (Psa 51:5 — "my sin"), communal (Neh 9:2 — "their sins and the iniquities of their fathers"), national (Isa 40:2 — "she has received from the Lord's hand double for all her sins"), and universal (Joh 1:29 — "of the world") scopes. The relational scope is thus comprehensive: no person, community, or nation stands outside the divine record's reach.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=15021, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Relational scope is evidenced at individual, communal, national, and universal levels (T4.5.1–T4.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=15022, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-13]** S — The 205-verse corpus does not explicitly connect the divine record to spiritual beings (angels or adversarial powers). The record operates between the human person and God directly; no angelic or adversarial spiritual agent is explicitly named as a mediator of the record in this corpus. The characteristic is constitutively a human-divine transaction.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=15023, S) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-13]** S — The 205-verse corpus does not explicitly identify sin as divine record as a site of adversarial activity in the sense of the devil or evil one operating through or against the record. VCG-03's Job-surveillance passages (Job 10:6; 13:23; 14:16) address God's scrutiny of Job but do not name adversarial activity as a factor. The accusation function (Isa 59:12 — "our sins testify against us") is the record's own testimony, not named as adversarial mediation.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=15024, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-13]** S — The 205-verse corpus does not evidence angelic ministry as a mediator of the divine record. The record is maintained by God directly and disclosed through prophetic speech (Isa 58:1; Mic 3:8) rather than through angelic agency.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=15025, S) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** S — The evidence is entirely silent on spiritual beings (adversarial or angelic) in relation to sin as divine record. The characteristic is constitutively a direct human-God relational transaction without named spiritual-beings mediation. Noted explicitly.

---

  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-13] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings — T4.6.1–T4.6.4 — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 18:20/20:9; Exo 32:21/34; Lev 16:21/26:18/21/24/28; Num 5:6; Deu 24:16; Jos 24:19; 1Ki 14:16/22/16:2; Psa 32:1/5/38:3/51:2/5; Isa 40:2/43:25/59:2; Jer 31:34; Eze 18:21/33:16; Hos 12:8; Amo 5:12; Mic 3:8; Neh 9:2; Job 10:6/14/13:23; Joh 1:29; Rom 3:25 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=15026, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Both — in the negative direction and through its resolution. (1) *Condition change — negative*: Isa 59:2 — the accumulated record transforms the person's condition from God-accessible to God-separated; the condition before and after the record's accumulation is qualitatively different — prayer now unheard, God's face hidden; (2) *Orientation change — negative*: Isa 3:9 — sin proclaimed without shame reveals an orientation transformed from conscience-engaged to conscience-deadened; (3) *Condition change — positive resolution*: Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the record's erasure transforms the condition from record-burdened to record-free; Psa 32:1 — "blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven" — the condition change is from guilty-standing to blessed-standing.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=15027, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Generally reversible through divine action, with one irreversible dimension. (1) *Reversible*: Eze 33:16 — "none of the sins that he has committed shall be remembered against him" — the record's account-against-the-person is reversible through repentance and divine erasure; Isa 43:25 — God blots out the record as a sovereign act; Jer 31:34 — the new covenant promises permanent erasure; (2) *Irreversible dimension*: Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the internal heart-engraving of the sin-pattern is presented as indelible without divine intervention (the iron pen, diamond point imagery); (3) *Neh 4:5 — the enemies' sins called not to be blotted out* — this prayer for irreversible judgment implies a state where the record can be fixed for reckoning, though the general pattern is erasability through divine action.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=15028, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Transformation evidence is extensive in both directions (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=15029, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — multiple sequences. (1) *Record-accumulation sequence*: 1Sa 12:19 — "we have added to all our sins this evil" — the sequence is prior-record + new-sin → accumulated record; Isa 30:1 — "add sin to sin" — the deliberate accumulation sequence; Lev 26:18-28 — the covenant-discipline escalation sequence: sin → first-level discipline → continued sin → sevenfold → continued sin → fury-level. (2) *Recognition-to-remedy sequence*: Lev 4:23 — sin made known → person realises → brings offering; Num 5:6/7 — realises guilt → confesses → makes restitution. (3) *Erasure sequence*: Jer 31:34 — sin remembered → new covenant initiated → sin remembered no more; Eze 18:21 — wicked person → turns from all sins → keeps statutes → lives.

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=15030, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The accumulation sequence (1Sa 12:19; Isa 30:1; Lev 26:18-28) reveals that the record compounds: each new sin adds to a prior balance; the divine response escalates in proportion to the accumulated record. The recognition-to-remedy sequence (Lev 4:23; Num 5:6/7) reveals that the moment of recognition is decisive: once the record is "made known," the remediation process begins. The erasure sequence (Jer 31:34; Eze 18:21) reveals that erasure is instantaneous in its divine-side declaration ("I will remember their sin no more") but is preceded by the person's volitional turning.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=15031, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Sequence is extensively evidenced across accumulation, recognition-to-remedy, and erasure patterns (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=15032, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Three primary mechanisms. (1) *Accumulation*: the record grows entry by entry through sinning acts; 1Sa 12:19 — "added to all our sins"; Isa 30:1 — "add sin to sin" — the mechanism is additive; (2) *Divine deferred reckoning*: Exo 32:34 — "in the day when I visit, I will visit their sin upon them" — the mechanism is deferred judgment: the record awaits an appointed day; Hos 13:12 — "kept in store" — sealed pending the moment of reckoning; (3) *Covenant-discipline escalation*: Lev 26:18-28 — the mechanism is escalating covenant consequence: sin produces discipline; continued sin produces sevenfold discipline; the mechanism is calibrated response designed to produce turning.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=15033, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. (1) *Individual context*: the record-accumulation mechanism operates entry by entry through personal acts; chet (VCG-01) — each person's record is individually maintained; (2) *Corporate-covenantal context*: the sevenfold discipline mechanism (Lev 26:18-28) operates at the national covenant level, triggering at thresholds of accumulated communal disobedience; (3) *Institutional-legacy context*: VCG-04 — the Jeroboam-sin legacy mechanism operates through royal representational transmission: the king's sin becomes the nation's record through leadership authority; (4) *Divine-surveillance context*: VCG-03 — God's active searching (Job 10:6; 14:16) is a continuous monitoring mechanism, not a threshold-triggered response.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=15034, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Mechanisms are extensively evidenced across four distinct contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=15035, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — extensively. The record directly produces physical suffering. Psa 38:3 — "there is no soundness in my flesh because of your indignation; there is no health in my bones because of my sin" — physical affliction is a direct consequence of the record's weight. Psa 25:18 — "consider my affliction and my trouble, and forgive all my sins" — affliction and sins are placed in parallel as co-present burdens requiring divine attention. Neh 9:37 — "they rule over our bodies and over our livestock as they please, and we are in great distress" — the national sin-record produces the suffering of foreign domination and loss of bodily freedom.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=15036, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Suffering *reveals* the record but does not produce it. Psa 38:3/18 — the physical suffering (no health in bones; no soundness in flesh) makes the person aware of the record's weight and produces the affective response of sorrow; the suffering is the record's consequence becoming visible. Jer 30:14/15 — "because your guilt is great, because your sins are flagrant, I have done these things to you" — suffering is the record made externally manifest, not a new contributor to the record. The record precedes the suffering; the suffering reveals and enforces the record's moral reality.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=15037, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — The relationship between the divine record and suffering is extensively evidenced (Psa 38:3; 25:18; Jer 30:14/15; Neh 9:37). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=15038, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — in both the negative and positive formation arcs. (1) *Negative formation*: the Jeroboam-sin legacy (VCG-04 — 46 verses) is the most extensive evidence of formation-through-record: kings who "walked in" and "did not depart from" the sins of Jeroboam were formed by this persistent sin-pattern into a character defined by it (1Ki 15:3 — "his heart was not wholly true to the Lord his God"). The record accumulates as the character is progressively formed toward sin. (2) *Positive formation*: Eze 33:16 — "he has done what is just and right; he shall surely live" — the person who turns from the record and maintains just conduct is being formed in the positive direction.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=15039, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The divine record is the *formation archive*: it retains the precise history of what the person has become through their choices. VCG-04's repeated formula — "walked in all the sins of Jeroboam... did not depart from them" — is a character-formation statement: the king has been formed by what he habitually walked in. Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — the sin-record's internal inscription is the formation dimension: the accumulated record is not only before God externally but has shaped the person internally through its engraving on the heart. The record's formation role is thus both archival (keeping the history) and constitutional (shaping the inner person through that history).

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=15040, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Formation evidence is extensive (VCG-04 — 46 verses of king-formation through sin-walking; Jer 17:1 — heart-engraving). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=15041, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — two opposed eschatological endpoints. (1) *The new-covenant erasure*: Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — the eschatological fullness of the new covenant is the permanent divine non-remembrance of the sin-record; Dan 9:24 — "seventy weeks are decreed... to put an end to sin, and to atone for iniquity, to bring in everlasting righteousness" — the record's complete termination and the installation of everlasting righteousness; (2) *Eschatological visitation*: Exo 32:34 — "in the day when I visit, I will visit their sin upon them"; Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store" — the sealed record awaits its appointed day of reckoning; Jer 14:10 — "he will remember their iniquity and punish their sins" — the deferred record has a final judgment appointment.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=15042, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-13]** E — For those whose record persists unaddressed: Hos 13:12 — the present sealed record anticipates the future reckoning (the stored record awaits its day); Exo 32:34 — the present deferred visitation anticipates the appointed day of full enforcement. For those whose record is being addressed: Jer 31:34 — the present partial experience of forgiveness anticipates the new-covenant fullness where the record is permanently erased; Dan 9:24 — the present sin-bearing anticipates the eschatological "end of sin" and the installation of everlasting righteousness. The record's present state anticipates either its eschatological enforcement or its eschatological erasure.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=15043, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Eschatological trajectory is extensively evidenced in both directions (T5.6.1 and T5.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=15044, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-13]** E — T2.8 identified four deposit dimensions: heart-engraving (Jer 17:1), relational separation (Isa 59:2), institutional legacy (VCG-04), stored divine record (Hos 13:12). Developmental consequences. (1) *Heart-engraving deposit*: Jer 17:1 — the iron-pen, diamond-point engraving develops into a progressively hardened inner character; the heart's inscription deepens with sustained sin-walking (VCG-04 — kings who walked in all the sins and did not depart); (2) *Relational separation deposit*: Isa 59:2 — the accumulated separation develops into a condition where prayer is no longer heard and God's face is permanently hidden; (3) *Institutional-legacy deposit*: VCG-04 — the 46-verse Jeroboam-sin legacy develops across royal generations into a national character defined by the founding leader's sin-record; (4) *Stored divine record*: Hos 13:12 — the sealed storage develops toward the eschatological day of visitation (Exo 32:34 — "in the day when I visit").

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=15045, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — the most extensive generational evidence in M10. (1) *VCG-04 (46 verses)*: the Jeroboam-sin legacy spans multiple royal generations — from Jeroboam's own act through dozens of successor kings who "walked in his sins and did not depart" — the sin-record is transmitted generationally as an institutional inheritance; (2) *Exo 34:7*: "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children and the children's children, to the third and the fourth generation" — the divine record's generational reach is explicitly named; (3) *Dan 9:16*: "for our sins, and for the iniquities of our fathers, Jerusalem and your people have become a byword" — the combined record of the present generation and their fathers is the basis for national judgment; (4) *Psa 51:5*: "in sin did my mother conceive me" — the inherited sin-record condition at birth names the generational constitutional transmission.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=15046, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-13]** E — T2.8 found the most extensive deposit evidence in M10. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the full developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=15047, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur most frequently within the 205-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — the conscience's persistent inner experience of the record overlaps directly with guilt as inner-being state; the objective record (CHAR-13) and its subjective experience (CHAR-17) co-occur throughout the Psalms corpus. (2) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Num 5:7 — "he shall confess his sin"; Psa 32:5 — "I will confess my transgressions" — confession is the characteristic behavioural response to the record's acknowledgment; the two are inseparable in the recognition-to-remedy sequence. (3) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: VCG-05 and VCG-04 throughout — the record is filled by wilful sinning acts; the two characteristics are causally related across the corpus. (4) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: VCG-04 — "walked in all the sins of Jeroboam... did not depart from them" — habitual defection is what accumulates the Jeroboam-sin legacy; CHAR-6 is the mechanism generating VCG-04's institutional record.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=15048, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-13 as the *moral-accounting dimension* of the M10 cluster: it receives the entries from CHAR-1 (wilful sinning acts) and CHAR-6 (habitual defection), generates the inner experience of CHAR-17 (guilt), and requires CHAR-3 (confession) for its address. CHAR-13 is the characteristic that converts sinning acts into a standing moral account. It is not the act (CHAR-1), not the habit (CHAR-6), not the inner experience (CHAR-17), but the objective ledger that sin creates before God — the archive that gives all other sinning characteristics their lasting moral weight.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=15049, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are extensively evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=15050, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-13]** E — CHAR-13 consistently *follows* CHAR-1 (wilful sinning acts create the record entries) and *precedes* CHAR-17 (the objective record generates the subjective experience of guilt) and CHAR-3 (the record's recognition prompts confession). The sequence is: wilful sinning act (CHAR-1) → record entry created (CHAR-13) → conscience registers (CHAR-17 guilt) → confession prompted (CHAR-3) → atonement/erasure. VCG-04's Jeroboam-sin legacy shows CHAR-13 following CHAR-6 (habitual defection generates the accumulated institutional record).

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=15051, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The relationship is *causal at every junction*: CHAR-1's sinning causally produces CHAR-13's record entry (Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed" → record requiring atonement). CHAR-13's record causally produces CHAR-17's guilt experience (Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — the objective record produces the conscience's inner suffering). CHAR-13's recognition causally enables CHAR-3's confession (Num 5:6/7 — realising guilt → confessing). The sequence is entirely causal: each characteristic in the sequence produces the next through its own operation.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=15052, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Sequential patterns (causal chain) are extensively evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=15053, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: Psa 51:3 — the record produces the persistent inner guilt experience; Eze 33:10 — "our sins are upon us, and we rot away because of them" — the record produces the inner despair that is the guilt experience's extreme form; (2) *Physical suffering*: Psa 38:3 — the record produces bodily deterioration as a secondary consequence; (3) *Relational separation*: Isa 59:2 — the accumulated record produces the condition of God's face hidden and prayer unheard; (4) *National judgment*: VCG-04 — the accumulated Jeroboam-sin record produces the prophetically announced national consequences (1Ki 14:16 — Israel given up; 2Ki 24:3 — Judah removed from sight).

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=15054, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: every sinning act (Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed") creates a record entry; CHAR-1 is the primary producer of CHAR-13's entries; (2) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: VCG-04's sustained walking in Jeroboam's sins — the habitual pattern generates the institutional record that defines successive kings' reigns; (3) *CHAR-4 (Conscience suppression)*: while not explicitly named in this corpus, the failure to address the record (leading to its accumulation) is enabled by conscience suppression — Jer 16:10 — the people's failure to identify their sin under judgment implies CHAR-4-like suppression enabling record accumulation.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=15055, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-13]** E — CHAR-13 is a constituent element of the broader concept of moral accountability before God. Within M10, CHAR-13 is the *ledger dimension* that gives CHAR-11 (universal condition), CHAR-1 (wilful sinning), and CHAR-6 (habitual defection) their moral permanence: without the divine record, sins would be transient acts; the record is what makes them moral facts retained before God. Conversely, CHAR-17 (guilt as inner-being state) constitutes the subjective experiential face of CHAR-13's objective reality.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=15056, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are extensively evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=15057, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. Chat.tat (H2403B — VCG-05, 113 verses) is the dominant shared vocabulary: this term is the primary root across CHAR-1 through CHAR-13 in M10. Chet (H2399 — VCG-01, 32 verses) is shared with CHAR-1's personal-liability register. Cha.ta (H2398 — verb — VCG-05) is shared across all M10 characteristics that use the verb form of sinning. The distinction of CHAR-13 is not exclusive vocabulary but the *objective-record register* of the shared vocabulary: chat.tat in CHAR-13 names sin as the moral fact retained before God, while in CHAR-1 it names the discrete act itself.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=15058, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. Chat.tat (H2403B) and cha.ta (H2398) share the same cha.ta root — the "miss the mark" semantic field. Chet (H2399) is the derived noun of the same root. All three terms in the 205-verse corpus share a single root architecture; the CHAR-13 distinction is the noun-condition-before-God deployment of the shared root rather than any exclusive lexical field. The NT hamartia (G0266 — Joh 1:29; hamartēma G0265 — Rom 3:25) deploys the equivalent Greek semantic field.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=15059, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The vocabulary sharing confirms that CHAR-13 is not a separate semantic domain but the *objective-record register* of the same cha.ta/hamartia semantic field that all M10 characteristics share. The characteristics are distinguished not by different vocabularies but by different registers of the same vocabulary: CHAR-1 (the act), CHAR-6 (the habitual pattern), CHAR-11 (the structural condition), CHAR-12 (the enslaving power), CHAR-13 (the retained objective record) are all names for different aspects of what the cha.ta root names as a totality. CHAR-13's distinctiveness is the record-dimension — sin as a retained moral fact — not a different vocabulary.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=15060, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Extensive vocabulary sharing across M10 (chat.tat/cha.ta/chet/hamartia root-family) is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=15061, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-13]** E — CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state) most closely resembles CHAR-13 — both address the person's standing in relation to sin's weight. The precise distinction: CHAR-13 is the *objective record* — the moral account existing before God regardless of the person's awareness or feeling (definition: "whether they feel it or not"); CHAR-17 is the *subjective inner experience* — the felt sense of guilt as the conscience's response to the record. The record (CHAR-13) exists before God independently; guilt (CHAR-17) is the inner-being experience that the record may or may not produce depending on conscience-engagement. CHAR-13 is the objective; CHAR-17 is the subjective experience of the same underlying reality.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=15062, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The overlap is at Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — where the objective record ("my sin") and the subjective experience ("ever before me" — the conscience's persistent presentation) co-occur in a single phrase. The precise boundary: "my sin" is CHAR-13's domain (the objective record); "ever before me" is CHAR-17's domain (the inner experience of the record being persistently present to conscience). The verse is at the boundary precisely because it names both the record and its inner-experiential dimension in a single statement.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=15063, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The distinction is one of *kind and subjectivity*: CHAR-13 is the objective moral fact; CHAR-17 is the subjective inner-being experience. They are different kinds of phenomena (objective account vs subjective experience) rather than different degrees of the same thing. The direction differs: CHAR-13 faces toward God (the record is before God); CHAR-17 faces toward the person's own inner awareness (guilt is the person's inner experience of what is before God).

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=15064, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=15065, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Several verses function as shared anchors. (1) Psa 51:3 — "my sin is ever before me" — shared anchor with CHAR-17 (guilt as inner-being state); (2) Psa 51:5 — "in sin did my mother conceive me" — shared anchor with CHAR-11 (universal condition — birth-origin framing); (3) Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart" — primary anchor for CHAR-13; also an anchor for the heart-dimension analyses of CHAR-4 and CHAR-17; (4) Lev 16:21 — Day of Atonement — shared anchor with CHAR-3 (confession — the high priest's confession over the goat) and the broader priestly atonement system; (5) Num 32:23 — "your sin will find you out" — shared with CHAR-1 (the act's inescapable consequence).

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=15066, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Psa 51:3 shared with CHAR-17 confirms that the objective record (CHAR-13) and the subjective guilt experience (CHAR-17) are the two faces of the same moral reality — they co-occur in the same verse because they are inseparable experientially even if analytically distinct. Jer 17:1's heart-engraving (shared with CHAR-4/17) reveals that the divine record has a heart-internal inscription dimension that connects it to conscience-suppression (CHAR-4) and guilt-experience (CHAR-17) at the constitutional level.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=15067, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Shared verse anchors are identified across multiple characteristics (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=15068, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The objective-record dimension (sin retained before God) is unique to CHAR-13 within M10. However: (1) The internal-inscription dimension (Jer 17:1 — heart-engraving) is shared with CHAR-4 (conscience suppression — the heart that has been written upon) and CHAR-17 (guilt — the heart's experiential register); (2) The confession-requiring dimension (Num 5:7; Psa 32:5) is shared with CHAR-3 (confession); (3) The accumulation dimension (1Sa 12:19; Isa 30:1) is shared with CHAR-6 (habitual defection — the pattern generating accumulation); (4) The generational-legacy dimension (VCG-04; Exo 34:7) is shared with any generational-transmission characteristic in M10.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=15069, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-13 as the *archival centre* of the M10 cluster: it shares dimensions with CHAR-1 (the acts that fill it), CHAR-3 (the confession that addresses it), CHAR-4 (the heart-inscription that is its internal form), CHAR-6 (the habits that accumulate it), and CHAR-17 (the guilt that is its inner-experiential face). CHAR-13 is the characteristic that all others feed into or respond to — it is the moral accounting dimension that gives the other characteristics their lasting significance before God.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=15070, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-13]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced across multiple adjacent characteristics (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.
