## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=15071, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Five term-groups across the 205 verses. (1) **H2403B chat.tat** (VCG-05 — 113 verses): the dominant noun — derived from H2398 cha.ta ("miss the mark"). As a noun, chat.tat names the sin-entity, the act-as-fact retained before God. The noun form makes sin a thing that persists: "the sin which he has committed" (Lev 4:23) — the committed sin is now an object that requires remedy. (2) **H2401/H2403A cha.ta.ah** (VCG-02 — 9 verses): a feminine noun form, naming sin as an objective moral category. Jer 17:1 — "the sin of Judah is written" — cha.ta.ah as the subject of the writing metaphor, naming sin as the inscribed entity. (3) **H2399 chet** (VCG-01 — 32 verses): the personal-liability noun — "that man shall bear his sin" (Num 9:13); "each one shall be put to death for his own sin" (Deu 24:16). Chet names sin as a personal burden carried by the individual. (4) **H2398 cha.ta** (verb — multiple VCG-05 verses): "whoever has sinned against me" (Exo 32:33); "your sin will find you out" (Num 32:23) — the verb form names the act that creates the record entry. (5) **G0266 hamartia / G0265 hamartēma** (NT — Joh 1:29; Rom 3:25): the Greek equivalents — hamartia (the sin of the world as a collective burden); hamartēma (individual sinful acts — Rom 3:25 — "former sins" passed over).

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=15072, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The grammatical range of chat.tat (H2403B) is the most analytically significant. (1) *Noun as subject of divine action*: Exo 32:32 — "if you will forgive their sin" — sin as the direct object of the forgiveness request; Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins" — sin as the object of divine non-remembrance; (2) *Noun in possessive construction*: "my sin" (Psa 38:3), "their sins" (Lev 26:18), "his sin" (Lev 4:26) — the possessive marks the personal ownership of the record; (3) *Noun as the subject of a finding*: Exo 32:34 — "I will visit their sin upon them" — sin as the direct object of divine visitation; Hos 13:12 — "his sin is kept in store" — sin as the object of divine storage. The grammatical range moves between object (of divine action) and possession (of the human person) — naming both the divine handling and the personal ownership of the record.

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=15073, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The semantic range of chat.tat within CHAR-13's corpus is the widest in M10: (1) *Sin as outcry before God*: Gen 18:20 — "their sin is very grave" — the weight registered before God; (2) *Sin as book-entry*: Exo 32:32/33 — the divine book; (3) *Sin as stored account*: Hos 13:12 — "kept in store"; (4) *Sin as heart-inscription*: Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart"; (5) *Sin as self-discovering reality*: Num 32:23 — "your sin will find you out"; (6) *Sin as testimony*: Isa 59:12 — "our sins testify against us"; (7) *Sin as burden borne double*: Isa 40:2 — "received from the Lord's hand double for all her sins." This range covers every aspect of the characteristic's definition: recorded, accumulated, named, not hidden, the person carries it.

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=15074, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes. The three noun forms distinguish aspects: (1) *Chat.tat (H2403B) — the act-as-retained-fact*: sin as the thing committed that now has objective standing; (2) *Chet (H2399) — the personal-liability dimension*: sin as what the individual bears ("bear his sin" — Num 9:13); (3) *Cha.ta.ah (H2401) — the category-requiring-covering dimension*: sin as the objective moral category that needs a covering act (Psa 32:1 — "whose sin is covered"). These three noun forms, while related, distinguish sin's facthood (chat.tat), its personal-bearing (chet), and its category-status requiring concealment (cha.ta.ah).

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=15075, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The structural opposite is named through the erasure-vocabulary rather than through a distinct term for the record-free state. The primary erasure terms: (1) *Kāsāh* (to cover — Psa 32:1; 85:2 — "covered all their sin"); (2) *Māḥāh* (to blot out — Isa 43:25; 44:22; Eze 33:16 — "blotted out"); (3) *Zākar lōʾ* (to remember no more — Jer 31:34; Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember"); (4) *Šālaḵ ʾaḥărê gēwkā* (to cast behind the back — Isa 38:17); (5) *Šālaḵ bimṣūlôt* (to cast into the depths — Mic 7:19). The structural opposite is the record-cleared state described through five distinct erasure metaphors.

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=15076, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — through the bearer-of-sin language. Num 9:13 — "that man shall bear his sin" (*nāśāʾ ḥeṭʾô* — "shall carry his chet"); Deu 24:16 — "each one shall be put to death for his own sin" (*ḥeṭʾô*). These constructions identify the person-type as "the one who bears sin" — the individual moral subject who carries a personal record. Isa 53:12 — "he bore the sin of many" — the servant's person-type is the sin-bearer, creating a positive person-type in contrast to the negative.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=15077, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Yes — the forgiveness-petition vocabulary. Exo 10:17 — "forgive my sin, please" (*sĕʾ-nāʾ ḥaṭṭāṯî*); Exo 34:9 — "pardon our iniquity and our sin" (*wĕsālaḥtā laʿāwōnênû ûlĕḥaṭṭāʾṯênû*); Psa 25:7 — "remember not the sins of my youth" — the petitionary vocabulary of seeking record-erasure from God is extensive throughout the corpus. The supplication-seeking is directed toward the removal of the record rather than toward receiving a quality.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=15078, E) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The vocabulary relationship reveals *continuity of the divine-record concept from OT to NT with eschatological resolution in the NT*. The OT develops the record through five term-groups (chat.tat, cha.ta.ah, chet, cha.ta verb, and the erasure vocabulary) across 203 of the 205 verses. The NT (Joh 1:29; Rom 3:25) deploys the equivalent Greek vocabulary (hamartia, hamartēma) to name the OT record's eschatological resolution: Joh 1:29 — "the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world" — the world's accumulated OT record is the burden the Lamb removes; Rom 3:25 — "former sins" passed over — the OT record's unresolved account is now addressed through Christ's propitiation.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=15079, E) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The collective singular "the sin of the world" (Joh 1:29 — *hē hamartia tou kosmou*) represents a NT deployment that gathers the entire accumulated OT-and-human divine record into a single noun-entity borne by the Lamb. While not a newly coined term, the *totalization* of the sin-record into a single collective entity ("the sin of the world") is a NT conceptual move: all individual, corporate, and generational records are gathered into one burden that the Lamb bears. This totalization metaphor is the NT's distinctive contribution to the characteristic's vocabulary.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=15080, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The vocabulary arc — from chat.tat's individual act-as-fact (Lev 4:23 — "the sin which he has committed") through chet's personal-bearing (Num 9:13 — "bear his sin") through cha.ta.ah's heart-inscription (Jer 17:1 — "engraved on the tablet of their heart") through the storage/book metaphors (Hos 13:12 — "kept in store"; Exo 32:32 — "your book") through the erasure vocabulary (Isa 43:25 — blotted out; Mic 7:19 — cast into the sea) to Joh 1:29's totalization ("the sin of the world" lifted by the Lamb) — reveals a characteristic whose vocabulary maps the complete divine-record lifecycle: creation of the entry, personal bearing, internal inscription, divine storage, escalating consequences, and finally eschatological erasure.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=15081, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The primary anchor is Jer 17:1: "The sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of diamond it is engraved on the tablet of their heart, and on the horns of their altars." *Cha.ta.ah* (H2403A/H2401 — "the sin of Judah") functions as the subject of the writing metaphor: sin is the content being inscribed. The double-instrument metaphor (iron pen + diamond point) functions as an intensifier of permanence: the record is inscribed with the hardest possible writing tools, implying the inscription cannot be self-erased. The dual location (heart tablet + altar horns) functions to name the record's internal (heart — private inner register) and external (altars — public institutional register) locations simultaneously.

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=15082, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Five literary forms across the 205 verses. (1) *Legal-liturgical code* (Lev 4-5; 16; Num 5; Deu 19-24): requires reading as prescriptive regulation governing the record's ritual handling; the priestly system is the institutional framework; (2) *Covenant treaty and sanction* (Lev 26; Deu 9): requires reading within the covenant-obligation context; the sevenfold discipline is calibrated covenant-sanction; (3) *Historical narrative* (Kings/Chronicles VCG-04): requires reading within the Deuteronomistic history's evaluative framework; the Jeroboam-sin formula is the historian's moral verdict; (4) *Wisdom poetry/Psalms* (Psa 25; 32; 38; 51; 59; 79; 85; 103; 109): requires reading as individual and communal prayer addressed to God; (5) *Prophetic oracle* (Isaiah; Jeremiah; Hosea; Amos; Micah; Ezekiel): requires reading within the covenantal-lawsuit or restoration-oracle framework.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=15083, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Three key argument structures. (1) *Jer 17:1's inscription argument*: premise — the sin of Judah is permanently inscribed; implication — it cannot be self-erased; conclusion — divine intervention is the only remedy. (2) *Num 32:23's inescapability argument*: premise — you have sinned against the Lord; conclusion — your sin will find you out; the argument is that the record's self-revelation is inevitable regardless of concealment effort. (3) *Isa 43:25's divine-erasure argument*: premise — God blots out transgressions for his own sake; conclusion — he will not remember sins; the argument is that the record's erasure is entirely a sovereign divine act motivated by God's own purposes, not human merit.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=15084, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Four contextual settings. (1) *Priestly-liturgical* (Lev 4-5; 16): the Temple/Tabernacle ritual system as the institutional setting for managing the record through sacrifice and atonement; (2) *Covenantal-national* (VCG-04 — Kings/Chronicles; Lev 26): the Sinai covenant as the framework within which the record's accumulation triggers escalating covenant sanctions; (3) *Individual prayer and penitential Psalms*: personal and communal prayer as the setting for the record's acknowledgment and petition for erasure; (4) *Prophetic confrontation and consolation*: the prophet as the one who declares the record to those who cannot see it (Isa 58:1; Mic 3:8) and announces its future erasure (Jer 31:34; Isa 43:25).

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=15085, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Jer 17:1 functions as the primary anchor: "The sin of Judah is written with a pen of iron; with a point of diamond it is engraved on the tablet of their heart, and on the horns of their altars." It most fully expresses CHAR-13's essential character because: (1) it names sin as a *written record* (the characteristic's definitive metaphor); (2) it uses the *hardest writing instruments* (permanence — what human effort cannot erase); (3) it locates the record *internally* (heart tablet — the inner dimension) *and externally* (altar horns — the institutional dimension); (4) it is the only verse in the 205-verse corpus that explicitly deploys the writing/engraving metaphor as CHAR-13's primary image.

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=15086, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Jer 17:1 uniquely reveals the *dual-location structure* of the divine record: it is simultaneously inscribed on "the tablet of their heart" (internal constitutional location) and "on the horns of their altars" (external institutional location). No other verse in the 205-verse corpus places these two locations in explicit parallel. This dual-location reveals: the divine record is not only a divine-side accounting (before God in heaven) but also an internal heart-inscription and an external institutional embedding — the record has three locations simultaneously: before God, within the heart, and in the institutional-cultic life of the community.

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=15087, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-13]** E — **Moral psychology** (specifically Jonathan Haidt's moral foundations theory and Paul Ricoeur's philosophy of guilt) and **jurisprudence** (legal record-keeping and the doctrine of precedent) provide the most illuminating frameworks. Moral psychology illuminates the gap between objective moral culpability and subjective guilt experience — precisely the gap CHAR-13 names (the record exists "whether they feel it or not"). Jurisprudence illuminates the record-keeping metaphors (Exo 32:32/33 — divine book; Hos 13:12 — kept in store; Jer 17:1 — written with iron pen) through the concept of a permanent legal record that cannot be self-expunged.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=15088, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Jurisprudence illuminates the divine-book metaphors (Exo 32:32/33) through the concept of a legal register: in law, a criminal record exists independently of the offender's subjective awareness or remorse; the record is the objective judicial fact of what was done. This maps precisely onto CHAR-13's definition — the record exists "whether they feel it or not." Moral psychology illuminates Jer 16:10's people ("What is our sin?") as a case of *moral disengagement* (Bandura) — the cognitive distancing mechanisms that prevent persons from recognising their own moral record. Both frameworks confirm that objective moral records and subjective moral awareness are distinct phenomena.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=15089, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-13]** E — The primary divergence: jurisprudence treats legal records as contingent human constructs — they can be expunged, sealed, or corrected through legal process initiated by human authorities. The verse evidence treats the divine record as permanent until God himself erases it: Jer 17:1 — iron pen, diamond point; Hos 13:12 — "kept in store." The erasure is entirely God's sovereign act (Isa 43:25 — "for my own sake"), not a human-initiated legal process. The divergence reveals that the divine record is not analogous to a human legal record: it is permanent, sovereignly kept, and can only be addressed by divine action — not self-erasure, not legal manoeuvre, not statute of limitations.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=15090, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-13]** E — Moral psychology surfaces the *intergenerational transmission* of guilt through communal-narrative processes — the way in which communities pass on moral records through story, ritual, and identity formation. VCG-04's Jeroboam-sin legacy is precisely this: a communally transmitted moral record that successive generations inherit through the narrative of their community's founding sin-pattern. The framework suggests that the mechanism of VCG-04's transmission is not merely legal or covenantal but also narrative and identity-forming — the Jeroboam-sin legacy functions as a shared moral identity narrative that each king either receives and perpetuates or breaks from. This narrative-identity dimension of the record's generational transmission is acknowledged but not fully theorised in the corpus. No further verse investigation is required — the gap is acknowledged as a human-science complement to the corpus's primarily covenantal framing.

---

T7 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       20/20 ✓ (T7.1.1–T7.1.10, T7.2.1–T7.2.6, T7.3.1–T7.3.4)
  2. Scope marker:       20× [CHAR-13] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 18:20; Exo 32:32/33; Lev 4:23; Num 9:13/32:23; Deu 24:16; Psa 32:1/85:2; Isa 38:17/40:2/43:25/44:22/53:12/58:1/59:12; Jer 16:10/17:1/31:34; Eze 33:16; Hos 13:12; Mic 7:19; Joh 1:29; Rom 3:25 — all in §3)
  → PASS. All 8 tiers complete.

## CHAR-14 — Forgiveness sought and received (189 rows: E=183 S=6 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=15091, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The 34-verse corpus reveals God's character across four dimensions. (1) *God as the one who provides the means of atonement*: Exo 29:36 — the daily sin offering is God's prescribed institution; Lev 23:27/28 — the Day of Atonement is God's appointed holy convocation; he has established the framework within which forgiveness is available. (2) *God as the one who does not count trespasses*: 2Cor 5:19 — "in Christ God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them" — the divine disposition is toward non-imputation, not toward accumulating the record against the person. (3) *God as the one who provides blood-atonement*: Mat 26:28 — "my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins" — God himself provides the atoning blood rather than requiring the creature to supply it. (4) *God as the one whose forgiveness has eschatological scope*: Mar 3:28 — "all sins will be forgiven the children of man" — God's forgiveness has universal potential scope, with only one exception (Mar 3:29 — blasphemy against the Holy Spirit).

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=15092, E) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — on the giving side. God is the one who forgives: 2Cor 5:19 — "God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses"; Col 2:13 — "God made alive together with him, having forgiven us all our trespasses"; Eph 1:7 — "the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace." The seeking side is human (the person seeks forgiveness); the granting side is divine. The characteristic thus involves both divine action (granting) and human action (seeking); Scripture attributes the granting explicitly to God.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=15093, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-14]** E — God never seeks forgiveness — he has no offense-record requiring another's pardon. The silence confirms the asymmetry at the characteristic's core: the seeking is constitutively creaturely (the fallen creature's movement toward God), while the granting is constitutively divine. This asymmetry reveals that the characteristic cannot be abstracted from the God-creature relationship: it names a specific direction of moral movement (creature → God in seeking; God → creature in granting) that requires both parties to be what they are.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=15094, E) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The characteristic serves the purpose of *restoring the God-human relationship ruptured by sin*. Lev 16:30 — "you shall be clean before the Lord from all your sins" — the purpose is restored purity before God; Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... God made alive" — the purpose is life where death held sway; Act 2:38 — repentance and baptism → forgiveness → receipt of the Holy Spirit — the purpose is access to the Spirit who was blocked by the sin-record. The characteristic thus serves the restoration of the person's full relational capacity before God.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=15095, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Entirely a response to the fallen condition. The priestly atonement system (VCG-01/03) presupposes accumulated sin; the forgiveness declarations (VCG-02) address the specific sin-record requiring removal. Lev 4:3 — "if it is the anointed priest who sins... he shall offer... for the sin that he has committed" — the whole framework is triggered by committed sin, which belongs to the fallen condition. The kip.pu.rim atonement system is God's provision for the fallen condition, not a designed feature of unfallen creation.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=15096, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the OT atonement system itself is eschatologically oriented. VCG-01's kip.pu.rim sacrifices are annual, repeated, provisional — Exo 30:10 — "once in the year throughout your generations" — each annual cycle points forward to a definitive resolution not yet accomplished. Mat 26:28 — "my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins" — the new covenant's blood-forgiveness is the eschatological fulfillment the OT system anticipated: a once-for-all atonement that permanently resolves the annual cycle's provisional character.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=15097, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The characteristic expresses the divine image through the image-bearer's capacity for *repentant return to God*: only a being made for God-relationship can seek forgiveness from God. Exo 32:30 — Moses "will go up to the Lord; perhaps I can make atonement for your sin" — the intercessory seeking is the image-bearer exercising the mediatorial capacity that belongs to the image's vocation. Act 2:38 — the capacity to repent, be baptised, and receive the Holy Spirit names the image-bearer's full moral-and-relational restoration capacity, which only makes sense for a being whose image-bearing orientation is toward God.

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=15098, E) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The seeking dimension is exclusively creaturely; the granting dimension is divine. The characteristic is thus relationally shared: God grants (2Cor 5:19; Col 2:13; Eph 1:7) and the creature seeks (Exo 32:30 — Moses; Act 2:38 — repentance; Act 10:43 — belief). The characteristic requires both poles: without the creature's seeking it is not enacted; without God's granting it is not completed. It is the most explicitly bi-directional characteristic in M10.

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=15099, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The presence of the seeking dimension reveals the image in its *fallen-but-recoverable* condition: the image bears the sin-record (CHAR-13) that requires forgiveness, is capable of recognising this (conscience), and has the volitional capacity to turn toward God in seeking (Act 2:38 — "repent"). Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... God made alive" — the image's condition before forgiveness is spiritual death; the forgiveness-received transforms the condition to life. The characteristic's presence thus reveals both the depth of the image's fallen condition (requiring forgiveness) and the fullness of its recovery capacity (able to seek, receive, and be made alive).

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=15100, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the most concentrated typological evidence in M10. The entire VCG-01 kip.pu.rim system (12 verses) is typological: the Day of Atonement (Lev 23:27/28; 25:9), the daily sin offerings (Exo 29:36; 30:10), and the priestly bearing of iniquity (Lev 10:17) all function as institutional types pointing toward the antitypical atoning work of Christ. Mat 26:28 — "my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins" — is the antitype declaration: the covenant blood replaces the animal blood; the permanent forgiveness replaces the annual provisional covering.

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=15101, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-14]** E — OT institutional type → NT Christological antitype. The direction is: the annual kip.pu.rim cycle (OT type) → the once-for-all blood of the new covenant (NT antitype — Mat 26:28). The priestly mediation (Lev 10:17 — priest bears the congregation's iniquity) → Christ's high-priestly mediation (the broader NT theological context, evidenced in this corpus by 2Cor 5:19 — God in Christ reconciling). The atonement money bringing Israel to remembrance (Exo 30:16) → Christ's name through which everyone who believes receives forgiveness (Act 10:43). The typological direction is consistently from OT ritual institution to NT Christological fulfillment.

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=15102, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Typological use is extensively evidenced (T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T0 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       12/12 ✓ (T0.1.1–T0.1.3, T0.2.1–T0.2.3, T0.3.1–T0.3.3, T0.4.1–T0.4.3)
  2. Scope marker:       12× [CHAR-14] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 29:36/30:10/16/32:30; Lev 4:3/10:17/16:30/23:27/28/25:9; Mat 26:28; Mar 3:28/29; Act 2:38/10:43; 2Cor 5:19; Eph 1:7; Col 1:14/2:13 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T1.

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=15103, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Forgiveness sought and received." Three signals. (1) *Forgiveness*: the moral-relational transaction at stake — not merely guilt-relief but the objective removal of the sin-record that separates the person from God; (2) *Sought*: the inner movement originates in the person — a volitional turning toward God to appeal for what only God can grant; Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized... for the forgiveness of your sins" — the seeking is a commanded human movement; (3) *Received*: the characteristic names completion — not merely seeking but the divine response of granting; Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven" — the declaration closes the transaction. The name's compound form ("sought and received") signals the characteristic's bi-directional structure: the human movement of seeking and the divine act of granting are both necessary constituents.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=15104, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Four term-groups. (1) **kip.pu.rim (H3725)** (VCG-01 — 12 verses): the noun-plural of kāpar — "coverings/atonements." The root kāpar means "to cover, to atone, to make expiation" — the mechanism is concealment/covering of the sin-record. The plural form names not a single act but the institution: the Day of Atonement as a structured series of covering acts. (2) **chat.tat (H2403B/H2403H)** (VCG-01/03): the sin-offering as the ritual vehicle of seeking forgiveness — the animal bears the sin that the person brings. (3) **cha.ta.ah (H2401)** (VCG-02): the sin named before God in the seeking-context (Exo 32:30/31 — Moses naming the great sin). (4) **hamartia (G0266) / hamartēma (G0265) / paraptōma (G3900)** (VCG-02): NT terms for the sin/trespasses whose forgiveness is declared through Christ's blood (Mat 26:28; Mar 3:28/29; Eph 1:7; Col 2:13).

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=15105, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-14]** E — All three. *Directional*: "sought" names a movement from the person toward God; "received" names the return movement from God to the person. The directional structure is toward-God then from-God — a bilateral movement that constitutes the full forgiveness-event. *Relational*: the characteristic is constitutively relational — it requires the person-God relationship as its operative field; forgiveness cannot be sought from or granted by anyone else (Mar 2:7 context — "who can forgive sins but God alone?" — referenced in CHAR-11 but structurally operative here). *Constitutional*: Act 2:38 — repentance + baptism → forgiveness → Spirit-reception — the constitutional change (receiving the Spirit) is the consequence of the characteristic's completion.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=15106, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The seeking dimension is a *volitional-relational act*: the will's turn toward God in appeal. Exo 32:30 — "I will go up to the Lord; perhaps I can make atonement for your sin" — Moses's going up is a volitional movement; Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized" — the repentance is an inner volitional turn. The receiving dimension is a *relational-condition change*: the person's standing before God is altered by the divine declaration. Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven" — the declaration changes the woman's relational condition before God. Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... made alive" — the received forgiveness is a constitutional condition change. The characteristic is thus a *volitional act (seeking) producing a relational-condition change (receiving)*.

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=15107, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The corpus reveals four constituent elements. (1) *The inner recognition*: Lev 4:14 — "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" — the sin must be recognised before it can be sought-for; (2) *The seeking act*: Exo 32:30 — Moses going up; Act 2:38 — repenting; Act 10:43 — believing; (3) *The mediatorial mechanism*: VCG-01/03 — the priestly/sacrificial system; VCG-02 — Christ's blood and name; (4) *The divine declaration or grant*: Lev 16:30 — "you shall be clean before the Lord"; Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven"; Col 2:13 — God "having forgiven us all our trespasses."

---

#### T1.2.3 (cf=15108, E) — What is the current best working description of this characteristic — encapsulating its constitutional location, the faculties it primarily engages, and its impact on the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Working description: *Forgiveness sought and received (CHAR-14) is the bi-directional characteristic in which the inner person moves volitionally toward God in appeal for the removal of the sin-record (the seeking dimension), and God responds by declaring, granting, or enacting the covering/removal of that record (the receiving dimension). The corpus evidences this through three registers: (1) The Levitical kip.pu.rim atonement system (VCG-01 — 12 verses): institutionalised annual and occasional seeking through the prescribed sacrifice and priestly mediation; (2) The Levitical sin-offering (VCG-03 — 10 verses): individual and communal seeking through the chat.tat offering system when specific sins become known; (3) Christological forgiveness declaration (VCG-02 — 12 verses): seeking through repentance and faith and receiving through Christ's atoning blood and authoritative declaration. The characteristic's primary anchor is Mat 26:28 — "my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins" — naming the definitive once-for-all mechanism that replaces the annual kip.pu.rim cycle.*

---

#### T1.3.1 (cf=15109, E) — What is the structural opposite of this characteristic — the inner-being reality that stands against or excludes it?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The structural opposite is the *unforgiven sin-record remaining in force*. On the human side: the failure to seek — Lev 4:14 implies that when sin "becomes known" and no remedy is sought, the record remains; Jos 24:19 (CHAR-13 corpus) — God "will not forgive your transgressions or your sins" names the condition of unforgiveness. On the divine side: Mar 3:29 — "whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit never has forgiveness, but is guilty of an eternal sin" — the single case in the corpus where the seeking-and-receiving is permanently foreclosed: the eternal sin that has no forgiveness names the absolute structural opposite.

---

#### T1.3.2 (cf=15110, E) — What does this characteristic explicitly exclude or resist?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The characteristic explicitly excludes: (1) *Self-generated forgiveness*: the priestly mediation (Lev 10:17 — priest bears the congregation's iniquity) and Christ's blood (Mat 26:28) both establish that forgiveness requires an external mediatorial mechanism; self-forgiveness is excluded as an option; (2) *Forgiveness without seeking*: Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized... for the forgiveness" — the grammatical structure makes repentance the condition of forgiveness-reception; passive receipt without seeking is not evidenced; (3) *Forgiveness for the eternal sin*: Mar 3:29 — the blasphemy against the Holy Spirit resists all seeking; the characteristic cannot operate in that direction.

---

#### T1.3.3 (cf=15111, E) — What is this characteristic not — where does it end and something else begin?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Three boundaries. (1) *Not CHAR-3 (Confession)*: confession is one of the seeking-acts that may accompany forgiveness-seeking (Num 5:7 in CHAR-13's context; Psa 32:5), but it is the speech-act of acknowledgment rather than the full characteristic of seeking-and-receiving forgiveness; CHAR-3 is a component that may precede CHAR-14. (2) *Not CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record)*: CHAR-13 is the record that requires forgiveness; CHAR-14 is the seeking and receiving of that record's removal — CHAR-13 is what CHAR-14 addresses. (3) *Not CHAR-11 (Sin as universal condition)*: CHAR-11 is the structural condition all persons stand in; CHAR-14 is the specific seeking-and-receiving event that addresses one dimension of that condition.

---

#### T1.4.1 (cf=15112, E) — In what distinct ways does this characteristic operate within the inner person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Three distinct inner modes. (1) *As volitional turning toward God*: Act 2:38 — "repent" (metanoeō — a change of mind and direction); Exo 32:30 — "I will go up to the Lord" — the will's movement toward God as the source of forgiveness is the primary inner mode; (2) *As self-affliction in seeking*: Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the Day of Atonement requires inner self-affliction as the seeking posture; (3) *As faith receiving the declaration*: Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name" — belief is the inner receptive mode in the NT register; Luk 7:47 — "her sins... are forgiven — for she loved much" — love is the inner receptive disposition evidencing that forgiveness has been received.

---

#### T1.4.2 (cf=15113, E) — Does this characteristic operate differently depending on context, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — significantly. (1) *Levitical priestly context* (VCG-01): the seeking operates through prescribed ritual (bringing the animal, laying hands, priestly action); the individual's inner seeking is mediated through the institutional framework; (2) *Individual-sin context* (VCG-03 — Lev 4:3/14/5:6/10): the seeking is triggered when the sin "becomes known" — an individual recognition moment generates the seeking-event; (3) *Intercessory context* (Exo 32:30/31 — VCG-02/03): Moses seeks forgiveness on behalf of others — the seeking is not for the seeker's own record but for another's; (4) *Christological-declarative context* (VCG-02 NT): seeking is through repentance/faith and receiving is through direct declaration (Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven").

---

#### T1.4.3 (cf=15114, E) — Does this characteristic have a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — and if so, how does it function?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — on both sides. (1) *Human-side speech*: Exo 32:31 — Moses verbally names the sin before God ("Alas, this people has sinned a great sin"); the speech-act of naming the sin before God is part of the seeking; (2) *Divine-side speech*: Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven" — the forgiveness declaration is a speech-act; Mat 26:28 — "for this is my blood of the covenant... poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins" — the institution-declaration at the Last Supper is speech announcing the mechanism of forgiveness; Act 10:43 — "to him all the prophets bear witness" — the prophetic testimony to forgiveness is a speech-tradition.

---

#### T1.5.1 (cf=15115, E) — What is the first or most immediate inner-being response to receiving or encountering this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Luk 7:47 — "she loved much" — the woman's love is the most immediate inner-being response to received forgiveness: the magnitude of forgiveness received produces a proportional love-response. Col 2:13 — "made alive" — the immediate inner response to forgiveness received is new life displacing prior death. Lev 16:30 — "you shall be clean before the Lord from all your sins" — the purity-status is the immediate declared condition following the atonement's completion. The immediate inner response thus ranges from the affective (love — Luk 7:47), through the vital (alive — Col 2:13), to the relational-status (clean — Lev 16:30).

---

#### T1.5.2 (cf=15116, E) — Is the immediate response consistent across the verse evidence, or does it vary?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Variable. Love (Luk 7:47), life (Col 2:13), cleanness (Lev 16:30), and Spirit-reception (Act 2:38 — "you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit") are all immediate responses named in the corpus. The variation reflects the different registers: the Levitical register names ritual purity-status (clean); the NT personal register names the affective and vital responses (love; alive); the Pentecost register names Spirit-reception. The consistent underlying element is relational restoration: forgiveness received restores the person to full standing before God, but this restoration is experienced differently across the corpus's registers.

---

#### T1.5.3 (cf=15117, E) — Where the verse evidence is silent on immediate response, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — For the kip.pu.rim atonement passages (VCG-01 — Exo 29:36; 30:10/16; Lev 23:27/28; 25:9; Num 5:8; 29:11; 2Ch 29:23/24), the immediate inner response of the individual receiving the atonement's benefit is not described. The corpus characterises the ritual mechanism and its purpose but not the individual worshipper's moment-by-moment inner experience of the atonement's completion.

---

#### T1.6.1 (cf=15118, E) — What does this characteristic produce in the inner being over time?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Three sustained inner-being effects. (1) *Love as sustained orientation*: Luk 7:47 — "he who is forgiven little, loves little" — the magnitude of sustained love is proportional to the magnitude of forgiveness received; forgiveness produces love as an ongoing inner orientation; (2) *Life replacing death*: Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... made alive" — the life produced by forgiveness received is a sustained constitutional change, not a momentary affect; (3) *Spirit indwelling*: Act 2:38 — "you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit" — the Spirit's indwelling following forgiveness is the most significant sustained inner-being consequence in the NT register.

---

#### T1.6.2 (cf=15119, E) — What states, qualities, capacities, or orientations does sustained exposure to or possession of this characteristic establish?

**[CHAR-14]** E — (1) *Cleanness before God*: Lev 16:30 — "clean before the Lord from all your sins" — the established condition of ritual-relational purity; (2) *Love-orientation*: Luk 7:47 — love as the sustained inner disposition of the forgiven person; (3) *Life-orientation*: Col 2:13 — made alive as the constitutional orientation replacing the death-in-trespasses state; (4) *Redemption-identity*: Col 1:14 — "in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins" — redemption as the established identity of the forgiven person; Eph 1:7 — "in him we have redemption through his blood, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace."

---

#### T1.6.3 (cf=15120, E) — Does the sustained effect differ from the immediate response — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. The immediate response is the declaration or initial condition change (Lev 16:30 — clean; Luk 7:48 — sins forgiven; Col 2:13 — made alive). The sustained effect is the ongoing inner orientation that forgiveness produces over time: love (Luk 7:47 — the "much-love" that flows from much-forgiveness), Spirit indwelling (Act 2:38 — the permanent gift), and redemption-identity (Col 1:14; Eph 1:7 — the "we have redemption" that is a present permanent possession). The sustained effect deepens and extends what the immediate response initiates.

---

#### T1.7.1 (cf=15121, E) — What inner conditions or orientations enable genuine reception of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Three enabling conditions. (1) *Recognition that sin has been committed*: Lev 4:14 — "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" — the recognition moment is the prerequisite; (2) *Repentance*: Act 2:38 — "repent... for the forgiveness of your sins" — the inner volitional turn is the explicit enabling condition in the NT; (3) *Faith*: Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins" — belief/faith is the specific inner condition through which forgiveness is received in the NT. Additionally: Lev 23:27 — self-affliction as the inner-posture of seeking on the Day of Atonement — the humbling of the self is an enabling condition for the ritual's efficacy.

---

#### T1.7.2 (cf=15122, E) — What inner conditions block, distort, or prevent reception?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Two blocking conditions. (1) *The unrecognised sin*: Lev 4:14 — sin "becomes known" only when it is recognised; while unrecognised, the seeking cannot begin (the sin must first be "made known" — T1.7.1); (2) *The eternal-sin condition*: Mar 3:29 — "whoever blasphemes against the Holy Spirit never has forgiveness, but is guilty of an eternal sin" — the eternal-sin condition is the one inner condition in the corpus that permanently forecloses forgiveness-reception. Lev 10:19 — Aaron's question about whether eating the sin offering while personally stricken with grief "would the Lord have approved" implies that a wrong inner state in the officiant can block the ritual's efficacy.

---

#### T1.7.3 (cf=15123, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Mar 3:29 — "guilty of an eternal sin" — the person guilty of the eternal sin is in a state of permanent unresolved guilt before God; the seeking cannot break through the condition. For the broader case (seeking without receiving), the corpus is largely silent: the pattern evidenced is that genuine seeking (repentance + faith — Act 2:38/10:43) produces reception. The case of seeking without receiving is not directly evidenced beyond the eternal-sin exception of Mar 3:29.

---

#### T1.8.1 (cf=15124, E) — What is the primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic from the programme's dimension vocabulary?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The primary dimension is **will/volition in its God-directed turning mode** — the will's movement toward God in seeking forgiveness. Act 2:38 — "repent" (metanoeō — change of mind + direction); Exo 32:30 — "I will go up to the Lord" — the volitional ascent; Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives" — faith as the will's trust-act directed toward Christ. The seeking dimension is constitutively volitional; the receiving dimension is relational-condition change that follows the volitional seeking. The will's God-directed movement is the primary inner-being faculty engaged.

---

#### T1.8.2 (cf=15125, E) — What evidence from the verse evidence supports this classification?

**[CHAR-14]** E — (1) Act 2:38 — "repent" — repentance is a volitional act named first in the seeking-sequence; (2) Exo 32:30 — "I will go up to the Lord" — the first-person future volitional statement; (3) Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives" — belief as the volitional trust-act; (4) Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the deliberate inner self-affliction is a volitional posture of seeking; (5) 2Ch 29:23 — "they laid their hands on them" — the hand-laying is the volitional act of identification with the offering's purpose.

---

#### T1.8.3 (cf=15126, E) — Does this characteristic carry secondary dimensions — and if so, what are they, and do they compete with the primary classification?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. (1) *Affective dimension*: Luk 7:47 — love as the seeking-dimension's inner disposition and the receiving-dimension's inner response; Lev 23:27 — self-affliction as the affective-penitential posture; (2) *Relational-standing dimension*: Lev 16:30 — cleanness before God; Col 1:14/2:13 — redemption identity and made-alive state; (3) *Spiritual-faculty dimension*: Act 2:38 — the Spirit's reception as the consequence of forgiveness received — a secondary inner-being consequence that opens a new dimension of the person's constitution.

---

  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-14] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓

  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
