---

## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=15127, E) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — through the Spirit's reception as the consequence of forgiveness. Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized every one of you in the name of Jesus Christ for the forgiveness of your sins, and you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit" — the Spirit's indwelling follows forgiveness and is its most direct spirit-level consequence. The forgiveness operates at the spirit level precisely because it opens the person to the Spirit who was previously blocked by the sin-record. Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... God made alive" — the death-to-life movement is a spirit-level restoration; the spiritual death produced by sin is reversed by forgiveness at the same constitutional level.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=15128, E) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The seeking dimension does not primarily originate at the spirit level: it originates in the will's volitional turning (Act 2:38 — "repent"), in the conscience's recognition (Lev 4:14 — "becomes known"), and in the community's ritual response. The receiving dimension, however, has a spirit-level consequence (Act 2:38 — Spirit received) and is enacted by God's sovereign act (2Cor 5:19 — "God was reconciling the world to himself"). The divine granting of forgiveness thus has a spirit-level source (God) even if the human seeking does not originate there.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=15129, E) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Spirit-level location (Act 2:38 — Spirit received after forgiveness; Col 2:13 — alive after dead-in-trespasses) reveals that forgiveness received operates at the deepest constitutional level of the person's capacity for God-relation: the Spirit's indwelling is the spirit-level consequence of the sin-record's removal. The forgiveness addresses what was blocking the spirit-level God-relationship (the sin-record creating separation — Isa 59:2 in CHAR-13's corpus) and opens the person to spirit-level restoration (Spirit received).

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=15130, E) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Spirit-level location is evidenced (Act 2:38 — Spirit received; Col 2:13 — made alive). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=15131, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. The movement is from outer to inner and from inner to restored. (1) *Outer-to-inner*: Lev 4:14 — the sin "becomes known" (external disclosure) → triggers inner recognition → volitional seeking begins; (2) *Inner-to-outer*: Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the inner posture of self-affliction is expressed through bodily behaviour; 2Ch 29:23 — "they laid their hands on them" — the inner identification with the offering is expressed through a bodily act; (3) *Outer-mechanism to inner-change*: the priestly blood ritual (outer) produces the condition of cleanness before God (inner relational standing — Lev 16:30); Christ's blood (outer historical event) produces the forgiveness of trespasses (inner debt cancelled — Eph 1:7).

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=15132, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The sequence across registers. (1) *Levitical sequence*: sin committed (inner act) → sin made known (recognition) → seeking initiated (will's movement) → offering brought (outer bodily act) → priestly mediation (institutional mechanism) → atonement accomplished (outer ritual completion) → cleanness before God (inner relational status change). (2) *NT sequence*: repentance (inner volitional turn) → baptism (outer bodily act) → forgiveness declared (relational status change) → Spirit received (spirit-level constitutional change — Act 2:38). (3) *Christological sequence*: Christ's blood poured out (outer historical event) → covenant established (relational framework) → forgiveness of sins (inner debt cancelled) → alive replacing dead (constitutional change — Col 2:13).

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=15133, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Constitutional movement is evidenced across all registers (T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=15134, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. Luk 7:47/48 — the woman's sins forgiven and her love-response — the forgiveness is received at the soul level of the person as a moral subject; it is her own sin-record that is addressed and her own love that responds. Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the self-affliction is a soul-level inner posture of the whole person before God. Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized every one of you" — the "every one of you" names individual soul-level moral subjects each making the seeking-movement.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=15135, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Soul-level location (Luk 7:47 — her sins; Lev 23:27 — self-affliction; Act 2:38 — every one of you) reveals that forgiveness sought and received is irreducibly personal: every moral subject must make the seeking-movement individually and receive the forgiveness individually. Col 2:13 — "having forgiven us all our trespasses" — the corporate "us" does not eliminate the individual dimension; the characteristic operates at the soul level of each person as a moral subject, not merely as a collective event.

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=15136, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (Luk 7:47/48; Lev 23:27; Act 2:38). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=15137, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — implicitly through the inner-disposition requirements. Lev 10:19 — Aaron's question about whether eating the sin offering while grief-stricken "would the Lord have approved" — implies the heart's inner state affects the ritual's acceptance; the heart's condition (grief, affliction) is relevant to whether the seeking is rightly oriented. Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — self-affliction in the Day of Atonement seeking is a heart-level posture, not merely an external act. The seeking posture required is at the heart level.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=15138, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Heart-location (Lev 10:19 — inner state affecting offering's validity; Lev 23:27 — affliction as inner-posture) reveals that the seeking dimension of the characteristic is not merely formal (bringing the prescribed offering) but requires an appropriate inner orientation at the heart level. The priestly officiants' inner state matters (Lev 10:19); the individual seeker's self-affliction matters (Lev 23:27); and in the NT register, Luk 7:47 — the woman's love is the heart's response to forgiveness received, confirming that the heart is the primary relational-receptive faculty engaged throughout.

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=15139, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Heart-location is evidenced implicitly (Lev 10:19; Lev 23:27; Luk 7:47). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=15140, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The mind is engaged through the recognition dimension. Lev 4:14 — "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" — the cognitive recognition that sin has been committed is the prerequisite for seeking; the mind's recognition is the gateway. Act 2:38 — "repent" (metanoeō — *meta* + *noeō* — a change of mind) — the NT seeking-term explicitly names the mind as the faculty undergoing change; metanoia is constitutively a cognitive reorientation.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=15141, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Mind-location (Lev 4:14 — recognition as prerequisite; Act 2:38 — repentance as metanoia) reveals that the seeking dimension requires a cognitive component: the mind must recognise the sin and reorient toward God. Metanoia (repentance) is precisely the mind's change of direction — the mind that was directed toward sin now turns toward God. The mind does not merely register the seeking intellectually; it undergoes the directional reorientation that is the seeking's volitional dimension.

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=15142, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Mind-location is evidenced (Lev 4:14; Act 2:38 — metanoeō). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=15143, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — *Faith/trust* as a distinct soul-level faculty is evidenced. Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name" — the believing (*pisteúōn* — present participle) is the soul-level receptive act through which forgiveness is received; faith is the faculty of trust-directed-toward-Christ through which the receiving dimension is completed. Faith is not merely cognitive (mind) or affective (heart) but a distinct trust-orientation of the whole person toward the forgiver.

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=15144, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The faith-level location (Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes... receives") reveals that the receiving dimension of the characteristic has a specific inner faculty through which it is received: trust directed toward Christ is the mechanism of reception. The OT parallel is faith-through-the-prescribed-means (bringing the animal, laying hands — 2Ch 29:23); the NT crystallises this as explicit personal faith in Christ's name. Faith is thus the soul-level faculty that bridges seeking and receiving: the one who believes is simultaneously seeking (expressing trust in the forgiving God) and receiving (the forgiveness comes to those who believe).

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=15145, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Faith-level location is evidenced (Act 10:43). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=15146, E) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the hands. 2Ch 29:23 — "they laid their hands on them" — the laying of hands on the sin-offering animals is the bodily act through which the person identifies with the offering and transfers the sin-burden to it; the hands are the body-part through which the seeking is physically enacted. Lev 16:21 (CHAR-13 corpus — the high priest laying hands on the scapegoat) provides the archetype. Additionally: blood as the bodily substance of atonement — Mat 26:28 — "my blood... poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins" — the body's blood is the specific physical substance through which NT forgiveness is achieved.

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=15147, E) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The hands-link (2Ch 29:23) does two things. (1) *Enacts identification*: laying hands on the animal identifies the person (or community) with the offering — the sin is symbolically transferred to the substitute through the physical contact; the seeking is not purely inner but requires bodily engagement to be complete. (2) *Grounds the seeking in the body*: the bodily act of hand-laying makes the inner seeking externally visible and communally enacted — the king and assembly together lay hands, making the corporate seeking a shared bodily act.

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=15148, E) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Body-part link (hands — 2Ch 29:23; blood — Mat 26:28) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=15149, E) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Bidirectional. (1) *Inner-to-outer (seeking)*: the inner recognition of sin and the volitional movement toward God (inner) is expressed through the outer bodily act of hand-laying (2Ch 29:23) — the seeking moves from inner to outer; (2) *Outer-to-inner (receiving)*: the outer historical event of Christ's blood being poured out (Mat 26:28 — outer) produces the inner forgiveness (the trespasses cancelled — Eph 1:7; Col 2:13 — inner). The characteristic thus moves bidirectionally: inner → outer in seeking; outer → inner in receiving.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=15150, E) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The bidirectional body-link reveals that forgiveness sought and received cannot be reduced to a purely interior event: the seeking requires bodily expression (hand-laying — 2Ch 29:23; baptism — Act 2:38 — a bodily act) and the receiving is grounded in a bodily historical event (blood poured out — Mat 26:28). The characteristic thus bridges the inner (volitional seeking, faith-reception) and the outer (bodily seeking-acts, historical atonement) — neither pole alone constitutes the complete characteristic.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=15151, E) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Body-characteristic link (hands — 2Ch 29:23; blood — Mat 26:28; baptism — Act 2:38) is evidenced and bidirectional (T2.7.1 and T2.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=15152, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — but in the positive direction, uniquely among M10 characteristics. (1) *Love as deposit*: Luk 7:47 — "she loved much" — the forgiveness received deposits love as an ongoing inner orientation; the deposit is the love produced by the magnitude of forgiveness received; (2) *Life as deposit*: Col 2:13 — "made alive" — the life-constitutional change is a permanent deposit; the formerly-dead person is now constitutionally alive; (3) *Spirit-indwelling as deposit*: Act 2:38 — the Holy Spirit is received as a gift following forgiveness — the Spirit's indwelling is the characteristic's most profound constitutional deposit; (4) *Redemption-identity as deposit*: Col 1:14; Eph 1:7 — "we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins" — the forgiven person carries a permanent identity-deposit: they are redeemed, they have received the forgiveness.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=15153, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Supporting: Luk 7:47 (love — affective deposit); Col 2:13 (alive — vital deposit); Act 2:38 (Spirit — spirit-level deposit); Col 1:14/Eph 1:7 (redemption identity — relational deposit). The OT register (kip.pu.rim — VCG-01) presents annual atonement deposits that are provisional: each year's deposit covers the current year but requires renewal; the deposit is temporary. The NT register (VCG-02) presents permanent deposits: "in him we have redemption" (Col 1:14 — present tense, permanent possession) vs. the OT "once a year" (Exo 30:10). The NT deposit is permanent; the OT deposit is provisional and renewable.

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=15154, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Deposit evidence is present in the positive direction: love (Luk 7:47), life (Col 2:13), Spirit (Act 2:38), redemption identity (Col 1:14; Eph 1:7). T5.7 will carry: the deposits are all positive; the OT deposits are provisional (annual renewal required); the NT deposits are permanent (present-tense possession — "we have redemption").

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=15155, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Two constitutional origins. (1) *From conscience recognition*: Lev 4:14 — "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" — the seeking begins in the conscience's recognition of committed sin; (2) *From God's provision of the means*: Exo 29:36 — the daily sin offering is God-instituted; Lev 23:27 — the Day of Atonement is God-appointed. The characteristic cannot originate in the person alone: God must first establish the means (the sacrificial system; Christ's blood) before the seeking can become effective. The origin is thus both from within the person (conscience recognition triggering seeking) and from God's prior provision (establishing the means without which the seeking would be empty).

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=15156, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Multiple — and structurally so. The seeking dimension originates in conscience recognition (Lev 4:14) and volitional turning (Act 2:38 — repent). The receiving dimension originates in God's provision of the atonement-mechanism (Exo 29:36 — God's daily sin-offering institution; Mat 26:28 — Christ's blood). These multiple origins reflect the characteristic's bi-directional structure: the seeking originates in the person; the receiving originates in God.

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=15157, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. (1) *Individual-sin context* (VCG-03 — Lev 4:3/14/5:6/10): the origin is in the individual's committed sin becoming known, triggering individual seeking; (2) *Communal-scheduled context* (VCG-01 — kip.pu.rim): the origin is in the annual calendar rhythm (Lev 23:27 — the tenth day of the seventh month) rather than in a specific sin-recognition moment; the community seeks collectively on the appointed day regardless of which specific sins are present; (3) *NT-faith context* (VCG-02): the origin is in the hearing of the prophetic testimony (Act 10:43 — "to him all the prophets bear witness") that produces belief, which produces forgiveness-reception.

---

T2 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       31/31 ✓ (T2.1.1–T2.1.4, T2.10.1–T2.10.3, T2.2.1–T2.2.3, T2.3.1–T2.3.3, T2.4.1–T2.4.3, T2.5.1–T2.5.3, T2.6.1–T2.6.3, T2.7.1–T2.7.3, T2.8.1–T2.8.3, T2.9.1–T2.9.3)
  2. Scope marker:       31× [CHAR-14] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 29:36/30:10; Lev 4:3/14/10:19/16:30/23:27/25:9; Num 5:8; 2Ch 29:23; Mat 26:28; Act 2:38/10:43; 2Cor 5:19; Eph 1:7; Col 1:14/2:13; Luk 7:47/48 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T3.


---

## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=15158, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The perceptive faculty is engaged at the point of recognition. Lev 4:14 — "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" — the becoming-known is the perceptive event: the sin is perceived as sin, triggering the seeking. Lev 4:3 — "if it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people" — the recognition that the priest's sin has spread guilt to the people requires perceptive awareness of this relational consequence. The perceptive faculty also engages in the receiving: Luk 7:47 — Jesus declares the perceptive principle — "she loved much" because "her sins, which are many, are forgiven" — perceiving the magnitude of one's forgiveness produces proportional love.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=15159, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness received *deepens* the perceptive faculty: Luk 7:47 — "he who is forgiven little, loves little" — the inverse implies that perceiving the magnitude of forgiveness received sharpens the person's perception of both their own moral condition and God's grace. The received forgiveness produces greater perceptive clarity about both the depth of the need (the many sins that were forgiven) and the depth of the gift (the love-response it generates).

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=15160, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The perceptive pattern reveals that the characteristic requires perceptive engagement at both ends: recognition of sin (Lev 4:14 — perceiving the committed sin) enables seeking, and recognition of forgiveness magnitude (Luk 7:47 — perceiving how much has been forgiven) deepens the response. The characteristic thus both depends on and deepens the perceptive faculty: impaired perception (unrecognised sin — no seeking initiated) blocks the characteristic; restored perception (forgiveness received and its magnitude perceived) deepens love.

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=15185, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Conscientiousness is engaged in the seeking dimension. The priestly mediation (VCG-01/03) requires conscientious performance: Lev 10:17/19 — Aaron's conscientiousness about whether the sin offering was properly conducted given his grief; the priestly duty to eat the sin offering in the sanctuary is a matter of conscientious compliance. 2Ch 29:23/24 — the king's command and the assembly's and priests' execution of the sin offering involve all three components of conscientiousness (moral awareness, volitional commitment, action). Exo 32:30 — Moses's "I will go up" combined with naming the sin (moral awareness) and going up (action) constitute a fully conscientious seeking-act.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=15186, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness received *restores and deepens* conscientiousness. Col 2:13 — "made alive" restores the constitutional capacity for full conscientious engagement; Act 2:38 — the Spirit received enables ongoing Spirit-directed conscientiousness. Luk 7:47 — the woman's post-forgiveness love-response demonstrates restored conscientiousness directed toward gratitude and service. The conscientiousness that was misdirected in sinning (Isa 5:18 — CHAR-13 context — drawing sin with deliberate effort) is redirected through forgiveness received toward love and God-directed action.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=15187, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The conscientiousness pattern reveals that the seeking dimension requires full conscientiousness (moral awareness of sin + volitional commitment to seek + action of seeking — Exo 32:30; Act 2:38; 2Ch 29:23/24), while the receiving dimension restores the person to a more fully functioning conscientiousness (Col 2:13 — alive; Act 2:38 — Spirit received). The characteristic is thus a conscientiousness event: conscientious seeking produces a restored conscientiousness through forgiveness received.

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=15188, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The relational capacity is the characteristic's primary operative field. 2Cor 5:19 — "in Christ God was reconciling the world to himself" — the entire characteristic is defined by the restoration of the God-human relational bond; reconciliation is the relational term for what forgiveness achieves. Lev 16:30 — "you shall be clean before the Lord" — the restored relational standing before God is the goal and result of the seeking-and-receiving. Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven" — the declaration directly names the relational status change.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=15189, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness received *restores and deepens* relational capacity. 2Cor 5:19 — "not counting their trespasses against them" — the removal of the sin-ledger from the relational account restores the God-relationship's full potential. Act 2:38 — Spirit received — the Spirit's indwelling is the deepest relational-capacity enhancement: the person is now indwelt by God himself, the deepest possible relational proximity. Luk 7:47 — the woman's love for Jesus is the evidence of restored relational capacity: the forgiven person's God-relationship capacity is deepened by the forgiveness received.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=15190, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The relational pattern reveals that forgiveness sought and received is the characteristic whose primary function is *relational restoration*: every other M10 characteristic (CHAR-1 through CHAR-13) describes some form of relational damage or moral failure; CHAR-14 is the characteristic that addresses and reverses that damage. 2Cor 5:19 — "reconciling the world to himself" — reconciliation is relational re-establishment; the characteristic exists to restore what sin severed. The relational pattern thus positions CHAR-14 as the therapeutic complement to the M10 cluster's diagnostic characteristics.

---

T3 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       33/33 ✓ (T3.1.1–T3.1.3, T3.2.1–T3.2.3, T3.3.1–T3.3.3, T3.4.1–T3.4.3, T3.5.1–T3.5.3, T3.6.1–T3.6.3, T3.7.1–T3.7.3, T3.8.1–T3.8.3, T3.9.1–T3.9.3, T3.10.1–T3.10.3, T3.11.1–T3.11.3)
  2. Scope marker:       33× [CHAR-14] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (S findings — T3.5.1–T3.5.3 — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 30:16/32:30/31; Lev 4:3/14/10:17/19/16:30/23:27; 2Ch 29:23/24; Mat 26:28; Luk 7:47/48; Act 2:38/10:43; 2Cor 5:19; Col 1:14/2:13 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Response approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T4 continues in the next response.


---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=15161, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — centrally through the recognition and repentance dimensions. Lev 4:14 — "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" — cognitive recognition is the prerequisite. Act 2:38 — "repent" (metanoeō — change of mind) — the cognitive reorientation is explicitly named as the seeking-act in the NT. Act 10:43 — "to him all the prophets bear witness" — the prophetic cognitive testimony provides the intellectual content that faith engages; the cognitive knowledge of who Jesus is and what the prophets said is what enables the believing-for-forgiveness.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=15162, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The characteristic *reorients* cognition (Act 2:38 — metanoeō is a cognitive turning) and *is enabled by* cognitive testimony (Act 10:43 — the prophets' witness). The cognitive faculty is not merely passive but actively reoriented: the repentant person's mind has changed direction toward God. The forgiveness received does not bypass cognition (it is not a mystical bypassing of the rational faculty) but operates through it (the prophetic testimony → belief → forgiveness — Act 10:43).

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=15163, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals that the characteristic is not anti-rational: the seeking requires cognitive recognition of sin (Lev 4:14), cognitive engagement with the prophetic testimony (Act 10:43), and cognitive reorientation through repentance (Act 2:38). But cognition alone does not complete the characteristic: Exo 32:30 — Moses knowing the sin and going up to seek atonement does not guarantee forgiveness; he says "perhaps I can make atonement" — the cognitive-volitional seeking is necessary but the divine response is not mechanical.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=15164, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — through the Day of Atonement's memorial function. Exo 30:16 — "that it may bring the people of Israel to remembrance before the Lord, so as to make atonement for your lives" — the atonement money brings Israel to God's remembrance; the memorial function is part of the seeking mechanism. Gen 41:9 (CHAR-13 corpus — same root) — "I remember my offenses today" — the memory of past failure that triggers the seeking. In the characteristic itself: the annual kip.pu.rim cycle (VCG-01) operates as a rhythmic memorial practice — annual remembrance that the sin-record requires covering.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=15165, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness received *transforms* the memory relationship to sin. Jer 31:34 (CHAR-13 corpus) — "I will remember their sin no more" — the received forgiveness changes the divine memory's relationship to the sin-record. For the human person: the forgiveness received does not erase the memory of what was forgiven (Luk 7:47 — the woman remembers her many sins as the backdrop of her love-response) but transforms the memory's charge: the remembered sin is now remembered as forgiven, not as outstanding.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=15166, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The memory pattern reveals an asymmetry in how forgiveness transforms memory: the divine memory is erased (Jer 31:34 — remembered no more); the human memory is not erased but recontextualised (Luk 7:47 — the many sins remembered as the measure of grace received). This asymmetry is analytically significant: the characteristic does not produce human amnesia about past sin but transforms the sin-memory's relationship to the self. The Exo 30:16 atonement-money bringing Israel to divine remembrance also shows the memorial function operating in the seeking-direction: the seeker needs to be remembered before God, not forgotten.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=15167, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — centrally on the receiving side. Luk 7:47 — "her sins, which are many, are forgiven — for she loved much. But he who is forgiven little, loves little" — love is the affective response that forgiveness received produces; the magnitude of love is proportional to the magnitude of forgiveness. Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the self-affliction on the Day of Atonement is an affective-penitential posture required on the seeking side. The characteristic thus engages affect at both ends: self-affliction in seeking; love in receiving.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=15168, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness received *deepens and redirects* the affective faculty. Luk 7:47 — the forgiveness produces love as a deepened affective capacity directed toward God; the previously sinful affective orientations of the woman are redirected by forgiveness toward love. The self-affliction of seeking (Lev 23:27) is not suppressed affect but *directed* affect: sorrow channelled into the seeking posture. Forgiveness received does not eliminate the affective capacity but transforms its direction (from sinful loves toward love of the forgiver).

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=15169, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The affective pattern reveals that Luk 7:47's love-magnitude-proportional-to-forgiveness-magnitude is the characteristic's defining affective insight: the depth of the affective response (love) is the measure of the depth of the received forgiveness. This principle reveals that the characteristic is not merely a legal transaction (sin-record cancelled) but has a proportional affective consequence: greater forgiveness received produces greater love; lesser forgiveness perceived produces lesser love. The affective faculty is the thermometer of forgiveness received.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=15170, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** S — The 34-verse corpus does not explicitly engage the creative faculty in connection with forgiveness sought and received. The characteristic operates through the volitional-seeking, cognitive-recognition, affective-love, and relational-restoration dimensions rather than through creative origination.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=15171, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** S — No direct evidence. The corpus is silent on the creative faculty's engagement with forgiveness sought and received.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=15172, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** S — The silence on creativity is consistent with the characteristic's focus on the restoration of the sin-broken relationship rather than on creative origination. Forgiveness restores what sin damaged; it does not primarily operate through the creative faculty. Noted.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=15173, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the volitional faculty is the primary faculty of the seeking dimension. Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized" — repentance is a volitional act; baptism is a volitional submission to the ritual. Exo 32:30 — "I will go up to the Lord" — the volitional first-person declaration of Moses's seeking. Lev 23:27 — the community must afflict themselves (volitional self-directed act) and present offerings (volitional act of bringing). Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes" — believing is the will's trust-commitment to Christ as the forgiver.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=15174, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness received *liberates* volition. Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... made alive" — the death-in-trespasses suppresses volitional freedom (the dead cannot will effectively); the forgiveness that makes alive restores the volitional capacity. Act 2:38 — "you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit" — the Spirit's indwelling following forgiveness is the source of renewed volitional capacity; the Spirit enables the will's ongoing God-directed freedom that sin had foreclosed.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=15175, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The volitional pattern reveals the characteristic's paradoxical structure: the seeking requires volition (repent, go up, believe), yet the received forgiveness produces a restored volition (alive, Spirit-empowered) that was not fully available before seeking. The will must be exercised in seeking (with whatever partial freedom remains) before the forgiveness received restores the will's fuller freedom. This is the characteristic's volitional dynamic: partial will exercises toward God → forgiveness received → fuller will restored.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=15176, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Agency is engaged through the seeking acts. 2Ch 29:23 — "they laid their hands on them" — the assembly's agentive act of hand-laying; 2Ch 29:24 — "the priests slaughtered them and made a sin offering" — the priestly agentive acts. Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized" — both repentance (inner agentive turn) and baptism (outer agentive submission) are acts of the person's agency. Exo 32:30/31 — Moses's agentive intercession: "I will go up to the Lord" and his verbal naming of the sin before God.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=15177, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The characteristic *requires and then restores* agency. The seeking requires genuine agentive acts (hand-laying, repentance, belief, intercession — as above); without agency the seeking cannot begin. The received forgiveness then restores deeper agency: Col 2:13 — made alive (the constitutional restoration that enables full agentive participation in the God-relationship); Act 2:38 — Spirit received (the agent of empowered action following forgiveness).

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=15178, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The agency pattern reveals that the characteristic is not a passive reception of divine action but a genuine agentive co-participation: the person must act (repent, believe, bring the offering, lay hands) for the forgiveness-transaction to be enacted. The divine side is equally agentive (God forgives, Christ's blood is poured out), but the human agency is not bypassed. The characteristic is thus a cooperative agentive event: human agency in seeking + divine agency in granting = forgiveness sought and received.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=15179, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the moral evaluation faculty is engaged on both sides. On the seeking side: Lev 4:14 — "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" — the moral evaluation must recognise the committed sin as sin before seeking can begin; Exo 32:31 — Moses morally evaluates the golden calf as "a great sin" before God. On the receiving side: Luk 7:47 — "he who is forgiven little, loves little" — implies a moral evaluation of the magnitude of forgiveness received; the person who evaluates themselves as needing little forgiveness receives and loves less.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=15180, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness received *corrects and deepens* moral evaluation. The woman of Luk 7:47 whose sins were many and who loved much had a more accurate moral self-evaluation (she recognised the many sins) than the Pharisee who loved little (implicitly evaluating himself as needing little forgiveness). The received forgiveness deepens moral evaluation by confirming what the seeker's moral evaluation recognised: that the sin-record was real, was many, and required this specific act of forgiving grace.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=15181, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals Luk 7:47's principle as the characteristic's moral-evaluation insight: accurate moral self-evaluation (many sins recognised) correlates with deeper seeking (the woman came to Jesus) and deeper receiving (her sins forgiven — Luk 7:48) and deeper love-response (she loved much). The moral evaluation faculty is not merely a prerequisite for seeking but is deepened through the receiving: the person who has received forgiveness for many sins has a more accurate moral picture of both their own failure and God's grace than the person who perceives little need.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=15182, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The conscience is the faculty that makes seeking possible. Lev 4:14 — "when the sin which they have committed becomes known" — the conscience registers the committed sin as the prerequisite for seeking. Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the self-affliction is the conscience's response made into a required posture; the Day of Atonement is structured around the conscience's collective engagement with the community's sin. Lev 10:19 — Aaron's question about whether his grief-stricken state would make the sin-offering unacceptable to God — the conscience is what registers the potential incongruity between inner state and ritual act.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=15183, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness received *relieves and cleanses* the conscience. Lev 16:30 — "you shall be clean before the Lord from all your sins" — the atonement's completion cleanses the conscience from the accumulated sin-burden; the conscience is no longer under the weight of unaddressed sin. The NT parallel: Heb 9:14 (outside this corpus but structurally evidenced through Mat 26:28 — Christ's blood) — Christ's blood cleanses the conscience from dead works. The conscience is thus both the faculty that generates the seeking (registering the sin) and the faculty that receives the forgiveness's relief.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=15184, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The conscience pattern reveals the characteristic's full cycle: conscience registers sin (Lev 4:14 — becomes known) → conscience motivates seeking (Lev 23:27 — self-affliction) → conscience confirms the inner-state requirement of the seeking (Lev 10:19 — Aaron's grief question) → forgiveness received cleanses the conscience (Lev 16:30 — clean before Lord). The conscience is thus the faculty that runs the characteristic's full circuit: from recognition through seeking to the cleanness received. The characteristic cannot operate without conscience engagement.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=15191, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the granting dimension is entirely God-to-human. (1) *Declaration*: Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven" — Jesus's direct declaration toward the woman; (2) *Non-imputation*: 2Cor 5:19 — "in Christ God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them" — God's sovereign decision not to count; (3) *Making alive*: Col 2:13 — "God made alive together with him, having forgiven us all our trespasses" — God is the subject of both the making-alive and the forgiving; (4) *Provision of the mechanism*: Exo 29:36 — God institutes the daily sin offering; Lev 23:27/28 — God appoints the Day of Atonement; Mat 26:28 — Jesus pours out covenant-blood "for many for the forgiveness of sins."

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=15192, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Two bases are evidenced. (1) *Grace — God's own initiative*: Eph 1:7 — "the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace" — the basis is the riches of divine grace, not human merit; 2Cor 5:19 — "God was reconciling" — God initiates; 2Ch 29:24 — "for all Israel" — the scope is determined by God's command through the king. (2) *Covenant blood*: Mat 26:28 — "this is my blood of the covenant... for the forgiveness of sins" — the covenantal blood-provision is the specific ground on which God grants forgiveness; the atonement-mechanism is the covenant-established basis.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=15193, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — God's disposition is one of *welcoming and ready forgiveness grounded in provided mechanism*. Mar 3:28 — "all sins will be forgiven the children of man, and whatever blasphemies they utter" — the scope of divine willingness to forgive is total (all sins, all blasphemies) with a single exception (Mar 3:29). Eph 1:7 — "according to the riches of his grace" — the richness of the forgiveness reveals the richness of the disposition: God does not forgive grudgingly but abundantly. Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness" — the universality of "everyone" reveals no favouritism in the disposition; the forgiveness is available to all who believe.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=15194, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — God-to-human operation is the defining dimension of the receiving side (T4.1.1–T4.1.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=15195, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the seeking dimension is entirely human-toward-God. Exo 32:30 — "I will go up to the Lord; perhaps I can make atonement for your sin" — Moses's movement upward toward God is the seeking's directional metaphor; Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized... for the forgiveness of your sins" — the commanded human movement toward God in repentance and baptism; Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives" — the believing is a movement of trust toward Christ; Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves and present a food offering to the Lord" — the self-affliction and offering are Israel's collective movement toward God.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=15196, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Acknowledgment of the sin*: Exo 32:31 — Moses names it "a great sin" before God; Lev 4:14 — recognition when sin "becomes known"; (2) *Self-affliction*: Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the inner posture of humbling and penitential self-examination; (3) *Faith toward the forgiver*: Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives" — trust directed toward Christ; Act 2:38 — "repent" (metanoeō) — cognitive and volitional reorientation toward God. All three are required: mere recognition without self-affliction is incomplete; self-affliction without faith in the specific forgiver (Christ's name — Act 10:43) does not produce reception.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=15197, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The seeking direction (human toward God) reveals that the God-human relationship, though ruptured by sin (Isa 59:2 in CHAR-13 — separation), remains accessible: God has not withdrawn so completely that seeking is futile. Exo 32:30 — Moses says "perhaps I can make atonement" — the "perhaps" acknowledges the uncertainty but the going-up confirms the accessibility. Mat 26:28 — God's provision of the blood "for the forgiveness of sins" means the movement toward God is not toward an empty divine absence but toward a God who has already provided the mechanism of reception. The seeking thus reveals a relationship that is damaged but not destroyed.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=15198, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Human-to-God operation is the defining dimension of the seeking side (T4.2.1–T4.2.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=15199, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — through intercessory and priestly mediation. (1) *Intercessory seeking on behalf of others*: Exo 32:30 — Moses seeks atonement for the people's sin, not his own — "I will go up to the Lord; perhaps I can make atonement for your sin"; (2) *Priestly bearing of others' iniquity*: Lev 10:17 — "it is most holy and has been given to you that you may bear the iniquity of the congregation, to make atonement for them" — the priest extends the seeking on behalf of the whole congregation; (3) *National priestly act*: 2Ch 29:24 — "the king commanded that the burnt offering and the sin offering should be made for all Israel" — the seeking is extended by king and priests to encompass all Israel.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=15200, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Priestly appointment and authority*: Lev 10:17 — the sin offering is "given to you" — the priest's authority to bear the congregation's iniquity is divinely granted, not self-assumed; the mediatorial role is received, not created; (2) *Proper inner disposition*: Lev 10:19 — Aaron's question about whether his grief would make the sin-offering unacceptable implies that the mediator's inner state matters; the priest must be in an appropriate inner condition to extend the seeking effectively on behalf of others.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=15201, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The extending person must have received the mediatorial appointment: Lev 10:17 — "given to you that you may bear the iniquity of the congregation" — the priestly appointment is the prerequisite for bearing others' iniquity. Exo 32:30 — Moses's authority to seek atonement for the people is grounded in his unique standing before God as Israel's covenant-mediator. In the NT: Mat 26:28 — Christ's blood is poured out "for many" — Christ's unique position as the covenant-establisher is what enables his extending of forgiveness to the many.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=15202, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — The giving/extension direction through intercession and priestly mediation is evidenced (Exo 32:30; Lev 10:17; 2Ch 29:24). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=15203, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the receiving dimension is the characteristic's completing act. Lev 16:30 — "you shall be clean before the Lord from all your sins" — the community receives the cleanliness that atonement confers; Luk 7:47/48 — the woman receives the forgiveness Jesus declares; Col 2:13 — "having forgiven us all our trespasses" — the "us" receives the comprehensive forgiveness; Act 2:38 — "you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit" — forgiveness enables receipt of the Spirit.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=15204, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Reception-enabling: (1) *Faith*: Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives" — belief is the specific reception-enabling condition in the NT; (2) *Repentance*: Act 2:38 — "repent... for the forgiveness" — repentance precedes and enables forgiveness-reception; (3) *Recognition*: Lev 4:14 — sin "becomes known" — recognition enables seeking which enables receiving. Reception-blocking: (1) *The eternal-sin condition*: Mar 3:29 — "never has forgiveness" — the one condition that permanently blocks reception; (2) *Unrecognised sin*: without recognition (Lev 4:14), the seeking cannot begin and reception cannot follow.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=15205, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The received-forgiveness inner state is the richest positive inner-being state described in M10. (1) *Clean before God*: Lev 16:30 — relational purity restored; (2) *Alive*: Col 2:13 — "God made alive" — constitutional life replacing death-in-trespasses; (3) *Loved and loving*: Luk 7:47 — "she loved much" — the received forgiveness produces the love-orientation; (4) *Redeemed*: Col 1:14; Eph 1:7 — "we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins" — permanent identity as redeemed; (5) *Spirit-indwelt*: Act 2:38 — "you will receive the gift of the Holy Spirit" — the deepest positive inner-being consequence.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=15206, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — The receiving direction is extensively evidenced (Lev 16:30; Luk 7:47/48; Col 1:14/2:13; Act 2:38). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=15207, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. Within the covenant community (VCG-01/03): the priestly atonement system operates within the established God-Israel covenant bond; the atonement is conducted within the Tabernacle/Temple as the designated relational space between God and Israel. Exo 30:16 — the atonement money brings Israel "to remembrance before the Lord" — the covenant relationship is the operative relational field. Across relational distance: Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins" — the scope extends beyond Israel to all who believe (Act 10 context — Cornelius, a Gentile); the NT register extends forgiveness across the prior relational boundary of covenant membership.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=15208, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The characteristic crosses covenantal boundaries in the NT register. Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness of sins through his name" — "everyone" in context (Cornelius the Gentile) explicitly crosses the Jewish-covenant boundary; Mar 3:28 — "all sins will be forgiven the children of man" — "children of man" (sons of Adam) is a universal human category, not a covenantal one; 2Cor 5:19 — "God was reconciling the world to himself" — "the world" is the broadest possible scope. The OT register (VCG-01/03) operates primarily within the covenant community; the NT register explicitly extends to all who believe.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=15209, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The relational scope expands across the canonical arc. OT: the kip.pu.rim system covers the covenant community — "for all Israel" (2Ch 29:24); Exo 30:16 — "the people of Israel." NT: Mat 26:28 — "for many" (the new covenant's scope); Mar 3:28 — "the children of man" (universal human scope); 2Cor 5:19 — "the world" (cosmic scope); Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes" (faith-scope crossing ethnic and covenantal boundaries). The scope moves from Israel → many → world → everyone who believes.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=15210, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Relational scope is evidenced at communal (Israel), many, world, and universal-faith levels (T4.5.1–T4.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=15211, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-14]** S — The 34-verse corpus does not explicitly name spiritual beings (angelic or adversarial) in connection with forgiveness sought and received. The characteristic operates directly between the human person and God through the priestly or Christological mechanism.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=15212, E) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Implicitly, through Mar 3:29. The "blasphemy against the Holy Spirit" that constitutes the eternal sin blocking all forgiveness implies an adversarial spiritual orientation: the one who blasphemes the Spirit has aligned against the divine agent of conviction and renewal in a way that forecloses the forgiveness-seeking. The adversarial dimension is implied by the Spirit being the agent of conviction (Joh 16:8/9 in CHAR-11's corpus) whose work is resisted in the eternal sin; but the adversarial agency is not explicitly named in this corpus.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=15213, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-14]** S — The 34-verse corpus does not evidence angelic ministry as a mediator of forgiveness sought and received. The mediation is priestly (Lev 10:17 — priest bears iniquity) and Christological (Mat 26:28 — Christ's blood), not angelic.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=15214, S) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** S — The spiritual beings interface is largely silent: T4.6.1 and T4.6.3 find no evidence of spiritual-beings mediation. T4.6.2 identifies an implicit adversarial dimension in Mar 3:29 (blasphemy against the Holy Spirit as forgiveness-foreclosing condition) but without explicitly naming adversarial agency. Noted.

---

T4 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T4.1.1–T4.1.4, T4.2.1–T4.2.4, T4.3.1–T4.3.4, T4.4.1–T4.4.4, T4.5.1–T4.5.4, T4.6.1–T4.6.4)

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=15215, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Both — and the transformation is the most comprehensive positive transformation in the M10 corpus. (1) *Condition change*: Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... God made alive" — the condition changes from death to life; Lev 16:30 — "you shall be clean before the Lord from all your sins" — from defiled to clean; (2) *Orientation change*: Luk 7:47 — "she loved much" — the orientation shifts from the sinful orientations that accumulated the many sins to the love-orientation produced by forgiveness received; Act 2:38 — "repent" (metanoeō — change of mind/direction) — the cognitive-volitional orientation is transformed in the seeking itself.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=15216, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Both dimensions evidenced. (1) *Provisional (OT register)*: VCG-01 — kip.pu.rim operates "once in the year throughout your generations" (Exo 30:10) — the annual cycle implies the covering is temporary and must be renewed; the OT transformation is real but provisional; (2) *Permanent (NT register)*: Col 1:14 — "in whom we have redemption, the forgiveness of sins" — present tense, permanent possession; Eph 1:7 — "in him we have redemption through his blood" — the blood-atonement produces a permanent transformation; (3) *Irreversibly foreclosed*: Mar 3:29 — "is guilty of an eternal sin" — the eternal-sin condition produces a permanent un-transformation (permanent unforgiveness); the inverse of the characteristic's positive transformation is also permanent.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=15217, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Transformation evidence is extensive (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=15218, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — two parallel sequences. (1) *Levitical sequence*: sin committed → sin becomes known (Lev 4:14) → seeking initiated (offering brought) → priestly mediation enacted (atonement performed) → forgiveness granted (Lev 16:30 — clean before Lord) → restored relational standing. (2) *NT sequence*: sin accumulated → conviction (Spirit's work — Joh 16:8/9 in CHAR-11) → repentance (Act 2:38 — metanoeō) → baptism (Act 2:38 — outward) → forgiveness declared (Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven") → Spirit received (Act 2:38) → love-orientation (Luk 7:47 — "she loved much") → life replacing death (Col 2:13).

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=15219, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The sequences reveal that forgiveness sought and received is a *progression through stages* rather than a single event. The Levitical sequence requires: recognition, bringing, mediation, completion — multiple stages each of which is necessary. The NT sequence requires: conviction, repentance, baptism, declaration, Spirit-reception — again multiple stages. The sequence reveals: no stage can be skipped; the recognition (Lev 4:14; Act 2:38 — repent) precedes the seeking; the seeking precedes the receiving; the receiving produces the transformation. The transformation (love, life, Spirit) is the endpoint not the starting point.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=15220, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Sequence is extensively evidenced in both Levitical and NT registers (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=15221, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Three distinct mechanisms across the VCGs. (1) *Substitutionary covering (kip.pu.rim — VCG-01)*: the mechanism is the prescribed animal bearing the sin-burden through priestly offering; the covering (*kāpar*) conceals the sin from divine judgment; Lev 10:17 — "bear the iniquity of the congregation"; (2) *Priestly atonement through blood (VCG-03)*: the mechanism is the sin-offering blood applied by the priest producing the condition "he shall be forgiven" (Lev 5:10; 19:22); (3) *Christological blood-covenant (VCG-02)*: Mat 26:28 — the mechanism is Christ's blood poured out establishing a new covenant within which forgiveness is available; 2Cor 5:19 — the mechanism is God's sovereign non-counting of trespasses through Christ.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=15222, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — three distinct context-mechanism pairs. (1) *Annual communal context (kip.pu.rim — VCG-01)*: collective ritual covering once per year; provisional; requires renewal; (2) *Individual-sin context (Levitical sin-offering — VCG-03)*: individual sin-offering when specific sin "becomes known"; point-of-recognition triggered; (3) *Universal-gospel context (NT — VCG-02)*: Christ's blood as once-for-all mechanism; received through faith/repentance; permanent; extending to all nations (Act 10:43). The mechanisms differ in scope (individual vs communal vs universal), frequency (annual vs occasion-triggered vs once-for-all), and permanence (provisional vs permanent).

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=15223, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Mechanisms are evidenced across three distinct contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=15224, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. Lev 23:27 — "you shall afflict yourselves" — the self-affliction required on the Day of Atonement is a prescribed form of suffering in the seeking context; the suffering is deliberately imposed as part of the seeking posture. Lev 10:19 — Aaron's grief ("and yet such things as these have happened to me") — the priest's personal suffering creates a question about the validity of his mediatorial seeking; suffering and seeking coexist in the same moment. Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses" — the death-state the forgiveness addresses is itself a form of existential suffering.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=15225, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Suffering *accompanies and contextualises* the seeking. Lev 23:27 — self-affliction is prescribed as the appropriate accompanying suffering for the annual seeking; the suffering is not incidental but integral to the seeking posture. Lev 10:19 — Aaron's grief tests whether the seeking can be effective under conditions of personal suffering. The suffering does not produce the characteristic (it is not affliction that causes forgiveness-seeking) but accompanies the seeking as its honest inner context: the person who seeks forgiveness is under the weight of what their sin has done, which is a form of suffering.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=15226, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — The suffering dimension is evidenced (Lev 23:27 — self-affliction; Lev 10:19 — grief; Col 2:13 — death-state as suffering). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=15227, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — as the characteristic that *initiates and sustains* positive character formation in M10. (1) *Initiating formation*: Col 2:13 — "made alive" — the forgiveness received is the constitutional moment that makes positive formation possible (the dead cannot be formed); Act 2:38 — Spirit received — the Spirit's indwelling is the formative agent for ongoing sanctification; (2) *Sustaining formation*: VCG-01 — the annual kip.pu.rim cycle sustains Israel's formation by repeatedly addressing the accumulated sin-record and restoring the community to a clean-before-God standing; the annual rhythm is formative in itself.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=15228, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Forgiveness sought and received is the *reset mechanism* of the formation arc: every time the sin-record accumulates (CHAR-13), the formation arc is disrupted; forgiveness sought and received is what resets the arc to a renewed starting position. Lev 16:30 — "you shall be clean before the Lord from all your sins" — the Day of Atonement is an annual formation-arc reset. Luk 7:47 — the woman's love-orientation following forgiveness is the formation arc's renewed direction. The characteristic is thus the arc's renewal mechanism, not merely an episodic relief.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=15229, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Formation evidence is present (T5.5.1 and T5.5.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=15230, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — the OT provisional system explicitly points toward the eschatological fullness. (1) *The annual cycle's forward orientation*: Exo 30:10 — "once in the year throughout your generations" — the generational repetition implies an endpoint where the annual repetition will no longer be necessary; the cycle points toward its own completion; (2) *The new-covenant blood*: Mat 26:28 — "this is my blood of the covenant... for the forgiveness of sins" — the new covenant's blood-forgiveness is the eschatological fulfillment that the annual kip.pu.rim cycle anticipated; (3) *Dan 9:24 (CHAR-13 corpus)*: "to put an end to sin, and to atone for iniquity, to bring in everlasting righteousness" — the eschatological atonement is the final fullness toward which all provisional atonement pointed.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=15231, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The OT kip.pu.rim system anticipates its own eschatological supersession: each annual Day of Atonement (Lev 23:27/28) is complete in itself yet points forward to the once-for-all atonement (Mat 26:28). For the NT believer: Eph 1:7 — "in him we have redemption, the forgiveness of our trespasses, according to the riches of his grace" — the present "we have" anticipates the eschatological fullness of riches of grace; Col 1:14 — "in whom we have redemption" — the present redemption-possession anticipates the final redemption-completeness. Act 2:38's Spirit-reception is the "deposit" of the eschatological inheritance (Eph 1:14 context — outside this corpus but implicit in the Spirit-gift).

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=15232, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced in both OT (annual cycle pointing forward) and NT (new-covenant blood as fulfillment; present redemption anticipating fullness) registers (T5.6.1 and T5.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=15233, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-14]** E — T2.8 identified four positive deposits: love (Luk 7:47), life (Col 2:13), Spirit (Act 2:38), redemption-identity (Col 1:14; Eph 1:7). Developmental consequences. (1) *Love deposit*: Luk 7:47 — "she loved much" — the love produced by forgiveness develops into sustained love-orientation; "he who is forgiven little, loves little" implies the developmental trajectory is directly proportional to the forgiveness received; (2) *Life deposit*: Col 2:13 — "made alive" — constitutional life enables all subsequent positive developmental capacity; without the life-deposit, positive formation cannot proceed; (3) *Spirit deposit*: Act 2:38 — the Spirit's indwelling is the formative agent for all subsequent sanctification; the Spirit's work depends on the forgiveness-reception that opened the Spirit's access; (4) *Redemption-identity deposit*: Col 1:14/Eph 1:7 — "we have redemption" — the permanent identity shapes the person's subsequent self-understanding and God-relationship.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=15234, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — in both registers. (1) *OT generational rhythm*: Exo 30:10 — "once in the year throughout your generations" — the kip.pu.rim cycle is explicitly generational; each generation participates in the seeking-and-receiving through the annual rhythm; Lev 25:9 — "on the Day of Atonement you shall sound the trumpet throughout all your land" — the annual proclamation is a generational formation practice; (2) *NT proclamation scope*: Act 10:43 — "to him all the prophets bear witness that everyone who believes in him receives forgiveness" — the universal proclamation to all generations is implied in "everyone who believes"; Mat 26:28 — "for many" encompasses all generations who receive through faith.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=15235, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-14]** E — T2.8 found positive deposit evidence. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---
