  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.


---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=15236, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur within or proximate to the 34-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record)*: the record (CHAR-13) is what CHAR-14 addresses; Exo 32:30 — Moses seeking to atone for "your sin" — the sin-record is the object of the seeking; Lev 16:30 — clean from all sins — the record cleared is the goal; (2) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Exo 32:31 — Moses confessing the great sin before God; the verbal naming precedes and accompanies the seeking; (3) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: the forgiveness received addresses the subjective guilt that accompanies the objective record; Lev 23:27 — self-affliction is the guilt-posture of seeking; Luk 7:47 — the woman's love reveals guilt resolved; (4) *CHAR-11 (Sin as universal condition)*: Mar 3:28 — "all sins will be forgiven the children of man" — the universal human sin-condition (CHAR-11) is the scope within which CHAR-14 operates.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=15237, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-14 as the *resolution characteristic* of M10: it is the only characteristic in the cluster whose primary function is the removal of what all other M10 characteristics create or describe. CHAR-1 through CHAR-13 characterise various dimensions of sin's presence, accumulation, and power; CHAR-14 is the characteristic that addresses the record (CHAR-13), relieves the guilt (CHAR-17), and reverses the condition (CHAR-11/12). CHAR-14 is structurally the therapeutic complement to M10's diagnostic characteristics.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=15238, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are extensively evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=15239, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-14]** E — CHAR-14 consistently *follows* CHAR-13 (sin as divine record) and CHAR-3 (confession): the record exists (CHAR-13) → confession is made (CHAR-3) → forgiveness is sought and received (CHAR-14). CHAR-14 consistently *precedes* restoration characteristics (Spirit indwelling — Act 2:38; life replacing death — Col 2:13; love-orientation — Luk 7:47). The sequence: CHAR-1/6 (sinning) → CHAR-13 (record accumulated) → CHAR-3 (confession) → CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought and received) → restored relational standing → positive formation.

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=15240, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The relationship is *causal at every junction*: the record (CHAR-13) causally necessitates the seeking (CHAR-14); the seeking causally enables the receiving; the receiving causally produces the transformation (love — Luk 7:47; life — Col 2:13; Spirit — Act 2:38). The forward sequence is causal: without the record there is no need to seek; without seeking there is no receiving; without receiving there is no love-life-Spirit consequence. The backward sequence is equally causal: without forgiveness received, the record stands; without the record standing, there is no occasion for seeking.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=15241, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Sequential patterns (causal chain) are extensively evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=15242, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — multiple. (1) *Love as orientation*: Luk 7:47 — forgiveness received produces love as the person's sustained affective orientation; (2) *Restored relational standing*: Lev 16:30 — cleanness before God is the relational condition CHAR-14 produces; (3) *Spirit indwelling*: Act 2:38 — the Spirit's reception follows forgiveness as its produced consequence; (4) *Life replacing death*: Col 2:13 — "God made alive" — the constitutional life produced by forgiveness received. Each of these produced consequences belongs to M11 (restoration) territory, consistent with the characteristic's cross-register flag.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=15243, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-14]** E — CHAR-14 is *enabled* by other characteristics rather than produced by them. (1) *CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record) creates the necessity*: the accumulated record is what makes forgiveness-seeking necessary; without the record, there is nothing to seek forgiveness for; (2) *CHAR-3 (Confession) often precedes*: the verbal naming of sin (CHAR-3) often accompanies or precedes the seeking (Exo 32:31 — Moses confessing before seeking); (3) *CHAR-4's reversal enables*: conscience engagement (the opposite of CHAR-4 — conscience suppression) is what triggers the seeking when sin "becomes known" (Lev 4:14).

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=15244, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-14]** E — CHAR-14 is a constituent element of M11 (Repentance, Forgiveness, Restoration) — all three VCGs carry the M11 cross-register flag. Within M10, CHAR-14 is the resolution-characteristic that constitutes the reversal of CHAR-13's record and CHAR-11's condition. CHAR-3 (confession) is a partial constituent of CHAR-14's seeking dimension (Exo 32:31 — Moses's verbal naming before God is part of his seeking act).

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=15245, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are extensively evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=15246, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. Chat.tat (H2403B/H2403H — VCG-01/03) is shared across M10 as the dominant sin-term; CHAR-14 deploys it specifically as the sin-offering vehicle of seeking. Cha.ta.ah (H2401 — VCG-02) is shared with CHAR-13's cha.ta.ah register. Hamartia (G0266 — VCG-02) is shared across the NT M10 characteristics; CHAR-14 deploys it in the forgiveness-received register ("for the forgiveness of sins" — Mat 26:28; Act 2:38). Paraptōma (G3900 — VCG-02) is shared with CHAR-13's NT register; CHAR-14 deploys it in the forgiveness-of-trespasses context (Eph 1:7; Col 2:13; 2Cor 5:19).

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=15247, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. The kip.pu.rim (H3725) root — kāpar — is unique to CHAR-14's VCG-01 within the M10 cluster; no other M10 characteristic uses the kip.pu.rim covering-atonement root. This makes kāpar/kip.pu.rim CHAR-14's distinctive root contribution to M10's vocabulary architecture. The chat.tat/cha.ta root is shared with all other M10 characteristics (the universal M10 root). The NT hamartia/paraptōma roots are shared with CHAR-11/12/13.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=15248, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The shared vocabulary (chat.tat/hamartia/paraptōma across M10) confirms that CHAR-14 operates within the same semantic field as all other M10 characteristics — it addresses the same sin that the other characteristics describe. CHAR-14's unique vocabulary contribution (kip.pu.rim — covering-atonement) is the specific conceptual mechanism that distinguishes the characteristic's distinctive operation: the covering/atoning function is CHAR-14's unique terminological contribution to M10 alongside the shared sin-vocabulary.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=15249, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Vocabulary sharing is evidenced (chat.tat/cha.ta/hamartia/paraptōma across M10; kip.pu.rim unique to CHAR-14). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=15250, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-14]** E — CHAR-3 (Confession) most closely resembles CHAR-14's seeking dimension — both involve the person's verbal/volitional movement toward God in acknowledgment of sin. The precise distinction: CHAR-3 is the specific speech-act of acknowledging and naming the sin (the verbal confession); CHAR-14 is the broader characteristic that encompasses not just the verbal acknowledgment but the full seeking-and-receiving transaction — the bringing of the offering (Lev 4:14/5:6), the priestly mediation (Lev 10:17), the divine declaration (Luk 7:48), and the consequent transformation (Col 2:13; Act 2:38). CHAR-3 is one component that may accompany CHAR-14; CHAR-14 encompasses more.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=15251, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The overlap is at Exo 32:31 — Moses's verbal naming of the sin before God ("this people has sinned a great sin. They have made for themselves gods of gold") — this is simultaneously a confession-act (CHAR-3) and the verbal component of a forgiveness-seeking act (CHAR-14). The precise boundary: the naming/acknowledging is CHAR-3's domain; the going-up-to-the-Lord to seek atonement (Exo 32:30) and the priestly-mediation seeking is CHAR-14's broader domain. CHAR-3 is enclosed within CHAR-14's seeking dimension.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=15252, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The distinction is one of *scope*: CHAR-3 is the verbal-acknowledgment component; CHAR-14 is the full seeking-and-receiving transaction. CHAR-3 names one element of the seeking; CHAR-14 names the complete event including recognition, seeking, mediation, divine response, and consequent transformation. The direction is the same (human toward God); the constitutional level is the same (will and conscience); but the scope differs: CHAR-3 ⊂ CHAR-14 (CHAR-3 is contained within CHAR-14 as one of its components).

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=15253, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=15254, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. (1) Exo 32:30/31 — shared with CHAR-13 (sin as divine record — Moses's naming of the great sin before God); (2) Mat 26:28 — "blood of the covenant... for the forgiveness of sins" — also anchors M11 characteristics (forgiveness declared, new covenant); (3) Act 2:38 — "repent and be baptized... for the forgiveness of your sins" — anchors M11 (repentance) and is structurally related to CHAR-3 (confession) and M11 (restoration through Spirit); (4) Col 2:13 — "dead in your trespasses... made alive" — shared with CHAR-11 (universal condition — death in trespasses) and CHAR-12 (enslaving power — the trespass-death condition).

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=15255, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Exo 32:30/31 shared with CHAR-13 confirms that the great-sin naming and the forgiveness-seeking are inseparable in Moses's intercession: the record (CHAR-13) and the seeking of its removal (CHAR-14) co-occur in the same passage because they are the two faces of the same relational moment. Col 2:13 shared with CHAR-11/12 confirms that CHAR-14's "made alive" is the direct reversal of CHAR-11's "dead in trespasses" — the universal condition (CHAR-11) is what CHAR-14 addresses at the life-death level.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=15256, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=15257, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Three of CHAR-14's dimensions are shared. (1) *The sin-record-addressed dimension* is shared with CHAR-13 (the record that CHAR-14 removes); (2) *The confession-verbal dimension* of seeking is shared with CHAR-3; (3) *The guilt-relief dimension* of receiving is shared with CHAR-17 (guilt as inner-being state — the subjective guilt that forgiveness relieves). CHAR-14's unique dimensions (kip.pu.rim covering mechanism; Christological blood-atonement; the love-magnitude-proportional-to-forgiveness principle) are not shared with other M10 characteristics.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=15258, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-14 as the *resolution hub* of M10: it shares dimensions with CHAR-13 (the record addressed), CHAR-3 (the confession accompanying seeking), and CHAR-17 (the guilt relieved). These shared dimensions name exactly what forgiveness sought and received does in the landscape: it addresses the objective record, encompasses the subjective confession, and relieves the inner guilt. CHAR-14's unique dimensions (kip.pu.rim; blood-covenant; love-magnitude) are the mechanisms and consequences that belong distinctively to the resolution-characteristic.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=15259, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-14]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced across three adjacent characteristics (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=15260, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Four term-groups. (1) **kip.pu.rim (H3725)** — plural noun from kāpar: "coverings, atonements." The root kāpar means to cover, to smear, to make expiation. The covering metaphor is the OT's primary forgiveness-image: sin is not destroyed but covered so that divine judgment does not fall upon it. The plural form kip.pu.rim names the institution rather than a single act: the Day of Coverings, the covering-system. (2) **chat.tat (H2403B/H2403H)** — the sin-offering: the animal whose death covers the sin; the term names both the sin committed and the offering that addresses it, revealing their inseparability in the OT economy. (3) **cha.ta.ah (H2401)** — the sin as the moral category requiring covering (Exo 32:30 — "make atonement for your sin"). (4) **hamartia (G0266) / hamartēma (G0265) / paraptōma (G3900)** — NT terms: hamartia as the sin whose forgiveness is declared (Mat 26:28 — "for the forgiveness of sins"); hamartēma (individual sinful act — Mar 3:28/29); paraptōma (trespass — Eph 1:7; Col 2:13; 2Cor 5:19 — the relational offence not counted against).

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=15261, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Kip.pu.rim (H3725) appears exclusively as a noun in the corpus — "Day of Atonement" (Lev 23:27/28; 25:9), "sin offering of atonement" (Num 29:11), "ram of atonement" (Num 5:8; Exo 30:16). The noun form names the atoning-institution rather than the atoning-act (kāpar as verb would name the act). Chat.tat appears as noun — sin/sin-offering (Lev 4:3/14/5:6/10/10:17/19; 2Ch 29:23/24) — consistently naming the offering-vehicle. In the NT: hamartia appears in the genitive of purpose — "for the forgiveness of sins" (*eis aphesin tōn hamartiōn* — Mat 26:28; Act 2:38) — naming sin as the object of forgiveness rather than as agent. The grammatical range: OT terms primarily nominal (institution-naming); NT terms primarily in purpose-constructions (naming what forgiveness addresses).

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=15262, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Kip.pu.rim within CHAR-14 covers: (1) the altar-consecration atonement (Exo 29:36 — purifying the altar); (2) the annual covering of the people (Lev 23:27/28; 25:9 — Day of Atonement); (3) the ransom-payment atonement (Exo 30:16 — atonement money for lives); (4) the guilt-offering atonement (Num 5:8 — ram of atonement). This range — from altar-purification through personal life-ransom through communal annual covering — reveals kāpar/kip.pu.rim as the most semantically versatile forgiveness-term in the OT. Hamartia in the NT covers: the sin whose forgiveness is announced (Mat 26:28), the sin that leads to eternal non-forgiveness (Mar 3:29), and the sin whose forgiveness opens Spirit-reception (Act 2:38).

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=15263, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes. The two primary term-pairs distinguish the mechanism from the object: (1) *Kip.pu.rim* names the covering-mechanism (the atonement institution and its operations); (2) *Chat.tat/hamartia/paraptōma* names the object being addressed (the sin/trespass requiring covering). Additionally: *hamartēma* (Mar 3:28/29) names individual specific sin-acts, distinguishing from hamartia's broader condition. The verb *aphiēmi* (to forgive, to release — implied in Luk 7:47/48 — "apheomai" — "are forgiven") names the divine releasing-act that accompanies the covering, complementing the noun-institutions with the verbal event of divine release.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=15264, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — Mar 3:29 — "never has forgiveness, but is guilty of an eternal sin" (*aiōniou hamartēmatos* — eternal sin). The "eternal sin" (*aiōnion hamartēma*) is the structural opposite of forgiveness received: the permanent guilt with no exit. The forgiveness-vocabulary (kip.pu.rim — covering; aphesis — release) is opposed by the eternal-guilt vocabulary (aiōnion hamartēma — permanent unforgiven sin-act). The structural opposite is thus lexically named rather than merely implied.

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=15265, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Implicitly. Luk 7:47 — "he who is forgiven little" (*hō oligon aphietai*) and "she loved much" — the contrast names two person-types: the much-forgiven and the little-forgiven. These are person-types defined by the magnitude of their forgiveness-reception. The priest (Lev 10:17 — "given to you that you may bear the iniquity") is the person-type of the forgiveness-mediator — the one through whom others seek and receive.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=15266, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Yes — Exo 32:30 — "perhaps I can make atonement for your sin" (*ûlây ăkappərâ bəʿad ḥaṭṭaṯkem*) — the "perhaps" (*ûlây*) is the seeking-term of uncertainty; Moses's going up to the Lord is the seeking-act itself. Act 2:38 — "repent" (*metanoeō*) — the NT seeking-term; Act 10:43 — "everyone who believes in him" (*pas ho pisteuōn eis auton*) — the believing is the faith-seeking act. The supplication vocabulary is embedded in the seeking-actions rather than in dedicated petition-terms.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=15267, E) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The relationship reveals *progressive mechanism-clarification*: the OT vocabulary (kip.pu.rim — covering; chat.tat — sin-offering) describes the mechanism institutionally without naming its ultimate ground; the NT vocabulary (hamartia aphesis — forgiveness of sins; "through his blood" — Eph 1:7; "blood of the covenant" — Mat 26:28) names the mechanism's ground (Christ's blood) and the institution's fulfillment. The OT system points forward through its provisional character (Exo 30:10 — "once in the year throughout your generations"); the NT declares the once-for-all fulfillment (Mat 26:28 — poured out "for many"). The relationship is type-to-antitype: OT covering mechanism → NT blood-atonement fulfillment.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=15268, E) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-14]** E — *Aphesis* (G0859 — forgiveness, release) is the specific NT term for the release/forgiveness granted — "the forgiveness of sins" (*aphesin tōn hamartiōn* — Mat 26:28; Act 2:38; Col 1:14). While aphesis has classical Greek precedent (release, pardon), its specific deployment for the divine act of sin-forgiveness through Christ's name is a NT development. The term names the receiving-side of the characteristic from the divine perspective: God's releasing of the sin-debt. Aphesis crystallises what kip.pu.rim described institutionally into a single declarative release-term.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=15269, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The vocabulary arc — from kāpar's covering-mechanism (OT — the sin covered by sacrifice) through chat.tat's sin-offering institution (OT — the animal bearing the sin) through aphesis's divine release-declaration (NT — God releasing the debt) through hamartia's forgiveness-scope (NT — the sin of the world whose Lamb takes it away — Joh 1:29) through paraptōma's not-counted-trespass (NT — 2Cor 5:19) — reveals a characteristic whose vocabulary moves from institutional-mechanism (how atonement is performed) to declarative-event (the moment of divine release). The arc is: ritual institution → sacrificial mechanism → priestly covering → divine declaration of release → permanent redemption-identity.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=15270, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The primary anchor is Mat 26:28: "for this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins." The primary terms function as: (1) *"blood of the covenant"* (*to haima mou tēs diathēkēs*) — the covenant-blood functions as the ground of forgiveness; the possessive ("my blood") identifies Christ as the one whose blood establishes the mechanism; (2) *"poured out for many"* (*to peri pollōn ekchunoumenon*) — the pouring-out (present passive participle) names the act of self-offering as the forgiveness-mechanism; (3) *"for the forgiveness of sins"* (*eis aphesin hamartiōn*) — the purpose clause names the telos: all of this is for the releasing of sins. The terms function together: mechanism (blood) + act (poured out) + scope (for many) + purpose (forgiveness).

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=15271, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Four literary forms. (1) *Legal-priestly code* (Lev 4; 5; 10; 16; 19; 23; 25; Num 5; 29; Exo 29; 30): prescriptive regulation of the atonement system; requires reading as divine instruction with institutional force; (2) *Narrative-dialogue* (Exo 32:30/31; 2Ch 29:23/24): historical narrative of specific seeking-events; requires reading within the dramatic-covenantal context; (3) *Dominical institution narrative* (Mat 26:28): the Last Supper as Jesus's instituting act; requires reading as a constitutive speech-act that establishes the new covenant's forgiveness-ground; (4) *Apostolic proclamation and epistle* (Act 2:38; 10:43; 2Cor 5:19; Eph 1:7; Col 1:14/2:13): proclamation of received forgiveness; requires reading within the gospel-mission and community-instruction context.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=15272, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Three key argument structures. (1) *Mat 26:28's institution argument*: this is my blood (ground) + poured out for many (mechanism) + for the forgiveness of sins (purpose) — the argument establishes the causal chain: covenant blood poured out produces forgiveness for the many who receive it. (2) *Act 2:38's sequence argument*: repent (human condition) + be baptized in Christ's name (human act) → forgiveness of sins (divine grant) + Holy Spirit received (divine consequence) — the argument establishes the seeking-sequence that produces receiving. (3) *Mar 3:28/29's scope-limit argument*: all sins and blasphemies will be forgiven (universal scope) BUT blasphemy against the Holy Spirit never has forgiveness (eternal limit) — the argument defines the characteristic's outer boundary by naming the single exception to its universal availability.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=15273, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Four contextual settings. (1) *Priestly-tabernacle/temple* (VCG-01/03 — Leviticus, Numbers): the sacred space as the designated location for forgiveness-seeking; the Temple as the divinely appointed site of the seeking-mechanism; (2) *National covenant renewal* (2Ch 29:23/24; Exo 32:30): the corporate-national context of seeking on behalf of the whole people; (3) *Dominical institution* (Mat 26:28): the Passover meal as the context within which Jesus announces the new covenant's forgiveness-ground; (4) *Gospel proclamation* (Act 2:38; 10:43): the Pentecost preaching and the Gentile mission as the contexts within which forgiveness is offered through faith/repentance.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=15274, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Mat 26:28 functions as the primary anchor: "for this is my blood of the covenant, which is poured out for many for the forgiveness of sins." It most fully expresses CHAR-14's essential character because: (1) it names the *mechanism* (blood of the covenant — the ground of forgiveness); (2) it names the *act* (poured out — the offering made); (3) it names the *scope* (for many — not merely individual); (4) it names the *purpose* (for the forgiveness of sins — the telos of the entire OT kip.pu.rim system fulfilled); (5) it is the hinge between the OT type (the annual atonement system) and the NT antitype (the once-for-all blood).

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=15275, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Mat 26:28 uniquely reveals the *new-covenant ground* of forgiveness: "my blood of the covenant... for the forgiveness of sins." No other verse in the 34-verse corpus names forgiveness as the explicit purpose of the covenant-establishing blood. The OT kip.pu.rim passages (VCG-01) establish the mechanism without naming the ultimate ground (Christ's blood); the NT forgiveness declarations (Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven") announce the result without explicitly naming the blood-ground; Mat 26:28 uniquely names both — the blood of the covenant (ground) and the forgiveness of sins (purpose) — in a single institutional declaration.

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=15276, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-14]** E — **Relational psychology** (specifically forgiveness research — Robert Enright's forgiveness therapy; Everett Worthington's REACH forgiveness model) and **jurisprudence/debt-cancellation theory** provide the most useful frameworks. Relational psychology illuminates the relational-restoration function: the characteristic's purpose (2Cor 5:19 — reconciliation) parallels what forgiveness research identifies as the restoration of the broken relational bond. Jurisprudence illuminates the non-imputation metaphor (2Cor 5:19 — "not counting their trespasses against them") through debt-cancellation: God's forgiveness is structurally analogous to a creditor choosing not to enforce a debt-claim.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=15277, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Relational psychology illuminates Luk 7:47's love-magnitude-proportional-to-forgiveness-magnitude principle: forgiveness research confirms that the depth of reconciliation experienced correlates with the perceived magnitude of the offense forgiven. The woman who loved much had received forgiveness for many sins — the magnitude of the forgiveness received produced a proportional love-response. This parallels Worthington's finding that deeper forgiveness (of greater offenses) produces more profound relational transformation. Debt-cancellation jurisprudence illuminates 2Cor 5:19 ("not counting their trespasses") through the concept of a creditor unilaterally voiding a debt — the debtor is released not because the debt was never real but because the creditor absorbs it.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=15278, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-14]** E — The primary divergence: relational psychology treats forgiveness as a process the offended party works through internally (primarily intra-psychological); the verse evidence treats forgiveness as a declarative act by God (Luk 7:48 — "your sins are forgiven") that produces an objective status-change in the forgiven person. The psychological framework focuses on the forgiver's internal process; the corpus focuses on the divine declaration that changes the forgiven person's relational standing. The divergence reveals that biblical forgiveness is not primarily a psychological process in the forgiver but a legal-relational declaration that changes the forgiven person's objective standing before God — independent of God's internal process (God does not need to "work through" forgiving in the psychological sense).

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=15279, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-14]** E — Relational psychology surfaces the *experience of the forgiven person integrating the forgiveness* — what it is like to live as a forgiven person over time, the process of accepting and internalising the objective status-change. The verse evidence addresses the transformation consequences (love — Luk 7:47; life — Col 2:13; Spirit — Act 2:38) but does not describe the process of the forgiven person integrating the forgiveness into their ongoing self-understanding. The gap: the subjective phenomenology of living as a forgiven person (distinct from the objective status-change). The gap is acknowledged as a human-science complement; no further verse investigation is required within this characteristic's scope.

---

T7 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       20/20 ✓ (T7.1.1–T7.1.10, T7.2.1–T7.2.6, T7.3.1–T7.3.4)
  2. Scope marker:       20× [CHAR-14] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 29:36/30:10/16/32:30; Lev 4:3/14/5:6/10/10:17/16:30/23:27/28/19:22/25:9; Num 5:8/29:11; 2Ch 29:23/24; Mat 26:28; Mar 3:28/29; Luk 7:47/48; Act 2:38/10:43; 2Cor 5:19; Eph 1:7; Col 1:14/2:13 — all in §3)
  → PASS. All 8 tiers complete.

## CHAR-15 — Generational sin (189 rows: E=168 S=21 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=15280, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The 8-verse corpus reveals God's character across three dimensions. (1) *God as the one who holds generations accountable to the ancestral record*: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — God addresses present Israel through the lens of ancestral failure; the present generation is addressed as the inheritors of what the first father began. (2) *God as the one who responds to prolonged corporate sin with judgment*: Jer 8:14 — "the Lord our God has doomed us to perish... because we have sinned against the Lord" — the corporate sinning of the present generation in continuity with the past produces divine doom; (3) *God as the redeemer who saves from ancestral backsliding*: Eze 37:23 — "I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them; and they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — God's redemptive purpose addresses the inherited sinning pattern from outside it.

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=15281, S) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-15]** S — No verse in the 8-verse corpus attributes the inherited sin-condition of generational sin to God. God appears as the one who addresses the condition (Isa 43:27 — naming the ancestral origin; Eze 37:23 — promising redemption from the backslidings) but not as the source or bearer of the condition. The characteristic is exclusively creaturely.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=15282, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The silence on God possessing the generational sin condition confirms its entirely fallen-creaturely nature: generational sin is the moral weight that accumulates through creaturely failure across generations. God cannot inherit sin because he has no predecessors whose sinning he would receive. Isa 43:27's structure — "your first father sinned" — presupposes a beginning-point of sinning in the first human ancestor, which God by definition does not share. The silence reveals that generational sin is the specific form of moral inheritance that belongs to finite creatures embedded in lineages, families, and nations — not to the eternal God who stands outside such transmission chains.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=15283, S) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The corpus provides no evidence of any positive created purpose for generational sin. The characteristic is presented entirely as a fallen-condition burden: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the prolonged state of sin is experienced as a problem requiring divine resolution, not as a serving-a-purpose feature. Eze 37:23's divine redemption-from-backsliding confirms the condition has no created-design function.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=15284, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Entirely a response to the fallen condition. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the origin is in the first father's sinning, which is the entrance of sin into the lineage; the mechanism requires a prior sinning-origin before transmission is possible. The creation-design had no first father sinning; the generational sin-condition begins with that sinning and propagates from it. The 8-verse corpus describes the pattern's sustained operation (Psa 78:17/32 — continuing to sin in the wilderness) and corporate confession of it (1Ki 8:47) — all post-fall dynamics.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=15285, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — toward two eschatological endpoints. (1) *Positive — divine cleansing*: Eze 37:23 — "I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them; and they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — the eschatological fullness of the covenant is the cleansing of the generationally accumulated sinning and the restored God-people relationship; (2) *Negative — curse*: Isa 65:20 — "the sinner a hundred years old shall be accursed" — the sinner whose characteristic is defined by sin faces eschatological curse even within the new-creation context of the surrounding prophecy.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=15286, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Generational sin reveals the divine image through its *transmission capacity*: only image-bearers who stand in lineages and covenant communities can transmit a morally weighted sinning-history to their descendants. The image was designed for community and continuity (the creation mandate involves generations — "be fruitful and multiply"); generational sin is the fallen-condition distortion of this design: what should transmit blessing, calling, and covenant transmits instead the sin-pattern and its moral weight. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — presupposes the image-bearer's capacity for a sinning act that can become the starting-point of a generational chain.

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=15287, S) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-15]** S — Exclusively creaturely. God has no ancestors whose sinning he could inherit; the transmission mechanism (lineage, family, national belonging) presupposes finite creatures embedded in historical succession. The characteristic is inapplicable to the divine nature.

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=15288, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The presence of generational sin reveals that the image-bearer's moral condition is not individually self-contained but socially and historically embedded: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the present image-bearer carries a moral liability whose origin precedes their individual existence. The image is thus not merely individually fallen (through personal sinning acts) but corporately and historically fallen through the accumulated sinning of predecessors. Psa 78:17/32 — the wilderness generation's persistent sinning despite wonders reveals an image whose sinning patterns are formed and reinforced across communal experience; the generational dimension deepens the image's fallen condition beyond what individual sinning alone would produce.

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=15289, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — through Isa 43:27's structure. "Your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — the naming of the "first father" as the origin-point of Israel's sinning functions as the typological ground: the ancestral sinning is the type that the present generation's condition fulfils. The first father's sin becomes the pattern that the mediators continued and the present nation inherits — a typological escalation-chain where the ancestral type is expanded and confirmed in each subsequent generation.

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=15290, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The direction runs from ancestral origin to present condition: the first father's sin (Isa 43:27) is the originating type whose consequences are now the present reality. The typological movement is temporal — from the founding sinning act forward through generations to the present corporate condition. The type (first father's sin) explains the current condition (Israel's moral liability before God). Eze 37:23's reversal — divine cleansing of the backslidings — points toward the antitype: the eschatological cleansing that breaks the generational chain.

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=15291, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Typological use is evidenced in Isa 43:27 (T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T0 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       12/12 ✓ (T0.1.1–T0.1.3, T0.2.1–T0.2.3, T0.3.1–T0.3.3, T0.4.1–T0.4.3)
  2. Scope marker:       12× [CHAR-15] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: 9/9 E findings cite evidence ✓ (T0.1.2, T0.2.1, T0.3.2 are S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Isa 43:27; Isa 64:5; Isa 65:20; Jer 8:14; Eze 37:23; 1Ki 8:47; Psa 78:17/32 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T1.

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=15292, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Generational sin." Two signals. (1) *Generational*: the defining mark is the transmission-across-generations dimension — the sin-condition is not merely the individual's own accumulated acts but enters through lineage, family, or national belonging; Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the present generation is addressed through the ancestral chain. (2) *Sin*: the transmitted condition is specifically a moral condition — guilt or moral liability, not merely a cultural or behavioural tendency. The name signals: a sin-condition whose origin and weight extends beyond the individual to encompass ancestors and the communities that shaped them.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=15293, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The single primary term across all 8 verses is **cha.ta (H2398)** — the verb "to sin, to miss the mark, to incur guilt." The verb form throughout this corpus is significant: generational sin is evidenced through the *act of sinning* as an inherited pattern, not through a distinct noun for the inherited condition itself. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" (*cha.ta*) — the past-tense verb names the ancestral sinning act that initiated the generational chain; Psa 78:17 — "they sinned still more" — the repeated sinning reinforces the pattern. No specialised vocabulary for the inherited-condition dimension exists in this 8-verse corpus; the inherited condition is described through the repeated sinning-verb applied to successive generations.

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=15294, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-15]** E — All three. *Directional*: the condition moves backward (from ancestors to present) and forward (from present to descendants); Isa 43:27's "first father sinned" is the backward direction; the ongoing pattern (Psa 78:17/32 — still sinning) is the forward continuation. *Relational*: the condition is constitutively relational — it operates within lineages, families, and covenant communities; it cannot be inherited without the relational bond of ancestry. *Constitutional*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the prolonged duration suggests the condition has become constitutional, not merely episodic.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=15295, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The corpus reveals a *corporate moral condition sustained across generations* — not primarily a discrete act but an embedded sinning-pattern that communities carry. Psa 78:17/32 — the wilderness generation sinned persistently in a pattern; Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the prolonged state confirms this is a condition, not a momentary act. However, the corpus also reveals individual participation in the corporate condition: 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned" — each member of the exiled community confesses within the corporate "we." The characteristic is thus a *corporate-condition in which individuals participate* rather than a purely individual condition.

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=15296, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The corpus reveals two constituent elements. (1) *The originating ancestral sinning act*: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — there is a founding sinful act from which the generational condition begins; (2) *The sustained corporate sinning pattern*: Psa 78:17/32; Isa 64:5 — successive generations continue in the sinning-pattern, deepening and extending the condition. The two elements are distinct: the origin (first father's act) and the continuation (each generation's own sinning within the inherited pattern) together constitute the full characteristic.

---

#### T1.2.3 (cf=15297, E) — What is the current best working description of this characteristic — encapsulating its constitutional location, the faculties it primarily engages, and its impact on the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Working description: *Generational sin (CHAR-15) is the corporate moral condition that originates in an ancestral sinning act (Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned") and is sustained across generations through the ongoing sinning of successive communities who inherit and continue the pattern. The inner person carries a moral liability not only for their own acts but for the accumulated sinning history of those before them. The corpus (8 verses, all cha.ta — VCG-01) evidences three dimensions: (1) The originating ancestral act (Isa 43:27); (2) The persistent corporate sinning pattern sustained across generations (Psa 78:17/32; Isa 64:5 — "a long time in our sins"); (3) The corporate confession that names the condition before God (1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned"; Jer 8:14 — "we have sinned against the Lord"). God's promise to cleanse the accumulated backsliding (Eze 37:23) names the eschatological resolution.*

---

#### T1.3.1 (cf=15298, E) — What is the structural opposite of this characteristic — the inner-being reality that stands against or excludes it?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The structural opposite is *generational faithfulness* — a lineage characterised by covenant-keeping rather than sinning. Eze 37:23's positive — "I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them; and they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — names the restored covenant-community as the opposite condition: cleansed from the accumulated pattern, restored to God-people relationship. The opposite of the "long time in our sins" (Isa 64:5) is the community that remembers God's ways and works righteousness (Isa 64:5 — "you meet him who joyfully works righteousness, those who remember you in your ways" — the positive foil stated in the same verse).

---

#### T1.3.2 (cf=15299, E) — What does this characteristic explicitly exclude or resist?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The characteristic explicitly excludes: (1) *Belief in God's wonders*: Psa 78:32 — "they still sinned; despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the persistent sinning is rooted in unbelief; belief in God's demonstrated power is what the sinning-pattern resists; (2) *Response to God's provision*: Psa 78:17 — "sinned still more against him, rebelling against the Most High in the desert" — the rebellion is against God's own desert provision; the sinning-pattern resists gratitude and appropriate response; (3) *Learning from the ancestral warning*: Isa 43:27 — the first father's sinning named as Israel's starting-point implies the warning that the pattern carries and which each generation resists receiving.

---

#### T1.3.3 (cf=15300, E) — What is this characteristic not — where does it end and something else begin?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Three boundaries. (1) *Not CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: CHAR-1 is the discrete individual wilful act; CHAR-15 is the inherited corporate condition within which individual sinning acts occur. Individual wilful sinning (CHAR-1) contributes to and expresses the generational condition (CHAR-15) but is not identical with it. (2) *Not CHAR-11 (Sin as universal condition)*: CHAR-11 is the structural ontological condition all persons share; CHAR-15 is the specific lineage-transmission mechanism. CHAR-11 is broader; CHAR-15 is a specific mode of entry into the condition. (3) *Not CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record)*: CHAR-13 is the accumulated moral account before God; CHAR-15 is the specific generational mechanism through which some of that account is inherited rather than personally accumulated.

---

#### T1.4.1 (cf=15301, E) — In what distinct ways does this characteristic operate within the inner person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Three inner modes evidenced. (1) *As an inherited unbelief disposition*: Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the sinning pattern is rooted in an inherited capacity for unbelief that does not yield to experienced divine power; (2) *As a prolonged inner condition*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the condition is not episodic but characterises the inner-being state over an extended time; (3) *As a pattern recognised and confessed corporately*: 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly" — the corporate inner recognition of the generational pattern is the conscience's response.

---

#### T1.4.2 (cf=15302, E) — Does this characteristic operate differently depending on context, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. (1) *Wilderness context* (Psa 78:17/32): the generational pattern operates through continued rebellion despite miraculous provision — God's visible acts are the context in which the inherited sinning persists; (2) *Exile context* (1Ki 8:47; Jer 8:14): the generational pattern has produced the condition of captivity; the confession occurs in exile, where the corporate consequences of the accumulated sin are most visible; (3) *Prophetic-covenantal context* (Isa 43:27; Isa 64:5): the pattern is named in its originating dimension (first father) and its prolonged-duration dimension ("a long time"), allowing both the origin and the extent to be addressed.

---

#### T1.4.3 (cf=15303, E) — Does this characteristic have a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — and if so, how does it function?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — the corporate confession is the speech-mode. 1Ki 8:47 — "saying, 'We have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly'" — the speech-act of corporate confession is the verbal form through which the generational pattern is acknowledged; Jer 8:14 — "because we have sinned against the Lord" — the corporate speech-act of naming the sin before God and the community. The corporate confessional "we" is the characteristic's distinctive communicative form: it is not individual private confession but communal speech that acknowledges the shared inherited condition.

---

#### T1.5.1 (cf=15304, E) — What is the first or most immediate inner-being response to receiving or encountering this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Corporate confession. 1Ki 8:47 — "they turn their heart in the land... and repent and plead with you... saying, 'We have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly'" — the immediate inner response to encountering the generational condition in exile is heart-turning, repentance, and verbal confession. Jer 8:14 — "we have sinned against the Lord" — the corporate recognition produces a speech-act of acknowledgment. The most immediate response is thus the collective recognition and naming of the condition before God.

---

#### T1.5.2 (cf=15305, E) — Is the immediate response consistent across the verse evidence, or does it vary?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Variable. 1Ki 8:47 and Jer 8:14 both show corporate confession as the immediate response in the exile-context. But Psa 78:17/32 shows the opposite: the immediate response to God's continued provision is *more sinning and unbelief*, not confession. The response thus depends on the moment in the generational cycle: in exile (where consequences are obvious), confession is the immediate response; in desert-provision (where consequences are not yet visible), persistent sinning is the immediate response.

---

#### T1.5.3 (cf=15306, E) — Where the verse evidence is silent on immediate response, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — For the prophetic passages (Isa 43:27; Isa 64:5; Isa 65:20; Eze 37:23), the immediate inner response of the person who hears the naming of the generational pattern is not directly described. The naming of the first father's sin (Isa 43:27) and the prolonged-sin condition (Isa 64:5) describe the condition without specifying the hearer's immediate inner response.

---

#### T1.6.1 (cf=15307, E) — What does this characteristic produce in the inner being over time?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Three sustained inner effects. (1) *Prolonged condition of sinning*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the sustained inner state is one of persistent sin-conditionedness over an extended period; (2) *Unbelief resistant to evidence*: Psa 78:32 — "in spite of all this, they still sinned; despite his wonders, they did not believe" — sustained generational sinning produces a hardened unbelief that does not yield to divine demonstration; (3) *Corporate doom*: Jer 8:14 — "the Lord our God has doomed us to perish... because we have sinned" — the sustained sinning produces an inner sense of hopelessness and doom within the corporate community.

---

#### T1.6.2 (cf=15308, E) — What states, qualities, capacities, or orientations does sustained exposure to or possession of this characteristic establish?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Three established states. (1) *Prolonged sin-conditionedness*: Isa 64:5 — "a long time in our sins" — the settled inner state of being-in-sin across generations; (2) *Rebellion-oriented inner disposition*: Psa 78:17 — "rebelling against the Most High" — the sustained sinning establishes a rebellious inner orientation toward God rather than trust; (3) *Defilement*: Eze 37:23 — "they shall not defile themselves anymore" — the sustained backslidings have produced a state of inner defilement that requires divine cleansing before it can be reversed.

---

#### T1.6.3 (cf=15309, E) — Does the sustained effect differ from the immediate response — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. The immediate response (corporate confession in exile — 1Ki 8:47; Jer 8:14) is an episodic event. The sustained effects (prolonged sinning — Isa 64:5; hardened unbelief — Psa 78:32; defilement — Eze 37:23) are constitutional conditions that develop over the entire generational span. The immediate confession (acute) gives way to — or coexists with — the sustained condition that requires divine redemption to address (Eze 37:23).

---

#### T1.7.1 (cf=15310, E) — What inner conditions or orientations enable genuine reception of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Corporate-community belonging*: membership in the lineage, family, or nation is the enabling condition for receiving the generational liability; Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the "your" addresses those who stand in the lineage of the first father; one cannot inherit what one is not lineally connected to; (2) *Inherited unbelief disposition*: Psa 78:32 — "they did not believe" — the unbelief that drives the generational pattern is itself enabled by the community's sustained failure to receive God's demonstrated faithfulness as a reason for trust.

---

#### T1.7.2 (cf=15311, E) — What inner conditions block, distort, or prevent reception?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The opposite of the generational pattern's unbelief: Isa 64:5 — "you meet him who joyfully works righteousness, those who remember you in your ways" — joyful righteousness-working and remembering God's ways are the inner conditions that resist the generational sinning pattern. These are the alternative inner orientations that break the cycle within a generation. Eze 37:23 — God's cleansing and saving — names the divine intervention that is ultimately what blocks the pattern when human inner conditions alone cannot.

---

#### T1.7.3 (cf=15312, S) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The corpus does not describe the inner-being state of the individual within the community who recognises the generational sin-pattern but stands apart from it. Isa 43:27 names the first father's sin; Eze 37:23 names the promise to cleanse; neither describes the inner experience of the individual who breaks the pattern within their generation.

---

#### T1.8.1 (cf=15313, E) — What is the primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic from the programme's dimension vocabulary?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The primary dimension is **corporate moral identity** — the person's constitutive belonging to a community whose accumulated sinning history shapes their inner moral condition. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — the present-tense "your" makes the ancestral sinning part of the current person's moral identity through their lineage. 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned" — the "we" is not merely a grammatical convenience but names the corporate moral subject as the primary dimension.

---

#### T1.8.2 (cf=15314, E) — What evidence from the verse evidence supports this classification?

**[CHAR-15]** E — (1) Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the ancestral sin is addressed to the present community as their inherited condition; (2) 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned" — the corporate "we" is the locus of the characteristic; (3) Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the possessive "our" names the shared corporate condition; (4) Jer 8:14 — "we have sinned against the Lord" — the corporate acknowledgment. All uses are corporate rather than individually-bounded.

---

#### T1.8.3 (cf=15315, E) — Does this characteristic carry secondary dimensions — and if so, what are they, and do they compete with the primary classification?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Two secondary dimensions. (1) *Temporal/historical dimension*: the condition extends across time — "a long time in our sins" (Isa 64:5); the temporal reach backward (to the first father) and forward (to the present and potentially future generations) is a secondary dimension that distinguishes CHAR-15 from merely corporate sin; (2) *Will/unbelief dimension*: Psa 78:32 — "they did not believe" — the unbelief that drives the sustained sinning is a secondary will-level dimension; the generational pattern is not merely structural but is sustained by each generation's own volitional unbelief.

---

  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-15] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T1.5.3, T1.7.3 are S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓

## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=15316, E) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — through the defilement and cleansing language. Eze 37:23 — "they shall not defile themselves anymore with their idols and their detestable things, or with any of their transgressions. But I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them" — the defilement and cleansing operate at the spirit level: idolatry defiles the inner person's spirit-orientation toward God; the divine cleansing restores it. The "shall be my people, and I will be their God" restoration names the spirit-level relational restoration that follows cleansing.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=15317, E) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Partially. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the originating act of the first father is a will-level sinning act rather than exclusively a spirit-level phenomenon. However, Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the unbelief that drives the persistent sinning is a spirit-level failure: the failure to trust God's power and faithfulness at the deepest level of the person's God-orientation. The generational pattern thus has a spirit-level dimension in its sustaining mechanism (unbelief) even if its origin is in a specific volitional act.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=15318, E) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Spirit-level location (Eze 37:23 — defilement through idolatry; unbelief — Psa 78:32) reveals that generational sin operates at the deepest constitutional level of the community's God-relationship: the idolatry that defiles (Eze 37:23) is a spirit-level misorientation of the community's allegiance from God toward idols; the unbelief (Psa 78:32) is the spirit-level failure of trust that perpetuates the sinning across generations. The divine cleansing required (Eze 37:23) is therefore spirit-level restoration, not merely behavioural correction.

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=15319, E) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Spirit-level location (defilement — Eze 37:23; unbelief — Psa 78:32) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=15320, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. The characteristic moves from inner to outer and from outer-consequence back to inner. (1) *Inner-to-outer*: the ancestral sinning acts (Isa 43:27 — inner volitional acts) produce outer national consequences (Jer 8:14 — "doomed us to perish... has given us poisoned water to drink"); the inner sinning generates the outer catastrophic condition; (2) *Outer-consequence to inner-recognition*: the outer exile-condition (1Ki 8:47 — "the land to which they have been carried captive") produces the inner heart-turning and confession ("turn their heart... repent and plead"); the outer consequence drives the inner recognition.

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=15321, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The movement sequence evidenced in 1Ki 8:47 and Jer 8:14: (1) ancestral sinning acts (inner) → accumulated moral condition across generations → outer covenant-judgment (exile, doom, poisoned water); (2) outer consequence (captivity, doom) → inner recognition and heart-turning → corporate confession ("we have sinned"). The sequence is: inner-sinning-acts → outer-judgment-consequences → inner-repentance-recognition → (potential) divine-restoration. The outer catastrophe serves as the mechanism that drives the inner sequence from acknowledgment to potential restoration.

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=15322, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Constitutional movement (inner → outer → inner) is evidenced (T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=15323, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart in the land to which they have been carried captive, and repent and plead with you" — the heart-turning is a soul-level act; the repentance is the will's movement; the pleading is the whole-person's appeal to God. The corporate "we" of the confession (1Ki 8:47; Jer 8:14) names each member of the community as a soul-level moral subject participating in the corporate condition.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=15324, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Soul-level location (1Ki 8:47 — heart-turning; Jer 8:14 — "we have sinned") reveals that generational sin, though corporate in origin, engages the individual soul-level moral subject: each person who confesses "we have sinned" participates personally in the corporate condition's acknowledgment. The characteristic is not merely an external legal-status imputed from outside but is genuinely carried at the soul level of each community member who stands within the lineage.

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=15325, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (1Ki 8:47; Jer 8:14). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=15326, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart in the land to which they have been carried captive, and repent" — the heart is explicitly named as the constitutional centre of the turning-toward-God movement; the heart is also implicitly the site of the prior condition that needs turning from. The heart's wrongly-oriented condition (having produced or sustained the generational sinning) requires turning as the response.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=15327, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Heart-location (1Ki 8:47 — "turn their heart") reveals that generational sin has penetrated to the person's moral-volitional centre: the heart must turn, which implies it was previously oriented in a wrong direction sustained by the generational pattern. The heart's turning is the required reversal of what the generational sin-condition has made the heart's default orientation. The heart is thus both the site of the condition's hold and the site of the potential turning that addresses it.

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=15328, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Heart-location is evidenced (1Ki 8:47). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=15329, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The mind is implicitly engaged through the unbelief dimension. Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the failure to believe (the cognitive-trust faculty) is the mind-level operation through which the generational sinning is perpetuated; the mind's refusal to integrate God's demonstrated power into trust is the cognitive dimension of the persistent pattern.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=15330, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Mind-location (Psa 78:32 — unbelief despite wonders) reveals that the generational sinning pattern operates partly through the mind's persistent refusal to draw the correct cognitive conclusion from God's actions. The mind that has witnessed divine wonders and still does not believe is exhibiting a constitutively resistant cognitive orientation — a mind-level component of the generational pattern that makes it self-perpetuating: even extraordinary evidence does not break the inherited unbelief-pattern in the mind.

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=15331, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Mind-location (unbelief — Psa 78:32) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=15332, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — *Will* as a distinct soul-level faculty is evidenced through the rebellion dimension. Psa 78:17 — "yet they sinned still more against him, rebelling against the Most High in the desert" — rebellion (*mārāh* — to be contentious, to rebel) is a volitional act: the will actively sets itself against God rather than passively drifting. The "still more" indicates an escalating volitional commitment to the sinning pattern. The will's active rebellion is the soul-level faculty through which the generational pattern is individually sustained.

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=15333, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Will-level location (Psa 78:17 — rebellion; Psa 78:32 — "they still sinned" — volitional persistence) reveals that generational sin, though transmitted through lineage, is actively maintained by each generation's own volitional choices: the inherited pattern requires individual volitional engagement to persist. The "still sinned" language (Psa 78:17/32 — "still more," "still sinned") names the will's active continuation of the inherited pattern rather than passive reception of it.

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=15334, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Will-level location is evidenced (Psa 78:17/32). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=15335, S) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The 8-verse corpus does not explicitly name a body part in connection with generational sin. The characteristic operates through the corporate moral condition, heart-turning, and volitional rebellion without explicit body-part vocabulary.

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=15336, S) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-15]** S — No body-part link is evidenced. This prompt is not applicable.

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=15337, S) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** S — No body-part link is evidenced in the 8-verse corpus. Noted explicitly.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=15338, S) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-15]** S — No body-part link is evidenced (T2.6.3). This prompt is not applicable.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=15339, S) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** S — No body-part link evidenced. The characteristic operates at the corporate-moral, heart, mind, and will levels without bodily-organic expression in this corpus.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=15340, S) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** S — No body-characteristic link is evidenced. Noted explicitly.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=15341, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — two deposit forms. (1) *Prolonged sin-conditionedness*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the extended duration of the sinning-condition is a constitutional deposit; the community has been formed by the sustained pattern into a people characterised by it; (2) *Defilement*: Eze 37:23 — "they shall not defile themselves anymore... I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them" — the defilement produced by the accumulated backslidings is the constitutional deposit requiring divine cleansing; the community's inner constitution has been defiled by the sustained sinning-pattern.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=15342, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Supporting: Isa 64:5 ("a long time in our sins" — duration as deposit); Eze 37:23 (defilement requiring cleansing — constitutional deposit). Contradicting or complicating: 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart... and repent" — the possibility of turning implies the deposit is not constitutionally irreversible; the heart can turn even within the accumulated condition. The deposit is real (long-duration sinning, defilement) but is addressable through divine intervention (Eze 37:23 — "I will cleanse them") and human heart-turning (1Ki 8:47).

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=15343, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Deposit evidence is present: prolonged sin-condition (Isa 64:5) and defilement (Eze 37:23). T5.7 will carry: the deposit is corporate-temporal (long duration) and spiritual-defiling; the deposit is addressable through divine cleansing (Eze 37:23) and not constitutionally irremovable.

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=15344, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the constitutional origin is in the first father's volitional sinning act. This is the explicit ancestral origin of the generational condition: the "first father" is the founding-originator whose sinning created the initial entry-point. The mediators' subsequent transgression (Isa 43:27 — "and your mediators transgressed against me") confirms that the condition was reinforced at each stage of institutional leadership, deepening what the first father began.

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=15345, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Both — a singular founding origin with multiple subsequent reinforcing origins. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — singular founding origin; "and your mediators transgressed against me" — multiple subsequent institutional transgressors who deepened and extended the condition. The origin is thus singular in its founding moment (first father) but multiplied through subsequent generations of leaders and communities who continued the pattern.

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=15346, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. Isa 43:27 traces the origin to the first father (the founding individual transgressor). Psa 78:17/32 traces the origin of the specific desert-generation pattern to their own willful rebellion and unbelief — within the same community's history, different generations have their own originating sinning-moments. The origin is thus both historically fixed (first father as the chain's start) and generationally repeated (each generation has its own originating sinning in continuation of the chain).
