## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=15347, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The perceptive faculty is engaged through its failure in the wilderness generation and its eventual engagement in exile. Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the wonders were perceived (the people witnessed God's acts) but the perceptive faculty failed to convert the witnessed evidence into belief; the perception registered but the moral-spiritual interpretation of what was perceived was blocked by the generational unbelief pattern. 1Ki 8:47 — the exiles' heart-turning occurs specifically when they perceive their situation in captivity; the external perception of their exile-condition enables the internal recognition of the sin-pattern.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=15348, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational sinning pattern *impairs* the perceptive faculty's moral-spiritual interpretation function. Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the wonders are visually perceived but the generational pattern of unbelief impairs the perceptive faculty's capacity to process what is seen into trust. The pattern bypasses the faculty's normal function: ordinarily, evidence of God's power would produce faith; the generational sinning pattern insulates the community against this normal response.

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=15349, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The perceptive pattern reveals that the generational sin-pattern creates a *perceptive insulation* against divine evidence: Psa 78:32 — wonders witnessed but not believed — the inherited unbelief filters out the faith-producing implications of divine acts. This perceptive insulation is one of the mechanisms that makes generational sin self-perpetuating: the community cannot easily break the pattern because even direct divine intervention (wonders) does not penetrate the perceptive barrier created by the inherited unbelief orientation.

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=15374, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Conscientiousness is engaged in the confession and repentance response. 1Ki 8:47 — "turn their heart... and repent and plead with you... saying, 'We have sinned'" — the turning, repenting, pleading, and confessing represent a full conscientiousness-engagement: moral awareness (recognising the sin), volitional commitment (turning, repenting), and action (pleading, confessing). The corporate confession is a conscientious act on the community's part in response to the generational condition.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=15375, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — In the active-sinning phase, the generational pattern *misdirects* conscientiousness: the community of Psa 78:17 is conscientiously rebelling — not passively drifting but actively choosing rebellion. In the confession phase (1Ki 8:47), the conscientiousness is correctly directed toward repentance and restoration-seeking. The generational pattern thus captures conscientiousness in the wrong direction during sinning and redirects it toward the right direction in confession.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=15376, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The conscientiousness pattern reveals that the generational sinning-pattern is not a failure of conscientiousness but its misdirection: Psa 78:17 — "rebelling against the Most High" — the rebellion is conscientiously enacted (deliberately, persistently). This makes the condition more entrenched, not less: a community that is conscientiously committed to a wrong direction is harder to turn than one that has merely drifted. The heart-turning (1Ki 8:47) requires redirecting a genuine conscientiousness rather than awakening a dormant one.

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=15377, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The relational capacity is constitutively engaged — generational sin is fundamentally a disrupted-relational condition. (1) *Disruption of God-community relationship*: Jer 8:14 — "the Lord our God has doomed us to perish" — the accumulated sinning has transformed the God-people relationship to one of doom; Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned... and your mediators transgressed against me" — the ancestral sin and mediatorial transgression are addressed as offenses against God's "me"; (2) *Potential restoration*: Eze 37:23 — "they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — the restored God-people relational formula is the eschatological goal.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=15378, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational pattern *damages and progressively severs* the relational capacity toward God. Jer 8:14's "doomed us to perish" — the God-relationship has deteriorated to a state of divine doom rather than divine provision. Isa 64:5 — "you were angry, and we sinned" — the divine anger and the human sinning form a deteriorating relational cycle. The relational capacity is not eliminated (the community can still address God — 1Ki 8:47 — plead with God) but its normal functioning (blessed God-people relationship) has been severely impaired by the accumulated sinning.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=15379, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The relational pattern reveals that generational sin is constitutively a relational deterioration accumulated across generations: each generation's sinning deepens the relational rupture with God (Psa 78:17 — "rebelling against the Most High"; Isa 43:27 — "your mediators transgressed against me"). The cumulative relational damage is what makes the condition's weight greater than any single generation's sinning would produce: the inherited relational deficit adds to each new generation's own relational failures. Eze 37:23's restoration — "they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — confirms that restoring the relational formula is precisely what the accumulated sinning had disrupted.

---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=15350, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — through the unbelief dimension. Psa 78:32 — "they did not believe" — belief is a cognitive-trust act; the failure to believe despite wonders is a cognitive failure to integrate evidence correctly. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the prophetic speech engages the cognitive faculty of the present generation as it hears its ancestral history named; the cognitive reception of this naming is required for the generational pattern to be addressed.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=15351, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational pattern *impairs* the cognitive integration of divine evidence into faith. Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the cognitive faculty received the evidence (wonders witnessed) but failed to produce the correct output (belief). The generational unbelief pattern short-circuits the cognitive pathway from evidence to trust: the inherited disposition toward not-believing overrides the cognitive faculty's normal evidence-processing function.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=15352, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals that generational sin impairs not raw cognitive capacity (the people perceived the wonders) but *cognitive-moral integration*: the capacity to interpret what is cognitively registered in light of the correct moral-theological framework. Psa 78:32's unbelief despite wonders is a failure of moral-theological cognition rather than sensory cognition. The generational pattern corrupts the interpretive framework through which evidence is processed rather than eliminating the capacity to perceive evidence.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=15353, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — through the explicit memory-failure dimension. Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — and the broader Psalm 78 context (a historical recital of God's acts that Israel failed to remember — the psalm's genre is anamnesis, calling the community to remember what was forgotten). Isa 64:5 — "those who remember you in your ways" — the positive foil states that *remembering* God's ways is what the generational sinning-pattern fails to do; the inherited condition is characterised by a failure of covenantal memory.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=15354, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational sinning pattern *impairs* covenantal memory. Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the failure to remember God's acts in a way that produces present trust is the memory-impairment. Isa 64:5 — "those who remember you in your ways" (the positive opposite of the generational sinners) — the generational sin-condition is characterised by the failure to remember God's ways in a life-shaping manner. The community forgets what it witnessed, and the forgetting enables the sinning to continue.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=15355, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The memory pattern reveals that generational sin perpetuates itself partly through *covenantal memory failure*: the community fails to transmit an effective memory of God's acts and character across generations, leaving each new generation without the formative memory that would orient them toward trust. Psa 78's genre (a historical recital of Israel's failures) is itself a corrective to this memory-failure: the psalm attempts to do what the generational pattern prevents — transmit an accurate covenantal memory that enables trust rather than repetition of the sinning-pattern.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=15356, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The affective faculty is engaged through the doom-despair dimension. Jer 8:14 — "the Lord our God has doomed us to perish and has given us poisoned water to drink, because we have sinned against the Lord" — the corporate recognition of doom produces an affective state of hopelessness: "why do we sit still?" — paralysis and despair. Isa 64:5 — "and shall we be saved?" — the question expresses an affective state of uncertainty about whether salvation is possible after the long duration of the sinning-condition.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=15357, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational pattern *deepens negative affect* over time. Jer 8:14's despair and Isa 64:5's salvific uncertainty are the affective consequences of the "long time in our sins": the prolonged duration of the condition deepens the affective state from acute sinning-recognition toward chronic hopelessness. The question "shall we be saved?" (Isa 64:5) names an affective state of uncertainty about liberation that the long duration of the generational pattern has produced.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=15358, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The affective pattern reveals that generational sin produces a characteristic affective trajectory: early persistence (Psa 78:17/32 — still sinning without apparent affective weight) → prolonged condition (Isa 64:5 — "a long time") → despair and doom-recognition (Jer 8:14 — "doomed to perish") → salvific uncertainty (Isa 64:5 — "shall we be saved?"). The long duration of the generational condition progressively deepens the negative affect from indifference through despair to fundamental doubt about the possibility of salvation.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=15359, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The 8-verse corpus does not explicitly engage the creative faculty in connection with generational sin. The characteristic operates through corporate moral identity, volitional rebellion, and memory-failure rather than through creative origination.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=15360, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** S — No evidence. The corpus is silent on the creative faculty's engagement with generational sin.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=15361, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The silence on creativity is consistent with the characteristic's focus on inherited corporate moral identity rather than individual creative capacity. Noted.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=15362, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — the volitional faculty is centrally engaged as both the sustainer and the potential breaker of the generational pattern. (1) *Sustaining*: Psa 78:17 — "rebelling against the Most High" — the will's active rebellion sustains the inherited sinning pattern; "sinned still more" implies escalating volitional commitment; (2) *Breaking*: 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart... and repent" — the turning is a volitional reversal; repentance is the will's decision to move in a different direction; Eze 37:23 — "they shall not defile themselves anymore" — the future volitional commitment to non-defilement is the pattern's reversal.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=15363, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational pattern *captures and progressively deepens* volitional commitment to sinning: Psa 78:17 — "sinned still more" — the volitional momentum is in the direction of increasing sin; the "still more" indicates the will becoming progressively more committed to the inherited direction rather than less. However, 1Ki 8:47 confirms the will retains the capacity to turn (the heart can be turned; repentance is possible), meaning the generational pattern does not eliminate volition but skews its default direction.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=15364, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The volitional pattern reveals the generational pattern's dynamic: the inherited sinning condition creates a volitional default-direction toward sin (each generation's "still sinning" — Psa 78:17/32) while the potential for heart-turning remains (1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart"). Generational sin is thus not volitional determinism but *volitional habituation*: the will's default is shaped by the inherited pattern, but the capacity for the decisive turn (repentance — 1Ki 8:47) is not eliminated. The will must actively turn against the inherited direction rather than merely choosing differently.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=15365, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — through both rebellion and confession. (1) *Agency in rebellion*: Psa 78:17 — "rebelling against the Most High in the desert" — the rebellion is an agentive act against God; (2) *Agency in confession*: 1Ki 8:47 — "turn their heart... and repent and plead" — the turning, repentance, and pleading are all agentive acts through which the community addresses the inherited condition; Jer 8:14 — "we have sinned against the Lord" — the corporate acknowledgment is an agentive speech-act.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=15366, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational pattern *misdirects* agency: the community's agentive capacity is deployed toward rebellion (Psa 78:17 — actively rebelling) rather than toward covenant-faithfulness. The "still more" (Psa 78:17) indicates escalating agentive investment in the wrong direction. However, the possibility of corporate repentance (1Ki 8:47) confirms that agency is not eliminated — it can be redirected through the heart-turning act.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=15367, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The agency pattern reveals that generational sin is not passive reception of a condition but requires active volitional-agentive participation: each generation actively rebels (Psa 78:17 — "rebelling") and "sins still more" (Psa 78:32). The generational pattern is thus sustained by genuine human agency at each stage, which is why the response also requires genuine agency — the active heart-turning (1Ki 8:47) rather than passive waiting for the inherited condition to dissolve.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=15368, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — through the corporate conscience-recognition in confession. 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly" — the moral evaluation faculty correctly identifies the condition ("sinned... perversely... wickedly") in the context of corporate confession; Jer 8:14 — "because we have sinned against the Lord" — moral causation is correctly identified. Isa 64:5 — "shall we be saved?" — the moral evaluation faculty raises the salvific question based on its recognition of the prolonged sinning-condition.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=15369, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational pattern *impairs* moral evaluation in the active sinning phase and *enables* it only under pressure of consequences. Psa 78:32 — "they still sinned; despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the moral evaluation faculty fails to correctly identify the significance of the wonders for the community's moral orientation. The moral evaluation faculty is only correctly engaged when the outer consequences are undeniable (1Ki 8:47 — in captivity; Jer 8:14 — facing doom). The generational pattern delays and distorts correct moral evaluation until catastrophe forces it.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=15370, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that generational sin produces a characteristic moral-evaluation delay: the community's moral evaluation faculty engages correctly only after the consequences of the sinning-pattern have become catastrophic (exile, doom — 1Ki 8:47; Jer 8:14). Before that threshold, the moral evaluation faculty is impaired by the generational unbelief-pattern (Psa 78:32 — correct evaluation suppressed by the inherited disposition). The pattern thus reveals moral evaluation as the last faculty to engage, not the first.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=15371, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — the corporate confession engages the conscience. 1Ki 8:47 — "saying, 'We have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly'" — the confession is the conscience's collective recognition and naming of the sinning-condition; the specificity of "perversely and wickedly" suggests a conscience that has registered the nature of the failure. Jer 8:14 — "because we have sinned against the Lord" — the corporate conscience identifies the moral cause of the doom.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=15372, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The generational pattern *suppresses* conscience in the active-sinning phase and activates it under extreme consequence. Psa 78:17/32 — persistent sinning despite wonders suggests a suppressed or numbed conscience (the pattern continues without apparent internal resistance). 1Ki 8:47 — the exile produces the conscience's awakening: the heart turns and the conscience speaks through the corporate confession. The generational pattern progressively numbs the conscience until external catastrophe pierces the suppression.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=15373, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The conscience pattern reveals that the generational sinning-pattern produces a progressively numbed corporate conscience: Psa 78:17/32 — the community continues sinning despite evidence, suggesting the corporate conscience is not generating effective resistance. The numbing is broken only by the extremity of exile and doom (1Ki 8:47; Jer 8:14) — catastrophic consequence is what the conscience required to pierce the generational suppression. The pattern thus shows conscience as the faculty most progressively suppressed by sustained generational sinning.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=15380, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — God operates toward the community bearing the generational sin-condition in three modes. (1) *Anger and judgment*: Isa 64:5 — "you were angry, and we sinned" — God's anger is a response to the accumulated sinning; the divine anger is experienced as a force operating against the community; Jer 8:14 — "the Lord our God has doomed us to perish and has given us poisoned water to drink, because we have sinned against the Lord" — divine doom and poisoned water are God's active judgment directed toward the community bearing the generational condition. (2) *Naming the ancestral origin*: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — God's prophetic word operates toward the present community by naming the generational chain; this is itself a relational act. (3) *Redemptive promise*: Eze 37:23 — "I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them; and they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — God's sovereign promise to save and cleanse operates toward the community from outside the condition.

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=15381, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Two bases are evidenced. (1) *Moral causation*: Jer 8:14 — "because we have sinned against the Lord" — the divine doom is explicitly caused by the accumulated sinning; God's judgment-response is morally grounded in what the generational pattern has produced; Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — the transgression against God is the basis of the relational deterioration. (2) *Sovereign redemptive purpose*: Eze 37:23 — "I will save them... and they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — the covenant-restoration promise is grounded in God's own sovereign purpose, not in the community's remediation of the condition; God acts because of who he is (the covenant God) not because the community has earned rescue.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=15382, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — God's disposition is one of *morally engaged anger moving toward sovereign redemption*. Isa 64:5 — "you were angry, and we sinned" — the anger is real and provoked by the sinning-pattern; God is not indifferent to the accumulated condition. Jer 8:14 — doom declared — the judgment is the consequence of the sustained sinning. But Eze 37:23 — "I will save them... I will cleanse them... they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — the disposition moves through judgment toward restoration: God's ultimate purpose is not the community's destruction under the generational burden but their cleansing and covenant-restoration.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=15383, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — God-to-human operation (anger, doom, naming, redemptive promise) is evidenced across all three modes (T4.1.1–T4.1.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=15384, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — through the corporate confession and repentance movement. 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart... and repent and plead with you... saying, 'We have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly'" — the movement toward God is explicit: turning, repenting, pleading, and confessing are all movements directed toward God from within the generational-sin condition. Jer 8:14 — "why do we sit still? Gather together; let us go into the fortified cities and perish there" — paradoxically, this text shows a movement away from seeking God (going to fortified cities) in hopeless acceptance of doom; the movement toward God is by contrast the preferred response named in 1Ki 8:47.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=15385, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Heart-turning*: 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart" — the heart's volitional reorientation from the sinning-direction toward God is the primary required posture; (2) *Repentance*: 1Ki 8:47 — "repent" — the inner volitional act of changing direction within the inherited condition; (3) *Honest confession*: 1Ki 8:47 — "saying, 'We have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly'" — the corporate speech-act of naming the condition accurately ("perversely and wickedly" — not minimising) is the verbal dimension of the required posture.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=15386, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The movement-toward-God (1Ki 8:47 — turning, repenting, pleading) reveals that the God-community relationship, though severely damaged by the accumulated generational sinning (Jer 8:14 — doomed), remains accessible for the seeking community. God has not withdrawn so completely that turning is futile: the conditional "if they turn their heart... then hear from heaven" (the broader 1Ki 8 prayer context) implies God will respond to the turning. The relationship is damaged but not destroyed by the generational weight; the movement toward God can still reach him.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=15387, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Human-to-God operation (turning, repenting, confessing — 1Ki 8:47; hopeless movement to fortified cities — Jer 8:14) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=15388, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — through the foundational ancestral act and through mediatorial leadership failure. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — the first father extended the sinning-condition to all subsequent generations through his originating act; the mediators extended and deepened it through their institutional transgressions. The extension mechanism is representational: the first father's position as the founding ancestor makes his sinning the starting-point of the lineage's condition; the mediators' position as covenant-leaders makes their transgressions part of what the community inherits.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=15389, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Representative position*: Isa 43:27 — the first father's position as the ancestral head and the mediators' positions as covenant-leaders are what makes their sinning generationally transmissible; ordinary community members do not extend the condition in the same way; (2) *The sinning acts themselves*: the extension requires actual sinning ("sinned... transgressed") — the representative position plus the sinning acts together create the transmissible condition.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=15390, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Isa 43:27 — the first father must have occupied the foundational ancestral position before his sinning could become generationally transmissible; the mediators must have been appointed to their leadership roles before their transgressions could be transmitted to those under their care. The extension requires both the representative position (received through appointment or birth-order) and the sinning act. Without the representative position, individual sinning does not generate the same generational transmission.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=15391, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — The giving/extension direction is evidenced through ancestral origin and mediatorial transgression (Isa 43:27). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=15392, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — the present generation receives the accumulated condition from the ancestors. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the "your" addresses the present community as the receivers of the ancestral condition; the present generation is characterised by the first father's sin without having personally committed it. Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the prolonged condition encompasses more than the present generation's own acts; the "long time" implies receipt of a condition that predates the present generation's own sinning.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=15393, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Receipt is constituted by lineage and community membership rather than by inner conditions. Isa 43:27 — the "your" addresses those who stand in the lineage of the first father; membership in the lineage is the enabling condition for receiving the transmitted condition. What blocks the condition's full hold is: 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart" — the volitional turning within the inherited condition is what prevents the condition from remaining unopposed; and Eze 37:23 — God's cleansing — the divine act is the ultimate blocking condition that removes the inherited accumulation.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=15394, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Eze 37:23 names the freed state: "they shall not defile themselves anymore with their idols and their detestable things, or with any of their transgressions. But I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them; and they shall be my people, and I will be their God." The freed inner state is: (1) *Non-defilement* — no longer participating in idolatrous defilement; (2) *Divine cleanliness* — cleansed by God's act; (3) *Covenant-people identity restored* — "they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — the relational formula restored.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=15395, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced (Isa 43:27 — ancestral condition received; Isa 64:5 — long-duration condition; Eze 37:23 — freed state). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=15396, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. Within the covenant community: the condition operates through the covenant-relational bonds of lineage, national identity, and shared worship history — Isa 43:27 addresses Israel specifically ("your first father," "your mediators"); Psa 78:17/32 addresses the wilderness generation as the covenant community. The condition is transmitted through the specific relational bonds of the covenant people's shared history. Beyond the covenant: the corpus does not address how generational sin operates outside Israel's specific covenant lineage.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=15397, S) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The 8-verse corpus addresses generational sin exclusively within the covenant community of Israel (Isa 43:27 — "your first father"; Psa 78:17/32 — Israel in the wilderness; 1Ki 8:47 — the exiles; Jer 8:14 — Judah; Eze 37:23 — Israel). No verse in the corpus explicitly extends the generational mechanism beyond the covenant community. The question of whether generational sin operates across covenantal boundaries (e.g. in non-Israelite lineages) cannot be answered from this corpus.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=15398, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The relational scope is communal-national within the covenant framework: Isa 43:27 — the condition encompasses the entire lineage from the first father through the mediators to the present community; Psa 78:17/32 — the wilderness community as a whole; 1Ki 8:47 — all the exiles; Eze 37:23 — the restoration encompasses "them" as a communal whole. The scope is not individual (each person's personal accumulation) but communal — the whole community stands in the inherited condition together.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=15399, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Relational scope (communal-national covenant) is evidenced; the question of non-covenant generational sin is silent in this corpus (T4.5.2). Noted.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=15400, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The 8-verse corpus does not explicitly name spiritual beings (angelic or adversarial) in connection with generational sin. The condition operates within the human-God covenant relationship without named spiritual-beings mediation.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=15401, S) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The corpus is silent on adversarial spiritual activity in connection with generational sin. The idolatry named in Eze 37:23 ("their idols and their detestable things") implies an adversarial spiritual dimension (idolatry involves false spiritual powers) but this is not explicitly named in the corpus.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=15402, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-15]** S — The 8-verse corpus does not evidence angelic ministry in connection with generational sin. The characteristic operates through ancestral-lineage transmission and covenant-community belonging rather than through angelic agency.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=15403, S) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** S — The spiritual beings interface is entirely silent across the 8-verse corpus. The characteristic is constitutively a human-community and human-God covenantal phenomenon without named spiritual-beings involvement. Noted explicitly.

---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=15404, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Both — in the negative direction and through its addressed state. (1) *Condition change — negative*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the prolonged sinning condition transforms the community from covenant-faithfulness to settled sinning-conditionedness; Eze 37:23 — "defile themselves... with their transgressions" — the condition transforms the community's inner constitution from clean to defiled; (2) *Orientation change — negative*: Psa 78:17 — "rebelling against the Most High" — the orientation becomes one of active rebellion; (3) *Positive transformation through divine act*: Eze 37:23 — "I will save them... cleanse them... they shall be my people" — the divine intervention transforms condition (defiled → cleansed) and orientation (rebellious → covenant-people identity restored).

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=15405, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Reversible through divine intervention and human heart-turning. 1Ki 8:47 — "if they turn their heart... and repent and plead with you" — the conditional structure indicates reversibility: the turning is possible even within the long-duration condition; Eze 37:23 — "I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them" — God's sovereign cleansing reverses what the accumulated sinning produced. The condition is not irremovable but requires the specific combination of volitional turning (human side) and divine cleansing (divine side) to reverse.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=15406, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Transformation evidence is present (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=15407, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — a trajectory across the corpus. (1) *Active sinning phase*: Psa 78:17 — "sinned still more... rebelling" — persistent sinning despite divine provision; (2) *Prolonged condition*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the sinning has become the settled state; (3) *Consequence and doom*: Jer 8:14 — "doomed to perish... poisoned water" — the accumulated sinning produces catastrophic external consequences; (4) *Corporate recognition and confession*: 1Ki 8:47 — "turn their heart... repent and plead... 'We have sinned'" — the consequence drives the recognition; (5) *Divine restoration promise*: Eze 37:23 — cleansed, restored covenant-people identity.

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=15408, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The sequence (active sinning → prolonged condition → catastrophic consequence → corporate recognition → divine restoration) reveals that generational sin follows a characteristic generational arc: the condition deepens over time (Isa 64:5 — "long time"), the consequences accumulate until they become undeniable (Jer 8:14 — doom), and it is the undeniability of the consequences that drives the recognition and confession (1Ki 8:47). The sequence reveals that the generational pattern does not self-correct from within — it requires external pressure (consequence) and external rescue (divine cleansing — Eze 37:23) to be addressed.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=15409, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Sequence is evidenced across the 8-verse corpus (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=15410, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Two distinct mechanisms. (1) *Ancestral-representational transmission*: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the founding ancestor's sinning act creates the condition that is transmitted through lineage; the mechanism is representational (the first father's position as head makes his sinning the lineage's inheritance); (2) *Generational perpetuation through unbelief and rebellion*: Psa 78:17/32 — each generation actively perpetuates the inherited pattern through its own unbelief and rebellion; the mechanism is volitional reinforcement of the inherited direction. The two mechanisms work together: the inherited starting-point is reinforced by each generation's own choices.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=15411, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. (1) *Founding context* (Isa 43:27): the mechanism is the first father's representational sinning act — a single founding event that creates the generational condition; (2) *Ongoing-generation context* (Psa 78:17/32): the mechanism is volitional continuation — each generation's own unbelief and rebellion; (3) *Institutional-leadership context* (Isa 43:27 — "your mediators transgressed"): the mechanism is institutional reinforcement — appointed leaders deepen the condition through their own transgressions; (4) *Exile-consequence context* (1Ki 8:47; Jer 8:14): the mechanism shifts to consequence-driven recognition — the outer judgment becomes the mechanism that drives the inner turning.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=15412, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Mechanisms are evidenced across four contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=15413, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — suffering is the direct product of the accumulated generational sinning. Jer 8:14 — "the Lord our God has doomed us to perish and has given us poisoned water to drink, because we have sinned against the Lord" — the suffering (doom, poisoned water) is explicitly caused by the accumulated sinning. 1Ki 8:47 — "in the land to which they have been carried captive" — the exile is the suffering-context within which the generational condition is recognised and confessed. Isa 64:5 — "shall we be saved?" — the uncertainty about salvation is itself a form of suffering arising from the prolonged sinning-condition.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=15414, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Suffering *reveals* the generational condition and *drives* its recognition. Jer 8:14 — the doom and poisoned water reveal the accumulated sinning as its cause; 1Ki 8:47 — the exile creates the context in which the heart-turning and confession occur; the suffering does not produce the condition (the condition precedes the suffering as its cause) but reveals it unmistakably and drives the recognition that the accumulated sinning has produced the catastrophic consequence.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=15415, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — The relationship between suffering and generational sin is evidenced (Jer 8:14 — doom as consequence; 1Ki 8:47 — exile as revelatory context). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=15416, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — as the characteristic that shapes the negative formation arc of the community across generations. Psa 78:17/32 — the wilderness generation's persistent sinning and unbelief despite wonders is the formation arc in its negative direction: each reinforced act of rebellion forms the community further into the sinning-pattern. Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the "long time" names the duration of the negative formation arc; the community has been shaped by the accumulated sinning into a people characterised by it.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=15417, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Generational sin is the *cross-generational formation arc's negative baseline*: each generation is formed within an inherited sinning-pattern that shapes their default moral orientation before they make any personal choices. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the formation-arc began before the present generation's existence; they were born into a community already formed by the ancestral condition. Eze 37:23's resolution — "they shall not defile themselves anymore" — names the positive formation arc that divine cleansing makes possible: a community no longer formed by the inherited defilement-pattern.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=15418, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Formation evidence is present (T5.5.1 and T5.5.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=15419, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — two eschatological endpoints. (1) *Positive — divine cleansing and covenant restoration*: Eze 37:23 — "I will save them from all the backslidings in which they have sinned, and will cleanse them; and they shall be my people, and I will be their God" — the eschatological fullness is the complete cleansing of the accumulated generational backsliding and the restored covenant-people identity; (2) *Negative — eschatological curse*: Isa 65:20 — "the sinner a hundred years old shall be accursed" — within the new-creation context of Isa 65, the sinner who persists in the sinning-pattern (even across a hundred years) faces the eschatological verdict of curse rather than blessing.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=15420, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-15]** E — For the community under the generational condition: Isa 64:5 — "shall we be saved?" — the present salvific uncertainty anticipates the eschatological question of whether God's cleansing will be received; Jer 8:14's doom anticipates the eschatological consequence of unresolved generational sinning. For the community promised cleansing: Eze 37:23's "I will save them and cleanse them" anticipates the eschatological fullness in which the accumulated backslidings are permanently resolved and the covenant-people identity is definitively restored.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=15421, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced in both positive (Eze 37:23 — cleansing) and negative (Isa 65:20 — curse) directions. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=15422, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-15]** E — T2.8 identified two deposit forms: prolonged sin-conditionedness (Isa 64:5) and defilement (Eze 37:23). Developmental consequences. (1) *Prolonged-condition deposit*: Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the developmental consequence is deepening entrenchment; the "long time" is not a static condition but a progressively deepening one; each generation adds its own sinning to the accumulated weight, and the salvific uncertainty ("shall we be saved?") increases as the duration extends. (2) *Defilement deposit*: Eze 37:23 — the accumulated backslidings produce a defilement that requires divine cleansing; the developmental consequence of sustained defilement is an increasingly deep need for the divine cleansing that only God can provide (Eze 37:23 — "I will cleanse them").

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=15423, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes — by definition. The characteristic is constitutively generational: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned... and your mediators transgressed" — the deposit of the first father's sinning is carried forward through successive generations to the present community. The generational consequence is the most directly evidenced form of deposit-carrying-forward in M10: the condition is received rather than individually generated, which is the characteristic's defining feature. Isa 64:5 — "in our sins we have been a long time" — the "long time" spanning generations confirms the deposit's transmission across generations as the characteristic's primary mechanism.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=15424, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-15]** E — T2.8 found deposit evidence. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=15425, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur within or proximate to the 8-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: Psa 78:17/32 — the persistent, escalating, willful rebellion ("sinned still more," "rebelling") within the generational pattern is CHAR-1's territory; the individual wilful sinning acts are how each generation perpetuates CHAR-15's inherited condition; (2) *CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record)*: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — the named ancestral sins constitute a divine record; the accumulated generational sinning of Isa 64:5 ("a long time in our sins") is the record's temporal depth; (3) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: 1Ki 8:47 — "saying, 'We have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly'" — the corporate confession is the seeking-acknowledgment characteristic; (4) *CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent)*: Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the persistent non-repentance that sustains the generational pattern.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=15426, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-15 as the *temporal-depth dimension* of M10: it is the characteristic that gives other M10 sinning characteristics (CHAR-1 — wilful sinning; CHAR-5 — refusal to repent) their cross-generational weight. CHAR-15 does not add a different kind of sinning but adds the dimension of historical accumulated depth to what other characteristics describe. The record (CHAR-13) acquires generational depth through CHAR-15; the wilful sinning (CHAR-1) acquires inherited momentum through CHAR-15. CHAR-15 is thus the temporal-historical dimension of M10's inner-being landscape.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=15427, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=15428, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-15]** E — CHAR-15 consistently *precedes* and *accompanies* CHAR-1 (wilful sinning): the inherited generational condition is the backdrop within which each generation's wilful sinning occurs. Isa 43:27's first father's sinning precedes all subsequent generations' sinning; Psa 78:17/32's wilderness generation sins within the inherited pattern. CHAR-15 also consistently *precedes* CHAR-3 (confession): the accumulated generational condition is what the corporate confession of 1Ki 8:47 acknowledges and addresses.

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=15429, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The relationship with CHAR-1 is *developmental*: the inherited sinning-condition (CHAR-15) provides the dispositional starting-point within which each generation's wilful sinning (CHAR-1) occurs; CHAR-1's acts develop and deepen CHAR-15's condition. The relationship with CHAR-3 (confession) is *causal*: the accumulated generational condition (CHAR-15) is what the corporate confession (CHAR-3) names and addresses; CHAR-15 causally requires CHAR-3 for its acknowledgment. The relationship with CHAR-13 (divine record) is *constitutive*: CHAR-15 is the generational depth-dimension of CHAR-13's accumulated record.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=15430, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Sequential patterns are evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=15431, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. (1) *Prolonged sinning-conditionedness*: Isa 64:5 — the "long time in our sins" is a condition CHAR-15 produces through generational accumulation; (2) *Corporate doom and suffering*: Jer 8:14 — "doomed to perish" — the accumulated generational sinning produces the condition of corporate judgment; (3) *Deepened unbelief*: Psa 78:32 — "despite his wonders, they did not believe" — the generational pattern produces a deepened unbelief that resists even extraordinary divine evidence; (4) *Defilement*: Eze 37:23 — the backslidings produce the defilement that requires divine cleansing.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=15432, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning) at the originating moment*: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — the first instance of CHAR-15 was produced by a CHAR-1 wilful sinning act at the founding moment; the generational condition begins with an individual's wilful sinning; (2) *CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent) sustained the pattern*: Psa 78:32 — "they still sinned" — the persistent refusal to repent is what allows the generational pattern to continue across generations; CHAR-5's operation prevents the correction that would end CHAR-15's continuation.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=15433, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-15]** E — CHAR-15 is the *temporal-historical constituent* of CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record): the generational accumulation is what gives CHAR-13's record its cross-generational depth (Isa 64:5 — "a long time in our sins"; Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned"). Within CHAR-15, CHAR-1 (wilful sinning acts) constitutes the mechanism of generational perpetuation: each generation's CHAR-1 acts are the content that fills CHAR-15's inherited framework.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=15434, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=15435, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. Cha.ta (H2398 — verb "to sin") is the only term in the 8-verse corpus; it is the universal M10 vocabulary term shared across CHAR-1 through CHAR-15. CHAR-15 deploys cha.ta exclusively in the past-tense narrative of ancestral and generational sinning: "sinned still more" (Psa 78:17 — *yôsîpû lachəṭô*), "they still sinned" (Psa 78:32 — *bĕkol-zōʾt chāṭĕʾû*), "your first father sinned" (Isa 43:27 — *chāṭaʾ ʾābîkā hāriʾšôn*). The vocabulary is entirely shared; CHAR-15 is distinguished only by the generational-ancestral context in which cha.ta is deployed.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=15436, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. Cha.ta (H2398) is the primary root of the entire M10 Hebrew vocabulary family: it generates chat.tat, chet, cha.ta.ah, and the verb cha.ta across all M10 characteristics. CHAR-15 uses only the verb cha.ta, sharing the root-level architecture with every other M10 Hebrew characteristic. The root-level architecture is fully shared; CHAR-15 has no distinctive root contribution.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=15437, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The total vocabulary sharing (only cha.ta used; no specialised vocabulary for the inherited-condition dimension) reveals that CHAR-15 is not a distinct semantic domain but the *temporal-generational register* of the shared cha.ta sinning vocabulary. The generational sin-condition is evidenced not through distinctive terminology but through the *context* of ancestral attribution (Isa 43:27 — "your first father") and prolonged duration (Isa 64:5 — "a long time"). CHAR-15's distinctiveness is contextual-temporal, not lexical.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=15438, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Extensive vocabulary sharing (cha.ta — universal M10 root) is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=15439, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-15]** E — CHAR-11 (Sin as universal condition) most closely resembles CHAR-15 — both address sin as a condition rather than a discrete act, and both apply to all persons. The precise distinction: CHAR-11 is the *ontological structural condition* all persons share (Rom 3:23 — "all have sinned and fall short" — individually constituted); CHAR-15 is the *lineage-transmitted moral liability* that enters through ancestral and communal belonging. CHAR-11 is universal without requiring ancestry; CHAR-15 is specifically transmitted through lineage and community belonging. Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — names the lineage-mechanism that is absent from CHAR-11's structural condition.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=15440, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The overlap is at the universality of the sinning condition: both CHAR-11 (all have sinned) and CHAR-15 (the community has sinned a long time, inheriting the first father's condition) describe everyone under the condition. The precise boundary: CHAR-11 is the condition of being-under-sin as a structural ontological reality; CHAR-15 is the historical-genealogical mechanism through which specific communities carry specific accumulated sinning-histories. CHAR-11 would apply even to a person with no community history; CHAR-15 requires the lineage-chain.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=15441, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The distinction is one of *kind and mechanism*: CHAR-11 is the ontological condition (structural, universal, without requiring a specific lineage); CHAR-15 is the transmission mechanism (genealogical-communal, historically specific, requiring ancestral belonging). They are different kinds of sin-condition: CHAR-11 is constitutional-structural; CHAR-15 is historical-relational. They operate at the same constitutional level (the whole person's standing before God) but through different mechanisms.

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=15442, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=15443, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Yes. (1) Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned" — shared with CHAR-13 (sin as divine record — the ancestral sin as the origin of a generational record); (2) Psa 78:17/32 — "they sinned still more... they still sinned" — shared with CHAR-1 (wilful sinning — the persistent escalating acts) and CHAR-5 (refusal to repent — the "still sinned despite wonders"); (3) Eze 37:23 — "save them from all the backslidings... cleanse them... my people" — shared with CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought and received — the divine cleansing) and M11 (restoration).

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=15444, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Isa 43:27 shared with CHAR-13 confirms that the ancestral sinning that begins CHAR-15's generational chain is simultaneously the starting entry in CHAR-13's divine record: the first father's sinning (CHAR-15 origin) is the first record-entry (CHAR-13 content). Psa 78:32 shared with CHAR-5 confirms that the sustained "still sinned despite wonders" is both CHAR-15's generational perpetuation mechanism and CHAR-5's refusal-to-repent territory: the two characteristics are the inside and outside faces of the same sustained sinning pattern.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=15445, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=15446, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-15]** E — Three of CHAR-15's dimensions are shared. (1) *The wilful-sinning-acts dimension* (each generation's own sinning) is shared with CHAR-1; (2) *The divine-record dimension* (the accumulated sinning before God) is shared with CHAR-13; (3) *The refusal-to-repent dimension* (the persistent "still sinned") is shared with CHAR-5. CHAR-15's unique dimension — the *lineage-transmission mechanism* (first father's sinning creating the chain; Isa 43:27) — is not shared with any other M10 characteristic studied.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=15447, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-15]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-15 as the *temporal-depth layer* of several other M10 characteristics: it adds the generational-historical dimension to CHAR-1's individual sinning acts, CHAR-13's divine record, and CHAR-5's refusal to repent. CHAR-15's unique contribution (the lineage-transmission mechanism) is what distinguishes it from these shared dimensions: no other M10 characteristic addresses how sin moves through family and national belonging across generations as its defining feature.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=15448, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-15]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced across three adjacent characteristics (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---
