## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=15694, E) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — through the standing-before-God dimension. Ezr 9:15 — "we are before you in our guilt, for none can stand before you because of this" — the guilt creates a spirit-level barrier between the person and God; the inability to stand before God is a spirit-level relational disruption. Hos 5:5 — "Israel and Ephraim shall stumble in his guilt; Judah also shall stumble with them" — the stumbling produced by guilt operates at the spirit-level of the community's orientation toward God. The characteristic's definition identifies it as "the conscience's recognition of moral liability before God" — the "before God" is the spirit-level relational field in which guilt operates.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=15695, E) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Partly. The guilt originates in an act (VCG-01 — violation that becomes known) or an inherited condition (VCG-02 — generational accumulation), not at the spirit level specifically. However, the guilt's registration as a moral-liability-before-God is spirit-level: Ezr 9:15 — "we are before you in our guilt" — the guilt-before-God orientation is the spirit-level dimension. The guilt originates in the act (will-level); the guilt-awareness before God is spirit-level.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=15696, E) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Spirit-level location (Ezr 9:15 — standing-disability before God; Hos 5:15 — God withdrawing until guilt acknowledged) reveals that guilt-as-inner-state disrupts the deepest level of the person's God-relationship. The guilt operates as a spirit-level barrier: the guilty person cannot stand before God (Ezr 9:15), God withdraws from the guilty community until they acknowledge (Hos 5:15), and the indelible stain remains before God regardless of self-cleansing effort (Jer 2:22). Spirit-level disruption is thus the characteristic's most profound consequence.

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=15697, E) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Spirit-level location is evidenced (Ezr 9:15; Hos 5:15; Jer 2:22). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=15698, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — extensively. (1) *Inner conscience → outer confession/restitution*: Lev 5:5 — guilt-realisation (inner) → confession (speech-act) → restitution (outer conduct); Num 5:7 — "confess his sin... make full restitution" — the inner guilt-state produces outer corrective action; (2) *Inner weight → physical posture*: Ezr 9:6 — "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face" — the inner guilt-weight produces the outer physical posture of downcast face; (3) *Outer violation → inner guilt*: Lev 5:2/3/4 — the outer act (touching unclean thing, rash oath) produces the inner guilt-state when it "comes to knowledge"; (4) *Inner guilt → bodily consequence*: Eze 4:17 — "rot away because of their punishment" — the guilt-as-punitive-weight is expressed in bodily decay.

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=15699, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Two primary sequences. (1) *Violation-to-guilt-to-remedy sequence*: outer act (sin/violation) → hidden → comes to knowledge → inner guilt-recognition (Lev 5:2-5) → inner guilt-state → inner confession → outer restitution; (2) *Accumulation-to-consequence sequence*: repeated violations → mounting guilt (Ezr 9:6 — "mounted up to the heavens") → divine judgment (outer consequences: captivity, sword — Ezr 9:7) → inner recognition (shame — Ezr 9:6) → corporate acknowledgment. Both sequences confirm: guilt moves between the inner (conscience-recognition, shame, weight) and the outer (violation that causes it, confession and restitution that address it, bodily/national consequences it produces).

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=15700, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Constitutional movement is extensively evidenced (T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=15701, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes. Gen 4:13 — "my punishment is greater than I can bear" — Cain's experience of unbearable punitive guilt is a soul-level existential statement: the whole person (soul-level) is overwhelmed; 1Sa 25:31 (pu.qah) — "pangs of conscience" — the conscience-stagger is a soul-level disruption of the person's inner equilibrium. Psa 65:3 — "when iniquities prevail against me" — the iniquities pressing against the person is a soul-level experience of guilt's weight.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=15702, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Soul-level location (Gen 4:13 — unbearable; 1Sa 25:31 — conscience-stagger; Psa 65:3 — iniquities prevailing) reveals that guilt-as-inner-state engages the whole person as soul-level moral subject. The guilt is not merely cognitive recognition or legal status but a felt condition of the whole inner person — Cain's "I cannot bear it" names the soul's total engagement with the punitive weight of guilt. The soul is the locus where the objective guilt-record (CHAR-13) is experienced subjectively as unbearable weight (CHAR-17).

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=15703, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (Gen 4:13; 1Sa 25:31; Psa 65:3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=15704, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes. Hos 10:2 — "their heart is false; now they must bear their guilt" — the false heart is explicitly named as the inner source that generates the guilt-burden; the heart's condition is what produces the guilt the person must bear. The heart-guilt connection is direct: false heart → guilt borne. Additionally: Gen 42:21 — "we saw the distress of his soul" — the brothers' conscience recognises that they ignored another's soul-distress; the conscience's retrospective assessment operates at the heart-level of moral awareness.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=15705, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Heart-location (Hos 10:2 — "false heart... must bear guilt") reveals that the guilt-state is rooted in the heart's dispositional condition: it is not merely the result of isolated acts but flows from the heart's sustained false-orientation. The heart generates the acts that create guilt entries, and the guilt-bearing is thus the heart's consequence. This connects CHAR-17 to CHAR-16 (sinner as moral character): the false heart that generates guilt is the heart of the sinner-type.

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=15706, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Heart-location is evidenced (Hos 10:2; Gen 42:21). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=15707, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The mind is engaged through the knowledge-dimension of guilt-recognition. Lev 5:2/3/4 — "when he comes to know it" — the cognitive event of coming-to-knowledge is the trigger for guilt-recognition; the mind's acquiring of information about the violation is what activates the guilt-state. Gen 42:21 — the brothers' retrospective cognitive connection of their past cruelty to their present suffering is a mind-level interpretive act that activates conscience-recognition of guilt.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=15708, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Mind-location (Lev 5:2/3/4 — "comes to know"; Gen 42:21 — cognitive retrospection) reveals that the guilt-state requires a cognitive trigger: the person must come to know the violation before guilt-recognition occurs. Guilt is not merely the feeling of having done wrong but requires the mind's engagement with the knowledge of what was done. This is why Lev 5:17/19 is significant: "though he did not know it, then realizes his guilt" — even guilt from unknown violations becomes subjectively active once the mind acquires knowledge.

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=15709, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Mind-location (knowledge as guilt-trigger — Lev 5:2/3/4; cognitive retrospection — Gen 42:21) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=15710, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — *Conscience* as the primary soul-level faculty of CHAR-17 is the defining element of the characteristic. 1Sa 25:31 (pu.qah) — "pangs of conscience" — the term directly names the conscience-faculty's experience of guilt-stagger; Lev 4:13/22/27; 5:2/3/4/5 — "realizes his guilt" (a.sham) — the repeated guilt-realisation pattern names the conscience as the recognition-faculty; Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt" — the acknowledgment is the conscience's output. The conscience is the primary inner-being faculty constituting the characteristic (per the definition: "conscience in its guilt-awareness mode").

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=15711, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Conscience-level location (1Sa 25:31 — pu.qah; Lev 4/5 sequences; Hos 5:15) reveals that CHAR-17 is constitutively a conscience-characteristic: the inner experience of guilt-as-inner-state IS the conscience operating in its guilt-awareness mode. The conscience is not merely a secondary faculty that registers guilt after it exists elsewhere; guilt-as-inner-state is the conscience's specific form of operation when moral liability is recognised. The conscience's stagger (pu.qah — 1Sa 25:31) is the single-term expression of what the guilt-state does to the conscience when it fully engages.

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=15712, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Conscience-level location is primary and defining (1Sa 25:31; Lev 4/5 sequences; Hos 5:15). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=15713, E) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — the face. Ezr 9:6 — "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you, my God" — the face is the body-part through which the guilt-state is expressed; the inability to lift the face is the guilt's bodily expression. Additionally: Aaron's forehead (Exo 28:38) — "on Aaron's forehead... Aaron shall bear any guilt" — the forehead is the body-part on which the high priest literally bore Israel's guilt; the guilt is physically located on his forehead before God. Isaiah's lips (Isa 6:7) — "this has touched your lips; your guilt is taken away" — the lips are the body-part through which Isaiah's guilt was located and from which it was removed.

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=15714, E) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Three distinct body-part functions. (1) *Face-avoidance* (Ezr 9:6): the downcast face before God names the body's expression of the guilt-state's shame dimension; the face cannot be lifted because the guilt precludes the standing-before-God that face-lifting implies; (2) *Forehead-bearing* (Exo 28:38): the forehead as the visible body-part that bore guilt before God establishes the priestly guilt-bearing as a physical and visible act — the guilt literally rests on the person's face/head in the representative's bearing; (3) *Lips-location* (Isa 6:7): the lips as the body-part of Isaiah's guilt names the speech-faculty as the site of his specific form of guilt (unclean lips before the holy God — Isa 6:5).

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=15715, E) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Body-part links (face — Ezr 9:6; forehead — Exo 28:38; lips — Isa 6:7) are evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=15716, E) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Bidirectional. (1) *Inner guilt → outer bodily expression*: Ezr 9:6 — the inner shame of guilt produces the outer bodily posture of downcast face; the inner condition is expressed through the body; (2) *Bodily location → guilt-borne*: Exo 28:38 — the guilt of Israel's holy offerings is borne specifically on Aaron's forehead; the guilt is located at the body-part as its point of representation; (3) *Body-part as guilt-site → divine removal*: Isa 6:7 — the guilt located at Isaiah's lips is removed by the coal touching that specific body-part; the removal addresses the guilt at its specific body-location.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=15717, E) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The bidirectional body-guilt link reveals that guilt-as-inner-state has a bodily-expressive and bodily-located dimension: it is not purely interior. Ezr 9:6's face-avoidance makes the guilt visible in the body's posture; Exo 28:38's forehead-bearing makes the guilt-bearing a physical act; Isa 6:7's lip-touching makes the removal a physical event. The body is thus the medium through which guilt is expressed, borne, and resolved — none of these are purely inner-spiritual events.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=15718, E) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Body-characteristic link (face, forehead, lips) is evidenced and bidirectional. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=15719, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Accumulated weight*: Ezr 9:6 — "our guilt has mounted up to the heavens" — the accumulated guilt is a measurable constitutional deposit that has risen above the head; (2) *Indelible stain*: Jer 2:22 — "the stain of your guilt is still before me" — the guilt-stain is an indelible deposit on the inner person that no self-generated cleansing removes; (3) *Punitive legacy*: Jer 32:18 — "you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them" — the guilt-deposit is transmitted intergenerationally; the deposit from the fathers' guilt becomes the children's inherited moral-consequential burden.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=15720, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Supporting: Ezr 9:6 (accumulation deposit — risen above head); Jer 2:22 (indelible stain deposit); Jer 32:18 (intergenerational deposit). Contradicting or complicating: Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — the indelible deposit is removable through divine action; Jer 33:8 — "I will cleanse them from all the guilt" — divine cleansing addresses even the accumulated deposit; the deposit is real but not permanently irremovable. The deposit is constitutionally present and deepens without resolution, but yields to divine cleansing.

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=15721, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Deposit evidence is present: accumulated weight (Ezr 9:6), indelible stain (Jer 2:22), intergenerational transmission (Jer 32:18). T5.7 will carry: the deposits are reversible through divine cleansing (Isa 6:7; Jer 33:8) but real and accumulating without it; the intergenerational deposit is the most severe form, reaching beyond the individual's lifetime.

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=15722, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Two constitutional origins. (1) *From an act that violates divine or moral standard*: Lev 5:2/3/4 — the specific acts (touching unclean thing, rash oath) that generate guilt when they become known; Lev 5:19 — "he has indeed incurred guilt before the Lord" — the act of commission creates the guilt; (2) *From accumulated inherited community condition*: Ezr 9:7 — "from the days of our fathers to this day we have been in great guilt" — the inherited communal accumulation is an origin prior to the individual's own acts. The constitutional origin thus operates at both the individual-act level and the inherited-community level.

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=15723, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Multiple — and the multiplicity across five VCGs is the corpus's richest inter-origin complexity. Individual unintentional acts (VCG-01 — Lev 4/5 sequences), communal accumulation across generations (VCG-02 — Ezr 9:7), guilt loaded onto a representative bearer (VCG-03 — Exo 28:38), punitive inheritance from ancestors (VCG-04 — Gen 19:15), and indelibility before divine memory (VCG-05 — Jer 2:22; Eze 21:23/24) are all distinct guilt-origins operating simultaneously in the 97-verse corpus.

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=15724, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes across all five VCG contexts. (1) *Individual-unintentional* (VCG-01): origin in specific acts that become known (Lev 5:2/3/4); (2) *Communal-accumulated* (VCG-02): origin in generational moral failure compounding over time (Ezr 9:7); (3) *Transferred-representative* (VCG-03): origin in another's guilt loaded onto the bearer (Exo 28:38; 1Sa 25:24 — Abigail volunteering); (4) *Punitive-inherited* (VCG-04): origin in a community's accumulated iniquity now entering its punitive phase (Eze 4:4/5/6); (5) *Divine-memory* (VCG-05): origin in guilt that God remembers and exposes (Eze 21:23/24 — "brings their guilt to remembrance").

---

---

## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=15725, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The perceptive faculty is engaged at the critical guilt-triggering moment. Lev 5:2/3/4 — "when he comes to know it" — the coming-to-know is a perceptive event: the person perceives or discovers the violation they had committed; perception of the violation triggers the guilt-recognition sequence. Gen 42:21 — "we saw the distress of his soul, when he begged us and we did not listen" — the brothers' guilt-recognition includes the retrospective perception of their brother's distress; they now perceive what they refused to perceive in the moment.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=15726, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The guilt-state *activates and deepens* retrospective moral perception. Gen 42:21 — the brothers, under current distress, now perceive their past cruelty with moral clarity that they lacked in the moment; the guilt-state deepens perception of past events. Eze 9:9 — "they say, 'The Lord has forsaken the land, and the Lord does not see'" — conversely, extreme guilt produces a distorted perception: the guilt-burdened community misperceives God as absent; the accumulated guilt impairs accurate perception of God's continued presence and attention.

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=15727, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The perceptive pattern reveals that the guilt-state operates in both directions on perception: it can sharpen retrospective moral perception (Gen 42:21 — seeing past cruelty clearly under guilt's pressure) and distort present-moment God-perception (Eze 9:9 — misreading God as absent). The productive use of the perceptive faculty under guilt is retrospective (perceiving what was done); the distortion is prospective (misperceiving God's response to the guilt).

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=15752, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Conscientiousness is engaged in the full guilt-to-remedy pipeline. Lev 6:4/5 — "realized his guilt and will restore... he shall restore it in full and shall add a fifth" — the combination of guilt-recognition (conscience), remedial initiative (will), and full-plus-fifth restitution (action with moral precision) constitutes a fully conscientious response to guilt. Num 5:7 — "he shall confess his sin that he has committed. And he shall make full restitution... adding a fifth to it and giving it to him to whom he did the wrong" — the conscientious precision (full restitution plus added fifth) names conscientiousness directed by guilt toward moral repair.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=15753, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The guilt-state *activates and directs* conscientiousness toward the wronged party and toward God. Lev 5:5 — "realizes his guilt... and confesses the sin he has committed" — the guilt-recognition activates the conscientious confession. Lev 6:4 — the restitution with added fifth is a conscientiously precise act; the guilt-state has directed the person's moral precision toward repair rather than self-interest. The guilt-state thus converts conscientiousness from its prior self-serving direction (the act of violation that created the guilt) toward the other-repairing direction.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=15754, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The conscientiousness pattern reveals that guilt-as-inner-state, when operating through engaged conscience, produces the highest form of conscientious moral action in M10: the Lev 4/5/6 and Num 5:7 restitution sequences are examples of conscientious precision (full restitution + added fifth) directed entirely toward the wronged party's restoration. This is the characteristic's functional value: guilt-recognition redirects conscientiousness from the self-serving direction that created the guilt toward the other-repairing direction that resolves it.

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=15755, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The relational capacity is constitutively engaged: guilt is always guilt-toward-someone. (1) *Guilt toward God*: Ezr 9:15 — "we are before you in our guilt" — the guilt-state exists in the person's relational standing before God; (2) *Guilt toward another person*: Gen 42:21 — "we are guilty concerning our brother" — guilt in the human horizontal relationship; 2Sa 19:19 — "not hold me guilty... how your servant did wrong on the day my lord the king left Jerusalem" — guilt as moral debt owed to the king; (3) *Guilt-transfer as relational act*: Exo 28:38 — Aaron bears Israel's guilt before God — a relational mediation act.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=15756, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Unaddressed guilt *severs* relational capacity; acknowledged guilt *opens* relational restoration. Ezr 9:15 — "none can stand before you because of this" — the guilt-state severs the standing-before-God; Hos 5:15 — God withdraws "until they acknowledge their guilt" — guilt-state severs the God-relationship; only acknowledgment reopens it. Conversely: Num 5:7 — "giving it to him to whom he did the wrong" — guilt acknowledged and restituted restores the damaged human relationship; the guilt-state that drove the restitution enables relational repair.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=15757, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The relational pattern reveals that guilt-as-inner-state is the inner experience of relational rupture: every guilt-entry in the corpus names a broken relationship (with God, with another person, with the covenant community). The guilt-state is the subjective inner experience of what the relational rupture feels like from the inside. Ezr 9:15 — "none can stand before you because of this" — the guilt is experienced as a relational barrier. The resolution pathway (confession, restitution — Lev 5:5/6:4) restores both the inner state and the relational standing simultaneously.

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=15728, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — through the knowledge-triggers and the retrospective-causal reasoning. Lev 5:17 — "though he did not know it, then realizes his guilt, he shall bear his iniquity" — the cognitive knowledge of the violation is what activates the guilt-state; knowledge is the guilt-trigger. Gen 42:21 — "that is why this distress has come upon us" — the brothers' cognitive causal reasoning (past guilt → present suffering) is a mind-level attribution of moral causation.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=15729, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The guilt-state *clarifies retrospective moral cognition* and can *distort prospective cognition*. Gen 42:21 — the brothers' current guilt-distress clarifies their cognitive understanding of their past act's moral significance. Conversely: Eze 21:23 — "to them it will seem like a false divination" — the guilt-bearing community misinterprets divine judgment as false prophecy; the accumulated guilt distorts the cognitive capacity to correctly interpret what God is doing. Guilt thus has a bi-directional cognitive effect: retrospective clarity, prospective distortion.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=15730, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals that guilt-as-inner-state is not exclusively a feeling but has a specific cognitive structure: it requires knowledge to activate (Lev 5:2/3/4 — "when he comes to know"), generates retrospective causal reasoning (Gen 42:21 — connecting past guilt to present suffering), and can distort prospective interpretation (Eze 9:9; Eze 21:23). The cognitive faculty is the gateway to guilt-recognition and is itself shaped (both clarified and distorted) by the guilt it mediates.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=15731, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — centrally. Eze 21:23/24 — "he brings their guilt to remembrance... because you have made your guilt to be remembered" — God's recall of guilt from memory is the mechanism of judgment; the guilt-record exists in God's memory; Eze 21:24 — "your transgressions are uncovered, so that in all your deeds your sins appear" — the guilt becomes visible when God's memory re-activates it. Gen 42:21 — the brothers' own memory of their past cruelty activates their guilt-recognition; their guilt is memory-mediated.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=15732, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Guilt *deepens morally significant memory*. Gen 42:21 — under the pressure of current distress, the brothers' memory of their past cruelty becomes vivid and morally interpretive; the guilt-state makes the past memorable in its moral dimensions. Conversely, the attempt to escape guilt through self-cleansing (Jer 2:22 — "wash yourself with lye") is an attempt to bypass the moral memory that guilt maintains; but the guilt "is still before me" — God's memory of the guilt persists regardless of the person's self-cleansing effort.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=15733, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The memory pattern reveals that guilt-as-inner-state is constitutively memory-dependent on two sides: (1) the guilt requires the person's memory of the violating act (or its coming-to-knowledge) to activate; (2) God's memory of the guilt constitutes the divine-record dimension that the guilt-state reflects (Eze 21:23 — God bringing guilt "to remembrance"). CHAR-17's subjective experience of guilt is the human-side experience of what CHAR-13's divine record holds on the God-side. The memory connection between the two characteristics is structural.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=15734, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — as one of its primary inner-being dimensions. Ezr 9:6 — "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you, my God" — shame and blush are the affective responses to guilt-recognition; Ezr 9:6 — this is the corpus's most explicit affect description; Gen 4:13 — "my punishment is greater than I can bear" — despair/overwhelm is the affective experience of guilt-as-punitive-weight; Hos 5:5 — "shall stumble in his guilt" — the stumbling includes an affective dimension of moral unsteadiness. 1Sa 25:31 — pu.qah — "pangs of conscience" — the term directly names the affective-experiential dimension of conscience-stagger under guilt.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=15735, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The guilt-state *activates and deepens negative affect*. Ezr 9:6 — shame activated by guilt-recognition; Gen 4:13 — despair deepened by the punitive-weight dimension. The accumulated guilt progressively deepens the affective burden: 2Ch 33:23 — "Amon incurred guilt more and more" — the progressive guilt-accumulation progressively deepens the affective weight without address. Conversely: Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — the removal of guilt is followed by the implicit affective liberation; the removal reverses the affective burden.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=15736, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The affective pattern reveals that shame (Ezr 9:6) is the primary affective expression of guilt-recognition (VCG-01/02) while despair/overwhelm (Gen 4:13) is the primary affective expression of guilt-as-punitive-weight (VCG-04). The affective spectrum runs from productive shame (Ezr 9:6 — that leads to acknowledgment before God) through paralysing despair (Gen 4:13 — "greater than I can bear"). The productive affect (shame leading to acknowledgment) enables the guilt-resolution process; the paralyzing affect (despair) precludes it.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=15737, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** S — The 97-verse corpus does not explicitly engage the creative faculty in connection with guilt as inner-being state. The characteristic operates through conscience, memory, affect, will, and relational-restitution rather than through creative origination.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=15738, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** S — No direct evidence. The corpus is silent on the creative faculty's engagement with guilt as inner-being state.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=15739, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** S — The silence is consistent with the characteristic's focus on the retrospective (backward-looking) conscience-recognition of past violation rather than the forward-looking creative faculty. Guilt-as-inner-state looks backward at what was done; creativity looks forward toward what might be made. Noted.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=15740, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — at both the guilt-generating and guilt-addressing levels. (1) *Will generating guilt*: Jer 3:13 — "acknowledge your guilt, that you rebelled against the Lord" — the guilt arose from a wilful act of rebellion; 2Ch 33:23 — "Amon incurred guilt more and more" — the progressive accumulation is a sustained volitional pattern; Hos 10:2 — "their heart is false" — the false-heart-driven choices generate the guilt; (2) *Will addressing guilt*: Lev 5:5 — guilt-realization → "confesses... and will restore" — the will's decision to confess and restore; Lev 6:4 — "realized his guilt and will restore" — the will's initiative in restitution.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=15741, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The guilt-state *redirects volition* toward remedial action when conscience operates correctly. Lev 6:4 — "realized his guilt and will restore" — the guilt-state produces a volitional impulse toward restitution; Num 5:7 — "he shall confess... and shall make full restitution" — the guilt-recognition is the volitional turning-point. However, the unaddressed guilt-state can *paralyse volition*: Gen 4:13 — "my punishment is greater than I can bear" — Cain's experience implies a volitional paralysis under overwhelming punitive guilt; 2Ch 33:23 — "incurred guilt more and more" — the will persisting in guilt-generating choices despite accumulation shows volition captured by the wrong direction.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=15742, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The volitional pattern reveals that guilt-as-inner-state is a volitional hinge-point: when the conscience's guilt-recognition engages correctly, it redirects the will toward restitution and confession (Lev 6:4; Num 5:7); when it is suppressed or overwhelmed, the will either persists in guilt-accumulation (2Ch 33:23) or is paralysed by the punitive weight (Gen 4:13). The guilt-state itself does not determine the volitional response — the will's direction (toward remedy or away from it) is what determines whether guilt becomes productive or destructive.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=15743, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Agency is engaged through the restitution-demands that guilt recognition generates. Lev 6:4 — "realized his guilt and will restore what he took" — the guilt-recognition produces agentive restitution; Num 5:7 — "he shall confess... shall make full restitution... giving it to him to whom he did the wrong" — the guilt-state drives a series of agentive acts (confession, restitution, giving). 1Sa 25:24 — Abigail's "on me alone, my lord, be the guilt" — a proactive agentive assumption of another's guilt-liability. Jer 3:13 — "only acknowledge your guilt" — the acknowledgment is an agentive act of moral self-declaration.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=15744, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — When conscience is engaged, guilt-state *activates and directs* agency toward remedial acts. The Lev 4/5/6 sequences show guilt-recognition consistently producing agentive responses: bringing offerings (Lev 5:6 — "shall bring to the Lord his compensation"), confessing (Lev 5:5; Num 5:7), making restitution (Lev 6:4; Num 5:7). The guilt-state does not eliminate agency but provides the moral impetus that redirects it from self-serving action toward other-repairing action.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=15745, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The agency pattern reveals the characteristic's most productive inner dynamic: guilt-recognition produces a specific agentive sequence — bringing an offering → confession → restitution — that is the conscience-to-action pipeline. The guilt-state is the inner impetus that keeps the pipeline flowing; without guilt-recognition, the agentive repairs would not occur. This is the characteristic's designed function (T0.2.1): equipping the person to recognise moral failure and initiate repair.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=15746, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — as the evaluative act that precedes guilt-recognition. Gen 42:21 — "in truth we are guilty" — the brothers' moral evaluation correctly assesses their past conduct as guilty; Gen 44:16 — "God has found out the guilt of your servants" — Judah's moral evaluation acknowledges divine discovery of guilt. Amo 8:14 — those "who swear by the Guilt of Samaria" — the community's moral evaluation has inverted: they treat the guilt-object (an idol) as worthy of oath-swearing.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=15747, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The guilt-state *clarifies moral evaluation* when conscience operates correctly and *distorts* it when suppressed. Gen 42:21 — the brothers' guilt-state sharpens their moral evaluation of past conduct; they now correctly assess what they did as guilty. Eze 9:9 — "they say, 'The Lord does not see'" — the community's suppressed guilt produces an inverted moral evaluation: they misassess God's awareness and therefore their accountability. The guilt-state's clarifying effect on moral evaluation is its productive mode; its distorting effect (when guilt is suppressed) is its destructive mode.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=15748, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that guilt-as-inner-state is both the product and the activator of moral evaluation: moral evaluation identifies the guilt (Gen 42:21 — "we are guilty") and the guilt-state in turn deepens the moral evaluation of both the past act and the present condition (Ezr 9:13 — "God exacted less than our guilt deserves" — a morally calibrated assessment of deserved versus received punishment). The moral evaluation faculty and the guilt-state are mutually reinforcing when operating correctly.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=15749, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The conscience is the defining faculty of CHAR-17. The definition identifies the characteristic as "the conscience's recognition of moral liability before God." Every instance of guilt-realisation in the corpus (Lev 4:13/22/27; 5:2/3/4/5; Num 5:6; Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt") is a conscience event. 1Sa 25:31 — pu.qah — "pangs of conscience" — the single Hebrew term that directly names the conscience's affective-somatic experience of guilt. Conscience IS the characteristic's primary inner faculty; guilt-as-inner-state is conscience in its guilt-awareness mode.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=15750, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The guilt-state *is* conscience operating; its presence confirms conscience is functioning. The absence of guilt-recognition signals conscience suppression. Lev 5:2-5's sequence (hidden → known → realized guilt) shows conscience operating correctly: it engages when knowledge arrives. Hos 5:15 — God withdrawing "until they acknowledge their guilt" — the withdrawal is designed to activate conscience; the guilt-acknowledgment confirms conscience has re-engaged. Hos 10:2 — "their heart is false; now they must bear their guilt" — the false heart suppresses conscience, so the guilt must be borne without the productive inner recognition that would initiate repair.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=15751, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The conscience pattern reveals that CHAR-17 is the conscience-characteristic of M10: every other M10 characteristic can be present without conscience engagement (sinning acts can occur; the divine record can accumulate), but CHAR-17 IS conscience engaging with the moral reality that the other characteristics name. The guilt-recognition sequences of Lev 4/5 define the conscience's productive mode: violation → hidden → revealed → realized-by-conscience → confession → remedy. The conscience is both the characteristic's faculty and its instrument.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=15758, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — God operates toward the guilt-bearing person in five modes. (1) *Strategic withdrawal to provoke recognition*: Hos 5:15 — "I will return again to my place, until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face" — God's withdrawal is purposeful; the relational absence is designed to provoke guilt-acknowledgment; (2) *Exposure of hidden guilt*: Jer 2:22 — "the stain of your guilt is still before me" — God's gaze penetrates concealment; Gen 44:16 — "God has found out the guilt of your servants"; (3) *Bringing guilt to remembrance in judgment*: Eze 21:23/24 — "he brings their guilt to remembrance... that they may be taken" — God reactivates the guilt-record at the moment of judgment; (4) *Removing guilt by sovereign act*: Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — God's agent removes the guilt; Jer 33:8 — "I will cleanse them from all the guilt"; (5) *Exacting less than guilt deserves*: Job 11:6 — "God exacts of you less than your guilt deserves" — divine mercy moderates the punitive dimension.

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=15759, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Two bases. (1) *Holiness that registers guilt infallibly*: Jer 2:22 — the guilt remains "before me" regardless of self-cleansing; Psa 69:5 — "the wrongs I have done are not hidden from you" — God's perfect moral knowledge is the basis for registering guilt that others cannot see; (2) *Covenant purpose that moves toward cleansing*: Jer 33:8 — "I will cleanse them from all the guilt of their sin against me, and I will forgive all the guilt" — the double-cleansing promise is grounded in God's covenantal purpose; Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — the removal is by God's own initiative and agent.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=15760, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — God's disposition is one of *precise moral justice moving toward purposeful mercy*. Job 11:6 — "God exacts of you less than your guilt deserves" — mercy moderates justice. Hos 5:15 — the withdrawal until guilt is acknowledged reveals a patient relational strategy rather than immediate destruction; God waits for the acknowledgment. Jer 33:8 — "I will cleanse them from all the guilt" — the totality of the cleansing-promise ("all the guilt") reveals an intention to fully resolve rather than partially address. The disposition is neither indifferent (God sees and responds — Jer 2:22) nor punitive-only (mercy moderates — Job 11:6; cleansing promised — Jer 33:8).

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=15761, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — God-to-human operation (withdrawal, exposure, remembrance-for-judgment, removal, mercy) is extensively evidenced (T4.1.1–T4.1.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=15762, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — through the acknowledgment-and-seeking movement. Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face, and in their distress earnestly seek me" — the guilt-acknowledgment is paired with the face-seeking of God; acknowledging guilt is simultaneously a movement toward God; Ezr 9:6 — "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you, my God" — even the shame-posture of guilt is directed toward God; the face may not be lifted, but the address is toward God. Gen 44:16 — "God has found out the guilt of your servants; behold, we are my lord's servants" — the confession before Joseph is simultaneously a posture of submission that mirrors the guilt-before-God movement.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=15763, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Acknowledgment*: Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt" — the first movement is honest inner acknowledgment; Jer 3:13 — "only acknowledge your guilt" — acknowledgment is the sine qua non; (2) *Confession*: Lev 5:5 — "confesses the sin he has committed" — verbal naming of what conscience has registered; Num 5:7 — "he shall confess his sin" — the speech-act before God; (3) *Restitution*: Lev 6:4 — "will restore what he took" — the movement toward God under guilt is not merely verbal but restitutive; the wronged person must be made whole.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=15764, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The human-to-God direction reveals that the God-relationship remains accessible to the guilt-bearing person precisely through the guilt-acknowledgment pathway. Hos 5:15 — God waits for acknowledgment as the condition of his return; the relationship is therefore not terminated by guilt but suspended pending acknowledgment. Ezr 9:6/15 — Ezra addresses God directly while bearing guilt ("I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you, my God") — the guilt-bearing person can still address God from within the guilt-state; the relationship remains open even while the guilt-barrier persists.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=15765, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Human-to-God operation (acknowledgment, confession, restitution — Hos 5:15; Lev 5:5; Num 5:7; Jer 3:13) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=15766, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — through the leadership-propagation and priestly-transference mechanisms. (1) *Leadership propagation*: Lev 4:3 — "if it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people" — the priest's sin extends guilt to the whole community; 1Ch 21:3 — "why should it be a cause of guilt for Israel?" — a leader's decision would propagate guilt to the nation; (2) *Voluntary self-assumption*: 1Sa 25:24 — "on me alone, my lord, be the guilt" — Abigail assumes Nabal's guilt for David; 2Sa 14:9 — "on me be the guilt, my lord the king" — the woman of Tekoa volunteers to bear the guilt from the king; (3) *Priestly representative bearing*: Exo 28:38 — Aaron bears Israel's cultic guilt on his forehead; Exo 28:43 — priests bear guilt if they enter improperly.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=15767, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Representative position*: Lev 4:3 — the anointed priest's position as representative of the community is what makes his sin guilt-propagating to all; 1Ch 21:3 — the king's position makes his census decision guilt-generating for Israel; (2) *Voluntary assumption*: 1Sa 25:24 — Abigail's "on me alone, my lord, be the guilt" — the voluntary self-insertion into the guilt-bearing position; 2Sa 14:9 — the woman's "on me be the guilt" — the willing assumption of another's liability. The conditions are distinct: representative-position guilt-propagation is structural; voluntary-assumption is agentive.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=15768, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — For representative-propagation: the person must hold the representative position (Lev 4:3 — the anointed priest; 1Ch 21:3 — the king) before their sin propagates to others. For priestly-bearing: Exo 28:38 — Aaron must be vested in the high-priestly garments with the plate "on Aaron's forehead" — the office and its vestments are prerequisites for the guilt-bearing function. For voluntary-assumption: 1Sa 25:24 — Abigail must have relational access and standing before David to make the guilt-transfer offer effective.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=15769, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — The giving direction is extensively evidenced through leadership propagation (Lev 4:3; 1Ch 21:3), voluntary self-assumption (1Sa 25:24; 2Sa 14:9), and priestly representative bearing (Exo 28:38). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=15770, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — through three reception modes. (1) *Individual incurrence*: Lev 5:19 — "he has indeed incurred guilt before the Lord" — the individual receives guilt through their own act; Lev 22:16 — "cause them to bear iniquity and guilt, by eating their holy things" — guilt is incurred through improper action; (2) *Communal inheritance*: Ezr 9:7 — "from the days of our fathers to this day we have been in great guilt" — the community receives inherited guilt across generations; Jer 32:18 — "you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them" — intergenerational guilt-reception; (3) *Voluntary assumption*: Exo 28:38 — Aaron receives Israel's guilt for bearing; 1Sa 25:24 — Abigail proposes to receive David/Nabal's guilt.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=15771, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Reception-enabling: (1) *Acknowledgment*: Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt" — acknowledgment is the condition of God's return and remedy-reception; (2) *Seeking*: Hos 5:15 — "seek my face... earnestly seek me" — the active movement toward God in distress enables remedy-reception; (3) *Confession + restitution*: Lev 5:5; Num 5:7 — the full guilt-to-remedy pathway. Reception-blocking: (1) *Self-concealment*: Jer 2:22 — attempting to wash away guilt blocks remedy because it substitutes self-effort for divine action; (2) *Pride*: Eze 16:49 — "pride... excess of food" — Sodom's pride blocked guilt-recognition and therefore remedy-reception; (3) *False-heart*: Hos 10:2 — "their heart is false; now they must bear their guilt" — the false heart cannot rightly receive the remedy.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=15772, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away, and your sin atoned for" — the immediate state is fitness for divine commission: Isaiah is sent immediately after the guilt-removal (Isa 6:8/9 — "here I am! Send me"); the guilt-removed state enables full engagement with divine purpose. Jer 33:8 — "I will cleanse them from all the guilt... and I will forgive" — the cleansed state is simultaneously a forgiven state; the inner-being condition is both guilt-free and forgiven. Psa 34:22 — "none of those who take refuge in him will be condemned" — the not-guilty state is characterised as taking refuge in God; the guilt-removed person is in the position of one sheltered rather than exposed.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=15773, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced across individual incurrence (Lev 5:19), communal inheritance (Ezr 9:7), and voluntary assumption (Exo 28:38; 1Sa 25:24). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=15774, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes. Within covenant relational bonds: Ezr 9:7 — the guilt operates as a generational burden within the sustained covenant community; the guilt has accumulated through the ongoing relational context of Israel-God covenant; Lev 22:16 — guilt incurred within the priestly service relationship. Across relational distance: Gen 42:21 — the brothers' guilt toward Joseph, now separated by years and captivity, remains operative across the relational distance; guilt persists through relational disruption without diminishing.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=15775, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The characteristic operates primarily within covenant contexts (VCG-01's Levitical sequences; VCG-02's accumulation in Israel; VCG-03's priestly transference; VCG-04's punitive consequence in Israel/Judah). However, it also crosses covenantal boundaries: Gen 4:13 — Cain's guilt before God precedes the formal covenant; Gen 42:21 — the brothers' guilt toward Joseph involves inter-personal guilt without explicit covenantal framing; Hab 1:11 — "guilty men, whose own might is their god" — the Babylonian armies bear guilt outside Israel's covenant. The guilt-state is not exclusively covenantal.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=15776, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The relational scope spans God-to-person (Ezr 9:15 — standing before God in guilt), person-to-God (Hos 5:15 — seeking God's face under guilt), person-to-person (Gen 42:21 — guilt concerning a brother; 2Sa 19:19 — guilt owed to the king), community-to-God (Ezr 9:7 — communal generational guilt), and person-to-community (Lev 4:3 — priest's sin bringing guilt on the people). The guilt-state operates across every relational dimension present in the corpus: no relational context is exempt from the possibility of guilt-bearing.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=15777, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Relational scope (vertical God-person, horizontal person-person, communal) is evidenced across all dimensions. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=15778, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-17]** S — The 97-verse corpus does not explicitly name spiritual beings (angelic or adversarial) in connection with guilt as inner-being state. The one near-exception is Isa 6:7 — the seraph touches Isaiah's lips and removes his guilt — but this is divine agent (seraph as God's messenger) acting on God's behalf, not an independent spiritual being mediating guilt.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=15779, S) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-17]** S — The corpus is silent on adversarial spiritual agency in connection with guilt as inner-being state. The guilt-state operates within the human-God covenant relationship without named adversarial involvement.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=15780, E) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Partially — through the seraph in Isa 6:7. "He touched my mouth and said: 'Behold, this has touched your lips; your guilt is taken away, and your sin atoned for'" — the seraph is the agent of guilt-removal; the angelic ministry mediates the guilt's taking-away. This is the sole angelic-mediation instance in the corpus, and it is for guilt-removal rather than guilt-communication.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=15781, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Adversarial spiritual beings (T4.6.1/T4.6.2) are entirely absent from the corpus. Angelic ministry (T4.6.3) is present in one instance — Isa 6:7 — as the agent of guilt-removal, not guilt-communication. The characteristic is primarily a direct human-God transaction; angelic involvement is singular and removal-directed.

---

  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-17] ✓

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=15782, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Both — in negative and positive directions. (1) *Negative condition change*: Ezr 9:6 — "our guilt has mounted up to the heavens" — the accumulating guilt transforms the community's moral condition from bearable to overwhelming; Hos 5:5 — "stumble in his guilt" — the guilt transforms stability into moral stumbling; (2) *Positive orientation change through acknowledgment*: Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face" — the guilt-acknowledgment transforms the inner orientation from God-avoiding to God-seeking; (3) *Positive condition change through removal*: Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — the guilt-removed state transforms the person's inner condition from guilty to fit-for-commission; Jer 33:8 — "I will cleanse them" — the cleansing is a condition transformation.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=15783, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Both dimensions evidenced. (1) *Reversible — through divine action*: Isa 6:7 — guilt taken away; Jer 33:8 — cleansed from all guilt; Psa 34:22 — not condemned when taking refuge; (2) *Progressive accumulation toward irreversibility*: 2Ch 33:23 — "incurred guilt more and more" — the progressive accumulation moves the condition toward greater difficulty of reversal; Jer 2:22 — the indelible stain that lye cannot remove suggests a depth of guilt that self-effort cannot reverse; (3) *Eschatological irreversibility*: Eze 21:25 — "the time of your final punishment" — once judgment arrives at the appointed final time, the guilt's consequential transformation is irreversible.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=15784, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Transformation evidence is extensive in all directions (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=15785, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — the most explicit inner-state sequence in M10 is in Lev 5:2-5: (1) violation committed (outer act); (2) "hidden from him" (inner unawareness — no guilt-state yet); (3) "when he comes to know it" (knowledge event — guilt-trigger); (4) "realizes his guilt" (guilt-recognition — guilt-state active); (5) "confesses the sin he has committed" (speech-act — guilt expressed outward); followed by the prescribed offering (outer remedy). A second sequence: Hos 5:15's broader arc: Israel in sin → God withdraws → distress → "acknowledge their guilt" → "seek my face" → God returns.

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=15786, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The Lev 5:2-5 sequence reveals that guilt-as-inner-state has a precise phenomenological structure: (1) the violation creates the objective moral liability (before conscience registers it); (2) the hidden state is the subjective gap where the liability exists but is not felt; (3) the coming-to-knowledge is the cognitive trigger; (4) the guilt-recognition is the conscience's subjective engagement with the moral reality; (5) the confession is the inner state's outward speech-form. The sequence reveals that CHAR-17 (subjective guilt-state) is temporally posterior to the act and posterior to knowledge — it requires both a violation and the knowledge of it to activate.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=15787, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Sequence is the most extensively evidenced structural feature of CHAR-17 — Lev 5:2-5 sequence; Hos 5:15 arc; Ezr 9:6-15 progression. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=15788, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Two primary change-mechanisms. (1) *Conscience-activation triggering remedy*: Lev 5:5 — "when he realizes his guilt... and confesses... and will restore" — the guilt-recognition mechanism activates confession and restitution; the guilt-state is the inner pressure that produces the remedial sequence; (2) *Divine-exposure producing recognition*: Hos 5:15 — God's withdrawal; Jer 2:22 — guilt remaining visible before God despite concealment; Eze 21:23/24 — God bringing guilt to remembrance — the exposure mechanism operates from the divine side, removing the person's self-concealment option and forcing acknowledgment.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=15789, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes. (1) *Individual-Levitical context* (VCG-01): mechanism is natural conscience-activation when violation "comes to knowledge" — an inner knowledge-trigger mechanism; (2) *Communal-accumulated context* (VCG-02): mechanism is the divine-consequence escalation — external catastrophes (sword, captivity — Ezr 9:7) that force internal recognition; (3) *Prophetic-judgment context* (VCG-05): mechanism is divine memory-activation — God "brings their guilt to remembrance" (Eze 21:23) at the appointed time; (4) *Priestly-transference context* (VCG-03): mechanism is representative-bearing — the guilt is addressed through the prescribed mediatorial institution rather than through individual conscience-activation alone.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=15790, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Mechanisms are evidenced across four distinct VCG contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=15791, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — extensively. (1) *Suffering as guilt's consequence*: Ezr 9:7 — "for our iniquities we... have been given into the hand of the kings of the lands, to the sword, to captivity, to plundering, and to utter shame" — the accumulated guilt produced this catalogue of suffering; Isa 24:6 — "inhabitants suffer for their guilt"; (2) *Suffering as guilt-recognition trigger*: Gen 42:21 — "that is why this distress has come upon us" — the brothers' current suffering triggers the retrospective guilt-recognition; their distress is simultaneously the guilt's consequence and the recognition-catalyst; (3) *Guilt as punitive suffering*: Gen 4:13 — Cain's "my punishment is greater than I can bear" — the punishment experienced is unbearable inner suffering from the punitive weight.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=15792, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Suffering *reveals* the guilt and *triggers* its recognition. Gen 42:21 — the brothers' current distress reveals their past guilt by creating the interpretive pressure that connects the two; suffering does not produce the guilt (the guilt preceded the suffering) but reveals it by providing the interpretive framework. Ezr 9:7 — captivity, sword, plundering are the outer sufferings that reveal the inner guilt-reality they resulted from; the suffering makes visible what the guilt-state was. Hos 5:15 — "in their distress earnestly seek me" — the distress (which is a form of suffering) is the condition that activates the seeking that follows guilt-acknowledgment.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=15793, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — The relationship between guilt and suffering is extensively evidenced (Ezr 9:7; Gen 4:13; Gen 42:21; Isa 24:6; Hos 5:15). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=15794, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — in both the negative and positive formation arcs. (1) *Negative formation*: 2Ch 33:23 — "Amon incurred guilt more and more" — the progressive guilt-accumulation without acknowledgment is a negative formation arc; the refusal to humble (unlike Manasseh his father) forms the person further into the guilt-bearing condition; (2) *Positive formation*: the Levitical guilt-to-remedy sequences (Lev 4/5/6 — realisation → confession → restitution → offering) constitute a moral formation practice: each guilt-recognition and remedy cycle forms the person in the pattern of conscience-engagement, honest acknowledgment, and remedial action.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=15795, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Guilt-as-inner-state is the *formation hinge*: it is the inner experience that either redirects the person toward God (when acknowledged — Hos 5:15's guilt-acknowledgment → seeking God's face) or deepens the formation away from God (when suppressed — 2Ch 33:23's Amon increasing guilt). The Lev 4/5/6 ritual sequences build guilt-acknowledgment into the community's regular formation practice: recurring guilt-recognition and remedy cycles train the conscience in honest self-assessment and corrective action. Guilt is thus the formation system's feedback mechanism.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=15796, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Formation evidence is present (T5.5.1 and T5.5.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=15797, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — two eschatological endpoints. (1) *Judgment enforcement of accumulated guilt*: Eze 21:25 — "the time of your final punishment" — the guilt-state has an appointed eschatological day of enforcement; Isa 24:6 — "inhabitants suffer for their guilt" in the earth's final desolation; (2) *Complete cleansing*: Jer 33:8 — "I will cleanse them from all the guilt of their sin against me, and I will forgive all the guilt" — the eschatological fullness is the total removal of all accumulated guilt; Hos 5:15 — God's return after guilt-acknowledgment anticipates the final restoration in which guilt's barrier between God and people is permanently removed.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=15798, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-17]** E — For the unaddressed guilt: Eze 21:23/24 — the present guilt remembered by God anticipates the eschatological day when it is "brought to remembrance... that they may be taken"; the present guilt-experience anticipates its eschatological enforcement. For the acknowledged guilt: Isa 6:7 — the present removal of Isaiah's guilt (lips touched, guilt taken away) anticipates the eschatological fullness of complete guilt-cleansing; Jer 33:8 — the future total cleansing is the eschatological fullness toward which present partial guilt-resolution points.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=15799, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced in both judgment (Eze 21:25; Isa 24:6) and restoration (Jer 33:8; Hos 5:15) directions. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=15800, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-17]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: accumulated weight (Ezr 9:6), indelible stain (Jer 2:22), intergenerational transmission (Jer 32:18). Developmental consequences. (1) *Accumulated-weight deposit*: Ezr 9:6 — the guilt rising above the head and to the heavens produces the developmental consequence of inability to stand before God (Ezr 9:15); the accumulation progressively removes the capacity for upright God-relationship; (2) *Indelible-stain deposit*: Jer 2:22 — the stain that no soap removes produces the developmental consequence of permanent divine visibility; the person cannot achieve concealment from God regardless of self-cleansing effort; (3) *Intergenerational-transmission deposit*: Jer 32:18 — "guilt of fathers to their children" — the developmental consequence is that each subsequent generation begins with an inherited guilt-burden added to the previous generation's deposit.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=15801, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — explicitly. Jer 32:18 — "you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them" — intergenerational guilt-transmission is named as a characteristic of how God's justice operates; the guilt-deposit from one generation becomes part of the next generation's inherited moral burden. Isa 14:21 — "prepare slaughter for his sons because of the guilt of their fathers" — the children bear consequence for ancestral guilt. Ezr 9:7 — "from the days of our fathers to this day we have been in great guilt" — the accumulated generational guilt-deposit extends from the founding ancestors to Ezra's present community.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=15802, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-17]** E — T2.8 found extensive deposit evidence. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.
