## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=15803, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur within or proximate to the 97-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record)*: Jer 2:22 — the indelible guilt before God mirrors CHAR-13's divine record; Eze 21:23/24 — God bringing guilt "to remembrance" directly engages CHAR-13's record-keeping; CHAR-17 is the subjective experience of what CHAR-13 holds objectively; (2) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Lev 5:5 — "realizes his guilt... and confesses" — guilt-recognition produces confession; the guilt-state and confession are inseparable in the VCG-01 sequences; (3) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received)*: Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — the guilt-removal is the forgiveness-event; Jer 33:8 — "I will cleanse them from all the guilt... and I will forgive" — guilt and forgiveness are the two sides of the same transaction; (4) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: Jer 3:13 — "acknowledge your guilt, that you rebelled" — the guilt arose from the rebellious sinning act; CHAR-1 generates the guilt that CHAR-17 registers.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=15804, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-17 as the *subjective-experiential face* of M10: while CHAR-13 holds the objective divine record and CHAR-1 names the sinning acts and CHAR-14 names the forgiveness pathway, CHAR-17 is what the person experiences on the inside of carrying the moral liability. CHAR-17 is the felt inner side of what CHAR-13 holds on the divine side. Its co-occurrence with CHAR-3 (confession) confirms that guilt-recognition is the conscience-activation that initiates the confession-act; its co-occurrence with CHAR-14 confirms that guilt is precisely what forgiveness addresses.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=15805, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are extensively evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=15806, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-17]** E — CHAR-17 consistently *follows* CHAR-1 (the sinning acts that generate the guilt-state) and *precedes* CHAR-3 (confession is what guilt-recognition triggers). CHAR-17 also consistently *precedes* CHAR-14's seeking dimension: Lev 5:5 — "when he realizes his guilt... and confesses" — the guilt (CHAR-17) precedes the confession (CHAR-3) which precedes the offering/forgiveness-seeking (CHAR-14). The sequence: CHAR-1 (sinning act) → CHAR-13 (record entry) → CHAR-17 (guilt-recognition) → CHAR-3 (confession) → CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought) → CHAR-14's receiving dimension (forgiveness granted).

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=15807, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The relationship with CHAR-1 is *causal*: the sinning act causally generates the guilt-state that CHAR-17 names. The relationship with CHAR-3 is *causal*: the guilt-recognition (CHAR-17) causally produces the confession (CHAR-3) — Lev 5:5's "realizes his guilt... and confesses." The relationship with CHAR-13 is *constitutive*: CHAR-17 is the subjective experience of what CHAR-13 holds; they are the two faces of the same moral reality. The full sequence is a causal chain: act → record → inner experience → confession → remedy.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=15808, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Sequential patterns (causal chain) are extensively evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=15809, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Lev 5:5 — "when he realizes his guilt... and confesses" — guilt-recognition produces the confession-act; (2) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received)*: Lev 5:6 — "he shall bring to the Lord as his compensation... a lamb or a goat, for a sin offering" — the guilt-recognition produces the forgiveness-seeking offering; (3) *Restitution acts*: Lev 6:4; Num 5:7 — "make full restitution" — guilt-recognition produces restitutive action toward the wronged party; (4) *Shame-affect*: Ezr 9:6 — "I am ashamed and blush" — guilt produces the shame-state that is itself a secondary inner-being consequence.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=15810, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: the sinning acts are the primary source of guilt-state entries — Jer 3:13 — "acknowledge your guilt, that you rebelled"; Lev 5:19 — "he has indeed incurred guilt before the Lord" — incurred by sinning; (2) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: 2Ch 33:23 — Amon's progressive guilt-accumulation results from habitual volitional defection rather than single acts; (3) *CHAR-11 (Sin as universal condition)*: Rom 5:19 (CHAR-16 corpus) — the sinner-condition produces the guilt that CHAR-17 registers subjectively; the universal sin-condition is the ontological source of the guilt-state's ongoing reality.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=15811, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-17]** E — CHAR-17 is the *subjective-experiential constituent* of CHAR-13's objective-record reality: CHAR-13 (the divine record) is the objective basis; CHAR-17 (guilt-as-inner-state) is the subjective experience of that basis from the inside. Together they are the two dimensions of what being-guilty means: the objective standing before God (CHAR-13) and the inner felt weight of that standing (CHAR-17). CHAR-17 also constitutes an element of CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought and received): the guilt-state is what is addressed when forgiveness is sought; it is the inner experiential content that CHAR-14's seeking and receiving resolve.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=15812, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are extensively evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=15813, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — but with distinctive vocabulary. A.von (H5771H — the guilt-as-transferable-burden sense of VCG-03) is shared across M10 with CHAR-13 and the broader iniquity cluster. However, a.sham (H0816 — guilt-realisation verb) and ash.mah (H0819 — guilt-condition noun) and pu.qah (H6330 — conscience-stagger) are specific to CHAR-17 within M10: the guilt-realisation vocabulary is CHAR-17's distinctive terminological contribution. The a.sham/ash.mah family does not appear in CHAR-13's chat.tat/chet vocabulary or CHAR-16's chat.ta/hamartōlos vocabulary.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=15814, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — a.von (H5771H/I) shares the iniquity-root with CHAR-13's broader vocabulary field. However, a.sham (H0816) and ash.mah (H0819) are from a distinct root (*ʾāšam* — to be guilty, to offend) that generates CHAR-17's distinctive guilt-realisation vocabulary. The *ʾāšam* root does not appear in other M10 characteristics' primary vocabulary: it is CHAR-17's distinctive root contribution alongside the shared a.von root.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=15815, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The distinctive *ʾāšam* root vocabulary (a.sham/ash.mah — guilt-realisation and guilt-condition) confirms that CHAR-17 is a semantically distinct characteristic within M10: it has its own primary vocabulary that does not appear in CHAR-1, CHAR-13, or CHAR-16's primary terms. The shared a.von root connects CHAR-17 to the broader guilt-iniquity field but does not dissolve the distinction. CHAR-17 is the *ʾāšam*-characteristic of M10: the subjective guilt-recognition and guilt-condition register that the *ʾāšam* root uniquely names.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=15816, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Partial vocabulary sharing (a.von — shared with M10 iniquity field) and distinctive vocabulary (a.sham/ash.mah — CHAR-17-specific) are evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=15817, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-17]** E — CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record) most closely resembles CHAR-17 — both address the person's standing in relation to sin's moral weight. The precise distinction: CHAR-13 is the *objective* moral account before God — the record exists whether the person feels it or not (CHAR-13's definition: "the person carries this record whether they feel it or not"); CHAR-17 is the *subjective* inner experience of moral liability — the conscience's felt recognition. CHAR-13 is God-side (held in divine memory); CHAR-17 is person-side (felt in human conscience). Both address the same moral reality from opposite directions.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=15818, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The overlap is at Ezr 9:6 — "our guilt has mounted up to the heavens" — where the accumulation is both objectively before God (CHAR-13 — the record mounted up) and subjectively experienced (CHAR-17 — Ezra's shame and inability to lift his face). The precise boundary: the mounting guilt-weight "before God" (objective record — CHAR-13) produces the inner shame and face-avoidance (subjective experience — CHAR-17). Ezr 9:6 sits at the boundary between both: it names the objective accumulation AND describes the subjective shame-response.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=15819, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The distinction is one of *direction and subjectivity*: CHAR-13 faces toward God (the record is held in divine memory before God); CHAR-17 faces toward the person (the guilt-state is experienced in human conscience). They are the same moral reality seen from different directions — divine-record-side (CHAR-13) and human-experience-side (CHAR-17). The distinction is directional rather than one of degree (both engage the same moral weight) or constitutional level (both operate at the whole-person level of moral standing).

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=15820, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=15821, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Several. (1) Lev 5:5 — "when he realizes his guilt... and confesses" — shared with CHAR-3 (confession) and CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought); (2) Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — shared with CHAR-14 (forgiveness received through priestly act) and CHAR-13 (divine record cleared); (3) Gen 4:13 — "my punishment is greater than I can bear" — shared with CHAR-13's punitive-dimension and potentially CHAR-15 (generational sin — the sin of Cain as the founding ancestral act); (4) Jer 2:22 — "stain of your guilt still before me" — shared with CHAR-13 (indelibility of divine record); (5) Exo 28:38 — Aaron bearing Israel's guilt — shared with CHAR-14 (priestly atonement system).

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=15822, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Lev 5:5 shared with CHAR-3 and CHAR-14 confirms that guilt-recognition (CHAR-17), confession (CHAR-3), and forgiveness-seeking (CHAR-14) are operationally inseparable: the same verse evidences all three because they are sequential stages of a single conscience-to-remedy pipeline. Isa 6:7 shared with CHAR-14 and CHAR-13 confirms that guilt-removal (the resolution of CHAR-17) is simultaneously forgiveness-received (CHAR-14) and divine-record-cleared (CHAR-13): the three characteristics resolve together in the same divine act.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=15823, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified across CHAR-3, CHAR-13, CHAR-14, and CHAR-15 (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=15824, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Three dimensions are shared: (1) *The objective-record dimension* (the guilt before God as a registered fact) is shared with CHAR-13; (2) *The confession-producing dimension* (guilt-recognition generating confession) is shared with CHAR-3; (3) *The forgiveness-resolution dimension* (the guilt-state requiring and receiving forgiveness) is shared with CHAR-14. CHAR-17's unique dimension — the *subjective inner experience of guilt* (the felt weight, the shame-posture, the conscience-stagger — pu.qah) — is not shared with any other M10 characteristic. This is the characteristic's irreducible contribution.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=15825, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-17 as the *experiential bridge* of M10: it bridges CHAR-13 (the objective record) to CHAR-3 (the confession) to CHAR-14 (the forgiveness sought). Without CHAR-17's subjective guilt-experience, the conscience would not be activated; without conscience activation, CHAR-3's confession and CHAR-14's seeking would not occur. CHAR-17 is the inner-experiential hinge that connects the objective moral reality (CHAR-13's record) to the remedial response (CHAR-3/14). Its unique subjective dimension (the felt weight, shame, conscience-stagger) is what makes the hinge function.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=15826, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-17]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced across three adjacent characteristics (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T6 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T6.1.1–T6.1.3, T6.2.1–T6.2.3, T6.3.1–T6.3.4, T6.4.1–T6.4.4, T6.5.1–T6.5.4, T6.6.1–T6.6.3, T6.7.1–T6.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-17] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 4:13; Exo 28:38; Lev 5:5/5:6/5:19/6:4; Num 5:7; 2Ch 33:23; Ezr 9:6/7; Isa 6:7; Jer 2:22/3:13; Eze 21:23/24; Hos 5:15 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T7.


---

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=15827, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Five primary term-groups. (1) **a.sham (H0816)** — verb "to be guilty, to become guilty, to realize one's guilt" — dominant in VCG-01's guilt-recognition sequences (Lev 4:13/22/27; 5:2/3/4/5/17/19; Num 5:6; Hos 5:15). The root (*ʾāšam*) means to offend, to incur guilt — the verb form names the becoming-guilty moment and the realising-of-guilt moment. (2) **a.shem (H0818)** — noun "guilty one" — Gen 42:21; Ezr 10:19. (3) **ash.mah (H0819)** — noun "guilt, guiltiness" — dominant in VCG-02's accumulation passages (Ezr 9:6/7/13/15; 2Ch 24:18/28:10/13/33:23). The noun form names the guilt-condition as an accumulated state. (4) **a.von (H5771H)** — "iniquity, guilt, liability" — VCG-03's transferable-burden register (Gen 44:16; Exo 28:38; 2Sa 14:9; Jer 33:8). (5) **a.von (H5771I)** — "punishment, punitive consequence" — VCG-04's punitive register (Gen 4:13; Eze 4:4/5/6). (6) **pu.qah (H6330)** — "staggering, stumbling" — 1Sa 25:31 — the single-term expression of conscience-stagger under guilt. No NT Greek appears in this corpus.

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=15828, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-17]** E — A.sham (H0816) spans the broadest grammatical range: (1) *Qal perfect* — "they realized their guilt" (Lev 4:13 — *wĕnĕʾšāmû*); (2) *Qal imperfect* — "he shall be guilty" / "he is guilty" (Lev 5:17/19); (3) *Qal participle* — "those who are guilty" (Judg 21:22 — *ʾāšēm*); (4) *Niphal* — "became guilty" (Gen 42:21 — *ʾăbāl ʾăšēmîm*). Ash.mah (H0819) functions as a noun throughout — "the guilt" (Ezr 9:6/7/13/15). A.von (H5771H) functions as a noun — "the guilt/iniquity" (Exo 28:38 — "bear the guilt"). Pu.qah (H6330) is a noun — "cause of stumbling/pangs" (1Sa 25:31). The grammatical range from verb (becoming-guilty moment) through noun (guilt-condition-state) maps the characteristic's arc from event to sustained state.

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=15829, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The *ʾāšam* family covers: (1) *The recognition moment*: Lev 5:5 — "when he realizes his guilt" — a.sham as the conscience-engagement event; (2) *The incurred state*: Lev 5:19 — "he has indeed incurred guilt before the Lord" — the objective guilt-status; (3) *The accumulated condition*: Ezr 9:6 — "our guilt has mounted up" — ash.mah as the communal guilt-weight; (4) *The condition requiring remedy*: Ezr 10:19 — "their guilt offering was a ram" — the guilt-condition addressed through ritual. A.von (H5771H) covers: *transferable moral burden* (Gen 44:16; Exo 28:38; 2Sa 14:9). A.von (H5771I) covers: *punitive consequence* (Gen 4:13 — "my punishment"; Eze 4:4/5/6 — symbolic bearing). Pu.qah covers: *conscience-stumbling* (1Sa 25:31 — the affective-somatic guilt-experience).

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=15830, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — the vocabulary actively distinguishes the recognition-moment (a.sham verb — VCG-01), the accumulated-condition (ash.mah noun — VCG-02), the transferable-burden (a.von H5771H — VCG-03), the punitive-consequence (a.von H5771I — VCG-04), and the conscience-stagger (pu.qah — VCG-05). These are five distinct terms/senses for five distinct aspects of the same guilt-phenomenon. The vocabulary diversity within CHAR-17 is the highest of any M10 characteristic studied — five distinct term-groups for one characteristic — reflecting the guilt-state's multi-dimensional inner reality.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=15831, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — implicitly. A.sham (H0816) in its negative form (*lōʾ yʾăšam* — "will not be guilty" or "not guilty") names the structural opposite. Psa 34:22 — "none of those who take refuge in him will be condemned" (a.sham negative) — the not-guilty state is named through the negated guilt-term. Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" — the taken-away state is the experiential opposite of the guilt-bearing state. Jer 33:8 — "cleanse them from all the guilt... and forgive" — the cleansed-and-forgiven state names the structural opposite.

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=15832, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — A.shem (H0818) — Gen 42:21 — "we are guilty" — functions as the person-type description (though not a dedicated agent-noun like chat.ta). The participial form of a.sham used in Judg 21:22 (*ʾāšēm*) and Hab 1:11 (*ʾāšēm* — "guilty men") approaches a person-type term: it names the person constituted by the guilt-state. Amo 8:14 — "the Guilt of Samaria" (*ʾašmat Šōmĕrôn*) uses ash.mah as a personalised title for the idol — an ironic inversion where the guilt-condition name becomes an idol-name.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=15833, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Yes — within the guilt-acknowledgment-seeking complex. Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face, and in their distress earnestly seek me" — *bāqaš* (to seek) paired with *ḥālāh* (to earnestly seek/implore) are the supplication-seeking terms following guilt-acknowledgment. Ezr 9:6's prayer posture — "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you, my God" — is itself a supplication-posture though not using a specific supplication-term. The seeking that follows guilt-acknowledgment is the characteristic's embedded supplication-direction.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=15834, S) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-17]** S — The 97-verse corpus contains no NT Greek vocabulary. The characteristic is exclusively OT Hebrew. The guilt-realisation vocabulary (*ʾāšam* family) is distinctively OT in its priestly-ritual framing (Lev 4/5/6 sequences) and its communal accumulation language (Ezr 9:6/7/13/15). The NT equivalent vocabulary (conscience — *syneidēsis*; guilt-consciousness) does not appear in this corpus. The OT-only character reflects the characteristic's primary locus in the Levitical ritual framework and the historical-confessional tradition of the exilic period.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=15835, S) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-17]** S — No NT vocabulary appears in the corpus. Whether NT-period coinage for guilt-as-inner-state exists cannot be determined from this corpus. Noted.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=15836, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-17]** E — The vocabulary arc — from a.sham's guilt-recognition verb (Lev 5:5 — the conscience-engagement moment) through ash.mah's accumulated guilt-condition (Ezr 9:6 — mounting to the heavens) through a.von H5771H's transferable guilt-burden (Exo 28:38 — Aaron bearing Israel's guilt on his forehead) through a.von H5771I's punitive-consequence dimension (Gen 4:13 — punishment greater than Cain can bear; Eze 4:4/5/6 — prophet bearing Israel's punishment) through pu.qah's conscience-stagger (1Sa 25:31 — pangs of conscience) through ash.mah's indelibility (Jer 2:22 — stain before God despite soap) to Isa 6:7's removal (guilt taken away) — reveals a characteristic whose vocabulary covers the complete guilt-arc from its conscience-recognition origin through its accumulation and weight through its transference and punishment-bearing to its indelible divine-visibility and finally its removal by divine act.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=15837, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Two primary anchors. (1) *Ezr 9:6* — "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you, my God, for our iniquities have risen higher than our heads, and our guilt (*ash.mah*) has mounted up to the heavens" — ash.mah functions as the subject of the mounting metaphor; the guilt-condition is named as the entity that has risen and accumulated; the verb "mounted up" (*gāḏlāh*) measures its quantity. The mounting metaphor names the accumulation dynamic of VCG-02 precisely. (2) *Lev 5:5* — "when he realizes his guilt (*yʾāšam*) in any of these and confesses the sin he has committed" — a.sham (Qal imperfect) functions as the guilt-recognition verb; its activation is the trigger for the confession that follows; the verb names the conscience's engagement event.

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=15838, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Four literary forms. (1) *Legal-priestly code* (Lev 4/5/6; Num 5; Exo 28): the guilt-recognition-and-remedy sequences are prescriptive law; requires reading as normative instruction defining the conscience-to-remedy pipeline; (2) *Post-exilic confession-prayer* (Ezr 9:6-15): Ezra's penitential prayer as the guilt-accumulation expression; requires reading as a corporate confessional act within the restoration context; (3) *Historical narrative* (Gen 4:13; Gen 42:21; Gen 44:16; 1Sa 25:24/31; 2Sa 14:9): requires reading within the narrative-theological context; the guilt-experiences are part of character and plot development; (4) *Prophetic oracle* (Jer 2:22; Jer 33:8; Eze 21:23/24; Hos 5:15): requires reading within the covenantal-accusation or restoration-promise framework.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=15839, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Three key argument structures. (1) *Lev 5:2-5's discovery-guilt-remedy argument*: premise — violation committed (possibly unknowingly); when it "comes to knowledge" — guilt is realized; conclusion — the guilt must be confessed and remedied; the argument establishes the knowledge-triggered conscience-pipeline as the normative guilt-to-remedy sequence. (2) *Jer 2:22's indelibility argument*: premise — you attempt self-cleansing with lye and soap; conclusion — "the stain of your guilt is still before me"; the argument establishes that guilt cannot be self-erased; only divine action removes it. (3) *Job 11:6's debt-moderation argument*: premise — "God exacts of you less than your guilt deserves"; conclusion — divine mercy moderates the full punitive weight of guilt; the guilt-state carries a debt whose full payment God withholds.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=15840, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Four contextual settings. (1) *Priestly-tabernacle/temple* (Lev 4/5/6; Num 5; Exo 28): the sanctuary as the designated space for guilt's ritual address; (2) *Exilic-confessional* (Ezr 9:6-15; 2Ch 28:10/13): the post-exilic community processing accumulated generational guilt; (3) *Personal narrative* (Gen 4:13; 42:21; 44:16; 1Sa 25:24/31): the guild-state experienced in personal and family crises; (4) *Prophetic-judgment* (Jer 2:22; Hos 5:15; Eze 21:23/24): the covenant lawsuit context in which guilt's indelibility and divine exposure are named.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=15841, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Ezr 9:6 functions as the primary anchor for the accumulation register: "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you, my God, for our iniquities have risen higher than our heads, and our guilt has mounted up to the heavens." It most fully expresses CHAR-17's essential character because: (1) it names the *inner affective dimension* (ashamed, blush); (2) it names the *posture disruption* (cannot lift face); (3) it names the *accumulation dynamic* (risen above head, mounted to heavens); (4) it locates guilt before God ("my God"); (5) it spans both individual (I am ashamed) and communal (our iniquities, our guilt) dimensions. The Lev 5:5 sequence is the primary procedural anchor for the conscience-recognition register.

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=15842, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Ezr 9:6 uniquely reveals the *proportional quantification of guilt*: guilt can be measured in spatial metaphors — "risen higher than our heads, mounted up to the heavens." This dimensional quantification names the guilt-state's felt inner experience more precisely than any other verse: it is not merely present but has a measurable upward trajectory, has exceeded a critical threshold (head-height), and has reached a cosmic scale (to the heavens). No other verse in the 97-verse corpus quantifies the guilt-state's felt weight through spatial measurement. The verse also uniquely pairs the individual shame-response ("I am ashamed") with the communal weight ("our guilt") in a single syntactical unit.

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=15843, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-17]** E — **Moral psychology** (specifically Paul Ricoeur's *The Symbolism of Evil*; Jonathan Haidt's moral emotions research) and **clinical psychology of guilt** (specifically June Tangney's distinction between guilt and shame; psychodynamic guilt theory) provide the most illuminating frameworks. Ricoeur's analysis of guilt as the "experience of the bound will" illuminates the Lev 5:17/19 sequences where guilt exists even without subjective awareness, and the Jer 2:22 indelibility. Tangney's guilt/shame distinction illuminates Ezr 9:6: the posture (blush, cannot lift face) is shame-expression while the content ("our guilt has mounted up") is guilt-cognition — the two coexist but are distinct inner states.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=15844, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Ricoeur's "bound will" illuminates Gen 4:13's "my punishment is greater than I can bear" — the punitive dimension of guilt binds Cain's will, constraining his inner freedom; the guilt-state is the experience of being under an obligation the will cannot discharge by itself. Tangney's shame/guilt distinction illuminates the difference between Ezr 9:6 (shame — I cannot lift my face; the self is exposed and diminished) and Lev 5:5 (guilt — I realise my guilt about a specific act; the focus is on what was done, not what the self is). The verse evidence confirms this distinction: productive guilt (Lev 5:5 → confession → remedy) differs from shame-paralysis (Ezr 9:6 → cannot lift face) precisely as clinical psychology distinguishes them.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=15845, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Clinical psychology treats guilt as primarily an intra-psychic phenomenon that can be "excessive" or "neurotic" when it persists beyond its adaptive function. The verse evidence treats guilt as constitutively relational and objective: Lev 5:19 — "he has indeed incurred guilt before the Lord" — the guilt is real and objective regardless of subjective excess or deficit. The divergence reveals that biblical guilt cannot be pathologised as "excessive" without distorting its nature: if the guilt corresponds to actual moral liability before God, it is appropriate regardless of its felt intensity. Clinical frameworks miss the objective relational dimension (before God) that makes the guilt's intensity proportional rather than neurotic.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=15846, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-17]** E — Clinical psychology surfaces the *reparative function* of guilt more precisely than the corpus does: Tangney's research shows that productive guilt consistently motivates reparative behaviour toward the wronged party. The verse evidence confirms this (Lev 6:4 — guilt → restitution; Num 5:7 — guilt → confession + full restitution + added fifth) but does not theorise the psychological mechanism by which guilt produces the motivation. The gap is the psychological mechanism of guilt-to-reparation motivation: the corpus describes the sequence but leaves the inner motivational mechanism implicit. No further verse investigation is required — the gap is a human-science complement to the corpus's prescriptive framework.

---

T7 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       20/20 ✓ (T7.1.1–T7.1.10, T7.2.1–T7.2.6, T7.3.1–T7.3.4)
  2. Scope marker:       20× [CHAR-17] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T7.1.8/7.1.9 are S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 4:13/42:21/44:16; Exo 28:38; Lev 4:13/22/27/5:2/3/4/5/17/19/6:4; Num 5:6/7; Judg 21:22; 1Sa 25:24/31; Ezr 9:6/7/13/15/10:19; Job 11:6; Psa 34:22; Isa 6:7; Jer 2:22/33:8; Eze 4:4/5/6/21:23/24; Hos 5:15; Amo 8:14 — all in §3)
  → PASS. All 8 tiers complete.

---

## CHAR-18 — Iniquity as accumulated moral crime (189 rows: E=179 S=10 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=15847, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The 162-verse corpus reveals God's character across four dimensions. (1) *God as moral auditor who measures and tracks iniquity to completion*: Gen 15:16 — "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" — God tracks iniquity as a measurable quantity with a threshold; Hos 13:12 — "the iniquity of Ephraim is bound up; his sin is kept in store" — God maintains a sealed record; Psa 90:8 — "you have set our iniquities before you, our secret sins in the light of your presence" — nothing is hidden from his omniscient gaze. (2) *God as just judge who acts when iniquity reaches its measure*: Isa 26:21 — "the Lord is coming out from his place to punish the inhabitants of the earth for their iniquity"; Jer 25:12 — after seventy years God punishes Babylon for their iniquity. (3) *God as sovereign pardoner who removes iniquity by his own act*: Isa 40:2 — "her iniquity is pardoned"; Mic 7:18/19 — "pardoning iniquity... he will tread our iniquities underfoot"; Jer 31:34 — "I will forgive their iniquity." (4) *God as the one wearied by accumulated iniquity*: Isa 43:24 — "you have wearied me with your iniquities" — the accumulation has a relational weight that strains even divine forbearance.

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=15848, S) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-18]** S — No verse in the 162-verse corpus attributes the iniquity-as-crime condition to God. God appears as the one who measures iniquity (Gen 15:16), remembers it (Hos 13:12; Eze 21:24), punishes it (Isa 26:21), bears its weight vicariously through the servant (Isa 53:6 — "the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all"), and removes it (Zec 3:4). God does not bear iniquity as a personal moral crime; the characteristic is exclusively creaturely.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=15849, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The silence confirms iniquity-as-accumulated-crime is constitutively creaturely: it is the human moral account of wrongdoing accumulated before the one who measures and judges it. Isa 43:24 — "you have wearied me with your iniquities" — positions God as the one upon whom iniquity's weight falls relationally; he is not the one who carries it as personal crime but the one who absorbs its relational burden. The silence is structurally necessary: God cannot commit moral crimes against himself or a higher authority, nor can he accumulate iniquity within himself. The very act of God pardoning iniquity (Mic 7:18) presupposes his total absence of it.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=15850, S) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-18]** S — The corpus provides no evidence of a positive created purpose for iniquity-as-accumulated-crime. The characteristic is entirely a fallen-condition category: the accumulation of moral wrongdoing before God. No verse presents iniquity-as-crime as a designed feature of the human person. Its nearest functional dimension is its measurability (Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete"), which implies a divinely calibrated accounting system, but this accounting system responds to wrongdoing rather than being its purpose.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=15851, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Entirely a response to the fallen condition. Psa 51:5 — "I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin did my mother conceive me" — iniquity characterises the womb-context of human existence, locating it at the constitutional origin of the fallen human condition but not at the original creation. Isa 53:6 — "all we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned — every one — to his own way; and the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all" — the universal iniquity arises from the self-directed turning of every person; the characteristic is the outcome of the fall's universal operation. No verse in the corpus situates iniquity-as-crime within the pre-fall created design.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=15852, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — two eschatological orientations. (1) *Judgment endpoint*: Gen 15:16 — "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" — the accumulation moves toward a divinely appointed threshold at which judgment falls; Jer 25:12 — after seventy years, Babylon's iniquity reaches its appointed reckoning; Isa 22:14 — "this iniquity will not be atoned for you until you die"; (2) *Removal endpoint*: Jer 50:20 — "in those days and in that time... iniquity shall be sought in Israel, and there shall be none" — the eschatological fullness is the complete dissolution of the iniquity-record; Zec 3:9 — "I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day"; Jer 31:34 — "I will forgive their iniquity, and I will remember their sin no more" — the new covenant fullness is iniquity entirely removed from divine memory.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=15853, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Iniquity-as-accumulated-crime expresses the image through its *inverted moral accounting*: the image-bearer is constituted as a moral being whose acts are weighed and measured before God (Gen 15:16 — iniquity measurable; Hos 13:12 — bound up in divine record). Only a being made in God's image can accumulate a moral account before him — only the image-bearer stands in the morally accountable relationship that makes iniquity possible. Psa 90:8 — "you have set our iniquities before you" — the "before you" is the relational positioning of the image-bearer before God; the capacity to accumulate iniquity in God's sight belongs to the image's constituted God-relationship.

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=15854, S) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-18]** S — Exclusively creaturely (T0.1.2 — God does not bear iniquity as personal moral crime). The characteristic requires the position of moral offender accumulating crime before a higher moral authority; God occupies no such position.

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=15855, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The presence of iniquity-as-accumulated-crime reveals the image in its *morally corrupted and accumulating fallen condition*: the image-bearer not only commits individual sinning acts but has accumulated a store of moral wrongdoing that constitutes a standing crime before God. Isa 1:4 — "a people laden with iniquity" — the weight of the accumulation is the image's fallen condition expressed as moral burden; Ezr 9:6 — "our iniquities have risen higher than our heads" — the accumulation has exceeded the person's capacity to address it through self-effort. The image is not destroyed but its moral account before God is deeply defiled and growing.

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=15856, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — through the servant-bearing passages (VCG-06 Isa 53 sub-set). Isa 53:5 — "he was crushed for our iniquities" — the servant's bearing of the people's accumulated iniquity is typological: the priestly scapegoat bearing (Lev 16:21/22 — VCG-01) is the OT type; the servant's bearing is the eschatological fulfilment-type pointing toward Christ. Isa 53:6 — "the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all" — the universal scope of the iniquity-bearing is the typological pinnacle: all accumulated human iniquity placed upon the one servant. Zec 3:4 — "I have taken your iniquity away from you" — Joshua's single-person iniquity-removal prefigures the land's total removal (Zec 3:9) and the new covenant's complete forgiveness (Jer 31:34).

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=15857, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-18]** E — From OT priestly institution and prophetic enactment toward NT Christological fulfilment. The scapegoat bearing Israel's iniquities and being sent to the wilderness (Lev 16:21/22 — VCG-01) is the cultic type; the servant who bears "the iniquity of us all" (Isa 53:6) is the prophetic type pointing toward Christ. Both types share the same structure: the iniquity of the many placed on the one bearer who removes it by carrying it away. The direction runs from human cultic institution (Lev 16) through prophetic prototype (Isa 53) toward divine fulfilment — from the human representative (Aaron; the scapegoat; the servant-figure) to the divine one who bears the iniquity of all.

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=15858, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Typological use is extensively evidenced (T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T0 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       12/12 ✓ (T0.1.1–T0.1.3, T0.2.1–T0.2.3, T0.3.1–T0.3.3, T0.4.1–T0.4.3)
  2. Scope marker:       12× [CHAR-18] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: 10/10 E findings cite evidence ✓ (T0.1.2, T0.3.2 are S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 15:16; Lev 16:21/22; Psa 51:5/90:8; Isa 1:4/22:14/26:21/40:2/43:24/53:5/6; Jer 25:12/31:34/50:20; Ezr 9:6; Hos 13:12; Mic 7:18/19; Zec 3:4/9 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T1.


---

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=15859, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Iniquity as accumulated moral crime." Three signals. (1) *Iniquity*: the English rendering of a.von (H5771G) — the OT's comprehensive term for moral wrongdoing at its deepest register; not merely a sinning act but the moral weight of that act accumulated before God; (2) *As accumulated*: the defining modifier — iniquity here is not the single act but the store of wrongdoing built up over time and generations; Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete" — the accumulation is quantifiable and progressive; (3) *Moral crime*: the "crime" dimension signals the forensic register — iniquity-as-crime stands before a judge (God) as an offence requiring adjudication. The name signals: the person's moral account of wrongdoing before God, built up over time, constituting a standing moral-legal liability.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=15860, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Single primary term throughout the entire 162-verse corpus: **a.von (H5771G)** — the crime/moral-wrong sub-entry of the a.von root. The root (*ʿāwôn*) spans three distinct senses within M10's framework: H5771G (crime/moral-wrong — CHAR-18), H5771H (guilt/liability — CHAR-17 VCG-03), and H5771I (punishment/consequence — CHAR-17 VCG-04). The H5771G crime-sense names the wrongdoing itself as a moral reality before God — what Job calls "my iniquity" in the sense of "my moral crime" (Job 7:21 — "take away my iniquity"; Job 13:23 — "how many are my iniquities?"). No NT Greek vocabulary appears in this corpus; the characteristic is exclusively OT Hebrew, reflecting the a.von root's primary OT-framework operation.

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=15861, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-18]** E — All three. *Directional*: iniquity has a trajectory of accumulation — Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete" — moving toward fullness; Hos 14:1 — "you have stumbled because of your iniquity" — the iniquity operates as a directional force pulling the person away from God. *Relational*: iniquity is always iniquity-before-God — Psa 90:8 — "you have set our iniquities before you" — the relational frame is the moral account in God's presence; Isa 43:24 — "you have wearied me with your iniquities" — iniquity is a relational burden between the person and God. *Constitutional*: Psa 51:5 — "I was brought forth in iniquity" — the iniquity-condition is constitutional, prior to individual acts; Eze 18:20 — "the wickedness of the wicked shall be upon himself" — it attaches constitutionally to the person who commits it.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=15862, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The corpus reveals CHAR-18 as an *objective moral-legal condition* before God — not primarily a subjective feeling (that is CHAR-17) but the actual crime-weight the person carries in God's moral ledger. The definition identifies it as "the inner dimension of accumulated moral wrongdoing — crime in its moral-spiritual sense." The characteristic's five dominant registers illuminate its nature: (1) personal moral crime individually owned (VCG-04 — Eze 18:20 — "upon himself"); (2) accumulated weight that overwhelms (VCG-05 — Psa 38:4 — "gone over my head"); (3) generational crime transmitted across family lines (VCG-02 — Exo 20:5); (4) iniquity in divine memory (VCG-03 — Hos 13:12 — "bound up"); (5) iniquity that ruins from within (VCG-07 — Pro 5:22 — "ensnare him").

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=15863, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The corpus reveals eight constituent elements corresponding to the eight VCGs. (1) *Priestly cultic bearing* (VCG-01 — 17V): the institutional mechanism for iniquity's ritual address; (2) *Generational transmission* (VCG-02 — 18V): iniquity crossing family lines; (3) *Divine memory* (VCG-03 — 28V): iniquity held in God's sight and record; (4) *Personal individual ownership* (VCG-04 — 29V): each person's own crime; (5) *Overwhelming accumulated weight* (VCG-05 — 30V): the mounting pressure that exceeds capacity; (6) *Pardon and removal* (VCG-06 — 21V): divine forgiveness resolving the moral debt; (7) *Self-destructive force* (VCG-07 — 15V): iniquity ruining from within; (8) *Heart-seated origin* (VCG-08 — 4V): iniquity originating in the will.

---

#### T1.2.3 (cf=15864, E) — What is the current best working description of this characteristic — encapsulating its constitutional location, the faculties it primarily engages, and its impact on the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Working description: *Iniquity as accumulated moral crime (CHAR-18) is the inner dimension of accumulated moral wrongdoing — crime in its moral-spiritual sense, the store of inner wrongdoing before God as a measured, generational, heart-seated reality. The corpus evidences eight registers: (1) Priestly bearing (VCG-01 — 17V): Aaron confessing Israel's iniquities over the scapegoat (Lev 16:21/22); (2) Generational transmission (VCG-02 — 18V): iniquity of fathers visited on children to the third and fourth generation (Exo 20:5); (3) Divine memory (VCG-03 — 28V): God's omniscient awareness — "iniquity of Ephraim is bound up; his sin is kept in store" (Hos 13:12); (4) Personal ownership (VCG-04 — 29V): each soul dies for its own crime — Eze 18:20; (5) Overwhelming weight (VCG-05 — 30V): "gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me" (Psa 38:4); (6) Pardon and removal (VCG-06 — 21V): servant bears/is crushed for iniquities of all (Isa 53:5/6/11); (7) Self-destructive force (VCG-07 — 15V): "ensnare him, held fast in cords of his sin" (Pro 5:22); (8) Heart-seated origin (VCG-08 — 4V): "taken their idols into their hearts... stumbling block of their iniquity" (Eze 14:3/4/7). Distinguished from CHAR-17 (the subjective experience of guilt) by being the objective moral-crime reality that subsists before God whether felt or not.*

---

#### T1.3.1 (cf=15865, E) — What is the structural opposite of this characteristic — the inner-being reality that stands against or excludes it?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The structural opposite is *moral blamelessness — the state of no iniquity upon the person*. Psa 32:2 — "blessed is the man against whom the Lord counts no iniquity, and in whose spirit there is no deceit" — the not-counted state is the explicit opposite; Jer 50:20 — "iniquity shall be sought in Israel, and there shall be none" — the eschatological opposite is iniquity's total absence; 2Sa 22:24 — "I was blameless before him, and I kept myself from guilt" — blamelessness is the active moral opposite, sustained through self-restraint. Eze 18:20 — "the righteousness of the righteous shall be upon himself" — the righteous person's moral account is the direct structural opposite of the wicked person's iniquity upon himself.

---

#### T1.3.2 (cf=15866, E) — What does this characteristic explicitly exclude or resist?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The characteristic explicitly excludes: (1) *Divine acceptance*: Lev 7:18 — offering eaten on the third day is "tainted" and the person "shall bear his iniquity" — iniquity nullifies the acceptance that the offering would have granted; (2) *Covenant standing*: Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God" — iniquity excludes unimpeded God-relationship; (3) *Answered prayer*: Isa 59:2 — "your sins have hidden his face from you so that he does not hear" — iniquity excludes divine responsiveness; (4) *Standing before God*: Psa 130:3 — "if you, O Lord, should mark iniquities, O Lord, who could stand?" — iniquity, if fully accounted, excludes every person from standing before God.

---

#### T1.3.3 (cf=15867, E) — What is this characteristic not — where does it end and something else begin?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three boundaries. (1) *Not CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: CHAR-17 is the subjective inner experience of moral liability — the conscience's felt recognition; CHAR-18 is the objective moral-crime reality before God that subsists whether the conscience registers it or not. Hos 12:8 — Ephraim denies iniquity can be found in him — but the iniquity is real (God knows — Hos 13:12); the denial is a CHAR-17 conscience-failure, not a CHAR-18 non-existence. (2) *Not CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: CHAR-1 is the discrete sinning act; CHAR-18 is the accumulated store of moral crime that sinning acts have produced; (3) *Not CHAR-15 (Generational sin)*: CHAR-15 is the transmission mechanism through generations; CHAR-18 includes the generational dimension (VCG-02) but is not limited to it — it also encompasses individual personal crime (VCG-04 — Eze 18:20).

---

#### T1.4.1 (cf=15868, E) — In what distinct ways does this characteristic operate within the inner person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Five distinct inner modes. (1) *As a measured, accumulating store*: Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete" — iniquity accumulates toward a threshold; 2Ch 33:23 (cross-ref) and Jer 25:12 — the store builds until judgment; (2) *As an overwhelming weight*: Psa 38:4 — "gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me" — the weight presses on the whole person; (3) *As a heart-seated originating force*: Eze 14:3 — "taken their idols into their hearts... stumbling block of their iniquity before their faces" — the iniquity begins in the heart's chosen orientation; (4) *As a self-destructive ensnaring force*: Pro 5:22 — "the iniquities of the wicked ensnare him, and he is held fast in the cords of his sin"; (5) *As a separating barrier*: Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God" — iniquity operates as a structural relational barrier.

---

#### T1.4.2 (cf=15869, E) — Does this characteristic operate differently depending on context, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes across the eight VCG registers. (1) *Priestly-cultic context* (VCG-01): iniquity operates as a transferable ritual burden addressed through institutional mediation (Lev 16:21/22 — Aaron confesses over scapegoat); (2) *Generational context* (VCG-02): iniquity operates as a transmitted moral inheritance visited on children (Exo 20:5); (3) *Divine-memory context* (VCG-03): iniquity operates as a sealed record in God's sight (Hos 13:12 — "bound up and kept in store"); (4) *Personal context* (VCG-04): iniquity operates as an individually owned crime that attaches to the person (Eze 18:20 — "upon himself"); (5) *Self-destructive context* (VCG-07): iniquity operates as an internal corrupting force that rusts from within (Eze 24:23 — "rot away in your iniquities").

---

#### T1.4.3 (cf=15870, E) — Does this characteristic have a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — and if so, how does it function?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — in three speech-modes. (1) *Confession*: Lev 16:21 — "Aaron shall confess over it all the iniquities of the people of Israel" — the verbal act of naming iniquity over the scapegoat; Psa 38:18 — "I confess my iniquity; I am sorry for my sin"; Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers"; (2) *Self-accusation*: Jer 14:7 — "though our iniquities testify against us" — iniquity is personified as a witness speaking against the person in God's court; (3) *Denial*: Hos 12:8 — "they cannot find in me iniquity or sin" — the self-deceived conscience's speech-denial of existing iniquity; Job 31:33 — "if I have concealed my transgressions... by hiding my iniquity in my heart" — the alternative is named as speech-concealment.

---

#### T1.5.1 (cf=15871, E) — What is the first or most immediate inner-being response to receiving or encountering this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The immediate responses vary across the corpus. (1) *Conscience-strike*: 2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him after he had numbered the people" — the immediate inner response to the iniquity is conscience activation; (2) *Shame and face-avoidance*: Ezr 9:6 — "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you, my God, for our iniquities have risen higher than our heads" — shame and the inability to face God are immediate; (3) *Request for removal*: 2Sa 24:10 — David immediately says "please take away the iniquity of your servant" — the immediate movement is toward plea for divine removal; (4) *Despair*: Psa 40:12 — "my iniquities have overtaken me, and I cannot see; they are more than the hairs of my head; my heart fails me" — the crushing weight produces immediate inner collapse.

---

#### T1.5.2 (cf=15872, E) — Is the immediate response consistent across the verse evidence, or does it vary?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Variable. The conscience-engaged person responds with shame and plea (Ezr 9:6; 2Sa 24:10); the overwhelmed person responds with despair (Psa 40:12; Psa 38:4 — "too heavy for me"); the self-deceived person shows no immediate response at all — Hos 12:8 — "they cannot find in me iniquity" — the iniquity is present but produces no internal response because the conscience is suppressed. Amo 9:10's sinners (CHAR-16) — presuming no disaster will come — illustrate the response-less extreme. The immediate response thus ranges from acute conscience-awareness (2Sa 24:10) through overwhelming despair (Psa 40:12) to complete denial (Hos 12:8).

---

#### T1.5.3 (cf=15873, E) — Where the verse evidence is silent on immediate response, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — For the generational-transmission register (VCG-02 — Exo 20:5; Deu 5:9; Lev 26:39-43), the immediate inner response of individual community members to inherited iniquity from ancestors is not described. The corpus names the transmission and its consequences (rotting in exile — Lev 26:39) but not the moment-by-moment inner experience of the person discovering they bear their fathers' iniquity as a personal burden.

---

#### T1.6.1 (cf=15874, E) — What does this characteristic produce in the inner being over time?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Four sustained inner-being effects. (1) *Progressive accumulation toward threshold*: 2Ch 28:13 (cross-ref) — "our guilt is already great, and there is fierce wrath against Israel" — the sustained accumulation intensifies toward crisis; Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete" — the accumulation is ongoing; (2) *Bodily and vital decay*: Psa 31:10 — "my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away" — sustained iniquity produces bodily deterioration; (3) *Increasing moral blindness*: Psa 36:2 — the wicked person "flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out" — sustained iniquity deepens self-deception; (4) *Self-destructive entrapment*: Pro 5:22 — "the iniquities of the wicked ensnare him, and he is held fast in the cords of his sin" — the sustained iniquity produces a self-created bondage from which the person cannot free himself.

---

#### T1.6.2 (cf=15875, E) — What states, qualities, capacities, or orientations does sustained exposure to or possession of this characteristic establish?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three established states. (1) *Separation from God*: Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you so that he does not hear" — sustained iniquity establishes structural relational distance from God; (2) *Moral self-deception*: Psa 36:2 — "he flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out and hated" — sustained iniquity establishes a false inner self-assessment; Hos 12:8 — the wealthy Ephraim's claim of clean hands alongside real iniquity; (3) *Ruinous self-destruction*: Eze 18:30 — "lest iniquity be your ruin" — the sustained iniquity establishes the ruin-trajectory as the person's inner direction.

---

#### T1.6.3 (cf=15876, E) — Does the sustained effect differ from the immediate response — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes. The immediate response (shame — Ezr 9:6; conscience-strike — 2Sa 24:10; despair — Psa 40:12) is episodic. The sustained effects (bodily decay — Psa 31:10; separation from God — Isa 59:2; moral self-deception — Psa 36:2; entrapment — Pro 5:22) are constitutional states established over time by unaddressed iniquity. The progression is: immediate shock of recognition → sustained accumulated weight → constitutional corruption of the inner person → ruination.

---

#### T1.7.1 (cf=15877, E) — What inner conditions or orientations enable genuine reception of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three enabling conditions. (1) *Humbled heart*: Lev 26:41 — "if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled and they make amends for their iniquity" — heart-humiliation is the enabling precondition for iniquity-acknowledgment; (2) *Conscience-strike*: 2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him" — the immediate conscience-event opens the path to acknowledgment; (3) *Divine discipline*: Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face, and in their distress earnestly seek me" — God's strategic withdrawal and the resulting distress activate the inner pressure toward acknowledgment.

---

#### T1.7.2 (cf=15878, E) — What inner conditions block, distort, or prevent reception?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three blocking conditions. (1) *Pride and self-flattery*: Psa 36:2 — "he flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out and hated" — pride generates a self-protective cognitive distortion; (2) *Wealth-induced false security*: Hos 12:8 — "I am rich; I have found wealth for myself; in all my labors they cannot find in me iniquity or sin" — material prosperity generates the illusion of moral cleanness; (3) *Stiff-necked will*: Exo 34:9 — Moses names Israel as "a stiff-necked people" requiring pardon precisely because the will is set against acknowledgment.

---

#### T1.7.3 (cf=15879, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Hos 12:8 names it: "I am rich... they cannot find in me iniquity" — the inner state is wealthy self-sufficiency generating false moral confidence. Psa 36:2 — "flatters himself in his own eyes" — the inner state is proud self-deception. Job 31:33 — "if I have concealed my transgressions... by hiding my iniquity in my heart" — the alternative to honest disclosure is conscious inner concealment; the person knows the iniquity but buries it deliberately. The denier's inner state thus has two forms: unconscious self-deception (Psa 36:2 — cannot see or hate the iniquity) and conscious concealment (Job 31:33 — hiding it in the heart).

---

#### T1.8.1 (cf=15880, E) — What is the primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic from the programme's dimension vocabulary?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The primary dimension is **the objective moral-crime account before God** — the person's standing in God's moral ledger as the one who has accumulated wrongdoing against him. This is the definition's "crime in its moral-spiritual sense." The characteristic is not primarily experiential (CHAR-17 is the experiential register) but constitutional: it subsists before God whether the person feels it or not (Hos 12:8 — Ephraim denies it; Hos 13:12 — God has it bound up and stored). The constitutional moral-crime account is the primary dimension; the felt-weight is secondary.

---

#### T1.8.2 (cf=15881, E) — What evidence from the verse evidence supports this classification?

**[CHAR-18]** E — (1) Gen 15:16 — "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" — the Amorites are not described as feeling their iniquity; it exists as a measured moral reality before God regardless of their inner awareness; (2) Hos 13:12 — "bound up; his sin is kept in store" — the bound-up condition is God's action, not Ephraim's subjective experience; (3) Hos 12:8 — Ephraim claims no iniquity while God knows it is real — the objective and subjective are explicitly divergent; (4) Eze 18:20 — "the wickedness of the wicked shall be upon himself" — the "upon himself" is an objective moral-legal attachment.

---

#### T1.8.3 (cf=15882, E) — Does this characteristic carry secondary dimensions — and if so, what are they, and do they compete with the primary classification?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — three secondary dimensions. (1) *Subjective weight dimension*: Psa 38:4 — "too heavy for me" — the accumulation is felt; Psa 40:12 — "my heart fails me" — the weight has an experiential dimension secondary to the objective reality; (2) *Social-relational dimension*: Eze 18:20 — the moral crime is between the person and God but also affects the person's standing in the community (Lev 19:8 — "that person shall be cut off from his people"); (3) *Generational-corporate dimension*: VCG-02 (Exo 20:5; Deu 5:9) — the iniquity has a corporate reach across family lines that makes it more than individual.

---

T1 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T1.1.1–T1.1.3, T1.2.1–T1.2.3, T1.3.1–T1.3.3, T1.4.1–T1.4.3, T1.5.1–T1.5.3, T1.6.1–T1.6.3, T1.7.1–T1.7.3, T1.8.1–T1.8.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-18] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
