Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 15:16; Exo 20:5/34:9; Deu 5:9; Lev 7:18/16:21/22/19:8/26:40/41; 2Sa 24:10; 1Ch (cross-ref noted); Ezr 9:6; Job 31:33; Psa 31:10/36:2/38:4/40:12/130:3; Pro 5:22; Isa 53:5/6/11/59:2; Jer 14:7/25:12; Eze 14:3/18:20/24:23; Hos 12:8/13:12/14:1; Amo 9:10 — all in §3 or noted as cross-ref)
  → PASS. Response approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T2 continues in the next response.


---

## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=15883, E) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — through the separation-from-God dimension. Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you so that he does not hear" — iniquity operates as a spirit-level barrier severing the person's communication with God; the separation is at the deepest relational level. Psa 32:2 — "blessed is the man against whom the Lord counts no iniquity, and in whose spirit there is no deceit" — the spirit is explicitly named as the locus where iniquity (or its absence) is assessed; the spirit's moral condition is the location of the iniquity-count.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=15884, E) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Partly — through the heart-seated VCG-08 register. Eze 14:3 — "these men have taken their idols into their hearts, and set the stumbling block of their iniquity before their faces" — the iniquity originates in the will's heart-level choice to receive idols inward; the crime begins at the spirit-level of the heart's allegiance. Job 15:5 — "your iniquity teaches your mouth, and you choose the tongue of the crafty" — the iniquity originates at the level of inner orientation that then drives speech. The spirit-level is the origin of the most deeply-rooted iniquity (VCG-08); other registres (VCG-01 cultic; VCG-04 personal act) originate at the will and conduct levels.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=15885, E) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Spirit-level location (Psa 32:2 — "in whose spirit there is no deceit" as iniquity's absence; Isa 59:2 — separation from God; Eze 14:3 — heart-originated idolatry as iniquity's source) reveals that iniquity-as-accumulated-crime is not merely a behavioural or social category but penetrates to the spirit-level of the person's God-relationship. The spirit-level iniquity (Eze 14:3 — idol-taking into the heart) is the most severe form precisely because it originates at the person's deepest inner level of allegiance and orientation. The separation it produces (Isa 59:2) is a spirit-level relational rupture.

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=15886, E) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Spirit-level location is evidenced (Psa 32:2; Isa 59:2; Eze 14:3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=15887, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — extensively. (1) *Inner heart → outer conduct*: Eze 14:3 — idols taken into the heart produce the stumbling block set before the face — the inner heart-level iniquity expresses outward; Job 15:5 — "your iniquity teaches your mouth" — the inner crime drives outer speech; (2) *Outer conduct → inner accumulation*: Psa 38:4 — acts of iniquity produce the accumulated inner weight that goes over the head; the outer acts generate the constitutional inner burden; (3) *Individual → communal/generational*: VCG-02 (Exo 20:5) — individual fathers' iniquity transmits to the community of descendants; the personal crime extends constitutionally to family lines; (4) *Inner → bodily*: Psa 31:10 — "my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away" — the inner crime produces bodily constitutional decay.

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=15888, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Two primary sequences. (1) *Heart-to-conduct-to-accumulation sequence*: heart takes in idols (VCG-08 — Eze 14:3) → outer conduct of iniquity → accumulation in divine record (VCG-03 — Hos 13:12 — "bound up") → weight on the person (VCG-05 — Psa 38:4) → separation from God (Isa 59:2); (2) *Confession-to-removal sequence*: iniquity acknowledged (Lev 26:40 — "confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers") → heart humbled (Lev 26:41) → priestly bearing (VCG-01 — Lev 16:21/22) → iniquity removed (VCG-06 — Isa 40:2; Zec 3:4). Both sequences confirm bidirectional constitutional movement: inward generation → outward consequence; inward acknowledgment → outward resolution.

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=15889, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Constitutional movement is extensively evidenced (T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=15890, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes. Eze 18:20 — "the soul who sins shall die. The son shall not suffer for the iniquity of the father" — the soul is the entity that bears the personal iniquity and its death-consequence; iniquity is constitutively a soul-level moral attachment. Psa 40:12 — "my iniquities have overtaken me... my heart fails me" — the soul-level collapse under the weight of iniquity; the iniquities have overtaken the person's inner core.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=15891, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Soul-level location (Eze 18:20 — "the soul who sins shall die"; Psa 40:12 — "my heart fails me") reveals that iniquity-as-accumulated-crime is not merely a legal abstraction but attaches to the whole person at their deepest level. Eze 18:20's "the soul who sins" uses *nepeš* as the total-person subject of iniquity: the iniquity belongs to the whole person (soul) not merely a faculty. The death-consequence falls on the soul-level entity; the iniquity is the soul's personal crime.

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=15892, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (Eze 18:20; Psa 40:12). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=15893, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — centrally in VCG-08. Eze 14:3 — "these men have taken their idols into their hearts, and set the stumbling block of their iniquity before their faces" — the heart is the explicit origin of the iniquity; the idols are taken into the heart as an act of will; Eze 14:4/7 repeat this heart-location pattern. Job 31:33 — "if I have concealed my transgressions as others do by hiding my iniquity in my heart" — the heart is the hiding-place of deliberately concealed iniquity; the crime can be buried at the heart-level to avoid acknowledgment.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=15894, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Heart-location (Eze 14:3/4/7 — idols taken into the heart; Job 31:33 — iniquity hidden in the heart) reveals two distinct heart-iniquity functions. (1) *The heart as iniquity's origin*: Eze 14:3 — the will chooses to receive idols inward; the heart is the site of the originating criminal allegiance that produces all subsequent iniquity; (2) *The heart as iniquity's hiding-place*: Job 31:33 — the heart can conceal acknowledged iniquity to avoid accountability; the hiding is a deliberate inner act. VCG-08's heart-seated register is thus the most foundational: it locates iniquity at the person's innermost will-level, making it the source-register from which other VCGs flow.

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=15895, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Heart-location is centrally evidenced in VCG-08 (Eze 14:3/4/7; Job 31:33). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=15896, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The mind engages through the self-assessment and concealment dimensions. Psa 36:2 — "he flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out and hated" — the mind's self-assessment is systematically distorted by pride; the cognitive self-evaluation cannot accurately perceive the iniquity it carries. Hos 12:8 — "in all my labors they cannot find in me iniquity or sin" — the mind's verdict on the self is false; the cognitive faculty wrongly assesses the iniquity-condition. Job 13:23 — "how many are my iniquities and my sins? Make me know my transgression and my sin" — Job asks for mind-level knowledge of his iniquity-count; the mind needs divine revelation to accurately know it.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=15897, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Mind-location (Psa 36:2 — self-flattery; Hos 12:8 — false self-assessment; Job 13:23 — need for divine disclosure) reveals that the mind's self-assessment of iniquity is constitutively unreliable without divine input. The mind flatters itself (Psa 36:2), generates false confidence (Hos 12:8), and requires God to reveal what it cannot see on its own (Job 13:23 — "make me know"). This unreliability is a symptom of the iniquity's effect on the mind itself: the accumulated crime distorts the very cognitive faculty that would assess it.

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=15898, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Mind-level location (self-assessment distortion — Psa 36:2; Hos 12:8; Job 13:23) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=15899, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — *Conscience* as a distinct soul-level faculty is evidenced. 2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him after he had numbered the people" — the heart-strike is a conscience event: the moral self-assessment faculty activates spontaneously in response to the iniquity; Psa 38:18 — "I confess my iniquity; I am sorry for my sin" — the conscience produces both confession and sorrow as its output; Lev 26:40/41 — "confess their iniquity... if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled" — the confession presupposes conscience engaging through heart-humiliation.

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=15900, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Conscience-level location (2Sa 24:10 — heart-strike; Psa 38:18 — confession and sorrow; Lev 26:40/41 — confession with humbled heart) reveals that conscience is the inner faculty that detects and responds to iniquity-as-accumulated-crime. However, unlike CHAR-17 where conscience is the primary faculty (the characteristic IS conscience in guilt-awareness mode), in CHAR-18 conscience is a secondary responder: the iniquity exists in the divine record independently of conscience (Hos 13:12 — "bound up" whether Ephraim recognises it or not); conscience detects what is already there. The conscience-engagement is productive when it produces confession (Psa 38:18) and suppressed when self-flattery operates (Psa 36:2).

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=15901, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Conscience-level location is evidenced as a secondary responder (2Sa 24:10; Psa 38:18; Lev 26:40/41). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=15902, E) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — three body-part links. (1) *Hands and fingers*: Isa 59:3 — "your hands are defiled with blood and your fingers with iniquity" — the hands and fingers are the body-parts through which iniquity is enacted; (2) *Bones*: Eze 32:27 — "whose iniquities are upon their bones" — iniquity clings to the bones of the fallen warriors; Psa 31:10 — "my bones waste away" — iniquity causes bodily skeletal decay; (3) *Head/shoulders*: Psa 38:4 — "my iniquities have gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me" — the head and shoulders are the body-parts over which the iniquity-burden overwhelms.

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=15903, E) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three distinct body-part functions. (1) *Hands as instruments of iniquity* (Isa 59:3): the hands name the body-part of active wrongdoing; iniquity is enacted through the body's working members; the moral crime has a bodily-enactment dimension; (2) *Bones as iniquity's permanent mark* (Eze 32:27): iniquity clings to the bones even in death, naming the body as the permanent carrier of the iniquity-record into Sheol; (3) *Head/shoulders as overwhelmed bearer* (Psa 38:4): the head-overflowing image names the body-part through which the accumulated weight is expressed; the burden metaphor uses the body's carrying capacity to name iniquity's weight.

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=15904, E) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Body-part links (hands/fingers — Isa 59:3; bones — Eze 32:27/Psa 31:10; head — Psa 38:4) are evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=15905, E) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Bidirectional. (1) *Inner crime → outer bodily enactment*: Eze 14:3 — heart-seated idolatry → Isa 59:3 — fingers with iniquity — the inner moral crime expresses through the body's active members; (2) *Outer bodily decay ← inner crime*: Psa 31:10 — "my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away" — the inner iniquity produces outward bodily deterioration; Eze 32:27 — "iniquities upon their bones" — the inner crime attaches as a permanent bodily mark; (3) *Bodily burden image for inner weight*: Psa 38:4 — "gone over my head; like a heavy burden" — the body's carrying experience images the inner weight of accumulated iniquity.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=15906, E) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The bidirectional body-iniquity link reveals that iniquity-as-accumulated-crime is not a purely inner-abstract condition but engages the body at both ends of its trajectory. The iniquity is enacted through the body (Isa 59:3 — fingers), accumulated as inner weight (Psa 38:4 — over the head), and expressed in bodily decay (Psa 31:10 — bones wasting). Addressing iniquity must therefore address both the inner moral-crime reality and its bodily manifestations; the body is the instrument, carrier, and bearer of the iniquity-burden simultaneously.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=15907, E) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Body-characteristic link (hands, bones, head) is evidenced and bidirectional. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=15908, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Divine-record deposit*: Hos 13:12 — "the iniquity of Ephraim is bound up; his sin is kept in store" — God maintains an accumulated sealed record; Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete" — the accumulation grows as a measured deposit; (2) *Body-bone deposit*: Eze 32:27 — "iniquities are upon their bones" — iniquity clings permanently to the person's physical remains; Psa 31:10 — bones wasting away — the physical deterioration is the bodily deposit of the crime; (3) *Generational deposit*: Exo 20:5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation" — the fathers' iniquity-deposit is transmitted as a generational moral inheritance.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=15909, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Supporting: Hos 13:12 (divine-record deposit — sealed); Eze 32:27 (bodily deposit — on bones); Exo 20:5 (generational deposit). Contradicting or complicating: Jer 50:20 — "iniquity shall be sought in Israel, and there shall be none" — the divine record is fully erasable through pardon; Zec 3:9 — "I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day" — the accumulated deposit is removable by divine sovereign act; Eze 18:20 — the father's iniquity-deposit does not automatically transfer to the son (the Eze 18 personal-accountability register contradicts the simple generational transfer of Exo 20:5 at the level of punishable liability). The deposit is real but divinely removable.

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=15910, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Deposit evidence is extensive: divine-record (Hos 13:12), bodily (Eze 32:27), generational (Exo 20:5). T5.7 will carry: all three deposit-forms are divinely removable (Jer 50:20; Zec 3:9; Jer 31:34) but constitute real accumulated moral-crime burdens until removed; the Eze 18:20 personal-accountability principle sets limits on the generational deposit's punishable transmission.

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=15911, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Two constitutional origins. (1) *Heart-level willful choice*: Eze 14:3 — "taken their idols into their hearts" — the iniquity originates in the will's inner choice to embrace what God forbids; Job 31:33 — hiding iniquity in the heart is a deliberate act; (2) *Inherited constitutional condition*: Psa 51:5 — "I was brought forth in iniquity, and in sin did my mother conceive me" — the iniquity-condition precedes any individual act of will; it characterises the womb-context of human existence. Both origins operate: VCG-08 (heart-seated) names the willful-choice origin; Psa 51:5 names the pre-personal constitutional origin.

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=15912, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Multiple — and the multiplicity across eight VCGs represents the greatest origin-complexity in M10. Individual willful acts (VCG-04 — Eze 18:20 — personal crime), heart-originated allegiance (VCG-08 — Eze 14:3), inherited pre-personal condition (Psa 51:5), generational transmission (VCG-02 — Exo 20:5), cultic failure (VCG-01 — Lev 5:1/17), and communal accumulation (VCG-05 — Ezr 9:6) are all distinct iniquity-origins operating simultaneously across the 162-verse corpus.

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=15913, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes across all eight VCG contexts. (1) *Priestly-cultic* (VCG-01): origin in specific cultic failures (Lev 5:1 — failure to testify; Lev 7:18 — eating peace offering on wrong day); (2) *Generational* (VCG-02): origin in ancestral idolatry transmitted across family lines (Exo 20:5); (3) *Divine-memory* (VCG-03): origin confirmed by God's comprehensive awareness (Psa 90:8 — "secret sins in the light of your presence"); (4) *Personal-individual* (VCG-04): origin in each person's own crime (Eze 18:20); (5) *Accumulated weight* (VCG-05): origin cumulative from multiple violations; (6) *Heart-seated* (VCG-08): origin in the will's inner allegiance choice (Eze 14:3).

---

## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=15914, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The perceptive faculty is engaged on both sides: the person's perception of their own iniquity and God's perception of all iniquity. (1) *God's perceiving of iniquity*: Psa 90:8 — "you have set our iniquities before you, our secret sins in the light of your presence" — God's perception penetrates all concealment; Jer 16:17 — "my eyes are on all their ways... nor is their iniquity concealed from my eyes" — divine perception is comprehensive; (2) *Human perception of own iniquity*: Job 13:23 — "make me know my transgression and my sin" — Job's perceptive faculty requires divine disclosure; Psa 36:2 — the wicked person's perception is so distorted he cannot perceive his own iniquity; Hos 12:8 — the wealthy Ephraim cannot perceive iniquity in himself.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=15915, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Iniquity *impairs* the perceptive faculty through its self-protective distortion mechanism. Psa 36:2 — the wicked person's self-flattery actively blocks accurate perception of his own iniquity; the iniquity distorts the very faculty that would perceive it. Hos 12:8 — Ephraim's wealth creates a perceptual blind spot; the accumulated iniquity is present (Hos 13:12 — "bound up") while perception sees nothing. Conversely, the conscience-engaged person's perception sharpens: 2Sa 24:10 — David's heart struck him — the perception of his own iniquity was immediate and accurate. Iniquity impairs perception when conscience is suppressed; it sharpens perception when conscience is engaged.

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=15916, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The perceptive pattern reveals an inverse relationship between the depth of iniquity's entrenchment and the accuracy of the person's self-perception: the more entrenched the iniquity (Psa 36:2 — the hardened wicked; Hos 12:8 — self-deceived Ephraim), the less accurately the person perceives it. The corrective is always from God's side: Job 13:23 — divine disclosure; Jer 14:7 — "our iniquities testify against us" — the iniquity itself becoming the witness that overrides false perception. God's perfect perception (Jer 16:17 — omniscient gaze) stands against the human's distorted self-perception.

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=15941, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Conscientiousness is engaged through both the iniquity's purposeful enactment and the remedy's precise requirements. (1) *Conscientiousness misdirected toward iniquity*: Isa 5:18 — "those who draw iniquity with cords of falsehood, who draw sin as with cart ropes" — the active, purposeful drawing of iniquity requires and directs conscientiousness; Eze 14:3 — the deliberate taking of idols into the heart; (2) *Conscientiousness directed toward remedy*: Lev 16:21 — Aaron's careful confessional act over the scapegoat; Lev 26:40/41 — the precise sequence of confession → heart-humiliation → making amends.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=15942, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Accumulated iniquity *misdirects and ultimately captures* conscientiousness. Isa 5:18 — conscientiousness is actively directed toward the pursuit of iniquity; the "drawing with cart ropes" image names the conscientious effort put into sinning. Pro 5:22 — the ensnaring of the will means that conscientiousness, once misdirected, cannot easily redirect; the cords that bind the will bind conscientiousness with it. The remedy sequences (Lev 16:21; Lev 26:40/41) show conscientiousness being redirected through precise ritual and confessional actions.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=15943, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The conscientiousness pattern confirms CHAR-18's active character: iniquity-as-accumulated-crime is not produced by passive moral drift alone but by conscientiously-directed wrongdoing (Isa 5:18 — cart ropes) and heart-level allegiance choices (Eze 14:3). The accumulated iniquity thus reflects the full investment of the person's conscientiousness faculty in the wrong direction. This is why its reversal requires the equally conscientious acts of confession (Lev 26:40), heart-humiliation (Lev 26:41), and word-taking (Hos 14:2) — conscientiousness must be redirected, not merely awakened.

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=15944, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The relational capacity is constitutively engaged: iniquity-as-crime is always crime before someone. (1) *Vertical God-relationship*: Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God" — the primary relational disruption; Isa 43:24 — "you have wearied me with your iniquities" — God's relational experience of Israel's accumulated crime; (2) *Horizontal human relationships*: Deu 19:15 — iniquity as a legal crime against another person requiring witnesses; 2Sa 3:8 — Abner charged with a fault concerning a woman; Jos 22:20 — Achan's iniquity affecting all Israel's communal standing.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=15945, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Accumulated iniquity *progressively severs* relational capacity. Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God" — the primary relational damage is God-separation; the iniquity acts as a structural barrier. Amo 3:2 — "therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities" — the covenant relationship that uniquely placed Israel before God is the context in which the iniquity is most damaging; intimate relational proximity intensifies rather than excuses accountability. The relational severing is the iniquity's most consequential effect.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=15946, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The relational pattern reveals that iniquity-as-accumulated-crime is constitutively relational in its definition and its damage: it is crime before God (Psa 90:8 — "before you"), separation from God (Isa 59:2), weariness of God (Isa 43:24), and the basis of covenant-accountability (Amo 3:2). The removal of iniquity is therefore simultaneously a relational restoration: Jer 31:34 — "I will forgive their iniquity, and I will remember their sin no more" — the forgiveness resolves the God-relationship simultaneously with clearing the record; Isa 40:2 — "her iniquity is pardoned... her warfare is ended" — the pardon ends the relational state of warfare.

---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=15917, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — through the self-assessment, concealment, and acknowledgment dimensions. Psa 36:2 — "he flatters himself in his own eyes" — the cognitive self-assessment is the faculty being distorted by pride-generated false evaluation; Job 31:33 — "hiding my iniquity in my heart" — the cognitive choice to conceal; Job 13:23 — "how many are my iniquities?" — the cognitive desire to enumerate and know one's moral-crime account; Jer 14:7 — "though our iniquities testify against us" — the cognitive recognition of iniquity as self-accusing witness.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=15918, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Iniquity *distorts* cognition at the self-assessment level and *clarifies* it when conscience operates correctly. Psa 36:2 — the flattering self-cognitive distortion: the mind generates false conclusions about the self's moral condition. Hos 12:8 — the cognitively confident "I am rich... no iniquity in me" is the distorted verdict. Conversely: 2Sa 24:10 — David's cognitive recognition "I have sinned greatly" is accurate; the conscience's correct functioning produces clear cognitive self-assessment. The iniquity distorts cognition in proportion to the suppression of conscience.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=15919, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals that iniquity-as-accumulated-crime is not cognitively self-disclosing: the person does not automatically know their iniquity-accumulation through unaided reason. Gen 15:16 — the Amorites are presumably unaware their iniquity is being measured toward completion; Jer 16:10 — "what is our iniquity? What is the sin that we have committed?" — the cognitively unaware community cannot identify its own crime without prophetic disclosure. The cognitive faculty requires external input (divine revelation, prophetic word, conscience-strike) to accurately assess the iniquity-account.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=15920, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — centrally through the divine memory register (VCG-03). Hos 13:12 — "the iniquity of Ephraim is bound up; his sin is kept in store" — God's memory is the storage medium for the iniquity-record; Hos 9:9 — "he will remember their iniquity; he will punish their sins" — divine memory is the mechanism that activates judgment; Psa 79:8 — "do not remember against us our former iniquities" — the plea asks God's memory not to function against them. Human memory also engages: Lev 26:40/41 — "confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers" — the generational confession requires memory of ancestral crimes.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=15921, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Iniquity *deepens* divine memory (the crime persists in God's record — Hos 13:12 — sealed) while *impairing* human memory of the iniquity's significance. Jer 16:10 — the community's question "what is our iniquity?" shows that the human memory of the community's own accumulated crime has faded; they cannot remember what they have done. The prophetic tradition's task is to restore the community's memory of its iniquity: Lam 2:14 — "they have not exposed your iniquity to restore your fortunes" — the false prophets failed to bring the iniquity back to communal memory. God's memory of the iniquity and human memory of it are thus inversely proportional in many cases.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=15922, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The memory pattern reveals that iniquity-as-accumulated-crime operates as a two-memory reality: it is perpetually held in God's memory (Hos 13:12 — "kept in store"; Psa 90:8 — "set before you") while simultaneously fading from human memory (Jer 16:10 — community's question). The contrast is the prophetic task: Jer 14:7 — "our iniquities testify against us" — the prophet restores the community's memory of its own crime by giving the iniquity a voice as a witness. The eschatological resolution addresses both memories: Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — God's memory is the one that must ultimately be resolved for the iniquity to be truly gone.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=15923, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — through the weight-experience and the sorrow dimension. Psa 38:4 — "like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me" — the iniquity-weight produces an affective experience of overwhelm; Psa 38:18 — "I am sorry for my sin" — the affect of sorrow accompanies confession of iniquity; Ezr 9:6 — "I am ashamed and blush to lift my face to you" — shame is the primary affective response to the recognised accumulation. Psa 40:12 — "my heart fails me" — the iniquities overtaking the person produce an affective collapse.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=15924, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The iniquity-weight *deepens negative affect* when engaged and *bypasses affect* when suppressed. Psa 38:4/40:12 — the recognised weight produces deepening grief, shame, and overwhelm. Hos 12:8 — "I am rich... no iniquity in me" — the suppressed iniquity produces no negative affect; the person feels prosperous confidence despite accumulating crime. The affect is thus a reliable indicator of conscience-engagement: shame and sorrow (Psa 38:18; Ezr 9:6) accompany honest acknowledgment; false confidence (Hos 12:8) accompanies suppression.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=15925, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The affective pattern reveals that shame (Ezr 9:6) and sorrow (Psa 38:18) are the productive affective responses to iniquity-recognition — they initiate the confession-to-removal pipeline (Lev 26:40/41 — heart-humiliation → confession → divine response). The unproductive affect is the false prosperity-confidence of the iniquity-denier (Hos 12:8). Between these poles: Psa 40:12's heart-failure is the overwhelm-affect of iniquity accumulation without address — not the productive sorrow that leads to confession but the paralysing despair that it has become too much.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=15926, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** S — The 162-verse corpus does not explicitly engage the creative faculty in connection with iniquity as accumulated moral crime. The characteristic operates through conscience, memory, will, and relational accountability rather than through creative origination.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=15927, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** S — No direct evidence. The corpus is silent on the creative faculty's engagement with iniquity as accumulated moral crime.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=15928, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** S — The silence is consistent with CHAR-18's backward-looking nature: the characteristic is the accumulated record of what has been done rather than the forward-looking creative generation of what might be. Noted.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=15929, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — as both the source and the addressed faculty. (1) *Will as source*: Eze 14:3 — "taken their idols into their hearts" — the will's inner choice to receive idols is the volitional origin of the heart-seated iniquity; Jer 16:12 — "because you have done evil, even more than your fathers" — volitional escalation; Psa 106:43 — "they were rebellious in their purposes" — the will's rebellious orientation generates iniquity; (2) *Will as the required faculty for change*: Lev 26:41 — "if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled" — the will must choose humility as the precondition for iniquity-address; Hos 14:2 — "take with you words and return to the Lord; say to him, 'Take away all iniquity'" — the will's initiating speech-act of return.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=15930, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The iniquity *captures and constrains* volition progressively. Pro 5:22 — "the iniquities of the wicked ensnare him, and he is held fast in the cords of his sin" — the accumulated iniquity becomes the cords that bind the will; voluntary choices have produced an involuntary bondage. Eze 18:30 — "lest iniquity be your ruin" — the warning presupposes the will can still turn; before the bondage becomes total, the will retains the capacity to repent. The progression is: free volitional choice of iniquity → accumulation → progressive bondage of will → ruin. The iniquity does not eliminate will immediately but ensnares it progressively.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=15931, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The volitional pattern reveals CHAR-18's most significant inner-being dynamic: accumulated iniquity progressively constrains the will that generated it. The will begins as free agent (choosing iniquity — Eze 14:3), produces the accumulation, and is then bound by what it chose (Pro 5:22 — "held fast in the cords"). This self-generated volitional bondage makes the removal of iniquity require external divine action (Zec 3:4 — "I have taken your iniquity away"; Mic 7:19 — "he will tread our iniquities underfoot") rather than merely the will's renewed effort — the will is no longer fully free to extract itself.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=15932, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Agency is engaged through the active expression of iniquity and the active pursuit of its remedy. (1) *Agency in iniquity's enactment*: Isa 59:3 — "your hands are defiled with blood and your fingers with iniquity" — the hands are the active members of iniquity's enactment; Isa 5:18 — "those who draw iniquity with cords of falsehood, who draw sin as with cart ropes" — active, purposeful pursuit of iniquity; (2) *Agency in iniquity's remedy*: Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity" — the verbal act of confession is an agentive decision; Hos 14:2 — "take with you words and return to the Lord" — the return-initiative requires agentive action.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=15933, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Accumulated iniquity *progressively impairs agency* through the ensnaring mechanism. Pro 5:22 — "he is held fast in the cords of his sin" — the agency that once freely chose iniquity is now constrained; Isa 64:6 — "our iniquities, like the wind, take us away" — the iniquities become an external force acting on the person who was formerly the agent; Isa 64:7 — "have made us melt in the hand of our iniquities" — the hand of iniquity has replaced the person's own agentive hand; the person is now acted upon by their own accumulated crimes.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=15934, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The agency pattern reveals CHAR-18's most distinctive feature: the transformation of the person from active agent of iniquity (Isa 5:18 — drawing iniquity) to passive subject of iniquity's power (Isa 64:6/7 — taken away, melted in its hand). The accumulated iniquity reverses the agency relationship: the person who chose the crime is eventually chosen by it — ensnared (Pro 5:22), swept away (Isa 64:6), and melted (Isa 64:7). Only external divine agency can reverse this (Mic 7:19 — God treads iniquities underfoot; Zec 3:9 — removes iniquity in a single day).

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=15935, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — through both the self-assessment and the divine assessment dimensions. (1) *Divine moral evaluation*: Gen 15:16 — God evaluates the Amorites' iniquity as "not yet complete" — a precise moral assessment; Ezr 9:13 — "God exacted less than our iniquities deserved" — a morally calibrated evaluation of deserved versus received punishment; (2) *Human moral evaluation*: Job 31:11/28 — "that would be an iniquity to be punished by the judges" — Job's moral evaluation categories iniquity as judicial crime; Jer 14:7 — "though our iniquities testify against us" — the prophetic moral evaluation that gives the iniquity a witnessing voice.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=15936, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Iniquity *distorts* the self-directed moral evaluation faculty. Psa 36:2 — "he flatters himself in his own eyes that his iniquity cannot be found out and hated" — the wicked person's self-directed moral evaluation cannot accurately assess the iniquity. Hos 12:8 — "they cannot find in me iniquity" — the moral evaluation of self is systematically skewed by wealth and pride. Conversely, the other-directed moral evaluation (Jer 14:7 — "our iniquities testify against us") is more accurate: the prophetic tradition's evaluation of the community's iniquity is correct. Iniquity impairs self-evaluation; it does not impair accurate evaluation by conscience or prophetic witness.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=15937, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals a paradox: the person most burdened with iniquity is least able to accurately evaluate it (Psa 36:2; Hos 12:8), while the person who honestly evaluates their own iniquity (Job 13:23 — asking God to count it; David in 2Sa 24:10 — "I have sinned greatly") is in the better moral position. The accurate moral evaluation of one's own iniquity-account is itself a moral virtue that the iniquity's accumulation actively erodes. The corrective is always external: prophetic witness (Jer 14:7), divine disclosure (Job 13:23 — "make me know"), and conscience-strike (2Sa 24:10).

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=15938, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Conscience is engaged as a secondary responder (unlike CHAR-17 where it is primary). 2Sa 24:10 — "David's heart struck him after he had numbered the people. And David said to the Lord, 'I have sinned greatly'" — the conscience activates spontaneously in response to recognised iniquity; Psa 38:18 — "I confess my iniquity; I am sorry for my sin" — conscience produces confession and sorrow; Lev 26:40/41 — confession requires the humbled heart that presupposes conscience-engagement.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=15939, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Accumulated iniquity *progressively suppresses* conscience. The VCG-07 self-destructive register shows the trajectory: Psa 36:2 — "flatters himself in his own eyes" — the conscience's capacity for self-assessment is destroyed by sustained iniquity; Hos 12:8 — Ephraim's conscience cannot register the iniquity it carries. The initial iniquity (VCG-04 — personal act) may trigger conscience (2Sa 24:10 — immediate strike); accumulated iniquity (VCG-05 — overwhelming weight) may paralyse conscience; deeply entrenched iniquity (VCG-08 — heart-seated) may silence conscience altogether (Psa 36:2).

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=15940, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The conscience pattern reveals the inverse relationship between iniquity's accumulation and conscience's functionality that distinguishes CHAR-18 from CHAR-17. In CHAR-17, conscience is the primary faculty and its engagement is the characteristic's core function. In CHAR-18, conscience is secondary: the iniquity exists in the divine record (Hos 13:12 — "bound up") independently of whether conscience registers it. The conscience pattern confirms CHAR-18 as objective and CHAR-17 as subjective: CHAR-18's iniquity accumulates regardless of conscience-state; CHAR-17 IS the conscience's response to that accumulation.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=15947, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — God operates toward the iniquity-bearing person in five modes. (1) *Measuring toward threshold*: Gen 15:16 — "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" — God tracks and measures the accumulation; (2) *Seeing and not forgetting*: Psa 90:8 — "you have set our iniquities before you, our secret sins in the light of your presence"; Jer 16:17 — "nor is their iniquity concealed from my eyes"; Hos 13:12 — "bound up; his sin is kept in store"; (3) *Visiting iniquity upon generations*: Exo 20:5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation"; (4) *Punishing at the appointed time*: Isa 26:21 — God comes out to punish the earth's inhabitants for their iniquity; Jer 25:12 — after seventy years, Babylon's iniquity is reckoned; (5) *Pardoning and removing*: Isa 40:2 — "her iniquity is pardoned"; Jer 31:34 — "I will forgive their iniquity, and I will remember their sin no more"; Zec 3:4 — "I have taken your iniquity away from you."

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=15948, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Two bases. (1) *Holiness that cannot ignore accumulated crime*: Psa 130:3 — "if you, O Lord, should mark iniquities, O Lord, who could stand?" — God's holiness is the basis for the marking; Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God" — the separation is the consequence of holiness encountering crime; (2) *Steadfast love and covenant faithfulness that moves toward forgiveness*: Exo 34:7 — "keeping steadfast love for thousands, forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin" — the forgiveness flows from steadfast love; Num 14:18/19 — "the Lord is slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, forgiving iniquity... please pardon the iniquity of this people, according to the greatness of your steadfast love"; Mic 7:18 — "pardoning iniquity... he does not retain his anger forever, because he delights in steadfast love."

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=15949, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — God's disposition is one of *precise moral justice held in tension with enduring steadfast love*. Exo 34:7 — God both forgives iniquity and visits it on children — both responses flow from the same character; Psa 78:38 — "yet he, being compassionate, atoned for their iniquity and did not destroy them; he restrained his anger often" — compassion repeatedly moderates full justice; Job 11:6 — "God exacts of you less than your guilt deserves" — mercy consistently moderates the full punitive application. The disposition is neither indifferent (Hos 13:12 — the record is maintained) nor implacably punitive (Mic 7:18 — he delights in steadfast love). The defining eschatological posture: Jer 50:20 — iniquity shall be sought and none found — total removal is God's ultimate goal.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=15950, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — God-to-human operation (measuring, seeing, visiting, punishing, pardoning) is extensively evidenced across all eight VCGs. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=15951, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — through the acknowledgment-confession-plea movement. Exo 34:9 — Moses pleads "pardon our iniquity and our sin, and take us for your inheritance" — the movement toward God is through honest naming and plea; Psa 38:18 — "I confess my iniquity; I am sorry for my sin" — the voluntary movement toward God through confession; Hos 14:2 — "take with you words and return to the Lord; say to him, 'Take away all iniquity'" — the commanded movement toward God with the iniquity explicitly named; Psa 25:11 — "for your name's sake, O Lord, pardon my guilt, for it is great" — the movement toward God specifically through the acknowledgment of iniquity's greatness as the basis for the plea.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=15952, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Humbled heart*: Lev 26:41 — "if then their uncircumcised heart is humbled and they make amends for their iniquity" — the uncircumcised (unreformed) heart must be humbled before movement toward God becomes possible; (2) *Honest verbal acknowledgment*: Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers"; Hos 14:2 — "take with you words" — the movement requires spoken words; (3) *Non-concealment*: Psa 32:5 — "I did not cover my iniquity; I said, 'I will confess my transgressions to the Lord,' and you forgave the iniquity of my sin" — the movement toward God requires uncovering what was concealed.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=15953, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The human-to-God direction reveals that the God-relationship remains accessible through iniquity-acknowledgment. Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face" — God waits for the acknowledgment; the relationship is suspended rather than terminated. Psa 130:3/4 — "if you, O Lord, should mark iniquities, O Lord, who could stand? But with you there is forgiveness, that you may be feared" — the juxtaposition reveals the relationship's structure: the iniquity-reality would make standing impossible (v.3) but God's forgiveness character makes the approach possible (v.4). Movement toward God under iniquity is possible precisely because God's character is forgiveness.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=15954, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Human-to-God operation (confession, plea, acknowledgment, return) is extensively evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=15955, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — through three extension mechanisms. (1) *Leadership propagation*: 1Sa 3:13/14 — Eli's iniquity extends to his house through his failure to restrain his sons; Lev 4:3 — "if it is the anointed priest who sins, thus bringing guilt on the people"; (2) *Generational transmission*: Exo 20:5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children to the third and fourth generation" — God visits the fathers' iniquity on descendants; Lam 5:7 — "our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities"; (3) *Representative loading*: Lev 16:21 — Aaron "confess over it all the iniquities of the people of Israel, and all their transgressions... and he shall put them on the head of the goat" — the community's iniquity is verbally transferred onto the representative goat.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=15956, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Representative position*: Lev 10:17 — "that you may bear the iniquity of the congregation" — the priests' position as representatives enables them to bear the community's iniquity; Lev 16:21 — Aaron's high-priestly office enables the transferral; 1Sa 3:13 — Eli's position as father and priest makes his iniquity community-affecting; (2) *Ancestral headship*: Exo 20:5 — "the fathers" whose idolatrous hatred of God generates the iniquity that is transmitted; the generational transmission operates through the ancestral position, not merely biological descent.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=15957, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — For priestly-ritual extension (Lev 16:21): Aaron must be fully vested in the high-priestly office and garments before the iniquity-transfer through confession is valid — the office is the prerequisite; Exo 28:38 — "it shall regularly be on his forehead" — the gold plate inscription is prerequisite for bearing iniquity. For generational extension (Exo 20:5): the fathers must have committed the originating idolatrous iniquity — "those who hate me" — the hatred of God is the prerequisite moral condition that generates the transmissible iniquity.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=15958, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — The giving/extension direction is evidenced through leadership propagation (1Sa 3:13; Lev 4:3), generational transmission (Exo 20:5; Lam 5:7), and representative loading (Lev 16:21). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=15959, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — through four reception modes. (1) *Personal incurrence*: Num 15:31 — "his iniquity shall be on him" — iniquity is received through one's own act and attaches to the person; Lev 5:17 — "he shall bear his iniquity" — the individual receives and carries the iniquity arising from their own violation; (2) *Generational inheritance*: Lam 5:7 — "our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities" — the present generation receives the iniquity-burden of deceased ancestors; Jer 32:18 — "you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them"; (3) *Priestly representative receiving*: Lev 10:17 — the priests receive the congregation's iniquity through the sin-offering; Num 18:1 — "you and your sons and your father's house with you shall bear iniquity connected with the sanctuary"; (4) *Servant's vicarious receiving*: Isa 53:6 — "the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all" — the servant receives the iniquity of all through divine appointment.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=15960, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Reception-enabling: (1) *Humbled uncircumcised heart*: Lev 26:41 — heart-humiliation is the precondition; (2) *Honest confession*: Lev 26:40 — verbal acknowledgment of both personal and ancestral iniquity; (3) *Return initiative*: Hos 14:2 — "take with you words and return to the Lord" — the active movement of return. Reception-blocking: (1) *Stiff-necked self-sufficiency*: Hos 12:8 — "I am rich... no iniquity in me" — false confidence blocks remedy; (2) *False prophetic assurance*: Lam 2:14 — "they have not exposed your iniquity to restore your fortunes" — false prophets who conceal rather than expose iniquity block the remedy by preventing honest acknowledgment; (3) *Pride*: Psa 36:2 — self-flattery that cannot see or hate the iniquity forecloses the acknowledgment that remedy requires.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=15961, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three states are evidenced. (1) *Fitness for divine commission*: Isa 6:7 — "your guilt is taken away" immediately precedes Isaiah's commissioning ("here I am! Send me" — Isa 6:8); the iniquity-removed state enables active divine engagement; (2) *Blessed state of not-counted iniquity*: Psa 32:2 — "blessed is the man against whom the Lord counts no iniquity, and in whose spirit there is no deceit" — the blessedness of the cleared account; (3) *Full relational restoration*: Jer 31:34 — "I will forgive their iniquity, and I will remember their sin no more" — the removed-iniquity state is one of complete relational restoration, including God's memory being cleared; Isa 40:2 — "her warfare is ended" — the relational conflict is resolved.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=15962, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced across personal incurrence (Num 15:31), generational inheritance (Lam 5:7), priestly reception (Lev 10:17), and servant's vicarious receiving (Isa 53:6). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=15963, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes. Within the covenant relational bond: Amo 3:2 — "you only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities" — the covenant relationship intensifies rather than excuses accountability; intimate knowledge makes the iniquity more weighty; Isa 43:24 — "you have wearied me with your iniquities" — God's weariness is a covenant-relational experience. Across relational distance: Gen 15:16 — the Amorites' iniquity is tracked by God without explicit covenant relationship; Jer 25:12 — Babylon's iniquity is reckoned after seventy years without covenant standing.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=15964, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The characteristic crosses covenantal boundaries. Gen 15:16 — the Amorites (outside Israel's covenant) accumulate iniquity before God; Jer 25:12 — Babylon's iniquity is measured and punished; Eze 28:18 — "the multitude of your iniquities" is addressed to the king of Tyre (outside Israel's covenant). The characteristic is universal in scope: Psa 130:3 — "if you, O Lord, should mark iniquities, O Lord, who could stand?" — the universality implies all persons, not merely covenant members, accumulate iniquity before God.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=15965, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The relational scope is universal and includes the non-human realm. Lev 18:25 — "the land became unclean, so that I punished its iniquity, and the land vomited out its inhabitants" — the land itself carries and is punished for the iniquity of its inhabitants; Isa 24:6 — "inhabitants suffer for their guilt; therefore the inhabitants of the earth are scorched" — universal human scope; Gen 15:16 — all nations accumulate iniquity before God. The relational scope extends from the individual soul (Eze 18:20 — "the soul who sins"), through the family (Exo 20:5), community (Amo 3:2), nation (Jer 25:12), and creation (Lev 18:25).

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=15966, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Relational scope (universal — individuals, families, nations, creation) is extensively evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=15967, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-18]** S — The 162-verse corpus does not explicitly name angelic or adversarial spiritual beings in connection with iniquity-as-accumulated-moral-crime as its primary agent. The characteristic operates within the direct human-God moral relationship. The seraph in Isa 6:7 (touching Isaiah's lips to remove guilt) is the nearest instance, but this falls in CHAR-17's removal dimension; the CHAR-18 iniquity-accumulation corpus does not feature named spiritual beings mediating its accumulation or maintenance.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=15968, S) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-18]** S — The corpus is silent on adversarial spiritual agency in connection with iniquity-as-accumulated-crime. The iniquity originates in human moral choices (Eze 14:3 — heart-seated idolatry; Isa 5:18 — drawn with cords of falsehood) and is maintained in God's record (Hos 13:12) without named adversarial involvement. The adversarial dimension is entirely human-to-God, not mediated by named spiritual adversaries in this corpus.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=15969, E) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Partly — through the Zec 3:4 removal instance. Zec 3:4 — "the angel said to those who were standing before him, 'Remove the filthy garments from him.' And to him he said, 'Behold, I have taken your iniquity away from you'" — the angelic mediator pronounces and effects Joshua's iniquity-removal. This is the sole angelic-mediation instance in the corpus, and it is for iniquity-removal (VCG-06) rather than iniquity-communication or accumulation.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=15970, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Adversarial spiritual beings (T4.6.1/T4.6.2) are entirely absent from the corpus. Angelic ministry (T4.6.3) appears in one instance — Zec 3:4 — as the agent of iniquity-removal, not accumulation. The characteristic operates as a direct human-God moral transaction; angelic involvement is singular and removal-directed.

---

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=15971, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Both — in the negative direction and through its removal. (1) *Negative condition change*: Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God" — the accumulation transforms the person's God-relational condition from proximity to separation; Psa 31:10 — "my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away" — physical condition is transformed by the iniquity's burden; (2) *Negative orientation change*: Isa 64:6/7 — "our iniquities, like the wind, take us away... have made us melt in the hand of our iniquities" — the person's orientation is swept and melted away from God; (3) *Positive transformation through removal*: Isa 40:2 — "her warfare is ended, her iniquity is pardoned" — the pardon transforms the condition from warfare to peace; Jer 31:34 — "I will forgive their iniquity" — the new covenant transformation produces a condition of complete pardon.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=15972, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Both dimensions evidenced. (1) *Reversible through divine action*: Jer 50:20 — "iniquity shall be sought in Israel, and there shall be none" — the iniquity record is entirely erasable; Zec 3:9 — "I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day"; Mic 7:19 — "he will tread our iniquities underfoot"; (2) *Eschatologically irreversible for some*: Isa 22:14 — "surely this iniquity will not be atoned for you until you die" — within the person's lifetime, no reversal; 1Sa 3:14 — "the iniquity of Eli's house shall not be atoned for by sacrifice or offering forever" — beyond sacrifice; (3) *Progressively deepening without address*: 2Ch 33:23 (cross-ref) — "incurred guilt more and more" — the accumulation without address deepens toward irreversibility.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=15973, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Transformation evidence is extensive in all directions (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=15974, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — two opposing sequences. (1) *Accumulation-to-ruin sequence*: heart takes in idols (Eze 14:3 — VCG-08 origin) → outer sinning acts (VCG-04 — personal crime) → divine record maintained (VCG-03 — Hos 13:12 — "bound up") → weight accumulates (VCG-05 — Psa 38:4 — "gone over my head") → self-destructive force (VCG-07 — Pro 5:22 — "held fast in cords") → ruin (Eze 18:30 — "lest iniquity be your ruin"); (2) *Confession-to-removal sequence*: iniquity acknowledged (Lev 26:40) → heart humbled (Lev 26:41) → priestly bearing (VCG-01 — Lev 16:21) → divine forgiveness (VCG-06 — Isa 40:2; Jer 31:34) → blessed state (Psa 32:2 — "no iniquity counted against him").

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=15975, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The accumulation sequence reveals CHAR-18's most distinctive inner-being dynamic: the progressive constitution of the person by their own iniquity. The sequence runs from originating inner choice (Eze 14:3 — heart-level allegiance) through accumulation in the divine record (Hos 13:12 — sealed) through felt weight (Psa 38:4) through self-destructive bondage (Pro 5:22) to ruin. Each stage represents a deeper constitutional entanglement with the iniquity. The removal sequence reveals that the reversal requires engaging each stage in reverse: acknowledging (confession), humbling (heart), addressing through priestly mediation (VCG-01), receiving divine pardon (VCG-06).

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=15976, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Sequence is the defining structural feature of CHAR-18 across its 8-VCG architecture. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=15977, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three change-mechanisms. (1) *Quantitative accumulation toward threshold*: Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete" — the accumulation mechanism measures crime toward a divinely appointed tipping point; when the threshold is reached, judgment falls; (2) *Self-ensnaring bondage*: Pro 5:22 — "the iniquities of the wicked ensnare him, and he is held fast in the cords of his sin" — the accumulated acts become the very restraints binding the will; the mechanism is self-generated bondage; (3) *Separation-barrier creation*: Isa 59:2 — "your iniquities have made a separation" — the accumulated iniquity acts as a structural barrier in the God-relationship; the mechanism is relational distance-creation.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=15978, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes across VCG registers. (1) *VCG-03 (divine memory)*: mechanism is divine-record preservation — Hos 13:12 — "bound up and kept in store" — God's memory is the mechanism that maintains the iniquity's juridical standing; (2) *VCG-02 (generational)*: mechanism is familial transmission — Exo 20:5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers on the children" — the generational line is the transmission medium; (3) *VCG-07 (self-destructive)*: mechanism is self-consuming ruination — Eze 18:30 — "lest iniquity be your ruin" — the iniquity turns destructively on its bearer; (4) *VCG-08 (heart-seated)*: mechanism is heart-level allegiance capture — Eze 14:3 — idols in the heart creating the stumbling block — the mechanism operates at the innermost level of will.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=15979, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Mechanisms are evidenced across all major VCG contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=15980, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — extensively. (1) *Suffering as iniquity's consequence*: Ezr 9:7 — "for our iniquities we... have been given into the hand of the kings of the lands, to the sword, to captivity, to plundering, and to utter shame" — captivity and sword are the accumulated iniquity's suffered consequences; Isa 50:1 — "for your iniquities you were sold" — national exile is iniquity's direct cost; Psa 107:17 — "some were fools through their sinful ways, and because of their iniquities suffered affliction" — affliction as personal consequence; (2) *Iniquity as measured by suffering*: Ezr 9:13 — "you have punished us less than our iniquities deserved" — the suffering is calibrated against the iniquity's weight.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=15981, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Suffering *reveals* iniquity (Hos 7:1 — "when I would heal Israel, the iniquity of Ephraim is revealed") and *is proportioned to* it (Ezr 9:13). Suffering does not produce the iniquity but serves as its diagnostic indicator: the affliction (Isa 50:1 — exile) reveals what the accumulated crime was. Jer 13:22 — "it is for the greatness of your iniquity that your skirts are lifted up and you suffer violence" — the greatness of the iniquity is indexed to the magnitude of the suffering. Gen 42:21 (CHAR-17 crossover) — the brothers' suffering leads them to recognise their past guilt; the suffering triggers iniquity-recognition but does not create the iniquity.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=15982, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — The relationship between iniquity and suffering is extensively evidenced (Ezr 9:7/13; Isa 50:1; Psa 107:17; Jer 13:22; Hos 7:1). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=15983, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — as the negative formation arc's primary driver. The accumulation-to-ruin VCG sequence (VCG-08 heart origin → VCG-04 personal crime → VCG-05 overwhelming weight → VCG-07 self-destruction) is itself a character formation arc: the person is progressively constituted by their accumulated iniquity into the ruined character type. Isa 1:4 — "a people laden with iniquity, offspring of evildoers" — the iniquity-laden condition is a generationally formed character; Psa 51:5 — "I was brought forth in iniquity" — the formation begins at birth within the iniquity-condition. Conversely, Mal 2:6 — "he turned many from iniquity" — the faithful priest's character is formed partly by actively redirecting others from the iniquity-path.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=15984, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Iniquity-as-accumulated-crime functions as the *negative formation baseline* from which all positive character formation must begin. Psa 51:5 — "brought forth in iniquity" — names the constitutional starting-point of fallen human formation; every character arc begins within the iniquity-condition. The Lev 26:39-43 sequence — "rot away in their enemies' lands because of their iniquity" → "if they confess their iniquity... and their uncircumcised heart is humbled... and they make amends" — shows iniquity as the negative formation state whose address (confession, humbling, amends) forms the person into a reconciled character capable of restored covenant relationship.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=15985, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Formation evidence is present through both the negative arc (Psa 51:5; Isa 1:4; Lev 26:39) and the positive counter-arc (Lev 26:40/41/43; Mal 2:6). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=15986, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — two eschatological endpoints. (1) *Judgment enforcement of accumulated iniquity*: Gen 15:16 — the Amorites' accumulation is moving toward its appointed threshold; Isa 13:11 — "I will punish the world for its evil, and the wicked for their iniquity"; Isa 26:21 — the Lord coming to punish the earth's inhabitants for their iniquity; (2) *Total removal*: Jer 50:20 — "iniquity shall be sought in Israel, and there shall be none... for I will pardon those whom I leave as a remnant" — the eschatological fullness is the complete dissolution of the iniquity-record; Zec 3:9 — "I will remove the iniquity of this land in a single day"; Jer 31:34 — "I will remember their sin no more" — God's memory of iniquity permanently cleared.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=15987, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-18]** E — For the unaddressed accumulation: Gen 15:16's "not yet complete" anticipates the eschatological day when it is complete and judgment falls; Hos 13:12's "bound up and kept in store" anticipates the day when the sealed record is opened for judgment. For the addressed iniquity: Zec 3:4's "I have taken your iniquity away" anticipates Zec 3:9's single-day removal for the whole land — individual removals point toward the eschatological total removal; Isa 53:6's "the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all" anticipates the new covenant's comprehensive forgiveness (Jer 31:34).

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=15988, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced in both judgment (Gen 15:16; Isa 13:11/26:21) and total removal (Jer 50:20; Zec 3:9; Jer 31:34) directions. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=15989, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-18]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: divine-record deposit (Hos 13:12 — bound up), bodily deposit (Eze 32:27 — on bones), generational deposit (Exo 20:5 — visited on children). Developmental consequences. (1) *Divine-record deposit*: Hos 13:12's sealed iniquity develops toward the moment of divine recall for judgment (Hos 9:9 — "he will remember their iniquity; he will punish their sins") — the sealed deposit is developing toward its appointed reckoning; Gen 15:16 — toward completion; (2) *Bodily deposit*: Psa 31:10 — "my strength fails because of my iniquity, and my bones waste away" — the developmental consequence is progressive bodily deterioration; the bodily deposit deepens into physical ruin; (3) *Generational deposit*: Lam 5:7 — "our fathers sinned, and are no more; and we bear their iniquities" — the developmental consequence is that each successive generation receives and must address an inherited moral-crime burden alongside their own.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=15990, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — most extensively of any M10 characteristic studied. Exo 20:5/34:7; Deu 5:9; Num 14:18 — the third-and-fourth-generation formula names the temporal extent of the generational consequence; Lev 26:39 — "because of their iniquity, and also because of the iniquities of their fathers they shall rot away like them" — the rotting is compounded across generations; Lam 5:7 — present generation bears fathers' iniquities; Jer 32:18 — "you repay the guilt of fathers to their children after them"; Isa 65:7 — "both your iniquities and your fathers' iniquities together." The generational consequence of iniquity is the most densely evidenced inter-generational moral dynamic in M10.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=15991, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-18]** E — T2.8 found extensive deposit evidence. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. The generational consequence register (VCG-02 — 18 verses) is CHAR-18's most distinctive contribution to M10's inter-generational theology. This prompt is superseded.

---
