Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 15:16; Exo 20:5/34:7; Deu 5:9; Num 14:18; Lev 26:39/40/41; 1Sa 3:14; Psa 31:10/32:2/38:4/51:5/107:17; Pro 5:22; Isa 1:4/13:11/26:21/40:2/50:1/53:6/59:2/64:6/7; Jer 13:22/31:34/32:18/50:20; Lam 5:7; Eze 14:3/18:30/32:27; Hos 7:1/9:9/13:12; Zec 3:4/9; Jer 31:34; Ezr 9:7/13; Mal 2:6; Isa 65:7 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.


---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=15992, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur within the 162-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record)*: Hos 13:12 — "bound up; his sin is kept in store" — iniquity-as-crime is held in the same divine record that CHAR-13 describes; Psa 90:8 — "you have set our iniquities before you, our secret sins in the light of your presence" — both CHAR-18's iniquity and secret sins are before God simultaneously; (2) *CHAR-15 (Generational sin)*: VCG-02 (18 verses — Exo 20:5; Lam 5:7) — the generational iniquity register overlaps directly with CHAR-15's transmission concern; (3) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received)*: VCG-06 (21 verses — Isa 40:2; Jer 31:34; Mic 7:18/19) — the pardon-and-removal register is the direct receiving-side of CHAR-14; (4) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Lev 16:21 — "confess over it all the iniquities"; Lev 26:40 — "confess their iniquity"; Psa 32:5 — "I did not cover my iniquity... I will confess" — confession is consistently paired with iniquity.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=15993, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-18 as the *objective-accumulation face* of M10: while CHAR-17 is the subjective-experiential face (the felt guilt), CHAR-18 is the objective moral-crime account that persists in God's record regardless of subjective awareness. CHAR-18 is simultaneously the content of CHAR-13's record (what is accumulated before God), the source of CHAR-15's generational concern (the transmitted moral heritage), the basis of CHAR-14's forgiveness transaction (what is pardoned), and the object of CHAR-3's confession (what is named before God). CHAR-18 is the substantive moral-crime content that all other M10 characteristics address, experience, transmit, or resolve.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=15994, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are extensively evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=15995, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-18]** E — CHAR-18 consistently *precedes* CHAR-3 (confession names the iniquity), CHAR-14 (forgiveness addresses the iniquity-debt), and CHAR-17 (the guilt-experience is the subjective response to the iniquity-reality). CHAR-18 consistently *follows* CHAR-1 (wilful sinning acts that generate the iniquity-accumulation) and CHAR-6 (habitual defection that compounds it). CHAR-18 consistently *accompanies* CHAR-13 (the divine record that holds what CHAR-18 accumulates). The sequence: CHAR-1 (acts) → CHAR-18 (accumulated in record) → CHAR-17 (felt) → CHAR-3 (confessed) → CHAR-14 (forgiven).

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=15996, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The relationship with CHAR-1 is *developmental*: sinning acts develop into the accumulated iniquity-store. The relationship with CHAR-17 is *constitutive*: CHAR-18 is the objective reality; CHAR-17 is the subjective experience of the same reality — the two are the two faces of one moral condition. The relationship with CHAR-3 is *causal*: the recognised iniquity causally generates the confession — Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity"; Psa 32:5 — "I did not cover my iniquity; I said, 'I will confess.'" The relationship with CHAR-14 is *causal*: the iniquity is what CHAR-14's forgiveness resolves — Isa 40:2 — "her iniquity is pardoned."

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=15997, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Sequential patterns (developmental, constitutive, causal) are extensively evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=15998, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: the objective iniquity-accumulation produces the subjective guilt-experience; Psa 38:4 — "my iniquities have gone over my head; like a heavy burden, they are too heavy for me" — the felt weight IS CHAR-17 being produced by CHAR-18's accumulation; (2) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Lev 26:40 — iniquity recognition produces confession; Psa 32:5 — "I did not cover my iniquity... I will confess"; (3) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought)*: the recognised iniquity produces the seeking of forgiveness — Psa 25:11 — "pardon my guilt, for it is great"; Hos 14:2 — "take away all iniquity."

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=15999, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: the discrete sinning acts generate the iniquity-accumulation; Jer 3:13 — "acknowledge your guilt, that you rebelled" — the rebellion (CHAR-1) produces the iniquity (CHAR-18); (2) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: Psa 106:43 — "they were rebellious in their purposes and were brought low through their iniquity" — habitual rebellious purposes generate accumulated iniquity; (3) *CHAR-15 (Generational sin transmission)*: the generational transmission mechanism (CHAR-15) is how VCG-02's generational iniquity reaches the present generation; CHAR-15's mechanism produces CHAR-18's generational deposit in each new generation.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=16000, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-18]** E — CHAR-18 is the *content dimension* of CHAR-13 (sin as divine record): CHAR-13's divine record contains what CHAR-18 names — the accumulated moral crimes that God holds in memory. CHAR-18 is what fills CHAR-13's record. CHAR-18's VCG-02 (generational iniquity — 18 verses) partially overlaps with CHAR-15's concern, but CHAR-15 focuses on the transmission mechanism while CHAR-18 focuses on what is transmitted (the iniquity-content). CHAR-18's VCG-06 (pardon and removal — 21 verses) is the resolution-register that CHAR-14 addresses from the seeking/receiving side.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=16001, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are extensively evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=16002, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The entire 162-verse corpus uses a single term: a.von (H5771G). This root is shared across M10 with CHAR-17 (which uses a.von H5771H — guilt-liability sense; and H5771I — punishment-consequence sense). The H5771G crime-sense that defines CHAR-18 is the broadest and most fundamental a.von sense. No NT Greek vocabulary appears in this corpus; the characteristic is exclusively OT Hebrew, sharing the a.von root-family with CHAR-17 but via a distinct sub-entry.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=16003, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — the a.von root (*ʿāwôn*) spans all three M10 sub-entries: H5771G (crime — CHAR-18), H5771H (guilt/liability — CHAR-17 VCG-03), H5771I (punishment/consequence — CHAR-17 VCG-04). The single root architecture connects CHAR-18's crime-content with CHAR-17's guilt-experience and punishment-consequence dimensions: the crime (CHAR-18), its inner experience of guilt (CHAR-17 H5771H), and its punitive consequence (CHAR-17 H5771I) are all grammatically unified under one root family. CHAR-18 is the H5771G sub-entry — the crime itself, before the guilt-experience and punishment-consequence.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=16004, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The shared a.von root reveals that CHAR-18 (crime) and CHAR-17 (guilt/punishment) are the same moral reality named at different levels. The a.von root covers the entire arc: the crime committed (CHAR-18 — H5771G), the guilt-liability it generates (CHAR-17 — H5771H), and the punitive-consequence it deserves (CHAR-17 — H5771I). This root-architecture confirms what T6.2's constitutive relationship finding names: CHAR-18 and CHAR-17 are two faces of the same moral reality, distinguished by the crime-dimension (objective, accumulated, in the divine record — CHAR-18) and the guilt-experience dimension (subjective, conscience-registered — CHAR-17).

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=16005, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Extensive root-level sharing (a.von family across CHAR-18 and CHAR-17) is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=16006, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-18]** E — CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record) most closely resembles CHAR-18 — both concern the accumulated moral account before God. The precise distinction: CHAR-13 is the *divine record*-characteristic — the account held in God's memory encompassing all sin-categories; CHAR-18 is one *content-type* within that record — specifically the crime-dimension of accumulated moral wrongdoing (a.von H5771G). CHAR-13 is the container; CHAR-18 is the primary content. Psa 90:8 — "you have set our iniquities before you" — the "before you" (CHAR-13's record) holds the iniquities (CHAR-18's content).

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=16007, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The overlap is at Hos 13:12 — "the iniquity of Ephraim is bound up; his sin is kept in store" — where CHAR-18's accumulated crime (a.von H5771G — "iniquity") and CHAR-13's record-keeping ("kept in store") converge in a single verse. The precise boundary: "the iniquity of Ephraim is bound up" (CHAR-18 — the crime-content that is bound) and "his sin is kept in store" (CHAR-13 — the record-keeping mechanism that stores it). CHAR-18 names what is accumulated; CHAR-13 names the divine-record system that holds it.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=16008, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The distinction is one of *content vs. container*: CHAR-13 is the divine-record system (the "kept in store" mechanism, the divine memory that holds all moral failures); CHAR-18 is the specific crime-content (the a.von H5771G accumulation) that is the primary content of that record. The distinction is not one of degree (both address the full weight of moral accumulation) but of level: CHAR-13 is at the system level (how God holds moral accounts); CHAR-18 is at the content level (what the primary moral-crime content is that the system holds).

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=16009, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=16010, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Several. (1) Lev 16:21/22 — Aaron confessing Israel's iniquities over the scapegoat — shared with CHAR-1 (the sinning acts being confessed), CHAR-3 (the confession act itself), CHAR-14 (the atonement mechanism); (2) Isa 53:5/6/11 — servant bearing our iniquities — shared with CHAR-14 (the forgiveness received through the servant) and CHAR-13 (the divine record cleared through vicarious bearing); (3) Exo 20:5 — "visiting the iniquity of the fathers" — shared with CHAR-15 (generational sin transmission mechanism); (4) Psa 51:5 — "brought forth in iniquity" — shared with CHAR-11 (sin as universal condition); (5) Jer 31:34 — "I will forgive their iniquity" — shared with CHAR-14 (new covenant forgiveness).

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=16011, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Lev 16:21/22 shared with CHAR-3, CHAR-14 confirms that the priestly confession-and-transfer mechanism addresses CHAR-18's accumulated crime, CHAR-3's confession, and CHAR-14's forgiveness simultaneously — they are the same atonement-event seen from three perspectives. Isa 53:5/6/11 shared with CHAR-14 confirms that the servant's iniquity-bearing is the ultimate forgiveness-event that CHAR-14 names as received: the iniquity (CHAR-18) is what the servant bears, which constitutes the forgiveness (CHAR-14). Psa 51:5 shared with CHAR-11 confirms that CHAR-18's accumulated iniquity-condition is the characterological expression of CHAR-11's universal sin-condition.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=16012, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified across CHAR-3, CHAR-11, CHAR-13, CHAR-14, CHAR-15 (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=16013, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three dimensions are shared: (1) *The divine-record dimension* (iniquity held in God's memory) is shared with CHAR-13; (2) *The generational-transmission dimension* (VCG-02) is shared with CHAR-15; (3) *The forgiveness-resolution dimension* (VCG-06 — pardon and removal) is shared with CHAR-14. CHAR-18's unique dimensions — (a) the objective-crime accumulation toward divine threshold (Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete"), (b) the heart-seated origin (VCG-08 — Eze 14:3), and (c) the self-destructive-force register (VCG-07 — Pro 5:22) — are not shared with any other M10 characteristic studied. These three constitute CHAR-18's irreducible analytical contribution.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=16014, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-18 as the *substantive moral-crime foundation* of M10: its shared dimensions all point to what other characteristics do with the iniquity it names (CHAR-13 holds it, CHAR-15 transmits it, CHAR-14 forgives it). CHAR-18's unique dimensions (accumulation-to-threshold, heart-seated origin, self-destructive force) are what make the iniquity itself distinct from mere acts (CHAR-1) or felt guilt (CHAR-17): it is a measured, heart-originated, self-destructive crime-accumulation — a category of moral reality that requires its own characteristic to describe.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=16015, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-18]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced across three adjacent characteristics (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T6 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T6.1.1–T6.1.3, T6.2.1–T6.2.3, T6.3.1–T6.3.4, T6.4.1–T6.4.4, T6.5.1–T6.5.4, T6.6.1–T6.6.3, T6.7.1–T6.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-18] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 15:16; Exo 20:5; Lev 16:21/22/26:40; Psa 25:11/32:5/38:4/51:5/90:8/106:43; Pro 5:22; Isa 40:2/53:5/6/11; Jer 31:34; Lam 5:7; Eze 14:3; Hos 13:12/14:2; Mic 7:18/19; Jer 3:13 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T7.

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=16016, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Single primary term throughout: **a.von (H5771G)** — the crime/moral-wrong sub-entry. The root (*ʿāwôn*) derives from a verbal root meaning "to be bent, crooked, twisted" — naming sin as moral deviance from the straight path. In its H5771G crime-sense, the noun names the accumulated moral wrongdoing itself — the twisted inner reality — not merely the act or the punishment. The root's "crookedness" metaphor is significant: iniquity is the constitutive moral distortion of the person's inner orientation, not merely an external act. The "crime" register distinguishes H5771G from the guilt-register (H5771H — the moral liability arising from the crime) and the punishment-register (H5771I — the penal consequence). No NT Greek appears in this 162-verse corpus.

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=16017, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-18]** E — A.von (H5771G) functions exclusively as a noun throughout the corpus, spanning singular and plural forms. (1) *Singular — personal*: Psa 32:5 — "I did not cover my iniquity" (*ʿăwōnî* — "my iniquity"); Eze 18:20 — "the wickedness of the wicked shall be upon himself" (*ʿăwōn* — singular noun); (2) *Plural — accumulated*: Psa 38:4 — "my iniquities have gone over my head" (*ʿăwōnōtāy* — "my iniquities" plural); Isa 53:6 — "the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all" (singular collective); Ezr 9:6 — "our iniquities have risen higher than our heads" (plural); (3) *Construct state — relational*: Gen 15:16 — "the iniquity of the Amorites" (*ʿăwōn hāʾĕmōrî* — genitive construct naming whose iniquity); Exo 20:5 — "iniquity of the fathers" (genitive). The grammatical range from singular personal to plural accumulated to collective universal (Isa 53:6) covers the full scope of the characteristic.

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=16018, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-18]** E — A.von (H5771G) within CHAR-18 covers: (1) *Measurable accumulation with threshold*: Gen 15:16 — "not yet complete" — quantifiable moral-crime buildup; (2) *Heart-seated crime*: Eze 14:3 — "stumbling block of their iniquity" rooted in idol-taking into the heart; (3) *Generationally transmitted crime*: Exo 20:5 — "iniquity of the fathers on the children"; (4) *Individually owned crime*: Eze 18:20 — "upon himself" — personal moral-legal attachment; (5) *Overwhelming weight*: Psa 38:4 — "gone over my head; like a heavy burden"; (6) *Self-destructive force*: Pro 5:22 — ensnaring cords; Isa 64:6 — wind carrying away; (7) *Vicariously borne*: Isa 53:6 — laid on the servant. The single term covers seven semantically distinct dimensions within the crime-register.

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=16019, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The 162-verse corpus uses a.von (H5771G) exclusively without supplementary terms that distinguish its aspects; the semantic range is carried by context and VCG structure rather than by distinct term-sets. Within individual verses, a.von is often paired with complementary terms: Exo 34:7 — "forgiving iniquity (*a.von*) and transgression (*pĕšaʿ*) and sin (*ḥaṭṭāʾt*)" — the three-term formula distinguishes iniquity (accumulated crime) from transgression (rebellious act) and sin (act of missing the mark), but CHAR-18 covers only the a.von H5771G register within this pairing.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=16020, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The structural opposite is named through negation and forgiveness vocabulary. Jer 50:20 — "iniquity shall be sought in Israel, and there shall be none" — the structural opposite is the state of no-iniquity-present; Psa 32:2 — "the man against whom the Lord counts no iniquity" — the not-counted state; Psa 18:23/2Sa 22:24 — "I kept myself from my guilt (*ʿāwōn*)" — the disciplined avoidance of iniquity. The structural opposite is thus named through negative constructions of the same term rather than through a distinct antonym.

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=16021, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — No dedicated person-type term within the a.von H5771G corpus; the characteristic is content-focused (the crime-reality) rather than person-type-focused (compare CHAR-16's chat.ta/hamartōlos agent-noun). The nearest person-type usage: Isa 1:4 — "a people laden with iniquity (*ʿăwōn*)" — describing the person/community by their iniquity-burden. The person-type dimension is expressed through the adjective or participial: Eze 33:8 — "you shall surely die" addressed to *rāšāʿ* (the wicked) who "shall die in his iniquity" — the wicked person is named separately from the iniquity they carry.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=16022, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Yes — within the iniquity-removal plea complex. Hos 14:2 — "take away all iniquity" (*śāʾ kol-ʿāwōn*) — the specific plea for iniquity-removal uses *śāʾ* (to lift, to take away); Psa 25:11 — "pardon my guilt" (*sālaḥtā laʿăwōnî* — "forgive my iniquity"); Exo 34:9 — "pardon our iniquity" — the supplication vocabulary for iniquity-removal consistently uses lifting/forgiving terms applied directly to a.von. The supplication is for removal of what CHAR-18 names.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=16023, S) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-18]** S — The corpus contains no NT Greek vocabulary. The characteristic is exclusively OT Hebrew. The a.von H5771G crime-register does not directly correspond to a single NT Greek term; NT equivalents (anomia — lawlessness; adikia — unrighteousness; hamartia — sin) distribute the semantic field differently. The absence of NT vocabulary in this corpus reflects the OT nature of the iniquity-as-crime concept in its full development. The Isa 53:5/6/11 servant-bearing passages are the theological bridge toward NT soteriology but use OT a.von terminology exclusively.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=16024, S) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-18]** S — No NT vocabulary appears in the corpus. This prompt cannot be addressed from the available verse-evidence. Noted.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=16025, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-18]** E — The vocabulary arc — a single term (a.von H5771G) across 162 verses from Gen 15:16 (Amorites' iniquity not yet complete — measurability) through Exo 20:5 (generational transmission) through Lev 16:21/22 (priestly scapegoat ritual — transference) through Psa 38:4 (overwhelming weight) through Isa 53:5/6/11 (servant bearing the iniquity of all — typological climax) through Jer 31:34 (new covenant forgiveness — eschatological resolution) through Zec 3:9 (iniquity of the land removed in a single day) to Mal 2:6 (faithful priest turning many from iniquity) — reveals the full moral-crime arc of accumulated iniquity in OT theology: from its measurable accumulation (Gen 15:16) through its overwhelming weight (Psa 38:4) to its vicarious bearing by the servant (Isa 53) to its new covenant dissolution (Jer 31:34). The single-term consistency across the full arc confirms CHAR-18 as the OT's primary vocabulary for the objective moral-crime accumulation that culminates in the servant's bearing.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=16026, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Three primary anchors. (1) *Gen 15:16* — "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" — a.von functions as the subject of the measurability statement; the iniquity is the entity being measured toward its threshold; the "not yet complete" (*lōʾ šālēm*) establishes the accumulation-toward-threshold mechanism. (2) *Eze 18:20* — "the wickedness of the wicked shall be upon himself" — a.von functions as the personal-attachment predicate; the iniquity is what adheres to the responsible individual. (3) *Isa 53:6* — "the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all" — a.von functions as the object of divine placement; the collective human iniquity is the content transferred to the servant.

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=16027, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Five literary forms. (1) *Legal-cultic code* (Lev 5:1/17/7:18; Num 5:15/31; 18:1/23): the Levitical law frames iniquity as a cultic liability requiring prescribed address; (2) *Covenant proclamation* (Exo 20:5/34:7; Deu 5:9): the Decalogue context frames iniquity within God's self-description as jealous — the most authoritative frame; (3) *Wisdom literature* (Job 7:21/10:6/13:23/15:5/31:11/33; Pro 5:22; Psa 32/38/90): requires reading within the lament, reflection, and didactic wisdom contexts; (4) *Prophetic oracle* (Isa 1:4/53:5/6/11/59:2; Jer 14:7/31:34; Eze 14:3/4/7/18:20; Hos 13:12; Mic 7:18/19): requires reading within covenant-lawsuit, judgment-oracle, and restoration-promise frameworks; (5) *Confessional prayer* (Ezr 9:6/7/13; Neh 9:2; Psa 51:2/5): requires reading as corporate penitential confession within the post-exilic restoration context.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=16028, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Four key argument structures. (1) *Gen 15:16's threshold argument*: premise — the Amorites' iniquity is accumulating; conclusion — judgment withheld until completion; structure — accumulation triggers threshold-judgment; (2) *Eze 18:20's personal-accountability argument*: premise — the soul who sins shall die; conclusion — the son shall not suffer for the father's iniquity; structure — crime attaches exclusively to the individual committing it (Eze 18 counter-principle to Exo 20:5's generational visiting); (3) *Isa 53:5/6/11's vicarious-bearing argument*: premise — our iniquities crushed him; the Lord laid on him the iniquity of us all; conclusion — by bearing our iniquities the servant makes many righteous; structure — guilt-transfer through representative suffering; (4) *Psa 130:3/4's holiness-forgiveness tension argument*: premise — if God fully marked iniquities, no one could stand; but — God is a forgiver; conclusion — forgiveness is what makes God-relationship possible; structure — iniquity's weight and God's forgiveness character in productive tension.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=16029, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Four contextual settings. (1) *Priestly-tabernacle context* (Lev 16:21/22; Num 18:1): the Day of Atonement as the annual setting for Israel's iniquity-bearing and removal; (2) *Covenant-proclamation context* (Exo 20:5 — Sinai theophany; Exo 34:7 — second tablets): the foundational moment of God's self-revelation as the one who both forgives and visits iniquity; (3) *Exile and return context* (Ezr 9:6/7; Neh 9:2; Lam 5:7; Jer 31:34): the post-exilic community processing accumulated generational iniquity and anticipating new covenant resolution; (4) *Prophetic-servant context* (Isa 53:5/6/11; Isa 40:2): the servant-poetry as the theological climax of the iniquity-bearing tradition.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=16030, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Two complementary primary anchors, each for a distinct register. (1) *Gen 15:16* — "the iniquity of the Amorites is not yet complete" — primary anchor for the measurable-accumulation register: it most fully reveals iniquity as a quantified moral-crime buildup moving toward a divinely calibrated threshold; (2) *Isa 53:6* — "all we like sheep have gone astray; we have turned — every one — to his own way; and the Lord has laid on him the iniquity of us all" — primary anchor for the iniquity-bearing register and the typological pinnacle: it names universal scope ("all"), universal self-directedness ("every one — to his own way"), and divine resolution ("the Lord has laid on him").

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=16031, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Gen 15:16 uniquely reveals the *divine accounting of iniquity as a threshold-measured process*: iniquity is not merely present but has a completion-point that God tracks precisely — "not yet complete" implies a specific measure at which it will be complete. No other verse in the 162-verse corpus names the iniquity-accumulation process this explicitly in terms of measured incompleteness-toward-completion. Isa 53:6 uniquely reveals the *triple universality of the iniquity-bearing resolution*: universal sheep-straying ("all we like sheep"), universal self-directedness ("every one — to his own way"), and universal transfer ("iniquity of us all") — three layers of universality in a single sentence naming the complete scope of CHAR-18's resolution.

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=16032, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-18]** E — **Social psychology of moral disengagement** (Albert Bandura's moral disengagement theory) and **legal-anthropological frameworks for crime and pollution** (Mary Douglas's *Purity and Danger*; Paul Ricoeur's *The Symbolism of Evil* — specifically the "defilement" and "sin" registers) provide the most illuminating lenses. Bandura illuminates the self-deception mechanisms (Psa 36:2 — self-flattery that blocks iniquity-perception; Hos 12:8 — moral disengagement through prosperity-rationalisation). Douglas's pollution framework illuminates why the land itself is affected (Lev 18:25 — land vomiting out inhabitants) and why iniquity requires ritual address (Lev 16:21/22 — scapegoat) — the moral crime operates as a contagious pollution requiring removal.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=16033, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Bandura's moral disengagement illuminates Psa 36:2's "flatters himself in his own eyes" as a classic moral disengagement mechanism — the wicked person reconstructs his self-image to neutralise the moral charge of the iniquity. Hos 12:8 — "I am rich; I have found wealth" — is the advantageous comparison mechanism: prosperity is used to discharge moral accountability. Douglas's pollution framework illuminates the VCG-01 priestly-bearing sequences (Lev 10:17; Lev 16:21/22) as pollution-removal rituals: the iniquity-as-defilement is physically transferred to the scapegoat and expelled to a remote area — the ritual mechanics are those of pollution-removal applied to moral crime.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=16034, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Bandura's framework treats moral disengagement as a cognitive error correctable through moral re-engagement. The verse evidence treats the iniquity as objective (Hos 13:12 — "bound up" regardless of disengagement) and ultimately requiring divine action for removal (Isa 53:6 — laid on the servant; Jer 31:34 — God forgives). The divergence reveals: biblical iniquity cannot be self-corrected through cognitive moral re-engagement; the crime exists in God's record independently of the person's moral-engagement state. Douglas's framework treats pollution as culturally constructed. The verse evidence treats iniquity as before God's own gaze (Jer 16:17 — "not concealed from my eyes") independently of cultural construction. Both frameworks miss the vertical-relational dimension (before God) that makes iniquity's removal require divine rather than merely human action.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=16035, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-18]** E — Bandura's framework surfaces the dynamics of *collective moral disengagement* — how groups rationalise shared iniquity — that parallels the communal self-deception evidenced in Jer 16:10 ("what is our iniquity?") and Hos 12:8. The gap is how communities maintain collective iniquity-denial despite accumulating evidence of divine displeasure: the social-psychological dynamics of communal conscience suppression are present in the corpus (Jer 16:10; Hos 12:8) but underspecified at the group-psychology level. No further verse investigation is required — the gap is a human-science complement; the corpus describes the communal denial-pattern without theorising its social mechanism.

---

T7 self-evaluation:

  2. Scope marker:       20× [CHAR-18] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T7.1.8/T7.1.9 are S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Gen 15:16; Exo 20:5/34:7; Lev 5:1/7:18/10:17/16:21/22/18:25/19:8/20:17; Num 5:15/31/18:1; Psa 18:23/32:2/5/36:2/38:4/51:5/90:8/130:3/4; Pro 5:22; Isa 1:4/40:2/53:5/6/11/59:2/64:6; Jer 14:7/16:10/17/31:34/50:20; Eze 14:3/4/7/18:20; Hos 12:8/13:12/14:2; Mic 7:18/19; Zec 3:4/9; Mal 2:6; 2Sa 22:24; Ezr 9:6/7/13; Lam 5:7 — all in §3)
  → PASS. All 8 tiers complete.

## CHAR-19 — Transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing (189 rows: E=182 S=7 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=16036, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The 147-verse corpus reveals God's character across five dimensions. (1) *God as the one who establishes boundaries and commands*: Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — the transgression is possible only because God has established a knowable order; transgression presupposes divine command; (2) *God as the one who counts and tracks transgressions*: Amos 1:3 — "for three transgressions of Damascus, and for four, I will not revoke the punishment" — God keeps a precise count toward a threshold; Dan 9:24 — "seventy weeks are decreed... to finish the transgression" — a divine timeline governs transgression's end; (3) *God as the one who does not pardon certain transgressions*: Exo 23:21 — "he will not pardon your transgression, for my name is in him" — some transgressions exceed the reach of pardon; Jos 24:19 — "he will not forgive your transgressions or your sins"; (4) *God as the one who pardons through steadfast love*: Mic 7:18 — "pardoning iniquity and passing over transgression for the remnant of his inheritance"; Isa 43:25 — "I, I am he who blots out your transgressions for my own sake"; (5) *God as the one who is pierced by transgression through the servant*: Isa 53:5 — "he was pierced for our transgressions" — the transgression's cost falls on the servant whom God appoints.

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=16037, S) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-19]** S — No verse in the 147-verse corpus attributes transgression to God. God appears as the one who establishes the boundaries crossed (Rom 4:15), counts transgressions (Amo 1:3), withholds or grants pardon (Exo 23:21; Mic 7:18), and absorbs their cost through the servant (Isa 53:5/8). The characteristic is exclusively creaturely.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=16038, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The silence is structurally necessary: transgression requires a higher boundary to cross, and God stands as the boundary-setter. Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — God is the law-giver whose command makes transgression possible; God cannot transgress his own commands. The silence reveals that transgression is constitutively creaturely — it can only exist where a creature stands under divine authority and chooses to cross the known line. God's absolute holiness (Exo 23:21 — "my name is in him" as ground for no pardon) is what makes the crossing possible as transgression.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=16039, S) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-19]** S — The corpus provides no evidence of a positive created purpose for transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing. The characteristic is entirely a fallen-condition response. Rom 4:15 — the boundary must exist before transgression can occur; Rom 5:20 — "the law came in to increase the trespass" — the law's purpose relative to transgression is to expose and define it, not to legitimise it. No verse presents transgression as a designed feature of the image-bearer.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=16040, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Entirely a response to the fallen condition, with one notable precedent-setting moment. Rom 5:14 — "the transgression of Adam, who was a type of the one who was to come" — Adam's transgression is the paradigmatic first instance; VCG-05's paraptōma passages (Rom 5:15/16/17/18) name the trespass as the historical entry-point of condemnation. The Amos threshold-formula (Amo 1:3/6/9/11/13/2:1/4/6) assumes accumulated transgression as the fallen condition across nations. The characteristic is not a designed feature but arises from the image-bearer's will choosing to cross the known boundary God has set.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=16041, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — two eschatological orientations. (1) *Judgment and finish*: Dan 9:24 — "seventy weeks are decreed about your people and your holy city, to finish the transgression, to put an end to sin" — the eschatological fullness is the divinely decreed completion and termination of transgression; Dan 8:23 — "when the transgressors have reached their limit" — a maximum is appointed; (2) *Pardon and removal*: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins"; Psa 103:12 — transgressions removed to the furthest distance; Eze 18:22 — "none of the transgressions he has committed shall be remembered against him" — forgiven transgressions have no future hold.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=16042, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression expresses the image through its *inverted exercise of the will*: the image-bearer is constituted with a will capable of obedience to God's known commands — only a being made in God's image can knowingly receive a command and choose to cross it. Rom 5:14 — Adam's transgression is the paradigmatic knowing-violation; Rom 4:15 — transgression requires prior knowledge of the law. The capacity to know the boundary and choose to cross it belongs to the image's rational-moral constitution. Isa 1:2 — "children have I reared and brought up, but they have rebelled against me" — the parental image reveals that the capacity for known defiance is what makes the rebellion a moral reality rather than mere animal deviation.

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=16043, S) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-19]** S — Exclusively creaturely (T0.1.2). The characteristic requires a known boundary to cross and a higher authority to transgress against; God occupies no such position. The characteristic is constitutively relational in a downward direction (the creature under the Creator).

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=16044, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The presence of transgression-as-deliberate-boundary-crossing reveals the image in its *wilfully defiant fallen condition*: the image-bearer's rational capacity to know God's command has been deployed against God rather than for him. Isa 48:8 — "from of old your ear has not been opened... from before birth you were called a rebel" — the image is so thoroughly characterised by transgression that it has become a defining label. Eze 2:3 — "nations of rebels who have transgressed against me... from their fathers to this very day" — the sustained multi-generational character of the transgression reveals an image whose volitional capacity for knowing defiance has become its dominant orientation.

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=16045, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through two typological axes. (1) *Adam-paradigm transgression* (VCG-05): Rom 5:14 — "Adam... a type of the one who was to come" — Adam's transgression is explicitly named as a type; the trespass's death-consequence (VCG-05 — Rom 5:15/16/17) is the negative type whose positive antitype is Christ's one act of righteousness; (2) *Servant-bearing* (VCG-07 cross-reference; VCG-06): Isa 53:5 — "he was pierced for our transgressions"; Isa 53:8 — "stricken for the transgression of my people" — the servant's bearing of transgression's cost is the OT type pointing toward Christ. Isa 53:12 — "numbered with the transgressors; yet he bore the sin of many, and makes intercession for the transgressors" — the intercession-for-transgressors is the typological bridge.

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=16046, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Two directions. (1) *Negative to positive type*: Adam's trespass (negative — VCG-05; Rom 5:14) is the type that Christ's obedience reverses (positive antitype); the direction is from fallen-human boundary-crossing toward divine-human righteousness-constituting. (2) *OT prophetic to NT Christological*: Isa 53:5/8's servant-bearing-transgression is the OT prophetic type pointing toward Christ's cross; the direction is from Isaiah's prophetic portrait (human servant) to Christ's actual vicarious suffering (divine fulfilment). Both directions: human type → divine antitype.

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=16047, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Typological use is evidenced (T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T0 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       12/12 ✓ (T0.1.1–T0.1.3, T0.2.1–T0.2.3, T0.3.1–T0.3.3, T0.4.1–T0.4.3)
  2. Scope marker:       12× [CHAR-19] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: 10/10 E findings cite evidence ✓ (T0.1.2, T0.3.2 are S — no citation required)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 23:21; Jos 24:19; Rom 4:15/5:14/15/16/17/18/20; Amos 1:3; Dan 8:23/9:24; Psa 103:12; Isa 1:2/43:25/48:8/53:5/8/12; Jer 2:8; Eze 2:3/18:22; Mic 7:18 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T1.


---

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=16048, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing." Three signals. (1) *Transgression*: the English rendering of the primary vocabulary family (pe.sha/pa.sha; parabasis); the word carries both the legal sense of crossing a line and the relational sense of betrayal; (2) *As deliberate*: the defining modifier — not inadvertent error (CHAR-2) but knowing, wilful choice; Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — a known boundary is required; (3) *Boundary-crossing*: the spatial-moral image that names the core act — the will steps over a line it knows to exist. The name signals: the most distinctively volitional category in M10 — the will that knows and chooses to defy.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=16049, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Four primary term-groups. (1) **pe.sha (H6588)** — noun "transgression, rebellion, breach" — OT dominant (most VCGs); root sense of breaking away from, revolting against; (2) **pa.sha (H6586)** — verb "to transgress, to rebel, to break away" — action-form of the same root; Eze 2:3 — "nations of rebels who have rebelled (*pa.sha*) against me"; (3) **sa.rah (H5627)** — noun "revolt, apostasy, turning aside" — VCG-02; Isa 1:5 — continued revolt despite punishment; Jer 28:16 — "spoken rebellion against the Lord"; (4) **parabasis (G3847) / parabatēs (G3848) / parabainō (G3845)** — NT "transgression/transgressor/to transgress" — VCG-04; Rom 4:15 — definitional: "where there is no law there is no transgression"; (5) **paraptōma (G3900)** — "trespass, false step" — VCG-05 — Adam's paradigm (Rom 5:15/16/17/18/20). The term-families converge on the same semantic core: the will choosing to step past a known boundary.

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=16050, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-19]** E — All three. *Directional*: transgression is movement across a boundary — the will moves from the permitted side to the forbidden side; Isa 59:20 — "those who turn from transgression" — the turn-from presupposes a direction-toward; Isa 31:6 — "turn to him from whom people have deeply revolted" — the revolt is a directional departure. *Relational*: transgression is always against someone — Jer 2:29 — "you have all transgressed against me"; Eze 2:3 — "rebelled against me"; Gen 31:36 — "what is my offense against you?" — the relational frame is constitutive. *Constitutional*: Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel" — transgression as a pre-personal constitutional label; Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression" — a hereditary identity.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=16051, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The corpus reveals CHAR-19 as a *volitional act-and-disposition complex*: primarily a wilful act of the will (the specific crossing) but also a dispositional orientation of the will that has become constitutive. (1) *Act dimension*: Amos 1:3/6/9 — specific counted acts that cross the threshold; Rom 4:15 — the law-violation as a specific act; Eze 18:28 — "all the transgressions that he had committed" — enumerable past acts; (2) *Dispositional dimension*: Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart" — an inner orientation that has become a voice; Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression" — transgression as identity; Isa 48:8 — "from before birth called a rebel" — a constitutive inner character.

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=16052, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The corpus reveals nine constituent elements corresponding to the nine VCGs. (1) *Against God in the covenant relationship* (VCG-01 — 27V): rebellion as relational betrayal; (2) *Personal moral breach — Job/Psalms/wisdom* (VCG-09 — 34V): inward-facing honest assessment; (3) *Interpersonal breach between persons* (VCG-08 — 19V): horizontal wrongdoing; (4) *Revolt — inner defiance speaking and acting* (VCG-02 — 7V): sa.rah's vocal and active inner rebellion; (5) *Oppressive weight crushing and enslaving* (VCG-07 — 14V): transgression as a burden-force; (6) *Pardoned and erased by God* (VCG-06 — 13V): divine forgiveness of rebellion; (7) *Accumulated crossing of divine tolerance threshold* (VCG-03 — 13V): the Amos count-formula; (8) *Law-violation — NT parabasis* (VCG-04 — 15V): the knowing-law-crossing register; (9) *Adam-paradigm foundational crossing* (VCG-05 — 5V): the paraptōma first-trespass.

---

#### T1.2.3 (cf=16053, E) — What is the current best working description of this characteristic — encapsulating its constitutional location, the faculties it primarily engages, and its impact on the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Working description: *Transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing (CHAR-19) is the wilful defiance of known moral or covenantal order — the will that knowingly crosses a boundary or breaches a covenant. The corpus evidences nine registers: (1) Against God in covenant (VCG-01 — 27V): "children have I reared... but they rebelled" (Isa 1:2) — relational betrayal; (2) Personal moral breach (VCG-09 — 34V): "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart" (Psa 36:1); (3) Interpersonal breach (VCG-08 — 19V): "love covers all offenses" (Pro 10:12); (4) Revolt (VCG-02 — 7V): "whole head is sick and whole heart faint" (Isa 1:5) — sa.rah's systemic collapse; (5) Oppressive weight (VCG-07 — 14V): "bound into a yoke... set upon my neck" (Lam 1:14); (6) Pardoned and erased (VCG-06 — 13V): "as far as east from west" (Psa 103:12); (7) Threshold crossing (VCG-03 — 13V): "for three transgressions... and for four" (Amo 1:3); (8) Law-violation (VCG-04 — 15V): "where there is no law there is no transgression" (Rom 4:15); (9) Adam-paradigm (VCG-05 — 5V): "one man's trespass" (Rom 5:15) — the foundational boundary-crossing. Distinguished from CHAR-1 (wilful sinning as act) by the emphasis on the knowing crossing of a known boundary, and from CHAR-18 (iniquity as accumulated crime) by being specifically the act-of-defiance rather than the accumulated moral-crime weight.*

---

#### T1.3.1 (cf=16054, E) — What is the structural opposite of this characteristic — the inner-being reality that stands against or excludes it?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The structural opposite is *faithful covenant-keeping — the will that remains within the known boundary*. Hos 14:9 — "the upright walk in them, but transgressors stumble in them" — the upright walker is the structural opposite; Psa 32:1/2 — "blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered... in whose spirit there is no deceit" — the no-deceit, no-transgression state; Rom 2:25 — "circumcision indeed is of value if you obey the law" — obedience to the law is the structural opposite of parabasis (transgression of the law); 2Sa 22:24 / Psa 18:23 — "I was blameless before him, and I kept myself from guilt" — the blamelessness of deliberate self-restraint within the boundary.

---

#### T1.3.2 (cf=16055, E) — What does this characteristic explicitly exclude or resist?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The characteristic explicitly excludes: (1) *Covenant membership*: Eze 20:38 — "I will purge out the rebels from among you... they shall not enter the land of Israel" — transgression excludes from the restored community; Eze 37:23 — no longer defiling themselves with transgressions is the condition for becoming "my people"; (2) *Divine pardon in certain cases*: Exo 23:21 — "he will not pardon your transgression"; Jos 24:19 — "he will not forgive your transgressions"; (3) *Wisdom and understanding*: Hos 14:9 — transgressors stumble in the Lord's right ways — the capacity to walk in wisdom is excluded by transgression; (4) *Divine prosper*: Pro 28:13 — "whoever conceals his transgressions will not prosper."

---

#### T1.3.3 (cf=16056, E) — What is this characteristic not — where does it end and something else begin?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three boundaries. (1) *Not CHAR-2 (Unintentional sinning)*: CHAR-2 lacks the knowing-deliberate dimension; Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — requires knowledge of the boundary; 1Ti 2:14 — "the woman was deceived and became a transgressor" — deception diminishes the deliberate dimension; (2) *Not CHAR-18 (Iniquity as accumulated moral crime)*: CHAR-18 is the accumulated crime-weight before God; CHAR-19 is the specific act of boundary-crossing that generates that accumulation; the two overlap (Exo 34:7 names both) but CHAR-19 is the act while CHAR-18 is the account; (3) *Not mere social offence*: Gen 50:17 — the brothers' transgression required forgiveness; but CHAR-19 is specifically the *knowing* crossing of a moral line, not merely offensive behaviour without moral definition.

---

#### T1.4.1 (cf=16057, E) — In what distinct ways does this characteristic operate within the inner person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Five distinct inner modes. (1) *As an inner voice*: Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart" — transgression operates as an internal counselling voice that drives out the fear of God; (2) *As a threshold-crossing act*: Amos 1:3/6/9 — the counted transgressions that reach the threshold; Rom 4:15 — the law-violation as specific crossing; (3) *As a dominating power*: Psa 19:13 — "great transgression" exercising dominion; Pro 29:6 — transgression ensnaring the evil man; (4) *As identity-level character*: Isa 48:8 — "called a rebel from birth"; Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression"; (5) *As relational rupture*: Isa 59:13 — "transgressing, and denying the Lord, and turning back from following our God" — the inner act of turning away is constitutive.

---

#### T1.4.2 (cf=16058, E) — Does this characteristic operate differently depending on context, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes across the nine VCG registers. (1) *Divine-covenant context* (VCG-01): transgression operates as relational betrayal of the nurturing God (Isa 1:2) — the relational betrayal dimension dominates; (2) *Legal context* (VCG-04): transgression operates as law-violation with judicial consequences (Rom 2:23 — "dishonor God by breaking the law"); (3) *Interpersonal context* (VCG-08): transgression operates as breach of trust or relational duty (Exo 22:9 — breach of custodial trust; Pro 17:9 — covering an offense seeks love); (4) *Threshold context* (VCG-03): transgression operates as a countable accumulation (Amos 1:3 — "for three... and for four") toward a divine tolerance limit; (5) *Revolt context* (VCG-02): transgression operates as vocal, active inner defiance that destroys the whole person (Isa 1:5 — "whole head sick and whole heart faint").

---

#### T1.4.3 (cf=16059, E) — Does this characteristic have a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — and if so, how does it function?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — in three speech-modes. (1) *Transgression as inner speech*: Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart" — the characteristic has an inner-voice dimension; (2) *Revolt expressed through verbal rebellion*: Isa 59:13 — "speaking oppression and revolt, conceiving and uttering from the heart lying words"; Jer 28:16 — "you have uttered rebellion against the Lord" — the spoken word is the revolt's expression; (3) *Confession of transgression*: Psa 32:5 — "I will confess my transgressions to the Lord" — the voluntary speech-act of naming and owning the transgression; Psa 38:18 — "I confess my iniquity"; Pro 28:13 — "he who confesses and forsakes them will obtain mercy."

---

#### T1.5.1 (cf=16060, E) — What is the first or most immediate inner-being response to receiving or encountering this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The immediate responses vary significantly. (1) *Shame and exposure*: Lam 1:22 — "because of all my transgressions; for my groans are many, and my heart is faint" — the immediate response is grief and inner collapse; Ezr 10:13 — "we have greatly transgressed in this matter" — corporate acknowledgment; (2) *Dominion over the will*: Psa 19:13 — "let them not have dominion over me!" — the immediate threat is enslavement; (3) *Self-justification and denial*: Pro 28:24 — "Whoever robs his father... says, 'That is no transgression'" — the immediate response in hardened conscience is denial; (4) *Despairing awareness*: Eze 33:10 — "surely our transgressions and our sins are upon us, and we rot away because of them. How then can we live?" — despairing recognition.

---

#### T1.5.2 (cf=16061, E) — Is the immediate response consistent across the verse evidence, or does it vary?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Variable across the VCG registers. In the personal-awareness register (VCG-09 — Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions, and my sin is ever before me") the immediate response is honest self-recognition. In the revolt register (VCG-02 — Isa 1:5 — the people continue to rebel despite being struck down) the immediate response is defiant persistence. In the oppressive-weight register (VCG-07 — Eze 33:10 — "we rot away") the immediate response is despair. In the interpersonal register (VCG-08 — Pro 28:24 — "that is no transgression") the immediate response can be conscience-suppression. The response ranges from honest recognition (Psa 51:3) through defiance (Isa 1:5) to denial (Pro 28:24).

---

#### T1.5.3 (cf=16062, E) — Where the verse evidence is silent on immediate response, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — For the threshold-accumulation register (VCG-03 — Amos 1:3/6/9 — nations whose transgressions are counted toward judgment), the immediate inner response of the transgressing nations to their own accumulation is not described. The corpus names the transgressions and the divine judgment-threshold but not the moment-by-moment inner experience of Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Moab, Ammon, Judah, and Israel as they commit and accumulate their counted transgressions.

---

#### T1.6.1 (cf=16063, E) — What does this characteristic produce in the inner being over time?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Four sustained inner-being effects. (1) *Systemic inner collapse*: Isa 1:5 — "the whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint" — sustained revolt produces comprehensive inner-being deterioration; (2) *Enslaving bondage*: Pro 29:6 — "an evil man is ensnared in his transgression" — the accumulated transgression becomes the trap; Lam 1:14 — "bound into a yoke... set upon my neck... my strength to fail" — the sustained burden produces physical and volitional incapacitation; (3) *Identity-level characterisation*: Isa 48:8 — "from before birth called a rebel" — sustained transgression becomes the defining identity label; Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression" — generationally transmitted; (4) *Moral blindness toward God*: Jer 2:29 — "you have all transgressed against me" yet they "contend" with God — the sustained transgressor has lost accurate moral self-assessment.

---

#### T1.6.2 (cf=16064, E) — What states, qualities, capacities, or orientations does sustained exposure to or possession of this characteristic establish?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three established states. (1) *Settled inner rebellion as orientation*: Eze 2:3 — "nations of rebels who have transgressed against me... from their fathers to this very day" — sustained transgression establishes multigenerational rebellion as the default orientation; (2) *Self-justifying posture*: Jer 2:29 — "why do you contend with me?" — the sustained transgressor adopts a self-justifying inner stance toward God; Pro 28:24 — "that is no transgression" — the sustained sinner develops an inner narrative that suppresses conscience; (3) *Separation from God's ways*: Hos 14:9 — "transgressors stumble in them" — sustained transgression establishes a constitutive incompatibility with God's right ways.

---

#### T1.6.3 (cf=16065, E) — Does the sustained effect differ from the immediate response — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes. The immediate response (shame — Lam 1:22; awareness — Psa 51:3; defiance — Isa 1:5) is episodic. The sustained effects (inner collapse — Isa 1:5; enslaving bondage — Lam 1:14; identity-characterisation — Isa 48:8; moral blindness — Jer 2:29) are constitutional developments of the will over time. The progression is: wilful act → repeated acts → habit → constitutive inner orientation of rebellion → identity-level rebel label (Isa 48:8) → stumbling in God's right ways as settled pattern (Hos 14:9).

---

#### T1.7.1 (cf=16066, E) — What inner conditions or orientations enable genuine reception of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three enabling conditions. (1) *Knowledge of the boundary*: Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — knowledge of the law is the prerequisite; transgression is definitionally impossible without prior awareness of the boundary being crossed; (2) *Inner absence of God-fear*: Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart; there is no fear of God before his eyes" — the absence of the fear of God is the enabling inner condition for transgression to take hold; (3) *Appetite of the will*: Pro 28:21 — "for a piece of bread a man will do wrong" — the will's desire is what transgression exploits; Pro 17:19 — "whoever loves transgression loves strife" — a positively-oriented inner affection toward transgression.

---

#### T1.7.2 (cf=16067, E) — What inner conditions block, distort, or prevent reception?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three blocking conditions. (1) *Self-flattery and pride*: Pro 28:24 — "that is no transgression" — the wilful verbal denial; Jer 2:29 — "why do you contend with me?" — the self-justifying contention against God; (2) *Love of transgression itself*: Pro 17:19 — "whoever loves transgression loves strife" — the positive inner affection toward transgression blocks recognition of it as wrongdoing; (3) *Deception*: 1Ti 2:14 — "the woman was deceived and became a transgressor" — external deception can partially diminish the deliberate dimension by distorting perception of the boundary.

---

#### T1.7.3 (cf=16068, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Isa 1:5 names it comprehensively: "why will you still be struck down? Why will you continue to rebel? The whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint" — the persistent transgressor's inner state is one of comprehensive moral-spiritual illness in both cognitive (head) and volitional (heart) faculties. Jer 2:29 — "why do you contend with me? You have all transgressed against me" — the inner state is self-righteous contention against God despite guilt. The persistent transgressor's inner state is thus a combination of structural inner deterioration (Isa 1:5 — head and heart incapacitated) and self-justifying posture (Jer 2:29) that produces inability to stand in God's right ways (Hos 14:9 — stumbling).

---

#### T1.8.1 (cf=16069, E) — What is the primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic from the programme's dimension vocabulary?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The primary dimension is **the will's volitional act of knowing defiance** — the definition's "wilful defiance of known moral or covenantal order." The will is the primary faculty: it knows the boundary (Rom 4:15 — law required), it chooses to cross (Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled"), it sustains the direction (Eze 2:3 — "to this very day"). Every other dimension (cognitive distortion, affective collapse, conscience suppression) is secondary to the will's primary volitional choice.

---

#### T1.8.2 (cf=16070, E) — What evidence from the verse evidence supports this classification?

**[CHAR-19]** E — (1) Rom 4:15 — transgression requires knowledge of the law → will can only transgress what it knows; (2) Isa 1:2 — "children have I reared... but they have rebelled" — the rebellion is wilful choice against received care; (3) Eze 18:30 — "repent and turn from all your transgressions" — the remedial call is to the will to turn; (4) Isa 31:6 — "turn to him from whom people have deeply revolted" — the revolt and its reversal are both volitional movements; (5) Pro 28:13 — "he who confesses and forsakes them" — the forsaking is a volitional act confirming that the committing was volitional.

---

#### T1.8.3 (cf=16071, E) — Does this characteristic carry secondary dimensions — and if so, what are they, and do they compete with the primary classification?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — three secondary dimensions. (1) *Cognitive*: Psa 36:1 — transgression speaks to the heart; the cognitive faculty is co-opted by the transgression's inner voice; Isa 58:1 — the people need the transgression declared to their awareness — the cognitive awareness of transgression may require external prompting; (2) *Affective*: Pro 17:19 — "whoever loves transgression loves strife" — the affective faculty of love can be directed toward transgression; Lam 1:22 — "my heart is faint" — transgression's sustained burden produces affective collapse; (3) *Social-relational*: VCG-08 (interpersonal breach — Pro 10:12/17:9/18:19) — transgression has a social dimension as offence between persons.

---

  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-19] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
