  → PASS. Response approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T2 continues in the next response.


---

## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=16072, E) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through the God-relationship and covenantal-allegiance dimensions. Eze 18:31 — "cast away from you all the transgressions that you have committed, and make yourselves a new heart and a new spirit!" — the call for a new heart and new spirit follows directly from the casting-away of transgressions; the spirit-level renewal is the designated remedy for transgression's spirit-level damage. Hos 8:1 — "they have transgressed my covenant and rebelled against my law" — covenant-transgression is a spirit-level relational rupture; 2Jo 9 — "everyone who goes on ahead and does not abide in the teaching of Christ, does not have God" — the transgression of going beyond Christ's teaching severs the spirit-level God-relationship.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=16073, E) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — the heart-origin of VCG-09 and the revolt-speech origin of VCG-02 both point to spirit-level origin. Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart" — the most explicit inner-origin statement in the corpus; the transgression speaks from the deepest inner level. Isa 59:13 — "conceiving and uttering from the heart lying words" — the revolt is conceived at the heart-level before being uttered. Eze 14:3 (CHAR-18 cross-reference level) — the idol-taking into the heart is the analogous spirit-level origin for iniquity; for CHAR-19, Psa 36:1's "deep in his heart" is the origin location.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=16074, E) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Spirit-level location (Eze 18:31 — new heart and new spirit as remedy; Psa 36:1 — speaks deep in the heart; Hos 8:1 — covenant-transgression) reveals that transgression-as-deliberate-boundary-crossing is not merely a surface behavioural category but a spirit-level reality that requires spirit-level renewal for its resolution. The fact that the remedy is a "new heart and new spirit" (Eze 18:31) confirms that the transgression has damaged the person at the deepest constitutive level. The God-relationship rupture (2Jo 9 — "does not have God") is the most severe spirit-level consequence.

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=16075, E) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Spirit-level location is evidenced (Psa 36:1; Eze 18:31; Hos 8:1; 2Jo 9). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=16076, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — extensively. (1) *Heart-inner to outer speech and act*: Psa 36:1 — transgression speaks deep in the heart → Isa 59:13 — "uttering from the heart lying words" → outer rebellion; (2) *Inner will → outer bodily consequence*: Lam 1:14 — "transgressions were bound into a yoke... set upon my neck; he caused my strength to fail" — the inner transgressions produce the bodily yoke and physical weakening; Isa 1:5 — inner revolt → "whole head is sick, whole heart faint" — constitutional physical-spiritual deterioration; (3) *Outer law-known → inner will crossing*: Rom 4:15 — the law (external) must exist before the inner will can generate the crossing — the constitutional movement is from external boundary to internal volitional act.

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=16077, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Primary sequence: (1) boundary established externally (divine law/covenant — Rom 4:15); (2) boundary known internally (cognitive awareness — Psa 36:1 — transgression speaks from within); (3) will chooses to cross (volitional act — Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled"); (4) act produces constitutional damage (Isa 1:5 — head sick, heart faint; Lam 1:14 — yoke on neck); (5) accumulation moves toward divine threshold (Amos 1:3 — "three... and for four"). Secondary (remedy) sequence: internal recognition → volitional turning (Eze 18:28 — "considered and turned away") → external confession (Psa 32:5) → divine pardon (Psa 103:12 — removed as far as east from west).

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=16078, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Constitutional movement is extensively evidenced (T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=16079, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes. Mic 6:7 — "shall I give my firstborn for my transgression, the fruit of my body for the sin of my soul?" — transgression is explicitly linked to the sin of the soul (*nepeš*); the soul is named as the site of the transgression's originating sinfulness. Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions, and my sin is ever before me" — the knowing and ever-present awareness is a soul-level existential condition; the transgression is experienced as a sustained inner-personal reality.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=16080, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Soul-level location (Mic 6:7 — "sin of my soul"; Psa 51:3 — "ever before me") reveals that transgression-as-deliberate-boundary-crossing is not merely a legal category but a soul-level moral reality that the person inhabits. The "ever before me" (Psa 51:3) names the sustained soul-level experiential weight of the acknowledged transgression. Mic 6:7's "sin of my soul" names the soul (*nepeš*) as the total-person subject of the transgression — the crossing implicates the whole person at their innermost level, not merely a faculty or action.

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=16081, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (Mic 6:7; Psa 51:3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=16082, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — most explicitly in the corpus. Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart; there is no fear of God before his eyes" — the heart is the named site from which transgression speaks; its location "deep in the heart" (*qereb*) names the innermost centre of the person. Isa 59:13 — "conceiving and uttering from the heart lying words" — revolt is conceived at the heart-level before outward utterance. Eze 18:31 — "cast away from you all the transgressions... and make yourselves a new heart and a new spirit!" — the remedy is a new heart, confirming transgression has damaged the existing heart.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=16083, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Heart-location (Psa 36:1 — "deep in his heart"; Isa 59:13 — "conceiving from the heart"; Eze 18:31 — new heart as remedy) reveals that CHAR-19's transgression is not primarily an external act but an inner-heart condition that drives external behaviour. Psa 36:1's "transgression speaks deep in the heart" is the single most important inner-being statement in the entire 147-verse corpus: it personifies transgression as an internal counsellor installed at the heart's deepest level, displacing the fear of God and steering the will toward rebellion. The heart is both the origin of transgression (where it speaks from) and the site requiring renewal when transgression is addressed (Eze 18:31).

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=16084, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Heart-location is the most explicitly evidenced constitutional location in the corpus (Psa 36:1; Isa 59:13; Eze 18:31). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=16085, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The mind is engaged through the knowledge-prerequisite and the cognitive assessment dimensions. Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — the mind must possess knowledge of the boundary before transgression is possible; the cognitive awareness is the enabling condition. Isa 46:8 — "remember this and stand firm, recall it to mind, you transgressors" — the transgressors' minds need to recall and fix the remembered truth; their transgression is connected to a cognitive failure of recall. Job 13:23 — "how many are my iniquities and my sins? Make me know my transgression and my sin" — the mind's need for divine illumination to know transgression-count.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=16086, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Mind-location (Rom 4:15 — knowledge-prerequisite; Isa 46:8 — recall failure; Job 13:23 — needs divine disclosure) reveals that transgression engages and distorts the cognitive faculty at two levels: (1) it requires the mind's prior knowledge of the law to be technically possible (Rom 4:15); (2) it progressively suppresses the mind's capacity to accurately assess its own transgression-state (Isa 46:8 — transgressors need to "recall it to mind"). The mind is both the prerequisite for transgression and a casualty of its sustained operation.

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=16087, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Mind-level location (knowledge-prerequisite — Rom 4:15; cognitive recall — Isa 46:8; disclosure need — Job 13:23) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=16088, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — *Will* as the primary soul-level faculty is the defining location of CHAR-19. The entire characteristic is built on the wilful act of crossing a known boundary — the will is the primary inner faculty. Isa 31:6 — "turn to him from whom people have deeply revolted" — the turning (the will's reversal) is the prescribed remedy, confirming the will is the primary locus. Eze 18:28 — "he considered and turned away from all the transgressions" — the turning of the will is the act that reverses the transgression's consequence. *Conscience* also appears: Pro 28:13 — "whoever conceals his transgressions will not prosper" — the concealment is a conscience-suppression act.

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=16089, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Will-level location (Isa 31:6 — "turn... from whom you revolted"; Eze 18:28 — "considered and turned away"; Eze 18:30 — "repent and turn from all your transgressions") reveals that CHAR-19 is the will-characteristic of M10: the defining inner-being faculty of transgression-as-deliberate-boundary-crossing is the will's volitional capacity to turn (in either direction — toward or away from God). Every remedy-call in the corpus addresses the will's turning: "turn from transgression" (multiple verses in VCG-06). Conscience-level is also engaged (Pro 28:13 — concealment suppresses conscience; Psa 36:1 — transgression speaks in place of conscience's God-fear).

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=16090, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Will-level and conscience-level locations are both evidenced (Isa 31:6; Eze 18:28/30; Pro 28:13; Psa 36:1). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=16091, E) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — three body-part links. (1) *Lips and mouth*: Pro 10:19 — "when words are many, transgression is not lacking" — the lips/mouth as the body-part through which transgression overflows; Pro 12:13 — "ensnared by the transgression of his lips"; Isa 59:13 — "uttering from the heart lying words"; (2) *Neck/shoulders*: Lam 1:14 — "transgressions were bound into a yoke... set upon my neck" — the neck/shoulders as the body-part bearing the yoke-burden of transgression; (3) *Head*: Isa 1:5 — "the whole head is sick" — sustained revolt-transgression incapacitates the whole head.

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=16092, E) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three distinct body-part functions. (1) *Lips as transgression's instrument* (Pro 10:19; Pro 12:13; Isa 59:13): the mouth is the outer organ through which the heart's transgression is expressed; "transgression of the lips" names the body's speaking-organ as the conduit for inner rebellion becoming outer act; (2) *Neck as transgression's burden-point* (Lam 1:14): the yoke on the neck images the transgression as an enslaving weight physically borne; the neck is the body-part that carries what the transgression has imposed; (3) *Head as revolt's victim* (Isa 1:5): the head incapacitated by sustained revolt names the body's commanding faculty as the location of systemic deterioration from persistent transgression.

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=16093, E) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Body-part links (lips/mouth — Pro 10:19/12:13/Isa 59:13; neck — Lam 1:14; head — Isa 1:5) are evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=16094, E) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Bidirectional. (1) *Inner heart → outer lips*: Psa 36:1 — transgression speaks deep in the heart → Isa 59:13 — "uttering from the heart lying words" — the inner transgression produces the outer speech-act through the lips; (2) *Outer transgressions → bodily burden*: Lam 1:14 — "my transgressions were bound into a yoke... set upon my neck" — the accumulated outer transgressions return as a physical-weight burden on the body; (3) *Sustained inner revolt → whole-body incapacitation*: Isa 1:5 — continued inner rebellion → "whole head is sick, whole heart faint" — the inner volitional revolt produces bodily systemic deterioration.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=16095, E) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The bidirectional body-transgression link reveals that transgression-as-deliberate-boundary-crossing operates through the body at both ends: the inner transgression is enacted through the body's speaking members (lips — Pro 12:13) and returns to burden the body's carrying members (neck — Lam 1:14). Addressing transgression thus requires addressing both: the inner heart-transgression (Psa 36:1 — where it speaks from) and the outer bodily expression (Pro 12:13 — lips). The body both mediates transgression outward and bears its returning burden.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=16096, E) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Body-characteristic link (lips, neck, head) is evidenced and bidirectional. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=16097, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Record held in divine count*: Amos 1:3 — "for three transgressions... and for four" — God maintains a counted record; Dan 8:13 — "the transgression that makes desolate" — a specific transgression enshrined in heavenly discourse; Dan 9:24 — "to finish the transgression" — the accumulated transgression must be completed/finished, implying it persists as a deposit until that moment; (2) *Identity-level labelling*: Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel" — the identity deposit of transgression precedes birth; Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression" — the deposit passes generationally; (3) *Oppressive weight-burden*: Lam 1:14 — the yoke-burden bound and set on the neck — a persistent bodily-moral weight.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=16098, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Supporting: Amos 1:3 (divine count-record); Isa 48:8 (identity deposit); Lam 1:14 (yoke-burden deposit). Contradicting or complicating: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins" — the divine-count deposit is erasable by God's sovereign choice; Psa 103:12 — "as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us" — the deposit is completely removable; Eze 18:22 — "none of the transgressions he has committed shall be remembered against him" — the accumulated record can be rendered null. The deposit is real and persistent but divinely erasable.

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=16099, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Deposit evidence is present: divine-count record (Amos 1:3; Dan 9:24), identity-level labelling (Isa 48:8; Isa 57:4), yoke-burden (Lam 1:14). T5.7 will carry: all deposit forms are divinely erasable (Isa 43:25; Psa 103:12; Eze 18:22) but real and persistent until erased; the identity-deposit (Isa 48:8 — "from before birth") is the most deeply-rooted and the hardest to address through human effort.

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=16100, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Two constitutional origins. (1) *Heart-level volitional choice*: Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart" — the heart is the named origin of the inner-voice that drives transgression; Isa 59:13 — "conceiving and uttering from the heart" — the conception is in the heart; (2) *Adamic inheritance*: Rom 5:15/16/17/18/20 (VCG-05 paraptōma) — "one man's trespass" entered condemnation for all; Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel" — the rebel-identity precedes personal choice; Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression" — inherited from parents. Both origins operate simultaneously: constitutional Adamic inheritance and individual volitional heart-choice.

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=16101, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Multiple — nine VCG registers name different originating contexts. Individual volitional choice (VCG-01 — Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled"), Adamic inheritance (VCG-05 — Rom 5:15/16), heart-level inner voice (VCG-09 — Psa 36:1), inherited national identity (VCG-03 — Amos — nations whose transgression is counted), deceptive persuasion (VCG-04 — 1Ti 2:14 — Eve deceived), and revolt-speech (VCG-02 — Jer 28:16 — false prophecy as revolt). The characteristic has the most diverse origin-set of any M10 characteristic studied, covering individual, communal, inherited, coerced, and heart-generated origins.

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=16102, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes across the nine VCG registers. (1) *VCG-01 covenant-against-God*: origin in the relational choice to betray the nurturing God (Isa 1:2); (2) *VCG-02 revolt*: origin in deliberate speech-act of false prophecy (Jer 28:16) or persistent refusal to stop despite judgment (Isa 1:5); (3) *VCG-03 threshold-crossing*: origin in specific national crimes (Amos — military brutality, slave-trading, desecration); (4) *VCG-04 law-violation*: origin in conscious law-knowledge (Rom 4:15); (5) *VCG-05 Adam-paradigm*: origin in the single primordial trespass (Rom 5:15); (6) *VCG-08 interpersonal*: origin in anger-driven social transgression (Pro 29:22 — anger causes much transgression).

---

---

## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=16103, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The perceptive faculty is engaged on both sides. (1) *God's perception of transgression*: Amos 5:12 — "for I know how many are your transgressions and how great are your sins" — divine perception is complete and precise; (2) *Human perception of own transgression*: Job 13:23 — "make me know my transgression and my sin" — Job's perceptive faculty requires divine disclosure; Isa 58:1 — "declare to my people their transgression" — the people need a prophet to perceive what they cannot self-perceive; (3) *Distorted perception*: Psa 36:2 (VCG-09 adjacent) — the wicked person cannot perceive his own transgression because self-flattery blocks the faculty.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=16104, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression *impairs* the perceptive faculty in the sustained sinner. Isa 58:1 — "declare to my people their transgression" — the people cannot perceive their own transgression without external prophetic declaration; Isa 46:8 — "recall it to mind, you transgressors" — the transgressors' perceptive faculty has lost its grip on what they know. Psa 36:1 — "there is no fear of God before his eyes" — the eyes (perceptive faculty) are the seat of the replaced God-fear; transgression displaces what the eyes perceive. However, conscience-engaged recognition sharpens perception: Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions, and my sin is ever before me" — the penitent's perception is acute.

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=16105, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The perceptive pattern reveals that transgression progressively impairs self-perception of the transgression itself: the further transgression is entrenched, the less the person perceives it (Isa 58:1 — needs prophetic declaration; Isa 46:8 — must be told to recall). The corrective always requires external input: God's declaration (Isa 58:1), prophetic courage (Mic 3:8 — "I am filled with power... to declare to Jacob his transgression"), or divine illumination (Job 13:23). God's perception of transgression (Amos 5:12 — "I know how many") never diminishes and stands against the human's progressively impaired self-perception.

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=16130, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Conscientiousness is engaged through both the deliberate commission and the concealment dimensions. (1) *Conscientiousness directed toward transgression*: Amos 4:4 — "come to Bethel and transgress; to Gilgal and multiply transgression" — the ironic command exposes that the people have been conscientiously committed to their transgressions; Isa 5:18 — "those who draw iniquity with cords... as with cart ropes" — conscientiousness applied to the pursuit of wrongdoing; (2) *Conscientiousness directed toward concealment*: Pro 28:13 — "whoever conceals his transgressions will not prosper" — the active concealment requires and directs conscientiousness toward covering the act.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=16131, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression *misdirects* conscientiousness toward the wrong object. The person conscientiously multiplies transgression (Amos 4:4) or conscientiously conceals it (Pro 28:13) rather than conscientiously obeying. The remedy redirects conscientiousness: Psa 32:5 — "I will confess my transgressions to the Lord, and you forgave the iniquity of my sin" — the confession-act deploys conscientiousness toward honest self-disclosure. Pro 28:13 — "he who confesses and forsakes them will obtain mercy" — the forsaking is the conscientious act of redirected will.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=16132, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The conscientiousness pattern confirms CHAR-19's active character: the deliberate boundary-crosser is not passively drifting but conscientiously pursuing the transgression (Amos 4:4) or its concealment (Pro 28:13). This conscientiousness applied to transgression is what makes CHAR-19 the most morally serious category in M10: not negligence but purposeful wrongdoing. The directional reversal of conscientiousness (from transgression-commission to transgression-forsaking) is the measurable inner sign of genuine repentance (Pro 28:13 — "confesses and forsakes").

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=16133, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The relational capacity is constitutively engaged: transgression is always transgression against someone. (1) *Against God*: Jer 2:29 — "you have all transgressed against me" — the divine relationship is the primary relational frame; Isa 1:2 — "children have I reared... but they have rebelled against me" — parental-relational betrayal; (2) *Against persons*: VCG-08 interpersonal (Gen 50:17 — the brothers' transgression against Joseph; Exo 22:9 — breach of custodial trust); (3) *Against the covenant community*: Lev 16:16/21 — "transgressions... all their sins" requiring the community's atonement ritual.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=16134, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression *severs* relational capacity in proportion to its object. Against God: Isa 59:2 (CHAR-18 adjacent) — "iniquities have made a separation between you and your God"; 2Jo 9 — going beyond Christ's teaching means "does not have God" — the God-relationship is severed; Eze 20:38 — "those who transgress against me... shall not enter the land of Israel" — excluded from covenant community. Against persons: Pro 18:19 — "a brother offended is more unyielding than a strong city" — the interpersonal transgression creates the hardest relational barrier.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=16135, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The relational pattern reveals that CHAR-19's defining relational consequence is the severity of the bond broken proportional to the depth of the prior relationship. Isa 1:2 — "children have I reared... but they have rebelled" — God's parental investment makes the betrayal-transgression more, not less, morally serious. Pro 18:19 — the closer the brother, the harder the barrier. This proportionality of relational rupture to relational depth is CHAR-19's distinctive relational finding: the most intimate relationships produce the most devastating transgression-consequences when they are crossed.

---

  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Psa 19:13/25:7/32:1/5/36:1/51:3/13; Pro 12:13/17:19/18:19/28:13/24/29:6; Isa 1:2/5/43:25/46:8/58:1/59:2/20; Jer 2:29; Lam 1:22; Eze 18:30/31/20:38/33:10; Hos 8:1; Amos 4:4/5:12; Mic 3:8; Dan 8:23/9:24; Rom 4:15/5:14; 1Ti 2:14; 2Jo 9; Pro 10:12/29:22; Gen 50:17; Exo 22:9; Lev 16:16/21 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Response approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T4 continues in the next response.


---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=16106, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — the cognitive faculty is the knowledge-prerequisite of transgression. Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — cognition of the law must precede transgression; the crossing is only transgression when the boundary is cognitively known. Isa 46:8 — "remember this and stand firm, recall it to mind, you transgressors" — the cognitive act of recall is addressed to transgressors; their problem is cognitive failure to retain what they know. Dan 8:23 — "when transgressors have reached their limit" — the accumulation has a cognitive measure.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=16107, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression *bypasses and suppresses* cognition in the sustained transgressor. Isa 46:8 — transgressors must be commanded to "recall it to mind" — their cognition has ceased to retain what they know. Pro 28:24 — "that is no transgression" — the deliberate cognitive reframing that suppresses accurate moral knowledge. Jer 2:29 — the people "contend" with God despite having all transgressed — their cognition has inverted, producing self-justifying reasoning instead of accurate self-assessment. Conversely, the cognitive faculty is deployed accurately by the penitent: Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions" — precise cognitive self-assessment.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=16108, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals the most distinctive analytical finding for CHAR-19: transgression requires cognition (Rom 4:15 — law must be known) yet progressively suppresses the cognitive capacity to accurately assess the transgression being committed (Isa 46:8; Pro 28:24). The knowing that makes transgression possible is the same knowing that transgression systematically undermines. The paradox of CHAR-19's cognitive dimension: it requires knowledge to begin and produces ignorance to sustain itself.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=16109, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through both divine memory and human memory. (1) *Divine memory of transgression*: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins" — forgiveness is defined as divine memory-non-retention; the default is that God remembers; Dan 9:24 — "to finish the transgression" — a temporally bounded divine memory; (2) *Human memory of transgression*: Isa 46:8 — "recall it to mind, you transgressors" — human memory of the transgressing condition is required; Psa 25:7 — "remember not the sins of my youth or my transgressions" — the plea for God not to remember the speaker's early transgressions.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=16110, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression *impairs* the person's self-directed memory (Isa 46:8 — must be commanded to recall) while being *deepened* in God's memory (Isa 43:25 — only God can choose not to remember). The person who transgresses progressively loses the memory of their transgression's significance (Isa 46:8 — "recall it to mind"); God's memory of the transgression grows more rather than less acute (Amos 5:12 — "I know how many"). Forgiveness inverts this: God actively chooses not to remember (Isa 43:25) while the forgiven person's memory of their transgression becomes the basis of teaching others (Psa 51:13 — "I will teach transgressors your ways").

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=16111, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The memory pattern reveals a double-inversion at forgiveness: God, who has been remembering the transgression (Amos 5:12), chooses to forget (Isa 43:25); the person, who had been forgetting their transgression (Isa 46:8), now correctly remembers it in order to teach from it (Psa 51:13). The memory reversal at forgiveness is constitutive: the forgiven transgressor's accurate memory of past transgression becomes the very resource for guiding other transgressors back (Psa 51:13 — "teach transgressors your ways, and sinners will return to you").

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=16112, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through the love-of-transgression dimension and the weight-grief dimension. (1) *Affection toward transgression*: Pro 17:19 — "whoever loves transgression loves strife" — the inner affective capacity of love is directed toward transgression itself; the positive emotional orientation toward transgression is a distinct inner condition; (2) *Grief and collapse under transgression's weight*: Lam 1:22 — "my heart is faint" — the affective collapse under accumulated transgressions; Eze 33:10 — "how then can we live?" — the desperate affective response to the burden of accumulated transgressions.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=16113, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression *misdirects* the affective faculty (Pro 17:19 — love directed toward transgression itself) and *deepens negative affect* under accumulation (Lam 1:22 — heart fainting; Eze 33:10 — despair). The primary affective distortion is Pro 17:19's "whoever loves transgression loves strife" — the love-faculty attached to transgression produces both the affective pleasure of wrongdoing and the consequent strife. The remedy produces positive affect: Psa 32:1 — "blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven" — forgiveness produces blessedness as the opposite affective state to transgression-burden.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=16114, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The affective pattern reveals that the love-of-transgression (Pro 17:19) is the most distinctive affective feature of CHAR-19 — it is the affective dimension in which the person actively enjoys the crossing. This is what distinguishes CHAR-19 from CHAR-2 (unintentional sin — which involves no affective pleasure in transgression): the deliberate boundary-crosser can develop a positive affective orientation toward the transgression itself. The resolution brings blessedness (Psa 32:1) that is the affective opposite of both the transgression's pleasure and its consequent burden.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=16115, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** S — The 147-verse corpus does not explicitly engage the creative faculty in connection with transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing. The characteristic operates through will, cognition, affect, conscience, and memory rather than through creative origination.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=16116, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** S — No direct evidence. The corpus is silent on the creative faculty's engagement with transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=16117, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** S — The silence on the creative faculty is consistent with CHAR-19's constitutive volitional nature: the characteristic is defined by the will's act of crossing a given boundary rather than the creative generation of new boundaries or possibilities. Noted.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=16118, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The volitional faculty is the primary faculty of CHAR-19 — the definition names it: "the will that knowingly crosses a boundary." (1) *Will as the agent of transgression*: Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled against me" — the will's choice of defiance; Eze 2:3 — "nations of rebels who have rebelled against me" — sustained volitional rebellion; Hos 8:1 — "they have transgressed my covenant" — the will's covenant-breaking; (2) *Will as the required faculty for remedy*: Eze 18:30 — "repent and turn from all your transgressions" — the repentance-turn addresses the will; Isa 59:20 — "those who turn from transgression" — the turning is volitional.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=16119, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression *progressively enslaves* the will that committed it. Psa 19:13 — "let them not have dominion over me!" — transgression seeks dominion over the will; Pro 29:6 — "ensnared in his transgression" — the will is trapped by what it chose; Pro 12:13 — "ensnared by the transgression of his lips" — the spoken transgression returns to ensnare the speaker's will. The progression is: free volitional act (the first crossing) → repeated crossings → accumulated ensnaring → dominion over the will. The will that chose transgression is progressively constrained by what it chose until it requires divine liberation.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=16120, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The volitional pattern reveals CHAR-19's central inner-being dynamic: the will that freely chose to cross the boundary is progressively enslaved by the transgression it chose. Psa 19:13 — "great transgression" seeking dominion over the will; Pro 29:6 — ensnared. This self-generated volitional bondage distinguishes CHAR-19 from all other M10 characteristics: the will is both the agent that creates the characteristic (the deliberate crossing) and the primary victim of its sustained operation (the ensnaring). The remedy requires the will to turn (Eze 18:30) — but this turning itself requires divine assistance (Eze 18:31 — "make yourselves a new heart and new spirit").

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=16121, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Agency is engaged through both the transgression's active commission and the active pursuit of its resolution. (1) *Agency in transgression's commission*: Isa 5:18 (CHAR-18 cross, but relevant here) — "those who draw iniquity with cords... sin as with cart ropes" — active, purposeful pursuit; Amos 4:4 — "come to Bethel and transgress" — ironic invitation to purposeful transgressive action; (2) *Agency in transgression's resolution*: Pro 28:13 — "he who confesses and forsakes them" — the forsaking is an agentive act; Eze 18:31 — "cast away from you all the transgressions" — the casting-away is a commanded agentive act of the will.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=16122, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Sustained transgression *ensnares and impairs* agency. Pro 12:13 — "ensnared by the transgression of his lips" — the transgressor's own acts become the trap constraining future agency; Pro 29:6 — "ensnared in his transgression" — the evil man's free agency is captured by what he has done. Psa 19:13 — "let them not have dominion over me" — the threat is that transgression assumes agentive control over the person. The remedy restores agency: Eze 18:31 — "cast away from you" — the casting-away is the agentive recovery act made possible by the new heart.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=16123, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The agency pattern confirms the volitional-enslavement dynamic: the person who actively transgressed (agentive commission) is progressively incapacitated in their agency (Pro 29:6 — ensnared; Psa 19:13 — dominion threatened). The active agency at commission (Amos 4:4 — "come and transgress") is replaced by the passivity of being held in transgression's grip (Pro 29:6). This is the characteristic's most significant inner-being consequence: the agent of the crossing becomes the captive of what they crossed into.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=16124, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through the self-assessment and prophetic-declaration dimensions. (1) *Self-assessment*: Job 34:37 — "he adds rebellion to his sin; he claps his hands among us" — Elihu's moral evaluation of Job's transgression; Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions" — the honest self-moral-assessment; (2) *Denial*: Pro 28:24 — "that is no transgression" — the morally evaluating faculty deployed to deny; Jer 2:29 — "why do you contend with me?" — moral evaluation inverted to self-justification; (3) *Prophetic declaration*: Mic 3:8 — "to declare to Jacob his transgression and to Israel his sin" — the prophet's moral evaluation corrects the community's self-evaluation.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=16125, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Sustained transgression *inverts* the moral evaluation faculty. Pro 28:24 — the moral evaluation faculty is used to deny transgression rather than recognise it; Jer 2:29 — the evaluating faculty produces self-justification ("why do you contend with me?") rather than self-recognition. The sustained transgressor's moral evaluation is not eliminated but redirected: it becomes skilled at moral rationalisation in the self-direction. The remedy restores accurate self-evaluation: Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions" — the conscience-engaged penitent's accurate moral self-assessment.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=16126, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that the most sophisticated moral-evaluation pathology in CHAR-19 is Pro 28:24's verbal denial — "that is no transgression" — the knowing transgressor who suppresses conscience through deliberate cognitive reframing. This is the characteristic's most distinctive inner-evaluation finding: the knowing dimension of CHAR-19 (Rom 4:15 — transgression requires law-knowledge) is precisely what the transgressor weaponises against accurate moral evaluation (Pro 28:24 — using their own knowledge of what counts as transgression to deny that their act qualifies).

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=16127, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — conscience is engaged as the interior monitor that transgression progressively suppresses. Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart; there is no fear of God before his eyes" — the transgression has displaced the God-fear that is conscience's marker; Pro 28:13 — "whoever conceals his transgressions will not prosper" — the concealment is an active conscience-suppression act. Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions, and my sin is ever before me" — the engaged conscience produces the constant inner awareness that precedes repentance.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=16128, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Psa 36:1's "transgression speaks... in his heart" is the most analytically significant conscience-impairment verse in M10: transgression has installed itself at the heart's innermost depth as a voice that displaces the God-fear which is conscience's foundational operation. The transgression speaks *in the place where conscience speaks*, displacing it with a counter-counsel that drives the will away from God. Pro 28:13 — the concealment of transgressions is the active conscience-suppression. The remedy (Psa 32:5 — "I will confess my transgressions to the Lord") is the conscience's recovery of its own voice.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=16129, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The conscience pattern reveals CHAR-19's most distinctive inner-being feature: Psa 36:1's "transgression speaks deep in his heart" names transgression as a pseudo-conscience — an inner voice that has taken up residence at the heart's deepest level and speaks in place of God-fear. The authentic conscience says "fear God and keep from transgression" (Psa 36:1's inverse); the transgression-voice says "do this and there is no consequence." This displacement of conscience by the transgression-voice is CHAR-19's defining inner dynamic, absent from all other M10 characteristics studied.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=16136, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — God operates toward the transgression-bearing person in five modes. (1) *Establishing the boundary that makes transgression possible*: Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — God's prior law-giving is the precondition; Exo 23:21 — "obey his voice; do not rebel against him" — the command precedes and enables transgression; (2) *Counting and tracking toward threshold*: Amos 1:3 — "for three transgressions... and for four, I will not revoke the punishment" — God's patient counting; Dan 9:24 — the decreed seventy weeks "to finish the transgression"; (3) *Withholding pardon in certain cases*: Exo 23:21 — "he will not pardon your transgression, for my name is in him"; Jos 24:19 — "he will not forgive your transgressions or your sins"; (4) *Blotting out by sovereign act*: Isa 43:25 — "I, I am he who blots out your transgressions for my own sake"; Psa 103:12 — removing transgressions as far as east from west; (5) *Declaring it through prophets*: Isa 58:1 — "declare to my people their transgression"; Mic 3:8 — "to declare to Jacob his transgression."

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=16137, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Two bases operating in tension. (1) *Holiness and covenant faithfulness that must reckon with transgression*: Exo 23:21 — "for my name is in him" — the divine name's honour is the basis for non-pardon in this case; Isa 66:24 — "the men who have rebelled against me... their worm shall not die" — divine holiness produces irrevocable judgment on persistent rebellion; (2) *Steadfast love that moves toward pardon*: Num 14:18 — "the Lord is slow to anger and abounding in steadfast love, forgiving iniquity and transgression"; Mic 7:18 — "pardoning iniquity and passing over transgression for the remnant of his inheritance... because he delights in steadfast love" — steadfast love is explicitly named as the basis of pardon.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=16138, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — God's disposition is *patient justice sustained by steadfast love*. Amos 1:3 — the three-and-four threshold formula shows patience exhausted only at a specific limit; God has counted and waited. Isa 43:25 — "for my own sake" — the pardon flows from God's own character rather than the transgressor's merit. Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel" — God identifies the rebel-identity before birth yet still speaks to the person through Isaiah; identification of the transgressor does not equal abandonment. The defining eschatological statement: Dan 9:24 — "to finish the transgression" — God has appointed an end to transgression; his ultimate disposition is toward transgression's completion and termination.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=16139, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — God-to-human operation (boundary-setting, counting, withholding/granting pardon, prophetic declaration) is extensively evidenced across all nine VCGs. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=16140, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through the acknowledgment-repentance-return movement. Psa 32:5 — "I will confess my transgressions to the Lord, and you forgave the iniquity of my sin" — the movement toward God is through verbal confession; Eze 18:30 — "repent and turn from all your transgressions, lest iniquity be your ruin" — the commanded movement is volitional repentance-turning; Isa 59:20 — "a Redeemer will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who turn from transgression" — the human turning is the condition for the divine coming; Hos 14:1 — "return, O Israel, to the Lord your God, for you have stumbled because of your iniquity."

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=16141, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Volitional turning*: Eze 18:28 — "he considered and turned away from all the transgressions that he had committed" — the turn is both cognitive (considered) and volitional (turned away); (2) *Honest verbal confession*: Psa 32:5 — "I said, 'I will confess my transgressions to the Lord'" — the non-concealment; Pro 28:13 — "he who confesses and forsakes them will obtain mercy"; (3) *Non-self-justification*: Lam 3:42 — "we have transgressed and rebelled, and you have not forgiven" — the honest acknowledgment without self-excuse, even while still bearing unforgiven guilt.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=16142, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The human-to-God direction reveals that the relationship remains accessible through the specific act of turning. Isa 59:20 — "a Redeemer will come to Zion, to those who turn from transgression" — God moves toward the one who turns; the turn is the signal of relational re-engagement. Isa 44:22 — "return to me, for I have redeemed you" — God's prior redemptive act is the ground for the human's return. The relationship is not terminated by transgression but suspended awaiting the turn: Hos 5:15 — "until they acknowledge their guilt and seek my face" — God waits, and the relationship is restored when the person moves.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=16143, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Human-to-God operation (confession, turning, acknowledgment, return) is extensively evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=16144, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through three extension mechanisms. (1) *Leadership propagation*: Isa 43:27 — "your first father sinned, and your mediators transgressed against me" — the mediators' transgression extended to the community they were meant to represent; Jer 2:8 — "the shepherds transgressed against me" — leaders' transgression affects those they lead; (2) *Anger-driven contagion*: Pro 29:22 — "a man of wrath stirs up strife, and one given to anger causes much transgression" — anger in the leader generates transgression in others; (3) *Communal multiplication*: Pro 29:16 — "when the wicked increase, transgression increases" — the social-contagion mechanism; Amos 4:4 — "come to Bethel and transgress... multiply transgression" — the social encouragement of communal transgression.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=16145, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Authority or representative position*: Isa 43:27 — "mediators" and "first father" — the representative position makes one's transgression community-affecting; Jer 2:8 — "shepherds" whose transgression affects the flock; (2) *Volitional invitation*: Amos 4:4 — "come to Bethel and transgress; to Gilgal, and multiply transgression" — the intentional invitation of others into transgressive practice; the extending person's will is engaged in actively recruiting others.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=16146, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — For leadership-propagation (Isa 43:27; Jer 2:8): the person must hold the representative office (mediator, shepherd) whose transgression carries community-weight. For anger-contagion (Pro 29:22): the person must have allowed anger to become their constitutive inner disposition — "one given to anger" — before that disposition generates multiple transgressions in the social environment. For communal invitation (Amos 4:4): the person must be already established in transgressive practice before they can credibly invite others to "come and transgress."

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=16147, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — The giving/extension direction is evidenced through leadership propagation (Isa 43:27; Jer 2:8), anger-driven contagion (Pro 29:22), and communal invitation (Amos 4:4; Pro 29:16). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=16148, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through three reception modes. (1) *Personal commission*: Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions" — personally owned; Rom 2:23 — "you who boast in the law dishonor God by breaking the law" — personally incurred; (2) *Generational inheritance*: Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression, offspring of deceit" — inheriting a transgression-identity from the parental generation; Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel" — the rebel-label received before personal choice; (3) *Adamic universal*: Rom 5:15/16/17/18 — "one man's trespass" producing "condemnation for all men" — the transgression received through the Adamic representative.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=16149, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Reception-enabling: (1) *Volitional turning*: Eze 18:28 — "considered and turned away from all the transgressions" — the turn is both prerequisite and expression of remedy-reception; (2) *Non-concealment*: Pro 28:13 — "he who confesses and forsakes them will obtain mercy" — open acknowledgment rather than concealment; (3) *Honest self-knowledge*: Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions" — accurate self-perception of the transgression-state. Reception-blocking: (1) *Concealment*: Pro 28:13 — "whoever conceals his transgressions will not prosper"; (2) *Self-justification*: Jer 2:29 — "why do you contend with me?" — self-defensive posture against God; (3) *Love of transgression*: Pro 17:19 — "whoever loves transgression loves strife" — affective attachment to the transgression forecloses remedy.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=16150, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three states evidenced. (1) *Blessedness*: Psa 32:1 — "blessed is the one whose transgression is forgiven, whose sin is covered" — the primary affective state is blessedness; (2) *Teaching capacity*: Psa 51:13 — "then I will teach transgressors your ways, and sinners will return to you" — the forgiven transgressor's inner state enables them to become a guide for other transgressors; the experience of forgiveness is converted into instructional wisdom; (3) *Relational restoration*: Eze 18:22 — "none of the transgressions he has committed shall be remembered against him; for the righteousness that he has done he shall live" — the forgiven state is one of full life-standing before God, unclouded by prior transgression-record.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=16151, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced across personal commission (Psa 51:3), generational inheritance (Isa 48:8; Isa 57:4), and Adamic universal (Rom 5:15/16). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=16152, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — relational depth intensifies the transgression's moral weight. Isa 1:2 — "children have I reared and brought up, but they have rebelled against me" — the parental-investment relationship makes the rebellion more morally severe; Pro 18:19 — "a brother offended is more unyielding than a strong city" — the closer the relational bond, the harder the barrier created by transgression. Across relational distance: Amos 1:3/6/9/11/13/2:1 — foreign nations (Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab) without covenant relationship still accumulate transgression before God and face judgment. Both distances operate; the covenant relationship intensifies accountability (Amos 3:2 — "you only have I known... therefore I will punish you").

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=16153, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The characteristic crosses covenantal boundaries extensively. VCG-03's Amos oracle-series (Amos 1:3–2:6) covers eight nations including six outside Israel's covenant: Damascus, Gaza, Tyre, Edom, Ammon, Moab all accumulate transgression that reaches God's threshold. Dan 8:23 — "when transgressors have reached their limit" — a non-Israelite (Seleucid) context. The parabasis-family NT verses (VCG-04) apply to all law-knowers regardless of ethnic covenant membership (Rom 2:23/25/27 — the law-boaster and the law-keeper, Jew and Gentile).

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=16154, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The relational scope is universal. Rom 5:15/16/17/18 — "one man's trespass led to condemnation for all men" — the Adamic trespass's scope is humanity-as-whole; Psa 130:3 (CHAR-18 adjacent) — "if you, O Lord, should mark iniquities, O Lord, who could stand?" implies universal human transgression-liability. The Amos oracle-series (eight nations) confirms that transgression is a cross-cultural, cross-covenant universal moral reality. The relational scope spans God-to-individual, individual-to-individual, leader-to-community, and Adam-to-all-humanity.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=16155, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Relational scope (universal — covenantal and non-covenantal, individual and corporate) is extensively evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=16156, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — through the Isa 53:12 servant-intercession passage and the Heb 2:2 angelic-law passage. Isa 53:12 — "he was numbered with the transgressors; yet he bore the sin of many, and makes intercession for the transgressors" — the servant (who is both human and divine agent) intercedes among the transgressors; this intercession is directed toward God on behalf of human transgressors. Heb 2:2 — "the message declared by angels proved to be reliable, and every transgression or disobedience received a just retribution" — angels are named as the mediators of the law whose reliability undergirds the transgression-retribution principle.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=16157, S) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-19]** S — The 147-verse corpus does not explicitly name adversarial spiritual beings (Satan/demons) in connection with transgression-as-deliberate-boundary-crossing. The transgression originates in human will (Psa 36:1 — speaks from the heart; Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled") without named adversarial instigation in this corpus.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=16158, E) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — in two instances. (1) *Law mediated by angels* (Heb 2:2): the law against which transgression is committed was "declared by angels" — angelic ministry is the delivery mechanism for the divine law that defines transgression's boundaries; (2) *Intercession for transgressors* (Isa 53:12): the servant "makes intercession for the transgressors" — the mediatorial intercession moves on behalf of transgressors toward God. Both instances place angelic/servant-mediatorial ministry in the framework of transgression's legal definition (Heb 2:2) and resolution (Isa 53:12).

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=16159, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Adversarial spiritual beings (T4.6.2) are absent from the corpus. Angelic mediation of the law (Heb 2:2) and servant-intercession for transgressors (Isa 53:12) are evidenced. The spiritual-beings interface is present in the law-definition side (angelic delivery — Heb 2:2) and the resolution side (intercession — Isa 53:12) but not in the transgression-generation side.

---
