  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=16160, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Both — in the negative direction and through its reversal. (1) *Negative condition change*: Isa 1:5 — sustained revolt produces "the whole head is sick, and the whole heart faint" — comprehensive constitutional deterioration; Lam 1:14 — transgressions "bound into a yoke... set upon my neck... he caused my strength to fail" — physical-volitional incapacitation; (2) *Negative orientation change*: Eze 2:3 — "nations of rebels who have transgressed against me... from their fathers to this very day" — sustained transgression establishes rebellion as the constitutive inner orientation; (3) *Positive transformation through repentance*: Eze 18:28 — "turned away from all the transgressions... he shall surely live" — the turning transforms the death-trajectory into life; Eze 18:31 — "make yourselves a new heart and new spirit" — the positive inner renewal follows transgression's casting-away.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=16161, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Both dimensions evidenced. (1) *Reversible through repentance and divine pardon*: Eze 18:28 — turning reverses the death-sentence; Psa 103:12 — "as far as the east is from the west, so far does he remove our transgressions from us" — fully reversible through divine act; Isa 44:22 — "I have blotted out your transgressions like a cloud" — total erasure; (2) *Irreversible for persistent rebels*: Isa 66:24 — "their worm shall not die, their fire shall not be quenched" — the end of persistent rebellion is permanent, irrevocable judgment; Exo 23:21 — "he will not pardon your transgression" — in specific contexts non-pardon is declared irrevocable; (3) *Conditionally limited*: Amos 1:3 — "I will not revoke the punishment" — once the threshold is reached, reversal of judgment is foreclosed even if individual repentance remains possible.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=16162, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transformation evidence is extensive in all directions (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=16163, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — two opposing sequences. (1) *Transgression-to-enslavement sequence*: known boundary (Rom 4:15 — law required) → will crosses (Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled") → transgression speaks from heart (Psa 36:1) → accumulation toward threshold (Amos 1:3 — counted) → enslaving grip (Pro 29:6 — ensnared; Psa 19:13 — dominion threatened) → yoke-burden (Lam 1:14) → systemic inner collapse (Isa 1:5 — head sick, heart faint); (2) *Recognition-to-restoration sequence*: self-knowing (Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions") → confession (Psa 32:5; Pro 28:13) → volitional turning (Eze 18:28) → divine blotting-out (Isa 43:25) → blessedness (Psa 32:1) → teaching capacity (Psa 51:13).

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=16164, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The transgression-to-enslavement sequence reveals the progressive self-generated bondage of the will: each stage tightens the grip. Psa 36:1 — transgression installed as the heart's inner voice → Pro 29:6 — the will ensnared → Lam 1:14 — yoke set on the neck → Isa 1:5 — systemic inner collapse. Each stage makes the next more difficult to reverse. The recognition-to-restoration sequence reveals that the reversal is possible at any stage before the eschatological threshold: Eze 18:28 — "he considered and turned away... he shall surely live." The sequence confirms: the earlier the turning, the less constitutionally embedded the transgression; but the turning remains possible until the divine appointment for judgment.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=16165, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Sequence (both transgression-to-enslavement and recognition-to-restoration) is extensively evidenced (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=16166, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three change-mechanisms. (1) *Threshold-accumulation triggering judgment*: Amos 1:3 — the "three and four" counting mechanism; Dan 8:23 — "when transgressors have reached their limit"; Dan 9:24 — "to finish the transgression" — the accumulation mechanism measures toward a divinely appointed tipping-point; (2) *Voice-displacement of God-fear*: Psa 36:1 — "transgression speaks deep in his heart; there is no fear of God before his eyes" — the mechanism is the progressive internal replacement of conscience's God-fear with transgression's counsel, producing behavioural change from within; (3) *Self-ensnaring bondage*: Pro 29:6 — "ensnared in his transgression" — the transgression-acts become the restraining mechanism on future will; Pro 12:13 — "ensnared by the transgression of his lips."

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=16167, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes across VCG registers. (1) *VCG-03 (threshold)*: mechanism is divine patience reaching its limit through counted accumulation (Amos 1:3/6/9); (2) *VCG-02 (revolt)*: mechanism is the degrading systemic effect of persistent inner defiance — Isa 1:5 — continued revolt despite punishment produces whole-person inner deterioration; (3) *VCG-07 (oppressive weight)*: mechanism is the weight-accumulation of individual transgressions binding into a yoke (Lam 1:14) — the additive weight mechanism; (4) *VCG-04 (law-violation)*: mechanism is the legal-standing nullification — Rom 2:25 — "your circumcision becomes uncircumcision" — transgression renders religious identity void by removing its legal validity.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=16168, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Mechanisms are evidenced across four distinct VCG contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=16169, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — extensively. (1) *Suffering as transgression's consequence*: Lam 1:5 — "the Lord has afflicted her for the multitude of her transgressions; her children have gone away, captives before the foe" — captivity as direct consequence; Jer 5:6 — "because their transgressions are many... therefore a lion shall strike them down" — savage judgment as consequence; Isa 50:1 — "for your transgressions your mother was sent away" — exile as transgression's consequence; (2) *Suffering proportioned to transgression*: Job 34:37 — "he adds rebellion to his sin" — Elihu frames Job's protest as adding transgression to existing suffering; the protest is itself a form of transgression that increases the burden.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=16170, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Suffering *reveals* transgression (Jer 5:6 — the savage beast-judgment reveals what the transgressions were) and can also *provoke further rebellion*: Isa 1:5 — "why will you still be struck down? Why will you continue to rebel?" — the people continue to transgress even while being punished; suffering does not break the transgressive will but may intensify its defiance. Job 34:37 — Elihu claims suffering has provoked Job's rebellion/transgression. Thus suffering reveals (through judgment) and can paradoxically both break and harden the transgressing will.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=16171, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — The relationship between transgression and suffering is evidenced (Lam 1:5; Jer 5:6; Isa 1:5; Isa 50:1; Job 34:37). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=16172, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — as both the negative formation arc's defining act and generational character-producer. (1) *Generational character formation*: Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression, offspring of deceit" — transgression is transmitted as a generational identity-character; Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel" — the rebel-character is assigned at birth, formed within a transgression-legacy; (2) *Individual character formation*: Eze 2:3 — "nations of rebels who have transgressed against me... from their fathers to this very day" — the multi-generational pattern becomes the defining character of the community.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=16173, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Transgression functions as the *negative formation arc's primary driver* across both individual and generational dimensions. The single volitional boundary-crossing (Adam's trespass — VCG-05 — Rom 5:15) produced the foundational formation-pattern that all subsequent transgressions repeat and deepen. The Amos nations whose transgressions are counted (VCG-03) have been formed into their transgression-patterns by sustained communal moral choices. Eze 18's personal-accountability counter-argument (VCG-06) establishes that even within transgression-legacy, the individual's formation arc is still active and the turning is still possible.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=16174, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Formation evidence is present through individual volitional development (Eze 18:28/30), generational transmission (Isa 48:8/57:4), and Adamic paradigm (Rom 5:15). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=16175, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — two eschatological endpoints. (1) *Judgment and finish*: Dan 9:24 — "seventy weeks are decreed... to finish the transgression, to put an end to sin" — transgression has a divinely decreed completion-point; Dan 8:23 — "when the transgressors have reached their limit, a king of bold face shall arise" — the eschatological crisis follows transgression reaching its maximum; Isa 66:24 — "their worm shall not die, their fire shall not be quenched" — the persistent rebels' eschatological state; (2) *Complete removal*: Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins"; Psa 103:12 — as far as east from west; Eze 18:22 — "none of the transgressions... shall be remembered against him" — forgiven transgressions have no eschatological standing.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=16176, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-19]** E — For the unrepentant: Amos 1:3's "three and four" accumulation anticipates the eschatological day when the threshold is finally reached for all nations and individuals (Dan 8:23 — "when transgressors have reached their limit"). The present counting is the pre-eschatological accumulation toward Dan 9:24's "finish the transgression." For the repentant: Psa 32:1's blessedness of forgiven transgression anticipates Eze 37:23's eschatological state — "they shall not defile themselves anymore with any of their transgressions... and they shall be my people, and I will be their God."

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=16177, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced in both judgment-completion (Dan 9:24; Isa 66:24) and total-removal (Isa 43:25; Psa 103:12; Eze 37:23) directions. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=16178, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-19]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: divine-count record (Amos 1:3), identity-level labelling (Isa 48:8), and yoke-burden (Lam 1:14). Developmental consequences. (1) *Divine-count deposit*: Amos 1:3's counted transgression develops toward the threshold (Dan 8:23 — "reached their limit") — the count grows until judgment; the developmental consequence is progressive approach to irrevocable judgment; (2) *Identity-level deposit*: Isa 48:8 — "called a rebel from before birth" develops into Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression" — the identity-label is transmitted generationally; the developmental consequence is the formation of transgression-identity as a hereditary character; (3) *Yoke-burden deposit*: Lam 1:14 — yoke on neck causing strength to fail — the developmental consequence is progressive physical-volitional incapacitation.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=16179, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes. Isa 57:4 — "children of transgression, offspring of deceit" — the transgression-identity is transmitted to the next generation; Isa 48:8 — "from before birth called a rebel" — the rebel-label precedes personal choice within a transgression-legacy family or community. The Amos oracle-series (VCG-03 — Amos 1:3–2:6) shows that national transgressions accumulate across generations before reaching the threshold; the children inherit both the accumulated transgression-count and its judgment-trajectory.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=16180, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-19]** E — T2.8 found deposit evidence. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---

T5 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       21/21 ✓ (T5.1.1–T5.1.3, T5.2.1–T5.2.3, T5.3.1–T5.3.3, T5.4.1–T5.4.3, T5.5.1–T5.5.3, T5.6.1–T5.6.3, T5.7.1–T5.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       21× [CHAR-19] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Psa 19:13/32:1/51:13/103:12; Pro 12:13/28:13/29:6; Isa 1:2/5/43:25/44:22/48:8/50:1/57:4/66:24; Jer 5:6; Lam 1:5/14; Eze 2:3/18:28/30/31/37:23; Amos 1:3/6/9; Dan 8:23/9:24; Rom 2:25/5:15; Exo 23:21; Job 34:37 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.


---

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=16181, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur within the 147-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-18 (Iniquity as accumulated moral crime)*: Exo 34:7 — "forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin" — paired explicitly; Lev 16:16/21 — both iniquities and transgressions require atonement; the three-term formula (iniquity, transgression, sin) recurs across the corpus; (2) *CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record)*: Amos 5:12 — "I know how many are your transgressions" — God's record of transgression; Isa 43:25 — "I will not remember your sins" — divine memory/record clearing; (3) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Psa 32:5 — "I will confess my transgressions to the Lord"; Lev 16:21 — "Aaron shall confess over it all the iniquities... all their transgressions"; (4) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received)*: VCG-06 entire (13V) — Psa 32:1/51:1/103:12/Isa 43:25/44:22/53:8/Eze 18:22/28/30/31/Mic 7:18 — the pardon-of-transgression register.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=16182, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-19 as the *volitional act-face* of M10: while CHAR-18 is the accumulated crime-weight and CHAR-17 is the subjective guilt-experience and CHAR-16 is the constituted sinner-identity, CHAR-19 is the specific volitional act of knowing defiance that generates all these. The three-term formula (iniquity, transgression, sin — Exo 34:7; Lev 16:21; Num 14:18) places CHAR-19 as one of the three primary moral-failure categories in the OT's foundational vocabulary. CHAR-19's co-occurrence with CHAR-3 (confession) and CHAR-14 (forgiveness) confirms it as the specific category that both require addressing.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=16183, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are extensively evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=16184, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-19]** E — CHAR-19 consistently *follows* CHAR-1 (wilful sinning acts that constitute the boundary-crossings) and *precedes* CHAR-18 (the accumulated crime-weight produced by the transgressions). CHAR-19 consistently *accompanies* CHAR-13 (transgression enters the divine record at the moment of commission). CHAR-19 consistently *precedes* CHAR-3 (confession specifically names the transgressions) and CHAR-14 (forgiveness specifically pardons them). The sequence: CHAR-1 (sinning act) → CHAR-19 (the crossing — the moment it is registered as transgression) → CHAR-18 (accumulated in the record as crime) → CHAR-13 (held in divine memory) → CHAR-17 (felt as guilt) → CHAR-3 (confessed) → CHAR-14 (pardoned).

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=16185, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The relationship with CHAR-1 is *constitutive*: the sinning act (CHAR-1) is the boundary-crossing that CHAR-19 names at its volitional-knowing dimension; they are the same act described at different levels. The relationship with CHAR-18 is *developmental*: the transgression (CHAR-19) develops into accumulated iniquity (CHAR-18) through repetition. The relationship with CHAR-13 is *simultaneous*: the transgression enters the divine record at the moment of commission. The relationship with CHAR-14 is *causal*: the transgression (CHAR-19) is what forgiveness (CHAR-14) resolves.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=16186, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Sequential patterns (constitutive, developmental, simultaneous, causal) are evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=16187, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-18 (Iniquity as accumulated crime)*: accumulated transgressions produce the iniquity-weight; Amos 1:3/6/9 — counted transgressions accumulate toward the crime-threshold; (2) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: the transgression's recognised weight produces the subjective guilt-state; Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions, and my sin is ever before me" — knowing the transgression produces the ever-present guilt-consciousness; (3) *CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character)*: sustained transgression produces the rebel-identity (Isa 48:8 — "called a rebel from birth"; Eze 2:3 — "nations of rebels").

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=16188, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: the sinning acts of CHAR-1 are the specific contents of the transgressions; CHAR-19 is CHAR-1 seen at the knowing-boundary-crossing level; (2) *Absence of CHAR-5 (Fear of God)*: Psa 36:1 — "there is no fear of God before his eyes" — the absence of God-fear (which CHAR-19's adjacent cluster M10 would not cover but the inner-being landscape would) is the enabling condition that allows transgression's inner voice to speak; (3) *Adam's paradigm* (VCG-05 — Rom 5:15): the first transgression produced all subsequent transgression-possibility through the Adamic constitution.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=16189, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-19]** E — CHAR-19's VCG-05 (Adam-paradigm paraptōma — 5 verses) is constitutively the origin-event of CHAR-11 (Sin as universal condition): the "one man's trespass" (Rom 5:15/16/17/18) is the foundational boundary-crossing that established the universal sin-condition (CHAR-11). CHAR-19 is thus a constituent element of CHAR-11 at the historical-origin level — CHAR-11's universal condition was created by CHAR-19's paradigmatic first act. CHAR-19's VCG-06 (pardon and erasure — 13 verses) overlaps with CHAR-14's receiving dimension: the transgression-pardon is what CHAR-14 receives.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=16190, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are extensively evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=16191, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The three-term formula (a.von/iniquity — CHAR-18, pe.sha/transgression — CHAR-19, chat.tat/sin — CHAR-1) appears across the corpus: Exo 34:7; Lev 16:21; Num 14:18; and many individual verse-pairings. The vocabulary of CHAR-19 (pe.sha/pa.sha) is distinct from CHAR-18's (a.von H5771G) and CHAR-1's (cha.ta family) while consistently paired with them. The NT parabasis-family (VCG-04) is unique to CHAR-19 in M10; the paraptōma (VCG-05) terms do not appear in other M10 characteristics studied.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=16192, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The pe.sha/pa.sha root (*pāšaʿ* — to break away, to rebel) does not share its root with other primary M10 Hebrew terms. The sa.rah root (*sārāh* — to turn aside, to revolt — VCG-02) also appears nowhere else in M10. The NT parabasis root (*para* + *bainō* — to go beside/past) is unique to CHAR-19 within M10. Root-level: CHAR-19 is the most terminologically distinct characteristic in M10, with four distinct term-families (pe.sha/pa.sha; sa.rah; parabasis-family; paraptōma) none of which is shared at the root level with other M10 characteristics.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=16193, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The vocabulary distinctiveness confirms that CHAR-19 is conceptually irreducible within M10: no other characteristic shares its primary vocabulary family (pe.sha/pa.sha root). The three-term formula (Exo 34:7 — iniquity, transgression, sin) positions CHAR-19 as one of the three fundamental moral-failure categories alongside CHAR-18 (iniquity) and CHAR-1 (sin); the three are distinct enough to require separate vocabularies. CHAR-19 is the boundary-crossing register; CHAR-18 is the accumulated-crime register; CHAR-1 is the missing-the-mark register — each requires its own term-family.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=16194, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Minimal root-level sharing is evidenced; CHAR-19 is the most terminologically distinct characteristic in M10 (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=16195, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-19]** E — CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning) most closely resembles CHAR-19 — both address the knowing, wilful dimension of sin. The precise distinction: CHAR-1 is the wilful sinning act (the missing-the-mark); CHAR-19 is specifically the boundary-crossing dimension of that act — the awareness of a known line being deliberately crossed. Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — CHAR-19 requires a specific known boundary; CHAR-1 can exist without the parabasis-structure of law-knowledge. CHAR-19 is the law-violation register; CHAR-1 is the broader wilful-act register.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=16196, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The overlap is at the three-term-formula verses: Exo 34:7 — "forgiving iniquity and transgression and sin" — where all three categories occur together and the boundary between them is not always explicitly marked. The precise boundary: CHAR-19 (pe.sha) is the rebellion/breach-of-relationship dimension; CHAR-1 (cha.ta) is the act-of-missing-the-mark dimension; CHAR-18 (a.von) is the accumulated-crime dimension. All three can describe the same sinful event from different analytical angles; the boundary between CHAR-19 and CHAR-1 is the knowing-boundary-crossing emphasis that pe.sha/pa.sha carries versus the directional-failure emphasis of cha.ta.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=16197, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The distinction is one of *kind with directional specification*: CHAR-1 is the genus (wilful sinning act — missing the mark); CHAR-19 is a species (the specific kind of wilful sinning that constitutes a known boundary-crossing/rebellion). CHAR-19 adds the relational-boundary-violation dimension to what CHAR-1 names as the act. The knowing-defiance of CHAR-19 is a kind-distinction from CHAR-1's act-of-missing (which can be less relationally oriented) — not merely a more severe instance of the same thing.

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=16198, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=16199, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Several. (1) Lev 16:16/21 — all iniquities and transgressions confessed over scapegoat — shared with CHAR-18 (iniquity) and CHAR-3 (confession); (2) Exo 34:7 — three-term formula — shared with CHAR-18 (iniquity) and CHAR-1 (sin); (3) Psa 51:1/3 — "blot out my transgressions; I know my transgressions" — shared with CHAR-17 (guilt-awareness) and CHAR-14 (forgiveness sought); (4) Isa 53:5/8 — "pierced for our transgressions; stricken for the transgression of my people" — shared with CHAR-14 (vicarious bearing as forgiveness) and CHAR-18 (iniquity crushing him); (5) Rom 5:15/16/17/18 (paraptōma) — shared with CHAR-11 (sin as universal condition) through the one-man's-trespass condemnation of all.

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=16200, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Lev 16:16/21 shared with CHAR-18 and CHAR-3 confirms that the atonement-event addresses CHAR-19 (transgression), CHAR-18 (iniquity), and CHAR-3 (confession) simultaneously — the priestly ritual encompasses all three dimensions of the same moral-failure reality. Isa 53:5/8 shared with CHAR-14 and CHAR-18 confirms that the servant's bearing addresses both CHAR-19's specific transgressions and CHAR-18's accumulated iniquities simultaneously — one redemptive act resolves both characteristics. Rom 5:15/16/17/18 shared with CHAR-11 confirms that CHAR-19's paradigmatic first transgression (Adam's trespass) is the event that created CHAR-11's universal sin-condition.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=16201, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified across CHAR-1, CHAR-3, CHAR-11, CHAR-14, CHAR-17, CHAR-18 (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=16202, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three dimensions are shared: (1) *The accumulated-crime dimension* (transgression producing accumulated weight) is shared with CHAR-18; (2) *The confession-producing dimension* (known transgression generating confession) is shared with CHAR-3; (3) *The forgiveness-resolution dimension* (transgression requiring and receiving pardon) is shared with CHAR-14. CHAR-19's unique dimensions — (a) the knowing-boundary-crossing emphasis (Rom 4:15 — requires law), (b) Psa 36:1's pseudo-conscience as inner voice of transgression, and (c) Amos 1:3's threshold-counting formula — are not shared with any other M10 characteristic studied.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=16203, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-19 as the *volitional-act gateway* of M10: its shared dimensions all point to what other characteristics do with what CHAR-19 produces (CHAR-18 accumulates it, CHAR-3 confesses it, CHAR-14 forgives it). CHAR-19's unique dimensions — the knowing-requirement (Rom 4:15), the inner-voice displacement of conscience (Psa 36:1), and the counted-threshold structure (Amos 1:3) — are what make transgression irreducible within M10: these cannot be derived from any other characteristic.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=16204, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-19]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced across three adjacent characteristics (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T6 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T6.1.1–T6.1.3, T6.2.1–T6.2.3, T6.3.1–T6.3.4, T6.4.1–T6.4.4, T6.5.1–T6.5.4, T6.6.1–T6.6.3, T6.7.1–T6.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-19] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 34:7; Lev 16:16/21; Num 14:18; Psa 32:1/36:1/51:1/3; Isa 43:25/44:22/48:8/53:5/8; Amos 1:3/5:12; Dan 9:24; Rom 4:15/5:15/16/17/18; Eze 2:3/18:22/28/30/31 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T7.

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=16205, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Four primary term-families. (1) **pe.sha (H6588)** / **pa.sha (H6586)** — noun/verb "transgression/to transgress, to rebel, to break away" — OT dominant (115 verses); root *pāšaʿ* means "to break away from, to revolt, to rebel" — naming the act as a relational-political breaking-away from authority. The root is the vocabulary of political rebellion applied to the moral-theological sphere; (2) **sa.rah (H5627)** — "revolt, turning aside, apostasy" — 7 verses in VCG-02; root *sārar* — to turn aside, to rebel — names the inner defiance as an orientation-reversal; (3) **parabasis (G3847) / parabatēs (G3848) / parabainō (G3845)** — NT "transgression/transgressor/to transgress" — 15 verses; Greek compound *para* (beside/past) + *bainō* (to go, to step) — literally "stepping past/beside" — the boundary-crossing spatial metaphor made explicit; (4) **paraptōma (G3900)** — "trespass, false step, lapse" — 5 verses (VCG-05 Adam-paradigm); Greek *para* + *piptō* (to fall beside) — a stumbling aside from the right path.

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=16206, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Wide across both OT and NT. (1) *Pe.sha (H6588)*: used as noun throughout — singular (Psa 32:1 — "whose transgression is forgiven") and plural (Psa 51:1 — "blot out my transgressions"; Amos 1:3 — "for three transgressions of Damascus"); (2) *Pa.sha (H6586)*: verb form — Qal active (Eze 2:3 — "who have rebelled against me"; Jer 2:29 — "you have all transgressed against me"); Hiphil causative (Amos 4:4 — "come to Bethel and transgress"); (3) *Parabasis (G3847)*: noun — "the transgression" (Rom 4:15; Gal 3:19; Heb 2:2/9:15); *parabatēs (G3848)*: agent-noun "transgressor" (Rom 2:25/27; Gal 2:18; Jam 2:9/11); *parabainō (G3845)*: verb "to transgress/break" (Mat 15:2/3; Act 1:25; 2Jo 9); (4) *Paraptōma (G3900)*: noun — "trespass" (Rom 5:15/16/17/18/20). The grammatical range — from noun to verb to agent-noun — covers the act (verb), the event (noun), and the person constituted by the act (agent-noun).

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=16207, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Pe.sha/pa.sha within CHAR-19 covers: (1) *Interpersonal breach*: Gen 31:36 — "what is my transgression?"; Gen 50:17 — forgiveness for brothers' transgression against Joseph; (2) *Covenant rebellion against God*: Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled against me"; Hos 8:1 — "transgressed my covenant and rebelled against my law"; (3) *Personal moral breach*: Psa 51:3 — "I know my transgressions"; Job 13:23 — "make me know my transgression"; (4) *Threshold-crossing accumulation*: Amos 1:3/6/9 — "for three transgressions... and for four"; (5) *Oppressive weight*: Lam 1:14 — "transgressions were bound into a yoke." Parabasis covers: (1) *Law-violation*: Rom 4:15; Heb 2:2; (2) *Tradition-breaking*: Mat 15:2/3. Paraptōma covers: (1) *Paradigmatic first trespass*: Rom 5:15/16/17/18; (2) *Law-increasing function*: Rom 5:20.

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=16208, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — the four term-families actively distinguish distinct aspects. (1) *Pe.sha/pa.sha* — the rebellion/breach-of-relationship register (OT); (2) *Sa.rah* — the revolt/turning-aside register (VCG-02 — inner defiance oriented against authority); (3) *Parabasis-family* — the law-violation/boundary-crossing register (NT — legally precise); (4) *Paraptōma* — the stumbling-aside/trespass register (NT — less legalistic, more moral-direction emphasis). The four families together cover: relational rebellion (pe.sha), inner defiant orientation (sa.rah), legal boundary-crossing (parabasis), and moral-path deviation (paraptōma) — four distinct angles on the same deliberate boundary-crossing reality.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=16209, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The structural opposite is named through obedience and faithfulness vocabulary. Exo 23:21 — "pay careful attention to him and obey his voice; do not rebel against him" — *šāmaʿ* (obey/listen) is the structural opposite of pa.sha (rebel). Hos 14:9 — "the upright walk in them, but transgressors stumble in them" — the upright walker (*yĕšārîm* — straight, upright) is the named opposite person-type. Rom 4:15 — the existence of law implies the existence of obedience as the alternative to transgression; Rom 5:19 — "by the one man's obedience the many will be made righteous" — obedience (the opposite of Adam's trespass) is explicitly named as the structural opposite.

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=16210, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes — multiple person-type terms. (1) *Pa.sha (H6586) as agent*: Isa 46:8 — "you transgressors" — direct person-type address; Isa 53:12 — "numbered with the transgressors"; (2) *Parabatēs (G3848)*: "transgressor" as agent-noun — Rom 2:25/27; Gal 2:18; Jam 2:9/11; (3) *Sa.rah (H5627)*: Deu 13:5 — "that prophet... taught rebellion" — the revolt-teacher as person-type; (4) *Psa 51:13* — "transgressors" as a class the forgiven person will teach — the person-type as a reachable group. CHAR-19 is the richest person-type vocabulary in M10.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=16211, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Yes. Psa 51:1 — "blot out my transgressions" — the specific plea using *māḥāh* (to blot out, erase); Isa 43:25 — God's own use of the same term — "I blot out your transgressions for my own sake"; Psa 103:12 — "so far does he remove our transgressions from us" — *rāḥaq* (to remove far off) as the metaphor of spatial-relational distance. The supplication vocabulary for transgression-resolution is specifically erasure/blotting-out and removal — stronger than mere covering.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=16212, E) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The OT/NT relationship reveals *continuity with legal precision*. The OT pe.sha/pa.sha rebellion-vocabulary (relational, covenantal, political) is conceptually continuous with the NT parabasis-vocabulary (legal, boundary-specific). Rom 4:15 — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — makes the parabasis-definition explicit: what the OT pe.sha named relationally, the NT parabasis names legally. The OT term covers rebellion broadly (covenant, relationship, authority); the NT term specifies the law-known-boundary-crossing structure. The paraptōma (VCG-05) adds the stumbling-aside dimension that the OT terms do not directly name. The NT vocabulary clarifies and specifies what the OT vocabulary named more broadly.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=16213, E) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Parabasis (G3847) and parabatēs (G3848) are the NT's distinctive terminological contribution to CHAR-19: the parabasis-family appears in the NT in technical theological usage (Rom 4:15; Gal 3:19; Heb 2:2/9:15; Jam 2:9/11) and names the legally-precise transgression that could only exist where law had been promulgated. Rom 4:15's definitional statement — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — appears to be a distinctly NT theological articulation of the parabasis concept, not replicated in the OT in this explicit form.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=16214, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-19]** E — The full vocabulary arc — from pe.sha's relational rebellion (Gen 31:36 — interpersonal; Isa 1:2 — "they have rebelled against me") through sa.rah's inner defiance (Isa 1:5 — "whole head sick and whole heart faint" from continued revolt) through the Amos threshold-counting (Amos 1:3 — "three and four") through Isa 53:5/8's servant pierced for transgressions through Dan 9:24's appointed finish through parabasis's law-violation precision (Rom 4:15) through paraptōma's Adam-paradigm (Rom 5:15/16/17/18/20) — reveals the complete arc of deliberate boundary-crossing from its first instance (Adam's trespass) through its OT relational-rebellion register through its eschatological appointed finish (Dan 9:24) through its NT legal precision to its resolution through the servant's bearing.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=16215, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Three primary anchors. (1) *Psa 36:1* — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart; there is no fear of God before his eyes" — pe.sha functions as the speaking-subject; transgression is personified as an inner voice that has taken up residence in the heart; the verse's key move is locating transgression as an internal counselling presence; (2) *Rom 4:15* — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — parabasis functions as the predicate of the conditional: law is the necessary precondition; the definitional structure reveals the law-boundary-crossing nature; (3) *Amos 1:3* — "for three transgressions of Damascus, and for four" — pe.sha functions as the counted moral-crimes of a nation; the counting formula reveals the threshold-measurement structure.

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=16216, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Five literary forms. (1) *Prophetic oracle/judgment* (Isa 1:2/48:8/58:1; Amos 1:3–2:6; Jer 2:8/29; Eze 2:3): requires reading within the covenant-lawsuit and judgment-announcement framework; (2) *Lament/penitential psalm* (Psa 51:1/3/13; Psa 32:1/5): requires reading within the personal confession-and-forgiveness register; (3) *Wisdom proverb* (Pro 10:12/19/12:13/17:9/19/18:19/28:13/29:6/22): requires reading within the practical-consequences framework; (4) *Legal-cultic code* (Lev 16:16/21; Exo 22:9/23:21): requires reading within the covenant-obligation framework; (5) *Epistle* (Rom 4:15/5:14/15; Gal 2:18/3:19; Heb 2:2/9:15; Jam 2:9/11): requires reading within the theological argument framework.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=16217, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Four key argument structures. (1) *Rom 4:15's conditional-definitional argument*: premise — the law brings wrath; premise — without law there is no transgression; conclusion — transgression is legally constituted; structure — the law's existence creates the category of transgression by establishing the boundary; (2) *Amos 1:3's threshold-judgment argument*: premise — Damascus has committed three and four transgressions; conclusion — I will not revoke the punishment; structure — accumulated threshold of violations triggers irrevocable judgment; (3) *Eze 18:28's turning-reverses-consequence argument*: premise — he committed transgressions; premise — he considered and turned from all of them; conclusion — he shall surely live; structure — volitional reversal dissolves the death-consequence; (4) *Rom 5:15/18's trespass-grace parallel*: premise — one man's trespass brought condemnation to all; conclusion — one act of righteousness brings justification for all; structure — the scale of the transgression's consequence is matched and exceeded by grace.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=16218, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Four contextual settings. (1) *Covenant-accusation / prophetic lawsuit context* (Isa 1:2; Jer 2:8; Eze 2:3; Amos 1:3–2:6): God as plaintiff, Israel/nations as defendant, transgression as the formal charge; (2) *Penitential-liturgical context* (Psa 51:1/3; Lev 16:16/21 — Day of Atonement): the sanctuary/prayer as the setting where transgression is confessed and addressed; (3) *Theological-argument context* (Rom 4:15/5:14/15; Gal 3:19; Heb 2:2/9:15): epistolary argument about law, transgression, and grace; (4) *Wisdom-instruction context* (Pro 10:12/19/12:13/28:13/29:6): practical teaching about transgression's relational and social consequences.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=16219, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Two complementary primary anchors. (1) *Psa 36:1* — "transgression speaks to the wicked deep in his heart; there is no fear of God before his eyes" — primary anchor for the inner-being location: it is the only verse in the 147-verse corpus that explicitly names where transgression lives and how it operates internally — as a speaking voice installed at the heart's deepest level displacing God-fear; (2) *Rom 4:15* — "where there is no law there is no transgression" — primary definitional anchor for the knowing-dimension: it names the law-knowledge requirement that makes transgression constitutively deliberate; no law = no transgression confirms that CHAR-19 is the knowing-boundary-crossing category.

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=16220, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Psa 36:1 uniquely reveals that transgression is not merely an act or a condition but an *inner voice* — a pseudo-conscience installed at the heart's core that speaks counsel in place of God-fear. No other verse in the 147-verse corpus names transgression as a counselling presence within the person; all other verses name it as an act, a record, a burden, or a relational status. Psa 36:1's personification of transgression as a speaking entity "deep in the heart" is CHAR-19's most analytically distinctive verse. Rom 4:15 uniquely reveals that transgression is *structurally constituted by law*: the boundary crossed makes the act transgression; without the known boundary, the same act is not transgression. This is the clearest definition of the deliberate-knowing dimension in the entire M10 corpus.

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=16221, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-19]** E — **Social-cognitive theory of moral agency** (Bandura's moral disengagement mechanisms) and **legal philosophy of transgression** (H.L.A. Hart's *The Concept of Law* — the "primary rules" violated by transgression; Paul Ricoeur's *The Symbolism of Evil* — the "sin" register as broken relationship) provide the most illuminating lenses. Bandura illuminates the moral-disengagement mechanisms by which known transgressors sustain their behaviour (Pro 28:24 — "that is no transgression" as euphemistic labelling; Jer 2:29 — moral justification through contention). Hart's legal philosophy illuminates Rom 4:15's definition: transgression requires a primary rule (the law) before the rule-violating act constitutes a transgression — no rule, no transgression.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=16222, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Bandura's moral disengagement illuminates Pro 28:24's "that is no transgression" as the classic euphemistic labelling mechanism: the transgressor redefines the act to escape moral self-condemnation. Jer 2:29 — "why do you contend with me?" — is the advantageous comparison and moral justification mechanism combined. Hart's primary/secondary rules framework illuminates why CHAR-19 is the most legally-structured M10 characteristic: it is the only characteristic in M10 that requires a known rule as its definitional precondition (Rom 4:15). Ricoeur's "sin" as broken relationship illuminates Isa 1:2's "children have I reared... but they have rebelled" — the transgression is a relational rupture with the father-God that Ricoeur identifies as the deepest OT moral category.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=16223, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Bandura's framework treats moral disengagement as a correctable cognitive error. The verse evidence treats the transgression as an objective moral reality before God (Amos 5:12 — "I know how many are your transgressions") independently of whether the transgressor engages or disengages morally. Hart's legal framework treats the law as a human social construct. Rom 4:15 treats the law as divinely promulgated, with transgression assessed against the divine standard rather than the social consensus. Ricoeur's framework treats sin primarily as lived human experience. Psa 36:1's "transgression speaks deep in the heart" adds an inner-agency dimension to transgression — as a pseudo-conscience that Ricoeur's phenomenological account does not quite capture.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=16224, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-19]** E — Hart's legal framework surfaces the concept of *secondary rules* (rules about rules) that CHAR-19 might engage: the question of when the threshold of transgression has been reached (Amos 1:3 — "three and four") is a secondary-rule question about how primary rule-violations are counted and adjudicated. The gap is the decision-procedure by which God adjudicates the threshold — the mechanism of "I will not revoke the punishment" after the three-and-four count. The verse evidence names the outcome (judgment after threshold) without specifying the decision-procedure. No further verse investigation is needed; the gap is a legal-philosophical complement to the corpus's theological declaration.

---
