## CHAR-20 — Faithlessness as covenant-breaking sin (189 rows: E=179 S=10 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=16414, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The 101-verse corpus reveals God's character across five dimensions. (1) *God as the one who is personally betrayed*: Lev 26:40 — "treachery that they committed against me"; Eze 39:23 — "they dealt treacherously with me"; Dan 9:7 — "treachery that they have committed against you" — the faithlessness is directed against God personally; it wounds the relational bond from within; (2) *God as the faithful covenant-keeper whose faithfulness is the measure of the betrayal*: Exo 21:8 — breaking faith implies a prior faith that was owed; 1Ch 10:13 — "Saul died for his breach of faith... he did not keep the command of the Lord" — faithfulness to divine command is the standard violated; (3) *God as the divine witness of covenant bonds*: Mal 2:14 — "the Lord was witness between you and the wife of your youth" — God himself is the named witness of the covenant whose breaking constitutes treachery; (4) *God as the one who judges treachery*: Eze 17:20 — "enter into judgment with him there for the treachery he has committed against me"; 2Ch 30:7 — "he made them a desolation"; (5) *God as the one who responds to confession of treachery*: Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery" — God's response to honest confession of faithlessness is embedded in the text; restoration follows confession.

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=16415, S) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-20]** S — No verse in the 101-verse corpus attributes faithlessness to God. God appears as the one against whom faithlessness is directed (Lev 26:40; Dan 9:7), as the faithful witness (Mal 2:14), as the judge of treachery (Eze 17:20), and as the faithful standard against which Israel's unfaithfulness is measured. The characteristic is exclusively creaturely.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=16416, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The silence is definitionally necessary: faithlessness requires a covenant bond whose violation constitutes the betrayal. God is the faithful party (the covenant-initiator) whose faithfulness is the very standard that makes human faithlessness intelligible as betrayal. Mal 2:10 — "Have we not all one Father? Has not one God created us? Why then are we faithless to one another?" — the appeal to God as common Father is the reason faithlessness toward one another is wrong; God's faithfulness is the constitutive ground that makes human faithlessness a moral failure. God cannot betray himself.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=16417, S) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-20]** S — The corpus provides no evidence of any positive created purpose for faithlessness. The characteristic is entirely a fallen-condition category: the breaking of the very covenant bonds that the human person was designed to inhabit and maintain. No verse presents unfaithfulness as a designed feature of the image-bearer.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=16418, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Entirely a response to the fallen condition. Hos 6:7 — "but like Adam they transgressed the covenant; there they dealt faithlessly with me" — the comparison to Adam's covenant-breach locates the faithlessness paradigm at the fall; Isa 48:8 — "from of old your ear has not been opened... I knew that you would surely deal treacherously" — the foreknown constitutional unfaithfulness presupposes the fallen condition. No verse situates faithlessness within the original created design; the image-bearer was constituted for covenant faithfulness, not its betrayal.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=16419, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — in two directions. (1) *Judgment endpoint*: 2Ch 30:7 — "he made them a desolation" — sustained faithlessness reaches its judgment-endpoint; Eze 39:23 — God hid his face and delivered to adversaries; 1Ch 10:13 — "Saul died for his breach of faith" — the individual death-verdict; (2) *Resolution and forgetting*: Eze 39:26 — "they shall forget their shame and all the treachery they have practiced against me, when they dwell securely" — the eschatological fullness is the dwelling-secure state in which the treachery has been fully resolved and its shame eclipsed; Lev 26:40-42 — "if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery... I will remember my covenant" — the covenant-memory restoration is the positive eschatological orientation.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=16420, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness expresses the image through its *inverted relational capacity*: the image-bearer is constituted as a covenant-capable being — one who can make, sustain, and break binding relational commitments. Only a being made in God's image can enter a covenant with God (as Israel did) and then betray it as a moral act. Mal 2:14 — "the Lord was witness between you and the wife of your youth, to whom you have been faithless, though she is your companion and your wife by covenant" — the covenant-witnessing capacity and the covenant-betrayal capacity belong to the same image-bearing creature. The capacity for faithfulness (the image's positive dimension) is the prerequisite for faithlessness (its fallen expression).

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=16421, S) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-20]** S — Exclusively creaturely (T0.1.2). God is the faithful standard; faithlessness is the creature's betrayal of the bond God constituted. The characteristic has no divine analogue.

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=16422, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness reveals the image in its *relationally broken condition*: the covenant-capable image-bearer has deployed their relational capacity against the covenant-partner rather than for them. 2Ch 36:14 — "all the officers of the priests and the people likewise were exceedingly unfaithful, following all the abominations of the nations. And they polluted the house of the Lord" — the image's relational capacity has been redirected from God-toward to God-against, and the consequence is the pollution of the very sanctuary that the image was designed to maintain. 1Ch 5:25 — "they broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods of the peoples of the land" — the image's capacity for covenantal devotion has been transferred to false objects.

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=16423, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through two typological axes. (1) *The marital-faithlessness type of Israel's unfaithfulness to God*: Jer 3:20 — "as a treacherous wife leaves her husband, so have you been treacherous to me, O house of Israel" — the marital faithlessness is the type of the Israel-God covenant betrayal; Mal 2:14-16 — marital faithlessness is treated with the same theological seriousness as covenant-faithlessness toward God; the marriage-covenant is the human type pointing toward the divine-Israel bond; (2) *Adam-type covenant-breaking*: Hos 6:7 — "like Adam they transgressed the covenant; there they dealt faithlessly with me" — Adam's covenant-breach is the founding type of which Israel's faithlessness is the national repetition.

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=16424, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The marriage-covenant type runs from human institution (marriage) toward divine-covenant reality: the faithlessness of a wife to her husband types the faithlessness of Israel to God. The direction is from human covenant-breaking (type) to divine-human covenant-breaking (antitype/reality). The Adam-type runs from the founding betrayal (Adam's covenant-breach) to the national repetitions (Israel's faithlessness to God). Both typological directions: human instance → divine-covenant reality.

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=16425, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Typological use is evidenced (T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T0 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       12/12 ✓ (T0.1.1–T0.1.3, T0.2.1–T0.2.3, T0.3.1–T0.3.3, T0.4.1–T0.4.3)
  2. Scope marker:       12× [CHAR-20] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: 10/10 E findings cite evidence ✓ (T0.1.2, T0.3.2 are S)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 21:8; Lev 26:40; 1Ch 5:25/10:13; 2Ch 30:7/36:14; Eze 17:20/39:23/26; Dan 9:7; Hos 6:7; Jer 3:20; Mal 2:10/14/15/16; Isa 48:8 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T1.


---

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=16426, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Faithlessness as covenant-breaking sin." Three signals. (1) *Faithlessness*: not the discrete act of sin but the dispositional inner quality of the person who breaks covenant — the opposite of the faithfulness (*ʾĕmûnāh*) that the image-bearer was designed to sustain; (2) *As covenant-breaking*: the defining context — faithlessness is not generic disloyalty but specifically the breaking of a known covenantal bond; Mal 2:14 — "your wife by covenant" — the covenant is what makes the faithlessness betrayal rather than mere preference-change; (3) *Sin*: the moral-theological classification — faithlessness is not merely relational failure but sin against God and the covenant-order. The name signals: the most relational sin-category in M10 — the will that dissolves what God constituted to be binding.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=16427, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two primary term-families, both exclusively OT Hebrew with no NT vocabulary. (1) **ma.al (H4603/H4604)** — verb/noun "to commit a breach of faith, to act unfaithfully, to be treacherous" — VCG-01 dominant (unfaithfulness toward God); the root implies a violation of sacred trust, a transgression of what is consecrated or owed; Lev 5:15 — "breach of faith" in holy things; 1Ch 10:13 — "breach of faith with the Lord"; (2) **ba.gad (H0898)** — verb "to act treacherously, to be faithless, to betray" — VCG-02/03 dominant (interpersonal and constitutional treachery); the root names the inner disposition of betrayal; Mal 2:14 — "been faithless" (*bāgadtāh*) to the wife of one's youth; Isa 48:8 — "deal treacherously" as a foreknown constitutional bent. Both terms name the relational-covenantal dimension of the betrayal as the defining feature.

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=16428, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-20]** E — All three. *Directional*: faithlessness is directed against someone — Lev 26:40 — "treachery that they committed against me"; Jer 3:20 — "treacherous to me, O house of Israel" — the direction is always against a covenant-partner; *Relational*: the characteristic is constitutively relational — Mal 2:14 — "though she is your companion and your wife by covenant" — the prior covenant-bond is what makes the faithlessness treachery rather than mere departure; *Constitutional*: Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel... I knew you would surely deal treacherously" — faithlessness is a constitutional inner bent that God foreknew; 2Ch 28:22 — "in time of his distress he became yet more faithless" — deepening under pressure confirms constitutional character.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=16429, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The corpus reveals CHAR-20 as primarily a *dispositional inner character that expresses through volitional acts of betrayal*. The three VCGs illuminate this: (1) VCG-01 — unfaithfulness toward God: Eze 14:13 — "a land sins against me by acting faithlessly" — faithlessness as the quality of a land's settled sin-posture; (2) VCG-02 — interpersonal treachery: Pro 11:3 — "the crookedness of the treacherous destroys them" — treachery as a crookedness of character; (3) VCG-03 — constitutional character: Isa 24:16 — "the traitors have betrayed, with betrayal the traitors have betrayed" — the person IS the betrayal; Isa 48:8 — foreknown constitutional bent. The characteristic is more disposition than discrete act — the will's settled orientation of betrayal.

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=16430, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Three constituent elements corresponding to the three VCGs. (1) *Unfaithfulness toward God* (VCG-01 — 57V): the primary covenant-betrayal directed upward; ma.al dominant; (2) *Relational treachery between persons* (VCG-02 — 34V): the horizontal faithlessness between persons within the covenant community; ba.gad dominant; (3) *Constitutional character of treachery* (VCG-03 — 10V): the settled inner character that IS faithlessness — the person who has become a traitor as a defining identity.

---

#### T1.2.3 (cf=16431, E) — What is the current best working description of this characteristic — encapsulating its constitutional location, the faculties it primarily engages, and its impact on the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Working description: *Faithlessness as covenant-breaking sin (CHAR-20) is the inner disposition of deliberate unfaithfulness — the will that breaks covenantal loyalty, betrays a trust, acts treacherously against God or persons. Three registers: (1) Unfaithfulness toward God (VCG-01 — 57V): the primary covenant-betrayal directed upward — Judah taken into exile "because of their breach of faith" (1Ch 9:1); Saul died "for his breach of faith" (1Ch 10:13); Ahaz became "yet more faithless" under distress (2Ch 28:22); (2) Relational treachery between persons (VCG-02 — 34V): the horizontal faithlessness within human covenant bonds — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless to the wife of your youth" (Mal 2:15); "like Adam they transgressed the covenant; there they dealt faithlessly with me" (Hos 6:7); marital ordeal (Num 5:12/27); (3) Constitutional inner character (VCG-03 — 10V): the settled identity of the betrayer — "the traitors have betrayed, with betrayal the traitors have betrayed" (Isa 24:16); "I knew you would surely deal treacherously" (Isa 48:8). Distinguished from CHAR-19 (transgression as boundary-crossing) by its relational-betrayal emphasis and from CHAR-18 (iniquity as accumulated crime) by its covenant-loyalty dimension. Cross-register flags with M13 (Truth/Faithfulness) and M31 (Faith/Unbelief) travel with all three VCGs.*

---

#### T1.3.1 (cf=16432, E) — What is the structural opposite of this characteristic — the inner-being reality that stands against or excludes it?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The structural opposite is *covenant faithfulness — the settled inner disposition of steadfast loyalty to the bond*. Psa 25:3 — "they shall be ashamed who are wantonly treacherous" paired with "none who wait for you shall be put to shame" — the waiting-on-God is the structural opposite of treacherous waiting-for-self; Psa 119:158 — "I look at the faithless with disgust, because they do not keep your commands" — the command-keepers are the structural opposite; Mal 2:15/16 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and let none of you be faithless" — the guarded-spirit faithfulness; Pro 11:3 — "the integrity of the upright guides them, but the crookedness of the treacherous destroys them" — integrity (*tummāh* — blamelessness, completeness) is the named structural opposite.

---

#### T1.3.2 (cf=16433, E) — What does this characteristic explicitly exclude or resist?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The characteristic explicitly excludes: (1) *God's continued presence*: Jos 22:31 — "today we know that the Lord is in our midst, because you have not committed this breach of faith" — the absence of faithlessness is the condition of divine presence; Eze 39:23 — "I hid my face from them" — faithlessness drives God's face away; (2) *Divine blessing and stability*: Pro 13:15 — "the way of the treacherous is their ruin"; Pro 11:3 — "the crookedness of the treacherous destroys them"; (3) *Standing in the covenant community*: Eze 20:38 (CHAR-19 adjacent) — rebels purged from among Israel; 2Ch 30:7 — those who were faithless made "a desolation"; (4) *Reliable relational presence*: Pro 25:19 — "trusting in a treacherous man in time of trouble is like a bad tooth or a foot that slips."

---

#### T1.3.3 (cf=16434, E) — What is this characteristic not — where does it end and something else begin?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Three boundaries. (1) *Not CHAR-19 (Transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing)*: CHAR-19 is the knowing-crossing of a known boundary (law-violation requiring a law — Rom 4:15); CHAR-20 is the betrayal of a relational covenant bond (requiring a prior trust relationship); CHAR-20 adds the intimate-bond dimension — the betrayal of someone who counted on you; (2) *Not CHAR-5 (Refusal to repent)*: CHAR-5 is the ongoing hard heart that refuses to turn; CHAR-20 is the active act of betrayal within an existing covenant; the unfaithful person may not yet have refused repentance — their unfaithfulness may trigger remorse (Ezr 10:6 — Ezra mourned the faithlessness); (3) *Not mere social unreliability*: CHAR-20 requires a covenant context — the prior bond that is broken; Pro 25:19 — the treacherous man in time of trouble — this enters CHAR-20 territory when the trust was covenantal rather than mere social expectation.

---

#### T1.4.1 (cf=16435, E) — In what distinct ways does this characteristic operate within the inner person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Five distinct inner modes. (1) *As settled inner disposition*: Isa 48:8 — foreknown constitutional bent of the will; 2Ch 28:22 — deepening under distress rather than breaking; (2) *As act of inner allegiance-transfer*: 1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods of the peoples" — the will's active transfer of loyalty to false objects; (3) *As hidden inner duplicity*: Jer 12:6 — "though they speak friendly words to you" — the treachery is concealed behind friendly appearance; (4) *As inner hardening*: 2Ch 28:22 — "in the time of his distress he became yet more faithless to the Lord" — the will deepens its unfaithfulness under circumstances that would normally generate repentance; (5) *As spirit-level orientation*: Mal 2:15/16 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless" — the spirit is the named locus requiring vigilance against faithlessness.

---

#### T1.4.2 (cf=16436, E) — Does this characteristic operate differently depending on context, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes across the three VCG registers. (1) *Vertical toward God* (VCG-01): faithlessness operates as direct covenant-betrayal against the God who constituted the bond; Eze 20:27 — treachery equated with blasphemy; the relational damage is at the deepest level; (2) *Horizontal toward persons* (VCG-02): faithlessness operates as betrayal of human trust within a known relational bond — marital (Num 5:12; Mal 2:14), fraternal (Job 6:15), civic (Judg 9:23); (3) *Constitutional character* (VCG-03): faithlessness operates as an identity-level character trait — Isa 24:16 (compounded betrayal), Isa 33:1 (the one who will be betrayed in turn), Isa 48:8 (foreknown from birth). VCG-01 is the primary theological register; VCG-02 the relational-human register; VCG-03 the character-level register.

---

#### T1.4.3 (cf=16437, E) — Does this characteristic have a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — and if so, how does it function?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — in two modes. (1) *Treachery through friendly speech*: Jer 12:6 — "they speak friendly words to you" while acting treacherously — the verbal dimension of faithlessness is the friendly-word mask over the betraying action; Psa 59:5 — "those who treacherously plot evil" — speech that plans betrayal; (2) *Confession of faithlessness*: Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery" — verbal acknowledgment before God; Ezr 10:2 — "we have broken faith with our God" — the community's verbal confession; Ezr 10:10 — "you have broken faith and married foreign women" — Ezra's verbal indictment. The speech-mode is either the mask of treachery (friendly words concealing betrayal) or its reversal through honest confession.

---

#### T1.5.1 (cf=16438, E) — What is the first or most immediate inner-being response to receiving or encountering this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The immediate responses vary. (1) *Grief and mourning in the witness*: Ezr 9:4 — "those who trembled at the words of the God of Israel... gathered around me while I sat appalled until the evening sacrifice" — the spiritually aware person's immediate response to witnessing faithlessness is appalled grief; Ezr 10:6 — Ezra "mourning over the faithlessness of the exiles," fasting and drinking nothing; (2) *Shame*: Dan 9:7 — "to us open shame" — the immediate experiential consequence; Psa 25:3 — "they shall be ashamed who are wantonly treacherous" — shame; (3) *Hardening*: 2Ch 28:22 — "in time of his distress he became yet more faithless" — the perverse response of deepened unfaithfulness; (4) *Despair and desolation*: Lam 1:2 — "all her friends have dealt treacherously with her" producing weeping bitterly.

---

#### T1.5.2 (cf=16439, E) — Is the immediate response consistent across the verse evidence, or does it vary?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Variable. Shame (Dan 9:7; Psa 25:3) is the appropriate affective response to recognised faithlessness. Grief and mourning (Ezr 9:4/10:6) is the prophetically-sensitive person's immediate response to witnessing others' faithlessness. Hardening (2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz becoming yet more faithless under distress) is the perverse immediate response of the constitutionally faithless person. Desolation (Lam 1:2 — weeping bitterly when friends deal treacherously) is the victim's immediate response to experienced faithlessness. The immediate response ranges from shame-to-repentance through grief-for-others through hardening to victimised desolation.

---

#### T1.5.3 (cf=16440, E) — Where the verse evidence is silent on immediate response, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — For VCG-03's constitutional-character register (Isa 24:16; Isa 33:1; Isa 48:8; Jer 3:8/11), the immediate inner response of the person who has been characterised by treachery from before birth (Isa 48:8) to their own constitutional unfaithfulness is not described. The corpus names the identity and its divine foreknowledge but not the moment-by-moment inner experience of the person constitutionally given over to faithlessness.

---

#### T1.6.1 (cf=16441, E) — What does this characteristic produce in the inner being over time?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Four sustained inner-being effects. (1) *Progressive hardening*: 2Ch 28:22 — "in the time of his distress he became yet more faithless" — the sustained disposition deepens rather than breaks; the will's faithlessness becomes more entrenched the more it is enacted; (2) *Eventual ruin*: Pro 11:3 — "the crookedness of the treacherous destroys them"; Pro 13:15 — "the way of the treacherous is their ruin"; Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the sustained treachery produces the destruction of the self; (3) *Exile and desolation*: 1Ch 9:1 — Judah exiled "because of their breach of faith"; 2Ch 30:7 — "he made them a desolation"; Dan 9:7 — "open shame... in all the lands to which you have driven them"; (4) *Communal pollution*: 2Ch 36:14 — "exceedingly unfaithful... polluted the house of the Lord" — sustained faithlessness corrupts what the person is entrusted to protect.

---

#### T1.6.2 (cf=16442, E) — What states, qualities, capacities, or orientations does sustained exposure to or possession of this characteristic establish?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Three established states. (1) *Crookedness of character*: Pro 11:3 — "the crookedness of the treacherous" — sustained faithlessness establishes a constitutive inner moral distortion; (2) *Capture by lust*: Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — sustained treachery establishes an inner captivity to craving as the driving force of the will; (3) *Desire for violence*: Pro 13:2 — "the desire of the treacherous is for violence" — the sustained faithless orientation establishes appetite for harm as the settled inner drive.

---

#### T1.6.3 (cf=16443, E) — Does the sustained effect differ from the immediate response — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes. The immediate response (shame — Dan 9:7; grief — Ezr 9:4; hardening — 2Ch 28:22) is episodic. The sustained effects (crookedness — Pro 11:3; captivity to lust — Pro 11:6; desire for violence — Pro 13:2; exile and desolation — 1Ch 9:1) are constitutional states established over time by unaddressed faithlessness. The most significant sustained effect is the progressive hardening: 2Ch 28:22's Ahaz — distress produced more faithlessness rather than repentance — the will has become constitutively set in the faithless direction and no longer responds to corrective pressure.

---

#### T1.7.1 (cf=16444, E) — What inner conditions or orientations enable genuine reception of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Three enabling conditions. (1) *Pride*: 2Ch 26:16 — "when he was strong, he grew proud, to his destruction. For he was unfaithful to the Lord his God" — pride is the inner condition that gives rise to faithlessness; Uzziah's strength produced pride, and pride produced the overstepping of God-given limits; (2) *Absence of spirit-vigilance*: Mal 2:15 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and let none of you be faithless" — the command to guard implies that failure to guard is what enables faithlessness; the spirit is the inner domain where faithlessness originates and must be resisted; (3) *Divided or misplaced allegiance*: 1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods of the peoples" — allegiance transferred to false objects enables faithlessness to the true covenant-partner.

---

#### T1.7.2 (cf=16445, E) — What inner conditions block, distort, or prevent reception?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Three blocking conditions. (1) *Spirit-vigilance*: Mal 2:15/16 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless" — the active inner guarding of the spirit is the most explicit faithlessness-prevention command in the corpus; it targets the exact origin-level of the characteristic; (2) *Fear of God*: 2Ch 30:7 — the fathers who were faithless are the negative example; their negative example presupposes the positive alternative of God-fearing loyalty; Ezr 9:4 — "all who trembled at the words of the God of Israel" — God-fear produces the response to faithlessness rather than the faithlessness itself; (3) *Honest awareness of covenant obligations*: Mal 2:14 — "the Lord was witness between you" — awareness that God witnesses the covenant prevents its casual breaking.

---

#### T1.7.3 (cf=16446, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-20]** E — 2Ch 28:22 names it most precisely: "in the time of his distress he became yet more faithless to the Lord — this same King Ahaz" — the inner state is a will that has become constitutively oriented away from God such that the very pressure that would normally drive a person toward God drives this person further from him. Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the inner state under sustained faithlessness is captivity to the will's own craving; the person is not free to turn toward God because the lust has assumed agentive control. The hardened-under-distress state of 2Ch 28:22 is CHAR-20's most extreme dispositional portrait.

---

#### T1.8.1 (cf=16447, E) — What is the primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic from the programme's dimension vocabulary?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The primary dimension is **the will's covenantal orientation — the inner disposition of loyalty or betrayal within a known relational bond**. Mal 2:15/16 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless" — the spirit is named as the inner locus requiring guarding; Isa 48:8 — the foreknown constitutional bent of the will toward treachery — the will's settled inner orientation is the characteristic's primary faculty. The characteristic is constitutively volitional and dispositional rather than cognitive or affective at the primary level.

---

#### T1.8.2 (cf=16448, E) — What evidence from the verse evidence supports this classification?

**[CHAR-20]** E — (1) Mal 2:15 — "guard yourselves in your spirit" — the spirit as the named locus of faithlessness-danger; (2) 2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz becoming "yet more faithless" under distress — the will's sustained dispositional hardening; (3) Isa 48:8 — "I knew you would surely deal treacherously" — the foreknown constitutional inner bent; (4) 1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods" — the will's active allegiance-transfer; (5) Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the will enslaved by its own faithless orientation.

---

#### T1.8.3 (cf=16449, E) — Does this characteristic carry secondary dimensions — and if so, what are they, and do they compete with the primary classification?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — three secondary dimensions. (1) *Affective*: Jer 3:20 — "as a treacherous wife leaves her husband" — the emotional dimension of marital faithlessness; Lam 1:2 — weeping bitterly as victim of treachery — the affective impact on the betrayed; (2) *Social-communal*: Mal 2:10 — "why then are we faithless to one another, profaning the covenant of our fathers?" — faithlessness has a horizontal social dimension within the covenant community; (3) *Speech-based*: Jer 12:6 — friendly words masking treachery; Ezr 10:2 — verbal confession of faithlessness — the communicative dimension is secondary but present.

---

  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T1.1.1–T1.1.3, T1.2.1–T1.2.3, T1.3.1–T1.3.3, T1.4.1–T1.4.3, T1.5.1–T1.5.3, T1.6.1–T1.6.3, T1.7.1–T1.7.3, T1.8.1–T1.8.3)

  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 26:40; Num 5:12/27; Judg 9:23; 1Ch 5:25/9:1/10:13; 2Ch 26:16/28:22/30:7/36:14; Ezr 9:4/10:2/6/10; Dan 9:7; Psa 25:3/119:158; Pro 11:3/6/13:2/15/25:19; Isa 24:16/48:8; Jer 3:20/12:6; Lam 1:2; Eze 14:13/20:27/39:23; Hos 6:7; Mal 2:10/14/15/16 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Response approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T2 continues in the next response.


---

## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=16450, E) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — most explicitly of any M10 characteristic studied. Mal 2:15 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and let none of you be faithless to the wife of your youth" — the spirit (*rûaḥ*) is the explicitly named locus requiring vigilance against faithlessness; Mal 2:16 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless" — repeated. The double injunction to "guard yourselves in your spirit" names the spirit as the level at which faithlessness must be resisted. This is the only verse in M10's studied corpus that names the spirit (*rûaḥ*) as the specific inner level from which a M10 characteristic must be guarded against.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=16451, E) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — Mal 2:15/16's "guard yourselves in your spirit" confirms that faithlessness originates at the spirit level: the guarding is against what would arise from within the spirit if left unguarded. 2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz becoming "yet more faithless" in distress — the progressive deepening of faithlessness is a spirit-level orientation-change under pressure. Isa 48:8 — "I knew you would surely deal treacherously" — the foreknown constitutional bent toward treachery is a spirit-level constitutional characterisation.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=16452, E) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Spirit-level location (Mal 2:15/16 — guard your spirit; Isa 48:8 — foreknown constitutional bent) reveals that faithlessness-as-covenant-breaking-sin is the deepest-rooted characteristic in M10: it originates at the spirit's innermost level of covenantal orientation. The "guard yourselves in your spirit" command is remarkable for its precision — it identifies the spirit as the site of the characteristic's generation, requiring active vigilance at the deepest inner level to prevent faithlessness from arising. No other M10 characteristic is addressed with a spirit-vigilance command.

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=16453, E) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Spirit-level location is the most explicitly evidenced constitutional dimension in the 101-verse corpus (Mal 2:15/16; Isa 48:8). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=16454, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes. (1) *Spirit → will → outer act*: the spirit-level faithlessness (Mal 2:15 — requiring spirit-guarding) produces the will's allegiance-transfer (1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods") which produces outer acts (2Ch 36:14 — "polluted the house of the Lord"); (2) *Inner disposition → bodily consequence*: Num 5:27 — the wife who broke faith with her husband; "the water that brings the curse shall enter into her and cause bitter pain, and her womb shall swell, and her thigh shall fall away" — the inner faithlessness produces specific bodily physical consequences through the ordeal; (3) *Individual → communal*: Jos 7:1 — Achan's individual faithlessness; "the anger of the Lord burned against the people of Israel" — the inner betrayal of one person radiates outward to the whole community.

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=16455, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Primary sequence: (1) spirit-level vulnerability (Mal 2:15 — spirit requiring guarding); (2) will's allegiance-orientation (1Ch 5:25 — transferring loyalty to false gods); (3) outer act of covenant-breaking (Lev 26:40 — "treachery they committed against me"); (4) communal consequence (Jos 7:1 — divine anger against all Israel); (5) national-eschatological consequence (1Ch 9:1 — exile; Dan 9:7 — open shame in all lands). The sequence confirms: inner spirit → volitional act → outer deed → communal impact → national judgment. Secondary (reversal) sequence: Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery" → Lev 26:42 — "I will remember my covenant" — confession reverses the sequence toward restoration.

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=16456, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Constitutional movement is extensively evidenced (T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=16457, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes. Eze 18:24 — "but when a righteous person turns away from his righteousness and does injustice... for the treachery of which he is guilty and the sin he has committed, for them he shall die" — the soul (*nepeš* implied in "he shall die") is the entity whose life is forfeit for the treachery; faithlessness is a soul-level moral liability. Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the lust-captivity is a soul-level bondage of the person's innermost desiring faculty.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=16458, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Soul-level location (Eze 18:24 — death for treachery; Pro 11:6 — soul-level lust-captivity) reveals that faithlessness is not merely a legal status or behavioural pattern but attaches to the whole person at their innermost level. Eze 18:24's death-verdict for treachery names the soul as the entity bearing the full weight of the faithlessness-consequence. The lust-captivity of Pro 11:6 names the soul's desiring faculty as captured by the very desire that drove the faithlessness.

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=16459, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (Eze 18:24; Pro 11:6). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=16460, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through the heart-posture passages. 2Ch 29:6 — "our fathers have been unfaithful and have done what was evil in the sight of the Lord our God. They have forsaken him and have turned away their faces from the habitation of the Lord and turned their backs" — turning faces and backs is the bodily expression of what the heart has done; the heart has turned away from God; 2Ch 26:16 — "when he was strong, he grew proud, to his destruction. For he was unfaithful to the Lord his God" — pride is a heart-condition (the heart lifted up) that produces faithlessness; Psa 73:15 — "if I had said, 'I will speak thus,' I would have betrayed the generation of your children" — the heart's inner deliberation about speech constitutes the potential treachery.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=16461, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Heart-location (2Ch 29:6 — turning away from God; 2Ch 26:16 — proud heart producing faithlessness; Psa 73:15 — heart's inner deliberation as potential betrayal) reveals that faithlessness operates at the heart's allegiance-level: the heart that has turned its face and back toward God (2Ch 29:6) is the heart in the faithlessness-condition. The heart's disposition of allegiance — toward God or away — is the constitutional location of faithlessness. When the heart turns away from God (2Ch 29:6 — "turned their backs"), the turn is faithlessness at its heart-origin.

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=16462, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Heart-location is evidenced (2Ch 29:6; 2Ch 26:16; Psa 73:15). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=16463, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The mind is engaged through the deliberative and knowing dimensions. Psa 73:15 — "if I had said, 'I will speak thus,' I would have betrayed the generation of your children" — the mental deliberation about speech constitutes potential treachery; the mind's deliberative choice is the locus at which faithlessness can be avoided or committed through speech. Psa 59:5 — "those who treacherously plot evil" — the mind's planning activity is named as treachery's cognitive mode.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=16464, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Mind-location (Psa 73:15 — deliberating whether to speak; Psa 59:5 — plotting evil) reveals that faithlessness engages the cognitive-deliberative faculty in two ways: (1) the mind can deliberate whether to speak (Psa 73:15) and thereby either commit or avoid betrayal through speech; (2) the mind can plan treachery (Psa 59:5 — those who treacherously plot evil). The mind is both the faculty that can restrain faithlessness through honest deliberation and the faculty that can plot and execute it.

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=16465, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Mind-level location (deliberation — Psa 73:15; plotting — Psa 59:5) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=16466, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — *Will* as the primary soul-level faculty is central to CHAR-20. 1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods of the peoples" — the will's active transfer of allegiance is the volitional core; Eze 18:24 — "when a righteous person turns away from his righteousness" — the turning is a volitional act; Lev 26:40 — "treachery that they committed against me, and also in walking contrary to me" — the will walking contrary. *Conscience* appears in Ezr 9:4 — "all who trembled at the words of the God of Israel... sat appalled" — the conscience-engaged response to observed faithlessness; Ezr 10:6 — mourning over the exiles' faithlessness.

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=16467, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Will-level location (1Ch 5:25 — allegiance-transfer; Eze 18:24 — turning away from righteousness; Lev 26:40 — walking contrary) reveals that faithlessness is a will-act at the level of covenant-allegiance: the will that breaks covenantal loyalty (the definition). Conscience-level location (Ezr 9:4/10:6 — appalled, mourning) reveals that a functioning conscience is deeply troubled by faithlessness — both in the self and in others. The conscience's response is grief rather than guilt-suppression when faithlessness is recognised.

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=16468, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Will-level and conscience-level locations are evidenced (1Ch 5:25; Eze 18:24; Ezr 9:4/10:6). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=16469, E) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — two body-part links. (1) *Face and back*: 2Ch 29:6 — "they have forsaken him and have turned away their faces from the habitation of the Lord and turned their backs" — the face and the back are the body-parts through which the inner faithlessness is expressed; turning the back on God is the bodily enactment of the heart's turning; (2) *Womb and thigh*: Num 5:27 — "her womb shall swell, and her thigh shall fall away" — the faithless wife's reproductive organs are the body-parts on which the curse of faithlessness falls; the bodily consequence is located at the very organ that embodied the covenant of marriage.

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=16470, E) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two distinct body-part functions. (1) *Face and back as bodily expression of inner allegiance-direction* (2Ch 29:6): turning the face toward God or away is the body's expression of the heart's covenant-orientation; the face-direction images the inner will's God-direction; turning the back is the bodily expression of the heart that has definitively turned away; (2) *Womb and thigh as the site of covenant-embodied consequence* (Num 5:27): the marriage covenant was embodied in the marital relationship; faithlessness in that embodied covenant produces bodily consequences at the embodied organs of the covenant's expression — the curse falls on what expressed the covenant.

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=16471, E) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Body-part links (face/back — 2Ch 29:6; womb/thigh — Num 5:27) are evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=16472, E) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Bidirectional. (1) *Inner faithlessness → outer bodily posture*: the heart's turning away from God (inner) produces the turning of the face and back (outer bodily expression — 2Ch 29:6); the inner disposition expresses through the body's directional posture; (2) *Inner faithlessness → bodily curse*: the inner act of marital faithlessness (Num 5:27) produces the bodily curse entering the womb; the inner betrayal produces the outward bodily consequence; (3) *Outer bodily posture → inner assessment of faithlessness*: 2Ch 29:6's "turned their backs" is also the observable sign by which the faithlessness is assessed — the outer posture is both expressive of and diagnostic for the inner condition.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=16473, E) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The bidirectional body-faithlessness link reveals that faithlessness cannot remain purely inner: it expresses through the body (turning backs — 2Ch 29:6) and returns upon the body (womb curse — Num 5:27). The most intimate covenant (marriage) produces the most bodily-specific consequence when broken. This confirms CHAR-20 as a constitutively embodied characteristic: the covenant bonds that faithlessness breaks are not purely spiritual but embodied in relational, social, and physical realities — and their breaking produces embodied consequences.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=16474, E) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Body-characteristic link (face/back; womb/thigh) is evidenced and bidirectional. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=16475, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Generational communal deposit*: 2Ch 30:7 — "your fathers and your brothers, who were faithless to the Lord God of their fathers, so that he made them a desolation" — the faithlessness of the fathers is what the children are warned not to repeat; the deposit is the pattern and consequence transmitted; 1Ch 9:1 — the exile as the deposit of accumulated faithlessness; (2) *Identity-level character deposit*: Isa 48:8 — "I knew that you would surely deal treacherously" — the constitutional bent foreknown from before birth is the deepest deposit; (3) *National shame deposit*: Dan 9:7 — "open shame... in all the lands to which you have driven them, because of the treachery that they have committed against you" — the shame persisting in every land of exile as the national deposit of faithlessness.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=16476, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Supporting: 2Ch 30:7 (generational deposit — desolation of fathers); Isa 48:8 (identity-level foreknown deposit); Dan 9:7 (national shame deposit). Contradicting or complicating: Lev 26:40-42 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery... I will remember my covenant" — the deposit is reversible through confession; Eze 39:26 — "they shall forget their shame and all the treachery" in the eschatological secure-dwelling — the deposit is fully resolved in restoration. The deposit is real and constitutionally significant but reversible through divine covenant-remembrance.

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=16477, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Deposit evidence present: generational communal (2Ch 30:7), identity-level foreknown (Isa 48:8), national shame (Dan 9:7). T5.7 will carry: all deposit forms yield to covenant-renewal (Lev 26:40-42 — confession → covenant-remembrance) and eschatological resolution (Eze 39:26 — shame forgotten in secure dwelling). The identity-level deposit (Isa 48:8) is the most deeply constitutionally embedded.

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=16478, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two constitutional origins. (1) *Spirit-level inner disposition*: Mal 2:15 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless" — the spirit is the named origin that requires guarding; the faithlessness arises from within the spirit when not actively resisted; (2) *Pride as the triggering condition*: 2Ch 26:16 — "when he was strong, he grew proud, to his destruction. For he was unfaithful to the Lord his God" — pride in the heart is the originating inner condition that produces the outward faithlessness; the pride precedes and generates the unfaithfulness.

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=16479, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Multiple — the three VCGs name distinct originating contexts. Spirit-level disposition (Mal 2:15 — VCG-03); pride-generated (2Ch 26:16 — VCG-01 Uzziah); allegiance-transfer (1Ch 5:25 — transferring loyalty to false gods — VCG-01); lust-captivity (Pro 11:6 — lust capturing the will — VCG-02); unguarded spirit (Mal 2:15 — VCG-02 marital). The characteristic has multiple originating pathways: all share the spirit-level as the constitutional location but reach that location through different triggering conditions (pride, misplaced desire, unguarded spirit).

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=16480, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes across all three VCG registers. (1) *VCG-01 (toward God)*: origin in the departure of the heart's covenant-allegiance from God — 2Ch 29:6 — "turned away their faces... turned their backs"; pride-generated (2Ch 26:16 — Uzziah); allegiance-transfer (1Ch 5:25 — to false gods); (2) *VCG-02 (between persons)*: origin in marital desire-violation (Num 5:12/27); anger-betrayal (Judg 9:23); lust-captivity (Pro 11:6); (3) *VCG-03 (constitutional character)*: origin in the foreknown constitutional bent of the will (Isa 48:8) — the origin precedes specific circumstances and is embedded in the person's inner character from before birth.

---

---

## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=16481, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The perceptive faculty is engaged through the God-perception dimension and the recognition-of-others' faithlessness dimension. (1) *God perceiving faithlessness*: Ezr 9:4 — "all who trembled at the words of the God of Israel" — the God-sensitive person perceives the community's faithlessness and is appalled; (2) *Recognising others' treachery*: Hab 1:13 — "why do you idly look at traitors?" — God's perception of treacherous persons who swallow up the righteous; Jer 12:6 — "though they speak friendly words to you" — the perceptive challenge is to see through the friendly words to the actual treachery beneath; (3) *God as the perceiver of hidden faithlessness*: Mal 2:14 — "the Lord was witness between you and the wife of your youth" — God's perception witnesses the covenant's breaking even when human witnesses may not.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=16482, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *impairs* the perceptive faculty toward accurate self-assessment and *distorts* perception of God. 2Ch 12:2 — "because they had been unfaithful to the Lord, Shishak king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem" — the people's unfaithfulness presumably preceded their recognising its consequences; the perceptive faculty failed to see the faithlessness until foreign invasion disclosed it. 2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz deepening in faithlessness under distress — the perceptive faculty fails to see faithlessness as the cause of the distress and instead deepens the response. Conversely: Ezr 9:4 — "those who trembled at the words of the God of Israel" — a functioning conscience-perception accurately registers others' faithlessness.

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=16483, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The perceptive pattern reveals that faithlessness systematically impairs the self-perception needed for recognition and repentance (2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz doesn't see faithlessness causing distress) while leaving intact the perception of others' faithlessness (Jer 12:6 — the prophet perceiving family members' treachery; Hab 1:13 — the prophet perceiving traitors swallowing the righteous). The perceptive asymmetry is characteristic of CHAR-20: the faithless person sees others' treachery but not their own; the covenant-faithful person sees faithlessness clearly in both directions.

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=16508, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Conscientiousness is engaged through the deliberate commission of faithlessness and the deliberate guarding against it. (1) *Conscientiousness misdirected toward faithlessness*: Psa 59:5 — "those who treacherously plot evil" — deliberately planned treachery; Jer 12:6 — "though they speak friendly words to you" — conscientious concealment of treachery; Isa 24:16 — "the traitors have betrayed, with betrayal the traitors have betrayed" — compounded, intensified, conscientious betrayal; (2) *Conscientiousness directed against faithlessness*: Mal 2:15/16 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless" — active conscientiousness required to resist faithlessness.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=16509, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *captures and redirects* conscientiousness toward treachery's execution and concealment. Jer 12:6's "friendly words" while planning betrayal — the conscientiousness is deployed toward the production of a false appearance of loyalty; Isa 24:16's compounded betrayal — the intensification of the treacherous act through repeated deliberate betrayals. The remedy requires conscientiousness to be redirected: Ezr 10:10 — "you have broken faith and married foreign women, and so increased the guilt of Israel" — the conscientious corporate acknowledgment.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=16510, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The conscientiousness pattern confirms that faithlessness is not passive drift but active deployment of the person's moral capacity in the wrong direction. Isa 24:16's triple intensification — "the traitors have betrayed, with betrayal the traitors have betrayed" — names the repeated, conscientious investment in betrayal. This active-conscientiousness dimension distinguishes CHAR-20 from passive weakness: the faithless person does not accidentally betray but conscientiously enacts and conceals the betrayal (Jer 12:6 — friendly words). The guarding-against-faithlessness command (Mal 2:15) requires equally conscientious investment in the opposite direction.

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=16511, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The relational capacity is the primary mode of CHAR-20's operation — it IS constitutively relational. (1) *Vertical relational damage*: Eze 39:23 — "they dealt so treacherously with me that I hid my face from them" — the divine-face-hiding is the ultimate relational consequence; 1Ch 10:13 — "Saul died for his breach of faith... he broke faith with the Lord" — death as the relational severance endpoint; (2) *Horizontal relational damage*: Lam 1:2 — "all her friends have dealt treacherously with her; they have become her enemies" — friends converted to enemies; Jer 12:6 — family members planning destruction behind friendly words; (3) *Marriage-covenant relational damage*: Mal 2:14 — "been faithless to the wife of your youth, though she is your companion and your wife by covenant" — the most intimate human bond broken.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=16512, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *severs* relational capacity in proportion to the depth of the prior bond. Eze 39:23 — God hiding his face — the deepest relational severance; Pro 18:19 (CHAR-19 adjacent) — "a brother offended is more unyielding than a strong city" — betrayal of the closest bond creates the hardest barrier. Lam 1:2 — friends becoming enemies — the relational conversion from intimacy to enmity. The relational damage is precisely calibrated to the prior covenant: the deeper the covenant, the more devastating the faithlessness.

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=16513, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The relational pattern reveals CHAR-20 as M10's most relational characteristic: the characteristic is constitutively defined by betrayal within a prior relational bond. Mal 2:14's "companion and wife by covenant" — the covenanted companionship is what makes the faithlessness devastating; Eze 39:23's "I hid my face" — the divine face-hiding is proportional to the intimacy of the divine-human covenant that was broken. Every other M10 characteristic can be described without naming a prior relational bond; CHAR-20 cannot: it requires the prior bond to exist before faithlessness is possible.

---
