---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=16484, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through the deliberation and the self-justification dimensions. Psa 73:15 — "if I had said, 'I will speak thus,' I would have betrayed the generation of your children" — the psalmist's cognitive deliberation about whether to voice his doubts recognises that the spoken thought could constitute betrayal; the cognitive evaluation of speech determines whether an act is faithless or faithful; Psa 59:5 — "those who treacherously plot evil" — deliberate cognitive planning constitutes the treachery.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=16485, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *distorts* cognition at the self-assessment level. 2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz becoming "yet more faithless" under distress — the cognition fails to identify faithlessness as the cause of suffering and therefore misreads the corrective signal; Jer 12:6 — "though they speak friendly words to you" — the treacherous family's cognition generates a false presentation of loyalty while planning betrayal, showing treachery's cognitive deception-mode. The double cognitive movement: the faithless person's cognition produces false appearances of loyalty (Jer 12:6) while blinding itself to its own faithlessness (2Ch 28:22).

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=16486, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals that faithlessness is particularly damaging to the cognitive faculty's self-assessment function: the faithless person cannot accurately read the signal that distress sends (2Ch 28:22) and cannot accurately present themselves to others (Jer 12:6 — friendly words masking treachery). The cognitive function is co-opted: used to plan treachery (Psa 59:5) rather than to assess covenant loyalty honestly. The corrective is Ezr 10:2 — "we have broken faith with our God" — the honest cognitive confession that accurately names what the person has done.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=16487, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through the communal memory and the eschatological forgetting dimensions. (1) *Communal warning-memory*: 2Ch 30:7 — "do not be like your fathers and your brothers, who were faithless to the Lord God of their fathers, so that he made them a desolation, as you see" — the appeal to visible historical memory of what faithlessness produced; (2) *Eschatological resolution of memory*: Eze 39:26 — "they shall forget their shame and all the treachery they have practiced against me, when they dwell securely" — the eschatological promise is that the memory of treachery will be eclipsed by secure dwelling; (3) *Divine covenant-memory triggered by confession*: Lev 26:42 — "I will remember my covenant with Jacob" — God's covenant-memory is reactivated by the confession of treachery.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=16488, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *suppresses* the corrective memory that would prevent further betrayal (2Ch 30:7 — the fathers' desolation should be remembered as a warning). Ezr 9:4 — the community's faithlessness was not remembered until Ezra confronted it. The corrective act of confession (Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery") is simultaneously a memory-act — recalling what was done against God. The eschatological resolution (Eze 39:26) involves a paradoxical forgetting of the treachery — not suppression but the eclipsing of shame by restored relationship.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=16489, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The memory pattern reveals that faithlessness and memory stand in inverse relationship: faithlessness is enabled when the memory of God's covenant-expectation and the consequences of past faithlessness is suppressed (2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz not remembering); faithlessness is addressed when memory is activated (Lev 26:40 — confession recalls the treachery). Eze 39:26's eschatological forgetting of shame is not the suppression of memory but its transcendence: the faithful-dwelling-secure state has so eclipsed the treacherous-past that the shame no longer defines the community's identity.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=16490, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through the grief-of-witness and the desolation-of-victim dimensions. (1) *Grief of witness*: Ezr 9:4 — Ezra "sat appalled" and fasted; Ezr 10:6 — "mourning over the faithlessness of the exiles, neither eating bread nor drinking water" — the spiritually attuned person's affective response to witnessing faithlessness is deep grief; (2) *Desolation of victim*: Lam 1:2 — "she weeps bitterly in the night, with tears on her cheeks; all her friends have dealt treacherously with her; they have become her enemies" — weeping bitterly as the victim's affective experience; (3) *Desire as faithlessness-driver*: Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the craving-affect is the inner driver of the faithlessness.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=16491, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *misdirects* affect toward the wrong object (Pro 11:6 — lust capturing the treacherous person's affective faculty) and *produces negative affect* in its victims (Lam 1:2 — bitter weeping) and spiritually-aware witnesses (Ezr 9:4/10:6 — grief and mourning). The lust-as-driver (Pro 11:6) and desire-for-violence (Pro 13:2) show that faithlessness co-opts the affective faculty and redirects it toward objects that betray rather than sustain the covenant. The productive affect: the grief of Ezra (Ezr 9:4/10:6) — the appalled response of a covenant-faithful conscience.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=16492, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The affective pattern reveals two characteristic affect-profiles for CHAR-20. (1) *The faithless person's affect*: lust (Pro 11:6), desire for violence (Pro 13:2) — affects directed toward the betrayal-target; (2) *The covenant-faithful person's affect in response to faithlessness*: Ezra's grief (Ezr 9:4/10:6), Lamentations' bitter weeping (Lam 1:2) — affects expressing the damage faithlessness causes. CHAR-20 is distinctive in generating intense negative affect in the faithful-witness (Ezra's night-mourning without food or water) — the most intense expressed grief-response in M10's studied characteristics for a non-self-directed sin.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=16493, S) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** S — The 101-verse corpus does not explicitly engage the creative faculty in connection with faithlessness as covenant-breaking sin. The characteristic operates through will, spirit, conscience, affect, and covenant-allegiance rather than through creative origination.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=16494, S) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** S — No direct evidence. The corpus is silent on the creative faculty's engagement with faithlessness.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=16495, S) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** S — The silence is consistent with CHAR-20's constitutively relational-covenantal nature: the characteristic is about the disposition within an existing bond, not creative generation of new realities. Noted.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=16496, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The will is the primary faculty of CHAR-20. (1) *Will as agent of allegiance-transfer*: 1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods of the peoples" — the will's active transfer of covenant-allegiance; Eze 18:24 — "when a righteous person turns away from his righteousness" — the will's volitional turning; (2) *Will as the subject of spirit-guarding*: Mal 2:15/16 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless" — the command is to the will to guard the spirit; (3) *Will as the faculty that deepens under pressure*: 2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz's will "became yet more faithless" — the will choosing faithlessness with increasing determination rather than turning.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=16497, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *progressively captures* the will. Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the faithless will is eventually captured by the very desire that drove it to betray; 2Ch 28:22 — the will that chose faithlessness progressively loses its freedom to turn toward God (each distress-event produced more faithlessness, not less). The will begins as the agent of betrayal (the choice to break faith) and ends as the captive of the betrayal's consequences (lust-captivity). The remedy (Lev 26:40 — "if they confess") requires the will's active engagement in the confession-act.

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=16498, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The volitional pattern reveals CHAR-20's most characteristic inner-being dynamic: the will that freely chose to betray is progressively enslaved by what it chose. 2Ch 28:22's Ahaz — distress producing more faithlessness — is the most extreme portrait of this dynamic: the will has become so constitutively set in the faithless direction that environmental pressure drives it further rather than correcting it. This volitional-hardening is distinct from CHAR-19's volitional-ensnaring (Pro 29:6 — ensnared in transgression) in that CHAR-20's hardening is specifically of the covenant-allegiance faculty rather than general will-bondage.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=16499, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Agency is engaged through both the commission and the communal extension of faithlessness. (1) *Individual agentive commission*: Eze 17:20 — "for the treachery he has committed against me" — the specific agentive act named as the ground of judgment; 1Ch 10:13 — "he broke faith with the Lord in that he did not keep the command" — the specific agentive choices constituting the faithlessness; (2) *Communal agentive extension*: Num 31:16 — "caused the people of Israel to act treacherously against the Lord in the incident of Peor" — Balaam's agency incited communal faithlessness; Jos 7:1 — Achan's individual agency attributed to all Israel.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=16500, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *captures and corrupts* agency through lust. Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the agency that began as free covenant-breaking becomes captivity to the lust that drove the betrayal; the traitor's agency is no longer free but driven by craving. Pro 13:2 — "the desire of the treacherous is for violence" — the settled desire has taken over the agentive orientation. The individual's faithlessness also compromises community agency (Jos 7:1 — one person's act attributed to all) — the faithless person's agency reaches beyond the individual to implicate the community.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=16501, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The agency pattern confirms CHAR-20's distinctive communal-reach: the agency of a single faithless person implicates and damages the agency of the whole community (Jos 7:1 — Achan; 1Ch 10:13 — Saul's faithlessness affecting all Israel). This communal-agency reach distinguishes CHAR-20 from characteristics whose consequences remain primarily individual. The leader's faithlessness (2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz; 2Ch 26:16 — Uzziah; 2Ch 36:14 — priests and people) consistently radiates to the community, showing that the agency of CHAR-20's faithlessness is constitutively social.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=16502, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through the prophetic assessment and the communal verdict dimensions. Jer 3:11 — "faithless Israel has shown herself more righteous than treacherous Judah" — God's moral evaluation comparing two levels of faithlessness; Jer 3:20 — "as a treacherous wife leaves her husband, so have you been treacherous to me, O house of Israel" — the prophetic moral evaluation naming the faithlessness precisely; Psa 119:158 — "I look at the faithless with disgust, because they do not keep your commands" — the psalmist's moral evaluation produces disgust.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=16503, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *distorts* self-directed moral evaluation while leaving other-directed evaluation intact. 2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz becoming more faithless under distress cannot accurately evaluate his own condition; 2Ch 26:16/18 — Uzziah overstepping priestly bounds until the priests challenge him — the self-directed moral evaluation was impaired by pride. Conversely: Psa 119:158 — "I look at the faithless with disgust" — the faithful person's moral evaluation of others' faithlessness is acute; Jer 3:11 — God's comparative moral evaluation is precise.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=16504, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that faithlessness carries a distinctive disgust-response in those who are faithful (Psa 119:158 — "I look at the faithless with disgust"). Disgust is the moral-evaluation affect that is generated by faithlessness in the covenant-faithful observer — stronger than mere disapproval. This disgust-response is unique in M10's studied characteristics: no other characteristic generates this specific evaluative-affective response from the observer. The disgust implies that faithlessness is experienced as morally polluting — an appropriate response to the betrayal of the most fundamental bonds.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=16505, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — conscience is centrally engaged. Ezr 9:4 — "all who trembled at the words of the God of Israel... gathered around me while I sat appalled" — the conscience-engaged community trembles; Ezr 10:6 — Ezra "mourning over the faithlessness of the exiles" — the conscience's grief-response to faithlessness; Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery" — the confession is a conscience-act; Psa 73:15 — "if I had said, 'I will speak thus,' I would have betrayed the generation of your children" — the conscience's deliberative checking of potential faithlessness-through-speech.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=16506, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness *suppresses* the conscience of the faithless person and *engages* the conscience of the covenant-faithful observer. The progressive deepening of Ahaz's faithlessness (2Ch 28:22) implies conscience-suppression — each opportunity for conscience to redirect is overridden by deepening faithlessness. Ezr 9:4's trembling observers represent the functioning conscience that registers others' faithlessness accurately. Mal 2:15 — "guard yourselves in your spirit" — is a conscience-engagement command: the guarding of the spirit requires conscience to be active and vigilant against faithlessness arising from within.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=16507, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The conscience pattern reveals CHAR-20's most distinctive M10 feature: Ezra's response (Ezr 9:4/10:6 — appalled, fasting, mourning overnight) is the most intense conscience-engagement with another's sin in M10's studied characteristics. The faithful conscience is proportionately more distressed by faithlessness (covenant-betrayal) than by other categories of sin — the relational-betrayal dimension of CHAR-20 activates the conscience's deepest relational-loyalty register. Mal 2:15's "guard yourselves in your spirit" names the conscience's role as internal watchman against faithlessness arising from within.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=16514, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — God operates toward the faithless person in five modes. (1) *Witnessing covenant bonds*: Mal 2:14 — "the Lord was witness between you and the wife of your youth" — God is the named witness of the covenant whose breaking is faithlessness; (2) *Declaring faithlessness through prophets*: Jer 5:11 — "the house of Israel and the house of Judah have been utterly treacherous to me, declares the Lord"; Eze 14:13 — "when a land sins against me by acting faithlessly" — prophetic declaration of the faithlessness; (3) *Judging faithlessness*: Eze 17:20 — "enter into judgment with him there for the treachery he has committed against me"; 1Ch 10:13 — Saul died for his breach of faith; (4) *Hiding his face*: Eze 39:23 — "they dealt so treacherously with me that I hid my face from them" — the divine-face withdrawal is the most intimate relational response; (5) *Responding to confession*: Lev 26:40-42 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery... I will remember my covenant" — God's covenant-memory restoration follows honest confession.

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=16515, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two bases. (1) *Covenantal faithfulness that cannot ignore betrayal*: Mal 2:14 — God as witness means the covenant's breaking is registered in divine witness-memory; Eze 39:23 — God hiding his face is grounded in the treachery's personal directedness "against me"; (2) *Steadfast love that moves toward restoration*: Lev 26:42 — "I will remember my covenant with Jacob, and I will remember my covenant with Isaac and my covenant with Abraham, and I will remember the land" — God's covenant-memory is the basis of restoration; the very covenant that was betrayed is the ground of the restoration. The faithfulness God displays in remembering the covenant despite its betrayal is the basis for the restoration possibility.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=16516, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — God's disposition is one of *personally wounded faithfulness sustaining covenant-memory toward restoration*. Ize 39:23 — "I hid my face" — the face-hiding is a personal response to personal betrayal; it is not mere judicial distance but relational withdrawal. Lev 26:42 — God remembers the covenant that was betrayed — the willingness to remember the very covenant whose breaking caused the wound reveals a disposition that sustains loyalty even through the experience of disloyalty. The eschatological resolution (Eze 39:26 — "they shall forget their shame") confirms: God's ultimate goal is not retribution but the restoration in which even the shame of the treachery is eclipsed.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=16517, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — God-to-human operation (witnessing, declaring, judging, face-hiding, covenant-restoring) is extensively evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=16518, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through the confession-and-return movement. Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery that they committed against me, and also in walking contrary to me" — the confession is the movement toward God; the treachery itself is named in the movement-toward; Ezr 10:2 — "we have broken faith with our God and have married foreign women from the peoples of the land, but even now there is hope for Israel" — the confession of faithlessness is simultaneously a movement toward God with hope; Ezr 10:10 — Ezra's declaration "you have broken faith and married foreign women" — the communal acknowledgment initiating the corporate return.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=16519, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Verbal acknowledgment of the specific treachery*: Lev 26:40 — "confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery" — the confession must name the faithlessness specifically, including generational dimensions; (2) *Humility (not pride)*: 2Ch 33:19 — Manasseh's prayer "and how God was moved by his entreaty" — the humbling (2Ch 33:12 — "he humbled himself greatly before the God of his fathers") is the counterpoint to 2Ch 26:16's pride that generated the faithlessness; humility is the required inner posture; (3) *Hope-maintained movement*: Ezr 10:2 — "but even now there is hope for Israel in spite of this" — movement toward God under faithlessness requires hope that the relationship remains accessible.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=16520, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The human-to-God direction reveals that the covenant relationship remains accessible through the specific act of naming the faithlessness before God. Lev 26:42 — "I will remember my covenant" following the confession — God's covenant-memory is reactivated by honest naming; Ezr 10:2 — "even now there is hope" — the movement is possible even after extensive faithlessness. The relationship is not terminated by faithlessness but suspended: Eze 39:23 — "I hid my face... and gave them into the hand of their adversaries" — the face-hiding is a relational suspension, not termination. The movement toward God with honest confession of treachery reopens the suspended relationship.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=16521, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Human-to-God operation (confession, humility, hope-maintained return) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=16522, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through three extension mechanisms. (1) *Leadership propagation*: Num 31:16 — "Balaam's advice caused the people of Israel to act treacherously against the Lord in the incident of Peor" — external counsel inciting communal faithlessness; 2Ch 28:19 — "Ahaz... had made Judah act sinfully and had been very unfaithful to the Lord" — the leader's faithlessness propagates to the nation; (2) *Marital betrayal of a spouse*: Num 5:12 — "if any man's wife goes astray and breaks faith with him" — spousal faithlessness; Mal 2:14 — "been faithless to the wife of your youth" — marital extension; (3) *False-friend betrayal*: Jer 12:6 — "even your brothers and the house of your father... have dealt treacherously with you" — family members extending faithlessness against the prophet.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=16523, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Prior position of trust*: Mal 2:14 — "your companion and your wife by covenant" — the treachery is enabled by the prior intimate covenant that created the trust-position; Jer 12:6 — brothers and family-members — the betrayal is possible because of the prior bond; Jos 7:1 — Achan's position within Israel as a covenant-member; (2) *Internal desire-orientation toward false objects*: 1Ch 5:25 — "whored after the gods of the peoples" — the will's desire transferred to false objects is the enabling condition that makes the covenant-faithlessness an act of inner betrayal rather than mere preference-change.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=16524, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The person must have been party to the covenant whose breaking constitutes the faithlessness. Mal 2:14 — "though she is your companion and your wife by covenant" — the prior covenant-membership is the prerequisite that makes the departure faithlessness rather than mere leaving. Jos 22:22 — "if it was in rebellion or in breach of faith against the Lord" — the eastern tribes were already covenant-members whose actions would constitute faithlessness; the covenant-membership precedes any faithlessness.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=16525, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — The giving/extension direction is evidenced through leadership propagation (Num 31:16; 2Ch 28:19), marital betrayal (Num 5:12; Mal 2:14), and false-friend betrayal (Jer 12:6). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=16526, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — through three reception modes. (1) *Personal commission*: Lev 26:40 — "treachery that they committed against me" — personally owned; 1Ch 10:13 — "Saul died for his breach of faith. He broke faith with the Lord" — named personal receipt of faithlessness as self-committed; (2) *Generational inheritance*: 2Ch 30:7 — "do not be like your fathers and your brothers, who were faithless" — the generational pattern received; Lev 26:40 — "the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery" — the fathers' faithlessness inherited as a pattern; (3) *Communal attribution*: Jos 7:1 — "the people of Israel broke faith" — individual faithlessness attributed to the whole community; Ezr 9:2 — "in this faithlessness the hand of the officials and chief men has been foremost" — communal reception of the faithlessness-charge.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=16527, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Reception-enabling: (1) *Humble confession*: Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery"; Ezr 10:2 — "we have broken faith with our God" — verbal acknowledgment; (2) *Spirit-vigilance*: Mal 2:15 — "guard yourselves in your spirit" — active inner watchfulness against further faithlessness; (3) *Hope retained*: Ezr 10:2 — "even now there is hope for Israel" — the hope-posture opens the door to remedy. Reception-blocking: (1) *Pride*: 2Ch 26:16 — "when he was strong, he grew proud" — pride generating faithlessness blocks the humility required for remedy; (2) *Hardening under distress*: 2Ch 28:22 — "became yet more faithless" — distress deepening the faithlessness forecloses remedy; (3) *Concealment*: 1Ch 10:13 — Saul's consulting a medium secretly — the concealment of divided loyalty blocks remedy.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=16528, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two states evidenced. (1) *Restored covenant relationship*: Lev 26:42-45 — "I will remember my covenant... I will not spurn them... I will be their God" — the forgiven-faithlessness state is one of restored covenant relationship with God; (2) *Forgotten shame in secure dwelling*: Eze 39:26 — "they shall forget their shame and all the treachery they have practiced against me, when they dwell securely in their land with none to make them afraid" — the fully-restored state is one in which the treachery-shame is eclipsed by the security of covenant-dwelling. The forgiven-faithlessness state is characterised by secure dwelling and forgotten shame — the opposite of the exile-and-open-shame state (Dan 9:7) that faithlessness produced.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=16529, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced across personal commission (1Ch 10:13), generational inheritance (2Ch 30:7; Lev 26:40), and communal attribution (Jos 7:1; Ezr 9:2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=16530, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — the prior relational bond is constitutive of faithlessness: there can be no faithlessness without it. Within the divine-covenant bond: Eze 20:27 — "your fathers blasphemed me, by dealing treacherously with me" — the blasphemy-equivalence of faithlessness is only possible within the intimate covenant relationship; Amos 3:2 (CHAR-19 adjacent) — "you only have I known of all the families of the earth; therefore I will punish you for all your iniquities" — the uniquely intimate covenant intensifies the accountability. Within marriage bonds: Mal 2:14 — "your companion and your wife by covenant" — the companionship-depth makes the faithlessness devastating.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=16531, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Primarily within covenantal contexts — unlike CHAR-19 (which operated universally across all nations in the Amos oracle-series), CHAR-20 is more bounded by the covenant-relationship as its definitional context. Judg 9:23 — the leaders of Shechem dealing treacherously with Abimelech — a non-explicitly covenantal political context; Hab 1:13 — traitors who swallow up the righteous — a general moral context. These indicate the characteristic can cross beyond explicit covenant contexts into any context where loyalty was owed, but the primary domain is covenantal.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=16532, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The relational scope spans: the divine-human covenant (VCG-01 — 57V), the marriage covenant (Num 5:12; Mal 2:14-16), the family bond (Jer 12:6 — family members), the civic-political bond (Judg 9:23 — Shechem leaders), and the friendship bond (Lam 1:2 — friends become enemies). The characteristic's scope is wherever covenant-loyalty was owed and has been broken. The primary and densest scope is the divine-Israel covenant (VCG-01); the secondary scope is the human relational bonds (VCG-02); the tertiary scope is the constitutional inner character (VCG-03).

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=16533, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Relational scope (divine-covenant, marriage, family, civic, friendship) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=16534, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-20]** S — The 101-verse corpus does not explicitly name angelic or adversarial spiritual beings in connection with faithlessness as covenant-breaking sin. The characteristic operates within the direct human-God and human-human covenantal relationship without named spiritual-being mediation.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=16535, E) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Partially — through the Judg 9:23 divine-instigation of treachery. "God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the leaders of Shechem, and the leaders of Shechem dealt treacherously with Abimelech" — a divine spirit is named as the instigating force behind the treachery; the treachery is divinely orchestrated as retribution. This is the only instance in the corpus where a spiritual agent is connected to the characteristic's operation, and it is divine rather than adversarial in nature.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=16536, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-20]** S — No angelic ministry is named in connection with faithlessness as covenant-breaking sin in the 101-verse corpus. The characteristic operates as a direct human-God and human-human covenantal transaction without angelic mediation.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=16537, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Angelic ministry (T4.6.3) is entirely absent. Adversarial spiritual beings (T4.6.1) are absent. One instance of divine-spirit instigation as retributive instrument (Judg 9:23) is evidenced, but this is divine rather than adversarial. The characteristic operates as a direct covenantal human-God transaction without regular spiritual-being mediation.

---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=16538, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Both — in the negative direction and through its resolution. (1) *Negative condition change*: 1Ch 9:1 — Judah "taken into exile in Babylon because of their breach of faith" — exile as the condition-transformation produced by faithlessness; Dan 9:7 — "open shame... in all the lands" — shame as the transformed relational condition; (2) *Negative orientation change*: 1Ch 5:25 — "whored after the gods of the peoples" — the inner orientation transferred from God to false objects; 2Ch 28:22 — "became yet more faithless" — the orientation deepening; (3) *Positive transformation through confession*: Lev 26:42-45 — covenant remembered and restored; Eze 39:26 — secure dwelling and forgotten shame — the condition transformed from exile-and-shame to security-and-restoration.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=16539, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Primarily reversible through covenant-confession, with exceptions. (1) *Reversible through confession*: Lev 26:40-42 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery... I will remember my covenant" — the covenant-memory restoration reverses the transformation; (2) *Individual irreversible case*: 1Ch 10:13 — "Saul died for his breach of faith" — the death-verdict is irreversible for Saul individually; the faithlessness that produced his death cannot be reversed posthumously; (3) *Progressive deepening toward irreversibility*: 2Ch 28:22 — Ahaz becoming "yet more faithless" — the progressive deepening suggests a trajectory toward irreversibility, but the corpus does not explicitly name Ahaz's faithlessness as beyond recovery.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=16540, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Transformation evidence is present in all directions (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=16541, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — two sequences. (1) *Faithlessness-to-judgment sequence*: spirit-level unguarded (Mal 2:15 inverse) → will's allegiance-transfer (1Ch 5:25 — whoring after gods) → covenant-breaking act (Lev 26:40 — "treachery they committed") → divine face-hiding (Eze 39:23) → exile and shame (Dan 9:7; 1Ch 9:1); (2) *Recognition-to-restoration sequence*: community appalled (Ezr 9:4) → corporate mourning (Ezr 10:6 — Ezra fasting) → confession (Ezr 10:2/10 — "we have broken faith") → humble acknowledgment (Lev 26:40 — naming generational treachery) → covenant-remembered (Lev 26:42) → secure dwelling and forgotten shame (Eze 39:26).

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=16542, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The faithlessness-to-judgment sequence reveals that faithlessness operates as a progressive relational severance: each stage removes the person/community further from the covenant-relationship until the divine face is hidden (Eze 39:23) and the physical exile follows (Dan 9:7; 1Ch 9:1). The recognition-to-restoration sequence reveals that the reversal path is initiated by honest communal witness (Ezr 9:4 — trembling) rather than individual insight alone, and requires explicit naming of the generational treachery (Lev 26:40 — "iniquity of their fathers in their treachery"). The sequence confirms: faithlessness is a relational progression away from God; restoration is a relational progression back.

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=16543, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Sequence is evidenced in both faithlessness-to-judgment and recognition-to-restoration directions (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=16544, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Three change-mechanisms. (1) *Allegiance-transfer mechanism*: 1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers, and whored after the gods of the peoples" — the mechanism is the will's transfer of primary covenantal loyalty to a rival object; the faithlessness operates by installing a competitor to the covenant-relationship at the level of the will's deepest allegiance; (2) *Progressive hardening under pressure*: 2Ch 28:22 — "in the time of his distress he became yet more faithless" — the mechanism of hardening under corrective pressure; distress, which would normally activate conscience, instead deepens the faithless orientation; (3) *Communal contagion*: Num 31:16 — "caused the people of Israel to act treacherously" — Balaam's advice as the incitement mechanism; pro 23:28 — "she lies in wait like a robber and increases the traitors among mankind" — treachery as a multiplying-contagion.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=16545, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes across the three VCGs. (1) *VCG-01 (toward God)*: mechanism is allegiance-transfer and progressive hardening (1Ch 5:25; 2Ch 28:22); (2) *VCG-02 (between persons)*: mechanism is relational-bond betrayal — the marital (Num 5:12/27 — the suspected wife's mechanism), the familial-betrayal (Jer 12:6 — friendly-word concealment of treachery), and the lust-capture (Pro 11:6); (3) *VCG-03 (constitutional character)*: mechanism is constitutional inner-bent (Isa 48:8 — foreknown before birth) that compounding over time produces the intensified treachery of Isa 24:16 ("the traitors have betrayed, with betrayal the traitors have betrayed").

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=16546, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Mechanisms are evidenced across three distinct VCG contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=16547, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — extensively. (1) *Suffering as faithlessness's consequence*: 2Ch 12:2 — "because they had been unfaithful to the Lord, Shishak king of Egypt came up against Jerusalem" — foreign invasion as faithlessness-consequence; Dan 9:7 — exile and open shame; 2Ch 30:7 — "he made them a desolation"; (2) *Suffering intensifying faithlessness*: 2Ch 28:22 — "in the time of his distress he became yet more faithless to the Lord" — the suffering of distress did not break the faithlessness but deepened it — suffering serves as an intensifier rather than a corrective for the constitutionally faithless; (3) *Suffering as faithful witness's response*: Ezr 9:4/10:6 — Ezra sitting appalled and fasting overnight — the suffering of grief is the faithful person's response to witnessing faithlessness.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=16548, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Suffering primarily *reveals* and potentially *deepens* faithlessness. 2Ch 12:2 — the invasion reveals the faithlessness as its cause; 2Ch 28:22 — the distress-deepening reveals that Ahaz's faithlessness is constitutionally embedded rather than situational. Suffering does not produce faithlessness directly but tests whether the faithlessness is situational or constitutional: for the situationally weak (Hezekiah-type — whose faithlessness breaks under suffering and produces repentance), suffering corrects; for the constitutionally faithless (Ahaz — 2Ch 28:22), suffering deepens the faithlessness.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=16549, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — The relationship between faithlessness and suffering is evidenced (2Ch 12:2; 2Ch 28:22; Dan 9:7; Ezr 9:4/10:6). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=16550, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — as both the negative formation arc's defining disposition and the generational character-producer. (1) *Generational character formation*: 2Ch 30:7 — "do not be like your fathers and your brothers, who were faithless to the Lord God of their fathers" — faithlessness is transmitted as a generational character-pattern; Lev 26:40 — "the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery" — the fathers' treachery enters the next generation's moral inheritance; (2) *Constitutional character deformation*: Isa 48:8 — "from before birth you were called a rebel... I knew you would surely deal treacherously" — the deepest formation arc produces the constitutional inner bent toward treachery.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=16551, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Faithlessness functions as the *relational-covenantal formation arc's primary driver* — it shapes the person's inner orientation toward or away from covenant-loyalty. The Ahaz narrative (2Ch 28:19/22/29:19) shows faithlessness as a formation arc that accumulated across a reign: each faithless act deepening the dispositional bent until even distress produced more faithlessness. The contrast with Manasseh (2Ch 33:19 — "all his sin and his faithlessness... before he humbled himself") shows that even deep faithlessness-formation can be reversed by humility — but the formation must be actively addressed.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=16552, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Formation evidence is present through individual volitional development (2Ch 28:22), generational transmission (2Ch 30:7; Lev 26:40), and Adamic type (Hos 6:7 — "like Adam they transgressed the covenant... dealt faithlessly"). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=16553, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — two eschatological endpoints. (1) *Judgment endpoint*: 1Ch 10:13 — "Saul died for his breach of faith" — death as faithlessness's personal eschatological verdict; Dan 9:7 — exile and open shame as the communal eschatological judgment; 2Ch 30:7 — "made them a desolation" — desolation as the nation's judgment; (2) *Restoration endpoint*: Eze 39:26 — "they shall forget their shame and all the treachery they have practiced against me, when they dwell securely in their land with none to make them afraid" — the eschatological fullness is secure dwelling in which the shame of treachery is forgotten; Lev 26:42 — "I will remember my covenant" — covenant-memory restoration as the eschatological goal.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=16554, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-20]** E — For the unrepentant: 2Ch 28:22's progressive deepening anticipates the eschatological endpoint of complete desolation (2Ch 30:7 — made them a desolation); the present hardening-under-distress is the pre-eschatological trajectory toward irreversible judgment. For the confessing community: Lev 26:40's honest confession anticipates Lev 26:42's covenant-restoration; Eze 39:26's secure-dwelling promise is the eschatological fullness that present confession moves toward.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=16555, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced in both judgment (1Ch 10:13; Dan 9:7) and restoration (Eze 39:26; Lev 26:42) directions. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=16556, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-20]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: generational-communal deposit (2Ch 30:7), identity-level foreknown deposit (Isa 48:8), national shame deposit (Dan 9:7). Developmental consequences. (1) *Generational deposit*: 2Ch 30:7's desolation of the faithless fathers becomes the warning inherited by the children; the developmental consequence is both the warning (don't repeat) and the pattern (the children have inherited a proven-faithless moral heritage); Lev 26:40 — "iniquity of their fathers in their treachery" — the generational deposit must be named and confessed by the next generation; (2) *Identity-level deposit*: Isa 48:8's pre-birth constitutional bent develops into the compounded treachery of Isa 24:16 and Jer 3:8/11; (3) *National shame deposit*: Dan 9:7's open shame in all lands of exile is the sustained developmental consequence of accumulated faithlessness.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=16557, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — most explicitly through Lev 26:40 which requires the confession to cover the fathers' treachery alongside one's own: "confess their iniquity and the iniquity of their fathers in their treachery" — the generational deposit is real enough that its confession is required. 2Ch 30:7 — "do not be like your fathers and your brothers, who were faithless... so that he made them a desolation" — the fathers' faithlessness produced a desolation that the children could visibly observe as warning. The generational consequence of faithlessness is thus both spiritual (pattern transmitted) and material (observable desolation).

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=16558, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-20]** E — T2.8 found deposit evidence. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---
