## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=16559, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur within the 101-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-19 (Transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing)*: Lev 26:40 — "confess their iniquity and... treachery... and walking contrary to me" — iniquity and treachery paired; Hos 6:7 — "like Adam they transgressed the covenant; there they dealt faithlessly with me" — the transgression (covenant-crossing) and faithlessness are named together in the same event; (2) *CHAR-18 (Iniquity as accumulated moral crime)*: 2Ch 33:19 — "all his sin and his faithlessness" — faithlessness named alongside iniquity; Eze 18:24 — "the treachery of which he is guilty and the sin he has committed" — treachery and sin paired; (3) *CHAR-3 (Confession)*: Lev 26:40 — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery"; Ezr 10:2 — "we have broken faith with our God"; (4) *CHAR-14 (Forgiveness sought and received)*: Lev 26:42 — "I will remember my covenant" as the divine response to confession of treachery.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=16560, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-20 as the *relational-covenantal face* of M10: while CHAR-19 names the boundary-crossing and CHAR-18 names the accumulated crime, CHAR-20 names the betrayal-of-relationship that these crossings and crimes constitute. CHAR-20 is the characteristic that gives M10's sin-categories their interpersonal-covenantal weight: Hos 6:7 — "like Adam they transgressed the covenant; there they dealt faithlessly with me" — the same event is both transgression (CHAR-19) and faithlessness (CHAR-20); CHAR-20 names the relational-betrayal dimension of what CHAR-19 names as the boundary-crossing.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=16561, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=16562, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-20]** E — CHAR-20 consistently *accompanies* CHAR-19 (Hos 6:7 — the same act is both transgression and faithlessness; Lev 26:40 — treachery named alongside walking contrary). CHAR-20 consistently *precedes* CHAR-3 (confession names the treachery: Lev 26:40; Ezr 10:2) and CHAR-14 (the forgiveness that restores the covenant: Lev 26:42). CHAR-20 consistently *follows* the positive M13 (faithfulness) dimension being abandoned: 1Ch 5:25 — "broke faith with the God of their fathers" presupposes prior faithfulness being given up.

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=16563, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The relationship with CHAR-19 is *constitutive* (they are two dimensions of the same sinful act seen from boundary-crossing and relationship-betrayal perspectives). The relationship with CHAR-3 is *causal*: the recognised faithlessness (CHAR-20) causally produces the confession (CHAR-3) — Ezr 10:2 — "we have broken faith with our God" is simultaneously recognition and confession. The relationship with CHAR-14 is *causal-sequential*: confession of faithlessness (CHAR-3) → covenant-remembered (CHAR-14 — Lev 26:42) — faithlessness addressed through the confession-to-forgiveness pipeline.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=16564, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Sequential patterns (constitutive with CHAR-19; causal with CHAR-3/14) are evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=16565, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-13 (Sin as divine record)*: the faithlessness enters and remains in the divine record — Dan 9:7 — God drove them away "because of the treachery that they have committed against you" — the treachery is remembered in God's record; Eze 39:26 — the treachery must be forgotten in restoration; (2) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: Ezr 9:4 — the community's faithlessness produced appalled guilt-grief in the faithful; 2Ch 33:19 — Manasseh's faithlessness produced recognition that required prayer and humbling; (3) *Exile and shame*: 1Ch 9:1/Dan 9:7 — faithlessness produces the outer condition of exile and shame.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=16566, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes. (1) *Pride (within CHAR-16 adjacent)*: 2Ch 26:16 — "when he was strong, he grew proud, to his destruction. For he was unfaithful to the Lord his God" — pride is the generating inner condition; (2) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: the habitual departure from God's ways (2Ch 28:22 — progressively deepening) is the generative condition for consolidated faithlessness; (3) *Misplaced desire (within CHAR-16 adjacent)*: Pro 11:6 — "the treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the lust that was present prior to the faithlessness is what drove the betrayal.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=16567, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-20]** E — CHAR-20 is the *relational-betrayal dimension* of CHAR-19 in verses where both occur: Hos 6:7 — "they transgressed the covenant; there they dealt faithlessly with me" — CHAR-19 (transgressed) and CHAR-20 (dealt faithlessly) are two analytic faces of the same covenant-breach. CHAR-20's VCG-01 (unfaithfulness toward God) is the sin-dimension that M13 (Truth/Faithfulness) addresses from the positive side and M31 (Faith/Unbelief) addresses from the faith-perspective; CHAR-20 IS the M10 expression of M13's positive characteristic's absence.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=16568, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=16569, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The ma.al family (H4603/H4604) is exclusive to CHAR-20 within M10 — it does not appear in CHAR-18's a.von vocabulary, CHAR-19's pe.sha/pa.sha vocabulary, or CHAR-17's a.sham vocabulary. Ba.gad (H0898) is also exclusive to CHAR-20 within M10. Both term-families are distinctive to CHAR-20 and not shared with adjacent M10 characteristics. The characteristic has the most exclusively distinctive vocabulary of any M10 characteristic studied — no term-overlap at all with other M10 vocabulary families.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=16570, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-20]** E — No root-level sharing with other M10 characteristics. The ma.al root (*māʿal* — to commit a breach, to be unfaithful) and the ba.gad root (*bāgad* — to act treacherously) are both distinct from all other M10 primary vocabulary roots (cha.ta, a.von, pe.sha, a.sham, hamartōlos, parabasis, etc.). CHAR-20 has the most terminologically isolated root-set in M10 — confirming the characteristic as a semantically distinct domain within the M10 cluster.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=16571, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The vocabulary distinctiveness (zero root-level sharing with any other M10 characteristic) confirms that CHAR-20 is a genuinely distinct conceptual domain within M10 that cannot be derived from or reduced to other M10 characteristics. The ma.al/ba.gad vocabulary uniquely names the covenantal-relational-betrayal dimension of moral failure. The cross-register flags (M13 Truth/Faithfulness; M31 Faith/Unbelief) confirm: CHAR-20 sits at the intersection of M10 (sin/guilt) and M13 (faithfulness) and M31 (faith) — it is the sin-characteristic that most directly engages the faithfulness-cluster.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=16572, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Zero root-level vocabulary sharing with any other M10 characteristic is evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). CHAR-20 is terminologically the most isolated characteristic in M10. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=16573, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-20]** E — CHAR-19 (Transgression as deliberate boundary-crossing) most closely resembles CHAR-20 — both address knowing, wilful departure from a known moral-covenantal order. The precise distinction: CHAR-19 names the *act of crossing the known boundary* (the step-past, the parabasis — law-violation requiring a law); CHAR-20 names the *relational betrayal-dimension* of that crossing (the faithlessness — the betrayal of a covenant relationship that was personal and binding). CHAR-19 can exist without prior intimate bond (the Amos nations crossing ethical thresholds without explicit covenant); CHAR-20 requires the prior intimate bond whose breaking constitutes betrayal.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=16574, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The overlap is at Hos 6:7 — "like Adam they transgressed the covenant; there they dealt faithlessly with me" — where the same act is both transgression (CHAR-19 — pe.sha, boundary-crossing) and faithlessness (CHAR-20 — ba.gad, covenant-betrayal). The precise boundary: "transgressed the covenant" (CHAR-19 — the violation of the boundary constituted by the covenant) and "dealt faithlessly with me" (CHAR-20 — the relational betrayal of God as covenant-partner). CHAR-19 sees the covenant as a boundary; CHAR-20 sees the covenant as a relationship. Both analyse the same act from different angles.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=16575, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The distinction is one of *relational depth and prior-bond requirement*: CHAR-19 is the boundary-crossing category (can operate without prior intimate bond — the Amos nations); CHAR-20 is the covenant-betrayal category (constitutively requires a prior covenant relationship). The distinction is thus not of degree (one more serious than another) or constitutional level (both operate at will and spirit-levels) but of the *type of relational context* that gives the sin its specific character: boundary (CHAR-19) versus bond (CHAR-20).

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=16576, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=16577, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Several. (1) Lev 26:40 — "confess their iniquity and... treachery" — shared with CHAR-18 (iniquity) and CHAR-3 (confession); (2) Hos 6:7 — "transgressed the covenant; dealt faithlessly with me" — shared with CHAR-19 (covenant-transgression); (3) Eze 18:24 — "treachery of which he is guilty and the sin he has committed, for them he shall die" — shared with CHAR-19 (the sinning) and potentially CHAR-17 (guilt); (4) 2Ch 29:6 — "our fathers have been unfaithful... they have forsaken him and have turned away their faces" — shared with CHAR-5 (refusal to repent) and CHAR-6 (habitual defection); (5) 1Ch 10:13 — "Saul died for his breach of faith... also consulted a medium" — shared with CHAR-1 (wilful sinning — the consulting a medium) and CHAR-19 (transgression of command).

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=16578, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Lev 26:40 shared with CHAR-18 and CHAR-3 confirms that the priestly-confessional framework addresses iniquity (CHAR-18), treachery (CHAR-20), and confession (CHAR-3) as a single moral-repair event. Hos 6:7 shared with CHAR-19 confirms that covenant-transgression is simultaneously boundary-crossing (CHAR-19) and relational-betrayal (CHAR-20) — the same theological event generates both characteristics and requires both to describe it fully. 1Ch 10:13 shared with CHAR-1 confirms that the specific sinning acts (consulting the medium) constitute the faithlessness of CHAR-20; CHAR-1 names the acts while CHAR-20 names their covenantal character.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=16579, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified across CHAR-1, CHAR-3, CHAR-18, CHAR-19 (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=16580, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two dimensions are partially shared: (1) *The covenant-boundary-crossing dimension* (the same act as CHAR-19) — partially shared with CHAR-19 where both analyse the same event (Hos 6:7); (2) *The confession-producing dimension* (faithlessness named and confessed) — partially shared with CHAR-3. CHAR-20's unique dimensions — (a) the spirit-vigilance-requirement (Mal 2:15/16 — guard yourselves in your spirit), (b) the hardening-under-distress dynamic (2Ch 28:22 — yet more faithless), (c) the constitutive-prior-bond requirement (the covenant whose breaking makes faithlessness possible), and (d) the cross-register M13/M31 flagging — are not shared with any other M10 characteristic studied.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=16581, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The minimal dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-20 as the most analytically distinct characteristic in M10: its unique dimensions (spirit-vigilance command, hardening-under-distress, prior-bond requirement, M13/M31 cross-register) are not derivable from any other M10 characteristic. The characteristic adds what no other M10 characteristic supplies: the constitutive covenantal-relational frame that gives M10's moral failures their deepest interpersonal weight. Every other M10 characteristic can be described in terms of acts, conditions, records, or identities; CHAR-20 requires a covenant-bond to have been and now be broken.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=16582, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-20]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced with minimal overlap (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T6 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T6.1.1–T6.1.3, T6.2.1–T6.2.3, T6.3.1–T6.3.4, T6.4.1–T6.4.4, T6.5.1–T6.5.4, T6.6.1–T6.6.3, T6.7.1–T6.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-20] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 26:40/42; 1Ch 5:25/9:1/10:13; 2Ch 26:16/28:22/29:6/33:19; Ezr 10:2; Dan 9:7; Pro 11:6; Isa 48:8; Jer 5:11; Eze 18:24/39:26; Hos 6:7; Mal 2:15/16 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T7.


---

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=16583, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two primary term-families, exclusively OT Hebrew (no NT vocabulary). (1) **ma.al (H4603/H4604)** — verb and noun "to commit a breach of faith, to act unfaithfully, to trespass against sacred things"; from a root meaning "to cover up, to conceal, to act covertly" — naming faithlessness as a concealed inner breach; the ma.al-faithlessness is the hidden betrayal of what was owed; used primarily for unfaithfulness toward God and sacred things (VCG-01 dominant); 1Ch 10:13 — "breach of faith with the Lord." (2) **ba.gad (H0898)** — verb "to act treacherously, to be faithless, to deal deceitfully"; root meaning relates to garment-covering (clothing that covers while hiding) or to dealing deceitfully; the ba.gad-treachery is the betrayal that wears a friendly face; used more for interpersonal treachery (VCG-02 dominant) and constitutional character (VCG-03). The two roots together reveal: ma.al names the sacred-covenantal breach; ba.gad names the relational-interpersonal betrayal.

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=16584, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Wide across both terms. (1) *Ma.al (H4603)*: verb form — "acted faithlessly" (*māʿal*) — Eze 14:13; 2Ch 26:16/36:14; noun form H4604 (*māʿal*) — "breach of faith" — 1Ch 10:13 — "died for his breach of faith"; "faithlessness" — Ezr 9:2/4; 2Ch 33:19. Both verb and noun cover the same semantic range; (2) *Ba.gad (H0898)*: Qal active — "dealt treacherously" (*bāgad*) — Jer 3:20; Mal 2:14; Hos 6:7; agent-noun — "the treacherous" (*bōgĕdîm*) — Psa 119:158 — "I look at the faithless"; Isa 24:16 — "the traitors have betrayed"; "treacherous" (*bāgĕdāh*) — Jer 3:8; Psa 25:3 — "those who are wantonly treacherous." The grammatical range — verb (act), noun (event/condition), and agent-noun (person) — covers the act, the condition, and the person constituted by the act.

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=16585, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Ma.al within CHAR-20 covers: (1) *Cultic/sacred trespass*: Lev 5:15 — "breach of faith... in any of the holy things"; Lev 6:2 — "breach of faith against the Lord by deceiving his neighbor"; (2) *Personal covenant-breaking with God*: 1Ch 10:13 — "breach of faith with the Lord in that he did not keep the command"; (3) *Corporate covenant-betrayal*: 1Ch 9:1 — "breach of faith" causing national exile; 2Ch 36:14 — "exceedingly unfaithful" as national disposition. Ba.gad within CHAR-20 covers: (1) *Marital faithlessness*: Num 5:12 — "breaks faith with him"; Mal 2:14 — "been faithless to the wife of your youth"; (2) *Corporate covenant-betrayal toward God*: Jer 3:20 — "treacherous to me, O house of Israel"; Hos 6:7 — "dealt faithlessly with me"; (3) *Constitutional inner character*: Isa 48:8 — "deal treacherously" as foreknown bent; Isa 24:16 — "the traitors have betrayed."

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=16586, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — the two term-families actively distinguish two aspects of the same faithlessness-reality. (1) *Ma.al* — the sacred-covenantal breach dimension: emphasises the violation of what is owed to God in the covenant or to sacred things; the hidden/covert character of the breach; (2) *Ba.gad* — the relational-betrayal dimension: emphasises the personal, interpersonal character of the treachery; the friendly-face-concealing-hostility dynamic (Jer 12:6); the constitutional settled character of the traitor (Isa 24:16; Isa 48:8). Together: ma.al names the sacred-breach; ba.gad names the relational-betrayal. Both are required to cover CHAR-20's full semantic range.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=16587, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The structural opposite is *faithfulness* (*ʾĕmûnāh* — steadfast faithfulness; *ḥesed* — steadfast covenant-love). Mal 2:15 — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and let none of you be faithless" — the guarded spirit is the faithful spirit, the structural opposite named by negation. Pro 11:3 — "the integrity of the upright guides them, but the crookedness of the treacherous destroys them" — *tummāh* (integrity, blamelessness) is the structural opposite. The cross-register flags (M13 — Truth/Faithfulness) confirm: the positive M13 faithfulness-characteristic is the structural opposite of CHAR-20's faithlessness.

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=16588, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — ba.gad generates the person-type. Isa 24:16 — "the traitors (*bōgĕdîm*) have betrayed" — the plural agent-noun names the person-class of traitors; Psa 119:158 — "I look at the faithless (*bōgĕdîm*) with disgust" — the faithless as a recognisable class; Pro 2:22 — "the treacherous (*bōgĕdîm*) will be rooted out of it." The *bōgĕd* (participle/agent-noun form of ba.gad) is CHAR-20's person-type term — the one whose inner character is constituted by treachery.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=16589, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Yes — within the confession complex. Lev 26:40 — "if they confess (*hithwaddû*) their iniquity and... their treachery" — the Hithpael of *yadah* (to confess, to acknowledge) is the supplication-verb preceding the covenant-remembering; Ezr 10:2 — "we have broken faith with our God" — the verbal acknowledgment is the seeking-term. The seeking is not of abstract forgiveness but specifically of covenant-remembering: Lev 26:42 — "I will remember my covenant" — the confession of treachery seeks the reactivation of the divine covenant-memory.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=16590, S) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-20]** S — The corpus contains no NT vocabulary. The characteristic is exclusively OT Hebrew. The ma.al/ba.gad vocabulary for covenant-faithlessness does not have direct NT Greek equivalents within the programme's studied corpus; NT equivalents would fall into M13 (faithfulness) or M31 (faith/unbelief) territory rather than M10. The OT-only character reflects the specific covenantal-relational framework of the Levitical, Deuteronomic, and prophetic traditions in which faithlessness is most precisely named. The absence of NT vocabulary is analytically significant: CHAR-20 is the M10 characteristic whose conceptual framework is most thoroughly OT-covenantal.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=16591, S) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-20]** S — No NT vocabulary appears in the corpus. The characteristic's conceptual content in the NT period would be addressed through the M13 and M31 clusters rather than through M10-specific vocabulary. Noted.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=16592, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-20]** E — The vocabulary arc — from Exo 21:8 (ma.al — breaking faith with a designated woman) through Lev 5:15/6:2 (ma.al — cultic breach of faith against holy things and God) through Num 5:12/27 (ma.al/ba.gad — marital faithlessness ordeal) through Jos 7:1/22:16-31 (ma.al — covenant-breach of the ban; Achan-to-Israel) through Chronicles' sustained ma.al-history (1Ch 9:1/10:13; 2Ch 12:2/26:16/28:19/22/29:6/30:7/36:14) through Ezra's communal confession (ma.al — Ezr 9:2/4/10:2/6/10) through the Psalms/Proverbs ba.gad-portrait of the traitor (Psa 25:3/119:158; Pro 11:3/6/13:2/15) through Isaiah's constitutional-character treachery (Isa 21:2/24:16/33:1/48:8) through Jeremiah/Ezekiel's covenant-betrayal accusation (Jer 3:20/5:11; Eze 14:13/17:20/39:23) through Malachi's spirit-vigilance command (Mal 2:14/15/16) — reveals the complete OT covenantal-faithlessness arc from individual sacred-trespass through marital-covenant-breach through national covenant-betrayal to constitutional inner character.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=16593, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Three primary anchors. (1) *1Ch 10:13* — "Saul died for his breach of faith (*māʿal*). He broke faith (*māʿal*) with the Lord in that he did not keep the command of the Lord" — ma.al functions as both the verdict-noun ("for his breach of faith") and the accusation-verb ("He broke faith") in the same verse; the double use names the faithlessness as both the cause of death (juridical function) and the specific act (evidential function); (2) *Mal 2:14* — "though she is your companion and your wife by covenant" and "to whom you have been faithless (*bāgadtāh*)" — ba.gad functions as the predicate naming the faithlessness within the most intimate covenantal relationship; (3) *Isa 48:8* — "I knew that you would surely deal treacherously (*bāgôd tibgôd*)" — the infinitive absolute + finite verb construction (*bāgôd tibgôd*) intensifies the certainty and totality of the foreknown treachery; the doubling is the Hebrew grammar of absolute certainty.

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=16594, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Four literary forms. (1) *Historical narrative* (1Ch 9:1/10:13; 2Ch 12:2/26:16/28:22/36:14; Jos 7:1/22:16-31): requires reading within the Chronicler's theological-historical framework where faithlessness is the summarising verdict on reigns and events; (2) *Legal-cultic code* (Lev 5:15/6:2/26:40; Num 5:12/27): requires reading within the covenant-obligation framework; the breaches are defined and remedied within the priestly system; (3) *Prophetic accusation/lament* (Jer 3:20/5:11; Eze 14:13/17:20/39:23; Hos 6:7; Isa 24:16/48:8): requires reading within the covenant-lawsuit framework; God or prophet as plaintiff accusing Israel of treachery; (4) *Wisdom proverb* (Pro 11:3/6/13:2/15/25:19; Psa 119:158): requires reading within the practical-consequences framework — treachery as a self-destructive character trait.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=16595, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Four key argument structures. (1) *1Ch 10:13's death-verdict argument*: premise — Saul broke faith with the Lord (specific acts: not keeping the command, consulting a medium); conclusion — "Saul died for his breach of faith"; structure — faithlessness to God produces death as its juridical consequence; (2) *2Ch 28:22's distress-deepening paradox*: premise — Ahaz was in distress; expected conclusion — distress produces repentance; actual conclusion — "became yet more faithless"; structure — constitutional faithlessness inverts the expected distress-correction sequence; (3) *Mal 2:14-16's spirit-vigilance argument*: premise — God was witness of the covenant with the wife of one's youth; conclusion — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and do not be faithless"; structure — divine witness of the covenant creates the obligation of spirit-level vigilance; (4) *Lev 26:40-42's confession-covenant argument*: premise — "if they confess their iniquity and... treachery"; conclusion — "I will remember my covenant"; structure — honest confession of faithlessness triggers divine covenant-memory.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=16596, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Four contextual settings. (1) *Chronicler's theological-historical verdict*: 1Ch 9:1/10:13; 2Ch 12:2/26:16/28:22/36:14 — the Chronicler's summary-assessments of reigns where faithlessness is the definitive inner characterisation of failure; (2) *Priestly-cultic ordeal and remedy*: Lev 5:15/6:2; Num 5:12/27 — the sanctuary as the setting for addressing faithlessness through prescribed ritual; (3) *Post-exilic communal confession*: Ezr 9:2/4/10:2/6/10 — the returned exile community processing accumulated faithlessness through corporate acknowledgment; (4) *Prophetic covenant-accusation*: Jer 3:20/5:11; Eze 14:13/17:20; Hos 6:7 — the prophet as covenant-plaintiff naming Israel's faithlessness before God.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=16597, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Two complementary primary anchors. (1) *Mal 2:15* — "guard yourselves in your spirit, and let none of you be faithless to the wife of your youth" — primary anchor for spirit-level location and the prevention-imperative: it is the only verse in M10 that explicitly names the spirit as the level requiring vigilance against a M10 characteristic, establishing that faithlessness originates at the spirit level and must be resisted there; (2) *1Ch 10:13* — "Saul died for his breach of faith. He broke faith with the Lord in that he did not keep the command of the Lord, and also consulted a medium, seeking guidance" — primary anchor for the judicial-consequence register: names faithlessness as the death-verdict cause, specifying the acts that constituted it, and naming it twice in the same verse as both verdict and offence-description.

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=16598, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Mal 2:15 uniquely reveals that faithlessness is *a spirit-level danger requiring active inner vigilance* — "guard yourselves in your spirit" — not merely a behavioural choice or external circumstance. No other verse in M10's 22-characteristic corpus names the spirit (*rûaḥ*) as the specific inner level requiring guarding against a sin-characteristic. 1Ch 10:13 uniquely reveals that faithlessness can function as *the single summarising verdict for an entire life's moral failure*: "Saul died for his breach of faith" — all of Saul's disobedience, divided loyalties, and occult consultation is captured in the single term *māʿal* (breach of faith); the term is capacious enough to encompass an entire reign's moral failure as its defining inner character.

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=16599, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-20]** E — **Attachment theory** (Bowlby and Ainsworth's framework of secure/insecure attachment and its role in generating betrayal-responses) and **covenant theology's anthropological implications** (Walter Brueggemann's *Theology of the Old Testament* — the covenantal partner as moral subject) provide the most illuminating frameworks. Attachment theory illuminates Pro 11:6's "treacherous are taken captive by their lust" — the insecure-attachment dynamic where the desire that should be secured by the covenant is instead captured by a rival object. Brueggemann's covenantal framework illuminates why Jer 3:20's "treacherous wife" image is theologically normative rather than merely illustrative — the covenant IS the defining relational structure.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=16600, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Attachment theory illuminates 2Ch 28:22's distress-deepening paradox: in disorganised attachment, the very person who should provide safety (God) becomes the object of increasing avoidance under distress, producing the paradox of distress driving the person further from the secure base rather than toward it. Ahaz's "became yet more faithless" under distress is the disorganised-attachment response: proximity-seeking toward God would relieve the distress but the faithless disposition makes God a threat rather than a refuge. Brueggemann's covenant framework illuminates why CHAR-20 has cross-register flags to M13 and M31: the covenant faithfulness (M13) is what CHAR-20 negates; the trust (M31) is what faithlessness dissolves.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=16601, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Attachment theory treats disorganised attachment as produced by early relational experience rather than chosen. The verse evidence treats faithlessness as a wilful choice (1Ch 10:13 — Saul's specific acts; Lev 26:40 — "treachery they committed against me") within a constitutionally bent will (Isa 48:8 — foreknown before birth). The divergence reveals: the biblical faithlessness has both a constitutional dimension (Isa 48:8 — attachment theory may illuminate this) and a volitional dimension (1Ch 10:13 — attachment theory underweights this). The constitutional and volitional dimensions co-exist; neither framework alone captures both.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=16602, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-20]** E — Attachment theory surfaces the dynamics of *re-attachment after betrayal* — how the betrayed party (God in VCG-01) can sustain the relationship through the betrayal and remain available for restoration. The verse evidence describes God hiding his face (Eze 39:23) and then restoring (Lev 26:42 — "I will remember my covenant") — but does not theorise the inner divine process by which the covenant-faithful God maintains availability through the betrayal. The gap is the mechanism of divine covenant-faithfulness sustaining the relationship through the experience of covenant-faithlessness. No further verse investigation is needed; the gap is a theological-anthropological complement.

---

T7 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       20/20 ✓ (T7.1.1–T7.1.10, T7.2.1–T7.2.6, T7.3.1–T7.3.4)
  2. Scope marker:       20× [CHAR-20] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T7.1.8/T7.1.9 are S)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 21:8; Lev 5:15/6:2/26:40/42; Num 5:12/27; Jos 7:1/22:16; 1Ch 9:1/10:13; 2Ch 12:2/26:16/28:22/36:14; Ezr 9:2/4/10:2/6/10; Psa 25:3/119:158; Pro 2:22/11:3/6/13:2/15/25:19; Isa 21:2/24:16/33:1/48:8; Jer 3:20/5:11; Eze 14:13/17:20/39:23; Hos 6:7; Mal 2:14/15/16 — all in §3)
  → PASS. All 8 tiers complete.
