---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=16673, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — the cognitive faculty is perversion's primary target in the NT. 1Ti 6:5 — "depraved in mind (*nous*)" — the mind is the specifically named faculty; the depravity is at the seat of understanding; 2Cor 11:3 — "your thoughts (*noēmata*) will be led astray from a sincere and pure devotion to Christ" — the thoughts are the target; Isa 29:16 — "you turn things upside down! Shall the potter be regarded as the clay?" — the cognitive assessment of the creator-creature relationship is inverted.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=16674, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion *corrupts* the cognitive faculty at its root. 1Ti 6:5 — "depraved in mind and deprived of the truth" — the two go together: mind-depravity produces truth-deprivation; the cognitive faculty cannot access truth when it is depraved. Isa 29:16 — the cognitive inversion is so complete the creature can say of its maker "He did not make me" — the most basic cognitive relationship (creature knowing its creator) is perverted. 2Cor 11:3 — the serpent-deceived leading astray of thoughts parallels Eve's deception — the cognitive faculty is corrupted by a deceptive external agent operating on it from outside.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=16675, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals that perversion produces a two-stage cognitive corruption: (1) primary cognitive faculty impairment (1Ti 6:5 — mind depraved) and (2) consequent truth-deprivation (1Ti 6:5 — "deprived of the truth"). The depraved mind cannot self-correct because the very faculty of self-assessment is what has been corrupted. Isa 29:16's creator-denial confirms: perversion corrupts cognition at the most fundamental level — the capacity to know the source of one's existence. The cognitive pattern makes CHAR-21 the most epistemically devastating characteristic in M10.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=16676, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The memory faculty is engaged through the forgetting-God pattern. Jer 3:21 — "they have perverted their way; they have forgotten the Lord their God" — the perversion of the way is explicitly linked to forgetting God; memory of God is what the perversion eliminates. Pro 2:17 (cross-context) — forgetting covenant; Deu 32:20 — the perverse generation — "children in whom is no faithfulness" — the absence of faithfulness implies the forgetting of the covenant-bond that faithfulness maintained.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=16677, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion *suppresses* the spiritually significant memory. Jer 3:21 — "perverted their way... forgotten the Lord their God" — the perversion of the life-direction produces the forgetting of God; the causal link is direct. The forgetting is not neutral loss but the active suppression of the memory that would recall the covenant-bond. This connects CHAR-21 to CHAR-20: the faithlessness that CHAR-20 describes is enabled by CHAR-21's perversion-induced forgetting of the covenant-partner (God).

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=16678, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The memory pattern reveals that perversion operates by displacing the spiritually anchoring memories (memory of God, of the covenant, of the created order) with the inward orientation toward distorted objects. Jer 3:21 — "perverted their way; forgotten the Lord" — the perversion and the forgetting are simultaneous and causally linked. The restoration from perversion (returning to God — Jer 3:12-13) requires the reactivation of memory (acknowledgment of guilt) alongside the volitional turning.

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=16679, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — in both the affective-attraction-to-perversion and the affective-distress-under-perversion directions. (1) *Affective attraction*: Pro 2:14 — "who rejoice in doing evil and delight in the perverseness of evil" — the will and emotion are positively oriented toward what is morally inverted; delight and rejoicing are the named affective states toward perversity; (2) *Affective distress*: Psa 38:6 — "I am utterly bowed down and prostrate; all the day I go about mourning" — the person under the weight of their own perversity mourns; the affect is grief and prostration.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=16680, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion *inverts* the affective faculty: what should produce shame produces delight (Pro 2:14 — rejoicing in evil) and what should be straightforward produces mourning (Psa 38:6 — bowed down under the weight of sin). The affective inversion is one of perversion's clearest markers: Gal 6:8 — "sows to his own flesh" implies an affective orientation toward fleshly desire that drives the corruption; 2Pe 1:4 — "corruption that is in the world because of sinful desire" — desire as the affective driver of corruption.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=16681, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The affective pattern reveals that Pro 2:14's "delight in the perverseness of evil" is the most analytically significant affective finding for CHAR-21: the person has achieved an affective positive-orientation toward what is inverted. This is not neutral wrongdoing but joyful embrace of perversity — the affective faculty has been so thoroughly perverted that evil's distortion is a source of pleasure. This affective-delight-in-perversity distinguishes CHAR-21 from characteristics where wrongdoing is reluctantly pursued or passively sustained.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=16682, E) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — through the evil-devising dimension. Pro 6:14 — "with perverted heart devises evil" — the devising is a creative-cognitive act; the perverted heart produces plans and schemes; Pro 16:30 — "whoever winks his eyes plans dishonest things" — the planning is a creative-cognitive activity directed toward evil; Isa 32:7 — "he plans wicked schemes to ruin the poor with lying words" — elaborate planning of perverse schemes.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=16683, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion *misdirects* the creative faculty toward evil's devising. Pro 6:14 — "continually devises evil" — the creative-cognitive capacity is captured and deployed toward schemes of harm and discord. The "continually" (*kol-hayyôm* — all the time) confirms that the perverted heart's creative capacity is perpetually directed toward evil rather than occasionally. The creative faculty is thus not eliminated by perversion but redirected — the perverted person can be diligently creative in scheming harm.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=16684, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The creative pattern reveals a significant distinction from CHAR-20 (faithlessness) and CHAR-19 (transgression): CHAR-21 is the only M10 characteristic studied that explicitly evidences engagement of the creative-devising faculty. Pro 6:14's "perverted heart devises evil continually" names the creative capacity as captured by perversion. This confirms CHAR-21 as M10's characteristic of active inner distortion rather than passive failure — the perverted heart is not merely wrong but actively, creatively wrong.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=16685, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes. (1) *Will actively embracing perversion*: Pro 2:14 — "who rejoice in doing evil and delight in the perverseness of evil" — the will positively orients toward perversity; Pro 16:30 — "whoever winks his eyes plans... he who purses his lips brings evil to pass" — the will executes the perverse plan; (2) *Will captured by corruption*: 2Pe 2:19 — "slaves of corruption... For whatever overcomes a person, to that he is enslaved" — the will is enslaved by what once it chose; Eph 4:22 — "old self... corrupt through deceitful desires" — the desires override the will's capacity for self-direction.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=16686, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion *captures and enslaves* the volitional faculty progressively. The initial active-will choice of perversity (Pro 2:14 — delight in perverseness) produces the enslavement of 2Pe 2:19 — "slaves of corruption"; whatever overcomes the person enslaves them. The will that freely chose perversity is progressively incapacitated by what it chose. The remedy (Eph 4:22-24 — "put off... put on") is a volitional act requiring renewal of the spirit of the mind (Eph 4:23 — "renewed in the spirit of your minds").

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=16687, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The volitional pattern reveals the same trajectory as CHAR-19 and CHAR-20: the will freely embraces perversity (Pro 2:14) and is progressively enslaved by it (2Pe 2:19). CHAR-21's distinctive contribution is the enslavement-mechanism precision: "whatever overcomes a person, to that he is enslaved" (2Pe 2:19) — the general principle that mastery produces slavery confirms that perversion's capture of the will is not unique to perversion but follows the universal mechanism of conquest-produces-slavery.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=16688, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Agency is engaged through both active commission and the reversal to passivity. (1) *Active agency*: Pro 6:14 — "devises evil, continually sowing discord" — active agentive commission; Rev 19:2 — "the great prostitute who corrupted the earth with her immorality" — active agentive corruption reaching to cosmic scale; Isa 32:7 — "plans wicked schemes to ruin the poor" — deliberately agentive; (2) *Passive captivity*: 2Pe 2:19 — "slaves of corruption" — the agency is captured; Jude 10 — "destroyed by all that they understand instinctively" — the active agent has become the passive victim of their own instinct.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=16689, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion *redirects and ultimately captures* agency. The active devising (Pro 6:14 — "devises evil continually") and scheming (Isa 32:7 — "plans wicked schemes") show agency being deployed toward corruption. 2Pe 2:19's "slaves of corruption" and Jude 10's "destroyed by instinct" show the endpoint: the formerly agentive person has become the captive and victim of what they once actively chose. The agency trajectory confirms CHAR-21's self-destructive character: active corruption produces passive destruction.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=16690, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The agency pattern reveals CHAR-21's most distinctive inner-being arc: the transition from active evil-devising agent (Pro 6:14; Isa 32:7) to passive instinct-driven captive (Jude 10 — destroyed by instinct; 2Pe 2:19 — enslaved). The reversal of agency — from the person who perverts to the person destroyed by their own perversion — is CHAR-21's defining developmental trajectory. The perverter becomes the perverted-by-their-own-perversion.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=16691, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — through the wisdom-hatred-of-perversion and the self-indictment dimensions. Pro 8:13 — "perverted speech I hate" — wisdom's moral evaluation explicitly names perversity as hated; Psa 38:6's confession and Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned and done wrong and acted wickedly" — the moral evaluation faculty engaged in corporate self-indictment; Pro 21:12 — "the Righteous One observes the house of the wicked; he throws the wicked down to ruin" — God's moral evaluation of perversity leading to judgment.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=16692, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion *inverts* the moral evaluation faculty. Isa 29:16 — the creature evaluating the potter as if the clay could assess the maker — the most extreme moral-evaluation inversion: the creature's evaluative faculty is so perverted it assesses the creator incorrectly. Pro 19:3 — "his heart rages against the Lord" after his own folly ruins his way — the moral evaluation faculty wrongly assigns blame outward to God rather than inward to the self. Pro 2:14 — "delight in the perverseness of evil" — the evaluative faculty assesses perversity as delightful rather than shameful.

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=16693, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals CHAR-21's deepest analytical contribution: Isa 29:16's creator-creature inversion is M10's most extreme moral-evaluation perversion. The creature's evaluative faculty has been so thoroughly inverted that it cannot assess the most fundamental moral relationship correctly (creator-creature). This cognitive-moral-evaluative inversion is what makes CHAR-21 "inner inversion" in its most radical sense: not merely doing wrong while knowing it wrong, but assessing wrongness as rightness and the creator as non-creator.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=16694, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — through the confession-producing and the conscience-bypassing dimensions. (1) *Confession-producing conscience*: Job 33:27 — "I sinned and perverted what was right" — the conscience produces the confession of perversity; Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned and done wrong" — communal conscience-confession; 1Ki 8:47 — "we have acted perversely" — the conscience-acknowledgment; (2) *Conscience-bypassing*: Pro 2:14 — "delight in the perverseness of evil" — the conscience has been bypassed to the point that perversity produces delight rather than shame; 2Pe 2:12 — "irrational animals... blaspheming" — the conscience is not operational in the animal-instinctual mode.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=16695, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion *progressively suppresses and bypasses* the conscience. The early-stage honest confession (Job 33:27; Dan 9:5 — conscience still operational) gives way to the mid-stage delight in perverseness (Pro 2:14 — conscience bypassed by affective inversion) to the end-stage animal-instinctual destruction (Jude 10; 2Pe 2:12 — conscience entirely non-operational). The trajectory: operational conscience → bypassed conscience → eliminated conscience. The characteristic's depth is indexed by the stage of conscience-suppression reached.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=16696, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The conscience pattern reveals CHAR-21's most analytically significant inner-being trajectory: the three-stage suppression of conscience from operational-recognition (Job 33:27 — "I perverted what was right") through delight-bypass (Pro 2:14 — enjoying perverseness) to animal-instinct elimination (Jude 10 — destroyed by instinct without reasoning). No other M10 characteristic studied evidences this three-stage conscience-trajectory with such precision. CHAR-21 is the conscience-elimination characteristic of M10.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=16703, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — God operates toward the perversion-bearing person in four modes. (1) *Hiding his face*: Deu 32:20 — "I will hide my face from them; I will see what their end will be, for they are a perverse generation" — God's response to perversity is relational withdrawal; (2) *Discipline rather than abandonment*: 2Sa 7:14 — "when he commits iniquity, I will discipline him with the rod of men... not take my steadfast love from him" — God disciplines the perverse act within sustained love; (3) *Observing and overturning*: Pro 21:12 — "the Righteous One observes the house of the wicked; he throws the wicked down to ruin" — God sees perversity and acts against it; (4) *Judging the perverting agent*: Rev 19:2 — "he has judged the great prostitute who corrupted the earth" — eschatological judgment of the corrupting agent; 1Cor 3:17 — "if anyone destroys God's temple, God will destroy him."

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=16704, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Two bases. (1) *Holiness and wisdom that hates perversity*: Pro 8:13 — "the fear of the Lord is hatred of evil. Pride and arrogance and the way of evil and perverted speech I hate" — God's character (wisdom, holiness) is constitutively opposed to perversity; (2) *Steadfast love that disciplines within relationship*: 2Sa 7:14 — "I will discipline him with the rod of men... but my steadfast love will not depart from him" — the steadfast love remains the relational basis even while discipline responds to perversity.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=16705, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — God's disposition is one of *holy opposition to perversity combined with disciplinary love toward the perverted person*. Pro 8:13 — wisdom hates perverted speech (holy opposition to the characteristic); 2Sa 7:14 — "my steadfast love will not depart from him" (disciplinary love toward the person). The distinction is important: God's hatred is of the perversity itself; his sustained commitment is to the person even as he disciplines the perverse conduct. Deu 32:20's face-hiding is for a time ("I will see what their end will be") rather than permanent abandonment.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=16706, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — God-to-human operation (face-hiding, discipline, observation-and-overturning, eschatological judgment) is evidenced across all VCGs. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=16707, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — through the confession-movement. 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned and have acted perversely and wickedly" — the exile's confession of perversity is the movement toward God (followed in 1Ki 8:48 by "if they return to you with all their mind and with all their heart"); Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned and done wrong and acted wickedly" — Daniel's prayer naming perversity as part of the comprehensive movement toward God; Job 33:27 — "I sinned and perverted what was right" — the individual's confession before God.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=16708, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Honest naming of the perversity*: 1Ki 8:47 — "acted perversely" — the confession must name the specific distortion, not generic sin; Dan 9:5 — the comprehensive self-indictment includes "done wrong" (a.vah) specifically; (2) *Putting off the old self*: Eph 4:22-24 — "put off your old self which... is corrupt" → "put on the new self, created after the likeness of God" — the movement requires deliberate interior action at the level of identity; (3) *Sowing to the Spirit rather than the flesh*: Gal 6:8 — the orientation-change from flesh-sowing to Spirit-sowing is the practical inner posture required.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=16709, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The human-to-God direction reveals that the perversion-corrupted person retains the capacity for movement toward God through confession and repentance. 1Ki 8:47-48 — the exile-caused perversion does not permanently foreclose return: "if they return to you with all their mind and with all their heart... and pray toward... this temple... then hear from heaven" — the sequence confession → return → prayer → divine hearing remains accessible. The perversion, even when it has produced generational consequences (exile), does not terminate the movement-toward-God pathway.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=16710, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Human-to-God operation (confession, putting off old self, Spirit-sowing) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=16711, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — through social corruption and the great-prostitute archetype. (1) *Social corruption contagion*: 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company ruins good morals" — the perverse person's company corrupts the morals of those in proximity; the perversion extends outward through social influence; (2) *Cosmic-scale corruption*: Rev 19:2 — "the great prostitute who corrupted the earth with her immorality" — the archetype of the perverted agent whose corruption extends to the whole earth; (3) *False-teacher corruption*: 2Cor 7:2 — "we have corrupted no one" — Paul's denial implies the false teachers did corrupt — they extend perversion to their hearers; 2Cor 11:3 — "as the serpent deceived Eve... your thoughts will be led astray" — the external corrupting agent working on the hearers.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=16712, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Two enabling conditions. (1) *Relational or positional access*: 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company" — the company-relationship is the access-point; 2Cor 11:3 — the false teacher's preaching relationship with the congregation; Rev 19:2 — Babylon's position in the world-system; (2) *Active deception*: 2Cor 11:3 — "as the serpent deceived Eve by his cunning" — cunning deception is the mechanism; the extension of perversion requires active misrepresentation rather than transparent wrongdoing.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=16713, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The person must have become constitutionally perverted before they can extend it. 2Pe 2:19 — "they themselves are slaves of corruption" — the false teachers who promise freedom are already enslaved to corruption before they extend it to others; the extending of perversion to others follows the consolidation of the perversion within the person. The vine-degeneracy pattern (Jer 2:21) applies: the vine must first turn wild from within before it produces wild fruit that corrupts the vineyard.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=16714, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — The giving/extension direction is evidenced through social contagion (1Cor 15:33), cosmic corruption (Rev 19:2), and false-teacher extension (2Cor 7:2/11:3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=16715, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — through three reception modes. (1) *Personal inner generation*: Pro 6:14 — "with perverted heart devises evil" — the perversity is generated from within; Eph 4:22 — "corrupt through deceitful desires" — the old self receiving corruption through its own deceitful desires; (2) *External social reception*: 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company ruins good morals" — good morals received from without initially are ruined by external corrupting company; 2Cor 11:3 — "your thoughts will be led astray" — the congregants receive corrupted thinking from the false teacher; (3) *Generational inheritance*: Deu 32:20 — "perverse generation" — the perversity characterises the generation inherited as a communal condition.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=16716, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Reception-enabling: (1) *Wisdom and God-fear*: Pro 2:12 — "wisdom delivers from men of perverted speech" — wisdom-possession is the remedy-enabling condition; (2) *Participation in divine nature*: 2Pe 1:4 — "having escaped from the corruption... you may become partakers of the divine nature" — divine nature participation is the remedy-reception; (3) *Spirit-sowing orientation*: Gal 6:8 — sowing to the Spirit rather than flesh enables life-reception rather than corruption-reception. Reception-blocking: (1) *Delight in perverseness*: Pro 2:14 — "delight in the perverseness of evil" — affective attachment to perversity blocks remedy; (2) *Animal-instinctual mode*: Jude 10; 2Pe 2:12 — the unreasoning-animal state forecloses remedy-reception.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=16717, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Three states evidenced. (1) *New self in God's likeness*: Eph 4:24 — "put on the new self, created after the likeness of God in true righteousness and holiness" — the addressed-perversion state is the new-self condition, reconstituted in the image-likeness; (2) *Participation in divine nature*: 2Pe 1:4 — "become partakers of the divine nature, having escaped from the corruption" — the escape from corruption is simultaneously entry into divine-nature participation; (3) *Renewed mind*: Eph 4:23 — "renewed in the spirit of your minds" — the depraved mind's remedy is the renewal at the spirit-level of the mind.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=16718, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — The receiving direction is evidenced across personal generation (Eph 4:22), social reception (1Cor 15:33; 2Cor 11:3), and generational inheritance (Deu 32:20). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=16719, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — relational proximity intensifies perversion's corrupting reach. 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company ruins good morals" — the company-relationship (close proximity) is the mechanism of corruption; Pro 16:28 — "a whisperer separates close friends" — the close friendship is precisely what perversity targets and destroys; 2Cor 11:3 — the pastoral relationship (Paul's anxiety for his congregation) is the context in which thoughts are led astray. Across distance: Rev 19:2 — Babylon's corruption reaches the whole earth — the corrupt agent of sufficient power corrupts even across relational distance.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=16720, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The characteristic crosses all boundaries. Lev 18:23/20:12 (VCG-04) — the sexual perversions apply universally regardless of covenant; Deu 16:19 — bribery-perversion of judgment is addressed to Israel's covenant courts but the principle applies universally; 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company ruins good morals" — a universal principle; Rev 19:2 — Babylon's corruption of the whole earth — crosses all covenantal and non-covenantal contexts. CHAR-21 is universal in scope: any human moral faculty can be twisted regardless of covenant membership.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=16721, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The relational scope is comprehensive: divine-human (Deu 32:20 — God hiding face; Isa 29:16 — creator-creature inversion), individual-personal (Pro 6:14 — perverted heart; Eph 4:22 — old self corruption), interpersonal-social (Pro 16:28 — close friends separated; 1Cor 15:33 — bad company), cosmic (Rev 19:2 — earth corrupted; Rom 8:21 — creation in bondage to corruption). CHAR-21 is M10's most cosmically-scoped characteristic: corruption extends from the individual inner faculty all the way to the whole earth and creation's bondage.

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=16722, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Relational scope (divine-human, individual, interpersonal, cosmic) is extensively evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=16723, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — through the serpent-type passage. 2Cor 11:3 — "as the serpent deceived Eve by his cunning, your thoughts will be led astray" — the serpent is named as the archetypal external corrupting agent; the serpent's deceptive cunning is the model for the false teachers' thought-corrupting strategy. The serpent-type names an adversarial spiritual agency as the paradigmatic external source of the perversion of thought.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=16724, E) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — through the serpent-archetype (2Cor 11:3). The false teachers operate with serpentine cunning to corrupt the congregation's thoughts — the adversarial activity is represented through the teacher-as-serpent-type. Rev 19:2's "great prostitute" operates as the cosmic adversarial corruption-agent who has corrupted the whole earth. Both figures function as adversarial agents whose perversion-activity is actively directed against the covenant community.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=16725, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-21]** S — No angelic ministry is named in connection with perversion as inner inversion in the 64-verse corpus. The serpent (2Cor 11:3) is adversarial rather than angelic. No beneficial angelic mediation of CHAR-21's content or resolution is evidenced.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=16726, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Adversarial spiritual agency is evidenced through the serpent-archetype (2Cor 11:3) and the cosmic-prostitute corruption-agent (Rev 19:2). Beneficial angelic ministry (T4.6.3) is absent. The spiritual-beings interface for CHAR-21 is primarily adversarial: the characteristic's extension to others operates through serpentine deception (2Cor 11:3) and cosmic-scale corrupting agency (Rev 19:2).

---

  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T4.1.1–T4.1.4, T4.2.1–T4.2.4, T4.3.1–T4.3.4, T4.4.1–T4.4.4, T4.5.1–T4.5.4, T4.6.1–T4.6.4)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-21] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T4.6.3 is S)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Deu 32:20; 2Sa 7:14; Pro 2:12/14/6:14/8:13/16:28/21:12; Isa 29:16; Jer 2:21; 1Ki 8:47/48; Dan 9:5; Job 33:27; Eph 4:22/23/24; Gal 6:8; 1Ti 6:5; 1Cor 3:17/15:33; 2Cor 7:2/11:3; 2Pe 1:4/2:12/19; Jude 10; Rom 8:21; Rev 19:2 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=16727, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Both. (1) *Negative condition change*: Jer 2:21 — "turned degenerate and become a wild vine" — the transformation of what was good into what is corrupt; 1Ti 6:5 — "depraved in mind and deprived of the truth" — a constitutional condition-change of the mind; (2) *Negative orientation change*: Jer 3:21 — "perverted their way; forgotten the Lord their God" — the directional orientation of life is twisted from God-toward to God-forgetting; (3) *Positive transformation through remedy*: Eph 4:22-24 — "put off... old self... put on the new self" — the transformation from the corrupt old self to the new-self condition; 2Pe 1:4 — "escaped from the corruption... partakers of the divine nature" — the escape-and-participation transformation.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=16728, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Both dimensions evidenced. (1) *Reversible through renewal*: Eph 4:23 — "renewed in the spirit of your minds"; 2Pe 1:4 — escape into divine nature; Gal 6:8 — Spirit-sowing producing eternal life; (2) *Eschatologically final for the unrepentant*: Rev 19:2 — Babylon judged and destroyed; 1Cor 3:17 — "God will destroy him" who destroys the temple; 2Pe 2:12 — "destroyed in their destruction" — the animal-instinctual corruption reaches its eschatological irreversible endpoint; (3) *Progressive deepening without address*: Eph 4:22 — "corrupt through deceitful desires" — the ongoing-tense verb (*phtheiromenos* — is being corrupted) indicates a progressive process that deepens without the intervention of the putting-off/putting-on.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=16729, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Transformation evidence is extensive in all directions (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=16730, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — two sequences. (1) *Perversion-to-destruction sequence*: perverted heart (Pro 6:14 — inner origin) → devises evil → sows discord (outer expression) → overthrown (Pro 21:12 — judgment); or: deceitful desire (Eph 4:22 — desire-level) → corrupt old self → enslaved to corruption (2Pe 2:19) → destroyed by instinct (Jude 10); (2) *Recognition-to-renewal sequence*: confession of perversity (1Ki 8:47; Dan 9:5) → movement toward God (1Ki 8:48 — "return to you with all their mind and with all their heart") → putting off old self (Eph 4:22) → renewal of mind's spirit (Eph 4:23) → participation in divine nature (2Pe 1:4).

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=16731, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The perversion-to-destruction sequence reveals that CHAR-21 operates as a progressive interior corruption that moves from the heart/desire-level origin through to complete self-destruction (Jude 10 — "destroyed by what they understand instinctively"). Each stage is a deeper constitutional corruption. The recognition-to-renewal sequence reveals that the address requires action at the same constitutional level as the corruption: the mind is depraved (1Ti 6:5) so the mind is renewed (Eph 4:23); the desire corrupts (Eph 4:22) so the Spirit-sowing counters it (Gal 6:8); the old self is corrupt so the new self is put on (Eph 4:24).

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=16732, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Sequence is evidenced in both perversion-to-destruction and recognition-to-renewal directions (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=16733, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Three change-mechanisms. (1) *Desire-corruption mechanism*: Eph 4:22 — "corrupt through deceitful desires" — deceitful desire is the active mechanism; the desire deceives (appearing good) while corrupting (delivering ruin); 2Pe 1:4 — "corruption that is in the world because of sinful desire" — desire-driven corruption is the world's universal mechanism; (2) *Cognitive-inversion mechanism*: Isa 29:16 — "you turn things upside down" — the cognitive faculty's inversion of the proper order; 1Ti 6:5 — mind-depravity producing truth-deprivation; (3) *Social-contagion mechanism*: 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company ruins good morals" — external social influence as the corruption mechanism; 2Cor 11:3 — serpentine deception as the thought-corruption mechanism.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=16734, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes across the five VCGs. (1) *VCG-01 (speech perversity)*: mechanism is the perverted heart driving perverted output (Pro 6:14) — heart → speech chain; (2) *VCG-02 (judgment perversion)*: mechanism is bribery corrupting the discernment faculty (Exo 23:8) — external material incentive distorting inner cognitive operation; (3) *VCG-03 (mind/will/way)*: mechanism is a.vah — the twisting of the moral way direction (Jer 3:21); (4) *VCG-04 (sexual perversion)*: mechanism is violation of created-order categories (te.vel — Lev 18:23); (5) *VCG-05 (corruption/decay)*: mechanism is deceitful desire (Eph 4:22) and social contagion (1Cor 15:33).

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=16735, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Mechanisms are evidenced across all five VCG contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=16736, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes. (1) *Suffering as perversity's consequence*: Psa 38:6 — "I am utterly bowed down and prostrate; all the day I go about mourning" — the perversity (sin-weight) produces bodily and inner prostration; Pro 13:6 — "sin overthrows the wicked" — perversion-driven sin overturns the wicked; Pro 19:3 — "a man's folly brings his way to ruin" — self-produced perversion brings self-produced ruin; (2) *Suffering as context of confession*: Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned and done wrong" — Daniel's comprehensive confession including perversity comes in the context of exile-suffering; 1Ki 8:47 — the exiles' confession of acting perversely comes from their place of captivity.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=16737, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Suffering *reveals* perversion's depth (the exile reveals what the perversity produced — 1Ki 8:47) and *triggers* confession (the exile-suffering is the context in which the people confess "we have acted perversely"). Suffering does not produce the perversity but exposes it as the cause of the present condition. Pro 19:3's "his folly brings his way to ruin, his heart rages against the Lord" — the suffering of ruin reveals perversity while simultaneously triggering the additional perversity of blaming God.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=16738, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — The relationship between perversion and suffering is evidenced (Psa 38:6; Pro 13:6/19:3; Dan 9:5; 1Ki 8:47). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=16739, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — as both the negative formation arc's structural corruption and the positive formation's counter-movement. (1) *Negative formation*: Deu 32:20 — "perverse generation" — the perversity characterises an entire generation's formed character; Eph 4:22 — "old self which belongs to your former manner of life" — perversity is embedded in the "former manner of life" as the formation that was; (2) *Positive counter-formation*: Eph 4:22-24 — the deliberate formation-reversal sequence: put off (the corrupt formation) → be renewed (Eph 4:23 — renewed in the spirit of your minds) → put on (the new formation in God's likeness).

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=16740, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Perversion functions as the *anti-formation process* — the progressive deterioration of what was formed as good into what is morally distorted. Jer 2:21 — "I planted you a choice vine... how then have you turned degenerate?" — the good formation (choice vine) becomes the corrupt formation (wild vine) through perversion's anti-formation process. Eph 4:22's "is being corrupted" (ongoing) confirms: perversion is not a one-time event but an ongoing anti-formation process that must be countered by the equally ongoing renewal of Eph 4:23.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=16741, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Formation evidence is present through generational character (Deu 32:20), the former-manner-of-life (Eph 4:22), and the counter-formation renewal (Eph 4:22-24). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=16742, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — two eschatological endpoints. (1) *Judgment of the perverting agent*: Rev 19:2 — "he has judged the great prostitute who corrupted the earth" — the cosmic-scale corruption agent faces God's true and just judgment; 1Cor 3:17 — "God will destroy him" who destroys the temple; Pro 10:31 — "the perverse tongue will be cut off"; (2) *Liberation from corruption*: Rom 8:21 — "the creation itself will be set free from its bondage to corruption and obtain the freedom of the glory of the children of God" — the eschatological fullness for CHAR-21 is the cosmic liberation from corruption; 2Pe 1:4 — having escaped from the corruption into divine nature as the individual eschatological orientation.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=16743, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-21]** E — For the unrepentant: 2Pe 2:12's "born to be caught and destroyed" anticipates the eschatological destruction that matches their corrupted nature; Jude 10's "destroyed by what they understand instinctively" names the present instinct-driven destruction as the eschatological pattern in miniature. For the addressed perversion: Rom 8:21's present "bondage to corruption" anticipates the eschatological "freedom of the glory of the children of God"; Eph 4:24's "put on the new self, created after the likeness of God in true righteousness and holiness" anticipates the eschatological fullness of the image-likeness restored.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=16744, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced in both judgment (Rev 19:2; 1Cor 3:17; Pro 10:31) and liberation (Rom 8:21; 2Pe 1:4) directions. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=16745, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-21]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: mind-depravity (1Ti 6:5), desire-corruption (Eph 4:22; 2Pe 2:19), bodily bondage (Rom 8:21). Developmental consequences. (1) *Mind-depravity deposit*: 1Ti 6:5 — "depraved in mind and deprived of the truth" — the developmental consequence is progressive truth-deprivation; the depraved mind cannot self-correct because the corrective faculty itself is corrupted; (2) *Desire-corruption deposit*: Eph 4:22 — ongoing "corrupt through deceitful desires" produces the developing enslavement of 2Pe 2:19 — "slaves of corruption"; the desire that initially corrupted becomes the master; (3) *Bodily bondage deposit*: Rom 8:21 — the creation's "bondage to corruption" awaits eschatological liberation — a sustained constitutional condition.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=16746, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes. Deu 32:20 — "a perverse generation, children in whom is no faithfulness" — the perversity characterises an entire generation inherited; the generational deposit is the settled perverse character of the people as a whole. 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company ruins good morals" — the social-contagion mechanism produces generational consequence as each corrupted generation corrupts the next through company-influence. The generational consequence of perversion is the formation of successively more corrupt generations whose "former manner of life" (Eph 4:22) is progressively more perverted.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=16747, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-21]** E — T2.8 found deposit evidence. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---

  2. Scope marker:       21× [CHAR-21] ✓

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=16748, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur within the 64-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-18 (Iniquity as accumulated moral crime)*: Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned and done wrong (*a.vah*) and acted wickedly" — the perversity (a.vah) is named alongside iniquity and wickedness; 1Ki 8:47 — "acted perversely (*a.vah*) and wickedly" — the three-tier moral failure formula again; (2) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned and have acted perversely" — sinning and perverting paired; Dan 9:5 — the comprehensive confession pairs all M10 categories; (3) *CHAR-19 (Transgression)*: Dan 9:5 — "done wrong and acted wickedly and rebelled (*pa.sha*)" — transgression alongside perversion; (4) *CHAR-16 (Sinner as moral character)*: 2Pe 2:12 — "irrational animals... born to be caught and destroyed" — the person constituted by corruption matches CHAR-16's constituted sinner-type.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=16749, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-21 as the *structural inner distortion* that underlies and generates M10's other characteristics. While CHAR-1 names the sinning act, CHAR-18 names the accumulated crime, and CHAR-19 names the boundary-crossing, CHAR-21 names the inner twisted condition that produces all these. 1Ki 8:47 and Dan 9:5 consistently place perversion (*a.vah*) alongside sinning (*cha.ta*) and wickedness — the three-tier formula in which CHAR-21's inner structural distortion is the companion of the act-characteristics. CHAR-21 is M10's inner-constitution characteristic.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=16750, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=16751, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-21]** E — CHAR-21 consistently *accompanies* CHAR-1 (sinning acts — the three-tier confession formula) and *precedes* the consequences of CHAR-18 (the accumulated crime that the perversion generates). CHAR-21 consistently *underlies* CHAR-19 (boundary-crossing proceeds from the twisted inner orientation — Jer 3:21 — "perverted their way and forgotten God") and CHAR-20 (faithlessness enabled by the perversion-induced forgetting). The primary sequence: CHAR-21's inner distortion (constitutive) → generates CHAR-1's sinning acts → accumulates as CHAR-18's crime.

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=16752, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The relationship with CHAR-1 is *generative*: CHAR-21's twisted heart (Pro 6:14 — "perverted heart devises evil") generates the sinning acts that CHAR-1 names; CHAR-21 is upstream of CHAR-1. The relationship with CHAR-18 is *developmental*: the perversion generates the accumulated crime over time. The relationship with CHAR-19 is *constitutive*: the perverted way (CHAR-21 — Jer 3:21) is what the transgression crosses from; perversion makes the person someone who transgresses rather than merely the act of transgressing.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=16753, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Sequential patterns (generative, developmental, constitutive) are evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=16754, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-1 (Sinning acts)*: Pro 6:14 — "perverted heart devises evil" — the perversion generates the evil-devising acts; (2) *CHAR-17 (Guilt as inner-being state)*: Job 33:27 — "I sinned and perverted what was right" produces the recognition-guilt confessed; 1Ki 8:47 — the community's guilt-confession includes the perversity; (3) *Social discord*: Pro 6:14 — "continually sowing discord"; Pro 16:28 — "a dishonest man spreads strife, a whisperer separates close friends" — perversion producing social breakdown; (4) *Forgetting God*: Jer 3:21 — "perverted their way; forgotten the Lord their God" — the perversion produces the forgetting that enables CHAR-20's faithlessness.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=16755, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes. (1) *Deceitful desire (within CHAR-16/M31 adjacent)*: Eph 4:22 — "corrupt through deceitful desires" — the desire-faculty's deception drives the corruption; 2Pe 1:4 — "sinful desire" as world-corruption's source; (2) *CHAR-6 (Habitual defection)*: Jer 2:21 — the vine that "turned degenerate" — the habitual turning from God is the generating condition for the degeneracy perversion; (3) *Bribery* (external material corruption — within social dynamics): Exo 23:8 — bribery produces judgment-perversion — an externally induced inner-corruption.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=16756, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-21]** E — CHAR-21 is the *inner-structural foundation* of M10's sin-acts: it is the constitutional distortion from which the sinning acts (CHAR-1), accumulated crimes (CHAR-18), and transgressions (CHAR-19) proceed. The "perverted heart" (Pro 6:14) that devises evil is what underlies and drives all the other M10 characteristics' overt expressions. CHAR-21 is uniquely positioned as M10's constitutional-distortion characteristic: the twisting of the inner moral faculty that generates the other characteristics' expressions.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=16757, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=16758, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The a.vah family (H5753A/B — VCG-03) is shared with M10's general iniquity vocabulary: Dan 9:5 and 1Ki 8:47 use a.vah alongside cha.ta (CHAR-1) and pa.sha (CHAR-19) in the three-tier formula — confirming shared root territory. The ftheirō/fthora (NT VCG-05) vocabulary does not appear in other M10 characteristics; it is distinctive to CHAR-21. Tah.pu.khah (VCG-01/03) and te.vel (VCG-04) and sa.laph (VCG-02) are exclusive to CHAR-21 within M10.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=16759, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The a.vah root (*ʿāwāh* — to be crooked, to twist) shares conceptual territory with the broader a.von family (CHAR-18) — both express moral crookedness/twisting — though at the root level a.vah and a.von are distinct Hebrew roots. The NT ftheirō/fthora vocabulary is unique to CHAR-21 within M10. Tah.pu.khah shares no root with other M10 characteristics.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=16760, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The vocabulary pattern reveals CHAR-21 as M10's richest (nine term-families) and most vocabulary-diverse characteristic. The partial overlap with CHAR-18 through a.vah/a.von confirms the conceptual adjacency: both characteristics address moral distortion but CHAR-18 focuses on the accumulated crime-weight while CHAR-21 focuses on the inner structural twisting. The NT's unique ftheirō/fthora vocabulary for CHAR-21 confirms it as the characteristic that most comprehensively engages the NT's corruption-theology language.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=16761, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Partial vocabulary overlap (a.vah/a.von conceptual adjacency) with CHAR-18 and extensive distinctive vocabulary (tah.pu.khah; te.vel; sa.laph; ftheirō/fthora) are evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=16762, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-21]** E — CHAR-18 (Iniquity as accumulated moral crime) most closely resembles CHAR-21 — both address the inner moral-distortion reality. The precise distinction: CHAR-18 names the accumulated crime-weight before God (the objective moral-legal account, measured and stored); CHAR-21 names the inner structural distortion of the moral faculty (the subjective twisted condition, the bent internal constitution). CHAR-18 is the record; CHAR-21 is the corruption of the inner instrument that generates the record. The perverted heart (CHAR-21 — Pro 6:14) produces the iniquity (CHAR-18 — the crime that accumulates).

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=16763, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The overlap is at the three-tier confession formula (1Ki 8:47; Dan 9:5) where a.vah (CHAR-21) appears alongside cha.ta (CHAR-1) and is paired with wickedness in contexts that also involve CHAR-18's accumulated crime. The precise boundary: a.vah in these contexts names the twisting-action quality of the sin (the "done wrong" as inwardly crooked) while CHAR-18's a.von names the accumulated crime-weight. The same act can be simultaneously CHAR-21 (perverted in its nature) and CHAR-18 (criminal in its standing before God).

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=16764, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The distinction is one of *focus and level*: CHAR-18 focuses on the accumulated moral-crime account (objective, juridical, before God); CHAR-21 focuses on the inner moral-faculty distortion (subjective, constitutional, the bent inner instrument). They operate at different analytic levels of the same moral failure: CHAR-18 at the juridical-record level; CHAR-21 at the constitutional-faculty level. The perverted heart (CHAR-21) is what generates the iniquity-record (CHAR-18).

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=16765, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=16766, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Several. (1) Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned and done wrong (*a.vah*) and acted wickedly and rebelled (*pa.sha*)" — shared with CHAR-1 (sinning), CHAR-18 (acted wickedly), CHAR-19 (rebelled); (2) 1Ki 8:47 — "we have sinned and acted perversely and wickedly" — shared with CHAR-1 and CHAR-18; (3) Eph 4:22 — "old self corrupt through deceitful desires" — shared with CHAR-1 (the sins arising from the old self) and potentially CHAR-16 (the sinner as moral character — the old self as the sinner-identity); (4) Pro 6:14 — "perverted heart devises evil, continually sowing discord" — CHAR-21's primary anchor; functions within CHAR-16's person-type characterisation as well.

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=16767, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Dan 9:5 shared with CHAR-1, CHAR-18, CHAR-19 confirms that the comprehensive confession formula encompasses all M10's primary sin-categories simultaneously — the confession is not sin-category-specific but covers the full inner-being moral reality in one act. This suggests CHAR-21's perversity (*a.vah* — "done wrong") is the inner-structural companion to every other M10 characteristic's specific expression: you cannot have sinning (CHAR-1), accumulated crime (CHAR-18), and transgression (CHAR-19) without the inner moral distortion (CHAR-21) that pervades them all.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=16768, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified across CHAR-1, CHAR-18, CHAR-19 (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=16769, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Two dimensions are partially shared: (1) *The accumulated-twisting-as-crime dimension* is partially shared with CHAR-18 (through the three-tier confession formula); (2) *The sinning-act dimension* is partially shared with CHAR-1 where a.vah's twisting produces acts. CHAR-21's unique dimensions — (a) the desire-as-corruption-mechanism (Eph 4:22 — "through deceitful desires"), (b) the three-stage conscience-suppression trajectory (operational → bypassed → eliminated), (c) the cosmic-liberation eschatology (Rom 8:21), and (d) the creative-faculty engagement through evil-devising (Pro 6:14 — "continually devises") — are not shared with any other M10 characteristic studied.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=16770, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The minimal dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-21 as the most constitutionally fundamental characteristic in M10: while other M10 characteristics describe acts, records, identities, and feelings, CHAR-21 describes the distorted inner instrument from which all the others proceed. The unique dimensions (desire-mechanism, conscience-elimination, cosmic scope, creative evil-devising) are what make CHAR-21 irreducible — they describe the inner moral faculty in its structural distortion, not any specific expression of that distortion.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=16771, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-21]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced with minimal overlap (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=16772, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Nine term-families spanning OT and NT. (1) **tah.pu.khah (H8419)** — "perverseness, frowardness" from root *hāpak* — "to turn, to overturn"; names what has been overturned from its proper state; (2) **la.zut (H3891)** — "perversity, crookedness" — Pro 4:24; (3) **sa.laph (H5557)** — "to subvert, to overthrow, to pervert" — root sense of overturning foundations; used for judgment-perversion (VCG-02); (4) **a.vah (H5753A/B)** — "to be crooked, to twist, to distort" — the verbal form "to do iniquity" by twisting what was straight; (5) **te.vel (H8397)** — "perversion, confusion" from a root meaning "to mix, to confuse" — names the violation of created-order categories; (6) **sur (H5494)** — "to turn aside, to degenerate" — the vine turning wild (Jer 2:21); (7) **cha.val (H2254B)** — "to destroy, to ruin, to corrupt" — M03 cross-register; (8) **nav.lut (H5040)** — "lewdness, shamelessness" — M07 cross-register (Hos 2:10); (9) **ftheirō (G5351) / fthora (G5356) / diaftheirō (G1311)** — NT "to destroy/corrupt/corruption" — the Greek family for moral and physical decay. Together the terms reveal: perversion as the overturning of the proper order (tah.pu.khah/sa.laph), the mixing of what should be kept separate (te.vel), the twisting of what should be straight (a.vah/sur), and the decay/ruin of what was good (cha.val/ftheirō/fthora).

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=16773, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Wide across all term-families. (1) *A.vah (H5753A/B)*: Qal active verb — "acted wickedly/perversely" (1Ki 8:47; Dan 9:5); participle — "one who commits iniquity" (Psa 106:6); noun form — "twisted mind" (Pro 12:8); (2) *Tah.pu.khah (H8419)*: noun — "perverseness" (Pro 2:12/14; Pro 6:14/8:13/16:28/23:33); (3) *Sa.laph (H5557)*: verb — "subverts/perverts" (Exo 23:8; Pro 21:12/22:12); (4) *Ftheirō (G5351)*: active verb — "corrupts" (1Cor 15:33; 2Cor 7:2; Rev 19:2); passive — "is being corrupted" (Eph 4:22 — *phtheiromenos*); (5) *Fthora (G5356)*: noun — "corruption/decay" (Rom 8:21; Gal 6:8; 2Pe 1:4/2:12/19; 1Cor 15:42/50). The grammatical range covers the act (verb — "perverts"), the condition (passive — "is being corrupted"), the noun-state (tah.pu.khah — "perverseness"), and the noun-consequence (fthora — "corruption/decay").

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=16774, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The terms collectively cover: (1) *Judgment-subversion*: sa.laph (Exo 23:8 — bribery subverting righteous cause); (2) *Speech-perversion*: tah.pu.khah (Pro 2:12 — perverted speech; Pro 10:31 — perverse tongue); (3) *Heart-perversion and evil-devising*: tah.pu.khah (Pro 6:14 — perverted heart devising evil); (4) *Personal moral twisting*: a.vah (1Ki 8:47; Dan 9:5 — "acted perversely"); (5) *Bodily-prostration from sin*: a.vah (Psa 38:6 — "utterly bowed down"); (6) *Created-order violation*: te.vel (Lev 18:23 — bestiality; Lev 20:12 — incest); (7) *Degenerative transformation*: sur (Jer 2:21 — turned degenerate wild vine); (8) *Desire-driven progressive corruption*: ftheirō/fthora (Eph 4:22; 2Pe 1:4); (9) *Cosmic bondage to decay*: fthora (Rom 8:21 — creation's bondage to corruption).

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=16775, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — the nine term-families actively distinguish distinct aspects. (1) *Tah.pu.khah* — the overturned/inverted quality (speech, heart-devising); (2) *Sa.laph* — the subverting of foundational structures (judgment); (3) *A.vah* — the twisting of personal moral direction; (4) *Te.vel* — the confusion of created-order categories; (5) *Sur* — the degenerative turning-aside from original purity; (6) *Cha.val* — the destructive corruption/ruin; (7) *Ftheirō/fthora* — the progressive decay through desire. No other M10 characteristic has this term-family breadth; CHAR-21 is M10's most terminologically complex characteristic.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=16776, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The structural opposite is implied through the wisdom and righteousness vocabulary. Pro 8:13 — wisdom's hatred of perverted speech implies wisdom (*ḥokmāh*) as the structural opposite; Pro 10:31 — "the mouth of the righteous brings forth wisdom, but the perverse tongue will be cut off" — the righteous mouth as the direct opposite; Eph 4:24 — "the new self, created after the likeness of God in true righteousness and holiness" — the new-self righteousness and holiness as the structural opposite of the old self's corruption (Eph 4:22).

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=16777, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — multiple person-type terms. Tah.pu.khah produces the person-type "the man of perverted speech" (Pro 2:12 — *ʾîš tahpukôt*); "the perverse tongue" (Pro 10:31/32) names the person-type by their organ; 1Ti 6:5 — "people who are depraved in mind" names the person-type by their faculty; 2Pe 2:12 — "like irrational animals, creatures of instinct, born to be caught and destroyed" — the ultimate perverted person-type named as the animal-instinctual creature.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=16778, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Yes — within the confession-prayer register. 1Ki 8:47 — "acting perversely... we have sinned" — the full confession-prayer including perversity-acknowledgment; Dan 9:5 — "we have sinned and done wrong" — Daniel's seeking-prayer names the perversity. The supplication is the naming of the perversity within prayer as the honest foundation of the God-turning. Psa 38:6's "bowed down and prostrate" is the bodily supplication-posture under the weight of perversion's consequences.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=16779, E) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The OT/NT relationship reveals *continuity with expansion into the cosmic and desire dimensions*. The OT terms (tah.pu.khah, a.vah, te.vel, sa.laph) address perversion primarily at the level of acts, speech, judgment, and specific category-violations. The NT ftheirō/fthora vocabulary expands this into: the desire as the active corruption-agent (Eph 4:22; 2Pe 1:4), the cosmic scope (Rom 8:21 — creation's bondage), the mind-specific depravity (1Ti 6:5), and the eschatological judgment (Rev 19:2). The NT adds the mechanistic precision (desire-driven) and the cosmic scope that the OT terms do not independently provide.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=16780, E) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-21]** E — *Diaftheirō (G1311)* — "to corrupt thoroughly, to destroy completely" — appears in 1Ti 6:5 and 2Cor 4:16 as the intensified form of ftheirō, naming a complete or thoroughgoing corruption/destruction. The dia- prefix intensifies: where ftheirō names corruption, diaftheirō names the thorough-through corruption that penetrates comprehensively. 1Ti 6:5's "depraved in mind" (*noenous diephtharmenōn* — minds thoroughly corrupted) uses diaftheirō's intensified form to describe the mind-specific comprehensive depravity.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=16781, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-21]** E — The vocabulary arc — from sa.laph's judgment-subversion (Exo 23:8 — bribery overturning justice) through tah.pu.khah's speech-perversion (Pro 6:14 — perverted heart; Pro 8:13 — wisdom hating perverseness) through a.vah's personal moral-twisting (1Ki 8:47; Dan 9:5 — "acted perversely") through te.vel's created-order violation (Lev 18:23 — bestiality as perversion) through sur's degenerative vine-turning (Jer 2:21) through cha.val's destructive corruption (Neh 1:7; Isa 32:7) through ftheirō's progressive desire-corruption (Eph 4:22) through diaftheirō's comprehensive mind-depravity (1Ti 6:5) through fthora's cosmic bondage-to-corruption (Rom 8:21) to Rev 19:2's judgment of the cosmic corrupting agent — reveals M10's most terminologically diverse characteristic spanning all levels from individual judgment-perception through speech through personal moral-twisting through created-category-violation through cosmic decay to eschatological judgment.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=16782, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Two primary anchors. (1) *Pro 6:14* — "with perverted heart (*tahpukôt*) devises evil, continually sowing discord" — tah.pu.khah functions as the qualifying adjective for the heart; the perverseness characterises the heart's operative condition from which the evil-devising proceeds; the heart's perverted state is the grammatical subject's inner characterisation; (2) *Eph 4:22* — "old self which belongs to your former manner of life and is corrupt (*phtheiromenos*) through deceitful desires" — ftheirō in the present passive participle ("being corrupted") functions as the active ongoing condition of the old self; the present tense confirms the progressive nature; the passive voice names the old self as the entity being acted upon by deceitful desires.

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=16783, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Five literary forms. (1) *Wisdom proverb* (Pro 2:12/14; 4:24; 6:14; 8:13; 10:31/32; 12:8; 16:28/30; 19:3; 23:33): requires reading within practical-moral instruction framework — perversity as a character-trait with practical consequences; (2) *Legal-cultic code* (Exo 23:8; Deu 16:19; Lev 18:23/20:12): requires reading within covenant-obligation framework — perversion as violation of God's established moral and created order; (3) *Penitential confession-prayer* (1Ki 8:47; Dan 9:5; 2Sa 24:17; Psa 38:6; Psa 106:6): requires reading within the comprehensive self-indictment register; (4) *Prophetic oracle* (Deu 32:20; Isa 29:16; Jer 2:21/3:21): requires reading within the covenant-accusation framework; (5) *Epistle* (Eph 4:22; 1Ti 6:5; 2Pe 1:4/2:12/19; Jude 10; Gal 6:8; Rom 8:21): requires reading within the theological-instructional framework.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=16784, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Four key argument structures. (1) *Exo 23:8's bribery-corruption argument*: premise — a bribe blinds the clear-sighted; conclusion — it subverts the cause of the righteous; structure — external corruption → inner blindness → injustice; (2) *Eph 4:22-24's old-self/new-self argument*: premise — the old self is being corrupted through deceitful desires; command — put it off; command — be renewed in the spirit of your mind; command — put on the new self in God's likeness; structure — diagnosis (corruption) → imperative (put off) → renewal → counter-formation; (3) *2Pe 2:19's mastery-slavery argument*: premise — these teachers are slaves of corruption; principle — "whatever overcomes a person, to that he is enslaved"; conclusion — they cannot offer freedom they do not have; structure — general mastery-principle applied to the specific corruption-case; (4) *Pro 6:14's heart-output argument*: premise — perverted heart; conclusion — devises evil continually; structure — inner faculty state → output character.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=16785, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Four contextual settings. (1) *Wisdom instruction context* (multiple Proverbs): the pupil being warned against perverse companions and speech; (2) *Covenant-accountability context* (Exo 23:8; Deu 16:19; Lev 18:23/20:12): justice and created-order violations named in covenant law; (3) *Exile-confession context* (1Ki 8:47; Dan 9:5; Neh 1:7): the exiled community processing accumulated perversity through comprehensive confession; (4) *NT ecclesial-ethical context* (Eph 4:22; 1Ti 6:5; 2Pe 2:12/19): false teachers' perversion threatening congregation, requiring counter-formation.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=16786, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Two complementary primary anchors. (1) *Pro 6:14* — "with perverted heart devises evil, continually sowing discord" — primary anchor for the OT inner-being perversity register: it is the corpus's most precise statement of perversity's inner location (heart), mechanism (devising), and mode (continually); the heart is named as perverted, the will is named as devising, the output is named as discord-sowing, and the frequency is named as continual; (2) *Eph 4:22* — "put off your old self, which belongs to your former manner of life and is corrupt through deceitful desires" — primary anchor for the NT progressive-corruption register: the present passive participle (*phtheiromenos* — "being corrupted") names the ongoing process; the mechanism is named (deceitful desires); the target is named (old self/former manner of life).

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=16787, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Pro 6:14 uniquely reveals that perversity in the heart produces *continual* evil-devising — the "continually" (*kol-hayyôm*) names an ongoing, unceasing output characteristic of the perverted heart. No other verse in the 64-verse corpus names the perversity as perpetually generative. This distinguishes CHAR-21 from characteristics where wrongdoing is episodic: the perverted heart does not occasionally devise evil but continually schemes it. Eph 4:22 uniquely reveals the *present-tense progressive corruption mechanism* — "is being corrupted" through deceitful desires — the present participle naming an active, ongoing process rather than a past event or future threat. These two verses together define CHAR-21's essence: the continually-devising perverted heart (Pro 6:14 — OT) and the progressively-corrupted old self through deceitful desire (Eph 4:22 — NT).

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=16788, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-21]** E — **Moral psychology of corruption** (specifically Philip Zimbardo's *The Lucifer Effect* on the situational triggers of corruption and evil) and **developmental psychology of character corruption** (specifically the corruption of virtue theory in Aristotle's *Nicomachean Ethics* — bad habituation producing bad character) provide the most illuminating frameworks. Zimbardo illuminates the situational triggers (Exo 23:8 — bribery as situation-corruption; 1Cor 15:33 — bad company as social-situational corruption). Aristotle's bad-habituation framework illuminates Pro 6:14's "continually devising" — the perverted heart's ongoing evil-devising is the habituation that has produced the perverted character.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=16789, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Zimbardo's situational corruption illuminates Exo 23:8 and Deu 16:19's bribery-perversion: the situation of receiving a bribe is itself a corrupting-trigger that distorts the otherwise functional discernment-faculty. 1Cor 15:33 — "bad company ruins good morals" — is the social-situational corruption mechanism that Zimbardo's framework precisely illuminates. Aristotle's bad-habituation framework illuminates Pro 6:14's "continually" — the ongoing evil-devising is both product and producer of the perverted character; the heart that continually devises evil is being progressively formed into a more thoroughly perverted heart.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=16790, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Zimbardo treats situational corruption as the primary driver (good people in bad situations do evil). The verse evidence treats the inner disposition (perverted heart — Pro 6:14) as primary, with situations as secondary triggers (Exo 23:8 — bribery triggers the perversion of what was already an assessable-corruptible judgment faculty). Aristotle treats character-formation as the product of freely chosen habituation. The verse evidence adds: Eph 4:22 — "is being corrupted through deceitful desires" — the desires themselves are named as *deceitful* (they appear good while corrupting); this is a mechanism Aristotle's voluntary-habituation framework underweights. The divergence reveals: biblical perversion operates through deceptive desire that appears good while producing corruption — a mechanism neither framework fully captures.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=16791, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-21]** E — Zimbardo's framework surfaces the *normalisation of corruption* — how gradual situational corruption makes perversity appear normal or even virtuous. The verse evidence includes this in the Pro 2:14 "delight in the perverseness of evil" — the affective normalisation of perversity — and Isa 29:16's creator-denial as the cognitive normalisation of the creature's inversion. But the social-psychological mechanism by which gradual exposure to corruption normalises it is underspecified in the corpus. The gap is acknowledged as a human-science complement; no further verse investigation is needed.

---

T7 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       20/20 ✓ (T7.1.1–T7.1.10, T7.2.1–T7.2.6, T7.3.1–T7.3.4)
  2. Scope marker:       20× [CHAR-21] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Exo 23:8; Deu 16:19; Lev 18:23/20:12; 1Ki 8:47; Dan 9:5; Neh 1:7; Psa 38:6/106:6; Pro 2:12/14/4:24/6:14/8:13/10:31/32/12:8/16:28/19:3/23:33; Isa 29:16/32:7; Jer 2:21/3:21; Eph 4:22/24; Gal 6:8; 1Ti 6:5; 2Pe 1:4/2:12/19; Jude 10; Rom 8:21; Rev 19:2; 1Cor 15:33/15:42/50; 2Cor 4:16 — all in §3)
  → PASS. All 8 tiers complete.
