## CHAR-22 — Injustice as moral failure of right conduct (189 rows: E=183 S=6 G=0 CS=0)

### T0

#### T0.1.1 (cf=16792, E) — What does the verse evidence reveal about the nature or character of God that this characteristic reflects or images?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The 72-verse corpus reveals God's character primarily through explicit negation of injustice. (1) *God structurally defined as without injustice*: Deu 32:4 — "a God of faithfulness and without iniquity, just and upright is he"; 2Ch 19:7 — "there is no injustice with the Lord our God, or partiality or taking bribes"; Psa 92:15 — "there is no unrighteousness in him"; Job 34:10 — "far be it from God that he should do wickedness, and from the Almighty that he should do wrong"; Zep 3:5 — "the Lord within her is righteous; he does no injustice"; (2) *God as the one who hates injustice*: Isa 61:8 — "I the Lord love justice; I hate robbery and wrong"; (3) *God as the righteous judge of injustice*: Rev 18:5 — God has remembered Babylon's crimes; 2Pe 2:9 — God keeps the unrighteous under punishment; (4) *God as the faithful rewarder against injustice*: Heb 6:10 — "God is not unjust so as to overlook your work."

---

#### T0.1.2 (cf=16793, E) — Does Scripture explicitly attribute this characteristic to God — and if so, what does that attribution reveal about its significance in the human person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The corpus explicitly tests and denies this: Rom 3:5 — "That God is unrighteous to inflict wrath on us? (I speak in a human way.)" — the suggestion of divine injustice is raised as a rhetorical foil and immediately repudiated; Job 36:23 — "who can say, 'You have done wrong'?" — the question is rhetorical, implying the answer is no one. God's absolute freedom from injustice is one of the most densely evidenced theological claims in this corpus: five verses directly assert it (Deu 32:4; 2Ch 19:7; Psa 92:15; Job 34:10; Zep 3:5). The characteristic is exclusively creaturely.

---

#### T0.1.3 (cf=16794, E) — Where Scripture is silent about God's possession of this characteristic, what does that silence suggest about the characteristic's place in the divine image?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The corpus is not silent — it is emphatically declarative. God's freedom from injustice is the defining standard against which all human injustice is measured. Deu 32:4's "without iniquity, just and upright is he" establishes the divine character as the ontological baseline for justice; Eze 18's detailed Ezekiel-chapters ground individual moral accountability in God's own justice. The repeated explicit denials confirm: injustice requires a moral order to violate, and God is that moral order's author and embodiment — he cannot violate what he constitutes.

---

#### T0.2.1 (cf=16795, S) — What does the verse evidence suggest about the purpose this characteristic serves in the human person as created — what does it equip the person to be, do, or become?

**[CHAR-22]** S — The corpus provides no evidence of any positive created purpose for injustice. The characteristic is entirely a fallen-condition phenomenon — a failure of the right-conduct faculty. No verse presents injustice as a designed feature of the image-bearer.

---

#### T0.2.2 (cf=16796, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic is part of the original created design, a response to the fallen condition, or both?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Entirely a response to the fallen condition. Eze 28:15 — "you were blameless in your ways from the day you were created, till unrighteousness was found in you" — the cherub's pre-fallen blamelessness establishes that injustice was not original to the created being; it was "found in" the being as an emergence from within, not as a design-feature. Eze 18:24 — "when a righteous person turns away from his righteousness and does injustice" — injustice enters when the person turns from the prior righteousness; the turn is from, not toward, the designed state.

---

#### T0.2.3 (cf=16797, E) — Is there evidence that this characteristic is oriented toward a future fullness — something the person is moving toward, not only what they currently are?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — two eschatological orientations. (1) *Judgment of the unjust*: 2Pe 2:9 — "the Lord knows how to... keep the unrighteous under punishment until the day of judgment" — the unrighteous's current condition anticipates the definitive judgment; Rev 18:5 — "God has remembered her iniquities" — the accumulated record awaits final reckoning; (2) *Eschatological elimination of injustice*: Zep 3:13 — "those who are left in Israel... shall do no injustice and speak no lies" — the purified remnant's future is characterised by injustice's total absence; the eschatological fullness is the restored right-conduct community.

---

#### T0.3.1 (cf=16798, E) — In what way does this characteristic express the divine image in the human person — what aspect of being made in God's likeness does it instantiate?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice expresses the image through its *inverted exercise of the moral-conduct faculty*: the image-bearer is constituted with the capacity for just conduct — to judge rightly, act fairly, and treat persons according to their worth (the M26 righteousness the cross-register flag tracks). Injustice is this same capacity deployed against its designed purpose. Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — the heart's deliberating capacity (a mark of the image's rational design) is deployed toward unjust schemes. The very faculty of moral reasoning that enables justice also enables injustice.

---

#### T0.3.2 (cf=16799, S) — Does the evidence suggest that this characteristic is shared between God and the human person, or is it exclusively a creaturely analogue to something in God?

**[CHAR-22]** S — Exclusively creaturely (T0.1.2). God is not only free from injustice but constitutes its standard-opposite. The characteristic has no divine analogue.

---

#### T0.3.3 (cf=16800, E) — What does the presence or absence of this characteristic in a person reveal about the condition of the divine image in that person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The presence of injustice reveals the image's *moral-conduct faculty in its perverted expression*: the image-bearer designed for just conduct (Lev 19:15 — "in righteousness shall you judge your neighbor") has turned their judicial, commercial, and interpersonal conduct away from God's standard. Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God.' They are corrupt, doing abominable iniquity" — the godless heart produces unjust conduct; the image's knowledge of God is what sustains just conduct, and its denial produces injustice. Job 18:21 — the unjust person "knows not God" — the absence of God-knowledge is the diagnostic marker of the unjust character.

---

#### T0.4.1 (cf=16801, E) — Does Scripture use this characteristic typologically — deploying it to point toward or participate in a reality beyond the immediate (covenantal, eschatological, christological)?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the Christological substitution type. 1Pe 3:18 — "Christ also suffered once for sins, the righteous for the unrighteous, that he might bring us to God" — the unjust (*adikoi*) are the precise moral category for whom the righteous Christ substitutes; the unrighteous/unjust person-type is the direct object of Christ's redemptive action. The unjust person-type (VCG-03 — *adikos*) is deployed typologically: the human category of the unjust points toward the divine provision of the righteous One who takes their place.

---

#### T0.4.2 (cf=16802, E) — If typological use is present, what is the direction of the typology — does the human instance point toward the divine, or does the divine instance establish the pattern for the human?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The typological direction is from human moral condition (*adikos* — the unjust) toward divine redemptive provision (the righteous Christ dying for them). The human instance (the unjust person) does not point toward the divine; rather the divine action (the righteous dying for the unjust) defines the human category's need and resolution. Direction: human injustice-condition → divine righteous-provision as its resolution.

---

#### T0.4.3 (cf=16803, E) — If no typological use is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Typological use is evidenced (T0.4.1 and T0.4.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

T0 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       12/12 ✓ (T0.1.1–T0.1.3, T0.2.1–T0.2.3, T0.3.1–T0.3.3, T0.4.1–T0.4.3)
  2. Scope marker:       12× [CHAR-22] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: 10/10 E findings cite evidence ✓ (T0.2.1, T0.3.2 are S)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Deu 32:4; 2Ch 19:7; Job 34:10/36:23; Psa 53:1/58:2/92:15; Isa 61:8; Eze 18:24/28:15; Zep 3:5/13; Rom 3:5; Heb 6:10; 1Pe 3:18; 2Pe 2:9; Rev 18:5 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T1.

## T1 — Inner Being Faculty in Operation (24 prompts)

---

### T1

#### T1.1.1 (cf=16804, E) — What is this characteristic called in the programme — and what does the name itself signal about its essential nature?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The programme names this characteristic "Injustice as moral failure of right conduct." Three signals. (1) *Injustice*: the antonym of justice (*mishpat*/dikaiosynē) — what violates the right standard in dealings with persons; (2) *As moral failure*: not merely legal breach but the inner-moral dimension — the heart's orientation toward wrong conduct against others; (3) *Of right conduct*: the failure is specifically of right-conduct (*tsedaqah*/*dikaiosynē*) — the M26 cross-register cluster. The name signals: M10's most explicitly other-directed characteristic — injustice is done *toward* persons and *against* right order; it cannot exist without a victim and a violated standard.

---

#### T1.1.2 (cf=16805, E) — What do the primary Hebrew and Greek terms reveal at the definitional level — before deeper lexical analysis begins?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Five primary term-families. (1) **a.vel / av.lah (H5766A/H5766B)** — noun/verb "injustice, wrong, wickedness, violence" — OT dominant (VCG-01/02); root *ʿāwal* — "to act unjustly, to be crooked"; (2) **av.val (H5767)** — "the unrighteous, the unjust person" — agent-noun (VCG-03 OT); (3) **a.vil (H5760)** — "ungodly, morally lawless" — VCG-03 OT (Job 16:11; Job 18:21); (4) **adikos (G0094)** — NT "unjust, unrighteous" — agent-noun/adjective (VCG-03 NT dominant); (5) **adikēma (G0092)** — NT "wrongdoing, crime, injustice" — the act-noun (Act 18:14; Rev 18:5). The terms cluster around two axes: the wrong-act dimension (a.vel/av.lah/adikēma — what is done) and the wrong-person dimension (av.val/a.vil/adikos — who the person is).

---

#### T1.1.3 (cf=16806, E) — Does the name carry directional, relational, or constitutional implications that orient the enquiry from the outset?

**[CHAR-22]** E — All three. *Directional*: injustice is always directed toward someone — Psa 58:2 — "your hands deal out violence on earth" — the violence is toward others; Eze 3:20 — "commits injustice" as an act against the righteousness-standard; *Relational*: injustice damages persons — Psa 43:1 — "from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me"; Job 6:29 — "let no injustice be done" to Job; *Constitutional*: Luk 16:10 — "one who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much" — injustice is a settled character trait scaling across all contexts; Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the constitutional insensibility to their own wrongdoing.

---

#### T1.2.1 (cf=16807, E) — What kind of inner-being phenomenon does this characteristic appear to be — an act, a disposition, a condition, a quality, or something else?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The corpus reveals CHAR-22 as simultaneously a *dispositional character trait* and a *volitional act*. (1) *Dispositional*: Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much" — a fixed inner character property that scales; Job 15:16 — "drinks injustice like water" — a thoroughly internalized disposition; Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — shamelessness as a stable character-trait; (2) *Volitional act*: Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence" — a deliberate inner devising; Eze 18:24 — "a righteous person... does injustice" — a specific act committed; Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — injustice as a named doable-and-stoppable act.

---

#### T1.2.2 (cf=16808, E) — Is this characteristic simple in structure or does it appear to have constituent elements at first encounter?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three constituent elements corresponding to the three VCGs. (1) *Judicial and commercial injustice* (VCG-01 — 20V): the corruption of right process — partial judging, fraudulent weighing, commercial deceit; (2) *Injustice as oppression and harm to persons* (VCG-02 — 33V): the doing of wrong against vulnerable persons — violence, deceit, verbal wrongdoing; (3) *Character of the unjust person* (VCG-03 — 19V): the person-type constituted by injustice — adikos, av.val, a.vil — whose character is the inner condition from which the acts proceed.

---

#### T1.2.3 (cf=16809, E) — What is the current best working description of this characteristic — encapsulating its constitutional location, the faculties it primarily engages, and its impact on the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Working description: *Injustice as moral failure of right conduct (CHAR-22) is the inner state/disposition of doing wrong toward persons — unjust conduct, unjust character, the moral faculty bent toward wrongdoing against others and against right order. Three registers: (1) Judicial and commercial injustice (VCG-01 — 20V): "you shall do no injustice in court" (Lev 19:15); "till unrighteousness was found in you" (Eze 28:15); (2) Injustice as oppression and harm to persons (VCG-02 — 33V): "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence on earth" (Psa 58:2); "the unjust knows no shame" (Zep 3:5); "drinks injustice like water" (Job 15:16); (3) Character of the unjust person (VCG-03 — 19V): "dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much" (Luk 16:10); "the righteous for the unrighteous" (1Pe 3:18). Cross-register flag: M26 (Righteousness/Justice) on all terms — CHAR-22 is M10's entry-point into the justice/righteousness cluster. Distinguished from CHAR-21 (perversion as inner inversion) by its other-directed focus: injustice is always toward persons and against right-order.*

---

#### T1.3.1 (cf=16810, E) — What is the structural opposite of this characteristic — the inner-being reality that stands against or excludes it?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The structural opposite is *righteousness/justice as right conduct* (*tsedaqah*/*dikaiosynē*) — named explicitly in multiple verses. Lev 19:15 — "in righteousness shall you judge your neighbor" — the impartial righteousness; Deu 32:4 — "just and upright is he" — the divine character as the standard; Zep 3:5 — "the Lord within her is righteous; he does no injustice" — God's righteousness as injustice's opposite; Psa 119:3 — "who also do no wrong, but walk in his ways" — the upright walk in God's ways as the structural opposite of doing wrong.

---

#### T1.3.2 (cf=16811, E) — What does this characteristic explicitly exclude or resist?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The characteristic explicitly excludes: (1) *Inheritance of God's kingdom*: 1Cor 6:9 — "the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God" — the unjust character disqualifies from the kingdom; (2) *Reliable trustworthiness*: Luk 16:11 — "if you have not been faithful in the unrighteous wealth, who will entrust to you the true riches?" — injustice forfeits the trust that enables greater stewardship; (3) *Standing before the righteous*: Pro 29:27 — "an unjust man is an abomination to the righteous" — injustice makes the person morally repugnant to those who value right conduct; (4) *Functioning as a judge*: 1Cor 6:1 — "does he dare go to law before the unrighteous?" — the unjust are unsuitable arbiters.

---

#### T1.3.3 (cf=16812, E) — What is this characteristic not — where does it end and something else begin?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three boundaries. (1) *Not CHAR-21 (Perversion as inner inversion)*: CHAR-21 is the inner structural distortion of the moral faculty; CHAR-22 is that distortion specifically expressed as wrong-conduct toward others; CHAR-21 is constitutive, CHAR-22 is other-directed and conduct-specific; (2) *Not CHAR-19 (Transgression)*: CHAR-19 is the knowing boundary-crossing; CHAR-22 adds the specific other-victim dimension — injustice is done *toward* persons, not merely *past* a known boundary; (3) *Not mere carelessness*: Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — injustice is a deliberate devising; Psa 64:6 — "they search out injustice, saying, 'We have accomplished a diligent search'" — the active, purposeful character distinguishes CHAR-22 from inadvertent wrong.

---

#### T1.4.1 (cf=16813, E) — In what distinct ways does this characteristic operate within the inner person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Four distinct inner modes. (1) *As a heart-devised scheme*: Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — injustice is planned from the heart's deliberating centre; Psa 64:6 — "they search out injustice" with "diligent search" — deliberately planned; (2) *As a thoroughly internalised appetite*: Job 15:16 — "a man who drinks injustice like water" — the characteristic has become the person's natural sustenance; (3) *As shameless character*: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the inner moral-response faculty is insensible; (4) *As a fixed scalar trait*: Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in very little is also dishonest in much" — the inner character is consistent at every scale.

---

#### T1.4.2 (cf=16814, E) — Does this characteristic operate differently depending on context, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes across the three VCG registers. (1) *Judicial context* (VCG-01): injustice operates as partiality and bribery — Psa 82:2 — "judge unjustly and show partiality to the wicked" — the relational-authority context shapes the judicial mode; Lev 19:15 — "you shall do no injustice in court" — the court context defines this mode; (2) *Oppression context* (VCG-02): injustice operates as violent harm against persons — Psa 58:2 — "your hands deal out violence on earth"; Job 15:16 — thoroughly internalised appetite for wrong; (3) *Character context* (VCG-03): injustice operates as a stable identity-level disposition — Luk 16:10 — fixed across small and large; shameless insensibility (Zep 3:5).

---

#### T1.4.3 (cf=16815, E) — Does this characteristic have a communicative or speech-based mode of operation — and if so, how does it function?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the verbal-wrongdoing dimension. Isa 59:3 — "your tongue mutters wickedness" — injustice is uttered; Job 6:30 — "is there any injustice on my tongue?" — the tongue as the potential organ of verbal injustice; Job 13:7 — "will you speak falsely for God and speak deceitfully for him?" — injustice as theological-speech falsehood; Zep 3:13 — "they shall do no injustice and speak no lies" — injustice and lying speech paired as the two modes being eliminated in the remnant. Speech is CHAR-22's secondary expressive mode after conduct.

---

#### T1.5.1 (cf=16816, E) — What is the first or most immediate inner-being response to receiving or encountering this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Varied across positions. (1) *Response of the one suffering injustice*: Job 6:29 — "please turn; let no injustice be done. Turn now; my vindication is at stake" — urgent plea for relief; Psa 43:1 — "vindicate me, O God, and defend my cause against an ungodly people, from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me" — immediate appeal to God for deliverance; (2) *Response of the righteous observer*: Psa 37:1 — "fret not yourself because of evildoers; be not envious of wrongdoers" — the warning against fretful envy; (3) *Non-response of the unjust*: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — no inner response at all.

---

#### T1.5.2 (cf=16817, E) — Is the immediate response consistent across the verse evidence, or does it vary?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Variable by position. The victim's response is urgent appeal and vindication-seeking (Job 6:29; Psa 43:1). The righteous observer's response is the temptation toward fretful envy (Psa 37:1) — addressed by the psalmist's counsel not to fret. The unjust person's inner response is shameless absence of response (Zep 3:5). The witness with a functioning conscience responds by committing to remove injustice (Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more").

---

#### T1.5.3 (cf=16818, E) — Where the verse evidence is silent on immediate response, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — For VCG-01's judicial context (Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice") and commercial context (Lev 19:35 — "do no wrong in judgment, in measures"), the immediate inner experience of the person who is actively withholding their hand is described in the negative (what they don't do) rather than the positive inner-experience dimension. The corpus names the restrained act but not the inner sensation of restraining.

---

#### T1.6.1 (cf=16819, E) — What does this characteristic produce in the inner being over time?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Four sustained effects. (1) *Thoroughgoing internalization*: Job 15:16 — "drinks injustice like water" — sustained injustice produces the condition where wrongdoing is as natural as drinking; (2) *Shamelessness*: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the conscience's shame-response is eliminated; (3) *Calamity*: Pro 22:8 — "whoever sows injustice will reap calamity; the rod of his fury will fail" — sustained injustice produces proportionate self-destruction; (4) *Exclusion from God's kingdom*: 1Cor 6:9 — "the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God" — the sustained character of injustice forecloses the eschatological inheritance.

---

#### T1.6.2 (cf=16820, E) — What states, qualities, capacities, or orientations does sustained exposure to or possession of this characteristic establish?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three established states. (1) *The unjust as fixed character-type*: Luk 16:10 — dishonest in very little and in much — a stable inner character that does not vary with scale or circumstance; (2) *God-ignorance*: Job 18:21 — "the place of him who knows not God" defines the unjust person's constitutive spiritual blindness; Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'... doing abominable iniquity" — godlessness and injustice as mutually constituting states; (3) *Standing under divine custody*: 2Pe 2:9 — "keep the unrighteous under punishment until the day of judgment" — the sustained unjust character produces sustained divine custody.

---

#### T1.6.3 (cf=16821, E) — Does the sustained effect differ from the immediate response — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes. The immediate response (victim's urgent plea — Job 6:29; Psa 43:1) is episodic. The sustained effects (water-drinking internalization — Job 15:16; shamelessness — Zep 3:5; fixed character-scale — Luk 16:10; divine custody — 2Pe 2:9) are constitutional states established through persistent injustice. The most significant shift: from the person who commits an act of injustice (Eze 18:24 — a righteous person who turns and commits injustice) to the person who IS the unjust (Luk 16:10 — the character-type whose dishonesty scales). The act-to-character trajectory is the defining sustained development of CHAR-22.

---

#### T1.7.1 (cf=16822, E) — What inner conditions or orientations enable genuine reception of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three enabling conditions. (1) *Heart-denial of God*: Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'... doing abominable iniquity" — the inner atheism of the heart produces the outer iniquity; Job 18:21 — "him who knows not God" — God-ignorance is the enabling inner condition; (2) *Self-trust rather than God-trust*: Hos 10:13 — "you have plowed iniquity; you have reaped injustice... because you have trusted in your own way and in the multitude of your warriors" — self-reliance displaces God-reliance and generates injustice; (3) *Material incentive*: 2Ch 19:7 — "partiality or taking bribes" — the external inducement that corrupts the inner orientation toward just conduct.

---

#### T1.7.2 (cf=16823, E) — What inner conditions block, distort, or prevent reception?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three blocking conditions. (1) *Fear of the Lord*: 2Ch 19:7 — "let the fear of the Lord be upon you. Be careful what you do, for there is no injustice with the Lord our God" — the fear of the Lord is the primary blocking condition that prevents judicial injustice; (2) *Active hand-withdrawal*: Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice" — the deliberate volitional restraint; (3) *Walking in God's ways*: Psa 119:3 — "who also do no wrong, but walk in his ways" — covenant faithfulness as the structural disposition that forecloses injustice.

---

#### T1.7.3 (cf=16824, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Zep 3:5 names it with the greatest diagnostic precision: "the unjust knows no shame" — the inner-being state is one of complete insensibility to the moral wrongness of their own conduct. Job 15:16 fills out the appetite-dimension: "a man who drinks injustice like water" — the person's deepest craving is for what wrongs others; the inner orientation is appetitive toward harm. Psa 53:1 names the cognitive-theological foundation: "There is no God" in the heart — the inner denial of divine accountability is the cognitive substructure of the shameless, water-drinking injustice.

---

#### T1.8.1 (cf=16825, E) — What is the primary inner-being dimension of this characteristic from the programme's dimension vocabulary?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The primary dimension is **the moral-conduct faculty oriented toward wrong in dealings with others** — the will's deployment of the capacity for just conduct in the wrong direction. Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — heart as the origin; Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice" — the will restraining the hand confirms the will as the governing faculty; Luk 16:10 — the fixed-character dishonesty confirms the dispositional-volitional level as primary.

---

#### T1.8.2 (cf=16826, E) — What evidence from the verse evidence supports this classification?

**[CHAR-22]** E — (1) Psa 58:2 — heart-devising is the named origin; (2) Eze 18:8 — hand-withholding confirms volitional control; (3) Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the will's capacity to cease confirms it governs; (4) Luk 16:10 — fixed character confirmed by scaling from small to much; (5) Eze 3:20 — "a righteous person turns from his righteousness and commits injustice" — the turn is volitional.

---

#### T1.8.3 (cf=16827, E) — Does this characteristic carry secondary dimensions — and if so, what are they, and do they compete with the primary classification?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — three secondary dimensions. (1) *Cognitive*: Job 18:21 — "him who knows not God" — injustice is paired with God-ignorance; Psa 53:1 — heart-atheism as the cognitive substructure; (2) *Speech*: Job 6:30; Isa 59:3; Zep 3:13 — the tongue as injustice's verbal outlet; (3) *Communal-social*: Lev 19:15 (judicial), Lev 19:35 (commercial), Psa 82:2 (judicial), Mic 3:10 (civic) — injustice operates across all social institutions.

---

T1 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T1.1.1–T1.1.3, T1.2.1–T1.2.3, T1.3.1–T1.3.3, T1.4.1–T1.4.3, T1.5.1–T1.5.3, T1.6.1–T1.6.3, T1.7.1–T1.7.3, T1.8.1–T1.8.3)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-22] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 19:15/35; Deu 32:4; 2Ch 19:7; Job 6:29/30/13:7/15:16/18:21/34:32; Psa 37:1/43:1/53:1/58:2/64:6/82:2/107:42/119:3; Pro 22:8/29:27; Isa 59:3; Hos 10:13; Mic 3:10; Zep 3:5/13; Eze 3:20/18:8/24/28:15; Luk 16:10/11; 1Cor 6:1/9; 1Pe 3:18; 2Pe 2:9 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Response approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T2 continues in the next response.


---

## T2 — Constitutional Location (31 prompts)

---

### T2

#### T2.1.1 (cf=16828, S) — Is this characteristic explicitly located at the spirit level in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-22]** S — The 72-verse corpus does not explicitly name the spirit (*rûaḥ/pneuma*) as the locus of injustice. The characteristic operates at the heart, hand, tongue, and conscience levels without direct spirit-level naming. This is analytically significant: unlike CHAR-20 (faithlessness), where Mal 2:15/16 explicitly calls for spirit-level guarding, CHAR-22 locates its operation at adjacent but distinct constitutional levels.

---

#### T2.1.2 (cf=16829, E) — Does the evidence indicate that this characteristic originates in or is primarily a spirit-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Partially — through the God-ignorance and heart-atheism passages that are adjacent to spirit-level. Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'" — the heart-level denial of God is what produces the injustice; Job 18:21 — "him who knows not God" — the God-ignorance that defines the unjust person is a spirit-level relational condition (not knowing God at the spirit-level of covenantal relationship). The characteristic does not originate explicitly at the spirit level but arises from a spirit-level God-relationship deficit that then manifests at the heart and conduct levels.

---

#### T2.1.3 (cf=16830, E) — What does spirit-level location reveal about the nature and depth of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The spirit-level silence (T2.1.1) combined with the God-ignorance passages (T2.1.2) reveals that CHAR-22 is primarily a heart-and-conduct characteristic rather than a spirit-level characteristic. The God-ignorance (Job 18:21; Psa 53:1) functions as the spirit-level precondition whose absence enables injustice to arise, but injustice itself is primarily characterised and located at the heart (Psa 58:2) and conduct (hands, tongue, commercial dealings) levels.

---

#### T2.1.4 (cf=16831, S) — If the evidence is silent on spirit-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** S — The spirit (*rûaḥ/pneuma*) is not explicitly named in the 72-verse corpus as the locus of injustice. Noted. The characteristic's constitutional home is primarily heart-and-conduct rather than spirit-level.

---

#### T2.10.1 (cf=16832, E) — Does this characteristic move across constitutional levels — from spirit to soul, from soul to body, or across boundaries in other directions?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — extensively. (1) *Heart → hands*: Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence on earth" — the explicit heart-to-hands sequence; Psa 7:3 — "if there is wrong in my hands" — the hands as the conduct-level expression of the inner moral state; (2) *Inner character → scaled behaviour*: Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in very little is also dishonest in much" — the inner character scales outward consistently to all behavioural contexts; (3) *Inner devising → social consequence*: Psa 64:6 — "they search out injustice... for the inward mind and heart of a man are deep" — the inward devising produces societal wrongdoing; Mic 3:10 — "build Zion with blood and Jerusalem with iniquity" — inner disposition produces civic corruption.

---

#### T2.10.2 (cf=16833, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the sequence or pattern of that movement?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Primary sequence: (1) God-ignorance at the heart-level (Psa 53:1 — "no God in heart") → (2) heart devises injustice (Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs") → (3) hands enact the devised wrong (Psa 58:2 — "your hands deal out violence on earth") → (4) social-civic corruption (Mic 3:10 — "build Zion with blood") → (5) divine judgment (Pro 22:8 — "reap calamity"; 2Pe 2:9 — kept under punishment). Secondary (reversal) sequence: Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice" (volitional restraint) → "executes true justice between man and man" (conduct-level) → "he shall surely live" (divine affirmation).

---

#### T2.10.3 (cf=16834, E) — If no constitutional movement is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Constitutional movement is evidenced (T2.10.1 and T2.10.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.2.1 (cf=16835, E) — Is this characteristic identified in the verse evidence as a soul-level phenomenon?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the whole-person character passages. Luk 16:10 — "one who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much" — the dishonesty is a character-property of the person (*anthrōpos*) at the whole-person level; 1Pe 3:18 — "the righteous for the unrighteous" — the *adikos* is named as the whole-person moral category; 2Pe 2:9 — "the unrighteous under punishment" — the whole person is held under divine custody for their injustice.

---

#### T2.2.2 (cf=16836, E) — What does soul-level location reveal about this characteristic's place in the innermost personal experience?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Soul-level location (Luk 16:10 — whole-person character; 1Pe 3:18 — person-category; 2Pe 2:9 — whole-person divine custody) reveals that injustice is not compartmentalised to specific acts but constitutes the person's moral character at the whole-person level. Luk 16:10's scaling from small to large confirms: the inner character of injustice pervades the whole-person across all behavioural contexts, making CHAR-22 a soul-level character trait rather than merely a collection of specific unjust acts.

---

#### T2.2.3 (cf=16837, E) — If the evidence is silent on soul-level location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Soul-level location is evidenced (Luk 16:10; 1Pe 3:18; 2Pe 2:9). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.3.1 (cf=16838, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the heart?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — most explicitly in Psa 58:2. "No, in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence on earth" — the heart is the explicitly named origin of the devised wrong; Psa 64:6 — "for the inward mind and heart of a man are deep" — naming the heart as the deep origin of the searched-out injustice; Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'" — the heart's atheism as the cognitive foundation of the unjust conduct.

---

#### T2.3.2 (cf=16839, E) — What does the heart-location reveal — what aspect of the heart's integrating function (knowing, willing, feeling, moral awareness) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Heart-location (Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs"; Psa 64:6 — "inward mind and heart... deep"; Psa 53:1 — heart-atheism) reveals that CHAR-22 originates in the heart's deliberating function: the heart does not passively receive injustice but actively *devises* it. The heart is the scheming centre — the same faculty that, when rightly oriented, devises justice, is here engaged in the deliberate planning of wrongs. Psa 64:6's "diligent search" confirms the deliberateness.

---

#### T2.3.3 (cf=16840, E) — If the evidence is silent on heart-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Heart-location is explicitly evidenced (Psa 53:1; 58:2; 64:6). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.4.1 (cf=16841, E) — Does the verse evidence locate this characteristic in the mind?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the devising and knowing dimensions. Psa 64:6 — "they search out injustice... for the inward mind (*lēb*) and heart of a man are deep" — the mind is paired with the heart as the deep origin of injustice-devising; Job 18:21 — "him who knows not God" — the knowledge-deficit is a cognitive-mind dimension; Isa 61:8 — "I love justice; I hate robbery and wrong" — God's mind's judgments about wrong define what injustice is.

---

#### T2.4.2 (cf=16842, E) — What does mind-location reveal — what aspect of the mind's function (thought, discernment, understanding) does this characteristic engage?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Mind-location (Psa 64:6 — inward mind devising; Job 18:21 — not knowing God) reveals that injustice operates at the cognitive level through two mechanisms: (1) *active deliberation toward wrong* (Psa 64:6 — "diligent search" for injustice — the mind's analytical capacity deployed toward planning wrong); (2) *God-knowledge deficit* (Job 18:21 — "knows not God") — the mind's failure to know God produces the characteristic's enabling condition.

---

#### T2.4.3 (cf=16843, E) — If the evidence is silent on mind-location, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Mind-level location (deliberation — Psa 64:6; God-knowledge deficit — Job 18:21) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.5.1 (cf=16844, E) — Does the verse evidence surface any soul-level location beyond heart and mind for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — *Will* is the primary operative faculty. Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice" — the will's active restraint; Eze 3:20 — "a righteous person turns from his righteousness and commits injustice" — the volitional turn; Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the will's capacity to cease. *Conscience* is engaged through the shame-dimension: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the conscience's shame-response is the relevant faculty, absent in the unjust; Job 6:29 — "let no injustice be done" — appeal to the conscience of Job's interlocutors.

---

#### T2.5.2 (cf=16845, E) — If so, what is that location, and what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Will-level location (Eze 18:8 — withholding; Eze 3:20 — turning; Job 34:32 — will-to-cease) reveals CHAR-22 as a will-governed characteristic: the will that chooses to withhold its hand from injustice (Eze 18:8) is the same will that, in the unjust person, deploys the hand in wrong. Conscience-level absence (Zep 3:5 — "knows no shame") is CHAR-22's most distinctive inner-being feature: the shamelessness diagnostic names the conscience's complete non-functioning as the marker of the thoroughly constituted unjust person.

---

#### T2.5.3 (cf=16846, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Will-level and conscience-level locations are evidenced (Eze 18:8; 3:20; Job 34:32; Zep 3:5). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.6.1 (cf=16847, E) — Does the verse evidence link this characteristic to a specific body part?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — three body-part links. (1) *Hands*: Psa 58:2 — "your hands deal out violence on earth"; Psa 7:3 — "if there is wrong in my hands"; Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice"; (2) *Tongue/lips/mouth*: Isa 59:3 — "your tongue mutters wickedness"; Job 6:30 — "is there any injustice on my tongue?"; Zep 3:13 — "speak no lies"; Mal 2:6 — "no wrong was found on his lips"; (3) *Fingers*: Isa 59:3 — "your hands are defiled with blood and your fingers with iniquity."

---

#### T2.6.2 (cf=16848, E) — If so, what is Scripture doing by making that link — is it emphatic, functional, expressive, indicative, or mediating?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three distinct body-part functions. (1) *Hands as injustice's primary enacting organs* (Psa 58:2; Psa 7:3; Eze 18:8): the hands are the body-parts through which the heart's devised injustice is enacted in the world; "withholds his hand from injustice" (Eze 18:8) confirms the hand as the conduct-level instrument the will either deploys or restrains; (2) *Tongue/lips as injustice's verbal instrument* (Isa 59:3; Job 6:30; Zep 3:13): the tongue expresses inward injustice verbally — "mutters wickedness" (Isa 59:3); (3) *Fingers as injustice's detailed-action organs* (Isa 59:3): the fingers represent the precision instruments of the wrongdoing — "your fingers with iniquity" naming the detailed physical execution of the unjust act.

---

#### T2.6.3 (cf=16849, E) — If no body-part link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Body-part links (hands — multiple; tongue/lips — multiple; fingers — Isa 59:3) are extensively evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.7.1 (cf=16850, E) — Where a body-characteristic link exists, which direction does it run — does the soul express through the body, does the body feed back to the soul, or does it run in both directions?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Primarily *inner-to-outer*. Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence" — the explicit sequence is heart → hands; the inner devising produces the outer enacting. Isa 59:3 — "your hands are defiled... your tongue mutters wickedness" — the defilement of hands and tongue is the outward expression of the inner moral corruption. The will's restraint (Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand") confirms the inner-to-outer direction: the will governs what the hand does. No verse clearly evidences the reverse direction (body-to-inner-corruption) for CHAR-22.

---

#### T2.7.2 (cf=16851, E) — What is the consequence of that directionality for understanding the characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The inner-to-outer directionality confirms that CHAR-22's body-engagement is expressive rather than generative: the hand and tongue *express* the inner injustice; they do not *create* it. The heart devises (inner); the hands enact (outer). This means addressing CHAR-22 requires addressing the inner origin (the heart — Psa 58:2) rather than merely restraining the hand's expression (though Eze 18:8's hand-withholding is the immediate conduct-level remedy). The inner-to-outer direction makes CHAR-22's remedy fundamentally a heart-transformation rather than a conduct-correction.

---

#### T2.7.3 (cf=16852, E) — If no body-characteristic link is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Body-characteristic link (hands, tongue, fingers) is evidenced and primarily inner-to-outer. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T2.8.1 (cf=16853, E) — Does sustained operation of this characteristic leave a constitutional deposit in the body or its design — including DNA or generational consequence?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — three deposit forms. (1) *Divine memory-record*: Rev 18:5 — "her sins are heaped high as heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities" — God's memory holds the accumulating record of injustice as a deposit; (2) *Fixed character deposit*: Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in very little is also dishonest in much" — the injustice is deposited as a stable character-trait that does not vary with scale; (3) *Shameless-conscience deposit*: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the sustained injustice deposits a conscience that is permanently insensible to its own wrongdoing.

---

#### T2.8.2 (cf=16854, E) — What evidence supports or contradicts this?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Supporting: Rev 18:5 (divine memory-deposit); Luk 16:10 (fixed character); Zep 3:5 (shameless conscience). Contradicting or complicating: Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the deposit is addressable through volitional repudiation; Eze 33:15 — "the wicked restores the pledge... not doing injustice, he shall surely live" — even the wicked person's injustice-deposit can be reversed through restoration and volitional change. The fixed-character deposit (Luk 16:10) is the most resistant — consistent dishonesty at all scales — but even this is not declared eschatologically irreversible until judgment.

---

#### T2.8.3 (cf=16855, E) — If the evidence is silent, note this explicitly. This finding feeds directly into T5.7.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Deposit evidence is present: divine memory (Rev 18:5), fixed character (Luk 16:10), shameless conscience (Zep 3:5). T5.7 will carry: the divine memory deposit (Rev 18:5) develops toward eschatological judgment; the fixed character deposit (Luk 16:10) develops toward either volitional reversal or eschatological exclusion from the kingdom (1Cor 6:9); the shameless conscience deposit (Zep 3:5) is the most constitutionally embedded and the hardest to reverse.

---

#### T2.9.1 (cf=16856, E) — Where does this characteristic originate constitutionally — is it generated from within the person, received from outside, bestowed by God, or carried generationally?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Two constitutional origins. (1) *Heart-level God-denial and devising*: Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'" — the heart's atheism as origin; Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — the heart's deliberating function as origin; (2) *Self-trust displacing God-trust*: Hos 10:13 — "you have plowed iniquity... because you have trusted in your own way" — the misplaced self-reliance as the originating disposition.

---

#### T2.9.2 (cf=16857, E) — What does the evidence reveal about whether the origin is singular or multiple?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Multiple across the three VCG registers. Heart-devising (VCG-02 — Psa 58:2/64:6); God-denial (VCG-01/03 — Psa 53:1; Job 18:21); self-trust (VCG-02 — Hos 10:13); material inducement (VCG-01 — 2Ch 19:7 — bribery); emergence from within a blameless being (Eze 28:15 — the cherub's internal emergence). CHAR-22 has diverse origins but all share the will-level as the constitutional location of the departure from right conduct.

---

#### T2.9.3 (cf=16858, E) — Does the origin of this characteristic change across different contexts evidenced in Scripture?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes across three VCG contexts. (1) *VCG-01 (judicial/commercial)*: origin in bribery-corruption of the discernment faculty (2Ch 19:7) and in the heart's devising of partial judgment (Psa 82:2 — "show partiality to the wicked"); (2) *VCG-02 (oppression and harm)*: origin in the heart's devising of violence (Psa 58:2) and self-trust (Hos 10:13); (3) *VCG-03 (character of the unjust)*: origin in the constitutional character-level — the person who IS unjust at every scale (Luk 16:10); the God-ignorance that defines the character-type (Job 18:21).

---

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T3.


---

## T3 — Inner Faculties (33 prompts)

---

### T3

#### T3.1.1 (cf=16859, E) — Does this characteristic engage the perceptive faculty — the inner senses including hearing, sight, taste, touch, smell, and spiritual discernment — and if so, which inner sense and how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The perceptive faculty is engaged through the God-perception deficit and the moral-perception blindness. Job 18:21 — "him who knows not God" — the unjust person lacks the perceptive recognition of God that would anchor just conduct; Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'" — the inner perceptive faculty fails to perceive God; Job 6:30 — "cannot my palate discern the cause of calamity?" — the moral perceptive faculty (palate as discernment metaphor) is engaged by the one seeking to avoid injustice; Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the conscience's perceptive-shame-response is absent.

---

#### T3.1.2 (cf=16860, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair the perceptive faculty in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *impairs* the moral perceptive faculty in two directions. (1) *God-perception impaired*: Psa 53:1 — the heart's "no God" assertion impairs the fundamental theological perception; Job 18:21 — not knowing God is the perceptive deficit; (2) *Shame-perception eliminated*: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the conscience's perceptive-shame-response is absent in the established unjust person. The perceptive impairment is progressive: at the beginning the person may still perceive injustice (Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more") but sustained injustice eliminates the shame-perception (Zep 3:5).

---

#### T3.1.3 (cf=16861, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with perception reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The perceptive pattern reveals that Zep 3:5's "the unjust knows no shame" is CHAR-22's most diagnostically significant inner-being statement: the absence of shame-perception is the marker of fully constituted injustice. The perceptive trajectory runs: God-perception diminished (Psa 53:1 — heart-atheism) → moral shame-perception eliminated (Zep 3:5). The restoration path requires the inverse: God-fear reactivated (2Ch 19:7 — "let the fear of the Lord be upon you") restoring the moral perceptive faculty.

---

#### T3.10.1 (cf=16886, E) — Does this characteristic engage conscientiousness — the integrated response of moral awareness, volition, and action — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — but misdirected. Psa 64:6 — "they search out injustice, saying, 'We have accomplished a diligent search'" — the self-congratulatory conscientiousness deployed toward finding injustice-opportunities; Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — the deliberate, conscientious planning of wrong. The "diligent search" of Psa 64:6 names a thoroughgoing conscientiousness directed toward wrongdoing.

---

#### T3.10.2 (cf=16887, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscientiousness in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *misdirects* conscientiousness toward wrong-doing rather than eliminating it. Psa 64:6's "diligent search" shows conscientiousness fully engaged but aimed at injustice. The self-congratulatory "we have accomplished a diligent search" shows the conscientiousness is intact and even proud of its achievement — the moral problem is the direction, not the capacity.

---

#### T3.10.3 (cf=16888, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscientiousness reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The conscientiousness pattern reveals that CHAR-22 is not a character of lazy moral failure but of active moral misdirection. Psa 64:6's "diligent search for injustice" is the hallmark of conscientiousness-in-wrong. The unjust person is not morally passive but morally active — the conscientiousness that could serve justice is deployed toward planning and executing wrong. This active, diligent conscientiousness toward injustice is CHAR-22's most analytically distinctive agency-feature.

---

#### T3.11.1 (cf=16889, E) — Does this characteristic engage the relational capacity — the constitutional equipment for genuine connection with another person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The relational capacity is constitutively engaged — injustice is always done *toward* persons. (1) *Against persons directly*: Job 6:29 — "let no injustice be done" to Job — the relational victim-dimension; Psa 43:1 — "from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me" — the unjust as relational predator; (2) *Against social-relational structures*: Lev 19:15 — "you shall do no injustice in court" — the judicial relationship; Mic 3:10 — "build Zion with blood" — the civic-relational structure corrupted; (3) *Against the God-relationship*: Jer 2:5 — "what wrong did your fathers find in me that they went far from me?" — the relationship with God as the relational frame against which injustice operates.

---

#### T3.11.2 (cf=16890, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair relational capacity in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *destroys* relational capacity from the side of the victim and *corrupts* it from the side of the agent. Lam 1:2 (CHAR-20 adjacent) — those who deal treacherously become enemies; the unjust agent's relational capacity is perverted into harm-relationship. The victim's relational experience is violated — Psa 43:1 — "deliver me from the deceitful and unjust man." The righteous avoid the unjust as an unsuitable relational partner (1Cor 6:1 — don't take grievances before the unrighteous) and find them abominable (Pro 29:27).

---

#### T3.11.3 (cf=16891, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with relational capacity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The relational pattern reveals CHAR-22 as M10's most structurally other-directed characteristic: injustice cannot exist without a victim. Every verse in the corpus either describes someone harmed by injustice or God defined as without it. The characteristic is defined by its relational direction (toward persons in wrong) — unlike CHAR-21 (perversion as inner structural distortion) which primarily characterises the inner faculty, CHAR-22 is defined by the relational harm it inflicts on others.

---
