  2. Scope marker:       33× [CHAR-22] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 19:15; 2Ch 19:7; Job 6:29/15:16/18:21/29:17/34:32; Psa 7:3/37:1/43:1/53:1/58:2/64:6/107:42; Pro 22:8/29:27; Isa 61:8; Jer 2:5; Hos 10:13; Mic 3:10; Hab 2:12; Zep 3:5; Eze 3:20/18:8/33:15; Luk 16:10; 1Cor 6:1/9; 2Pe 2:9; Rev 18:5 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Response approaching ceiling. Pausing here; Tier T4 continues in the next response.


---

## T4 — Relational Interface (24 prompts)

---

#### T3.2.1 (cf=16862, E) — Does this characteristic engage the cognitive faculty — knowing, understanding, discerning — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through deliberation and God-knowledge. Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — the cognitive devising faculty; Psa 64:6 — "they search out injustice, saying, 'We have accomplished a diligent search.' For the inward mind and heart of a man are deep" — cognitive deliberation toward injustice; Job 18:21 — "him who knows not God" — the God-knowledge deficit as cognitive failure; Psa 53:1 — the cognitive proposition "there is no God" asserted in the heart.

---

#### T3.2.2 (cf=16863, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair cognition in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *misdirects and impairs* cognition. Psa 64:6's "diligent search" for injustice shows cognition misdirected — the analytical capacity deployed toward planning wrong rather than right. Psa 53:1's heart-atheism shows cognition impaired at the foundational level: the mind that denies God cannot accurately perceive the moral order; Job 18:21 — the unjust person "knows not God" — the cognitive God-knowledge deficit produces the inner condition that enables injustice.

---

#### T3.2.3 (cf=16864, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with cognition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The cognitive pattern reveals two levels: (1) *Surface-level misdirection* — the mind is fully deployed but toward wrong ends (Psa 64:6 — diligent search for injustice); (2) *Foundation-level deficit* — the God-knowledge that would anchor just cognition is absent (Job 18:21). The most analytically significant finding: Psa 64:6 — "for the inward mind and heart of a man are deep" — Scripture itself acknowledges the cognitive depth from which injustice's devising emerges; the deep mind is the site of injustice's planning.

---

#### T3.3.1 (cf=16865, E) — Does this characteristic engage the memory faculty — the holding and retrieving of inner-being reality across time — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through divine memory and communal warning-memory. (1) *Divine memory*: Rev 18:5 — "God has remembered her iniquities" — God's memory retains the accumulating record of injustice; (2) *Memory of God's justice*: Psa 107:42 — "the upright see it and are glad, and all wickedness shuts its mouth" — the righteous remember God's just acts which silence wickedness; (3) *Communal warning-memory*: 2Sa 7:10 — "violent men shall afflict them no more, as formerly" — the memory of past injustice informs the promise.

---

#### T3.3.2 (cf=16866, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair memory in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *suppresses* the memory of God's justice that would check wrong conduct. Psa 53:1 — "there is no God" in the heart — the denial of God is simultaneously a suppression of the memory of God's moral authority; Hos 10:13 — "trusted in your own way" — self-reliance suppresses the memory of God's justice. The restorative act of God displaying his justice (Psa 107:42 — "the upright see it and are glad") reactivates the memory that silences wickedness.

---

#### T3.3.3 (cf=16867, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with memory reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The memory pattern reveals that injustice is sustained by suppressing the memory of God's justice-standard. The unjust person's cognitive subsystem operates with God's justice-memory blocked: their "diligent search for injustice" (Psa 64:6) replaces what the God-fearing person's memory of divine justice would naturally prevent. The reversal requires reactivating divine-justice memory (2Ch 19:7 — "let the fear of the Lord be upon you... there is no injustice with the Lord our God").

---

#### T3.4.1 (cf=16868, E) — Does this characteristic engage the affective faculty — feeling and emotional experience — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the God-hatred of injustice and the human affective responses. (1) *Divine affect*: Isa 61:8 — "I the Lord love justice; I hate robbery and wrong" — God's love and hatred as the defining affective standard; (2) *Righteous person's fretful response*: Psa 37:1 — "fret not yourself because of evildoers; be not envious of wrongdoers" — the inner fretfulness and envy that injustice's apparent prosperity evokes in the righteous; (3) *Victim's pleading response*: Job 6:29 — "let no injustice be done... my vindication is at stake" — the urgency of the plea; Psa 43:1 — "deliver me from the deceitful and unjust man" — the affect of urgent appeal.

---

#### T3.4.2 (cf=16869, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair affect in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *eliminates* the appropriate shame-affect in the unjust person (Zep 3:5 — "knows no shame") and *provokes* distress-affect in the righteous (Psa 37:1 — fretfulness; Psa 43:1 — urgent appeal). The double affective pattern: the unjust person feels nothing (no shame); the righteous person feels the fretful impact of witnessing injustice going apparently unpunished. The absence of affect in the unjust and its presence in the righteous-witness is CHAR-22's most distinctive affective feature.

---

#### T3.4.3 (cf=16870, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with affect reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The affective pattern reveals that Zep 3:5's shamelessness diagnostic names the affective elimination that constitutes the fully formed unjust character. The unjust person who "drinks injustice like water" (Job 15:16) has eliminated the disgust-affect that should naturally accompany wrongdoing. The righteous observer's fretfulness (Psa 37:1) is itself named as a danger to be resisted — the psalmist's counsel against fretful envy toward wrongdoers shows that the righteous person's affective response to injustice must also be governed.

---

#### T3.5.1 (cf=16871, E) — Does this characteristic engage the creative faculty — imagination and the capacity to originate — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the devising and searching dimensions. Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — devising is a creative-cognitive act; Psa 64:6 — "they search out injustice, saying, 'We have accomplished a diligent search'" — the analytical-creative effort applied to finding opportunities for injustice; Hab 2:12 — "woe to him who builds a town with blood and founds a city on iniquity" — the creative-constructive faculty deployed toward building civic structures on injustice.

---

#### T3.5.2 (cf=16872, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair creativity in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *misdirects* the creative faculty toward unjust ends. Psa 58:2's "devise wrongs" and Psa 64:6's "diligent search for injustice" show the creative-analytical capacity engaged toward planning wrong. Hab 2:12's city-building on iniquity shows the constructive faculty deployed toward civic injustice. The creative capacity is not eliminated but redirected from creative right-order building to creative wrong-order building.

---

#### T3.5.3 (cf=16873, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with creativity reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The creative pattern confirms Psa 58:2's characterisation: injustice is not a passive failure but an active devising — the heart creatively plans wrong before the hands enact it. Psa 64:6's "diligent search" for injustice names the systematic, analytical effort the unjust person invests in finding opportunities. This active-creative wrongdoing dimension distinguishes CHAR-22 from accidental or negligent wrong: the unjust person is a creative agent of planned wrongdoing.

---

#### T3.6.1 (cf=16874, E) — Does this characteristic engage the volitional faculty — the capacity to choose — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The will is the primary governing faculty of CHAR-22. (1) *Will enacting injustice*: Eze 3:20 — "a righteous person turns from his righteousness and commits injustice" — the volitional turn; Psa 58:2 — devising and dealing out violence — the will committing and executing; (2) *Will restraining from injustice*: Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice" — the will's active restraint; (3) *Will choosing to cease*: Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the volitional commitment to stop; Eze 33:15 — "walks in the statutes of life, not doing injustice" — the will walking away.

---

#### T3.6.2 (cf=16875, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair volition in the person — including its three aspects: capacity, interaction with other characteristics, and the constraints under which it operates?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Sustained injustice *progressively captures* the will. The initial volitional choice to commit injustice (Eze 3:20 — "turns and commits") develops toward the fixed-character dishonesty of Luk 16:10 — consistent across small and large — where the will's choices are no longer deliberated afresh but flow from the constituted unjust character. The will that could have withheld (Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand") becomes the will that consistently enacts wrong at every scale. The remedy calls the will back to volitional choice: Job 34:32 — "I will do it no more."

---

#### T3.6.3 (cf=16876, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with volition reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The volitional pattern reveals CHAR-22's distinctive trajectory: from volitional act (Eze 3:20 — commits injustice) through fixed character-scale (Luk 16:10 — dishonest in all) to shameless insensibility (Zep 3:5 — knows no shame). The will begins as the agent of injustice and progressively becomes the captive of the unjust character it has formed. The reversibility remains open until judgment (Eze 33:15 — the wicked can still stop doing injustice and live) but grows more constitutionally embedded with each sustained choice.

---

#### T3.7.1 (cf=16877, E) — Does this characteristic engage the agency faculty — the capacity to act, initiate, and make happen — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Agency is central — CHAR-22 is defined by what is *done* toward others. (1) *Active agentive wrongdoing*: Psa 58:2 — "your hands deal out violence on earth"; Rev 19:2 (adjacent) — corrupting the earth; Rev 18:5 — crimes heaped high; (2) *Agentive harm*: Job 29:17 — "I broke the fangs of the unrighteous" — the unjust person as a predatory agent; 2Sa 7:10 — "violent men shall afflict them no more" — the violent as active agents of harm; (3) *Agentive civic injustice*: Mic 3:10 — "who build Zion with blood" — agency deployed toward constructing unjust social structures.

---

#### T3.7.2 (cf=16878, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair agency in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *constitutes* a stable agentive identity. Luk 16:10 — consistent dishonesty across all scales — the agency is consistently deployed toward wrong; Job 15:16 — drinking injustice like water — the agency operates with wrongdoing as its natural medium. Pro 22:8 — "whoever sows injustice will reap calamity, and the rod of his fury will fail" — the agentive power ("rod of his fury") is itself disabled by the injustice's consequence. Agency in CHAR-22 is not impaired but is constitutively directed toward wrongdoing.

---

#### T3.7.3 (cf=16879, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with agency reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The agency pattern reveals that CHAR-22 is M10's most agentively other-directed characteristic: every expression of injustice requires a victim and an agent. Job 29:17 — "I broke the fangs of the unrighteous" — the metaphor names the unjust as a predatory agent; Psa 58:2 — "your hands deal out violence on earth" — the active distribution of violence across the earth. CHAR-22's agent is not merely a person who does wrong but a person who systematically does wrong *toward* others — its defining agentive orientation is harm-toward-persons.

---

#### T3.8.1 (cf=16880, E) — Does this characteristic engage the moral evaluation faculty — the capacity to assess against a standard of right, wrong, good, and true — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the abomination judgments and the comparative moral-evaluation passages. Deu 25:16 — "all who act dishonestly are an abomination to the Lord your God" — God's moral evaluation as the standard; Pro 29:27 — "an unjust man is an abomination to the righteous" — the righteous person's moral evaluation generating disgust; 1Cor 6:9 — "the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God" — the eschatological moral evaluation.

---

#### T3.8.2 (cf=16881, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair moral evaluation in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice *inverts* the self-directed moral evaluation faculty while the God-centred moral evaluation remains intact in others. Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — self-directed moral evaluation eliminated; Pro 29:27 — "an unjust man is an abomination to the righteous" — the righteous person's moral evaluation of the unjust is acute. The unjust person cannot accurately evaluate their own injustice (shame absent) while the righteous observer evaluates it precisely (abomination).

---

#### T3.8.3 (cf=16882, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with moral evaluation reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The moral evaluation pattern reveals that Pro 29:27's "abomination to the righteous" is CHAR-22's most striking moral-evaluation statement: injustice evokes the same disgust-response in the righteous as idolatry evokes before God. This abomination-response to injustice confirms that CHAR-22 is not a minor character-failing but a fundamental violation of the moral order that the righteous person's moral evaluation faculty correctly identifies as morally repugnant.

---

#### T3.9.1 (cf=16883, E) — Does this characteristic engage the conscience — the acute inner witness of sin, guilt, and conviction — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the shame-diagnostic and the confession passages. (1) *Conscience absent in the unjust*: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the conscience's shame-function is inoperative; (2) *Conscience operational in the just*: Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the conscience-driven commitment to cease; Job 6:29 — "let no injustice be done" — the conscience's appeal against unjust treatment; (3) *Conscience seeking vindication*: Psa 7:3 — "if there is wrong in my hands" — the conscience's honest self-examination.

---

#### T3.9.2 (cf=16884, E) — Does this characteristic enable, deepen, bypass, or impair conscience in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Sustained injustice *eliminates* the conscience's shame-function. The trajectory: (1) early-stage conscience still operational — Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" (conscience still responds); (2) advanced-stage conscience bypassed — Job 15:16 — "drinks injustice like water" (injustice is natural, no resistance); (3) full elimination — Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" (conscience fully non-operational). The shamelessness diagnostic marks the conscience's complete elimination as the end-stage of sustained injustice.

---

#### T3.9.3 (cf=16885, E) — What does the pattern of engagement or non-engagement with conscience reveal about the nature of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The conscience pattern reveals that Zep 3:5's "the unjust knows no shame" is the defining diagnostic of CHAR-22's most severe inner-being state — the conscience is completely insensible. This shamelessness is the inverse of the righteous person's shame-capacity: the righteous person's conscience responds to wrong with shame (enabling correction); the unjust person's conscience produces no response (foreclosing self-correction). CHAR-22's shamelessness is its most analytically distinctive inner-being marker.

---

### T4

#### T4.1.1 (cf=16892, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating from God toward the human person — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — God operates toward the injustice-bearing person in five modes. (1) *Defining his own character as without injustice*: Deu 32:4; 2Ch 19:7; Psa 92:15; Job 34:10; Zep 3:5 — God's character is the standard against which injustice is measured; (2) *Hating injustice*: Isa 61:8 — "I hate robbery and wrong" — God's moral opposition is expressed as hatred; (3) *Commanding against injustice*: Lev 19:15/35 — "you shall do no injustice" — direct divine command; 2Ch 19:7 — "let the fear of the Lord be upon you"; (4) *Judging injustice*: 2Pe 2:9 — "keep the unrighteous under punishment until the day of judgment"; Rev 18:5 — "God has remembered her iniquities"; (5) *Rewarding faithfulness against injustice*: Heb 6:10 — "God is not unjust so as to overlook your work and the love that you have shown."

---

#### T4.1.2 (cf=16893, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the basis on which God extends this characteristic — is it conditional, unconditional, covenantal, or responsive?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Two bases. (1) *God's intrinsic righteousness*: Deu 32:4 — "without iniquity, just and upright is he" — God's own character as the basis; Zep 3:5 — "the Lord... is righteous; he does no injustice; every morning he shows forth his justice" — God's consistent daily justice is the basis; (2) *God's love of justice*: Isa 61:8 — "I the Lord love justice" — the affective commitment to justice as the basis for hating its opposite; Heb 6:10 — "God is not unjust" — God's own justice-character prevents overlooking faithful service.

---

#### T4.1.3 (cf=16894, E) — What does God's extension of this characteristic reveal about his disposition toward the human person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — God's disposition is one of *righteous opposition to injustice combined with sustained moral patience toward the unjust person*. 2Pe 2:9 — "keep the unrighteous under punishment until the day of judgment" — not immediate destruction but sustained custody; Rev 18:5 — God "remembers" (retains in mind rather than acting immediately) — patience before final reckoning. Eze 33:15 — the wicked who stops doing injustice "shall surely live" — God's disposition sustains the possibility of reversal. The eschatological judgment (Rev 18:5; 2Pe 2:9) is deferred precisely because God's disposition holds open the path to repentance while maintaining the certainty of final accountability.

---

#### T4.1.4 (cf=16895, E) — If the evidence is silent on God-to-human operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — God-to-human operation (defining standard, hating, commanding, judging, rewarding) is extensively evidenced across all VCGs. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.2.1 (cf=16896, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in the human person's movement toward God — in seeking, supplication, worship, or covenant — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through the confession-and-cessation movement. Job 34:32 — "teach me what I do not see; if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the movement toward God is through acknowledgment and volitional commitment to cease; Eze 33:15 — "the wicked restores the pledge... not doing injustice, he shall surely live" — the movement toward God through restitution and conduct-change; 2Sa 3:34 — "as one falls before the wicked you have fallen" — the lament implies the expectation of justice from God for unjust death.

---

#### T4.2.2 (cf=16897, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the inner posture required for this movement?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three required inner postures. (1) *Volitional commitment to cease*: Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the will's commitment to stop; (2) *Active restitution*: Eze 33:15 — "restores the pledge, gives back what he has taken by robbery" — the movement toward God under injustice requires concrete material restitution, not merely inner intention; (3) *Hand-withholding*: Eze 18:8 — "withholds his hand from injustice, executes true justice between man and man" — the active volitional restraint paired with positive just conduct.

---

#### T4.2.3 (cf=16898, E) — What does the human-to-God direction of this characteristic reveal about the person's relationship with God?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The human-to-God direction reveals that the relationship remains accessible through cessation and restitution. Eze 33:15 — "he shall surely live; he shall not die" — the full relational restoration following cessation of injustice; Job 34:32 — the movement is through acknowledging unseen injustice and committing to stop. The relationship is not terminated by injustice but held under the condition of God's patient custody (2Pe 2:9) awaiting either repentance or final judgment.

---

#### T4.2.4 (cf=16899, E) — If the evidence is silent on human-to-God operation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Human-to-God operation (volitional commitment to cease, restitution, hand-withholding) is evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.3.1 (cf=16900, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being extended by one person toward another — and if so, how does it operate in that giving?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — as the characteristic's defining mode. (1) *Judicial extension*: Psa 82:2 — "how long will you judge unjustly and show partiality to the wicked?" — judges extending injustice to those who appear before them; (2) *Violent extension*: 2Sa 7:10 — "violent men shall afflict them" — the violent extending harm; Psa 58:2 — "your hands deal out violence on earth" — distributing injustice outward; (3) *Commercial extension*: Lev 19:35 — "do no wrong in judgment, in measures of length or weight or quantity" — commercial relationships as the context for extending injustice to counterparties; (4) *Civic-structural extension*: Mic 3:10 — "who build Zion with blood" — leaders extending injustice to the whole city through structural wrongdoing.

---

#### T4.3.2 (cf=16901, E) — What inner conditions or orientations in the giver enable genuine extension of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three enabling conditions. (1) *Authority or positional access*: Psa 82:2 — judges who have positional authority to determine others' outcomes; Lev 19:35 — the seller who controls the weights and measures; Mic 3:10 — leaders who direct civic construction; (2) *Appetitive internalization*: Job 15:16 — "drinks injustice like water" — the thoroughly internalized appetite enables effortless extension to others; (3) *God-ignorance*: Job 18:21 — "him who knows not God" — the absence of divine accountability in the inner life removes the restraint that would prevent extending injustice to others.

---

#### T4.3.3 (cf=16902, E) — What does the evidence reveal about what the person must have received or become before they can genuinely give this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The extending person must have developed the unjust character-trait (Luk 16:10 — dishonest across all scales) and suppressed the shame-response (Zep 3:5 — knows no shame) before they can consistently extend injustice to others. For the judicial context (Psa 82:2), they must also have received a bribe or partiality-inducement (2Ch 19:7 — "partiality or taking bribes") that has corrupted the inner judgment-faculty before extending unjust judgments to those before them.

---

#### T4.3.4 (cf=16903, E) — If the evidence is silent on the giving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — The giving/extension direction is the characteristic's primary mode — evidenced through judicial (Psa 82:2), violent (2Sa 7:10; Psa 58:2), commercial (Lev 19:35), and civic-structural (Mic 3:10) contexts. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.4.1 (cf=16904, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic being received by a person from another — and if so, how does it operate in that reception?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — extensively as victim-experience. (1) *Personal receipt*: Job 6:29 — "let no injustice be done" — Job as the intended recipient; Psa 43:1 — "from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me" — the psalmist as the one receiving unjust oppression; (2) *Communal receipt*: 2Sa 7:10 — "violent men shall afflict them" — Israel as collective recipient; Psa 125:3 — "the scepter of wickedness shall not rest on the land allotted to the righteous, lest the righteous stretch out their hands to do wrong" — the righteous as recipients of the pressure of injustice's rule; (3) *Cosmic receipt*: Rev 18:5 — the whole earth as the recipient of Babylon's accumulated injustice.

---

#### T4.4.2 (cf=16905, E) — What inner conditions enable or block reception of this characteristic from another person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Reception-enabling (for the unjust person): (1) *Openness to seeing unseen iniquity*: Job 34:32 — "teach me what I do not see" — the posture of openness to correction; (2) *Volitional commitment to cease*: Job 34:32 — "I will do it no more"; Eze 33:15 — not doing injustice. Reception-blocking: (1) *Shamelessness*: Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame" — the insensibility to wrong forecloses remedy-reception; (2) *God-denial*: Psa 53:1 — "there is no God" in the heart — forecloses the fear-of-God correction (2Ch 19:7); (3) *Self-trust*: Hos 10:13 — "trusted in your own way" — forecloses God-dependence.

---

#### T4.4.3 (cf=16906, E) — What is the inner-being state of the person who encounters this characteristic from another but does not receive it?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three states evidenced. (1) *Living rather than dying*: Eze 33:15 — "he shall surely live; he shall not die" — the cessation of injustice restores the life-condition; Eze 18:26/33:18 — the death that doing injustice would have brought is averted; (2) *Restored integrity*: Zep 3:13 — "those who are left in Israel... shall do no injustice and speak no lies" — the purified remnant's restored integrity; (3) *Trustworthy stewardship*: Luk 16:11 — the inverse implies that faithfulness with unrighteous wealth qualifies for true riches — cessation of injustice restores the capacity to be trusted.

---

#### T4.4.4 (cf=16907, E) — If the evidence is silent on the receiving direction, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — The receiving direction (victim-experience) is the characteristic's secondary mode alongside the extending direction — evidenced through Job (6:29), psalmist (43:1), Israel (2Sa 7:10), and cosmic (Rev 18:5) contexts. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.5.1 (cf=16908, E) — Does the evidence indicate whether this characteristic operates differently within existing relational bonds versus across relational distance or difference?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes. Within intimate bonds: Jer 2:5 — "what wrong did your fathers find in me that they went far from me?" — God's rhetorical question to Israel as his covenant-people; the closest relational bond (covenant-parent/child) makes the "no injustice" claim most morally weighty. Within judicial bonds: Psa 82:2 — judges who should protect those before them; the positional bond intensifies the injustice. Across relational distance: the nations in Amos 1–2 (CHAR-19 adjacent) commit cross-boundary injustice; Rev 18:5 — Babylon's corruption reaching the whole earth across all relational distances.

---

#### T4.5.2 (cf=16909, E) — Does this characteristic operate within covenantal contexts only, or does it cross covenantal boundaries?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The characteristic crosses all boundaries. VCG-01's universal Levitical commands (Lev 19:15/35) apply to all Israel regardless of specific relational context; Deu 25:16 — "all who act dishonestly are an abomination" — universal condemnation; Mat 5:45 — "he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust" — God sustains even the unjust regardless of covenant membership; Rev 18:5 — Babylon's accumulated injustice crosses all covenantal and non-covenantal contexts.

---

#### T4.5.3 (cf=16910, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the relational scope of this characteristic — who is included and who is not?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The relational scope is comprehensive: divine (Jer 2:5 — what wrong in God?), judicial-positional (Psa 82:2), commercial (Lev 19:35), interpersonal-violent (2Sa 7:10; Psa 58:2), civic-structural (Mic 3:10), and cosmic (Rev 18:5; Rom 3:5). CHAR-22 is M10's most institutionally comprehensive characteristic: injustice operates across every human institution (judicial, commercial, civic, political) and every relational scale (individual, community, nation, cosmic).

---

#### T4.5.4 (cf=16911, E) — If the evidence is silent on relational boundaries, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Relational scope (divine, judicial, commercial, interpersonal, civic, cosmic) is extensively evidenced. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T4.6.1 (cf=16912, S) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to other spiritual beings — angelic or adversarial — and if so, how?

**[CHAR-22]** S — The 72-verse corpus does not explicitly name angelic or adversarial spiritual beings in connection with injustice as moral failure of right conduct. The characteristic operates within the direct human-God and human-human relational framework without named spiritual-being mediation.

---

#### T4.6.2 (cf=16913, E) — Is this characteristic a site of adversarial activity — something that can be attacked, distorted, or weaponised by adversarial spiritual powers?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Partially — through Eze 28:15/18's cherub passage. "You were blameless in your ways from the day you were created, till unrighteousness was found in you... by the multitude of your iniquities, in the unrighteousness of your trade you profaned your sanctuaries" — the cherub's injustice (unrighteousness found within a created being) is the closest the corpus comes to naming a spiritual being engaged in CHAR-22. The injustice emerges from within the cherub rather than from an external adversarial agent.

---

#### T4.6.3 (cf=16914, S) — Is this characteristic communicated, strengthened, or mediated through angelic ministry in the evidence?

**[CHAR-22]** S — No angelic ministry is named in connection with injustice in the 72-verse corpus. The characteristic operates as a direct human moral-conduct failure without angelic mediation.

---

#### T4.6.4 (cf=16915, E) — If the evidence is silent on the spiritual beings interface, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Adversarial spiritual beings are absent from the corpus (T4.6.1). Angelic ministry (T4.6.3) is absent. The cherub passage (Eze 28:15/18) addresses injustice emerging within a created spiritual being but not adversarial agency operating against humans. CHAR-22 operates as a direct human moral-conduct failure within the human-God framework.

---

  1. Prompt count:       24/24 ✓ (T4.1.1–T4.1.4, T4.2.1–T4.2.4, T4.3.1–T4.3.4, T4.4.1–T4.4.4, T4.5.1–T4.5.4, T4.6.1–T4.6.4)
  2. Scope marker:       24× [CHAR-22] ✓
  3. Citation discipline: All E findings cite specific verses or VCGs ✓ (T4.6.1/T4.6.3 are S)
  4. No cross-tier refs:  ✓
  5. Distinct openings:   ✓
  6. Verse-corpus check:  ✓ (all cited: Lev 19:15/35; Deu 32:4; 2Sa 7:10; 2Ch 19:7; Job 6:29/15:16/18:21/34:32; Psa 43:1/53:1/58:2/82:2/92:15/107:42/125:3; Pro 22:8; Isa 61:8; Jer 2:5; Hos 10:13; Mic 3:10; Zep 3:5/13; Eze 18:8/26/28:15/18/33:15; Luk 16:10/11; Mat 5:45; 1Cor 6:9; Heb 6:10; 1Pe 3:18; 2Pe 2:9; Rev 18:5; Rom 3:5 — all in §3)
  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T5.


---

## T5 — Transformation, Sequence, Mechanism (21 prompts)

---

### T5

#### T5.1.1 (cf=16916, E) — Does this characteristic produce transformation in the person — and if so, does it change the person's condition, the person's orientation to their condition, or both?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Both. (1) *Negative condition change*: Hos 10:13 — "you have plowed iniquity; you have reaped injustice" — the condition is transformed into one of self-produced harvest of wrong; Pro 22:8 — "whoever sows injustice will reap calamity" — calamity as the transformed condition; (2) *Negative orientation change*: Psa 53:1 — "there is no God" in the heart — the fundamental orientation toward God is negated; Hos 10:13 — "trusted in your own way" — self-trust as the transformed orientation; (3) *Positive transformation through cessation*: Eze 33:15 — "not doing injustice, he shall surely live" — the condition of life restored; Zep 3:13 — the remnant "shall do no injustice" — the total elimination of injustice as orientation in the purified community.

---

#### T5.1.2 (cf=16917, E) — Is the transformation produced by this characteristic reversible or irreversible in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Both dimensions evidenced. (1) *Reversible through cessation and restitution*: Eze 33:15 — the wicked who stops doing injustice and restores pledges "shall surely live"; Job 34:32 — "I will do it no more" — volitional reversibility; (2) *Eschatologically finalised for the persistent unjust*: 1Cor 6:9 — "the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God" — the final eschatological irreversibility for the character-level unjust; 2Pe 2:9 — "keep the unrighteous under punishment until the day of judgment" — sustained divine custody awaiting final judgment; (3) *Character-level resistance*: Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in very little is also dishonest in much" — the fixed character-scale suggests deep constitutional resistance to reversal, though Eze 33:15 keeps the path open.

---

#### T5.1.3 (cf=16918, E) — If the evidence is silent on transformation, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Transformation evidence is extensive in both negative and positive directions (T5.1.1 and T5.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.2.1 (cf=16919, E) — Does the verse evidence describe a sequence of inner states through which this characteristic moves the person — a before, during, and after?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — two sequences. (1) *Injustice-to-judgment sequence*: God-ignorance (Job 18:21 — "knows not God") → heart-devising of wrong (Psa 58:2; Psa 64:6) → enacting with hands (Psa 58:2 — "hands deal out violence") → sowing injustice (Pro 22:8) → reaping calamity (Pro 22:8) → God remembering (Rev 18:5) → judgment (2Pe 2:9); (2) *Cessation-to-life sequence*: acknowledging unseen iniquity (Job 34:32 — "teach me what I do not see") → committing to cease (Job 34:32 — "I will do it no more") → restoring pledges (Eze 33:15) → not doing injustice (Eze 18:8) → walking in statutes of life (Eze 33:15) → living (Eze 33:15 — "he shall surely live").

---

#### T5.2.2 (cf=16920, E) — What are those states, and what does the sequence reveal about how this characteristic works?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The injustice-to-judgment sequence reveals that CHAR-22 operates as a progressive harvest cycle: the heart devises → the hands sow → calamity is reaped. Pro 22:8's agricultural metaphor names the mechanism precisely: sowing injustice produces calamity as its crop — the consequence is proportionate and natural, not arbitrary. The cessation-to-life sequence reveals that the reversal requires engaging the same sequence in reverse: volitional acknowledgment (replacing heart-denial) → restitution (replacing violent taking) → righteous conduct (replacing injustice) → life (replacing the calamity-harvest).

---

#### T5.2.3 (cf=16921, E) — If the evidence is silent on sequence, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Sequence is evidenced in both directions (T5.2.1 and T5.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.3.1 (cf=16922, E) — What mechanism does this characteristic use to produce change in the person — discipline, encounter, gradual formation, sudden transformation, or something else?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three change-mechanisms. (1) *Harvest-sowing mechanism*: Pro 22:8 — "whoever sows injustice will reap calamity" — the agricultural mechanism; injustice is the seed that produces calamity as its proportionate harvest; Hos 10:13 — "you have plowed iniquity; you have reaped injustice" — the plowing produces the harvest; (2) *Heart-to-hands chain*: Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence" — the inner devising is the mechanism that drives the outer enactment; (3) *Scaling mechanism*: Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in very little is also dishonest in much" — the inner character-trait scales consistently outward; the mechanism is character-consistency, not circumstance-response.

---

#### T5.3.2 (cf=16923, E) — Does the evidence distinguish between mechanisms in different contexts?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes across three VCG registers. (1) *VCG-01 (judicial/commercial)*: mechanism is partiality and bribery corrupting the discernment faculty (2Ch 19:7; Psa 82:2) — the material incentive distorts inner judgment; (2) *VCG-02 (oppression)*: mechanism is the heart devising and hands enacting (Psa 58:2) — the deliberate inner-to-outer violence chain; (3) *VCG-03 (character of the unjust)*: mechanism is the scaling character-consistency (Luk 16:10) — the same inner character produces injustice at every scale through the same fixed-trait mechanism.

---

#### T5.3.3 (cf=16924, E) — If the evidence is silent on mechanism, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Mechanisms are evidenced across all three VCG contexts (T5.3.1 and T5.3.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.4.1 (cf=16925, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic operating in relation to suffering or affliction — as a response to it, a product of it, or a context for it?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — in two directions. (1) *Injustice producing suffering in victims*: 2Sa 7:10 — "violent men shall afflict them" — the injustice of violent men directly produces affliction in Israel; Job 6:29 — "let no injustice be done" — Job's suffering is produced by injustice threatening his vindication; Psa 43:1 — "from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me" — the unjust man's oppression as the source of the psalmist's affliction; (2) *Injustice producing suffering in the agent*: Pro 22:8 — "whoever sows injustice will reap calamity" — the agent's own suffering as harvest of their injustice; Job 24:20 — "wickedness is broken like a tree" — the injustice-agent's eventual structural collapse.

---

#### T5.4.2 (cf=16926, E) — Does suffering deepen, test, reveal, or produce this characteristic in the person?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Suffering *reveals* injustice as its cause (Hos 10:13 — "you have reaped injustice" reveals the iniquity that was plowed) and *is produced* by injustice (Pro 22:8 — calamity as injustice's harvest). Psa 125:3 — "the scepter of wickedness shall not rest on the land allotted to the righteous, lest the righteous stretch out their hands to do wrong" — sustained exposure to injustice's rule produces the potential to do wrong even in the righteous. Suffering under injustice can therefore *generate* injustice in the victim when the pressure becomes sustained enough.

---

#### T5.4.3 (cf=16927, E) — If the evidence is silent on the relationship to suffering, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — The relationship between injustice and suffering is evidenced (2Sa 7:10; Job 6:29; Psa 43:1; Pro 22:8; Psa 125:3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.5.1 (cf=16928, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic participating in the longer arc of character formation and sanctification — shaping the person over time toward greater likeness?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — as both the negative formation arc's scaling property and the eschatological character-type. (1) *Negative formation*: Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in very little is also dishonest in much" — injustice as a forming character-property that consolidates across all scales; the habituation to small injustices forms the character that then commits large ones; (2) *Emergence within a formed being*: Eze 28:15 — "you were blameless in your ways... till unrighteousness was found in you" — injustice can emerge within an already-formed, previously blameless being; formation does not immunize against the emergence.

---

#### T5.5.2 (cf=16929, E) — What does the evidence reveal about the role of this characteristic in that longer arc?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Injustice functions as the *other-directed expression of the fallen character's formation*: as the inner character is formed toward wrongdoing (heart-devising — Psa 58:2; God-ignorance — Job 18:21; self-trust — Hos 10:13), the outward conduct toward others correspondingly becomes unjust. Luk 16:10's scaling principle names the formation-mechanism precisely: small-scale dishonesty is not a minor failing but the formation of the character-trait that will scale to large injustice. Formation in injustice is incremental but consistent.

---

#### T5.5.3 (cf=16930, E) — If the evidence is silent on formation and sanctification, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Formation evidence is present through character-scaling (Luk 16:10), emergence within the previously blameless (Eze 28:15), and the cessation-and-restoration arc (Eze 33:15). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.6.1 (cf=16931, E) — Does the verse evidence point this characteristic toward an eschatological fullness — a future state toward which its present operation is oriented?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — two eschatological endpoints. (1) *Judgment of the unjust*: 2Pe 2:9 — "keep the unrighteous under punishment until the day of judgment" — current custody awaiting final judgment; Rev 18:5 — God remembers Babylon's accumulated iniquities awaiting judgment; 1Cor 6:9 — "the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom of God" — the definitive eschatological exclusion; (2) *Total elimination of injustice in the remnant*: Zep 3:13 — "those who are left in Israel shall do no injustice and speak no lies" — the eschatological fullness is the community that no longer practices injustice; Mat 5:45 — "sends rain on the just and on the unjust" — even common grace extends to the unjust until the eschatological separation.

---

#### T5.6.2 (cf=16932, E) — What does the present experience of this characteristic anticipate about its future fullness?

**[CHAR-22]** E — For the persistent unjust: 2Pe 2:9's current custody under punishment anticipates the day-of-judgment verdict; Rev 18:5's "God has remembered" anticipates the reckoning at which the heaped-high sins are adjudicated. For the addressing-injustice person: Eze 33:15's "he shall surely live" anticipates the eschatological life of the purified community (Zep 3:13 — "do no injustice"); the present act of withholding the hand from injustice (Eze 18:8) anticipates the eschatological remnant's permanent non-injustice.

---

#### T5.6.3 (cf=16933, E) — If the evidence is silent on eschatological trajectory, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Eschatological trajectory is evidenced in both judgment (2Pe 2:9; 1Cor 6:9; Rev 18:5) and elimination (Zep 3:13) directions. This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T5.7.1 (cf=16934, E) — Where T2.8 has identified a constitutional deposit from sustained operation of this characteristic, what developmental consequence does that deposit produce over time?

**[CHAR-22]** E — T2.8 identified three deposit forms: divine memory-record (Rev 18:5), fixed character (Luk 16:10), shameless conscience (Zep 3:5). Developmental consequences. (1) *Divine memory deposit*: Rev 18:5 — "her sins are heaped high as heaven" — the developmental consequence is the accumulating height of the record, moving toward the threshold of judgment; God's sustained memory of injustice develops toward the eschatological accounting; (2) *Fixed character deposit*: Luk 16:10 — dishonest across all scales — the character-deposit develops toward complete character-consolidation as the unjust type (VCG-03); the small-scale dishonesty develops into the large-scale character-consistency; (3) *Shameless conscience deposit*: Zep 3:5 — develops toward complete moral insensibility — the progression from shame-capable to shame-insensible.

---

#### T5.7.2 (cf=16935, E) — Does the evidence indicate generational consequence — a deposit carried forward beyond the individual?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — through two passages. (1) Psa 125:3 — "the scepter of wickedness shall not rest on the land allotted to the righteous, lest the righteous stretch out their hands to do wrong" — sustained injustice's rule over the land creates generational pressure that could corrupt even the righteous across time; (2) Deu 32:4 — God's "without iniquity" character established in the context of the Song of Moses addressing a perverse generation (Deu 32:20 — CHAR-21 adjacent) — the generational injustice context implies that the community's accumulated injustice creates the condition in which the next generation is formed.

---

#### T5.7.3 (cf=16936, E) — If T2.8 found no deposit, note this explicitly and close T5.7.

**[CHAR-22]** E — T2.8 found deposit evidence. T5.7.1 and T5.7.2 address the developmental and generational consequences. This prompt is superseded.

---

T5 self-evaluation:
  1. Prompt count:       21/21 ✓ (T5.1.1–T5.1.3, T5.2.1–T5.2.3, T5.3.1–T5.3.3, T5.4.1–T5.4.3, T5.5.1–T5.5.3, T5.6.1–T5.6.3, T5.7.1–T5.7.3)
  2. Scope marker:       21× [CHAR-22] ✓

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T6.

## T6 — Inter-characteristic Relationships (24 prompts)

---

### T6

#### T6.1.1 (cf=16937, E) — Which adjacent characteristics appear most frequently alongside this one in the verse evidence?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Four adjacent characteristics co-occur within the 72-verse corpus. (1) *CHAR-21 (Perversion as inner inversion)*: Eze 28:15 — "till unrighteousness was found in you" alongside the cherub's perversion; the inner structural distortion (CHAR-21) produces the unjust conduct (CHAR-22); (2) *CHAR-18 (Iniquity as accumulated moral crime)*: Eze 28:18 — "by the multitude of your iniquities, in the unrighteousness of your trade" — a.von (iniquity) and a.vel (injustice) paired; Rev 18:5 — "her sins are heaped high as heaven, and God has remembered her iniquities (*adikēmata*)" — adikēma and accumulation; (3) *CHAR-1 (Wilful sinning)*: Psa 53:1 — "doing abominable iniquity (*a.vel*)" alongside sinning (*cha.ta*); (4) *CHAR-19 (Transgression)*: Eze 18:24 — "does injustice and does the same abominations" — injustice and rebellion paired.

---

#### T6.1.2 (cf=16938, E) — What does the pattern of co-occurrence reveal about this characteristic's place in the inner-being landscape?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The co-occurrence pattern reveals CHAR-22 as the *other-directed conduct face* of M10: while CHAR-21 is the inner structural distortion and CHAR-18 is the accumulated crime-weight, CHAR-22 is the specific expression of those inner states as wrongdoing toward persons and against right order. CHAR-22 is M10's character at the interface between inner state (where CHAR-21 and CHAR-18 operate) and outer conduct (the harmful treatment of others). It is also M10's institutional-social characteristic: injustice corrupts judicial, commercial, and civic institutions.

---

#### T6.1.3 (cf=16939, E) — If no significant co-occurrence patterns emerge, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Co-occurrence patterns are evidenced (T6.1.1 and T6.1.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.2.1 (cf=16940, E) — Does the verse evidence show this characteristic consistently preceding, following, or accompanying another characteristic in a sequence?

**[CHAR-22]** E — CHAR-22 consistently *follows* CHAR-21 (the inner perversion generates the unjust conduct: Psa 58:2 — heart devises → hands enact) and CHAR-18 (the accumulated iniquity produces the specific acts of injustice). CHAR-22 consistently *precedes* CHAR-17 (the guilt-state produced by the recognised injustice: Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity" — the conscience still functioning). CHAR-22 consistently *accompanies* CHAR-13 (sin as divine record): Rev 18:5 — "God has remembered her iniquities" — the injustice is simultaneously committed (CHAR-22) and recorded (CHAR-13).

---

#### T6.2.2 (cf=16941, E) — What does the sequence reveal — is the relationship causal, developmental, or correlational?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The relationship with CHAR-21 is *generative*: the inner structural distortion (CHAR-21 — perverted heart — Pro 6:14) generates the unjust conduct (CHAR-22 — hands dealing violence — Psa 58:2). The relationship with CHAR-18 is *correlational*: injustice (CHAR-22) and iniquity (CHAR-18) co-occur in the same events (Eze 28:18 — "iniquities... unrighteousness of your trade") as twin aspects of the same moral failure. The relationship with CHAR-17 is *temporal*: the recognised injustice (CHAR-22) produces the guilt-state (CHAR-17) when the conscience is still operational.

---

#### T6.2.3 (cf=16942, E) — If no sequential pattern is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Sequential patterns (generative, correlational, temporal) are evidenced (T6.2.1 and T6.2.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.3.1 (cf=16943, E) — Does this characteristic produce another characteristic in the verse evidence — and if so, which one, and by what mechanism?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes. (1) *Calamity*: Pro 22:8 — "whoever sows injustice will reap calamity" — the calamity is CHAR-22's direct consequence (not itself a M10 characteristic but the outer result); (2) *CHAR-17 (Guilt)*: Job 34:32 — "if I have done iniquity, I will do it no more" — the recognised injustice produces the guilt-state that motivates cessation; (3) *Suffering in victims*: 2Sa 7:10/Psa 43:1 — injustice produces the affliction and distress in those it is done toward; (4) *Exclusion from kingdom*: 1Cor 6:9 — "the unrighteous will not inherit the kingdom" — the sustained unjust character produces the eschatological exclusion.

---

#### T6.3.2 (cf=16944, E) — Is this characteristic produced by another — and if so, which one?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes. (1) *CHAR-21 (Perversion)*: the perverted heart (Pro 6:14 — CHAR-21) generates the hand-enacted violence (Psa 58:2 — CHAR-22); the inner structural distortion produces the outer unjust conduct; (2) *God-denial (heart-atheism — Psa 53:1)*: the fool's heart-assertion "there is no God" produces the unjust conduct — the theological-cognitive failure drives the moral-conduct failure; (3) *Self-trust*: Hos 10:13 — "trusted in your own way" — the misplaced self-reliance produces the harvested injustice.

---

#### T6.3.3 (cf=16945, E) — Is this characteristic a constituent element of another, or does another characteristic constitute part of this one?

**[CHAR-22]** E — CHAR-22 is the *conduct-expression dimension* of CHAR-21: the inner structural distortion (CHAR-21 — perverted heart) expresses outward as unjust conduct toward others (CHAR-22 — hands dealing violence). CHAR-22's VCG-03 (character of the unjust person — adikos) is the person-type dimension that M26 (Righteousness/Justice) addresses from its positive side: the adikos is the person whose M26 character is absent. CHAR-22 IS M10's structural opposite of M26's righteousness-characteristic.

---

#### T6.3.4 (cf=16946, E) — If no causal or constitutive relationship is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Causal and constitutive relationships are evidenced (T6.3.1–T6.3.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.4.1 (cf=16947, E) — Does this characteristic share vocabulary terms with other characteristics in the programme?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The a.vel/av.lah (H5766A/B) root shares the *ʿāwal* base with the broader iniquity vocabulary family adjacent to CHAR-18's a.von (H5771G — different root but conceptual adjacency). The NT adikos (G0094) shares the alpha-privative *a-* + *dike* (justice) construction with other NT justice-vocabulary, placing CHAR-22 at the interface of M10 and M26. The av.val (H5767) agent-noun is closely adjacent to the a.vel family.

---

#### T6.4.2 (cf=16948, E) — Does vocabulary sharing extend to root-level architecture — a shared root that generates terms across two or more characteristics?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The a.vel/av.lah root (*ʿāwal* — to act unjustly) does not share its root with CHAR-18's a.von (*ʿāwōn*) despite conceptual adjacency. The NT adikos/adikēma family shares the Greek *dik-* root with the positive M26 vocabulary (*dikaiosynē* — righteousness; *dikē* — justice) — confirming CHAR-22's structural position as the antonym-characteristic of M26 at the root level.

---

#### T6.4.3 (cf=16949, E) — What does vocabulary sharing reveal about the conceptual relationship between characteristics?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The adikos/adikēma vocabulary sharing with M26's *dik-* root confirms CHAR-22 as M10's M26-interface characteristic: injustice is definitionally the negation of justice/righteousness. The M26 cross-register flag on all CHAR-22 terms is linguistically grounded — adikos literally means "not-just." CHAR-22 is the only M10 characteristic whose primary NT vocabulary is the direct lexical negation of an adjacent cluster's positive characteristic.

---

#### T6.4.4 (cf=16950, E) — If no significant vocabulary sharing is evidenced, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Root-level sharing (adikos/adikēma as direct negation of M26's dik- root) and conceptual adjacency (a.vel/*ʿāwal* adjacent to a.von/*ʿāwōn*) are evidenced (T6.4.1–T6.4.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.5.1 (cf=16951, E) — Which adjacent characteristic most closely resembles this one — and what precisely distinguishes them?

**[CHAR-22]** E — CHAR-21 (Perversion as inner inversion) most closely resembles CHAR-22 — both address the inner moral faculty's departure from the right moral standard. The precise distinction: CHAR-21 is the *inner structural distortion of the moral faculty* (the twisted heart, the depraved mind, the perverted speech — all primarily constitutional); CHAR-22 is the *other-directed expression of that distortion as unjust conduct toward persons* (the devised wrong, the violent hand, the false weight, the partial judgment). CHAR-21 is constitutive; CHAR-22 is relational and conduct-specific.

---

#### T6.5.2 (cf=16952, E) — Where the evidence shows apparent overlap, what is the precise boundary?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The overlap is at Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence on earth" — where CHAR-21 (the perverted heart — inner inversion) and CHAR-22 (the hands dealing violence — unjust conduct toward others) are sequential in the same verse. The precise boundary: the devising in the heart belongs to CHAR-21 (perversion of the inner faculty's deliberative capacity); the hands dealing violence belongs to CHAR-22 (the unjust conduct enacted toward persons). CHAR-21 covers the inner moral faculty's distortion; CHAR-22 covers the outer harmful conduct that distortion produces.

---

#### T6.5.3 (cf=16953, E) — Is the distinction between this characteristic and its nearest neighbour one of degree, kind, direction, or constitutional level?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The distinction is one of *direction*: CHAR-21 is primarily *inward* (the twisted inner constitution); CHAR-22 is primarily *other-directed* (the unjust conduct toward persons). Both operate at the heart/will level, but CHAR-21's defining orientation is the inner distortion of the moral faculty itself, while CHAR-22's defining orientation is the harm done to others through that distorted faculty's expression. The same perverted heart (CHAR-21 — Pro 6:14) produces the hands that deal violence (CHAR-22 — Psa 58:2) — the distinction is directional.

---

#### T6.5.4 (cf=16954, E) — If no significant distinction work is required, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Distinction work is required and evidenced (T6.5.1–T6.5.3). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.6.1 (cf=16955, E) — Does any verse in this characteristic's evidence base also function as a primary anchor in another characteristic's study?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Several. (1) Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence" — shared with CHAR-21 (heart-devising as perversity) and CHAR-1 (the wilful sinning acts); (2) Eze 18:24 — "a righteous person turns away from his righteousness and does injustice" — shared with CHAR-20 (faithlessness — turning from righteousness) and CHAR-19 (transgression — the turning as crossing); (3) Psa 53:1 — "the fool says in his heart, 'There is no God'... doing abominable iniquity" — shared with CHAR-21 (perversion as inner inversion of the moral foundation); (4) 1Pe 3:18 — "the righteous for the unrighteous" — shared with the M26 (Righteousness) cluster's Christological usage.

---

#### T6.6.2 (cf=16956, E) — What does the shared anchor reveal about the relationship between the two characteristics?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Psa 58:2 shared with CHAR-21 confirms the heart-to-hands chain as the boundary between CHAR-21 (heart-devising) and CHAR-22 (hand-enacted violence); both characteristics are present in the same verse at different levels. Eze 18:24 shared with CHAR-20 confirms that the turning from righteousness to injustice is simultaneously CHAR-20's faithlessness (covenant-betrayal) and CHAR-22's injustice (unjust conduct) — the same volitional turn generates both characteristics. 1Pe 3:18 shared with M26 confirms CHAR-22 as M10's structural point of contact with M26's righteousness-cluster.

---

#### T6.6.3 (cf=16957, E) — If no shared verse anchors are identified, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Multiple shared verse anchors are identified across CHAR-1, CHAR-19, CHAR-20, CHAR-21, and M26 (T6.6.1 and T6.6.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

#### T6.7.1 (cf=16958, E) — How many of this characteristic's confirmed analytical dimensions are shared with another characteristic in the programme?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Two dimensions are partially shared: (1) *The heart-devising dimension* (Psa 58:2) is partially shared with CHAR-21 (perversion of the inner faculty); (2) *The volitional-turn dimension* (Eze 18:24 — turns and commits injustice) is partially shared with CHAR-20 (faithlessness — the covenant-turn). CHAR-22's unique dimensions — (a) the shamelessness diagnostic (Zep 3:5 — "the unjust knows no shame"), (b) the scaling character-consistency (Luk 16:10 — dishonest across all scales), (c) the M26 direct-negation (adikos as "not-just"), and (d) the institutional-social scope (judicial, commercial, civic, cosmic) — are not shared with any other M10 characteristic studied.

---

#### T6.7.2 (cf=16959, E) — What does the pattern of dimensional sharing reveal about the relationship between this characteristic and those it shares dimensions with?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The minimal dimensional sharing confirms CHAR-22 as analytically distinct within M10: its unique dimensions (shamelessness as diagnostic, scaling character-consistency, M26-interface, institutional scope) describe injustice's specific other-directed character that no other M10 characteristic covers. CHAR-22 is M10's conduct-toward-others characteristic — it is the only M10 characteristic that is primarily defined by what is done to other persons and to social institutions rather than primarily by what happens within the inner person.

---

#### T6.7.3 (cf=16960, E) — If dimensional sharing data is not yet available, note this explicitly.

**[CHAR-22]** E — Dimensional sharing is evidenced with minimal overlap (T6.7.1 and T6.7.2). This prompt is superseded.

---

  → PASS. Proceeding to Tier T7.

## T7 — Lexical, Literary, and Human Science (20 prompts)

---

### T7

#### T7.1.1 (cf=16961, E) — What are the primary Hebrew and Greek terms for this characteristic — and what do their root meanings reveal?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Five primary term-families. (1) **a.vel (H5766A)** / **av.lah (H5766B)** — noun/verb "injustice, wrong, violence, wickedness" — root *ʿāwal* meaning "to act unjustly, to be crooked in dealings"; names the act and condition of moral-conduct wrongness; (2) **av.val (H5767)** — "the unjust one, the unrighteous person" — agent-noun from *ʿāwal*; (3) **a.vil (H5760)** — "ungodly, morally lawless" — from a root denoting moral worthlessness; (4) **adikos (G0094)** — NT "unjust, unrighteous" — Greek compound *a-* (not) + *dikē* (justice, right); literally "not-right/not-just"; the alpha-privative construction names injustice as the negation of justice; (5) **adikēma (G0092)** — NT "wrongdoing, crime, injustice" — the act-noun; the specific deed of injustice. Together the terms reveal: injustice as crookedness in dealings (*ʿāwal*), as person-type (*av.val/adikos*), and as specific legal-moral crime (*adikēma*) — three levels of the same moral failure.

---

#### T7.1.2 (cf=16962, E) — What is the grammatical range of the primary term (noun, verb, adjective, participle) — and what does that range reveal about how the characteristic operates?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Wide across OT and NT. (1) *A.vel (H5766A)*: noun — "injustice/wrong" (Lev 19:15 — "do no injustice in court"; Job 34:10 — "far be it from God that he should do wrong"); (2) *Av.lah (H5766B)*: noun — "wickedness/injustice" (Psa 58:2 — "devise wrongs"; 2Ch 19:7 — "no injustice with the Lord"); (3) *Av.val (H5767)*: agent-noun — "the unrighteous" (Job 27:7 — "be as the unrighteous"; Job 29:17 — "broke the fangs of the unrighteous"); (4) *Adikos (G0094)*: adjective/noun — "unjust/unrighteous" (Mat 5:45 — "on the just and on the unjust"; Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in a very little"; 1Cor 6:9 — "the unrighteous will not inherit"); (5) *Adikēma (G0092)*: act-noun — "wrongdoing/crime" (Act 18:14 — "a matter of wrongdoing"; Rev 18:5 — "her iniquities"). The grammatical range covers the condition (a.vel — wrong-state), the act (adikēma — wrong-deed), and the person (av.val/adikos — wrong-person).

---

#### T7.1.3 (cf=16963, E) — What is the semantic range of the primary term — across what breadth of meaning does it operate?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The terms collectively cover: (1) *Judicial injustice*: a.vel (Lev 19:15 — "no injustice in court"; Psa 82:2 — "judge unjustly"); (2) *Commercial injustice*: a.vel (Lev 19:35 — "no wrong in measures"); (3) *Violent oppression*: av.lah (2Sa 7:10 — "violent men"; Psa 58:2 — "deal out violence"); (4) *Speech-injustice*: av.lah (Job 6:30 — "is there any injustice on my tongue?"; Isa 59:3 — "tongue mutters wickedness"); (5) *Character-level injustice*: adikos (Luk 16:10 — "dishonest in very little and in much"); (6) *Legal-actionable wrongdoing*: adikēma (Act 18:14 — "a matter of wrongdoing or vicious crime"); (7) *Cosmic-scale crime*: adikēma (Rev 18:5 — "God has remembered her iniquities").

---

#### T7.1.4 (cf=16964, E) — Does the vocabulary include terms that distinguish distinct aspects of this characteristic — disposition versus act, received versus given, condition versus quality?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — the term-families distinguish three aspects. (1) *The wrong-act aspect*: a.vel/av.lah/adikēma — the specific doing of wrong; (2) *The wrong-character aspect*: av.val/adikos — the person constituted by injustice; (3) *The wrong-condition aspect*: av.lah as a state that can "dwell in one's tents" (Job 11:14) or be "drunk like water" (Job 15:16). These three aspects (act, character, condition) are linguistically distinguished and analytically separable — CHAR-22 operates at all three levels simultaneously.

---

#### T7.1.5 (cf=16965, E) — Does the vocabulary include a term for the structural opposite or absence of this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — and uniquely explicitly. Deu 32:4 — "without iniquity (*av.lah*), just and upright is he" — the structural opposite is "just and upright" (*tsaddiq* and *yashar*) explicitly named in opposition; Zep 3:5 — "the Lord is righteous; he does no injustice (*av.lah*)" — *tsaddiq* (righteous) as the structural opposite; Mat 5:45 — "on the just and on the unjust" — *dikaios* (just/righteous) is the direct Greek structural opposite of *adikos*. The M26 cross-register cluster provides the full structural opposite vocabulary.

---

#### T7.1.6 (cf=16966, E) — Does the vocabulary include a person-type term — a term for the one who habitually possesses or exercises this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — two distinct person-type terms. (1) *Av.val (H5767)*: OT agent-noun — "the unrighteous one" (Job 27:7; 29:17; 31:3; Zep 3:5) — the OT person-type characterised by injustice as a defining moral identity; (2) *Adikos (G0094)*: NT adjective-as-noun — "the unjust" (Mat 5:45; Luk 18:11; Act 24:15; Rom 3:5; 1Cor 6:1/9; Heb 6:10; 1Pe 3:18; 2Pe 2:9) — the NT person-type; used in eight NT verses, making it the most frequently deployed person-type term across the Phase 9 characteristics.

---

#### T7.1.7 (cf=16967, E) — Does the vocabulary include a supplication or seeking term — a term for the act of seeking this characteristic from another?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Yes — within the victim's plea register. Job 6:29 — "please turn; let no injustice be done" — the direct plea that no injustice be enacted; Psa 43:1 — "vindicate me, O God, and defend my cause against an ungodly people, from the deceitful and unjust man deliver me" — the plea for deliverance from the unjust as a seeking-term; Job 22:23 — "if you return to the Almighty you will be built up; if you remove injustice far from your tents" — Eliphaz's condition framing removal of injustice as the human seeking-act.

---

#### T7.1.8 (cf=16968, E) — What does the relationship between the OT Hebrew vocabulary and the NT Greek vocabulary reveal about continuity or development of this characteristic across the Testaments?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The OT/NT relationship reveals *conceptual continuity with legal-forensic precision in the NT*. The OT a.vel/av.lah vocabulary covers injustice broadly: judicial, commercial, violent, verbal. The NT adikos/adikēma vocabulary focuses on: the person-type (adikos — the unjust as a moral category) and the legal-actionable wrong (adikēma — the demonstrable crime). Rom 3:5 — "that God is unrighteous to inflict wrath" — uses adikos in a precisely theological-forensic argument. 1Pe 3:18 — "the righteous for the unrighteous" — deploys adikos in the Christological substitution context. The NT narrows and sharpens the OT's broader injustice vocabulary toward theological and forensic precision.

---

#### T7.1.9 (cf=16969, E) — Is there a term newly coined in the NT period for this characteristic — and if so, what does that coinage reveal?

**[CHAR-22]** E — *Adikēma (G0092)* — "wrongdoing, crime, injustice" — is the NT's distinctive contribution: the act-noun that names the specific legally-actionable deed of injustice rather than the character or condition. Act 18:14 — "if it were a matter of wrongdoing (*adikēma*) or vicious crime" — frames the term within Roman legal procedure; Rev 18:5 — "God has remembered her iniquities (*adikēmata*)" — deploys the term for cosmic-scale accumulated wrongdoings. The *adikēma* term frames injustice as a legally demonstrable, juridically accountable wrong — a precision the OT vocabulary does not provide with this specificity.

---

#### T7.1.10 (cf=16970, E) — What does the full vocabulary arc reveal about this characteristic's complete semantic range?

**[CHAR-22]** E — The vocabulary arc — from a.vel's judicial prohibition (Lev 19:15 — "do no injustice in court") through av.lah's violent-oppression register (Psa 58:2 — "your hands deal out violence") through av.val's person-type naming (Job 27:7/29:17 — "the unrighteous") through adikos's NT person-type precision (1Cor 6:9 — "the unrighteous will not inherit"; 1Pe 3:18 — "the righteous for the unrighteous") through adikēma's legal-crime specificity (Act 18:14; Rev 18:5) — reveals the complete arc of injustice from its most concrete prohibitions (judicial, commercial) through its person-type constitution to its legal-theological precision and eschatological reckoning. The arc confirms CHAR-22 as M10's most institutionally specific characteristic and M26's direct antonym-partner.

---

#### T7.2.1 (cf=16971, E) — What is the function of this characteristic's primary term within its primary verse — what role does it play in the sentence and argument?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Three primary anchors. (1) *Psa 58:2* — "in your hearts you devise wrongs (*av.lah*); your hands deal out violence on earth" — av.lah functions as the object of the devising: it is what the heart schemes; its position as the scheming's content names it as the substance of the planned wrongdoing; (2) *Luk 16:10* — "one who is dishonest (*adikos*) in a very little is also dishonest (*adikos*) in much" — adikos functions as the character-predicate; its repetition in both clauses names dishonesty/injustice as the consistent character-property; (3) *Deu 32:4* — "without iniquity (*av.lah*), just and upright is he" — av.lah functions as the negated quality; its explicit negation establishes injustice as the standard-opposite of God's character.

---

#### T7.2.2 (cf=16972, E) — What literary form carries the primary verse evidence (narrative, psalm, wisdom, prophecy, epistle, apocalyptic) — and what does that form require for responsible interpretation?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Five literary forms. (1) *Covenant law* (Lev 19:15/35; Deu 25:16): direct divine prohibition; (2) *Wisdom literature* (Job corpus — 6:29/30/11:14/13:7/15:16/22:23/27:4/29:17/31:3/34:10/32/36:23; Pro 22:8; Psa 37:1/58:2/64:6): requires reading within the practical-moral and theodicy frameworks; (3) *Prophetic oracle* (Isa 59:3/61:8; Jer 2:5; Eze 3:20/18:8/24/26/28:15/18/33:13/15/18; Hos 10:13; Zep 3:5/13): requires reading within the covenant-accusation and judgment-oracle frameworks; (4) *Historical narrative/theological assessment* (2Sa 3:34/7:10; 2Ch 19:7): requires reading within the Chronicler's theological framework; (5) *NT epistle and gospel* (Mat 5:45; Luk 16:10/11/18:11; Rom 3:5; 1Cor 6:1/9; Heb 6:10; 1Pe 3:18; 2Pe 2:9): requires reading within the theological-ethical argument and gospel-proclamation frameworks.

---

#### T7.2.3 (cf=16973, E) — What is the logical structure of key arguments in the verse evidence — what are the premises and conclusions?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Four key argument structures. (1) *Luk 16:10's scaling argument*: premise — one who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much; conclusion — dishonesty/injustice is a character-property, not a scale-dependent behaviour; structure — small-to-large scaling as character-consistency proof; (2) *Deu 32:4's definitional-negation argument*: premise — God is "without iniquity, just and upright"; structure — God's character is defined partly through the explicit negation of injustice; injustice is thus defined by what God is not; (3) *Pro 22:8's harvest argument*: premise — whoever sows injustice; conclusion — will reap calamity; structure — agricultural metaphor: the proportionate natural consequence of inner wrong; (4) *1Pe 3:18's substitution argument*: premise — "the righteous for the unrighteous"; purpose — "that he might bring us to God"; structure — Christ's righteousness applied to the unrighteous condition provides the basis for restored relationship.

---

#### T7.2.4 (cf=16974, E) — What contextual setting carries the primary verse evidence (judicial, liturgical, covenantal, communal, eschatological) — and what does that setting reveal?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Four contextual settings. (1) *Covenant-law context* (Lev 19:15/35; Deu 25:16): the Sinai community receiving direct divine prohibition in specific institutional settings (court, marketplace); (2) *Wisdom-theodicy context* (Job corpus): Job's repeated denial of injustice in his own conduct while contending with friends who charge it; (3) *Exile-judgment context* (Eze 3:20; 18:8/24/26/33:13/15/18): Ezekiel's personal-accountability framework addressing the exiles' individual moral responsibility; (4) *NT eschatological-ethical context* (1Cor 6:9; 1Pe 3:18; 2Pe 2:9): the NT's framing of injustice as the condition requiring Christ's substitution and facing eschatological judgment.

---

#### T7.2.5 (cf=16975, E) — Does any verse function as the primary anchor for this characteristic — the verse that most fully and directly expresses its essential character?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Two complementary primary anchors. (1) *Psa 58:2* — "no, in your hearts you devise wrongs; your hands deal out violence on earth" — primary anchor for the inner-to-outer mechanism: it most precisely names the constitutional sequence (heart → hands) and the double location (inner devising + outer enacting) that defines CHAR-22's inner-being operation; (2) *Luk 16:10* — "one who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much" — primary anchor for the character-consistency principle: it most precisely names the scaling property that makes injustice a character-trait rather than a situational behaviour and confirms that CHAR-22 is a dispositional characteristic.

---

#### T7.2.6 (cf=16976, E) — What does the primary anchor verse reveal that no other verse reveals?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Psa 58:2 uniquely reveals the explicit *constitutional sequence* of injustice: "in your HEARTS you devise wrongs" followed immediately by "your HANDS deal out violence" — naming both the inner origin (heart) and the outer instrument (hands) in direct sequential connection. No other verse in the 72-verse corpus makes this constitutional sequence so explicit. Luk 16:10 uniquely reveals the *scale-independence* of the unjust character: dishonesty in very little and dishonesty in much are the same character-trait; the unjust person is not situationally unjust but constitutively unjust regardless of stakes. Together: Psa 58:2 names the mechanism (heart-to-hands); Luk 16:10 names the character-stability (scales across all contexts).

---

#### T7.3.1 (cf=16977, E) — Which human science framework (psychology, moral philosophy, developmental psychology, sociology, anthropology, or other) is most useful as an interpretive lens for this characteristic?

**[CHAR-22]** E — **Procedural justice theory** (Tyler and Lind's group-value model of justice — people care deeply about fair process, not just fair outcomes) and **institutional corruption theory** (Dennis Thompson's *Ethics in Congress* on systemic/institutional rather than individual corruption) provide the most illuminating frameworks. Tyler's procedural justice illuminates why Lev 19:15's judicial injustice is so damaging — the court is the prototypical procedural-justice institution; its corruption strikes at the legitimacy of social order. Thompson's institutional corruption illuminates Mic 3:10 — "build Zion with blood" — how injustice becomes embedded in civic and governmental structures rather than remaining merely individual.

---

#### T7.3.2 (cf=16978, E) — Where the human science framework illuminates the verse evidence — making a finding more coherent or complete — what does it reveal?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Tyler's procedural justice illuminates why the corpus concentrates 20 verses on judicial and commercial injustice (VCG-01): these are the procedural-justice institutions whose fairness is foundational to social trust. Psa 82:2 — "judge unjustly and show partiality to the wicked" — the judge's partial judgment violates procedural fairness, generating the loss of social legitimacy that Psa 82's lament addresses. Thompson's institutional corruption illuminates Mic 3:10's "build Zion with blood" — the injustice has been institutionalised into the civic structure itself; it is no longer individual wrong but structural wrong embedded in the city's foundation.

---

#### T7.3.3 (cf=16979, E) — Where the verse evidence and the human science framework diverge, what does the divergence reveal?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Tyler's procedural justice treats institutional fairness as primarily a social-psychological concern (people's sense of being respected in procedures). The verse evidence treats judicial injustice as a violation of divine standard (Deu 32:4 — God "without iniquity") before it is a violation of social expectations. Thompson's institutional corruption treats systemic injustice as primarily a structural-political problem. The verse evidence treats it as a moral-inner problem: Psa 58:2 — "in your hearts you devise wrongs" — the systemic civic injustice (Mic 3:10) begins in individual hearts before it is embedded in structures. Both frameworks underweight the inner-constitutional origin (heart) that the biblical corpus consistently names as primary.

---

#### T7.3.4 (cf=16980, E) — Does the human science framework surface any aspect of this characteristic that the verse evidence has not yet addressed — and does that absence require further verse investigation?

**[CHAR-22]** E — Thompson's institutional corruption surfaces the *systemic normalisation of injustice* — how institutional structures can make individual wrongdoing appear legitimate or even required. Psa 82:2's judges have been doing this: systematically showing partiality to the wicked. The gap is the social-psychological mechanism by which individual judicial injustice (Psa 82:2 — showing partiality) is normalised across the judicial institution. No further verse investigation is needed; the gap is a human-science complement to the corpus's internal anatomy of injustice's social consequences.
